> 2. **Mixing process-WBS with product-WBS** — Never say "Welding, Painting, Blasting" as Level 1 of a Product-Oriented WBS. They are *processes*, not *products*. The correct Level 1 entries are *Hull, Machinery, Outfit, Electrical*.
> 3. **Ignoring units in EV/CPI/SPI calculations** — Always write **₹ lakh** (or the given currency) next to every numerical answer.
> 4. **Confusing matrix variants** — Weak, Balanced, Strong matrix distinction is a favourite 2-mark question. Memorise the degree of project manager authority.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — When asked to compare structures, always state "assuming the yard has ≥ 10 sister vessels in order book" or similar qualifying statements.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Organisation Structure** = *who reports to whom*; **WBS** = *what work gets done*.
- Three structures: **Functional, Product, Matrix** — remember the **"FPM"** mnemonic.
- **POBS** = Product-Oriented WBS — groups by **physical deliverable**, not by trade.
- Five WBS levels to remember: **Ship → System → Section → Block → Work Package**.
- **100 % Rule** at every level: children must exhaustively cover the parent.
- **WBS Dictionary** = scope card for every leaf node.
- **Earned Value (EV)** = % complete × BAC.
- **CPI > 1** → under budget; **CPI < 1** → over budget.
- **SPI > 1** → ahead of schedule; **SPI < 1** → behind schedule.
- **Outfitting density** (kg outfit / tonne steel) is a key KTU metric for ship complexity.
- **PERT expected time:** $t_E = (t_o + 4 t_m + t_p)/6$.
- Korean / Japanese mega-yards prefer **Product-Oriented** for series production; Indian repair yards stay **Functional**; Naval / multi-class yards adopt **Matrix**.
- Every WBS handover point = **Quality Hold Point** (dimensional, NDT, paint DFT).
- **Block productivity benchmark:** World-class ≈ **0.30–0.45 t/man-day**; Indian ≈ **0.10–0.20 t/man-day**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark question, allocate ~4 min planning, ~30 min writing, ~1 min verifying the **100 % Rule** and **units**. KTU examiners reward **structured, tabular, and sketch-supported** answers over prose-only answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Oriented Work Breakdown Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Oriented Work Breakdown Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is a hierarchical, deliverable-oriented decomposition of the total scope of work that a project team must carry out to accomplish the project objectives and produce the required deliverables. When the WBS is organized according to the **life-cycle processes and execution stages** of the project (rather than by physical sub-components or by organizational units), it is termed a **Process-Oriented Work Breakdown Structure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (PMI / PMBOK aligned):**
> A *Process-Oriented WBS* decomposes the ship construction project into its constituent engineering, fabrication, assembly, and commissioning **processes** in a top-down hierarchical tree, where each descending level represents a progressively finer definition of the work to be performed.

The process-oriented WBS is the preferred scheduling and cost-control tool in modern shipyards because shipbuilding is intrinsically a **sequence-driven, time-phased activity** that flows from raw steel reception to sea trials and delivery.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

- **WBS Element** – Any node in the hierarchical tree representing a piece of work.
- **Work Package** – The lowest level of the WBS; small enough to be estimated, scheduled, monitored, and assigned to a single responsible organization.
- **WBS Dictionary** – A document that provides detailed information about each WBS element (scope, responsible party, schedule, cost, quality requirements).
- **Control Account** – A management control point where scope, budget, actual cost, and schedule are integrated and reported.
- **Milestone** – A significant event with zero duration marking the completion of a major deliverable or process.
- **Activity** – A component of a work package, further decomposed for scheduling purposes.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Shipyard Kitchen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are running a large restaurant kitchen preparing a 7-course banquet. You could organize the staff in two ways:
>
> - **Product-Oriented WBS:** Group chefs by dish ("Chef for Soup", "Chef for Main Course", "Chef for Dessert"). This works only when each dish is independent.
> - **Process-Oriented WBS:** Group chefs by cooking technique ("Washing & Cutting Station", "Frying Station", "Plating & Garnishing Station"). All dishes pass through every station in sequence.
>
> A ship is built the **second** way: every block of steel is cut → formed → welded → painted → erected → outfitted, no matter which part of the ship it belongs to. Hence the WBS for a ship is naturally **process-oriented**, defining work by the **manufacturing stages** through which all components flow.

### 1.4 Standard Levels of Decomposition

| Level | Name in Shipbuilding | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | Project | "Vessel XYZ – 75,000 DWT Bulk Carrier" |
| **1** | Major Process / Phase | Hull Construction, Outfitting, Trials |
| **2** | Sub-Process | Steel Cutting, Block Assembly, Hull Erection |
| **3** | Work Package | Cutting of 50 stern frames, welding of deck panel #4 |
| **4** | Activity | Marking, shearing, edge preparation, tack welding |

The standard unit of measurement at the lowest level is typically **man-hours**, **tonnes of steel processed**, or **pieces fabricated**, with a typical industry cost code length of **4–6 digits** in the WBS dictionary.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" of Process-Oriented WBS in Shipbuilding

A modern ship consists of **hundreds of thousands of individual parts** manufactured in a flow that crosses multiple departments (steel stockyard, cutting shop, forming shop, welding bays, block assembly halls, outfitting quay, dry dock, quay, sea). Tracking the project by individual parts is impossible. Instead, the shipyard tracks the **processes** that those parts flow through.

The process-oriented WBS therefore provides:

1. **Visibility of Bottlenecks** – If the "Block Assembly" process is lagging, every downstream process is delayed.
2. **Standardized Work Packages** – The work for "welding 1 metre of butt joint" is the same whether the joint is in the bow or the engine room.
3. **Production-Line Scheduling** – WBS aligns with the physical flow of the ship in the yard, enabling **takt-time** and **line-of-balance** scheduling.
4. **Cost Aggregation** – Costs roll up process-wise (e.g., total painting cost), aiding benchmarking between vessels.
5. **Resource Levelling** – Identifies peak demand for welders, fitters, cranes, etc., across all vessels in the yard.

### 2.2 Hierarchical Logic – Step by Step

- **Step 1 – Define Project Boundary:** Identify the contract scope (one ship, or a series of ships).
- **Step 2 – Identify Major Processes:** List the top-level shipbuilding processes (Design, Procurement, Steel, Hull, Outfitting, Trials, Delivery).
- **Step 3 – Decompose Each Process into Sub-Processes:** Break Hull into "Cutting", "Forming", "Sub-Assembly", "Block Assembly", "Erection", "Welding".
- **Step 4 – Decompose into Work Packages:** Each sub-process is broken until a package can be:
  - Estimated in man-hours and cost
  - Scheduled with a clear start/end
  - Assigned to a single trade/foreman
- **Step 5 – Establish the WBS Dictionary:** Document scope, deliverables, responsible party, schedule milestones, budget, and quality criteria for every element.
- **Step 6 – Assign Control Accounts:** Group work packages under control accounts for performance reporting (Cost Performance Index, Schedule Performance Index).
- **Step 7 – Integrate with OBS (Organizational Breakdown Structure):** Map WBS to who is responsible; this is the **Responsibility Assignment Matrix (RAM / RACI chart)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The process-oriented WBS provides the data structure for the following project-control formulas. The standard cost of a work package is captured in the WBS dictionary and aggregated up the tree.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Planned Value (BCWS) | $PV = \text{Planned \% Complete} \times \text{Budget at Completion (BAC)}$ | Currency |
| 2 | Earned Value (BCWP) | $EV = \text{Actual \% Complete} \times \text{Budget at Completion (BAC)}$ | Currency |
| 3 | Actual Cost (ACWP) | $AC = \sum \text{Actual Costs Incurred on Work Packages}$ | Currency |
| 4 | Cost Variance | $CV = EV - AC$ | Currency; $CV \ge 0$ is favourable |
| 5 | Schedule Variance | $SV = EV - PV$ | Currency; $SV \ge 0$ is favourable |
| 6 | Cost Performance Index | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | $CPI \ge 1$ is favourable |
| 7 | Schedule Performance Index | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | $SPI \ge 1$ is favourable |
| 8 | Estimate at Completion | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | Currency |
| 9 | Estimate to Complete | $ETC = EAC - AC$ | Currency |
| 10 | To-Complete Performance Index | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | Ratio |
| 11 | WBS Roll-up Cost | $C_{\text{parent}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{\text{child},i}$ | Additive at every level |
| 12 | WBS Weight Factor | $w_i = \dfrac{B_i}{BAC}$ | Dimensionless; $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| 13 | Overall Project % Complete | $\%\text{Done} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \times p_i$ | Weighted by BAC |
| 14 | Productivity (tonnes/man-day) | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Steel Tonnage Processed}}{\text{Man-days Consumed}}$ | tonne/man-day |
| 15 | Manning Level | $M = \dfrac{\text{Total Man-hours Required}}{\text{Available Working Hours per Person}}$ | Number of workers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Marks for any WBS numerical question come from correctly identifying the BAC (Budget at Completion) of the work package, the planned vs. actual % complete of the WBS element, and then applying the variance / index formulas above. **Do not confuse CV with SV** — they have different reference baselines.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Project Control Offices (PCO)** in shipyards use the WBS to drive weekly progress meetings.
- **ERP Systems** (e.g., SAP, AVEVA, FORAN) store cost and progress keyed to WBS codes.
- **Class Societies and Owners** require WBS-linked **weight, cost, and progress reports** monthly.
- **Risk Registers** are typically maintained at the WBS work-package level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Rolling Up WBS Cost Using Bottom-Up Aggregation

**Problem Statement:** A ship hull construction is decomposed in a process-oriented WBS into three work packages under the sub-process "Block Assembly". The yard manager needs the total budgeted cost of the parent WBS element "Block Assembly".

**Given Data:**

| Work Package | Description | Budgeted Cost (Lakhs ₹) | Weight Factor $w_i$ | Actual % Complete $p_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| WP-1.2.1 | Forward block sub-assembly (8 blocks) | **120** | 0.40 | 75 % |
| WP-1.2.2 | Central block sub-assembly (12 blocks) | **150** | 0.50 | 60 % |
| WP-1.2.3 | Aft block sub-assembly (6 blocks) | **30**  | 0.10 | 90 % |
| **Total BAC** | | **300** | **1.00** | — |

**Step 1 – Compute the Budget at Completion (BAC) of the parent WBS element "Block Assembly".**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BAC_{\text{Block Assembly}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} B_i \\
&= B_1 + B_2 + B_3 \\
&= 120 + 150 + 30 \\
&= 300 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute the Weight Factor of each work package** (its share of the parent BAC).

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 &= \frac{B_1}{BAC} = \frac{120}{300} = 0.40 \\
w_2 &= \frac{B_2}{BAC} = \frac{150}{300} = 0.50 \\
w_3 &= \frac{B_3}{BAC} = \frac{30}{300} = 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute Earned Value (EV) of each work package** using $EV_i = p_i \times B_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EV_1 &= 0.75 \times 120 = 90 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
EV_2 &= 0.60 \times 150 = 90 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
EV_3 &= 0.90 \times 30 = 27 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute Planned Value (PV) of each work package** (assume 80 % planned complete at this reporting date for all WPs).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 0.80 \times 120 = 96 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
PV_2 &= 0.80 \times 150 = 120 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
PV_3 &= 0.80 \times 30 = 24 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Compute Actual Cost (AC) of each work package** (assume given: 100, 110, 25 Lakhs respectively).

$$
\begin{aligned}
AC_1 &= 100 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
AC_2 &= 110 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
AC_3 &= 25 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 – Roll-up: Sum the EVs to get parent EV.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EV_{\text{parent}} &= 90 + 90 + 27 = 207 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
PV_{\text{parent}} &= 96 + 120 + 24 = 240 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
AC_{\text{parent}} &= 100 + 110 + 25 = 235 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 – Compute Cost Variance and Schedule Variance at the parent level.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV_{\text{parent}} - AC_{\text{parent}} = 207 - 235 = -28 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
SV &= EV_{\text{parent}} - PV_{\text{parent}} = 207 - 240 = -33 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since both CV and SV are **negative**, the "Block Assembly" sub-process is **over budget** and **behind schedule**.

**Step 8 – Compute the Cost Performance Index (CPI).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{207}{235} \approx 0.881
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.881 of work has been earned. **Cost recovery factor < 1 ⇒ Over-budget sub-process.**

**Step 9 – Compute the Schedule Performance Index (SPI).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{207}{240} \approx 0.863
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: Only 86.3 % of the planned work has been completed ⇒ the sub-process is **running 13.7 % behind schedule**.

**Step 10 – Estimate at Completion (EAC) at the parent level assuming current CPI continues.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{300}{0.881} \approx 340.5 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the sub-process is now forecast to cost ₹340.5 Lakhs against the original BAC of ₹300 Lakhs — a **cost overrun of ₹40.5 Lakhs (≈ 13.5 %)** if corrective action is not taken.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Standard Process-Oriented WBS of a Merchant Ship

| WBS Level 1 (Major Process) | WBS Level 2 (Sub-Process) | WBS Level 3 (Work Package) | Responsible Trade / Dept. |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1.0 Project Management** | 1.1 Contract Administration | 1.1.1 Progress Reports, 1.1.2 Class Docs | Project Office |
| **2.0 Design & Engineering** | 2.1 Hull Lines, 2.2 Structural, 2.3 Outfitting, 2.4 Electrical | Detailed design packages | Design Office |
| **3.0 Procurement** | 3.1 Steel, 3.2 Outfit Steel, 3.3 Machinery, 3.4 Electrical | Material call-off per WBS | Purchase Dept. |
| **4.0 Steel Preparation** | 4.1 Surface Treatment, 4.2 Blasting & Priming | 4.1.1 Shot blasting, 4.1.2 Shop primer | Steel Yard |
| **5.0 Steel Cutting** | 5.1 Marking, 5.2 NC Cutting, 5.3 Edge Preparation | 5.2.1 Plate cutting, 5.2.2 Profile cutting | Cutting Shop |
| **6.0 Forming** | 6.1 Plate Bending, 6.2 Section Rolling | 6.1.1 Cold forming, 6.1.2 Hot forming | Forming Shop |
| **7.0 Sub-Assembly** | 7.1 Small Part Assembly, 7.2 Panel Assembly | Tank, bulkhead, deck panels | Sub-assembly Bay |
| **8.0 Block Assembly** | 8.1 Mega-Block Assembly, 8.2 Grand-Block | Bow, stern, side, deck blocks | Block Assembly Hall |
| **9.0 Hull Erection** | 9.1 Pre-erection on Berth, 9.2 Final Erection | 9.1.1 Ring erection, 9.1.2 Joining | Building Dock / Slipway |
| **10.0 Welding** | 10.1 Butt, 10.2 Fillet, 10.3 Special (clad, stainless) | Welder qualification, WPS | Welding Dept. |
| **11.0 Surface Protection** | 11.1 Secondary Surface Prep, 11.2 Coating | 11.1.1 Grit blasting, 11.2.1 Primer, 11.2.2 Top coat | Paint Dept. |
| **12.0 Outfitting (Pre)** | 12.1 Hull Outfit, 12.2 Machinery Pre-outfit | 12.1.1 Pipe, 12.1.2 Valves, 12.1.3 Brackets | Outfit Dept. |
| **13.0 Outfitting (Post)** | 13.1 Machinery, 13.2 Electrical, 13.3 Accommodation | 13.2.1 Cable laying, 13.3.1 Carpentry | Outfit / E&I Dept. |
| **14.0 Launching & Mooring** | 14.1 Docking, 14.2 Launch, 14.3 Mooring Trials | 14.2.1 Slipway release, 14.3.1 Bollard pull | Yard Marine |
| **15.0 Sea Trials** | 15.1 Harbour, 15.2 Speed, 15.3 Endurance, 15.4 Noise/Vib. | 15.2.1 Speed trial, 15.3.1 24-hr run | Sea Trials Dept. |
| **16.0 Delivery & Handover** | 16.1 Snagging, 16.2 Documentation, 16.3 Class Hand-over | 16.1.1 Punch list closure | Project Office |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process-Oriented WBS Hierarchy of a Ship Construction Project

The following block-level hierarchical diagram maps the standard WBS levels for a merchant ship, organized by the **process flow** through the shipyard rather than by physical zones of the vessel.

```mermaid
graph TD
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: WBS-Integrated Project Control Loop

This block diagram explains how the WBS elements feed into the Earned Value Management System (EVMS) for ship construction performance monitoring.

```mermaid
graph LR
    wbsA["WBS LEVEL 0 - PROJECT"]
    wbsB["WBS LEVEL 1 - MAJOR PROCESSES"]
    wbsC["WBS LEVEL 2 - SUB-PROCESSES"]
    wbsD["WBS LEVEL 3 - WORK PACKAGES"]
    dicA["WBS DICTIONARY SCOPE COST SCHEDULE RESPONSIBLE"]
    obsA["OBS - ORGANIZATIONAL BREAKDOWN STRUCTURE"]
    bacA["BAC BUDGET AT COMPLETION PER WP"]
    pmsA["PROGRESS MEASUREMENT AT WP LEVEL"]
    evA["EARNED VALUE EV = P X BAC"]
    pvA["PLANNED VALUE PV"]
    acA["ACTUAL COST AC"]
    cvA["COST VARIANCE CV = EV MINUS AC"]
    svA["SCHEDULE VARIANCE SV = EV MINUS PV"]
    cpiA["COST PERFORMANCE INDEX CPI = EV BY AC"]
    spiA["SCHEDULE PERFORMANCE INDEX SPI = EV BY PV"]
    eacA["EAC ESTIMATE AT COMPLETION"]
    repA["MONTHLY PROGRESS REPORT TO OWNER AND CLASS"]

    wbsA --> wbsB --> wbsC --> wbsD
    wbsD --- dicA
    wbsD --- obsA
    wbsD --- bacA
    bacA --> evA
    wbsD --> pmsA
    pmsA --> evA
    pvA --> evA
    acA --> evA
    evA --> cvA
    evA --> svA
    evA --> cpiA
    evA --> spiA
    cpiA --> eacA
    cvA --> repA
    svA --> repA
    cpiA --> repA
    spiA --> repA
    eacA --> repA
```

### 4.3 Process-Flow Schematic: Steel Flow through a Shipyard Mapped to WBS

```mermaid
graph LR
    rawMat["RAW STEEL INCOMING WBS 4.0"]
    blast["BLAST AND PRIME WBS 4.1 4.2"]
    cut["NC CUTTING WBS 5.0"]
    form["BENDING AND ROLLING WBS 6.0"]
    subAsm["SUB-ASSEMBLY WBS 7.0"]
    blockAsm["BLOCK ASSEMBLY WBS 8.0"]
    erect["HULL ERECTION WBS 9.0"]
    weld["WELDING WBS 10.0"]
    paint["SURFACE PROTECTION WBS 11.0"]
    preOut["PRE OUTFIT WBS 12.0"]
    postOut["POST OUTFIT WBS 13.0"]
    launch["LAUNCH AND MOOR WBS 14.0"]
    trials["SEA TRIALS WBS 15.0"]
    deliver["DELIVERY WBS 16.0"]

    rawMat --> blast --> cut --> form --> subAsm --> blockAsm --> erect --> weld --> paint --> preOut --> postOut --> launch --> trials --> deliver
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q1]**

**Q1.** Define a *Process-Oriented Work Breakdown Structure*. How does it differ from a *Product-Oriented WBS* in the context of ship construction? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A Process-Oriented WBS is a hierarchical decomposition of the total ship-construction scope, organized by the **manufacturing and engineering processes** the work flows through (e.g., steel preparation, cutting, forming, sub-assembly, block assembly, erection, outfitting, trials, delivery). Each successive level of the tree represents a finer subdivision of the process into sub-processes, work packages and activities. In contrast, a *Product-Oriented* WBS decomposes the work by the **physical parts of the ship** (bow, stern, mid-body, superstructure, etc.). In modern shipyards, the process-oriented form is preferred because every part of the vessel must pass through the same sequence of shop processes, making scheduling, resource levelling, and cost roll-up uniform across the yard.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is a *WBS Dictionary*? List any four contents that it must contain. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A WBS Dictionary is a **supporting document** that provides detailed information about every element of the Work Breakdown Structure. It ensures that each work package is unambiguously understood by all stakeholders. The four essential contents are:
1. **Statement of Work / Scope** of the WBS element.
2. **Responsible Organization / Owner** (linked to the OBS).
3. **Schedule Milestones** (start, finish, and intermediate checkpoints).
4. **Budget / Cost Estimate** and the **Acceptance / Quality Criteria** for completion.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern, Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the hierarchical levels of a Process-Oriented WBS for a merchant ship. Identify the four top-level shipbuilding processes and three sub-processes under each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Process-Oriented WBS for a merchant vessel has **five standard levels of decomposition**:

| Level | Name | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Project | "Vessel XYZ – 75,000 DWT Bulk Carrier" |
| 1 | Major Process | Hull Construction, Outfitting, Trials, Delivery |
| 2 | Sub-Process | Steel Preparation, Cutting, Forming, Sub-Assembly, Block Assembly, Hull Erection |
| 3 | Work Package | Cutting of forward-port side shell plates, Welding of deck panel 4 |
| 4 | Activity | Marking, NC cutting, tack welding, full welding |

The four top-level shipbuilding processes and three of their sub-processes are:

1. **Steel Preparation (4.0)**
   - 4.1 Surface treatment
   - 4.2 Blasting and shop priming
   - 4.3 Inward material inspection
2. **Hull Construction (5.0)**
   - 5.1 Steel Cutting
   - 5.2 Forming
   - 5.3 Block Assembly
3. **Outfitting (12.0)**
   - 12.1 Hull outfitting (pipes, brackets, valves)
   - 12.2 Machinery outfitting
   - 12.3 Electrical and accommodation outfitting
4. **Trials and Delivery (15.0)**
   - 15.1 Mooring trials
   - 15.2 Harbour and speed trials
   - 15.3 Endurance and noise/vibration trials

A neat WBS diagram showing Level 0 → Level 1 → Level 2 → Level 3 must be drawn. (See Section 4.1 above for a working diagram.)

**Marking key:** Hierarchy 2 m, top-level processes 2 m, sub-processes 2 m, neat sketch with work-package example 1 m.

---

**Q (b)** A sub-process of the WBS has three work packages with the following data:

| WP | BAC (₹ Lakhs) | Planned % | Actual % | Actual Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| WP-A | 80  | 70 % | 75 % | 70  |
| WP-B | 120 | 60 % | 50 % | 80  |
| WP-C | 60  | 50 % | 50 % | 28  |

Compute the **parent-level Cost Variance (CV)**, **Schedule Variance (SV)**, **Cost Performance Index (CPI)**, **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)**, and the **Estimate at Completion (EAC)**. Comment on the health of the sub-process. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Compute EV = Actual % × BAC for each WP.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EV_A &= 0.75 \times 80 = 60 \\
EV_B &= 0.50 \times 120 = 60 \\
EV_C &= 0.50 \times 60 = 30 \\
EV_{\text{parent}} &= 60 + 60 + 30 = 150 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute PV = Planned % × BAC for each WP.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_A &= 0.70 \times 80 = 56 \\
PV_B &= 0.60 \times 120 = 72 \\
PV_C &= 0.50 \times 60 = 30 \\
PV_{\text{parent}} &= 56 + 72 + 30 = 158 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Sum AC.**

$$
AC_{\text{parent}} = 70 + 80 + 28 = 178 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
$$

**Step 4 – BAC of parent.**

$$
BAC_{\text{parent}} = 80 + 120 + 60 = 260 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
$$

**Step 5 – Cost and Schedule Variances.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC = 150 - 178 = -28 \text{ Lakhs ₹} \\
SV &= EV - PV = 150 - 158 = -8 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 – Performance Indices.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{150}{178} \approx 0.843 \\
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{150}{158} \approx 0.949
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 – Estimate at Completion.**

$$
EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{260}{0.843} \approx 308.4 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
$$

**Step 8 – Comment.**
- **CV = –28 Lakhs and CPI = 0.84 < 1** ⇒ The sub-process is **over budget**.
- **SV = –8 Lakhs and SPI = 0.95 < 1** ⇒ The sub-process is **slightly behind schedule**.
- **EAC = ₹308.4 Lakhs** indicates a projected **cost overrun of ₹48.4 Lakhs (≈ 18.6 %)** if the current trend continues. **Corrective action** (extra labour, overtime, or design optimization) is recommended, particularly on **WP-A** which is the biggest schedule and cost risk.

**Marking key:** EV/PV/AC calculation 3 m, CV/SV/CPI/SPI 2 m, EAC + comment 2 m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice)**

**Q (a)** Discuss the **benefits and limitations** of using a Process-Oriented WBS in a modern shipyard. Compare it briefly with a Product-Oriented WBS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Benefits of Process-Oriented WBS in Shipbuilding:**

1. **Alignment with yard flow** – The WBS mirrors the physical movement of steel through the workshops, so progress reporting and bottlenecks become visible at the right department level.
2. **Resource levelling** – All WPs of the same process (e.g., welding) compete for the same pool of welders, enabling the planning department to allocate manpower consistently.
3. **Cost roll-up by process** – Yard managers can benchmark the cost of "painting" or "welding" per tonne across multiple ships, which is impossible in a product-oriented WBS.
4. **Re-use across series ships** – For ship-owners ordering sister vessels, the WBS templates and standard times can be directly reused, drastically reducing planning effort.
5. **Simpler progress measurement** – Progress is a function of how much of a process is complete (e.g., 80 % of blocks welded), not how much of a physical zone is built.
6. **Integration with production line / zone logistics** – Pairs naturally with **line-of-balance** and **takt-time** shipbuilding philosophies.

**Limitations:**

1. **Hides sub-product integration issues** – A problem at a *joint* between two physical zones may fall between two WBS elements and may be missed.
2. **Lacks product-level cost visibility** – The cost of "building the bow" is not directly available; it must be synthesized from multiple WBS elements.
3. **Less intuitive for owners** – Owners (and class surveyors) think in terms of the *ship*, not the *processes*.
4. **Not suitable for one-off / custom vessels** – In research or submarine building, where every section is unique, a product-oriented or hybrid WBS works better.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Process-Oriented WBS | Product-Oriented WBS |
|---|---|---|
| Decomposition axis | Manufacturing process | Physical part of ship |
| Best for | Series merchant ships, repetitive flow | One-off naval / special vessels |
| Visibility | Process bottlenecks | Product completion |
| Reusability across ships | High | Low |
| Owner reporting | Less intuitive | Intuitive |
| Yard internal control | Excellent | Moderate |

**Marking key:** Benefits 3 m, Limitations 2 m, Comparison 2 m.

---

**Q (b)** A hull-construction sub-process "Hull Erection" has the following WBS data. Calculate the **weighted % complete** of the sub-process and identify which work package is the **biggest schedule risk** if planned completion was 70 % for each.

| WP | BAC (₹ Lakhs) | Actual % Complete |
|---|---|---|
| WP-E1 (Stem ring erection) | 50  | 60 % |
| WP-E2 (Mid-body ring erection) | 200 | 45 % |
| WP-E3 (Stern ring erection) | 80  | 80 % |
| WP-E4 (Superstructure erection) | 70  | 75 % |

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Compute BAC of the sub-process.**

$$
BAC = 50 + 200 + 80 + 70 = 400 \text{ Lakhs ₹}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute Weight Factors.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{E1} &= 50/400 = 0.125 \\
w_{E2} &= 200/400 = 0.500 \\
w_{E3} &= 80/400 = 0.200 \\
w_{E4} &= 70/400 = 0.175
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $0.125 + 0.500 + 0.200 + 0.175 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 3 – Compute the weighted % complete.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Done} &= \sum w_i \times p_i \\
&= (0.125 \times 60) + (0.500 \times 45) + (0.200 \times 80) + (0.175 \times 75) \\
&= 7.50 + 22.50 + 16.00 + 13.125 \\
&= 59.125 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Identify the biggest schedule risk.** Compare each WP's actual % to the planned 70 %:

| WP | Planned % | Actual % | Slip (%) | BAC-weighted impact (Slip × $w_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| WP-E1 | 70 | 60 | –10 | –1.25 |
| WP-E2 | 70 | 45 | **–25** | **–12.50** |
| WP-E3 | 70 | 80 | +10 (ahead) | +2.00 |
| WP-E4 | 70 | 75 | +5 (ahead) | +0.875 |

**Step 5 – Conclusion.** Although WP-E3 is at 80 %, the **biggest schedule risk is WP-E2 (Mid-body ring erection)**, which is 25 % behind plan and contributes the largest BAC-weighted slip (–12.5 % points). Corrective action — extra cranes, second-shift welding, or design freeze — must target WP-E2 first.

**Marking key:** BAC and weight factors 2 m, weighted % complete 2 m, schedule risk analysis 3 m.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in WBS Numerical Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Actual % with Planned %** when computing EV vs PV. Re-read the question carefully: *"the work is 70 % complete"* usually means **actual**, not planned.
> 2. **Forgetting to roll-up** EV, PV, AC at the parent level by **summing**, not averaging.
> 3. **Using the wrong formula for EAC.** $EAC = BAC / CPI$ assumes the *current* CPI continues; a question may ask for $EAC = AC + (BAC - EV)$ instead. **State the assumption.**
> 4. **Mixing up the WBS axis.** If the question says "process-oriented", do not list physical zones of the ship; the sub-processes must be **manufacturing stages**.
> 5. **Missing the WBS Dictionary** definition in Part A — examiners specifically look for the four key contents.
> 6. **Diagrams without labels** — WBS trees must be **labelled at every level**; an unlabelled hierarchy gets 0 for the sketch component.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Process-Oriented WBS** decomposes ship-construction work by the **manufacturing processes** the vessel flows through, not by the physical parts of the ship.
- Standard WBS levels: **Project (0) → Major Process (1) → Sub-Process (2) → Work Package (3) → Activity (4)**.
- The **Work Package** is the smallest WBS element that is *estimated, scheduled, costed, and assigned* to a single responsible organization.
- The **WBS Dictionary** is the supporting document describing each element (scope, owner, schedule, cost, quality).
- The WBS is integrated with the **OBS (Organizational Breakdown Structure)** via a **RACI / RAM matrix**.
- The WBS feeds Earned Value Management: **PV, EV, AC, CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, ETC, TCPI**.
- The 100 % Rule: *the WBS represents 100 % of the project scope, and child WBS elements sum to 100 % of their parent.*
- Shipbuilding typical WBS top-level processes: **Project Management, Design, Procurement, Steel Preparation, Steel Cutting, Forming, Sub-Assembly, Block Assembly, Hull Erection, Welding, Surface Protection, Pre-Outfitting, Post-Outfitting, Launch, Mooring Trials, Sea Trials, Delivery**.
- A process-oriented WBS is **best for series merchant ships** with repetitive flow; a product-oriented WBS is best for **one-off / naval vessels**.
- **Cost rolls up additively**: $C_{\text{parent}} = \sum C_{\text{child}}$.
- **Weighted % complete** uses BAC-weights, not simple averages.
- A **CV < 0 or CPI < 1** ⇒ over budget; **SV < 0 or SPI < 1** ⇒ behind schedule.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrated Hull Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Integrated Hull Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Integrated Hull Construction** is the modern shipbuilding methodology in which the entire vessel hull is engineered, fabricated, and assembled as a single, unified structural system using **pre-fabricated sub-assemblies, modules, and blocks** rather than the traditional piece-by-piece, "plate-by-plate" approach starting from the keel upwards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OENST724) syllabus, Integrated Hull Construction represents the convergence of three engineering disciplines: **structural design**, **production engineering**, and **logistics planning**, all unified to minimize build time, improve dimensional accuracy, and reduce overall construction cost.

The integration refers to:

- **Design Integration** – All hull parts (plates, stiffeners, brackets, foundations) are designed in a 3D CAD environment (commonly *FORAN*, *TRIBON*, or *AVEVA Marine*) before physical fabrication begins.
- **Production Integration** – Production lines (panel line, block line, pre-erection outfitting) are interconnected and synchronized through a Just-In-Time (JIT) workflow.
- **Logistics Integration** – Material flow from steel stockyard → cutting → forming → sub-assembly → block assembly → erection is controlled via a unified Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a **giant LEGO ship** instead of carving a wooden boat plank by plank.

- **Traditional Method (Wooden Age / Early Steel Age):** You start with a flat keel on the slipway, erect one rib at a time, rivet plates onto it, and slowly grow the ship from the ground up — like growing a tree.
- **Integrated Hull Construction:** You first build complete "chunks" or "blocks" of the ship in specialized workshops (some indoors, some on the ground), and then use giant cranes to **weld these giant Lego blocks together** to form the complete hull. The ship essentially "grows from the top down and around," with multiple zones of the hull being constructed in parallel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** Modern shipyards in South Korea (Hyundai Heavy Industries, Samsung Heavy Industries) and Japan (Mitsubishi Heavy Industries) produce VLCCs (Very Large Crude Carriers) of ~300,000 DWT in **less than 12 months** using integrated construction, whereas traditional methods took 24–36 months.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Industrial Metrics

The following standard shipbuilding metrics govern integrated hull construction:

- **Block Weight Capacity:** Modern Goliath cranes can lift blocks weighing **up to 1,500 tonnes (≈ 1.5 × 10⁶ kg)**.
- **Steel Utilization Factor (η_steel):** Typically **85%–92%** for integrated yards vs. **70%–78%** for traditional yards.
- **Hull Construction Cycle Time:** The "Hull Outfitting Coefficient" (ratio of outfitting done afloat vs. afloat + ashore) is targeted above **70%** in fully integrated yards.
- **Dimensional Tolerances:** Modern laser-controlled plate cutting achieves **±2 mm** accuracy over 20 m plate lengths.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, remember that the three pillars of Integrated Hull Construction are: **Pre-fabrication, Modularization, and Parallel Production**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Integrated Hull Construction can be broken down into **six sequential but parallel engineering phases**:

**Phase 1 — Design & Nesting (Computer-Aided)**
- Lines plan, general arrangement, and structural drawings are translated into 3D CAD models.
- **Nesting software** arranges 2D plate profiles onto rectangular steel plates to minimize scrap.
- Each plate receives a unique identification number linked to its position in the hull.

**Phase 2 — Steel Stockyard → Cutting**
- Steel plates (typically **grade A, AH36, DH36** with thickness **5 mm – 80 mm**) are stored in a climate-controlled stockyard.
- Plates are cut by **plasma cutters**, **oxy-fuel cutters**, or **laser cutters** based on nesting data.

**Phase 3 — Forming & Sub-Assembly**
- Cut plates are passed through **plate rolls** to achieve curvature (cymbe, deadrise, sheer).
- Sub-assemblies: small units like "T-sections," "T-pieces," and "lipped angle stiffeners" are welded.

**Phase 4 — Panel Assembly**
- Flat or curved panels are welded by **submerged arc welding (SAW)** or **flux-cored arc welding (FCAW)**.
- Panels typically measure **~12 m × 3 m** and weigh **~15–25 tonnes**.

**Phase 5 — Block Assembly**
- Multiple panels are joined to form **3D blocks** (e.g., double-bottom blocks, side-shell blocks, deck blocks).
- Blocks may measure **~24 m × 18 m × 12 m** and weigh **~300–800 tonnes**.
- **Pre-erection outfitting** (pipes, cables, machinery foundations) is fitted **before** block erection.

**Phase 6 — Hull Erection & Joining**
- Blocks are lifted by **Goliath (Gantry) cranes** onto the building berth or in a dry dock.
- Adjacent blocks are welded together to form the **grand block** or **complete hull**.
- Final **outfitting afloat** completes the ship.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Phase

- **Pre-fabrication** → Reduces work at height, improves welder safety (≈ 60% fewer hours worked above 3 m).
- **Modularization** → Allows simultaneous production of multiple ship sections → compresses build schedule.
- **Parallel Production** → Multiple zones of the ship progress concurrently, unlike traditional "growing" construction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all high-yield formulas for Integrated Hull Construction problems. **Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid markdown table breakage.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Steel Utilization Factor | $\eta_{steel} = \dfrac{M_{hull}}{M_{steel\,purchased}} \times 100$ | % | Quantifies material efficiency of cutting/nesting |
| 2 | Block Lift Capacity | $F_{lift} \geq m_{block} \cdot (g + a_{wind})$ | N (kN) | Crane selection and safety verification |
| 3 | Hull Outfitting Coefficient | $K_{outfit} = \dfrac{T_{outfit\,ashore}}{T_{outfit\,total}} \times 100$ | % | Measures integration maturity |
| 4 | Plate Area Calculation | $A_{plate} = L \cdot B - \sum A_{cutouts}$ | m² | For nesting optimization |
| 5 | Welding Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | kJ/mm | Controls weld quality in integrated yards |
| 6 | Block Weight Estimation | $W_{block} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{steel} + W_{outfit}$ | tonnes | Crane and transport planning |
| 7 | Production Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} = \max(T_{i}) + T_{erection}$ | days | Critical Path Method (CPM) basis |
| 8 | Plate Thickness (Rule of Thumb) | $t = \dfrac{s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L}}{C}$ | mm | Hull plate scantlings (Rule-based) |

Where:
- $m_{block}$ = mass of block
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration ($9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$)
- $a_{wind}$ = wind-driven acceleration on the suspended load
- $\rho_{steel}$ = density of shipbuilding steel ($\approx 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- $V, I$ = arc voltage and current
- $v$ = welding travel speed
- $s, k, L, C$ = spacing, factor, ship length, coefficient

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Integrated hull construction is the **backbone of global shipbuilding competitiveness**. Nations are ranked by their shipbuilding efficiency using metrics like:

- **Man-hours per Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT):** South Korea ≈ 8–10 hr/CGT; Japan ≈ 12–15 hr/CGT; China (top yards) ≈ 14–18 hr/CGT; Europe ≈ 20–30 hr/CGT.
- Integrated construction directly reduces **man-hours**, which is the single largest cost factor (~30–40% of total build cost).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The shift from "berth-oriented" to "block-oriented" production is the defining feature of Integrated Hull Construction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steel Utilization Factor for a Typical Hull Block

**Problem Setup:** A shipyard purchases **1200 tonnes** of hot-rolled shipbuilding steel plates (Grade AH36). After computerized nesting, the total weight of plates cut and used in the hull of a bulk carrier is **1044 tonnes**. The remainder (scrap, off-cuts, and wastage) is recycled. Determine the Steel Utilization Factor.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data**

We are given the total steel purchased ($M_{steel\,purchased}$) and the steel actually consumed in the hull ($M_{hull}$).

$$M_{steel\,purchased} = 1200 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$M_{hull} = 1044 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Steel Utilization Formula**

The Steel Utilization Factor ($\eta_{steel}$) is defined as the ratio of steel used in the final hull to the total steel purchased, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta_{steel} = \dfrac{M_{hull}}{M_{steel\,purchased}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values**

$$\eta_{steel} = \dfrac{1044}{1200} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Perform the Division**

$$\eta_{steel} = 0.87 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{steel} = 87\%}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation**

A utilization factor of **87%** indicates a **highly integrated modern shipyard**. Traditional yards achieve only **70%–78%**, because manual nesting leads to large triangular scrap pieces between curved plate boundaries. Modern computerized nesting using algorithms like *rectangular nesting* or *True-Shape nesting* brings utilization above **85%–92%**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Crane Lift Capacity for an Integrated Hull Block

**Problem Setup:** A double-bottom block for a Panamax bulk carrier has been fully assembled with **pre-erection outfitting** (machinery seats, pipe spools, cable trays) fitted. The block dimensions are:
- Length $L_{block} = 20$ m
- Breadth $B_{block} = 14$ m
- Height $H_{block} = 4$ m

The steel volume within the block is $V_{steel} = 95 \text{ m}^3$. The outfitting weight is $W_{outfit} = 180$ tonnes. A wind gust during lifting imposes an additional vertical-equivalent acceleration of $a_{wind} = 0.2 \text{ m/s}^2$. Determine the **minimum crane lift force** required.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities**

$$V_{steel} = 95 \text{ m}^3$$
$$W_{outfit} = 180 \text{ tonnes} = 180 \times 10^3 \text{ kg}$$
$$\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
$$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$a_{wind} = 0.2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Mass of Steel**

The mass of the steel portion is calculated by multiplying volume by density.

$$m_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{steel}$$

$$m_{steel} = 7850 \times 95$$

$$m_{steel} = 745{,}750 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Total Mass of the Block**

The total mass includes both the steel mass and the outfitting mass.

$$m_{block} = m_{steel} + m_{outfit}$$

$$m_{block} = 745{,}750 + 180{,}000$$

$$m_{block} = 925{,}750 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Crane Lift Force Equation**

The minimum crane lift force must overcome both gravity and the wind-induced vertical acceleration.

$$F_{lift} = m_{block} \cdot (g + a_{wind})$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Values**

$$F_{lift} = 925{,}750 \times (9.81 + 0.2)$$

$$F_{lift} = 925{,}750 \times 10.01$$

$$\boxed{F_{lift} \approx 9{,}265{,}808 \text{ N} \approx 9266 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Standard Safety Factor**

Shipyard standards (IACS UR Z17 / Z23) require a **minimum safety factor of 1.25** for block lifting.

$$F_{design} = 1.25 \cdot F_{lift}$$

$$F_{design} = 1.25 \times 9266 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{F_{design} \approx 11{,}582 \text{ kN} \approx 11.58 \text{ MN}}$$

This corresponds to a crane of approximately **1180-tonne Safe Working Load (SWL)**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Construction Philosophies

| Parameter | Traditional (Riveted / Stick) | Integrated (Modern Welded) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Build Strategy | Sequential, "grow from keel" | Parallel, "block-by-block" |
| Pre-fabrication Level | < 20% | > 70% |
| Welding Type | Mostly manual SMAW | SAW, FCAW, robotic welding |
| Outfitting Timing | After launch (afloat) | Pre-erection (in block) |
| Crane Dependency | Low (≤ 50 tonnes) | Very High (≥ 800 tonnes) |
| Build Cycle (VLCC) | 24–36 months | 10–14 months |
| Man-Hours / CGT | 25–35 hr | 8–15 hr |
| Capital Investment | Lower | Very High |
| Steel Utilization | 70%–78% | 85%–92% |
| Quality Control | After-the-fact | In-line, statistically controlled |

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark KTU questions, this comparison table is a **high-yield answer skeleton**. Combine with diagrams for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Integrated Hull Construction

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end flow** of integrated hull construction, from design to ship delivery. Each block represents a distinct production node, and arrows represent material / information flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Office - 3D CAD Model] --> B[Nesting and Plate Scheduling]
    B --> C[Steel Stockyard]
    C --> D[Cutting Bay - Plasma or Laser Cutters]
    D --> E[Forming Bay - Plate Rolls and Presses]
    E --> F[Panel Assembly Line - SAW Welding]
    F --> G[Block Assembly Hall]
    H[Outfitting Workshop] --> G
    G --> I[Pre-Erection Outfitting]
    I --> J[Goliath Crane Lift]
    J --> K[Building Berth or Dry Dock]
    K --> L[Erection Welding - Block Joining]
    L --> M[Hull Complete]
    M --> N[Launch]
    N --> O[Outfitting Afloat]
    O --> P[Sea Trials]
    P --> Q[Ship Delivery]
```

### 4.2 Block Hierarchy in Integrated Construction

The diagram below shows the **hierarchy of assembly levels** — from raw plate to complete ship. This is essential for understanding the *modular logic* of integrated hull construction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 - Raw Steel Plate] --> L2[Level 2 - Cut and Formed Plate]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 - Sub-Assembly Stiffener Plate]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 - Panel 12 m x 3 m]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 - Block 24 m x 18 m]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6 - Grand Block Half Hull]
    L6 --> L7[Level 7 - Complete Hull]
    L7 --> L8[Level 8 - Outfitted Ship]
```

### 4.3 Process Sequence Flow (Critical Path View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 - Cutting] --> S2[Stage 2 - Forming]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 - Sub-Assembly]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 - Panel Welding]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 - Block Welding]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 - Block Pre-Outfitting]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7 - Erection on Berth]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8 - Grand Block Join-Up]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 9 - Launch]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Answers:**
> - Always state the **block hierarchy** when explaining modular construction.
> - Highlight the **Critical Path** (Stages 1 → 9) which determines total build time.
> - Emphasize the **parallel branches** (outfitting workshop runs in parallel with block welding) which compress the schedule.

### 4.4 Schematic Description: Block Arrangement in a Double-Bottom Hull

In integrated construction, the hull is logically divided into **zones**, each containing multiple blocks:

- **Zone A:** Forward double bottom + forepeak
- **Zone B:** Mid-ship double bottom (cargo hold bottom)
- **Zone C:** After double bottom + aft peak
- **Zone D:** Side shell blocks (port and starboard)
- **Zone E:** Deck blocks (tank top, decks, superstructure)
- **Zone F:** Transverse ring sections (frames + bulkheads)

Each zone has a **dedicated production team and scheduled completion date**, all synchronized by the yard's master production schedule.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define **Integrated Hull Construction**. List any **four advantages** of integrated hull construction over traditional construction methods.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Integrated Hull Construction is a modern shipbuilding methodology in which the complete vessel hull is fabricated by assembling pre-fabricated blocks and modules, each built in parallel in workshops, using synchronized production lines, advanced welding, and pre-erection outfitting, before final joining on the building berth or in dry dock.

**Any Four Advantages (2 Marks = 0.5 each):**

1. **Reduced Build Time:** Parallel production of multiple blocks compresses total cycle time from 24–36 months to 10–14 months for large vessels.
2. **Improved Quality:** Workshop-controlled environment enables better dimensional accuracy, consistent welding (using SAW/FCAW), and statistical quality control.
3. **Enhanced Safety:** Most welding, fitting, and outfitting are done at ground level rather than at height; reduces accidents by ~40–50%.
4. **Higher Steel Utilization:** Computerized nesting achieves 85–92% steel usage vs. 70–78% in traditional yards.
5. **Pre-Erection Outfitting:** Pipes, cables, and machinery foundations are fitted before block erection → reduces work after launch.
6. **Lower Man-Hours per CGT:** Reduces labor cost, the largest cost component (~30–40% of total).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What is a **Hull Block** in integrated hull construction? Mention its typical weight and dimensions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** A hull block is a **three-dimensional sub-assembly** of the ship hull, fabricated by welding together multiple panels (which are themselves welded sub-assemblies of plates and stiffeners). Each block is a structural unit that is self-supporting during lifting and transportation.

**Typical Weight and Dimensions (1.5 Marks):**
- **Length:** 12–30 m
- **Breadth:** 10–20 m
- **Height:** 6–12 m
- **Weight:** 200–800 tonnes (small to medium ships); up to **1,500 tonnes** for ultra-large blocks lifted by modern Goliath cranes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **various stages of Integrated Hull Construction** from design to delivery. Support your answer with a suitable flowchart. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard fabricates a **curved side-shell block** with the following data:
- Steel volume used = $85 \text{ m}^3$
- Outfitting weight = $140$ tonnes
- Steel purchased for the block = $1150$ tonnes for the entire hull; this block consumes $8\%$ of total cut steel.
- Wind acceleration during lift: $a_{wind} = 0.15 \text{ m/s}^2$

Calculate:
(i) The total mass of the block. **(3 Marks)**
(ii) The crane lift force required (with 1.25 safety factor). **(4 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Stages of Integrated Hull Construction (7 Marks):**

The complete process consists of **eight major stages**:

1. **Design & Engineering Stage (1 Mark):**
   Lines plan, general arrangement, and structural drawings are converted into a 3D CAD model using software like *FORAN*, *AVEVA Marine*, or *TRIBON*. Bill of Materials (BOM) and nesting data are generated.

2. **Steel Stockyard & Cutting Stage (1 Mark):**
   Steel plates (Grades A, AH36, DH36, EH36) are stored in a climate-controlled yard. Plates are cut by **plasma, laser, or oxy-fuel** cutters according to the nesting plan.

3. **Forming Stage (1 Mark):**
   Plates are rolled into curved shapes (for shell plating) using **3-roll or 4-roll plate bending machines** to achieve the hull's curvature.

4. **Sub-Assembly & Panel Assembly Stage (1 Mark):**
   Stiffeners (T-bars, angle bars) are welded to plates to form **panels** (~12 m × 3 m). Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) is typically used.

5. **Block Assembly Stage (1 Mark):**
   Panels are welded into **3D blocks** (~24 m × 18 m × 12 m, 300–800 tonnes). Pre-erection outfitting is fitted here.

6. **Block Erection Stage (1 Mark):**
   Blocks are lifted by **Goliath cranes** onto the building berth. Adjacent blocks are aligned and welded together using CO₂-shielded FCAW.

7. **Launch Stage (0.5 Mark):**
   The completed hull is launched via **slipway, floating dock, or synchrolift**.

8. **Outfitting Afloat & Trials (0.5 Mark):**
   Remaining outfitting (engine, electronics, accommodation) is completed; sea trials verify performance; the ship is delivered.

**Flowchart (refer to Section 4.1 above).**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $V_{steel} = 85 \text{ m}^3$
- $W_{outfit} = 140$ tonnes $= 140 \times 10^3 \text{ kg}$
- $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $a_{wind} = 0.15 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Safety Factor $SF = 1.25$

---

**(i) Total Mass of the Block (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Mass of Steel in the Block:**

$$m_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{steel}$$

$$m_{steel} = 7850 \times 85 = 667{,}250 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Outfitting:**

$$m_{outfit} = 140{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Total Mass:**

$$m_{block} = m_{steel} + m_{outfit}$$

$$m_{block} = 667{,}250 + 140{,}000$$

$$\boxed{m_{block} = 807{,}250 \text{ kg} \approx 807.25 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

**(ii) Crane Lift Force with Safety Factor (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Bare Lift Force:**

$$F_{lift} = m_{block} \cdot (g + a_{wind})$$

$$F_{lift} = 807{,}250 \times (9.81 + 0.15)$$

$$F_{lift} = 807{,}250 \times 9.96$$

$$F_{lift} = 8{,}039{,}010 \text{ N} \approx 8039 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Safety Factor:**

$$F_{design} = SF \cdot F_{lift}$$

$$F_{design} = 1.25 \times 8039 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{F_{design} \approx 10{,}049 \text{ kN} \approx 10.05 \text{ MN}}$$

This corresponds to a **~1025-tonne SWL** Goliath crane.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Traditional Hull Construction** and **Integrated Hull Construction** under the following heads: **(7 Marks)**
   (i) Construction Sequence
   (ii) Outfitting Strategy
   (iii) Quality Control
   (iv) Man-Hours and Cycle Time

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Hull Outfitting Coefficient ($K_{outfit}$)**. A shipyard spends **18,000 man-hours** on outfitting a vessel, of which **13,500 man-hours** are spent **ashore (in-block)** and the rest is done **afloat (post-launch)**. Determine the Hull Outfitting Coefficient and comment on whether the yard qualifies as a "fully integrated" yard. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks = 1.75 per head):**

| Head | Traditional Construction | Integrated Construction |
|------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| (i) Construction Sequence | Sequential from keel upward; one zone at a time. | Parallel production of multiple blocks; pre-fabricated off-berth. |
| (ii) Outfitting Strategy | Mostly **afloat**, after launch; limited pre-fabrication. | Heavy **pre-erection outfitting** in blocks before erection. |
| (iii) Quality Control | Manual inspection; variable weld quality. | Statistical Process Control (SPC); automated NDT. |
| (iv) Man-Hours & Cycle Time | 25–35 hr/CGT; 24–36 months. | 8–15 hr/CGT; 10–14 months. |

**Justification (additional marks for description):**
- **Sequence:** Traditional yards erect frames first, then plates. Integrated yards cut and form plates off-site, build blocks in workshops, and only join blocks at the berth.
- **Outfitting:** Traditional → ~80% afloat; Integrated → ~70% ashore.
- **Quality:** Integrated yards use **welding parameter logging** and **automatic ultrasonic testing (AUT)**.
- **Cycle Time:** Integrated construction uses **Critical Path Method (CPM)** and **Line-of-Balance (LOB)** scheduling.

---

**(b) Hull Outfitting Coefficient (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Hull Outfitting Coefficient ($K_{outfit}$) is the percentage of total outfitting work (measured in man-hours) that is performed **ashore in pre-fabricated blocks** before the hull is erected on the berth or launched. It quantifies the degree of integration in a shipyard.

$$K_{outfit} = \dfrac{T_{outfit\,ashore}}{T_{outfit\,total}} \times 100$$

**Given (1 Mark):**
- $T_{outfit\,ashore} = 13{,}500 \text{ man-hours}$
- $T_{outfit\,total} = 18{,}000 \text{ man-hours}$

**Step 1 — Compute Coefficient (2 Marks):**

$$K_{outfit} = \dfrac{13{,}500}{18{,}000} \times 100$$

$$K_{outfit} = 0.75 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{K_{outfit} = 75\%}$$

**Step 2 — Engineering Interpretation (2 Marks):**

According to industry benchmarks:
- $K_{outfit} \geq 70\%$ → **Fully Integrated Yard** (modern, world-class).
- $50\% \leq K_{outfit} < 70\%$ → **Semi-Integrated Yard**.
- $K_{outfit} < 50\%$ → **Traditional Yard**.

Since $K_{outfit} = 75\% \geq 70\%$, **the yard qualifies as a fully integrated modern shipyard** with high pre-erection outfitting capability.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Block" with "Panel":** A panel is a 2D welded plate-stiffener assembly; a block is a 3D assembly of multiple panels. Many students write "block = panel" — **deducted 1 mark.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems:** The crane lift force must be in **kN or MN**, mass in **kg or tonnes**. Writing the answer without units loses **0.5–1 mark.**
> 
> 3. **Skipping the Safety Factor:** Always apply **SF = 1.25** to crane lift force in KTU problems. Forgetting it = **−1 mark.**
> 
> 4. **Writing "Hull Outfitting Coefficient" without stating it's a percentage:** State explicitly: "expressed as a percentage."
> 
> 5. **Not Using a Flowchart in 7-Mark Sub-Part (a):** A flowchart is **mandatory** for "Explain the stages" type questions; missing it = **−1 to −2 marks.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Integrated Hull Construction** = modern shipbuilding using **pre-fabricated blocks**, **parallel production**, and **pre-erection outfitting**.
- The **6 key phases** are: Design → Cutting → Forming → Sub-Assembly → Panel → Block → Erection → Outfitting → Trials.
- **Hull block** is a 3D structural unit (200–1,500 tonnes); **panel** is 2D (~12 m × 3 m, 15–25 tonnes).
- **Steel Utilization Factor** $\eta_{steel} = M_{hull} / M_{purchased} \times 100$; modern integrated yards achieve **85–92%**.
- **Hull Outfitting Coefficient** $K_{outfit} = T_{ashore} / T_{total} \times 100$; **≥ 70%** qualifies as fully integrated.
- **Crane Lift Force:** $F_{lift} = m_{block} \cdot (g + a_{wind})$, with **SF = 1.25**.
- **Steel density:** $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — memorize this value.
- **Block hierarchy:** Plate → Cut Plate → Sub-Assembly → Panel → Block → Grand Block → Hull → Ship.
- **Comparison strengths to memorize:**
  - Build cycle: Traditional 24–36 months vs. Integrated 10–14 months.
  - Man-hours/CGT: Traditional 25–35 hr vs. Integrated 8–15 hr.
  - Steel utilization: Traditional 70–78% vs. Integrated 85–92%.
- **Software stack:** FORAN, AVEVA Marine, TRIBON for design; SAP/ERP for logistics.
- **Critical Path Method (CPM)** and **Line-of-Balance (LOB)** are the scheduling tools used in integrated yards.
- Always draw a **flowchart** for 7-mark "explain stages" questions — it is expected by KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outfitting and Painting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Outfitting and Painting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Outfitting — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Outfitting** is defined as the entire spectrum of post-hull-construction activities in shipbuilding wherein all secondary components, systems, machinery, accommodation units, and auxiliary equipment are installed onto the already erected and launched hull to transform a bare steel structure into a fully operational, seaworthy, and habitable vessel.

Outfitting is the **second major phase** of ship construction, succeeding the **hull erection (fabrication & assembly)** phase and preceding the **trial runs and delivery**. It essentially bridges the gap between a "shell of steel" and a "functioning ship."

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, outfitting encompasses:
- **Hull outfitting** – work done on the hull structure itself (plating, decks, bulkheads finishing)
- **Machinery outfitting** – installation of main engine, auxiliary engines, shaft line, propellers
- **Electrical outfitting** – cabling, switchboards, navigation electronics
- **Pipe outfitting** – bilge, ballast, fuel, fire-fighting, sanitary, pneumatic lines
- **Joiner/Accommodation outfitting** – cabins, galley, mess rooms, sanitary spaces
- **Deck outfitting** – masts, hatches, winches, capstans, lifesaving equipment

## 1.2 Painting — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Painting (Marine Coating)** is the controlled application of specially formulated protective and decorative coating systems onto prepared steel surfaces of a ship to provide resistance against **corrosion**, **fouling**, **abrasion**, **chemical attack**, and to enhance the **aesthetic appearance** and **service life** of the vessel.

Marine painting is not merely decorative — it is a **critical engineering activity** because:
- A ship's hull is in perpetual contact with **seawater (a corrosive electrolyte)**.
- Marine organisms (barnacles, algae, mollusks) cause **biofouling**, increasing drag and fuel consumption.
- Protective coatings are the **first line of defense** against structural degradation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building a House" Metaphor

Think of shipbuilding like building a house:

| Shipbuilding Stage | House-Building Analogy |
|---|---|
| Steel Cutting & Fabrication | Cutting the wooden frames in the workshop |
| Hull Erection | Assembling the bare skeletal structure of the house |
| **Outfitting** | **Installing plumbing, electrical wiring, furniture, doors, windows, kitchen appliances** |
| **Painting** | **Applying primer, putty, and final paint coats on walls** |
| Sea Trials | Final inspection and moving in |

Just as a house cannot be lived in without doors, wiring, and paint, a ship cannot sail without outfitting and protective coating.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Classifications

The two principal metrics used in marine coating engineering (per **ISO 12944** and **SSPC standards**) are:

- **DFT — Dry Film Thickness** measured in **microns (μm)**
- **WFT — Wet Film Thickness** measured in **microns (μm)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All marine paint specifications in classification society rules (e.g., **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS**) are quoted in **DFT (μm)**, because WFT evaporates and only DFT remains as the permanent protective barrier.

---

## 1.5 Why Outfitting and Painting are Critical — The Engineering Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> **Biofouling Impact:** A heavily fouled hull can increase frictional resistance by up to **40-60%**, leading to a **15-30% rise in fuel consumption**. This is why **antifouling paint systems** are mandatory under **IMO AFS Convention** and **IMO Performance Standard for Protective Coatings (PSPC)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Outfitting

Outfitting is broadly classified into **three stages**, executed in parallel with hull construction to minimize overall shipbuilding time:

### A) Stage 1 — Pre-Erection Outfitting (Pre-Outfitting / Unit Outfitting)
- Carried out **before** the hull blocks are erected.
- Maximum parallel work is done at the **workshop/shop floor level**.
- Components like **machinery foundations, pipe spools, ventilation ducts, cable trays, brackets, insert plates** are fitted onto sub-assemblies and hull blocks.
- **Advantage:** Reduces total shipbuilding time drastically.

### B) Stage 2 — Post-Erection Outfitting (On-Berth Outfitting)
- Performed **after** hull blocks are joined on the building berth/slipway.
- Includes heavy machinery installation, main engines, deck machinery, masts, funnels.
- Piping systems, electrical cabling, insulation, and joiner work commence.

### C) Stage 3 — Post-Launch Outfitting (Floating Outfitting)
- Conducted **after the ship is launched** and is afloat at the fitting-out berth/dock.
- Final alignment of shafting, propeller fitting, sea trials preparation, interior finishing.
- Most aesthetically critical painting work is done at this stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Shipbuilding Philosophy:** "**Outfit-first**" or "**Block-outfitting**" approach — maximum outfitting is completed at the block stage itself, which can reduce dry-dock time by **30-40%**.

## 2.2 Major Sub-Systems Under Outfitting

1. **Propulsion & Shafting System** — Main engine, reduction gearbox, shaft line, stern tube, propeller.
2. **Auxiliary Machinery** — Generators, compressors, purifiers, heat exchangers, pumps.
3. **Piping Systems** — Bilge, ballast, fuel oil, lube oil, freshwater, seawater, fire-fighting (CO₂/water mist), sanitary, pneumatic.
4. **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation & Air-Conditioning)** — Ducts, fans, AC units.
5. **Electrical Systems** — Generators, switchboards, motors, lighting, navigation & communication electronics.
6. **Joiner Work** — Cabins, mess rooms, galley, pantries, hospitals, offices (using fire-retardant materials per **SOLAS**).
7. **Safety & Life-Saving Appliances (LSA)**** — Lifeboats, liferafts, life jackets, fire extinguishers, EEBD.
8. **Navigation Equipment** — Radar, GPS, ECDIS, gyrocompass, echo sounder, GMDSS.
9. **Deck Machinery** — Windlasses, capstans, winches, cranes, hatch covers.

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Painting

### Why paint a ship?
- **Corrosion prevention:** Seawater contains ~3.5% dissolved salts → highly corrosive.
- **Antifouling:** Prevents marine organism attachment.
- **Identification:** Hull color, boot-top, company logo.
- **Aesthetic appeal.**
- **Fire retardancy** (special intumescent coatings).
- **Cargo compatibility** (tank coatings for chemical/product tankers per **IMO PSPC**).

### How is paint applied?

The five-step marine painting process:

1. **Surface Preparation** — Removing mill scale, rust, old paint, contaminants. Standard: **ISO 8501-1 (Sa 2½, Sa 3)** for blast cleaning, or **St 2/St 3** for hand/power tool cleaning.
2. **Primer Application** — Anti-corrosive primer (e.g., epoxy/zinc-rich primer).
3. **Intermediate Coat(s)** — Build-up coat (e.g., epoxy tie coat / build coat).
4. **Top Coat (Finish Coat)** — Provides color, gloss, UV resistance (e.g., polyurethane).
5. **Antifouling (for underwater hull)** — Self-polishing copolymer (SPC) or controlled depletion polymer (CDP) antifouling paint.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Painting Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically derived from volume solids (%)** — the percentage of paint that remains as solid film after the solvent evaporates.

### Master Formulae Table

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **WFT from DFT** | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT \times 100}{VS}$ | μm | VS = Volume Solids (%) |
| 2 | **DFT from WFT** | $DFT = \dfrac{WFT \times VS}{100}$ | μm | |
| 3 | **Theoretical Spreading Rate (TSR)** | $TSR = \dfrac{VS \times 10}{DFT}$ | m²/L | At 100% transfer efficiency |
| 4 | **Practical Spreading Rate (PSR)** | $PSR = TSR \times \eta$ | m²/L | η = Transfer efficiency (0.5–0.85) |
| 5 | **Paint Volume Required** | $V = \dfrac{Area}{PSR}$ | Litres | |
| 6 | **Paint Weight Required** | $W = V \times SG$ | kg | SG = Specific Gravity of paint |
| 7 | **Total DFT (multi-coat)** | $DFT_{total} = \sum DFT_i$ | μm | Sum of all individual coats |
| 8 | **Total WFT** | $WFT_{total} = \sum WFT_i$ | μm | |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Pipes `\|` Symbol:** In KTU exam answer sheets, when writing absolute values (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), use the LaTeX `\vert` command. In our tables, we use $\vert$ separator syntax as well to avoid breaking markdown rendering.

### Boundary Conditions & Assumptions

- **Volume Solids (VS)** for typical marine paints:
  - Epoxy primer: **50–65%**
  - Polyurethane topcoat: **50–60%**
  - Antifouling (SPC): **55–65%**
  - Zinc-rich primer: **30–45%**
- **Transfer Efficiency (η)**:
  - Brush application: **60–70%**
  - Roller: **70–80%**
  - Conventional air spray: **50–65%**
  - Airless spray: **70–85%**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are used by:
- **Shipyards** for paint procurement and cost estimation.
- **Classification societies** to verify coating compliance.
- **Marine surveyors** during dry-dock surveys.
- **Ship owners** for coating maintenance budgets (annual ~2% of newbuilding cost goes to coatings).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of WFT from DFT

### Logical Premise
When a paint is applied, it is in a **wet state** containing both solid binder and volatile solvent. After drying, the solvent evaporates and only the solid binder remains as the **dry film**.

Let:
- $V_{wet}$ = Volume of wet paint applied
- $VS$ = Volume Solids (in %)
- $V_{dry}$ = Volume of dry film remaining

By definition of Volume Solids:

$$
VS(\%) = \frac{V_{dry}}{V_{wet}} \times 100
$$

Since film thickness is proportional to volume (for a given area $A$):

$$
VS = \frac{DFT \times A}{WFT \times A} \times 100 = \frac{DFT}{WFT} \times 100
$$

Rearranging for WFT:

$$
WFT = \frac{DFT \times 100}{VS}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since VS is always < 100%, WFT is **always greater than DFT**. For example, with a 50% VS paint and required 100 μm DFT, the wet film must be 200 μm thick.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Spreading Rate (TSR)

### Logical Premise
Spreading rate = Area covered per unit volume of paint.

Consider 1 Litre (1000 cm³) of paint with Volume Solids VS%.
The solid content in 1 Litre:

$$
V_{solids} = \frac{VS}{100} \times 1000 \text{ cm}^3 = 10 \times VS \text{ cm}^3
$$

This solid volume, when spread as a uniform film of thickness DFT (in μm), covers an area:

$$
A = \frac{V_{solids}}{DFT}
$$

Converting units: 1 cm³ = 10⁶ μm × 10⁻⁴ m²/μm × 10⁻⁴ … let's do it cleanly:

$$
1 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ of film } = \frac{1 \text{ cm}^2 \times 1 \text{ cm}}{DFT(\mu m)} = \frac{10^4 \text{ mm}^2 \times 10 \text{ mm}}{DFT(\mu m)}
$$

Standard industry derivation gives the elegant formula:

$$
TSR \left(\frac{m^2}{L}\right) = \frac{VS(\%) \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units check:** If VS = 60% and DFT = 100 μm, then TSR = $\dfrac{60 \times 10}{100}$ = **6 m²/L**. This means 1 litre of this paint will cover 6 m² at 100 μm DFT (theoretically).

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style)

### Problem Statement
> *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]* A ship's underwater hull requires an antifouling coating with a specified DFT of **150 μm**. The antifouling paint has Volume Solids = **60%** and Specific Gravity = **1.4**. The application method (airless spray) has a transfer efficiency of **75%**. Calculate:
> (a) Wet Film Thickness
> (b) Theoretical Spreading Rate
> (c) Practical Spreading Rate
> (d) Volume of paint required to coat an area of **5000 m²**
> (e) Weight of paint required in kg

### Given Data
- DFT = 150 μm
- VS = 60%
- SG = 1.4
- η = 75% = 0.75
- Area A = 5000 m²

### Part (a) — Wet Film Thickness

$$
WFT = \frac{DFT \times 100}{VS}
$$

Substituting:

$$
WFT = \frac{150 \times 100}{60}
$$

$$
WFT = \frac{15000}{60} = 250 \text{ μm}
$$

**WFT = 250 μm** ✓

### Part (b) — Theoretical Spreading Rate

$$
TSR = \frac{VS \times 10}{DFT}
$$

$$
TSR = \frac{60 \times 10}{150}
$$

$$
TSR = \frac{600}{150} = 4 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**TSR = 4 m²/L** ✓

### Part (c) — Practical Spreading Rate

$$
PSR = TSR \times \eta
$$

$$
PSR = 4 \times 0.75 = 3 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**PSR = 3 m²/L** ✓

### Part (d) — Volume of Paint Required

$$
V = \frac{A}{PSR}
$$

$$
V = \frac{5000}{3} = 1666.67 \text{ Litres}
$$

**V ≈ 1666.67 L** ✓

### Part (e) — Weight of Paint Required

$$
W = V \times SG
$$

$$
W = 1666.67 \times 1.4 = 2333.33 \text{ kg}
$$

**W ≈ 2333.33 kg (≈ 2.33 tonnes)** ✓

### Summary Table

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| WFT | **250 μm** |
| TSR | **4 m²/L** |
| PSR | **3 m²/L** |
| Volume | **1666.67 L** |
| Weight | **2333.33 kg** |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Coat Painting System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Real marine paint systems consist of **multiple coats**. The total DFT is the **sum** of individual coat DFTs, but total paint consumption must account for each coat separately because each may have different VS%.

### Example: Typical Epoxy Antifouling System

| Coat | Type | DFT (μm) | VS (%) | TSR (m²/L) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Epoxy Primer (anti-corrosive) | 75 | 55 | 7.33 |
| 2 | Epoxy Build Coat | 125 | 60 | 4.80 |
| 3 | Tie Coat | 50 | 50 | 10.00 |
| 4 | Antifouling (SPC) | 150 | 60 | 4.00 |
| **Total** | | **400 μm** | | |

For an area of **10,000 m²**, the total paint volume (assuming airless spray η = 0.8):

$$
V_{coat1} = \frac{10000}{7.33 \times 0.8} = 1705.6 \text{ L}
$$

$$
V_{coat2} = \frac{10000}{4.80 \times 0.8} = 2604.2 \text{ L}
$$

$$
V_{coat3} = \frac{10000}{10.00 \times 0.8} = 1250.0 \text{ L}
$$

$$
V_{coat4} = \frac{10000}{4.00 \times 0.8} = 3125.0 \text{ L}
$$

$$
V_{total} = 1705.6 + 2604.2 + 1250.0 + 3125.0 = 8684.8 \text{ L}
$$

**Total paint required ≈ 8685 L ≈ 8.685 m³** — a substantial quantity highlighting the importance of coating in ship economics.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Outfitting Process Flow — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Blocks Fabricated] --> B{Stage 1: Pre-Outfitting at Block Stage}
    B --> B1[Machinery Foundations Welded]
    B --> B2[Pipe Spools Fitted]
    B --> B3[Cable Trays Mounted]
    B --> B4[Ventilation Ducts Installed]
    B1 --> C[Hull Block Erection on Berth]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D{Stage 2: On-Berth Outfitting}
    D --> D1[Main Engine Installation]
    D --> D2[Deck Machinery Fitting]
    D --> D3[Mast and Funnel Erection]
    D --> D4[Piping System Completion]
    C --> E[Launching]
    E --> F{Stage 3: Floating Outfitting}
    F --> F1[Shaft Alignment and Propeller Fitting]
    F --> F2[Joiner Work and Interior Finishing]
    F --> F3[Final Painting and Cosmetic Coating]
    F --> F4[Sea Trials Preparation]
    F1 --> G[Sea Trials]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Ship Delivery to Owner]
```

## 4.2 Marine Painting System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Steel Substrate] --> P2{Surface Preparation}
    P2 --> P21[ISO 8501-1 Sa 2.5 Blast Cleaning]
    P21 --> P3[Shop Primer - 20 to 40 microns]
    P3 --> P4[Anti-Corrosive Primer Epoxy - 75 microns]
    P4 --> P5[Build Coat Epoxy - 125 microns]
    P5 --> P6[Tie Coat - 50 microns]
    P6 --> P7{Topside vs Underwater}
    P7 --> P71[Topside: Polyurethane Finish - 50 microns]
    P7 --> P72[Underwater: Antifouling SPC - 150 to 200 microns]
    P71 --> P8[Total System DFT 300 to 400 microns]
    P72 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Coating Layer Functions — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Layer_Stack
        L1[Top Coat - Color and UV Resistance] --> L2[Intermediate Coat - Barrier Protection]
        L2 --> L3[Anti-Corrosive Primer - Corrosion Inhibition]
        L3 --> L4[Surface Preparation - Anchor Profile]
    end
    L4 --> ST[Steel Hull Substrate]
    L1 --> ENV[Marine Environment]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define **Outfitting** in ship construction. List any **four major sub-systems** covered under outfitting.

### Model Answer

**Definition:**
Outfitting is the second major phase of ship construction, following hull erection, in which all auxiliary systems, machinery, equipment, accommodation, and finishing components are installed onto the hull to make it operational and seaworthy.

**Four major sub-systems:**
1. **Propulsion and Shafting System** — Main engine, reduction gear, shaft line, propeller.
2. **Piping Systems** — Bilge, ballast, fuel oil, freshwater, fire-fighting, sanitary.
3. **Electrical Systems** — Generators, switchboards, motors, navigation electronics.
4. **Joiner/Accommodation Work** — Cabins, mess rooms, galley, sanitary spaces.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is the difference between **DFT** and **WFT**? Why is **DFT** the controlling parameter in marine coating specifications?

### Model Answer

**DFT (Dry Film Thickness):** The thickness of the paint film **after** all volatile solvents have evaporated. It is the permanent, residual protective layer.

**WFT (Wet Film Thickness):** The thickness of the paint film **immediately after application**, while it is still wet and contains solvents.

**Why DFT controls specifications:**
- WFT is transient and evaporates; only DFT remains as the actual barrier.
- WFT depends on application method and conditions (humidity, temperature).
- DFT directly determines the **corrosion protection capability** and is verifiable using non-destructive gauges (e.g., **Elcometer**).
- Classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) specify DFT, not WFT.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** 

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **three stages of outfitting** with their respective activities, advantages, and the modern "**outfit-first**" shipbuilding philosophy.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **classification of marine paints** based on function and location, with at least **two examples** for each category.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Three Stages of Outfitting

**Stage 1: Pre-Erection / Pre-Outfitting (Block Outfitting)**
- Conducted on **sub-assemblies and hull blocks** before they are erected on the building berth.
- Maximum work is shifted to the workshop for parallel execution.
- Activities: Welding of pipe supports, machinery seatings, cable tray brackets, ventilation duct mounting, installation of smaller pumps and motors.
- **Advantage:** Drastically reduces total construction time.

**Stage 2: On-Berth / Post-Erection Outfitting**
- Carried out after hull blocks are joined on the berth/slipway but **before launching**.
- Heavy and large equipment is installed: main engine, auxiliary generators, deck machinery (winches, windlasses), masts, funnels.
- Piping system completion, electrical cabling, insulation, and structural painting.

**Stage 3: Floating / Post-Launch Outfitting**
- Conducted **after the ship is launched** and moored at the fitting-out berth.
- Final shaft alignment, propeller fitting, rudder fitting.
- Joiner work, interior furnishing, final cosmetic painting.
