- Loading of bunkers, lub oil, freshwater for sea trials.

**"Outfit-First" Philosophy:**
The modern shipbuilding approach shifts maximum outfitting work to the **block fabrication stage**, leveraging modular construction. Benefits include:
- Reduced dry-dock/berth occupancy time (by 30–40%).
- Better quality control (workshop environment).
- Parallel workflows → faster overall delivery.
- Lower labor cost per ton of ship.

#### Part (b) — Classification of Marine Paints

| Category | Function | Location | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Anti-Corrosive Paints** | Prevent rust on steel | All steel surfaces | Zinc-rich epoxy primer, epoxy primer |
| **Antifouling Paints** | Prevent marine organism growth | Underwater hull | SPC (Self-Polishing Copolymer), CDP, Foul-release silicone coatings |
| **Topside / Decorative Paints** | Color, gloss, UV resistance | Above waterline hull, superstructure | Polyurethane, alkyd enamel |
| **Boot-Top Paints** | Resist alternate wet-dry cycles | Boot-top zone (waterline ±) | High-build epoxy, chlorinated rubber |
| **Tank Coatings** | Protect against cargo/ballast | Cargo tanks, ballast tanks | Epoxy phenolic, pure epoxy, zinc silicate |
| **Fire-Retardant Coatings** | Resist fire spread | Bulkheads, decks | Intumescent coatings |
| **Specialty Coatings** | Specific functions | Specific areas | Anti-skid deck paint, heat-resistant paint for funnels |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the relationship between **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** and **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** in terms of Volume Solids. State the formula for **Theoretical Spreading Rate (TSR)**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A ship's topside requires a polyurethane topcoat with specified **DFT = 50 μm**. The paint has **Volume Solids = 55%** and **Specific Gravity = 1.2**. The application method (brush) has a transfer efficiency of **65%**. Calculate the **volume of paint (in litres)** required to coat an area of **2000 m²**, and the **weight in kg**.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Definitions:**
- $VS$ = Volume Solids (%) = volume fraction of paint that becomes solid film.
- $DFT$ = Dry Film Thickness (μm)
- $WFT$ = Wet Film Thickness (μm)

**Derivation:**

For a paint with Volume Solids $VS\%$, the dry volume is $VS/100$ times the wet volume. Since thickness is proportional to volume:

$$
VS\% = \frac{V_{dry}}{V_{wet}} \times 100 = \frac{DFT}{WFT} \times 100
$$

Solving for WFT:

$$
WFT = \frac{DFT \times 100}{VS}
$$

Solving for DFT:

$$
DFT = \frac{WFT \times VS}{100}
$$

**TSR Derivation:**

Consider 1 litre (1000 cm³) of paint with VS% solids.
Solid volume per litre = $\dfrac{VS}{100} \times 1000 = 10 \times VS$ cm³.

This solid film, spread at DFT μm thick, covers:

$$
A = \frac{10 \times VS \text{ cm}^3 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^2/\text{cm}^2}{DFT(\mu m) \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}/\mu m}
$$

Standard industry result:

$$
TSR = \frac{VS \times 10}{DFT} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{L}]
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- DFT = 50 μm
- VS = 55%
- SG = 1.2
- η = 0.65
- Area = 2000 m²

**Step 1 — Theoretical Spreading Rate:**

$$
TSR = \frac{VS \times 10}{DFT} = \frac{55 \times 10}{50} = \frac{550}{50} = 11 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Practical Spreading Rate:**

$$
PSR = TSR \times \eta = 11 \times 0.65 = 7.15 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of Paint:**

$$
V = \frac{A}{PSR} = \frac{2000}{7.15} = 279.72 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Weight of Paint:**

$$
W = V \times SG = 279.72 \times 1.2 = 335.66 \text{ kg}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Volume ≈ **279.72 L**
- Weight ≈ **335.66 kg**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply Transfer Efficiency (η)** — many students compute TSR only and forget to multiply by η, leading to a 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — keeping SG in g/cm³ while volume is in litres → always confirm units are consistent (1 L × SG = kg if SG is unitless relative to water).
> 3. **Mixing up WFT and DFT** — if the question gives WFT, do not convert using the WFT formula; use $DFT = (WFT \times VS)/100$.
> 4. **Summing DFT of multi-coat systems incorrectly** — total DFT is the **sum**, but total paint volume is **NOT** proportional to total DFT because each coat has different VS%.
> 5. **Not stating the area clearly in numerical problems** — examiners often deduct 1 mark if units are not specified.
> 6. **Writing absolute value `|x|` using pipe symbol in answer sheets** — although not penalized in handwritten exams, it's good LaTeX practice to use `\vert` in digital submissions.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Outfitting & Painting**

### Outfitting
- ✔ Outfitting = Second phase of shipbuilding, **after hull erection**.
- ✔ **Three Stages:** Pre-erection (block), On-berth, Floating (post-launch).
- ✔ Modern philosophy = **"Outfit-First"** → maximum work at block stage.
- ✔ Subsystems: Propulsion, Piping, Electrical, HVAC, Joiner, Safety, Navigation, Deck machinery.
- ✔ Parallel execution of outfitting with hull erection saves time and cost.

### Painting
- ✔ Painting is **not decorative** — it is **engineering-critical corrosion/fouling protection**.
- ✔ Five-step process: **Surface prep → Primer → Build coat → Tie coat → Finish/Antifouling**.
- ✔ Surface preparation standard: **ISO 8501-1** (Sa 2½ for blast cleaning).
- ✔ Classification by location: **Topside, Boot-top, Underwater, Tanks, Decks**.
- ✔ Classification by function: **Anti-corrosive, Antifouling, Decorative, Fire-retardant, Tank lining**.

### Master Formulas (Memorize)
- ✔ $WFT = \dfrac{DFT \times 100}{VS}$
- ✔ $TSR = \dfrac{VS \times 10}{DFT}$ m²/L
- ✔ $PSR = TSR \times \eta$
- ✔ $V = \dfrac{Area}{PSR}$
- ✔ $W = V \times SG$

### Standards & Regulations
- ✔ **ISO 12944** — Corrosion protection of steel structures.
- ✔ **ISO 8501-1** — Surface preparation grades.
- ✔ **SSPC / NACE** — American surface prep standards.
- ✔ **IMO PSPC** — Performance Standard for Protective Coatings.
- ✔ **SOLAS** — Safety of Life at Sea (life-saving & fire protection).
- ✔ **MARPOL** — Antifouling regulations (AFS Convention → ban on TBT).

### Numerical Problem Strategy
- ✔ Step 1: List given data.
- ✔ Step 2: Identify what's asked.
- ✔ Step 3: Apply correct formula.
- ✔ Step 4: Maintain unit consistency.
- ✔ Step 5: Mention transfer efficiency and surface wastage.
- ✔ Step 6: Final answer with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grouping and Work Breakdown Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Grouping and Work Breakdown Structure # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition: Grouping in Ship Construction **Grouping** in ship construction refers to the systematic division of a ship's hull into smaller, independently manageable **sub-assemblies** (called *groups* or *blocks*) that are fabricated in parallel at different workstations and later joined together to form the complete vessel. Each group contains the necessary plates, stiffeners, frames, and outfit items required for that section of the ship. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Grouping is the foundation of **modern modular shipbuilding**. Without proper grouping, the parallel production philosophy of shipyards would collapse into serial, inefficient workflows. The most common grouping practices in KTU-aligned Naval Architecture syllabus are: - **Longitudinal Grouping** – dividing the ship along its length (Fore, Mid, Aft, Engine Room sections). - **Transverse Grouping** – dividing the ship across its breadth (Port and Starboard halves). - **Vertical Grouping** – dividing by decks and superstructures. - **Combined/Block Grouping** – the industry standard where 3D rectangular blocks are built. ## 1.2 Formal Definition: Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) A **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is a **hierarchical, deliverable-oriented decomposition** of the total ship construction project into progressively smaller and more manageable work elements. It transforms the abstract concept of "build a ship" into a tree of discrete, assignable, and measurable tasks. > [!NOTE] > **Definition (PMI / KTU Reference):** A WBS is *"a deliverable-oriented hierarchical decomposition of the total work scope to be carried out by the project team to accomplish the project objectives and create the required deliverables."* ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Lego & Lego-Room Approach Imagine you want to build a **three-storey house**: | Approach | Shipbuilding Equivalent | Outcome | |---|---|---| | **Brick-by-brick** (traditional) | **Plating-on-skeleton** method (old shipyards) | Slow, sequential, high dependency | | **Pre-fabricated rooms** then stacked (modern) | **Block/group construction** | Fast, parallel, fewer dependencies | | **Detailed inventory list** of every nut, bolt, brick | **Work Breakdown Structure** | Clarity, costing, scheduling | **Intuition:** *Grouping* is the **physical act** of slicing the ship into pieces. *WBS* is the **paperwork/plan** that documents exactly what each piece contains, who builds it, when, and at what cost. They are inseparable: you cannot group effectively without a WBS, and a WBS without physical grouping is just a document. ## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics - **Block Weight Range:** **50 – 400 tonnes** (modern shipyards crane capacity dictates this). - **Block Length:** Typically **12 – 24 m** (limited by workshop bay length). - **WBS Levels:** **5 to 8 levels** in standard shipbuilding projects. - **Work Package Duration:** **40 to 400 man-hours** (optimal control unit). > [!NOTE] > **Constants Used:** 1 tonne = 1000 kg; 1 metre (m) = 3.28 ft. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principles of Grouping in Shipbuilding The grouping of a ship's structure is governed by six engineering principles: 1. **Symmetry Principle:** Groups are designed symmetrically about the centerline so that port and starboard units can be built from the same jig/loft template. 2. **Weight & Crane Capacity Principle:** The mass of any group must not exceed the lifting capacity of the available gantry/goliath crane, with a **safety factor of 1.25** typically applied. 3. **Transportability Principle:** The group must physically fit through workshop doors and be transportable by self-propelled modular trailers (SPMTs). 4. **Outfitting Density Principle:** Groups located in machinery spaces (high piping density) should be **larger and heavier** to maximize pre-outfitting benefits. 5. **Joint Accessibility Principle:** Group boundaries must fall on flat plate surfaces, away from knuckle points, to facilitate automatic welding. 6. **Sequencing Principle:** Groups must be erected in a sequence that maintains longitudinal and transverse stability of the hull at every stage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Group?** — *Parallelism of work, reduction of on-board work (improves safety & weather independence), better quality control in sheltered workshops, and reduced construction time.* ## 2.2 Types of Grouping Strategies ### A. Zone Grouping The ship is divided into **zones** based on functional areas. Outfitting tasks within a zone are completed *before* the block is erected. - **Zones:** Accommodation zone, Machinery zone, Cargo zone, Bow zone, Stern zone. ### B. Block Grouping The hull is divided into **3-dimensional blocks**. Each block is a complete structural unit with shell plating, decks, bulkheads, and pre-installed outfit. > [!NOTE] > **Modern shipyards use "Block Outfitting"** where 60–80% of total outfitting is completed **before** the block reaches the slipway. ### C. Pre-erection Grouping Small sub-assemblies (panels, stiffener sub-groups) are welded into **erection units** before being hoisted to the berth. ## 2.3 Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) — Levels A standard KTU-level ship construction WBS contains the following levels: | Level | Name | Typical Content | Example | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | Project | Entire ship | "Vessel TBN-2024" | | 1 | Major Area | Major disciplines | Hull, Machinery, Outfit, Electrical | | 2 | Sub-system | Functional groups | Hull Structure, Hull Steel | | 3 | Zone/Area | Spatial zones | Forward, Midship, Aft | | 4 | Block/Group | 3D assembly unit | Block F-12, Block M-05 | | 5 | Sub-assembly | Panel or unit | Side shell panel, Transverse bulkhead panel | | 6 | Part/Fabrication | Individual piece | Plate, stiffener, bracket | | 7 | Operation | Single task | Cut, weld, fit, paint | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU-board-tested and used in shipyard productivity calculations.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W_{block} \le \dfrac{W_{crane}}{SF}$ | Maximum allowable block weight | tonnes | | 2 | $W_{crane}$ = Safe Working Load (SWL) | Crane capacity | tonnes | | 3 | $SF = 1.25$ (standard) | Safety Factor | dimensionless | | 4 | $N_{blocks} = \dfrac{L_{OA} \times B \times D}{V_{unit}}$ | Approximate number of blocks | count | | 5 | $V_{unit}$ = 12 × 18 × 8 = 1728 m³ | Typical block volume | m³ | | 6 | $T_{project} = \dfrac{W_{steel}}{P \cdot N_{teams}}$ | Total construction time | months | | 7 | $P$ = Productivity per team | tonnes/month | t/month | | 8 | $N_{teams}$ = Parallel work teams | dimensionless | count | | 9 | $C_{unit} = C_{mat} + C_{lab} + C_{OH}$ | Cost of a WBS work package | ₹ / $ | | 10 | $OH$ = Overhead (typically 25% of $C_{lab}$) | Overhead rate | ₹ / $ | **Where:** - $L_{OA}$ = Length Overall - $B$ = Breadth - $D$ = Depth - $W_{steel}$ = Total steel weight ## 2.5 Real-World Utility In commercial shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, Hyundai Heavy Industries), WBS is the **single most important document** used for: - **Cost control** (each WBS element has a budget code). - **Schedule planning** (each WBS element has start/finish dates). - **Earned Value Management (EVM)** (progress is measured at WBS Level 4–5). - **Procurement** (material call-off is generated from Level 6 parts). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Block Weight from Crane Capacity **Problem Statement:** A shipyard has a gantry crane with a Safe Working Load (SWL) of **250 tonnes**. Determine the maximum permissible block weight using the standard shipbuilding safety factor. **Given:** - $W_{crane} = 250$ tonnes - $SF = 1.25$ (standard shipbuilding practice) **Step 1 — State the governing formula:** $$W_{block} \le \dfrac{W_{crane}}{SF}$$ This says the **block weight must be less than or equal to** the crane's safe capacity divided by the safety factor. The factor of safety accounts for dynamic loads (swinging), rigging mass, and asymmetric loading. **Step 2 — Substitute the known values:** $$W_{block} \le \dfrac{250 \text{ t}}{1.25}$$ **Step 3 — Perform the arithmetic:** $$W_{block} \le 200 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** > The design team must now split the hull into groups such that **no individual block exceeds 200 tonnes**. If a particular section (e.g., double-bottom tank) weighs more, it must be sub-divided into smaller erection units. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Estimating Number of Blocks for a Vessel **Problem Statement:** Estimate the number of blocks for a Panamax bulk carrier with the following dimensions: - $L_{OA} = 225$ m - $B = 32.2$ m - $D = 19.9$ m **Step 1 — Compute the total hull envelope volume:** $$V_{hull} = L_{OA} \times B \times D$$ $$V_{hull} = 225 \times 32.2 \times 19.9$$ Performing the multi-stage multiplication: $$\begin{aligned} 225 \times 32.2 &= 7245 \\ 7245 \times 19.9 &= 144{,}175.5 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Define the unit block volume:** A standard block in this size class is typically **24 m long × 18 m wide × 8 m high**: $$V_{unit} = 24 \times 18 \times 8$$ $$V_{unit} = 3456 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Apply the empirical block count formula (corrected for volumetric efficiency):** Modern shipyards achieve approximately **30–40%** volumetric packing efficiency (because the hull is not a solid box). Using $\eta = 0.35$: $$N_{blocks} = \dfrac{V_{hull} \times \eta}{V_{unit}}$$ $$N_{blocks} = \dfrac{144{,}175.5 \times 0.35}{3456}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate:** $$N_{blocks} = \dfrac{50{,}461.4}{3456} \approx 14.6$$ Rounding up to a whole number: **$N_{blocks} \approx 15$ blocks.** **Step 5 — Sanity check:** A Panamax bulker typically has **12 to 20 blocks** in practice. Our estimate of 15 lies comfortably within this band, validating the formula. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: WBS Decomposition of a Ship Hull The following table traces one path down a WBS for a generic cargo vessel: | Level | WBS Code | Element Name | Responsible Party | Cost Centre | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | SHIP-001 | Vessel Hull Complete | Project Manager | P-100 | | 1 | HULL | Hull | Hull Superintendent | H-100 | | 2 | HULL-ST | Hull Steel | Steel Foreman | H-200 | | 3 | HULL-ST-FWD | Forward Region | Zone Engineer | H-310 | | 4 | HULL-ST-FWD-B07 | Block F-07 (Bow Block) | Block Foreman | H-311 | | 5 | HULL-ST-FWD-B07-SS | Side Shell Sub-assembly | Sub-assembly Lead | H-311-A | | 6 | HULL-ST-FWD-B07-SS-P12 | Plate P-12 (5.5 × 2.2 m) | Fabricator | H-311-A-12 | | 7 | TASK-CUT-12 | Cut Plate to Shape | Cutter | OP-001 | **Reading the matrix:** - Going **down** the WBS = increasing detail. - Going **across** = increasing parallel work scope. - Each row has a **single owner** (the **8th – 80% rule** of WBS: each element ≤ 8 levels deep, ≥ 80 hours of work, single accountable owner). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Ship Hull Grouping — Top-Down Block Layout The following diagram shows how a typical cargo ship is grouped into blocks along its length, with the engine room zone highlighted as a separate grouping because of its dense outfitting. ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["BOW BLOCK F-01
Approx 180 t"] nodeB["FWD CARGO BLOCK F-02
Approx 195 t"] nodeC["FWD ENGINE ROOM BLOCK M-03
Approx 220 t"] nodeD["AFT ENGINE ROOM BLOCK M-04
Approx 220 t"] nodeE["AFT CARGO BLOCK A-05
Approx 195 t"] nodeF["STERN BLOCK A-06
Approx 175 t"] nodeG["UPPER DECK GROUP UD-07
Approx 110 t"] nodeH["ACCOMMODATION BLOCK ACC-08
Approx 95 t"] nodeA --- nodeB nodeB --- nodeC nodeC --- nodeD nodeD --- nodeE nodeE --- nodeF nodeG --- nodeB nodeG --- nodeE nodeH --- nodeC classDef critical fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px classDef standard fill:#e8f4fd,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:1px classDef outfit fill:#eafaf1,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:1px class nodeC,nodeD critical class nodeA,nodeB,nodeE,nodeF standard class nodeG,nodeH outfit ``` > **Legend:** > - **Red (critical):** Engine-room blocks — heaviest outfitting density. > - **Blue (standard):** Hull structural blocks. > - **Green (outfit-heavy):** Superstructure and accommodation blocks. --- ## 4.2 Work Breakdown Structure — Hierarchical Tree The following block-flow architecture illustrates the WBS hierarchy as a top-down decomposition, showing how a single project decomposes from the vessel level all the way down to individual welding tasks. ```mermaid graph TD L0["LEVEL 0
PROJECT
Vessel TBN-2024"] L1A["LEVEL 1
HULL"] L1B["LEVEL 1
MACHINERY"] L1C["LEVEL 1
OUTFIT"] L1D["LEVEL 1
ELECTRICAL"] L2A["LEVEL 2
HULL STEEL"] L2B["LEVEL 2
HULL OUTFIT"] L3A["LEVEL 3
FORWARD ZONE"] L3B["LEVEL 3
MIDSHIP ZONE"] L3C["LEVEL 3
AFT ZONE"] L4A["LEVEL 4
BLOCK F-07"] L4B["LEVEL 4
BLOCK M-12"] L5A["LEVEL 5
SIDE SHELL PANEL"] L5B["LEVEL 5
DECK PLATE PANEL"] L5C["LEVEL 5
TRANSVERSE BHD"] L6A["LEVEL 6
PLATE P-12"] L6B["LEVEL 6
STIFFENER T-08"] L7A["LEVEL 7
CUT TASK"] L7B["LEVEL 7
WELD TASK"] L7C["LEVEL 7
FIT-UP TASK"] L0 --> L1A L0 --> L1B L0 --> L1C L0 --> L1D L1A --> L2A L1A --> L2B L2A --> L3A L2A --> L3B L2A --> L3C L3A --> L4A L3B --> L4B L4A --> L5A L4A --> L5B L4B --> L5C L5A --> L6A L5A --> L6B L6A --> L7A L6A --> L7B L6B --> L7C ``` --- ## 4.3 Production Flow — From Part to Vessel This functional architecture flow shows how a single plate travels through every WBS level, from raw material to installed part of the completed ship. ```mermaid graph LR stepA["RAW STEEL PLATE
Stores"] stepB["LEVEL 6
Cut and Shape"] stepC["LEVEL 5
Sub-assembly Fit-up"] stepD["LEVEL 5
Sub-assembly Welding"] stepE["LEVEL 4
Block Assembly"] stepF["LEVEL 4
Block Welding"] stepG["LEVEL 4
Pre-outfitting"] stepH["LEVEL 3
Erection on Slipway"] stepI["LEVEL 2
Hull Joining"] stepJ["LEVEL 1
Outfit and Commission"] stepK["LEVEL 0
Sea Trials and Delivery"] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define grouping as applied to ship construction. List any four principles governing the selection of group boundaries.** **Model Answer:** Grouping in ship construction is the process of dividing the ship's hull structure into a number of smaller, independently buildable sub-assemblies called groups or blocks, which are later joined together during erection on the slipway or in the dry dock. **Four Principles of Grouping:** 1. **Crane Capacity Principle:** The weight of any group must be less than or equal to the crane's Safe Working Load divided by a safety factor of 1.25. 2. **Symmetry Principle:** Groups should be symmetric about the centerline to enable the use of a single template for port and starboard fabrication. 3. **Joint Accessibility Principle:** Group boundaries must lie on flat, accessible plate surfaces to facilitate automatic welding and NDT inspection. 4. **Outfitting Density Principle:** Blocks containing machinery, tanks, or pipe-heavy areas should be sized to permit maximum pre-outfitting before erection. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)? Mention its significance in shipbuilding projects.** **Model Answer:** A Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) is a hierarchical, deliverable-oriented decomposition of the total ship construction work scope into progressively smaller and more manageable work packages. It breaks the project down from the vessel level (Level 0) through major areas (hull, machinery, outfit, electrical) down to individual fabrication tasks (Level 7). **Significance in Shipbuilding:** 1. **Cost Control:** Provides a cost account for every element, enabling accurate budgeting and earned-value analysis. 2. **Schedule Planning:** Each WBS element can be assigned start and finish dates, dependencies, and resources. 3. **Responsibility Assignment:** Establishes a single accountable owner for every work package (RACI matrix input). 4. **Progress Measurement:** Provides the basis for physical and financial progress reporting to owners and classification societies. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always state the governing formula before substituting numbers.** Students who skip this lose 2 marks instantly. > 2. **Include units (t, m, m³) in every numerical answer.** Omission of units is a common 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Mention the Safety Factor explicitly** in crane-related calculations. > 4. **Do not confuse Block Outfitting with Zone Outfitting** — examiners test this distinction every year. > 5. **Draw labelled diagrams wherever the question asks for "explain with sketch."** --- ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of grouping strategies used in modern ship construction with neat sketches. Discuss the advantages of block construction over the old plating-on-skeleton method. **(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard has a gantry crane with SWL of **320 tonnes**. Determine the maximum permissible block weight using a safety factor of 1.25. If the total steel weight of the vessel is **12,000 tonnes** and the yard has **4 parallel teams** each with a productivity of **250 tonnes/month**, calculate the total construction time in months. #### Model Solution **(a) Grouping Strategies and Advantages:** **Types of Grouping:** 1. **Zone Grouping:** The ship is divided along its length into zones (Forward, Midship, Aft, Accommodation). All outfitting tasks within a zone are completed before the block is erected. 2. **Block Grouping:** The hull is divided into 3-dimensional blocks. Each block contains shell plating, decks, bulkheads, and pre-installed outfit items such as pipes, cables, and machinery seats. 3. **Pre-erection Grouping (Panel Grouping):** Smaller flat or curved panels with stiffeners welded in are assembled before being hoisted into position. 4. **Transverse Grouping:** The ship is split across the beam into sections, typically used for very large vessels where longitudinal sections would be too heavy. **Advantages of Block Construction over Plating-on-Skeleton:** | # | Advantage | Explanation | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Parallel Production** | Multiple blocks are built simultaneously, reducing total build time. | | 2 | **Weather Independence** | Most work is done indoors, eliminating rain/wind delays. | | 3 | **Higher Quality** | Better welding conditions (no wind) → lower defect rates. | | 4 | **Improved Safety** | Workers operate at ground level instead of high scaffolding. | | 5 | **Higher Pre-outfitting** | 60–80% outfit completed before erection → less on-board work. | | 6 | **Better Resource Utilisation** | Workshops can run at steady-state throughput. | **Sketch (refer to SECTION 4.1):** Top-down view of a ship divided into forward, midship, and aft blocks. **(b) Numerical Calculation:** **Part (i) — Maximum Block Weight:** Given: $W_{crane} = 320$ t, $SF = 1.25$. $$W_{block} \le \dfrac{W_{crane}}{SF} = \dfrac{320}{1.25}$$ $$\boxed{W_{block} = 256 \text{ tonnes}}$$ **Part (ii) — Total Construction Time:** Given: $W_{steel} = 12{,}000$ t, $N_{teams} = 4$, $P = 250$ t/month per team. Applying the formula: $$T_{project} = \dfrac{W_{steel}}{P \cdot N_{teams}}$$ $$T_{project} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{250 \times 4} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{1000}$$ $$\boxed{T_{project} = 12 \text{ months}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** Each team works on a different block in parallel. With 4 teams, the effective combined throughput is $4 \times 250 = 1000$ t/month. Dividing 12,000 tonnes by this throughput yields 12 months of pure steel-fabrication time. (Outfitting and sea trials are additional.) --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a clear block diagram, explain the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** of a ship construction project. State the "8-to-80 Rule" applied to WBS. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a vessel with $L_{OA} = 180$ m, $B = 28$ m, $D = 16$ m, estimate the number of blocks required, assuming a unit block size of 24 m × 16 m × 7 m and a volumetric packing efficiency of 35%. #### Model Solution **(a) WBS Diagram and the 8-to-80 Rule:** The Work Breakdown Structure is a tree-like hierarchical decomposition of the project scope. For a ship, the WBS typically has the following levels: - **Level 0 — Project:** The complete vessel. - **Level 1 — Major Areas:** Hull, Machinery, Outfit, Electrical. - **Level 2 — Sub-systems:** Hull Steel, Hull Outfit, Propulsion, etc. - **Level 3 — Zones:** Forward, Midship, Aft. - **Level 4 — Blocks:** Block F-01, Block M-03, Block A-05, etc. - **Level 5 — Sub-assemblies:** Side shell panel, deck plate panel, transverse bulkhead. - **Level 6 — Parts:** Individual plates, stiffeners, brackets. - **Level 7 — Operations:** Cutting, fit-up, welding, blasting, painting. **Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.2 above):** Top-down tree showing decomposition from Level 0 (Project) to Level 7 (Operations). **The 8-to-80 Rule:** This is a project management heuristic for sizing WBS work packages: - **"8":** A WBS should not have more than **8 levels** of decomposition for any branch. Beyond 8 levels, the structure becomes unmanageable. - **"80":** The lowest-level work package should consume **no less than 80 hours** of effort (typically 40–400 hours). If a package is too small (< 8 hours), it should be merged upward; if too large (> 800 hours), it should be decomposed further. **Purpose:** Ensures the WBS is detailed enough for accurate control but not so granular that administrative overhead exceeds productive work. **(b) Numerical Calculation — Number of Blocks:** Given: - $L_{OA} = 180$ m - $B = 28$ m - $D = 16$ m - Unit block: $24 \times 16 \times 7$ m - $\eta = 0.35$ **Step 1 — Hull envelope volume:** $$V_{hull} = L_{OA} \times B \times D = 180 \times 28 \times 16$$ $$\begin{aligned} 180 \times 28 &= 5040 \\ 5040 \times 16 &= 80{,}640 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Unit block volume:** $$V_{unit} = 24 \times 16 \times 7 = 2688 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Apply block count formula:** $$N_{blocks} = \dfrac{V_{hull} \times \eta}{V_{unit}}$$ $$N_{blocks} = \dfrac{80{,}640 \times 0.35}{2688}$$ **Step 4 — Numerator evaluation:** $$80{,}640 \times 0.35 = 28{,}224 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 5 — Final division:** $$N_{blocks} = \dfrac{28{,}224}{2688} \approx 10.5$$ Rounding up: **$N_{blocks} = 11$ blocks.** **Validation:** For a 180 m vessel of this size class, real shipyards typically use 10–14 blocks. Our result of 11 is consistent with industry practice. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1** - **Grouping = physical slicing of the hull** into blocks; **WBS = paperwork/plan** that documents each slice. - **Six principles of grouping:** symmetry, crane capacity, transportability, outfitting density, joint accessibility, sequencing. - **Crane capacity formula:** $W_{block} = W_{crane} / SF$, where **SF = 1.25** (always). - **Block count formula:** $N_{blocks} = (L_{OA} \times B \times D \times \eta) / V_{unit}$. - **Block weight range:** **50 – 400 tonnes** in modern yards. - **Block outfitting:** 60–80% of outfitting is completed *before* block erection — this is the single biggest time-saver. - **WBS levels:** 0 (Project) → 1 (Area) → 2 (Sub-system) → 3 (Zone) → 4 (Block) → 5 (Sub-assembly) → 6 (Part) → 7 (Operation). - **8-to-80 Rule:** ≤ 8 WBS levels deep; lowest package ≥ 80 hours of work. - **Each WBS element must have ONE accountable owner** — this is the basis of the RACI matrix. - **Always include units** in numerical answers to avoid 1-mark KTU deduction. - **Plating-on-skeleton = old, serial, weather-dependent.** **Block construction = modern, parallel, weather-independent.** - **Engine room blocks are heaviest** due to dense outfitting — typically 220+ tonnes.

topics.title: Zone Construction Method for Hull Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Zone Construction Method for Hull Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

The **Zone Construction Method** is a modern, productivity-driven shipbuilding technique in which the entire hull of a vessel is logically and physically divided into a series of independent structural units called **zones** (or **block groups**), each comprising multiple sub-blocks. These zones are fabricated **in parallel** at different workstations or even different shipyards, and then transported, lifted, and joined together on the building berth or in a floating dock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Zone Construction is the **industry-standard method** adopted worldwide by all major shipyards (Hyundai Heavy Industries, Daewoo, Mitsui, Fincantieri, Mazagon Dock, Cochin Shipyard) because it converts a single, sequential shipbuilding project into a **manufacturing-style production line** with concurrent activities.

### Key Terminology Table

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Zone** | A large longitudinal section of the hull (e.g., Forward, Amidships, Aft) treated as one independent fabrication unit. |
| **Block** | A pre-fabricated sub-assembly of plates and stiffeners, forming part of a zone. |
| **Erection Block** | The largest block that can be lifted by the available gantry crane at the shipyard. |
| **Grand Block** | Two or more smaller blocks pre-joined at ground level before final erection. |
| **Unit** | The smallest pre-fabricated piece (e.g., a single panel with welded stiffeners). |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Building a Ship like Building a Skyscraper:**
> Imagine you are constructing a **30-storey building**. The old way is to build it floor by floor, one at a time (this is the traditional *Pyramid Method*). The **Zone Construction Method** is like a contractor deciding to fabricate **5 complete floor sections (zones)** simultaneously at 5 different sites, complete with plumbing, electrical wiring, and painting done beforehand. Then a giant crane lifts each finished zone and stacks it on top of the others. The total time collapses dramatically because painting, plumbing, and welding inside each zone were already done while the foundation was still being cast.

For a ship, the "floors" are the **zones (Forward, Mid-body, Aft)**, the "pre-fabricated floor sections" are the **grand blocks**, and the "giant crane" is the **Goliath Crane** or **Heavy-Lift Crane** (typically **600 tonnes to 1,200 tonnes** Safe Working Load) found in modern shipyards.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard zone width:** Usually **20–30 metres** longitudinally, depending on the ship's Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP).
- **Typical erection block weight:** **80–250 tonnes** for merchant vessels; up to **600–1,200 tonnes** for very large crude carriers (VLCCs) and naval aircraft carriers.
- **Number of zones per hull:** Typically **3 to 7 zones** (commonly 3: Forward, Amidships, Aft).
- **Cycle time reduction target:** **40–60%** reduction compared to the traditional keel-up pyramid method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metric vs. Imperial:** KTU 2024 syllabus uses **SI metric units (metres, tonnes, mm)** as per international shipbuilding standards (IACS — International Association of Classification Societies).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Logic of Zone Construction

The Zone Construction Method follows a strict **top-down, decomposed workflow**. Below is the operational logic broken into logical stages:

1. **Hull Zoning (Engineering Phase):**
   The complete hull GA (General Arrangement) drawing is logically subdivided longitudinally into **3 to 7 zones** along the ship's length. The division lines are chosen at locations of **low structural stress** to ease the final welded joint — typically at transverse bulkheads or frame stations where double-bottom tanks terminate.

2. **Block Decomposition (Planning Phase):**
   Each zone is further subdivided into **erection blocks** whose individual mass must not exceed the **crane capacity (Safe Working Load, SWL)** of the yard's heaviest gantry crane.

3. **Sub-Block Fabrication (Workshop Phase):**
   Smallest fabrication units (**panels**, **assemblies**) are cut, welded, and stiffened on horizontal platens in the steel workshop.

4. **Pre-Outfitting:**
   Piping, cable trays, machinery seats, and insulation are installed **inside the block while it is still on the ground**. This is the **single largest productivity gain** of the zone method, because the same work at height on the berth would consume 5× more man-hours.

5. **Pre-Painting (Blasting & Coating):**
   Steel surfaces are blasted to **Sa 2.5** standard (ISO 8501-1) and coated with shop primer at ground level — much safer and cheaper than painting at height.

6. **Grand Block Assembly:**
   Multiple sub-blocks are tack-welded together on the ground to form **Grand Blocks** (also called *Mega-Blocks*), reducing the number of high-altitude welds.

7. **Erection (Berth Phase):**
   Cranes lift the Grand Blocks onto the building berth in a pre-planned sequence, joining them zone-by-zone.

8. **Final Joining & Outfitting:**
   Zone-to-zone welds are completed, superstructure is mounted, and final commissioning (hose testing, electrical loop checks, sea trials) is performed.

### Why Zone Construction Works — The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why parallel zones?** → Eliminates the *single critical path* of traditional construction. Multiple teams work simultaneously.
- **Why ground-level pre-outfitting?** → Removes work-at-height, improves weld quality (flat/down-hand positions), reduces insurance and accident costs.
- **Why pre-painting?** → Saves **30–40%** of total paint consumption (no overspray loss) and eliminates the need for scaffolding.
- **Why grand blocks?** → Reduces the **number of on-berth welds** (each berth weld costs roughly **3× a shop weld** in man-hours).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Number of zones | $N_z = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{BP}}{L_z} \right\rceil$ | dimensionless |
| Block mass limit | $M_{block} \leq M_{crane,SWL} \times k_{safety}$ | tonnes |
| Safety factor | $k_{safety} = 0.75$ (typical for dynamic lifting) | dimensionless |
| Man-hour savings | $\Delta MH = MH_{trad} - MH_{zone}$ | man-hours |
| Productivity Index | $P = \dfrac{DWT}{MH_{total}}$ | tonnes/man-hour |
| Total erection welds | $W_{total} = N_{blocks} \times W_{joint}$ | metres of weld |
| Grand block weld share | $\eta_{GB} = \dfrac{W_{ground}}{W_{total}}$ (target $\geq 70\%$) | dimensionless |

> **Notation:** $L_{BP}$ = Length Between Perpendiculars, $L_z$ = zone length (typically 20–30 m), $DWT$ = Deadweight Tonnage, $MH$ = Man-Hours.

### Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Commercial Shipyards:** All major yards use Zone Construction for bulk carriers, tankers, container ships.
- **Naval Shipbuilding:** Aircraft carriers (e.g., INS Vikrant at Cochin Shipyard) are built zone-by-zone because of crane-lift logistics.
- **Modular Shipbuilding:** The ultimate extension — entire engine rooms or accommodation blocks are built as one zone, lifted in by Straddle Carriers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Optimal Number of Zones for a Hull

**Given:**
- Length Between Perpendiculars: $L_{BP} = 180 \text{ m}$ (a Panamax bulk carrier).
- Maximum permissible zone length for stress-free jointing: $L_{max} = 30 \text{ m}$.
- Yard crane SWL: $W_{crane} = 100 \text{ tonnes}$.
- Safety factor: $k_{safety} = 0.75$.

**Step 1: Compute minimum number of zones required by length.**

$$
N_z^{min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{BP}}{L_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{180}{30} \right\rceil = \lceil 6 \rceil = 6
$$

> *Logic:* Ceiling function ensures the last zone is not larger than 30 m; if division is not exact, the remainder forms an additional (smaller) zone.

**Step 2: Compute the maximum permissible block mass.**

$$
M_{block}^{max} = W_{crane} \times k_{safety} = 100 \times 0.75 = 75 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> *Logic:* The crane is rated at 100 t SWL under static conditions; dynamic lifting, swinging, and sling mass reduce usable capacity to 75% of the static rating.

**Step 3: Compute the number of grand blocks per zone.**

Assume average grand-block mass for a 30 m zone is $m_{GB} = 65 \text{ t}$ (after pre-outfitting adds ~10% weight).

$$
N_{GB}^{zone} = \left\lceil \dfrac{m_{zone,total}}{m_{GB}} \right\rceil
$$

Total steel mass for a typical 180 m bulk carrier = ~10,000 t.

$$
m_{zone,total} = \dfrac{10000}{6} \approx 1667 \text{ t}
$$

$$
N_{GB}^{zone} = \left\lceil \dfrac{1667}{65} \right\rceil = \lceil 25.6 \rceil = 26 \text{ grand blocks/zone}
$$

**Step 4: Total grand blocks for the hull.**

$$
N_{GB}^{total} = N_z^{min} \times N_{GB}^{zone} = 6 \times 26 = 156 \text{ grand blocks}
$$

**Step 5: Total berth welds (critical path indicator).**

Each grand block requires **4 joining welds** (2 transverse + 2 longitudinal flanges), average length **6 m**.

$$
W_{berth} = N_{GB}^{total} \times 4 \times 6 = 156 \times 24 = 3744 \text{ m of weld}
$$

Compare this with the **Pyramid Method**, where on-berth welds often exceed **15,000 m**. The zone method reduces berth welding by:

$$
\eta_{reduction} = \dfrac{15000 - 3744}{15000} \times 100\% \approx 75\%
$$

> *Logic:* This 75% reduction directly translates into **berth occupancy time reduction** (a key KTU exam takeaway).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Zone Construction vs. Traditional Methods

| Parameter | Pyramid Method (Old) | Sandwich Method (Intermediate) | Zone Construction (Modern) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Work Direction | Keel-up, sequential | Keel + side simultaneously | Parallel zones |
| On-berth welding share | ~85% | ~50% | ~25% |
| Pre-outfitting on berth | 100% | 60% | 15% |
| Painting at height | Yes | Partial | Minimal |
| Cycle time (180 m bulker) | 12 months | 8 months | 5–6 months |
| Productivity (t/man-hour) | 0.5 | 1.2 | 2.5–3.0 |

### 3.3 Derivation — Productivity Gain (Numerical Example)

**Given:**
- DWT = 82,000 t (Kamsarmax bulker).
- Pyramid method man-hours: $MH_{trad} = 250{,}000$.
- Zone method man-hours: $MH_{zone} = 110{,}000$.

**Step 1: Productivity — Pyramid Method.**

$$
P_{trad} = \dfrac{DWT}{MH_{trad}} = \dfrac{82000}{250000} = 0.328 \text{ t/man-hour}
$$

**Step 2: Productivity — Zone Method.**

$$
P_{zone} = \dfrac{DWT}{MH_{zone} = 110000} = 0.745 \text{ t/man-hour}
$$

> Note: Modern Korean yards reach 3.0+ t/man-hour using automated panel lines; 0.745 is a conservative manual estimate suitable for KTU problems.

**Step 3: Percentage productivity improvement.**

$$
\Delta P \,(\%) = \dfrac{P_{zone} - P_{trad}}{P_{trad}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.745 - 0.328}{0.328} \times 100\% \approx 127\%
$$

> *Logic:* A 127% improvement means the zone method is **more than twice as productive** as the pyramid method for the same vessel.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Zone Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull GA Drawing] --> B[Engineering Zoning: Forward, Amidships, Aft]
    B --> C1[Forward Zone Workshop]
    B --> C2[Amidships Zone Workshop]
    B --> C3[Aft Zone Workshop]
    C1 --> D1[Panel Fabrication]
    C2 --> D2[Panel Fabrication]
    C3 --> D3[Panel Fabrication]
    D1 --> E1[Sub-Block Assembly]
    D2 --> E2[Sub-Block Assembly]
    D3 --> E3[Sub-Block Assembly]
    E1 --> F1[Pre-Outfitting Piping and Cable]
    E2 --> F2[Pre-Outfitting Piping and Cable]
    E3 --> F3[Pre-Outfitting Piping and Cable]
    F1 --> G1[Pre-Painting and Blasting]
    F2 --> G2[Pre-Painting and Blasting]
    F3 --> G3[Pre-Painting and Blasting]
    G1 --> H1[Grand Block Assembly]
    G2 --> H2[Grand Block Assembly]
    G3 --> H3[Grand Block Assembly]
    H1 --> I[Building Berth Erection]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Zone-to-Zone Welding]
    J --> K[Superstructure Mounting]
    K --> L[Sea Trials and Delivery]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hull Zoning Layout (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHIPHULL[Hull of Vessel Top View]
        Z1[Forward Zone: 0 m to 30 m]
        Z2[Amidships Zone: 30 m to 90 m]
        Z3[Aft Zone: 90 m to 120 m]
        Z4[Engine Room Zone: 120 m to 150 m]
        Z5[Stern Zone: 150 m to 180 m]
    end
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z3 --> Z4
    Z4 --> Z5
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Steel Plates and Sections] --> PANEL[Panel Line: Cutting and Welding]
    PANEL --> ASSEMBLY[Assembly Shop: Stiffener Fitting]
    ASSEMBLY --> SUBBLOCK[Sub-Block Stage]
    SUBBLOCK --> PREFIT[Pre-Outfitting Bay]
    PREFIT --> PREPAINT[Pre-Painting Booth]
    PREPAINT --> GRANDBLOCK[Grand Block Joining Area]
    GRANDBLOCK --> CRANE[Heavy Lift Crane: 600 t SWL]
    CRANE --> BERTH[Building Berth or Dock]
    BERTH --> JOINING[Zone-to-Zone Welding]
    JOINING --> OUTFITTING[Final Outfitting]
    OUTFITTING --> TRIAL[Sea Trials]
    TRIAL --> DELIVERY[Vessel Delivery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each rectangular node is a workstation. The **three parallel branches** at the top of the workflow (Forward / Amidships / Aft) represent the **simultaneous zones** — this is the visual signature of the Zone Construction Method. In the Pyramid Method, all branches would collapse into a single sequential chain.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define the Zone Construction Method for hull blocks and state any two of its advantages over the traditional keel-up method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Zone Construction Method is a shipbuilding technique in which the entire hull is divided longitudinally into independent zones (Forward, Amidships, Aft, etc.), and each zone is further sub-divided into erection blocks. These blocks are fabricated, outfitted, and painted in parallel workshops and then lifted by heavy gantry cranes onto the building berth for final joining.

**Two Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Reduced cycle time:** Parallel fabrication cuts total construction time by 40–60%.
2. **Higher pre-outfitting percentage:** Piping, cabling, and painting are done at ground level, improving quality and reducing work-at-height hazards.

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark per point with proper sentence formation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is meant by a "Grand Block" in zone construction? Why is its use preferred over fitting individual small blocks directly on the berth?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A **Grand Block** (or Mega-Block) is a large structural assembly formed by joining two or more smaller sub-blocks at ground level **before** they are lifted onto the building berth.

**Preference Reason (1.5 marks):**
1. **Reduces on-berth welding:** A 200 t grand block replaces ~10 smaller blocks, eliminating ~30 joining welds at dangerous heights.
2. **Better weld quality:** Ground-level welding is done in flat/down-hand position (gravity-assisted), giving superior penetration compared to overhead welds on the berth.
3. **Faster berth clearance:** Berth time is the most expensive resource; fewer lifts = faster berth turnover.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Zone Construction Method** for building the hull of a merchant vessel. List **four** key zones typically identified. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Zone Construction Method** with the **Pyramid (Keel-Up) Method** and the **Sandwich Method** in terms of: (i) direction of work, (ii) on-berth welding share, (iii) cycle time, and (iv) productivity index. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Zone Construction Method Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** The hull is divided longitudinally into 3–7 zones. Common zones for a merchant vessel are:
   - **Zone I:** Forward (bow region, including forecastle and forepeak tank).
   - **Zone II:** Amidships (cargo hold region for tankers/bulkers, or accommodation for passenger ships).
   - **Zone III:** Aft (engine room, funnel base, steering gear compartment, stern frame region).

2. **Workflow (3 marks):**
   - Plates and sections are received in the **steel stockyard**.
   - **Panel fabrication** on automated lines (cutting, edge preparation, sub-panel welding).
   - **Sub-block assembly** with stiffeners and brackets fitted.
   - **Pre-outfitting** — piping, HVAC ducts, cable trays, and machinery foundations installed on the ground.
   - **Pre-painting** — blasting to ISO 8501-1 Sa 2.5 and application of shop primer.
   - **Grand block assembly** — multiple sub-blocks welded into one mega-block.
   - **Berth erection** — Goliath crane lifts grand blocks in a pre-defined sequence; zones are joined by transverse and longitudinal welds.
   - **Final outfitting, launching, sea trials, delivery.**

3. **Sketch (2 marks):** A neat longitudinal section of the hull showing the four zones labelled, with arrows indicating crane lifts and joining welds at zone boundaries.

**(b) Comparative Table (7 marks — 1.75 marks per parameter):**

| Parameter | Pyramid Method | Sandwich Method | Zone Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction of work | Keel-up, strictly sequential | Keel + side simultaneously | Parallel, all zones simultaneously |
| On-berth welding share | ~85% | ~50% | ~25% |
| Cycle time (180 m bulker) | ~12 months | ~8 months | ~5–6 months |
| Productivity Index (t/man-hour) | 0.3–0.5 | 1.0–1.5 | 2.5–3.0 |

**Conclusion (any 1 mark for summary):**
The Zone Construction Method is superior in all four parameters because it converts a sequential shipbuilding project into a **parallel manufacturing process**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** A ship of **LBP = 150 m** is to be built using the Zone Construction Method. The maximum allowable zone length is **25 m** and the yard crane SWL is **80 tonnes**. Calculate: (i) the minimum number of zones, (ii) the maximum block mass after applying a safety factor of 0.75, and (iii) the total number of grand blocks if the total steel mass is **8,000 tonnes** and the average grand block mass is **60 tonnes**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a flowchart the **block fabrication sequence** in the Zone Construction Method, clearly distinguishing between **shop-work (ground level)** and **berth-work (on the slipway)** activities. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Minimum number of zones (2 marks):**

$$
N_z^{min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{BP}}{L_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{150}{25} \right\rceil = \lceil 6 \rceil = 6 \text{ zones}
$$

**(ii) Maximum block mass (2 marks):**

$$
M_{block}^{max} = W_{crane} \times k_{safety} = 80 \times 0.75 = 60 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(iii) Total grand blocks (3 marks):**

$$
m_{zone,total} = \dfrac{8000}{6} \approx 1333.3 \text{ t}
$$

$$
N_{GB}^{zone} = \left\lceil \dfrac{1333.3}{60} \right\rceil = \lceil 22.2 \rceil = 23 \text{ grand blocks/zone}
$$

$$
N_{GB}^{total} = 6 \times 23 = 138 \text{ grand blocks}
$$

**(b) Flowchart Explanation (7 marks):**

**Shop-Work (Ground Level) — 4 marks:**
1. Steel stockyard → shot blasting and priming of plates.
2. CNC cutting of plates into panels.
3. Panel welding on down-hand positioners.
4. Stiffener and bracket fitting on sub-assemblies.
5. Sub-block assembly.
6. Pre-outfitting (piping, cabling, ventilation, machinery foundations).
7. Pre-painting (full coat at ground level).
8. Grand block joining (multiple sub-blocks tack-welded together).

**Berth-Work (Slipway / Dock) — 3 marks:**
1. Crane lifting of grand blocks (max 60 t per the numerical above).
2. Positioning on pre-laid keel blocks.
3. Zone-to-zone welding (only ~25% of total weld length).
4. Final outfitting of superstructure, masts, funnels.
5. Launching, sea trials, delivery.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in the number of zones — students often write $150/25 = 6$ but miss the case when LBP is not a perfect multiple (e.g., 155 m → 7 zones, not 6.2).
> 2. **Confusing SWL with the maximum block mass** — students frequently use 80 t directly instead of applying the 0.75 safety factor (correct answer: 60 t).
> 3. **Mixing up Pyramid, Sandwich, and Zone Methods** in comparative answers — examiners deduct 1 mark per wrong attribution. Memorise the **on-berth welding percentages (85 / 50 / 25)**.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch in Q.A(a)** — KTU awards 2 marks strictly for the diagram; a verbal description alone fetches only 5/7.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Zone Construction = Parallel hull subdivision** into 3–7 zones (Forward, Amidships, Aft commonly).
- [ ] **Crane SWL × 0.75** = Maximum permissible block mass for dynamic lifting.
- [ ] **Pre-outfitting at ground level** is the single biggest productivity driver.
- [ ] **On-berth welding share** reduces from ~85% (Pyramid) → ~50% (Sandwich) → ~25% (Zone).
- [ ] **Productivity (t/man-hour)** rises from ~0.4 → ~3.0 in modern Korean/Japanese yards.
- [ ] **Grand Block** = multiple sub-blocks pre-joined on the ground to minimise on-berth welds.
- [ ] **ISO 8501-1 Sa 2.5** is the standard surface preparation before shop primer application.
- [ ] **Number of zones** = ceiling of (LBP ÷ max zone length, typically 25–30 m).
- [ ] **Cycle time reduction** = 40–60% compared to traditional keel-up method.
- [ ] **Always include a sketch** in 7-mark descriptive answers; KTU awards 1.5–2 marks for a labelled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outfitting and Painting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Outfitting and Painting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — OUTFITTING AND PAINTING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Outfitting — Formal Definition

**Outfitting** is the post-hull-construction phase of shipbuilding comprising the installation of all machinery, equipment, fittings, systems, and components necessary to make a bare steel hull (or aluminum hull) into a fully operational, seaworthy, habitable, and certified vessel. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, outfitting is broadly classified into **hull outfitting**, **machinery outfitting**, **electrical outfitting**, and **living/operational outfitting**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Outfitting* refers to the totality of work carried out on a ship after the major structural fabrication and erection phases are complete, encompassing the fitting of deck machinery, auxiliary systems, piping, cables, accommodation, safety gear, navigation equipment, and the application of protective coatings, undertaken both prior to and following the launch of the vessel.

### 1.2 Painting — Formal Definition

**Painting** (more precisely, marine coating application) is the systematic application of protective and decorative coating systems onto prepared steel surfaces of a ship. The purpose is threefold: **corrosion protection**, **anti-fouling** (prevention of marine growth on the underwater hull), and **aesthetic/identification finishing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Painting is Considered Part of Outfitting in KTU Syllabus:**
> In the KTU 2024 Module 1 framework, painting and coating are intentionally grouped with outfitting because both activities are predominantly performed on the berth/quayside after the hull is structurally complete. They share identical workflow dependencies (surface preparation, staging, environmental control, drying time) and are scheduled together in the shipyard production plan.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **building a house**:
- The brick walls, roof, and concrete frame are the **hull structure** (steel fabrication + block erection).
- After the structure is up, you bring in plumbers, electricians, carpenters, tilers, painters, AC technicians, and furniture installers — **all of this is outfitting**.
- Painting the house protects the walls from rain, gives a smooth finish, and adds beauty — exactly the role of **marine painting** on a ship.

| Ship Building Phase | House Construction Analogy | % of Total Work |
|---|---|---|
| Steel fabrication | Brickwork + RCC | ~25% |
| Block erection | Roof + walls standing | ~15% |
| **Outfitting** | Plumbing, electrics, carpentry, painting | **~50–60%** |
| Trials & commissioning | Interior decoration + handover | ~5–10% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric (Bold):** Outfitting typically accounts for **50% to 60%** of the total man-hours expended in building a merchant ship, making it the single largest cost and time driver in modern shipbuilding.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Outfitting Work

Outfitting is logically partitioned into functional sub-systems. Understanding these categories is mandatory for KTU 2024 Module 1 questions.

#### A. Hull Outfitting (Structural Fittings)
These are fittings welded or bolted to the hull structure itself.
- **Hatches** (cargo hatch covers, coamings, cleats)
- **Bulwarks and freeing ports**
- **Mooring fittings** — bollards, bitts, fairleads, Panama chocks, roller chocks
- **Towing fittings** — towing brackets, towing pad eyes
- **Fenders** (for tugs, harbor vessels)
- **Side and bow doors** (for ferries, Ro-Ro vessels)
- **Rudder and stern frame** accessories
- **Masts, king posts, derrick posts, cargo cranes**

#### B. Deck Machinery Outfitting
Mechanical equipment mounted on deck for cargo, anchoring, and mooring operations.
- **Anchor windlass** (forward)
- **Mooring winches** (forward and aft)
- **Cargo winches, derricks, cranes** (for general cargo ships)
- **Hatch cover operating gear** (hydraulic cylinders, wire rope systems)
- **Capstans**

#### C. Machinery Outfitting
- **Main propulsion engine** and reduction gearbox
- **Auxiliary engines (generators)**
- **Boilers, pumps, compressors, purifiers, heat exchangers**
- **Shafting, propellers, stern tubes**
- **Steering gear**

#### D. Piping & Ventilation Outfitting
- **Sea-water cooling, ballast, fire-fighting, bilge, sanitary, fuel-oil, lube-oil, compressed air, steam** systems
- **Ducting** for supply/exhaust ventilation
- **Air-conditioning plants**

#### E. Electrical & Electronic Outfitting
- **Main switchboard, emergency switchboard, distribution boards**
- **Generators, transformers, batteries, cables, lighting**
- **Navigation equipment** — gyro compass, magnetic compass, radar, ARPA, ECDIS, GPS, echo sounder, speed log, AIS
- **Communication equipment** — GMDSS station, VHF, MF/HF, INMARSAT
- **Automation and alarm systems**

#### F. Accommodation / Joiner Outfitting
- **Cabins, mess rooms, galley, pantries, laundry, hospital**
- **Toilets, bathrooms, public spaces**
- **Insulation, panelling, false ceilings, floor coverings**
- **Furniture, fittings, joinery work**

#### G. Safety & Life-Saving Outfitting
- **Lifeboats, life rafts, life buoys, life jackets, immersion suits**
- **Fire-fighting equipment** — extinguishers, fire hoses, hydrants, CO₂ systems, sprinkler systems
- **Emergency escape breathing devices (EEBDs)**
- **Pyrotechnics, line-throwing apparatus**

### 2.2 Stages of Outfitting (Time-Based Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-yield KTU topic** — frequently asked in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| Stage | When Performed | Typical Work | Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-outfitting (Pre-launch outfitting)** | On the building berth, before launch | Ducts, cable trays, pipe hangers, foundations, double-bottom piping, shaft tunnel fittings, masts, deck fittings | Maximises parallel work; reduces total build time |
| **Post-launch outfitting** | After launch, in the fitting-out berth/quay | All heavy/oversized items, funnels, lifeboats, deck machinery, electronic equipment, accommodation | Safer working conditions; level quay |
| **Quayside outfitting** | Alongside fitting-out quay | Complete outfitting, commissioning, testing | Most of the work happens here |
| **Dockside outfitting** | In dry dock (before delivery) | Underwater hull painting, anodes, sea valves, propeller polishing, boot-top painting | Access to underwater parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern KTU Concept: "Outfitting-First" or "Block Outfitting" Strategy**
> In contemporary shipbuilding (which KTU 2024 explicitly emphasizes), outfitting work is increasingly done **at the block fabrication stage itself** — i.e., panels and 3D blocks are outfitted in the shop before being erected. This shifts outfitting from a sequential post-hull activity to a **parallel pre-erection activity**, drastically reducing the critical path.

### 2.3 Painting — Functional Objectives

Marine coatings serve four distinct engineering functions:

1. **Barrier Protection:** Isolates steel from water, oxygen, and salts (the corrosion triad).
2. **Cathodic / Sacrificial Protection:** Anti-corrosive pigments (zinc, aluminium) act as sacrificial anodes.
3. **Anti-fouling:** Prevents barnacles, algae, and molluscs from colonising the underwater hull (which would increase drag, fuel consumption by up to **30%**).
4. **Aesthetic & Identification:** Company colours, ship name, port of registry, IMO markings, boot-top, draft marks.

### 2.4 Paint System Architecture (Layer-by-Layer)

A standard marine paint system has **4 to 5 layers**, applied in strict order:

| Layer | Function | Typical DFT (Dry Film Thickness) | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Surface Preparation** | Expose clean steel | N/A (removes rust, mill scale) | Sa 2½ blast (ISO 8501-1) |
| **2. Primer (Shop Primer / Blast Primer)** | Temporary + initial adhesion | 15–25 µm | Zinc silicate, epoxy primer |
| **3. Anti-corrosive (AC) coats** | Long-term corrosion barrier | 100–200 µm per coat × 2–3 coats | Epoxy, polyurethane |
| **4. Tie coat / Intermediate** | Bonding between AC and top coat | 50–100 µm | Epoxy tie coat |
| **5. Top coat (Finish)** | UV resistance, colour, gloss | 50–75 µm | Polyurethane, acrylic |
| **6. Anti-fouling (AF)** *(underwater only)* | Prevent marine growth | 100–300 µm | Self-polishing copolymer (SPC), TBT-free |

### 2.5 Ship Areas & Their Paint Systems

Different zones of the ship require different coating strategies due to varying exposure.

| Zone | Location | Exposure | Paint System | DFT (µm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Underwater hull** | Below waterline | Seawater + fouling | Primer + Epoxy AC + Anti-fouling | 300–500 |
| **Boot top** | ±0.5 m around load line | Alternate wet/dry | Tough epoxy + AF | 250–400 |
| **Topside** | Above waterline, hull sides | Atmosphere + splash | Epoxy AC + Polyurethane top | 200–300 |
| **Deck** | Exposed decks | Foot traffic + cargo | Epoxy + non-skid polyurethane | 250–350 |
| **Superstructure** | Above deck house | UV, weather | Primer + PU top coat | 150–200 |
| **Internal tanks** (ballast, cargo) | Internal | Cargo + seawater | Pure epoxy (chemical-resistant) | 300–600 |
| **Accommodation interior** | Internal | Cosmetic | Emulsion / alkyd | 50–100 |

### 2.6 Surface Preparation Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 mandates familiarity with **ISO 8501-1** visual assessment of surface cleanliness.

| Standard | Description | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Sa 1** | Light blast | Low-grade structures |
| **Sa 2** | Thorough blast | General industrial |
| **Sa 2½** | Very thorough blast | **Standard for marine AC** |
| **Sa 3** | White metal blast | Highest-grade tanks, chemical carriers |

Other prep methods: **power tool cleaning (St 2, St 3)**, **water jetting (WJ)**, **acid pickling**, **mechanical grinding**.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam-essential for paint quantity calculations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theoretical Coverage (Spread Rate)** | $C = \dfrac{10 \times V_s}{d}$ | m² / litre | $V_s$ = volume solids (%), $d$ = DFT in µm |
| 2 | **Wet Film Thickness** | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT \times 100}{V_s}$ | µm | Inverse of coverage derivation |
| 3 | **Paint Quantity Required** | $Q = \dfrac{A \times d}{10 \times V_s}$ | litres | $A$ = area in m² |
| 4 | **Actual Quantity (with wastage)** | $Q_{actual} = Q \times (1 + W)$ | litres | $W$ = wastage factor (typically 0.20 to 0.40) |
| 5 | **Volume Solids** | $V_s = \dfrac{DFT \times 100}{WFT}$ | % | Measured experimentally |
| 6 | **DFT from multiple coats** | $DFT_{total} = \sum d_i$ | µm | Sum of individual DFTs |
| 7 | **Spreading rate at given WFT** | $C = \dfrac{1000}{WFT}$ | m² / litre | Pure volumetric relation |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipe `|` symbols are used in the table above** to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel economy:** A clean, smooth hull (well-maintained coating) saves **10–15% fuel** per voyage.
- **Dry-dock interval:** Quality coating extends the dry-docking cycle from 2.5 to **5 years**.
- **Steel preservation:** Correct coating systems can extend ship life beyond **25–30 years**.
- **IMO compliance:** PSPC (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings) under SOLAS regulates ballast tank coatings since 2008.
- **Environmental regulations:** Ban on TBT (tributyltin) anti-fouling has driven **TBT-free SPC** and **fouling-release silicone coatings**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Paint Coverage (Spread Rate)

**Problem:** Derive the formula $C = \dfrac{10 \times V_s}{d}$ relating paint coverage to volume solids and dry film thickness.

**Given:**
- 1 litre of paint = $1000 \text{ cm}^3$
- Volume solids percentage = $V_s$ %
- Required Dry Film Thickness = $d$ µm

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Express 1 litre in cubic centimetres:**
$$
1 \text{ L} = 1000 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the volume of non-volatile (solid) content in 1 litre of paint:**
The volume solids represent the fraction of paint that remains as film after the solvent evaporates.
$$
V_{solids} = 1000 \times \dfrac{V_s}{100} = 10 V_s \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Express DFT in consistent units (cm):**
$$
1 \text{ µm} = 10^{-4} \text{ cm}
$$
$$
d \text{ µm} = d \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate volume, area, and thickness (fundamental geometry):**
For a uniform film, $\text{Volume} = \text{Area} \times \text{Thickness}$.
$$
\text{Area covered} = \dfrac{V_{solids}}{d_{cm}} = \dfrac{10 V_s}{d \times 10^{-4}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**
$$
\text{Area covered} = \dfrac{10 V_s \times 10^{4}}{d} = \dfrac{10^{5} \times V_s}{d} \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to square metres** ($1 \text{ m}^2 = 10^4 \text{ cm}^2$):
$$
C = \dfrac{10^{5} \times V_s / d}{10^{4}} = \dfrac{10 \times V_s}{d} \text{ m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Final result:**
$$
\boxed{C = \dfrac{10 \times V_s}{d}}
$$

This is the **theoretical coverage** at 100% transfer efficiency. In practice, application losses (overspray, spillage, brush retention) reduce this to **60–80%** of theoretical.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** A ship's topside area to be painted is **2400 m²**. The paint has a volume solids of **60%** and a required DFT of **125 µm**. Assuming a wastage factor of **30%**, calculate the total paint quantity in litres required for **two full coats**.

**Step 1 — Theoretical coverage per coat:**
$$
C = \dfrac{10 \times V_s}{d} = \dfrac{10 \times 60}{125}
$$
$$
C = \dfrac{600}{125} = 4.8 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical paint per coat (no wastage):**
$$
Q_{coat} = \dfrac{A}{C} = \dfrac{2400}{4.8}
$$
$$
Q_{coat} = 500 \text{ L per coat}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical paint for 2 coats:**
$$
Q_{2coats} = 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply wastage factor (30%):**
$$
Q_{actual} = Q_{2coats} \times (1 + W) = 1000 \times 1.30
$$
$$
\boxed{Q_{actual} = 1300 \text{ L}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification using the direct formula:**
$$
Q_{actual} = \dfrac{A \times d \times N \times (1 + W)}{10 \times V_s} = \dfrac{2400 \times 125 \times 2 \times 1.30}{10 \times 60}
$$
$$
Q_{actual} = \dfrac{780{,}000}{600} = 1300 \text{ L} \checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Outfitting Decision Table

| Decision Parameter | Pre-launch Option | Post-launch Option | KTU-Recommended Modern Practice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heavy machinery | Difficult (no crane) | Easy (quay crane) | Pre-install on blocks |
| Piping | Easy (open access) | Restricted (overhead cable trays) | Pre-outfit blocks |
| Cable trays | Easy | Difficult (overhead) | Pre-outfit blocks |
| Surface painting | Touch-up only | Full painting | Paint blocks before erection |
| Masts/Funnels | Pre-erect on berth | Erect post-launch | Pre-erect if stability allows |
| Accommodation joinery | Shop-built modules | Site-built | **Pre-fabricated cabins** |
| Cost per hour | High (scaffold needed) | Medium | **Lowest in block outfitting** |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coating Selection by Exposure Zone

| Zone | Primary Hazard | Required Coating Property | Typical Resin |
|---|---|---|---|
| Underwater hull | Biofouling, corrosion | Anti-fouling + barrier | SPC + epoxy |
| Boot top | Wet-dry cycling | Flexibility + AF | Epoxy + AF |
| Topside | UV, salt spray | UV resistance | Polyurethane |
| Ballast tank | Seawater, alternation | Epoxy, IMO PSPC | Pure epoxy (≥320 µm) |
| Cargo oil tank | Crude oil, H₂S | Chemical resistance | Epoxy-phenolic |
| Engine room | Oil, heat | Heat + oil resistance | Alkyd, silicone |
| Galley | Hygiene | Washable, food-safe | Epoxy, PU |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Outfitting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steel Plate Cutting] --> B[Block Fabrication]
    B --> C[Pre-Outfitting at Block Stage]
    C --> D[Erection on Building Berth]
    D --> E[Pre-Launch Outfitting]
    E --> F[Launch of Hull]
    F --> G[Post-Launch Quayside Outfitting]
    G --> H[Painting and Coating Application]
    H --> I[Dockside Outfitting in Dry Dock]
    I --> J[Sea Trials]
    J --> K[Commissioning and Delivery]

    C --> C1[Pipe Spools]
    C --> C2[Cable Trays]
    C --> C3[Deck Foundations]
    C --> C4[Blast and Shop Primer]

    G --> G1[Deck Machinery]
    G --> G2[Funnel and Masts]
    G --> G3[Accommodation Units]
    G --> G4[Electrical Equipment]

    H --> H1[Topside Painting]
    H --> H2[Deck Painting]
    H --> H3[Superstructure Finish]

    I --> I1[Underwater Hull AF]
    I --> I2[Boot Top Coating]
    I --> I3[Sacrificial Anodes]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Paint System Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Prepared Steel Substrate Sa 2.5] --> L2[Shop Primer 15 to 25 microns]
    L2 --> L3[Anti-Corrosive Epoxy Coat 1 100 to 150 microns]
    L3 --> L4[Anti-Corrosive Epoxy Coat 2 100 to 150 microns]
    L4 --> L5[Tie Coat 50 to 75 microns]
    L5 --> L6[Polyurethane Top Coat 50 to 75 microns]
    L4 -.Underwater Only.-> L7[Anti-Fouling SPC 100 to 300 microns]
    L7 --> L8[Seawater Exposure]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Ship Zones and Their Paint Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Underwater Hull] --> P1[Epoxy Primer plus Anti Fouling]
    Z2[Boot Top] --> P2[Epoxy plus Antifouling]
    Z3[Topside] --> P3[Epoxy plus Polyurethane]
    Z4[Deck] --> P4[Epoxy plus Non Skid PU]
    Z5[Superstructure] --> P5[Primer plus Polyurethane]
    Z6[Ballast Tank] --> P6[Pure Epoxy PSPC]
    Z7[Cargo Hold] --> P7[Epoxy Phenolic]
    Z8[Engine Room] --> P8[Alkyd plus Silicone]
    Z9[Accommodation] --> P9[Emulsion plus Alkyd]
```

### 4.4 Outfitting Sequence Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Receive Outfitting Drawing] --> S2[Material Take Off and Procurement]
    S2 --> S3[Pre Fabrication Shop Work]
    S3 --> S4[Block Stage Outfitting]
    S4 --> S5[Berth Stage Outfitting]
    S5 --> S6[Quay Stage Outfitting]
    S6 --> S7[Dock Stage Outfitting]
    S7 --> S8[Pre Delivery Inspection]
    S8 --> S9[Sea Trials and Handover]
    S9 --> S10[Class Survey and Delivery]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define outfitting in ship construction. List any four categories of outfitting work.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Outfitting is the post-structural phase of shipbuilding in which all auxiliary systems, machinery, equipment, fittings, and protective coatings are installed on the hull to convert it into a fully operational, seaworthy, and class-certified vessel.

**Four Categories (1 mark, 0.25 each):**
1. **Hull outfitting** — hatches, bollards, bulwarks
2. **Machinery outfitting** — main engine, auxiliaries
3. **Electrical/Electronic outfitting** — generators, navigation, communication
4. **Accommodation outfitting** — cabins, galley, mess rooms

> [!NOTE]
> Any 4 of the 7 categories listed in Section 2.1 will fetch full marks.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is the purpose of anti-fouling paint? Name two commonly used anti-fouling systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Purpose (1.5 marks):** Anti-fouling paint is applied to the underwater hull to **prevent the attachment and growth of marine organisms** (barnacles, algae, mussels, tube worms). Fouling increases hull roughness, drag, fuel consumption (up to **30%**), and reduces vessel speed.

**Two systems (1.5 marks, 0.75 each):**
1. **Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC) anti-fouling** — biocide (copper/zinc pyrithione) is released at a controlled rate as the resin slowly hydrolyses.
2. **Fouling-release silicone coatings** — non-biocidal, ultra-smooth surface that organisms cannot grip firmly (eco-friendly).

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning the **TBT ban (2008, IMO AFS Convention)** will earn a bonus half-mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Full internal choice must be offered between Question A and Question B.

---

#### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **stages of outfitting** in modern shipbuilding with their relative advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **marine paint system** layer-by-layer for the **underwater hull** of a merchant ship. Include functions and typical DFTs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stages of Outfitting (7 marks):**

Outfitting in modern shipbuilding is conducted in **four distinct stages**, scheduled to overlap with structural work for maximum efficiency:

1. **Pre-outfitting (Block Outfitting) — 2 marks**
   Performed at panel/3D block stage inside the fabrication shop.
   - Pipe spools, ducting, cable trays, equipment foundations, blast and shop primer are fitted.
   - **Advantage:** Work done at ground level with jigs; superior quality; up to 70% of outfitting hours can be absorbed here.
   - Reduces the critical path dramatically.

2. **Berth (Pre-launch) Outfitting — 1.5 marks**
   Performed on the building berth/slipway before launch.
   - Installation of: double-bottom pipework, shaft tunnel fittings, sea chests, mast/funnel bases, deck plating close-outs.
   - Limited by working at height; weather-dependent.

3. **Quayside (Post-launch) Outfitting — 2 marks**
   Performed at the fitting-out quay, with the vessel afloat.
   - Heaviest items installed: deck machinery, lifeboats, accommodation blocks, electronic equipment, funnels, superstructure modules.
   - Most productive stage; work runs in parallel with commissioning.

4. **Dockside Outfitting (in Dry Dock) — 1.5 marks**
   Final underwater work before delivery.
   - Underwater hull anti-fouling, boot-top, propeller polishing, sacrificial anodes, sea-valve grinds.
   - Followed by dock flooding, sea trials, and delivery.

**(b) Underwater Hull Paint System (7 marks):**

The underwater hull is the most aggressively attacked zone — constant seawater, biofouling, cathodic reaction. The system has **5 distinct layers**:

| Layer | Description | DFT (µm) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Surface preparation to Sa 2½** | N/A | Removes rust, mill scale, salts |
| 2 | **Shop primer (zinc silicate)** | 20 | Initial adhesion, temporary protection |
| 3 | **Anti-corrosive epoxy coat 1** | 150 | First barrier against water/O₂ |
| 4 | **Anti-corrosive epoxy coat 2** | 150 | Total barrier; 300 µm epoxy stack |
| 5 | **Anti-fouling (SPC)** | 200 | Releases biocide, prevents biofouling |

**Total DFT ≈ 520 µm.** Each layer must be applied within the **re-coat window** specified by the manufacturer (typically 24–72 hours at 23°C).

**Key specification:** The system must comply with **IMO PSPC** for new vessels and class society rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV).

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **outfitting work performed at the block fabrication stage** in modern shipyards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier's topside area is **3200 m²**. The epoxy anti-corrosive paint has a volume solids of **55%** and a specified DFT of **125 µm** per coat. Calculate the **total paint quantity in litres required for two coats, including a wastage factor of 25%**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Outfitting (7 marks):**

A typical 3D block in modern shipbuilding (e.g., a 20×20×8 m cargo-hold block) is fitted out **before** being erected onto the ship. The block fabrication shop contains:

1. **Pipe and ventilation spool fabrication** — pipes cut, welded, tested, then bolted onto block brackets.
2. **Equipment foundation installation** — engine seats, winch beds, generator foundations welded to the block.
3. **Cable tray and penetration sleeves** — pre-welded to the block structure.
4. **Blasting and shop primer application** — automated shot-blast line, then robotic spray booth applies 20 µm zinc primer.
5. **Duct supports, outfit hangers, and brackets** — welded to internals.
6. **Pre-fabricated accommodation units** — toilet modules, galley units, cabin pods fitted in the shop.
7. **Inspection and NDT** — hydrostatic tests, NDT of welds, dimensional survey.

**Advantages:** Work at ground level, controlled environment, parallel with steelwork, reduced total build time by 30–40%.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Area $A = 3200 \text{ m}^2$
- Volume solids $V_s = 55$ %
- DFT per coat $d = 125 \text{ µm}$
- Number of coats $N = 2$
- Wastage $W = 25$ % = 0.25

**Step 1 — Theoretical coverage per coat:**
$$
C = \dfrac{10 \times V_s}{d} = \dfrac{10 \times 55}{125} = \dfrac{550}{125}
$$
$$
C = 4.4 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Paint per coat (theoretical):**
$$
Q_{1} = \dfrac{A}{C} = \dfrac{3200}{4.4} = 727.27 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3 — Paint for two coats (theoretical):**
$$
Q_{2} = 2 \times 727.27 = 1454.55 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Including 25% wastage:**
$$
Q_{actual} = 1454.55 \times (1 + 0.25) = 1454.55 \times 1.25
$$
$$
\boxed{Q_{actual} = 1818.18 \text{ L} \approx 1819 \text{ L}}
$$

**Verification by direct formula:**
$$
Q_{actual} = \dfrac{A \times d \times N \times (1+W)}{10 \times V_s} = \dfrac{3200 \times 125 \times 2 \times 1.25}{10 \times 55}
$$
$$
Q_{actual} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{550} = 1818.18 \text{ L} \checkmark
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Outfitting & Painting:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "fitting out" with "outfitting":** Fitting out is the *post-launch* phase only; outfitting is the *entirety* of post-fabrication work including pre-launch. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this distinction.
>
> 2. **Missing the units in coverage calculation:** Students often write $C = \dfrac{V_s}{d}$ instead of $\dfrac{10 V_s}{d}$ and lose the unit conversion step (**1 mark** lost).
>
> 3. **Forgetting the wastage factor:** Numerical answers without $(1 + W)$ are marked incomplete (**1–2 marks lost**).
>
