> 4. **Naming only "paint" instead of a "paint system":** Painting is a *layered system*, not a single coat. Always mention primer → AC → tie coat → top coat (or AF for underwater).
>
> 5. **Ignoring ISO 8501-1:** The examiner expects mention of **Sa 2½** for marine grade surface preparation, not just "clean steel".
>
> 6. **Saying "outfitting is 30% of work":** Wrong. Correct range is **50–60%** of total man-hours.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Outfitting = 50–60%** of total shipbuilding man-hours.
- [ ] Outfitting is classified into **7 categories**: Hull, Machinery, Piping, Electrical, Accommodation, Safety, Painting.
- [ ] Four **stages**: Pre-outfitting (block) → Berth (pre-launch) → Quayside (post-launch) → Dockside (in dry dock).
- [ ] **Block outfitting** = modern, fastest, parallel-work strategy.
- [ ] Painting functions: **Barrier, Cathodic, Anti-fouling, Aesthetic**.
- [ ] Standard paint system: **Primer → AC × 2 → Tie coat → Top coat** (or **AF** for underwater).
- [ ] Surface preparation standard: **Sa 2½** (ISO 8501-1) is the marine benchmark.
- [ ] Coverage formula: $C = \dfrac{10 V_s}{d}$ (m²/L).
- [ ] Paint quantity: $Q = \dfrac{A \times d \times N \times (1 + W)}{10 \times V_s}$.
- [ ] **DFT** (dry film thickness) is what the specification calls out; **WFT** is what the applicator measures wet.
- [ ] TBT anti-fouling is **banned globally** since 2008 (IMO AFS Convention).
- [ ] **IMO PSPC** (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings) governs ballast tank coatings.
- [ ] Dry-docking interval: **2.5 years (poor coating) vs 5 years (good coating)**.
- [ ] A well-coated hull saves **10–15%** fuel; a fouled hull can lose **30%** speed.
- [ ] **Anti-fouling** is applied only on the **underwater hull + boot top**.
- [ ] **Polyurethane** top coat is used for UV resistance on topside/superstructure.
- [ ] Always cite **volume solids (%)** in paint calculations — never assume 100%.
- [ ] Volume of paint solids per litre = $10 V_s$ cm³ (1-litre can contains $10 V_s$ cm³ of dry film).
- [ ] Total DFT of a system = **sum of individual coat DFTs** (e.g., 2 × 150 µm epoxy = 300 µm).
- [ ] Painting is **always** a part of outfitting in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage and Preparation of Material - Straightening of Plates and Rolled Sections
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Storage and Preparation of Material - Straightening of Plates and Rolled Sections

# Storage and Preparation of Material: Straightening of Plates and Rolled Sections

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

In shipbuilding, **steel plates and rolled sections** arrive at the shipyard in various forms from the rolling mills and steel plants. Due to the rolling process, handling, transportation, and stacking, these materials often develop **distortions, residual stresses, and surface imperfections** before they reach the fabrication stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage** refers to the systematic preservation of steel stock (plates, sections, bars) in the stockyard under controlled conditions to prevent **corrosion, mechanical damage, and dimensional inaccuracies** prior to fabrication.
>
> **Preparation of Material** is the sequence of operations performed before any cutting, forming, or assembly begins. It includes **identification, cleaning, surface preparation, marking, nesting, and straightening**.

**Straightening** is the process of **removing undesirable deformations** (such as buckles, camber, sweep, twist, and edge waves) from plates and rolled sections so that they conform to the required flatness, straightness, and geometric tolerances demanded by the shipbuilding specification and classification society rules.

### 1.2 Types of Plate Distortions (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Distortion Type | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Buckle** | Out-of-plane local or general waviness of the plate surface |
| **Edge Wave** | Waviness concentrated along the longitudinal edges |
| **Crown / Sag** | Overall curvature across the plate surface |
| **Twist** | Rotational distortion where corners are not coplanar |
| **Sweep** | Bowing along the longitudinal axis of a section |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you receive a large cardboard sheet delivered from a warehouse. During transit, the cardboard develops slight curls and waves. Before you can cut it precisely for a school project, you must **flatten it** by pressing, rolling, or warming it. **Steel plates in shipyards behave the same way**, but they are far heavier and require industrial-scale machinery to flatten. Plates are "born distorted" at the rolling mill and must be "disciplined" before they enter the ship.

### 1.4 Storage of Material — Key Engineering Points

- Plates are stored **flat on level ground**, supported by **timber or concrete skids**.
- **Grades of steel (Grade A, B, D, AH, DH, EH)** must be stored **separately** to prevent mix-ups.
- **Identification** is done via **color coding, stamping, and stencilling** at the mill.
- Storage area must be **drained, elevated, and sheltered** to prevent rust.
- Rolled sections (angles, channels, bulb plates, T-bars, I-beams) are stored on **racks or timber supports** to prevent sagging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical storage tolerance:** Plates should not exceed a stack height of approximately **600 mm to 800 mm** to avoid permanent distortion under self-weight.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Straightening is Necessary

When steel is hot-rolled at the mill, it cools unevenly — the surface cools faster than the core. This creates **residual thermal stresses** locked within the plate. Combined with mechanical handling (crane lifting, stacking), the plate develops:

- **Buckles** (local out-of-plane displacements)
- **Crown** (transverse curvature)
- **Sweep** (longitudinal curvature of sections)

If these distortions are not removed, the resulting welded assemblies will:
1. Be **out of tolerance** (failing classification society inspection).
2. Induce **additional residual welding stresses**, leading to **distortion during fabrication**.
3. Cause **mismatch** at plate-to-plate and plate-to-section joints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Classification societies such as **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping), Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, and Bureau Veritas** specify flatness tolerances. A typical tolerance is **1 mm per 1000 mm length**, measured with a straightedge.

## 2.2 Methods of Straightening — Plate Classification

### A. Cold Straightening Methods (Temperature ≤ 100 °C)

Performed at ambient temperature using mechanical force. Suitable for **thin to medium plates (up to ~25 mm)** with **minor distortions**.

#### (i) Press Straightening (Stamping)
- Plate is placed on a **flat anvil bed**.
- A **hydraulic press** applies a localized point load at the **apex of the buckle**.
- The press "stamps" the high points downward, flattening the plate.
- Most common in shipyards for **general plate straightening**.

#### (ii) Roller Straightening (Levelling Rolls)
- Plate passes through a series of **staggered rollers** (typically 5, 7, 9, or 11 rolls).
- Rollers bend the plate in **opposing directions repeatedly**.
- The yield point is exceeded in a cyclic fashion, causing the plate to "remember" the flat shape.
- **Multi-roll roller levelers** are continuous-feed machines suitable for high-volume yards.

#### (iii) Hammer Straightening
- Used for **small localized buckles**.
- Operator strikes the high spot with a **sledgehammer or wooden mallet** on a flat anvil.
- Highly skill-dependent; largely replaced by mechanical methods in modern yards.

#### (iv) Streightening by Tension (Stretching)
- Plate is gripped at edges and pulled in tension.
- Suitable for **removing sweep and overall camber**.
- Uses **hydraulic stretching jacks** mounted on the bed.

### B. Hot Straightening Methods (Line Heating / Flame Straightening)

Performed by **localized heating with an oxy-fuel torch** followed by **controlled cooling (water or air)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Line Heating:** When a narrow strip of plate is heated to **600 °C – 750 °C** (below the transformation temperature), it expands. The surrounding cooler material restrains this expansion, creating **compressive plastic strain**. On cooling, the heated zone **contracts more than the surrounding material**, producing a net **shortening** of that strip. This shortening pulls the plate flat — analogous to how a hot-water bath shrinks a wrinkled shirt.

#### Types of Line Heating Patterns:

| Pattern | Use |
|---|---|
| **Spot Heating** | Small isolated buckles |
| **Line Heating (Transverse)** | Longitudinal sweep / edge waves |
| **Triangular Heating** | Localized depressions |
| **V-Heating** | Removal of sharp distortions at joints |

### 2.3 Straightening of Rolled Sections

Rolled sections include **angles, channels, T-bars, I-beams, bulb plates, and flat bars**. Common distortions and remedies:

| Section Type | Typical Distortion | Straightening Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Angles** | Twisting, bow | Roller straightener, press straightener |
| **Channels** | Web buckles, flange sweep | Press straightener on flange edges |
| **I-Beams / H-Beams** | Web buckle, flange tilt | **Web stiffener press**, hydraulic gag press |
| **Bulb Plates** | Edge wave along bulb | Roller leveller, line heating |
| **T-Bars** | Twist | Press straightener |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **longitudinal stiffeners** (commonly used as ship frames), the **7-roll or 9-roll angle straightening machine** is used. The angle is fed through rollers that progressively correct twist and bow.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flatness tolerance (general) | $\Delta \le \dfrac{L}{1000}$ | mm | Allowable deviation over length L |
| Line heating temperature | $T_h = 600 \text{ to } 750$ | °C | Working temperature range |
| Plate thermal expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | Length change due to heat |
| Coefficient of linear expansion (steel) | $\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6}$ | per °C | Material constant |
| Roller leveler pitch (typical) | $p = 150 \text{ to } 300$ | mm | Distance between rolls |
| Press capacity (light plate) | $F = 100 \text{ to } 500$ | tonnes | Hydraulic straightening press |
| Sag stress in stacked plates | $\sigma_{sag} = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot t^2}$ | N/mm² | Self-weight bending stress |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **steel coefficient of thermal expansion** $\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6} /°\text{C}$ is a **board-exam favourite** value. Memorize it.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Straightening is critical in:
- **Hull plate fabrication** (shell plating, deck plating)
- **Sub-assembly welding stations** for modular shipbuilding
- **Offshore platform jacket legs** where dimensional accuracy is paramount
- **Naval warship construction** where class-approved tolerances are extremely strict

> [!NOTE]
> Modern shipyards use **robotic line-heating systems** where **3D plate scanners** identify distortion zones and **CNC-controlled torches** automatically execute the heating pattern. This represents the marriage of traditional metallurgy and Industry 4.0 automation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flatness Tolerance as a Limiting Criterion

### Statement
The classification society permissible flatness deviation $\Delta$ over a gauge length $L$ is given by:

$$
\Delta_{max} = \frac{L}{1000}
$$

### Derivation Logic
This is **not a derived physical formula** but a **standardized tolerance rule** adopted by major classification societies (IRS Pt.3, Lloyd's). However, it can be **justified from first principles** by considering the maximum allowable angular deviation between the actual surface and the reference plane.

Let $\theta$ be the maximum angular deviation. For small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ in radians.

$$
\theta = \frac{\Delta}{L}
$$

For the rule of "1 mm per 1000 mm":

$$
\theta = \frac{1}{1000} \text{ rad} = 0.001 \text{ rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\theta = 0.001 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.0573°
$$

This corresponds to a **very mild angular deviation** of approximately **3.4 arc-minutes**, ensuring structural continuity and weld fit-up.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Contraction Strain in Line Heating

### Statement
The thermal strain produced on cooling in a line-heated plate strip is given by:

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot (T_h - T_0)
$$

### Derivation Logic

Consider a strip of plate of original length $L_0$ at ambient temperature $T_0$.

**Step 1:** When heated to temperature $T_h$, the strip expands freely to length $L_h$:

$$
L_h = L_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T_h - T_0) \right]
$$

**Step 2:** The surrounding plate **restrains** this expansion (since the surrounding plate is cooler). The heated strip is forced to remain at length $L_0$ while hot, generating a **compressive stress**.

**Step 3:** On cooling back to $T_0$, the strip wishes to contract by the amount $\Delta L$:

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot (T_h - T_0)
$$

**Step 4:** Since this contraction is restrained by the surrounding plate, the strip ends up **shorter than its original length** by $\Delta L$. The plastic compressive strain locked in during heating becomes a **residual tensile contraction strain** after cooling:

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \alpha \cdot (T_h - T_0)
$$

### Numerical Example
For steel plate with $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°\text{C}$ and $T_h - T_0 = 650 °\text{C}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 650 = 7.8 \times 10^{-3}
$$

This is a strain of **0.78 %**, which is sufficient to flatten a buckle of considerable amplitude.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Self-Weight Sag Stress in Plate Storage

### Statement
The bending stress induced by self-weight in a plate stacked on two supports is given by:

$$
\sigma_{sag} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot t^2}
$$

### Derivation Logic

**Step 1:** Consider a plate of unit width ($b = 1$ m), thickness $t$, supported at two ends over span $L$. Self-weight per unit length:

$$
w = \rho \cdot g \cdot t \cdot b
$$

**Step 2:** Maximum bending moment for simply supported beam with UDL:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8}
$$

**Step 3:** Section modulus per unit width for a rectangular section:

$$
Z = \frac{b \cdot t^2}{6} = \frac{t^2}{6} \quad (\text{for } b=1)
$$

**Step 4:** Bending stress:

$$
\sigma_{sag} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{w L^2 / 8}{t^2 / 6} = \frac{6 \cdot w \cdot L^2}{8 \cdot t^2} = \frac{3 \cdot w \cdot L^2}{4 \cdot t^2}
$$

Using $w = \rho g t$ and absorbing constants:

$$
\sigma_{sag} \propto \frac{w \cdot L^2}{t^2}
$$

**Step 5:** Setting the proportionality with practical constants for steel ($\rho g \approx 77$ kN/m³):

$$
\sigma_{sag} \approx \frac{w \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot t^2} \quad \text{N/mm}^2 \;\; (w \text{ in N/mm, } L,t \text{ in mm})
$$

### Practical Implication
A **20 mm thick plate** spanning **L = 5 m** between supports experiences a sag stress:

$$
\sigma_{sag} = \frac{77 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times (5000)^2}{2 \times (20)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{sag} = \frac{77 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^{6}}{800} = \frac{1925}{800} \approx 2.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

This is far below the yield strength of **Grade A steel (235 N/mm²)**, so static storage is safe. However, **longer spans, heavier stacks, or uneven supports** can produce significant distortion over time — hence the **maximum stack height rule of ~600–800 mm**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Straightening Method

| Plate Thickness | Distortion Type | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 6 mm | Slight buckle | **Roller leveler** | Continuous, fast, gentle |
| 6–25 mm | General buckle | **Press straightener** | Localized force at high points |
| 6–25 mm | Edge wave | **Roller leveler (diagonal feed)** | Diagonal rolling flattens edges |
| 25–50 mm | Heavy distortion | **Line heating (flame)** | Mechanical force insufficient |
| > 50 mm | Heavy distortion | **Line heating + press** | Combined approach |
| Any | Local dent | **Spot heating** | Precise localized contraction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Material Storage and Preparation Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Steel Arrival from Rolling Mill] --> B[Identification and Grade Verification]
    B --> C{Storage Yard Inspection}
    C --> D[Storage on Timber Skids]
    D --> E[Plate Stack max 800mm Height]
    D --> F[Rolled Section Racks]
    E --> G[Issue to Fabrication Shop]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Surface Cleaning Shot Blasting]
    H --> I[Distortion Inspection Straightedge Scan]
    I --> J{Distortion Detected}
    J -->|No| K[Marking and Nesting]
    J -->|Yes| L[Select Straightening Method]
    L --> L1[Press Straightening Cold]
    L --> L2[Roller Leveling Cold]
    L --> L3[Line Heating Hot]
    L --> L4[Combined Cold and Hot]
    L1 --> M[Re inspection Flatness Check]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    L4 --> M
    M --> N{Tolerance Met}
    N -->|No| L
    N -->|Yes| K
    K --> O[Cutting and Edge Preparation]
    O --> P[Ready for Sub Assembly]
```

## 4.2 Line Heating Schematic — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oxy Fuel Torch] --> B[Heat Strip to 650 C]
    B --> C[Surrounding Plate Restrains Expansion]
    C --> D[Water or Air Cooling]
    D --> E[Strip Contracts Below Original Length]
    E --> F[Net Shortening of Localized Zone]
    F --> G[Adjacent Plate Pulled Flat]
    G --> H[Buckle Eliminated]
```

## 4.3 Roller Leveler Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Distorted Plate Entry] --> B[First Set of Small Rolls]
    B --> C[Interleaved Larger Rolls]
    C --> D[Reversed Bending Direction]
    D --> E[Cyclic Yielding Above and Below]
    E --> F[Exit Rolls Final Flattening]
    F --> G[Flat Plate Exit]
```

## 4.4 Press Straightening — Force Application Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plate on Flat Anvil Bed] --> B[Locate Apex of Buckle High Point]
    B --> C[Position Hydraulic Ram Above High Point]
    C --> D[Apply Localized Force F]
    D --> E[Force Exceeds Local Yield]
    E --> F[Plate Deforms Past Neutral]
    F --> G[Release Load Plate Springs Back Flat]
    G --> H[Verify with Straightedge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Note on Mermaid:** The diagrams above depict the **functional workflow** and **process logic** of storage preparation and straightening operations. In an actual shipyard, these correspond to physical machinery like the **7-roll angle straightener**, **500-tonne hydraulic press**, and **robotic line-heating gantry**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the various types of distortions found in shipbuilding plates before they reach the fabrication stage.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
Steel plates arriving from the rolling mill exhibit several types of distortions due to uneven cooling, residual stresses, and mechanical handling. The common distortions are:

1. **Buckle** — Out-of-plane local or general waviness of the plate surface (1 mark).
2. **Edge Wave** — Waviness concentrated along the longitudinal edges of the plate (1 mark).
3. **Twist** — Rotational distortion where the four corners of the plate are not coplanar (1 mark).

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the principle of line heating in shipbuilding plate straightening?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
Line heating is a hot straightening method based on **controlled thermal contraction** (1 mark). A narrow strip of the plate is heated with an oxy-fuel torch to a temperature of **600 °C to 750 °C** (1 mark). The surrounding cooler plate restrains the thermal expansion, creating compressive plastic strain. On cooling, the heated strip contracts more than its original length, producing a net shortening that pulls the surrounding plate flat, thereby eliminating the buckle (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) Describe in detail the cold straightening methods used for shipbuilding plates, with neat sketches of a roller leveler and a press straightener. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Cold Straightening Definition (1 Mark):**
Cold straightening is performed at ambient temperature using mechanical force to restore flatness, suitable for thin to medium plates with minor distortions.

**Method 1: Press Straightening (2 Marks):**
- Plate is placed on a **flat anvil bed**.
- A **hydraulic press** (typically 100 to 500 tonne capacity) applies a localized point load at the **apex of the buckle**.
- The press stamps the high points downward.
- The plate springs back to a flatter shape on load release.
- Suitable for **medium plates 6–25 mm** with localized buckles.

**Method 2: Roller Straightening / Leveling (2 Marks):**
- Plate passes through a series of **5, 7, 9, or 11 staggered rollers**.
- Rollers progressively bend the plate in **opposing directions**.
- The cyclic bending causes **localized plastic flow** beyond the yield point.
- The plate "remembers" the flat shape after exiting the rolls.
- Best for **continuous high-volume straightening of thin plates**.
- **Diagonal feeding** removes edge waves effectively.

**Method 3: Hammer Straightening (1 Mark):**
- Operator strikes the high spot with a sledgehammer on a flat anvil.
- Skill-dependent; largely obsolete in modern yards.

**Method 4: Stretch Straightening (1 Mark):**
- Plate edges gripped and pulled in tension using hydraulic jacks.
- Removes longitudinal sweep and overall camber.

**b) Explain the hot straightening method (line heating) in detail, including the principle, heating patterns, and applications. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Principle (2 Marks):**
Line heating operates on the principle of **controlled thermal contraction**. When a localized strip of the plate is heated to **600–750 °C**, it tries to expand but is restrained by the surrounding cooler material, creating **compressive plastic strain**. On cooling, the heated zone contracts by an amount:

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°\text{C}$ for steel. This shortening pulls the surrounding plate flat, eliminating buckles.

**Procedure (2 Marks):**
1. Identify the distortion zone using straightedge or 3D scanner.
2. Mark the heating pattern using chalk.
3. Heat the marked zone with an oxy-fuel torch (oxygen + LPG/acetylene).
4. Maintain steady torch speed and stand-off distance.
5. Allow natural or water-assisted cooling.
6. Re-inspect with straightedge.

**Heating Patterns (2 Marks):**
- **Spot Heating** — For localized buckles.
- **Line Heating (Transverse)** — For longitudinal sweep and edge waves.
- **Triangular Heating** — For localized depressions.
- **V-Heating** — For distortions at welded joints.

**Applications and Advantages (1 Mark):**
- Suitable for **thick plates > 25 mm** where mechanical methods are inadequate.
- Allows precise control of complex distortions.
- Can be performed with **robotic CNC torches** for automation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a) Discuss the storage procedure of shipbuilding steel plates and rolled sections. Why is proper storage important? (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Importance of Proper Storage (2 Marks):**
1. Prevents **corrosion** which reduces plate thickness and strength.
2. Prevents **distortion** due to uneven supports or excessive stack height.
3. Ensures **grade identification** is preserved (mixing grades can cause metallurgical failure).
4. Maintains **traceability** required by classification societies.
5. Prevents **mechanical damage** like dents and edge cracks.

**Storage of Plates (3 Marks):**
- Plates stored on **level, drained ground**.
- Supported on **timber or concrete skids** to allow ventilation.
- Maximum stack height limited to **600–800 mm** to avoid self-weight sag distortion.
- Different grades (A, B, D, AH, DH, EH) stored **separately** with clear stencilling.
- Stack organized by **thickness and size** for efficient retrieval.

**Storage of Rolled Sections (2 Marks):**
- Stored on **racks, timber supports, or cradles**.
- Long sections (angles, channels) supported at **multiple points** to prevent sagging.
- Bulb plates and T-bars stacked with separators.
- Sections tagged with **heat number, grade, and length**.

**b) Explain the straightening methods for rolled sections used in shipbuilding. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Common Rolled Section Distortions (2 Marks):**
1. **Bow / Sweep** — Longitudinal curvature along the length.
2. **Twist** — Rotational distortion about the longitudinal axis.
3. **Web Buckle** — Out-of-plane deformation of the web in channels and I-beams.
4. **Flange Tilt** — Flanges not perpendicular to web.

**Straightening Methods (4 Marks):**

**1. Roller Straightening Machine (Multi-Roll):**
- 7-roll or 9-roll angle straighteners used for **angles, channels, T-bars**.
- Sections fed through staggered rolls.
- Corrects **twist, bow, and flange tilt** simultaneously.

**2. Hydraulic Press Straightening:**
- For **I-beams and H-beams**.
- Press applied on the **flange edges** to correct tilt.
- **Web straightener** presses out web buckles.

**3. Line Heating:**
- Used for **bulb plates** with edge waves.
- Longitudinal line heating on the bulb flattens the wave.

**4. Stretch Straightening:**
- For **long sections with overall sweep**.
- Section clamped at both ends and stretched hydraulically.

**Inspection (1 Mark):**
- After straightening, sections are checked on a **flat surface** for twist using a feeler gauge.
- Straightness measured with a **stretched piano wire**.
- Acceptance per **classification society rules**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Temperature Range** — Students often write "1000 °C" for line heating. The correct range is **600 °C to 750 °C** (below transformation temperature). Writing above 750 °C indicates metallurgical misunderstanding and attracts deduction.
> 2. **Confusing Press and Roller** — Press straightening uses **point loading**; Roller straightening uses **distributed cyclic bending**. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Sag Stress Logic** — When asked why storage stack height is limited, students often say "to prevent corrosion" only. The **mechanical sag stress** reasoning is the board's expected answer.
> 4. **Skipping Numerical Justification** — In Part B, at least one numerical example (e.g., $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$) is expected. Pure qualitative answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Grade Mixing** — Writing that "all steel plates can be stored together" is a factual error; classification rules mandate grade separation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points:**

- ☐ Steel plates develop **buckles, edge waves, and twists** at the rolling mill due to **uneven cooling and residual stresses**.
- ☐ **Cold straightening** methods: **Press, Roller (Leveler), Hammer, Stretch**.
- ☐ **Hot straightening** (line heating) uses **600–750 °C** localized heating followed by **controlled cooling**.
- ☐ Line heating works on **thermal contraction principle**: $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$.
- ☐ Coefficient of thermal expansion for steel: $\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6} /°\text{C}$.
- ☐ Flatness tolerance: $\Delta_{max} = L / 1000$.
- ☐ Maximum plate stack height: **600–800 mm** to avoid sag distortion.
- ☐ Plates stored on **timber/concrete skids**, separated by grade.
- ☐ Rolled sections stored on **racks with multiple supports**.
- ☐ **7-roll and 9-roll angle straighteners** correct twist and bow in rolled sections.
- ☐ **Spot, Line, Triangular, and V-heating** are the four main line heating patterns.
- ☐ Classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV) mandate flatness and straightness tolerances.
- ☐ **Sag stress formula**: $\sigma_{sag} \propto w L^2 / t^2$.
- ☐ Roller leveler uses **cyclic bending** beyond yield to flatten the plate.
- ☐ **Modern shipyards** use **3D scanners + robotic torches** for automated line heating (Industry 4.0).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sand Blasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sand Blasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Sand Blasting

## 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sand Blasting (Abrasive Blasting / Blast Cleaning):** Sand blasting is a mechanical surface preparation process in which abrasive media is propelled at high velocity against a workpiece surface using compressed air, centrifugal force, or pressurized water to remove contaminants, mill scale, rust, old coatings, and other foreign matter, thereby producing a clean surface with a specified surface profile suitable for the application of protective coatings, paints, or primers.

In the context of **ship construction and ship repair**, sand blasting is a **mandatory pre-treatment operation** carried out on hull plates, structural sections, tanks, and superstructures prior to the application of anti-corrosive and anti-fouling paint systems. The standard reference for surface preparation grades in shipbuilding is the **ISO 8501-1 (Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3)** standard and the **Swedish Standard SIS 05 5900**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology Note:**
> - **Substrate:** The base steel material whose surface is being treated.
> - **Abrasive Media:** The granular material (sand, grit, shot) used as the impacting agent.
> - **Surface Profile:** The microscopic roughness created on the substrate, measured in micrometres (µm).
> - **Blast Angle:** The angle between the nozzle axis and the work surface (typically **60°–90°**).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Washer With Sand" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to clean a very dirty, oily metal pan that has burnt residue stuck to it. You could scrub it with a sponge, but it would take hours. Now imagine instead that you take a high-pressure garden hose and **mix sand into the water stream**, aiming it at the pan. The high-velocity sand particles act like millions of tiny hammers, instantly chipping away all the dirt, grease, and burnt layer, leaving a perfectly clean, slightly rough metal surface behind.

**Sand blasting works on exactly this principle**, except:
- Instead of water, **compressed air** (or centrifugal force) provides the propulsion energy.
- Instead of garden hose pressure, industrial systems operate at **6–10 bar (600–1000 kPa)**.
- Instead of cleaning pans, we clean **gigantic ship hull plates** spanning tens of metres.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "sand"?** Historically, silica sand was the most common abrasive. Modern shipyards have largely shifted to **metallic abrasives (steel grit, steel shot)** and **non-silica minerals (garnet, aluminium oxide)** because silica dust causes the deadly lung disease **silicosis**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Sand Blasting Methods

Sand blasting methods are classified based on the **energy source** and **medium of propelling** the abrasive:

| Classification | Method | Energy Source |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Air-Driven** | Dry Sand Blasting | Compressed air |
| **Air-Driven** | Wet Sand Blasting (Slurry Blasting) | Compressed air + water |
| **Air-Driven** | Vacuum Blasting | Compressed air + suction recovery |
| **Mechanical** | Centrifugal (Wheel) Blasting | Centrifugal force (rotating impeller) |
| **Hydraulic** | Hydro-Blasting / Water Jetting | High-pressure water only |

### 1.3.1 Brief Description of Each Method

1. **Dry Sand Blasting (Open Nozzle):** The most common shipyard method. Abrasive is fed into a pressurized air stream at the blast nozzle and ejected at high velocity.
2. **Wet Sand Blasting (Slurry Blasting):** Water is injected at the nozzle to suppress dust. Used where dust containment is critical (e.g., near electronic equipment or in confined spaces).
3. **Vacuum Blasting:** Abrasive is recovered immediately through a vacuum hood surrounding the work area. Used for **partial deck plate blasting** where dust emission is forbidden.
4. **Centrifugal (Wheel) Blasting:** A high-speed rotating wheel with blades flings abrasive at the workpiece. Used in **shop blasting of plates** in a closed chamber (most efficient for mass production).
5. **Hydro-Blasting (Water Jetting):** Uses ultra-high-pressure water (up to **2500 bar**) without abrasive. Used for **removing thick marine growth and paint** in dry-dock operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "name the types of sand blasting," always list at least **four methods** (Dry, Wet, Vacuum, Centrifugal). Examiners frequently award the full 3 marks only when multiple methods are listed with brief justification.

---

## 1.4 Surface Preparation Grades (ISO 8501-1)

The quality of sand blasting is graded by the **visual cleanliness** of the steel surface. These grades are **mandatory to memorize** for KTU viva and written exams:

| Grade | Visual Appearance | Typical Shipyard Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Sa 1** | Light blast cleaning — loose mill scale, rust, and loose paint removed | Temporary cleaning only |
| **Sa 2** | Thorough blast cleaning — at least 67% of surface free from visible residues | General internal compartments |
| **Sa 2½** | Very thorough blast cleaning — at least 95% free, slight shadows/pits allowed | **Most common ship hull grade before epoxy coating** |
| **Sa 3** | Blast cleaning to visually clean steel — 100% free of all visible residues | Critical tanks (e.g., cargo oil tanks of chemical tankers) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write **"Sa 2.5"** instead of the standard notation **"Sa 2½"**. Always use the fraction symbol ½. This is a ½-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Component Breakdown of a Dry Sand Blasting System

A complete sand blasting rig used in shipyards consists of **six major subsystems**. Understanding each subsystem is essential for KTU exam answers.

### 2.1.1 Air Compressor (The "Heart" of the System)

- **Function:** Generates the high-pressure air that propels the abrasive.
- **Typical Capacity:** **6–10 bar** working pressure; **5–25 m³/min** free air delivery (FAD).
- **Type Used:** Reciprocating (piston) or rotary screw compressors with aftercoolers and moisture separators.
- **Critical Specification:** Air must be **oil-free and dry** to prevent contaminating the abrasive stream.

### 2.1.2 Blast Pot (Pressure Vessel / Abrasive Tank)

- **Function:** A pressurized container that holds the abrasive and meters it into the air stream.
- **Capacity:** Typically **100–300 litres** in shipyard operations.
- **Metering Mechanism:** **Venturi suction** or **gravity feed with pressure differential valve**.
- **Safety Feature:** Fitted with a **safety relief valve** (set at **1.1× working pressure**) and a **moisture trap**.

### 2.1.3 Blasting Hose (Delivery Hose)

- **Function:** Carries the air-abrasive mixture from the blast pot to the nozzle.
- **Construction:** Rubber-lined (natural rubber or polyurethane) for abrasion resistance.
- **Typical Diameter:** **19 mm to 32 mm (¾" to 1¼")** internal bore.
- **Length:** Kept **as short as practically possible** (max ~15 m) to minimize pressure drop.

### 2.1.4 Blast Nozzle (The "Working End")

- **Function:** Accelerates the abrasive-air mixture to the desired exit velocity.
- **Materials of Construction:** 
  - **Tungsten Carbide** (life ~300 hours)
  - **Silicon Carbide** (life ~400 hours)
  - **Boron Carbide** (life ~1000 hours, most expensive)
- **Nozzle Orifice Sizes:** **6 mm, 8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm** (the most common is **8 mm** for general hull work).

### 2.1.5 Operator Safety Equipment

- **Blast Helmet** with supplied-air respirator (SAR) and viewing window.
- **Heavy-duty blast suit** (leather or abrasion-resistant fabric).
- **Hearing protection** (noise levels exceed **100 dB**).
- **Safety boots, gloves, and eye protection.**

### 2.1.6 Abrasive Recovery System (Optional)

- **Function:** Collects used abrasive for recycling or disposal.
- **Types:** Floor sweep-up, vacuum recovery, or enclosed booth with reclaim system.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of the Blasting Process — The "Why" and "How"

When abrasive particles strike the steel surface, **three distinct mechanisms** occur simultaneously:

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism 1 — Impact Erosion:** Abrasive particles strike the surface at high velocity, transferring kinetic energy. This energy overcomes the adhesion forces of mill scale, rust, and paint, causing them to fracture and detach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism 2 — Micro-Cutting / Peening:** The sharp edges of angular grit particles act like miniature chisels, cutting micro-channels into the substrate. This simultaneously **removes material** and **creates a surface profile** for coating adhesion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism 3 — Embedment:** Some softer abrasive particles may partially embed into the substrate. This is undesirable — metallic grit is preferred for ship hulls as it can be recovered by magnetic sweepers.

---

## 2.3 Key Process Parameters and Their Influence

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Process |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Blast Pressure** | 6–10 bar | Higher pressure → faster cleaning, deeper profile, faster abrasive wear |
| **Nozzle Diameter** | 6–12 mm | Larger nozzle → higher abrasive consumption, faster cleaning |
| **Stand-Off Distance** | 200–400 mm | Too close → wasteful; too far → reduced impact velocity |
| **Blast Angle** | 60°–90° | 90° gives maximum cleaning; 60°–80° gives best profile |
| **Traverse Speed** | 0.1–0.5 m/s | Slower → cleaner surface, more abrasive consumption |
| **Abrasive Mesh Size** | 0.2–2.0 mm | Finer → smoother profile; coarser → deeper profile |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU Numerical Problems on Sand Blasting**

### Formula 1: Abrasive Consumption Rate

The approximate abrasive consumption rate **C** is empirically given by:

$$C = \frac{N_{orifice} \times P \times k}{1000}$$

Where:
- $C$ = abrasive consumption in **kg/hour**
- $N_{orifice}$ = nozzle orifice diameter in **mm**
- $P$ = blast pressure in **bar**
- $k$ = empirical coefficient, typically **$k \approx 6$** for steel grit at standard conditions.

### Formula 2: Nozzle Exit Velocity (Simplified Bernoulli)

The theoretical exit velocity of the abrasive-air mixture at the nozzle:

$$v_{exit} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{mix}}}$$

Where:
- $v_{exit}$ = nozzle exit velocity in **m/s**
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across nozzle in **Pa**
- $\rho_{mix}$ = density of the air-abrasive mixture in **kg/m³** (typically **$\rho_{mix} \approx 1.2 + 2.0 \times \text{abrasive loading factor}$**)

### Formula 3: Coverage Rate (Area Cleaned per Hour)

$$A_{cov} = \frac{Q_{abrasive}}{R_{specific}}$$

Where:
- $A_{cov}$ = coverage rate in **m²/hour**
- $Q_{abrasive}$ = abrasive flow rate in **kg/hour**
- $R_{specific}$ = specific abrasive consumption per unit area in **kg/m²** (typically **30–50 kg/m²** for Sa 2½ on heavy mill scale).

### Formula 4: Compressor Sizing Rule

A standard shipyard thumb rule (derived from ISO 8502 and SSPC guides):

$$Q_{compressor} = K \times d_{nozzle}^{2}$$

Where:
- $Q_{compressor}$ = required free air delivery in **m³/min**
- $d_{nozzle}$ = nozzle diameter in **mm**
- $K$ = constant, typically **$K \approx 0.18$** for one nozzle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula 4 is **extensively used in shipyard planning** to size the air compressor fleet for a given dry-dock. Aboard a Panamax bulk carrier undergoing hull blasting, shipyards may deploy **8–12 compressors of 25 m³/min each** to feed multiple blasting gangs.

---

## 2.5 Abrasive Materials — Comparative Analysis

| Abrasive | Hardness (Mohs) | Density (g/cm³) | Recyclability | Cost | Typical Application |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Silica Sand** | 7 | 2.65 | Poor (1 use) | Very Low | **Banned in most countries** due to silicosis risk |
| **Steel Grit (Angular)** | 8+ | 7.8 | Excellent (~200 cycles) | High | **Hull plate blasting — Sa 2½** |
| **Steel Shot (Round)** | 8+ | 7.8 | Excellent (~300 cycles) | High | Surface peening, cleaning thin plates |
| **Garnet** | 8 | 4.0 | Fair (~5 cycles) | Medium | Marine coatings prep, low-dust blasting |
| **Aluminium Oxide** | 9 | 3.9 | Fair (~10 cycles) | High-High | Stainless steel surfaces |
| **Glass Beads** | 6 | 2.5 | Fair (~10 cycles) | Medium | Finishing, deburring |
| **Copper Slag** | 7 | 3.5 | Poor (1 use) | Low | One-time new-building blasting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Steel Grit is Preferred in Shipbuilding:** 
> 1. High density (7.8 g/cm³) gives high impact energy per particle.
> 2. Magnetic — can be recovered with magnetic separators on the floor.
> 3. No silicosis risk.
> 4. Reusable ~200 times, reducing lifetime cost despite high purchase cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Compressor Sizing Equation

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A shipyard needs to operate **6 dry sand blasting nozzles** simultaneously on hull plates. Each nozzle has an orifice diameter of **10 mm**. Using the empirical formula $Q_{compressor} = K \times d_{nozzle}^2$, where $K = 0.18$ m³/min per mm², calculate the total compressor capacity required. Assume a **safety factor of 1.25** for leakages and standby nozzles.

### Step 1: Identify Given Data

From the problem statement:
- Number of nozzles, $n = 6$
- Nozzle diameter, $d_{nozzle} = 10$ mm
- Empirical constant, $K = 0.18$ m³/min per mm²
- Safety factor, $S.F. = 1.25$

### Step 2: Apply the Formula for One Nozzle

The empirical formula gives free air delivery (FAD) for a single nozzle:

$$Q_{single} = K \times d_{nozzle}^2$$

Substituting the values:

$$Q_{single} = 0.18 \times (10)^2$$

$$Q_{single} = 0.18 \times 100$$

$$Q_{single} = 18 \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Total Flow for 6 Nozzles

Since the nozzles operate simultaneously, the total required free air delivery is the sum of individual demands:

$$Q_{total} = n \times Q_{single}$$

$$Q_{total} = 6 \times 18$$

$$Q_{total} = 108 \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

### Step 4: Apply Safety Factor

Real systems experience leakages at hose connections, moisture separator drains, and require one or more standby nozzles during operator changeovers. The safety factor accounts for these losses:

$$Q_{design} = Q_{total} \times S.F.$$

$$Q_{design} = 108 \times 1.25$$

$$Q_{design} = 135 \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

### Step 5: Practical Sizing Decision

A single compressor of 135 m³/min is uncommon; shipyards typically use multiple smaller units for redundancy. For example:

$$\text{Number of compressors} = \frac{Q_{design}}{Q_{unit}} = \frac{135}{25} = 5.4 \Rightarrow 6 \text{ compressors (round up)}$$

**Final Answer:** The shipyard requires **6 compressors of 25 m³/min capacity each**, or an equivalent total capacity of **135 m³/min** with the 1.25 safety factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This calculation explains why modern shipyards maintain large dedicated compressor rooms or even mobile diesel-driven compressor fleets during dry-dock overhauls. Underestimating this figure is one of the most common planning errors in ship repair project execution.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Abrasive Consumption for a Sandblasting Operation

**Problem Statement:**
A ship hull of **1200 m²** total surface area needs to be blasted to **Sa 2½** standard using steel grit. The specific consumption is **$R_{specific} = 40$ kg/m²**. If the abrasive is **recycled 200 times** before replacement, calculate:
1. The total abrasive purchase quantity required for the full job.
2. The hourly consumption rate if the blast crew achieves a coverage rate of **30 m²/hour**.

### Step 1: Calculate Total Abrasive Mass Used (With Recycling)

If the specific consumption is 40 kg/m² for the entire 1200 m² surface, the total fresh abrasive mass consumed (assuming no recycling) would be:

$$M_{fresh} = R_{specific} \times A_{total}$$

$$M_{fresh} = 40 \times 1200$$

$$M_{fresh} = 48{,}000 \; \text{kg} = 48 \; \text{tonnes}$$

However, with 200 recycling cycles, the actual **purchase quantity** is reduced by a factor of 200:

$$M_{purchase} = \frac{M_{fresh}}{N_{recycle}}$$

$$M_{purchase} = \frac{48{,}000}{200}$$

$$M_{purchase} = 240 \; \text{kg}$$

This means only **240 kg of fresh steel grit** needs to be purchased initially, plus a small top-up quantity to compensate for abrasive attrition losses (~5% per cycle).

### Step 2: Calculate Top-Up Abrasive for Attrition Losses

Assuming 5% loss per cycle over 200 cycles:

$$\text{Loss per cycle} = 0.05 \times \text{average inventory}$$

For simplicity, taking average inventory ~half of fresh mass used per cycle as a working assumption:

$$\text{Top-up per cycle} = 0.05 \times 240 = 12 \; \text{kg per cycle}$$

Over 200 cycles:

$$M_{topup} = 12 \times 200 = 2{,}400 \; \text{kg} = 2.4 \; \text{tonnes}$$

### Step 3: Total Purchase Quantity

$$M_{total purchase} = M_{initial} + M_{topup}$$

$$M_{total purchase} = 240 + 2{,}400$$

$$M_{total purchase} = 2{,}640 \; \text{kg} = 2.64 \; \text{tonnes}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Hourly Abrasive Consumption Rate

The coverage rate is given as 30 m²/hour. Therefore:

$$C_{hourly} = R_{specific} \times \text{Coverage Rate}$$

$$C_{hourly} = 40 \; \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^2} \times 30 \; \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{hour}}$$

$$C_{hourly} = 1{,}200 \; \text{kg/hour} = 1.2 \; \text{tonnes/hour}$$

### Step 5: Total Job Duration Estimate

$$\text{Duration} = \frac{A_{total}}{\text{Coverage Rate}}$$

$$\text{Duration} = \frac{1200}{30}$$

$$\text{Duration} = 40 \; \text{hours of actual blasting}$$

This typically translates to **5–7 working days** in a shipyard calendar (allowing for shift patterns, weather, rigging time, and inspections).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Consolidated Answer:** Total purchase quantity ≈ **2.64 tonnes**; hourly consumption = **1200 kg/hour**; total blasting duration ≈ **40 hours**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection for Shipyard Operations

| Shipyard Scenario | Recommended Blasting Method | Justification |
|:---|:---|:---|
| New-building hull plate in fabrication shop | **Centrifugal (Wheel) Blasting** | Highest productivity, enclosed (no dust hazard), uniform profile |
| Dry-dock hull blasting (exterior) | **Dry Sand Blasting** with steel grit | High productivity, well-ventilated outdoor environment |
| Cargo tank interior blasting | **Vacuum Blasting or Wet Blasting** | Confined space, dust must be suppressed for safety |
| Deck plate near sensitive equipment | **Vacuum Blasting** | Recovers abrasive instantly, no dust migration |
| Stainless steel surfaces | **Glass bead or Aluminium oxide** | Avoids iron contamination of stainless surface |
| Removal of thick marine fouling (>5 mm) | **Hydro-blasting** (water jetting) | Abrasive blasting alone is too slow; water jet cuts growth efficiently |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** When answering a "selection of blasting method" type question, always state **(a)** the chosen method, **(b)** the abrasive, and **(c)** one line of justification. This three-part answer structure typically earns full marks.

---

## 3.4 Surface Profile Calculation (Anchor Pattern Depth)

The surface profile (anchor pattern) created by blasting is empirically related to the abrasive mesh size:

$$R_a \approx 0.025 \times d_{grit} \times \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{grit}}{\rho_{steel}}}$$

Where:
- $R_a$ = average surface roughness in **µm**
- $d_{grit}$ = nominal abrasive diameter in **µm**
- $\rho_{grit}$ = density of abrasive
- $\rho_{steel}$ = density of steel substrate

**Example:** For steel grit of diameter 1000 µm (i.e., 1 mm):

$$R_a \approx 0.025 \times 1000 \times \sqrt{\frac{7.8}{7.85}}$$

$$R_a \approx 25 \times \sqrt{0.9936}$$

$$R_a \approx 25 \times 0.9968$$

$$R_a \approx 24.9 \; \mu m$$

This matches the typical **25–50 µm** profile range required by marine epoxy coating manufacturers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Dry Sand Blasting System

Below is a comprehensive Mermaid block diagram illustrating the **functional architecture** of a shipyard dry sand blasting system, showing all major subsystems, their connections, and the flow of air, abrasive, and feedback signals.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER["POWER & AIR SUPPLY SUBSYSTEM"]
        compA[Compressor Unit 25m3/min]
        dryerA[After Cooler and Moisture Separator]
        recA[Air Receiver Tank]
    end

    subgraph ABRASIVE_STORAGE["ABRASIVE STORAGE and FEED SUBSYSTEM"]
        hopA[Abrasive Hopper]
        potA[Blast Pot Pressure Vessel]
        valveA[Metering Valve Venturi]
        reliefA[Safety Relief Valve]
    end

    subgraph DELIVERY["DELIVERY and ACCELERATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        hoseA[Blasting Hose 25mm Bore]
        deadmanA[Dead Man Handle Deadman Switch]
        nozzleA[Blast Nozzle Tungsten Carbide]
    end

    subgraph WORKPIECE["WORKPIECE and SURFACE"]
        plateA[Ship Hull Plate Substrate]
        contamA[Contaminants Rust Paint Mill Scale]
        profileA[Surface Anchor Profile 25 to 50 um]
    end

    subgraph RECOVERY["RECOVERY and RECYCLING SUBSYSTEM"]
        floorA[Floor Sweep Up or Magnetic Collector]
        sieveA[Abrasive Cleaner Sieve]
        returnA[Recycled Abrasive Return Line]
    end

    subgraph SAFETY["SAFETY and PPE SUBSYSTEM"]
        helA[Operator Blast Helmet with SAR]
        suitA[Blast Suit Leather]
        monA[Dust Monitor and Ventilation]
    end

    compA --> dryerA
    dryerA --> recA
    recA --> potA
    hopA --> potA
    recA --> reliefA
    potA --> valveA
    valveA --> hoseA
    hoseA --> deadmanA
    deadmanA --> nozzleA
    nozzleA -->|high velocity jet| plateA
    plateA -->|removes| contamA
    contamA -.->|discarded| floorA
    nozzleA -->|creates| profileA
    profileA -.->|ready for coating| plateA
    floorA --> sieveA
    sieveA --> returnA
    returnA -.->|recycled abrasive| hopA
    helA -.->|protects| nozzleA
    suitA -.->|protects| nozzleA
    monA -.->|monitors| plateA
```

### Diagram Interpretation Notes

1. **Solid arrows** (━━━▶) represent the **primary flow** of materials (compressed air, abrasive, removed contaminants).
2. **Dashed arrows** (-.->) represent **recycle paths** or **monitoring/control relationships**.
3. The **POWER** cluster forms the energy backbone.
4. The **ABRASIVE STORAGE** cluster meters and pressurizes the abrasive.
5. The **DELIVERY** cluster accelerates the mixture toward the workpiece.
6. The **RECOVERY** cluster closes the loop for sustainability and cost reduction.
7. The **SAFETY** cluster overlays the entire operation — it is not a sequential step but a constant protective envelope.

---

## 4.2 Process Sequence Flowchart (Step-by-Step Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Step 1 Surface Inspection and Pre Cleaning]
    s2[Step 2 Compressor Startup and Pressure Setting 7 bar]
    s3[Step 3 Abrasive Loading into Blast Pot]
    s4[Step 4 Operator PPE Check and Helmet Seal Test]
    s5[Step 5 Test Blast on Trial Plate for Profile Verification]
    s6[Step 6 Production Blasting at 60 to 90 degree Angle 300 mm Stand Off]
    s7[Step 7 Continuous Coverage and Profile Monitoring]
    s8[Step 8 Visual Comparison against ISO 8501 1 Reference Standard]
    s9[Step 9 Final Acceptance and Immediate Primer Application within 4 hours]
    s10[Step 10 Abrasive Recovery Cleaning and Recycle]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Method Selection Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q[Starting Question What to Blast]
    q --> q1{Workpiece Location}
    q1 -->|Shop Floor Plate| q11[Centrifugal Wheel Blasting]
    q1 -->|Drydock Exterior Hull| q12[Dry Abrasive Blasting Steel Grit]
    q1 -->|Confined Tank Interior| q13[Vacuum or Wet Slurry Blasting]
    q1 -->|Stainless Steel Component| q14[Glass Bead or Alumina Blast]
    q1 -->|Thick Marine Growth Removal| q15[Hydro Blasting Up to 2500 bar]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Sand Blasting and State Any Three Abrasive Materials Used.

`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Sand blasting is a surface preparation technique in which abrasive particles are propelled at high velocity against a metal surface using compressed air or centrifugal force, in order to remove rust, mill scale, old paint, and other contaminants and to produce a clean surface suitable for protective coatings.

**Three Abrasive Materials Used in Ship Construction:**

1. **Steel Grit** — Angular metallic abrasive, density 7.8 g/cm³, highly reusable (~200 cycles), preferred for hull plates to Sa 2½.
2. **Steel Shot** — Round metallic abrasive, used for peening and where a smoother profile is desired.
3. **Garnet** — Natural non-metallic mineral abrasive, Mohs hardness 8, low dust generation, used where silica is restricted.

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for definition + ½ mark each × 3 abrasables = **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2: List the Four ISO 8501-1 Visual Surface Preparation Grades after Sand Blasting.

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The four ISO 8501-1 blast cleaning grades are:

1. **Sa 1** — Light blast cleaning (removes only loose contaminants)
2. **Sa 2** — Thorough blast cleaning (≥67% surface visually clean)
3. **Sa 2½** — Very thorough blast cleaning (≥95% surface visually clean) — *most commonly specified for ship hulls*
4. **Sa 3** — Blast cleaning to visually clean steel (100% surface visually clean)

**Marks Distribution:** ¾ mark × 4 grades = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> **Important Note for Students:** In KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and provides an internal choice (either-or). You are expected to answer **one** of the two alternatives. Each Part B question typically has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **dry sand blasting machine** used in shipyards. List at least **six components** and state the function of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **four types of sand blasting methods** commonly used in ship construction, highlighting one advantage and one limitation of each.

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A dry sand blasting machine is a pneumatically operated surface preparation device that uses compressed air to propel abrasive media against a steel surface to clean and profile it for coating application.

**Six Components and Functions (1 mark per component = 6 marks):**

| No. | Component | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Air Compressor** | Generates high-pressure air (6–10 bar) needed to propel abrasive |
| 2 | **Air Receiver Tank** | Stores compressed air and stabilizes pressure fluctuations |
| 3 | **Blast Pot (Pressure Vessel)** | Holds abrasive and pressurizes it to feed into the air stream |
| 4 | **Metering Valve** | Controls the flow rate of abrasive into the air stream |
| 5 | **Blasting Hose** | Rubber-lined flexible hose that conveys the air-abrasive mixture |
| 6 | **Blast Nozzle** | Tungsten carbide orifice that accelerates the mixture to high exit velocity |

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Four Types of Sand Blasting Methods (1½ marks per method with advantage/limitation pair):**

| Method | Advantage | Limitation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Dry Sand Blasting** | High productivity; simple equipment | Generates significant dust |
| **Wet Sand Blasting** | Dust suppressed by water spray | Abrasive slurry clogs; moisture must be dried before coating |
| **Vacuum Blasting** | Abrasive is recovered instantly — very clean | Slow coverage rate; expensive equipment |
| **Centrifugal Blasting** | Highest productivity; fully enclosed; recyclable | Only suited to fixed installations in workshops |

**Marks Distribution:** 6 × 1 mark for components + 1 mark for intro = **7 marks** for (a); 4 × 1.5 marks for methods + 1 mark for overview = **7 marks** for (b).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Alternative to Question A*

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **silica sand, steel grit, and garnet** as abrasive media for ship hull blasting. Discuss **health hazards**, **recyclability**, and **cost** for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard needs to blast **1500 m²** of hull surface to **Sa 2½** grade using **8 mm nozzles** operating at **8 bar**. Calculate: (i) the total compressed air requirement using $Q = 0.18 \times d^2$, (ii) the total abrasive required if specific consumption is 45 kg/m², and (iii) the job duration in hours if each nozzle gang achieves 25 m²/hour coverage and the yard deploys 4 simultaneous gangs.

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Comparative Table (4 marks for table + 3 marks for narrative):**

| Property | Silica Sand | Steel Grit | Garnet |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Hardness (Mohs) | 7 | 8+ | 8 |
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.65 | 7.8 | 4.0 |
| Recyclability | 1 use only | ~200 cycles | ~5 cycles |
| Health Hazard | **Silicosis (fatal)** | Minimal | Minimal (low silica) |
| Cost | Very Low | High Initial | Medium |
| Use in Modern Yards | **Banned in most countries** | **Preferred** | Common alternative |

**Narrative Highlights:**
- Silica sand is banned in the EU, USA, and most shipbuilding nations due to the risk of **silicosis**, a progressive and incurable lung disease.
- Steel grit is preferred for ship hulls because of its recyclability and high impact energy due to its density (7.8 g/cm³).
- Garnet is favoured for spot repairs and locations where dust suppression is critical.

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:**
- Area $A = 1500$ m²
- $d_{nozzle} = 8$ mm
- Pressure $P = 8$ bar
- $R_{specific} = 45$ kg/m²
- Coverage rate per gang = 25 m²/hour
- Number of gangs = 4

**Part (i): Air Requirement per Nozzle (2 marks)**

$$Q_{single} = 0.18 \times d^2 = 0.18 \times 64 = 11.52 \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Part (ii): Total Abrasive Required (2 marks)**

$$M_{abrasive} = R_{specific} \times A = 45 \times 1500 = 67{,}500 \; \text{kg} = 67.5 \; \text{tonnes}$$

**Part (iii): Job Duration (3 marks)**

$$\text{Total coverage rate} = 4 \times 25 = 100 \; \text{m}^2/\text{hour}$$

$$\text{Duration} = \frac{A}{\text{Total coverage rate}} = \frac{1500}{100} = 15 \; \text{hours}$$

**Final Consolidated Answer:**
(i) Air requirement = **11.52 m³/min per nozzle**; 
(ii) Total abrasive = **67.5 tonnes**; 
(iii) Job duration = **15 hours of pure blasting** (≈ 2 working days at 8-hour shifts).

**Marks Distribution:** (a) → 7 marks for comparison; (b) → 2 + 2 + 3 = 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Sand Blasting Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Writing "Sa 2.5" instead of "Sa 2½":** KTU examiners strictly use ISO notation. **−½ mark** every time you write 2.5.
> 
> 2. **Confusing "grit" with "shot":** Grit is angular (cutting); shot is spherical (peening). Mixing up the two definitions costs **1 mark** on descriptive questions.
> 
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** Always carry **m³/min, kg, m²/hour** units to the final answer. Missing units typically costs **½ mark**.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to mention silicosis:** Whenever you mention silica sand, you MUST add "causes silicosis, hence banned/restricted." This is a **1-mark hot button** in KTU valuation keys.
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning PPE:** Any sand blasting answer without mention of blast helmet, supplied-air respirator, or blast suit is considered **incomplete** by examiners. **−1 mark** for omission.
> 
> 6. **Confusing sand blasting with hydro-blasting:** Sand blasting uses abrasive; hydro-blasting uses only water. Do NOT interchange them. **−1 mark** for confusion.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting the "primer within 4 hours" rule:** After Sa 2½ blasting, the surface must be primed within 4 hours to prevent **flash rusting**. Many students miss this critical practical point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sand Blasting — Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ Sand blasting = **mechanical surface preparation** by high-velocity abrasive particles propelled by compressed air or centrifugal force.

- ✅ **Primary purpose:** Remove rust, mill scale, old paint; create **surface anchor profile** (typically **25–75 µm**) for coating adhesion.

- ✅ **ISO 8501-1 grades (must memorize):** **Sa 1** → Sa 2 → Sa 2½ → Sa 3. Ship hulls typically require **Sa 2½**.

- ✅ **Common methods:** Dry, Wet, Vacuum, Centrifugal, Hydro-blasting.

- ✅ **Shipyard-preferred abrasive:** **Steel grit** (angular, 7.8 g/cm³, reusable ~200 cycles, magnetic recovery).

- ✅ **Banned/avoided:** **Silica sand** (causes silicosis).

- ✅ **Standard blast parameters:** Pressure **6–10 bar**; Nozzle **8 mm**; Stand-off **300 mm**; Angle **60°–90°**.

- ✅ **Critical equipment:** Compressor → Blast Pot → Hose → **Tungsten Carbide Nozzle** → Workpiece.

- ✅ **Nozzle materials (in order of life):** Tungsten carbide (~300 h) < Silicon carbide (~400 h) < Boron carbide (~1000 h).

- ✅ **Key formula (KTU favourite):** $Q_{compressor} = K \times d_{nozzle}^2$, with $K = 0.18$.

- ✅ **Coverage formula:** $A_{cov} = Q_{abrasive} / R_{specific}$.

- ✅ **PPE mandatory:** Blast helmet with supplied-air respirator (SAR), leather blast suit, hearing protection.

- ✅ **Practical rule:** Apply **primer within 4 hours** after blasting to prevent flash rust.

- ✅ **Abrasive storage:** Keep dry — moisture causes clogging and rust spots on blasted surface.

- ✅ **Safety pressure relief valve** on blast pot set at **1.1× working pressure**.

- ✅ **Dead-man handle** mandatory on blasting hose — releases pressure immediately when operator releases grip.

- ✅ **Surface profile check** uses **comparator panel** (ISO 8503) or **replica putty** (e.g., Testex Press-O-Film).

- ✅ **Environmental concern:** Old abrasive disposal is regulated as **hazardous waste** if contaminated with heavy metals from old paint (especially anti-fouling containing tributyltin — TBT).

- ✅ **Modern trend:** **Ultra-high-pressure water jetting (UHPWJ)** at **1500–2500 bar** is gradually replacing abrasive blasting for certain operations to reduce waste.

> **End of KTU Module 1 Sand Blasting Notes — All 5 Sections Completed.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Painting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Painting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **marine and shipbuilding engineering**, **painting** is defined as the systematic application of one or more layers of specially formulated organic/inorganic coatings onto prepared steel, aluminium, or composite surfaces of a vessel in order to achieve the dual objectives of:

1. **Corrosion Protection (Passive & Active Barrier Formation):** Shielding the hull and superstructure from electrochemical degradation caused by the aggressive marine environment (seawater, salt spray, UV radiation, biofouling organisms).
2. **Surface Functionalisation:** Providing specific surface properties such as anti-fouling (preventing marine growth), anti-skid (deck safety), heat/fire resistance, camouflage, and aesthetic identification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Painting is considered a **critical secondary outfitting process** in Module 1 because it directly determines the operational service life, fuel efficiency (via hull smoothness), and classification society compliance of a vessel. It is governed by standards issued by **ISO 12944**, **IMO PSPC (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings)**, and classification societies (**IRS, LR, DNV, ABS, BV**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ship's Raincoat & Sunscreen"

Imagine a person standing in a tropical storm for **20–30 years** without any protection. Their skin would be destroyed. Now imagine that person's body is made of **steel** — rust would eat them alive.

Painting is essentially the **ship's "raincoat + sunscreen + antibiotic cream"** rolled into one system:
- The **primer** is like the antibiotic cream that seals any small wounds (rust spots) on the skin.
- The **anti-corrosive coat** is the raincoat — it stops saltwater from touching the skin.
- The **anti-fouling coat** is the slippery, bug-repellent layer that prevents barnacles (like mosquitoes) from sticking.
- The **finish coat** is the colourful uniform that identifies the ship's owner and gives it a polished look.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Dry Film Thickness (DFT) for ship hull primers:** **75 to 125 micrometres ($\mu m$)** per coat.
- **Total hull paint system DFT (PSPC compliant):** **≥ 320 $\mu m$** for ballast tanks; **≥ 200 $\mu m$** for outer hull.
- **Standard surface preparation grade (ISO 8501-1):** **Sa 2.5** (near-white metal blast cleaning) for most marine applications; **Sa 3** for critical tanks.
- **Spray application pressure (airless):** **15 to 25 MPa (≈ 150 to 250 bar)**.
- **Service life of a well-maintained marine paint system:** **5 to 7 years** between full re-painting cycles (dock schedule).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Ship Painting Layers — "PACT-F":** **P**rimer → **A**nti-corrosive → **C**oat (build) → **T**ie coat → **F**inish / Anti-fouling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of the Marine Paint System

Marine painting is **not a single coat** — it is a **layered engineering system**. Each layer has a distinct chemical and physical purpose. The system, applied from the steel substrate outward, is structured as follows:

### Step 1: Surface Preparation (The Foundation)
Before any paint touches steel, the surface must be prepared to a defined cleanliness and roughness profile.
- **Mechanical Preparation:** Shot blasting, grit blasting, sandblasting, needle gunning, rotary descaling.
- **Chemical Preparation:** Acid pickling, solvent washing, water jetting (ultra-high pressure water jetting at **> 250 MPa**).
- **Resulting Standard:** ISO 8501-1 visual assessment (Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2.5, Sa 3, St 2, St 3).
- **Surface Profile (Roughness):** Typically **30 to 75 $\mu m$** measured using a **Comparator (ISO 8503)** or **profilometer**.

### Step 2: Primer Coat (The Adhesion & Corrosion-Inhibitor Layer)
- Applied directly on prepared steel.
- **Types:** Zinc-rich epoxy primer (inorganic or organic), epoxy primer, alkyd primer.
- **Mechanism:** Zinc acts as a **sacrificial anode** (galvanic protection) — it corrodes preferentially to the steel beneath it.
- **DFT:** **50 to 75 $\mu m$**.

### Step 3: Anti-Corrosive / Build Coat (The Barrier Layer)
- Increases total DFT to enhance barrier protection.
- Often high-build epoxy (HB epoxy) with micaceous iron oxide (MIO) for tortuous-path moisture resistance.
- **DFT per coat:** **100 to 150 $\mu m$**.

### Step 4: Tie Coat / Intermediate Coat
- Ensures chemical compatibility between the anti-corrosive epoxy and the topcoat.
- Prevents **delamination** and **intercoat failure**.
- Usually a thin (25 to 50 $\mu m$) epoxy or polyurethane intermediate.

### Step 5: Finish Coat / Anti-Fouling (The Functional Skin)
- **For Underwater Hull:** Anti-fouling paint (contains biocides like cuprous oxide $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$, copper pyrithione, zinc pyrithione, or modern silicone/fluoropolymer foul-release coatings).
- **For Topsides & Superstructure:** Polyurethane finish (UV-resistant, weather-proof, glossy).
- **For Decks:** Epoxy anti-skid with aggregate (aluminium oxide, quartz, or carborundum).
- **DFT:** **50 to 100 $\mu m$**.

## 2.2 Areas of a Ship and Their Specific Paint Systems

| **Area of Ship** | **Environmental Exposure** | **Recommended Paint System** | **Typical Total DFT** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Underwater Hull (Bottom) | Constant seawater immersion + biofouling | Zinc primer + Epoxy build + Anti-fouling | 250 – 400 $\mu m$ |
| Boot-Top Zone | Alternate wet/dry (tidal) | Epoxy + Strong anti-fouling | 300 – 450 $\mu m$ |
| Topside (Above waterline hull) | Salt spray, UV, mechanical | Epoxy primer + Polyurethane finish | 200 – 300 $\mu m$ |
| Superstructure | Heavy UV, weather | Epoxy primer + Polyurethane finish | 150 – 250 $\mu m$ |
| Main Deck | Foot traffic, cargo, oil | Epoxy primer + Epoxy anti-skid finish | 300 – 500 $\mu m$ |
| Ballast Tanks (PSPC) | Alternate wet/dry, structural | Pure epoxy (zinc-free or zinc-rich) | ≥ 320 $\mu m$ |
| Engine Room | Heat, oil, chemicals | Alkyd or epoxy heat-resistant | 100 – 200 $\mu m$ |
| Cargo Holds (bulk carriers) | Abrasion from cargo impact | Epoxy abrasion-resistant | 200 – 300 $\mu m$ |

## 2.3 Paint Application Methods

| **Method** | **Mechanism** | **Best For** | **Limitations** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brush** | Manual bristle application | Small areas, touch-ups, primer stripe coats | Slow, uneven film thickness |
| **Roller** | Cylinder application | Flat large surfaces, decks | Limited to flat areas |
| **Conventional Spray** | Compressed air atomises paint | General finishing | High overspray loss (~30-40%) |
| **Airless Spray** | Hydraulic pressure atomises paint (no air mixing) | High-build epoxy, large hull areas | Equipment cost; safety risk from high pressure |
| **Electrostatic Spray** | Charged paint particles attracted to grounded substrate | Complex shapes, reduced overspray | Requires conductive primers |
| **Plural Component Spray** | Two-component paint mixed at nozzle | Fast-curing polyurethanes, epoxies | Expensive equipment |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Theoretical Paint Coverage | $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Volume Solids \%} \times 10}{\text{DFT}}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{L}$ | Estimating paint quantity required |
| Wet Film Thickness (WFT) | $\text{WFT} = \dfrac{\text{DFT}}{\text{Volume Solids \%}} \times 100$ | $\mu m$ | Setting spray gun during application |
| Spreading Rate | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{\text{Solid Content \%} \times \text{Paint Density}}{\text{DFT}}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{L}$ | Practical paint consumption estimate |
| Paint Quantity Required | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Area} \times \text{DFT}}{\text{Volume Solids \%} \times 10}$ | Litres | Procurement planning |
| Cathodic Protection Current (related to anti-fouling) | $I = i_a \times A$ where $i_a$ = anodic current density, $A$ = anode area | Amperes | Sacrificial anode sizing context |
| Hull Roughness Penalty (fuel loss) | $\Delta P \propto \overline{k}^{1/3}$ where $\overline{k}$ = average roughness | — | Justifies smooth anti-fouling finish |
| Absolute Value (use $\vert$ in LaTeX) | $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ | — | Table-safe notation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A well-applied anti-fouling system can reduce hull friction by **5 to 15%**, translating to **8 to 20%** fuel savings. This is why painting is treated as a **performance-critical discipline** and not just a cosmetic one. Classification societies levy heavy penalties for paint system non-compliance.

## 2.5 Key Standards & Regulatory Framework

- **ISO 12944:** Corrosion protection of steel structures by protective paint systems (defines corrosivity categories C1 to CX).
- **ISO 8501-1:** Surface preparation grades (Sa, St, etc.).
- **ISO 8503:** Surface roughness comparators.
- **IMO PSPC (Resolution MSC.215(82)):** Performance Standard for Protective Coatings — mandatory for all new ships since 2008.
- **IACS UR Z17 / Z23:** Unified Requirements for coating systems.
- **Classification Society Paint Schedules:** LR, DNV, ABS, BV, IRS each publish their own approved paint manufacturer lists.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Estimating Paint Quantity for a Ship's Underwater Hull

> **Problem:** A bulk carrier has an underwater hull surface area of **4500 m²**. The paint system consists of:
> - 1 coat of zinc-rich epoxy primer at **DFT = 75 $\mu m$**, Volume Solids = **65%**
> - 2 coats of epoxy build at **DFT = 125 $\mu m$** each, Volume Solids = **70%**
> - 1 coat of anti-fouling at **DFT = 100 $\mu m$**, Volume Solids = **55%**
>
> An application loss factor of **30%** is assumed. Calculate the total litres of paint required.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Recall the paint quantity formula:

$$
Q = \frac{\text{Area} \times \text{DFT}}{\text{Volume Solids \%} \times 10}
$$

This formula arises because:
- $1 \text{ L of paint} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ }\mu m \times 1 \text{ m}^2 = 10^6 \text{ }\mu m \cdot \text{m}^2$ per litre (of $100\%$ solids).
- Hence, the volume needed for a $\text{DFT} = d$ film on area $A$ is $\frac{A \times d}{10^6}$ litres of pure solids.
- Since only $\text{VS}\%$ of the paint becomes solid, we divide by $\text{VS}/100$.

**Step 2:** Compute volume of solid required (ignoring losses).

For primer:

$$
Q_{\text{primer, solid}} = \frac{4500 \times 75}{65 \times 10} = \frac{337500}{650} = 519.23 \text{ L (of solids equivalent)}
$$

For epoxy build (2 coats):

$$
Q_{\text{build, solid}} = 2 \times \frac{4500 \times 125}{70 \times 10} = 2 \times \frac{562500}{700} = 2 \times 803.57 = 1607.14 \text{ L}
$$

For anti-fouling:

$$
Q_{\text{AF, solid}} = \frac{4500 \times 100}{55 \times 10} = \frac{450000}{550} = 818.18 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3:** Total solid-equivalent volume:

$$
Q_{\text{total, solid}} = 519.23 + 1607.14 + 818.18 = 2944.55 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the application loss factor. A 30% loss means **70% efficiency**:

$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total, solid}}}{0.70} = \frac{2944.55}{0.70} = 4206.5 \text{ L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Approximately **4207 litres of paint** (combined across all coats) is required for the underwater hull of this bulk carrier. In procurement, this figure is rounded up by **5–10%** to allow for wastage, mixing losses, and re-work.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Wet Film Thickness (WFT) Setting for Spray Application

> **Problem:** A high-build epoxy with 78% volume solids is to be applied to achieve a DFT of 150 $\mu m$. What WFT should the applicator set using a wet film comb?

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the WFT formula:

$$
\text{WFT} = \frac{\text{DFT}}{\text{Volume Solids \%}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
\text{WFT} = \frac{150}{78} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio:

$$
\frac{150}{78} = 1.9231
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by 100:

$$
\text{WFT} = 192.31 \text{ }\mu m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The applicator should set the spray gun such that the WFT immediately after spraying measures approximately **192 $\mu m$** using a wet film comb. As solvents evaporate during curing, the film will shrink to the target **150 $\mu m$ DFT**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Surface Preparation Method

| **Method** | **Initial Cost** | **Speed** | **Surface Quality** | **Dust Level** | **Best Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hand Tool (St 2/St 3) | Very Low | Slow | Low–Medium | Low | Touch-ups, small patches |
| Power Tool (St 3) | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | Repair yards, small areas |
| Dry Abrasive Blast (Sa 2.5) | Medium | Fast | High | **Very High** | New-building halls |
| Wet Abrasive Blast | Medium | Medium | High | Low | In-situ maintenance, port work |
| Ultra-High Pressure Water Jet (>250 MPa) | High | Medium | Very High (Sa 3) | None | In-service hull maintenance |
| Acid Pickling | Low (chemicals) | Slow | Variable | Liquid waste | Specific steel components |

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Paint System Selection by Ship Type

| **Ship Type** | **Critical Paint Concern** | **Primary Coating System** | **Key Driver** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oil Tanker | Cargo tank coating (PSPC) | Pure epoxy, phenolic epoxy | Chemical resistance to crude oil |
| Bulk Carrier | Cargo hold abrasion | Epoxy with abrasion additive | Impact resistance from iron ore/coal |
| Container Ship | Hull smoothness for fuel | Silicone foul-release + epoxy | Fuel efficiency, slow steaming |
| LNG Carrier | Cryogenic tank coating | Special cryogenic epoxy | $-163^\circ C$ LNG temperature |
| Naval Warship | Signature management | Radar-absorbing paint, anti-fouling | Stealth, magnetic signature |
| Fishing Vessel | Cost-effectiveness | Alkyd-based conventional system | Low capital outlay |
| Cruise Ship | Aesthetics & hygiene | Polyurethane with anti-graffiti | Passenger-facing appearance |
