# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) in the context of ship production planning. List its hierarchical levels from top to bottom.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** in shipbuilding is a hierarchical decomposition of the total vessel construction work into smaller, manageable, and assignable units. It enables parallel work, progress tracking, and clear responsibility allocation.

**Hierarchical Levels (top to bottom):**
1. **Vessel** (complete ship)
2. **Zone** (e.g., forward, amidships, aft — typically 6–8 zones)
3. **Unit** (large assembly group)
4. **Block** (3D structural assembly, 100–600 tonnes)
5. **Sub-block** (smaller 3D assembly)
6. **Panel** (2D plate with stiffeners, smallest WBS unit)

*(Full marks for clear hierarchy with ship-specific terminology.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is the Critical Path Method (CPM)? Why is it essential in ship production planning?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**CPM Definition:** The Critical Path Method is a project scheduling technique that identifies the longest sequence of dependent activities in a project network, determining the **minimum project duration** and the activities that cannot be delayed without delaying the entire project.

**Importance in Ship Production:**
- Identifies **bottleneck activities** (e.g., outfitting, block erection)
- Helps allocate **scarce resources** (cranes, skilled welders) to critical tasks
- Enables **delay impact analysis** before actual disruption
- Supports **contractual milestone commitment** with shipowners

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hierarchical Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** of a modern merchant ship built using the **block construction method**. Discuss how this WBS enables parallel production. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship construction project has the following activities. Draw the network and determine the **critical path** and project duration. Also compute the **total float** for each non-critical activity. **(7 marks)**

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (weeks) |
|---|---|---|
| A | — | 3 |
| B | — | 5 |
| C | A | 4 |
| D | B | 6 |
| E | C | 5 |
| F | D | 4 |
| G | E, F | 7 |
| H | G | 4 |

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — WBS and Block Construction (7 marks):**

**Block Construction Method:** In this method, the hull is divided into 3D blocks built in workshops (away from the berth) and later joined at the erection berth or dry dock. The WBS hierarchy is:

$$\text{Vessel} \rightarrow \text{Zone} \rightarrow \text{Unit} \rightarrow \text{Block} \rightarrow \text{Sub-block} \rightarrow \text{Panel}$$

**Block Diagram Description (7 marks distribution):**
- *Vessel level* (1 mark): Whole ship, owner-specified
- *Zone level* (1 mark): Typically 6–8 zones — fore, parallel body forward, engine room, parallel body aft, aft, superstructure
- *Unit level* (1 mark): Mega-assemblies combining 5–15 blocks
- *Block level* (2 marks): 3D units weighing 100–600 tonnes, built on shop floor
- *Panel/Sub-block level* (1 mark): 2D plate-stiffener assemblies forming building blocks
- *Parallel production benefit* (1 mark): Workshop blocks and berth erection proceed simultaneously, compressing build time by 30–40%

**Part (b) — CPM Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Paths:**

Path 1: A → C → E → G → H
Path 2: B → D → F → G → H

**Step 2 — Compute Path Durations:**

$$T_1 = 3 + 4 + 5 + 7 + 4 = 23 \text{ weeks}$$

$$T_2 = 5 + 6 + 4 + 7 + 4 = 26 \text{ weeks}$$

**Step 3 — Critical Path:**

$$T_{\text{CP}} = \max(23, 26) = 26 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Critical Path} = B \rightarrow D \rightarrow F \rightarrow G \rightarrow H}$$

**Step 4 — Float Calculation for Non-Critical Activity C and E:**

For Activity C (on Path 1):
- Path 1 duration = 23 weeks
- Difference = $26 - 23 = 3$ weeks
- Float for C and E (both on Path 1) = **3 weeks**

**Interpretation:** Activities A, C, E can each be delayed up to 3 weeks without delaying the project. The critical activities (B, D, F, G, H) require **zero-delay management** and maximum resource priority.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **4M framework** of resource planning in a shipyard. Discuss how each M is balanced during the production planning of a 30,000 DWT bulk carrier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has principal dimensions $L = 170$ m, $B = 28$ m, $D = 15$ m. Using the empirical formula $W = C \cdot L \cdot (B + D)$ with $C = 0.045$, calculate:
   (i) The hull steel weight.
   (ii) The total steel required including 20% outfitting steel.
   (iii) The raw steel to be procured assuming 88% utilization.
   (iv) If the standard man-hour rate is 45 man-hours per tonne and the yard employs 200 workers working 8 hours/day, how many working days are required for steel work alone? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 4M Framework (7 marks):**

The **4M framework** is a resource-planning model originating from Japanese shipbuilding (Toyota Production System adapted). For a 30,000 DWT bulk carrier:

**1. Man (Manpower):** (2 marks)
- Welders (largest share — ~40% of workforce)
- Fitters, plumbers, electricians, painters
- Engineers and supervisors
- **Planning action:** Trade-wise man-hour loading aligned to zone/block schedule

**2. Material:** (2 marks)
- Steel plates, sections, pipes, valves, electrical cables
- **Planning action:** JIT procurement linked to block erection dates; stock yard turnover optimization

**3. Machine:** (1.5 marks)
- Goliath cranes (300–1000T capacity), CNC plate cutting, welding manipulators
- **Planning action:** Equipment schedule synchronized to avoid crane conflicts

**4. Money/Space:** (1.5 marks)
- Berth allocation, workshop space, cash flow tied to milestone payments
- **Planning action:** Linear berth schedule for parallel hull builds

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Hull Steel Weight:** (2 marks)

$$W = C \cdot L \cdot (B + D)$$

$$W = 0.045 \times 170 \times (28 + 15)$$

$$W = 0.045 \times 170 \times 43$$

$$W = 7.65 \times 43 = 328.95 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(ii) Total Steel with 20% Outfitting:** (1.5 marks)

$$W_{\text{total}} = 328.95 \times 1.20 = 394.74 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(iii) Raw Steel Procurement at 88% Utilization:** (1.5 marks)

$$W_{\text{raw}} = \frac{394.74}{0.88} = 448.57 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(iv) Working Days Required:** (2 marks)

Total man-hours needed:

$$MH_{\text{total}} = 394.74 \times 45 = 17,763.3 \text{ man-hours}$$

Available man-hours per day with 200 workers:

$$MH_{\text{day}} = 200 \times 8 = 1,600 \text{ man-hours/day}$$

Working days:

$$D = \frac{17,763.3}{1,600} = 11.10 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 12 \text{ working days}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 20% outfitting addition** in part (ii) — costs 1.5 marks
> 2. **Dividing instead of multiplying** by 1.20 in total steel — common error
> 3. **In part (iv)**, students often forget to convert **per worker per day** correctly: 200 workers × 8 hours = 1,600 man-hours/day, not 200 hours
> 4. **Rounding errors:** Always round UP the final day count (11.10 → 12 days), as partial days are not productive in practice
> 5. **For CPM questions:** Many students calculate path durations but forget to **explicitly state which path is critical** — KTU examiner awards 2 marks for clear identification

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Revision Checklist — Production Management Planning**

- ✅ **WBS hierarchy (memorize order):** Vessel → Zone → Unit → Block → Sub-block → Panel
- ✅ **Block construction is the default modern method** — 90%+ of commercial ships
- ✅ **Critical Path = longest path** in the network; activities on it have **zero float**
- ✅ **Float** = $LS - ES$ (or $LF - EF$); represents delay tolerance
- ✅ **4M Framework:** Man, Material, Machine, Money/Space
- ✅ **Six Standard Milestones:** Steel Cutting → Keel Laying → Block Erection → Launching → Trials → Delivery
- ✅ **Empirical Steel Weight Formula:** $W = C \cdot L \cdot (B + D)$ with $C$ ranging 0.035–0.06 depending on ship type
- ✅ **Steel density** for volume-to-weight conversion: $\rho_s = 7.85 \, t/m^3$
- ✅ **Standard man-hour rate:** 30–60 MH per tonne of steel
- ✅ **Productive hours per worker per year** in shipyards: ~1,800 hours
- ✅ **Berth Occupancy Ratio (BOR)** is a key yard efficiency metric
- ✅ **JIT (Just-In-Time)** material flow is now industry standard — reduces stockyard cost
- ✅ **Liquidated Damages (LD)** clauses penalize delays at 0.1–0.3% of contract value per day
- ✅ **Gantt charts** for visual scheduling; **CPM** for critical path; **PERT** for uncertainty
- ✅ Always quote **ship-specific examples** in answers (bulk carrier, tanker, container ship) to earn full KTU marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview — Ship Design ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ship Design** is the systematic engineering process of translating an owner's operational requirement into a complete technical specification of a vessel, encompassing hull form geometry, structural arrangement, machinery selection, systems integration, and regulatory compliance. It is a multi-objective optimization problem constrained by hydrodynamics, structural mechanics, economics, and statutory rules. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, ship design refers to the **iterative sequence of activities** that begins with the owner's mission statement (cargo, route, speed, capacity) and terminates with a fully dimensioned lines plan, general arrangement, structural drawing set, and specification document suitable for shipyard construction. ## 1.2 Phases of Ship Design (KTU Syllabus Mapping) The design process is conventionally partitioned into **four sequential phases**: | Phase | Output | Designer | |---|---|---| | **Conceptual / Feasibility Design** | Feasibility report, choice of principal dimensions | Owner + Naval Architect | | **Preliminary / Contract Design** | General Arrangement (GA), Lines Plan, Specifications, Pricing | Design Office / Shipyard | | **Detailed / Functional Design** | Structural drawings, system diagrams, workshop documentation | Shipyard Design Department | | **Production / Construction Design** | Cutting plans, assembly sequences, work instructions | Production Engineering | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Building" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of designing a ship like designing a **multi-storey floating apartment that must also move efficiently through water**. Before construction, you must decide the *footprint* (length, beam), the *height* (depth), the *number of floors* (decks), the *lift capacity* (cargo gear), the *MEP systems* (pipes, electrical, HVAC), and finally — because it floats — the *underwater hull shape* (like designing a submarine hull that surfaces only at the top). Just as a civil engineer must balance **loads, materials, aesthetics, codes, and budget** for a building, a naval architect must balance **buoyancy, resistance, stability, structural strength, classification rules, and commercial return** for a vessel. ## 1.4 Fundamental Design Constraints Every ship design is bounded by **four universally applicable constraints**: - **Physical Constraints** — Gravity, buoyancy, wave action, hydrostatic pressure. - **Statutory Constraints** — SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, Flag State regulations. - **Classification Constraints** — Rules of IACS societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, BV, ABS, ClassNK). - **Commercial Constraints** — Capital cost, operating cost (OPEX), cargo earning, return on investment. ## 1.5 The Key Performance Triangle > [!IMPORTANT] > The three apexes of naval architectural design are **Speed**, **Capacity (Deadweight)**, and **Resistance (Power)**. Improving any one apex typically worsens at least one of the other two — this is the **design trade-off paradox**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Design Spiral (Watson–Gillmer Concept) The classical **Design Spiral** formalizes the iterative nature of ship design. The naval architect starts at the centre with a rough estimate and spirals outward, refining each parameter as the constraints tighten. **Step-by-step logic of the Design Spiral:** - **Step 1 — Define the Mission:** Specify cargo type, route, service speed, and annual trading pattern. - **Step 2 — Estimate Principal Dimensions:** Length $L_{PP}$, Breadth $B$, Depth $D$, Draft $T$. - **Step 3 — Compute Displacement:** Use estimated volume to obtain displacement $\Delta$. - **Step 4 — Estimate Resistance & Power:** Apply Holtrop or Hollenbach methods. - **Step 5 — Check Stability:** GM, GZ curve, range of positive stability. - **Step 6 — Iterate:** If any check fails, modify dimensions and **return to Step 2**. - **Step 7 — Finalize:** Lock dimensions, freeze GA, freeze lines plan. ## 2.2 Principal Dimensions and Their Ratios The **form of the hull** is governed by six non-dimensional ratios that act as design knobs: $$ \frac{L}{B}, \quad \frac{B}{T}, \quad \frac{D}{T}, \quad \frac{C_B}, \quad \frac{C_P}, \quad \frac{C_M} $$ Where: - $L/B$ → **Length-to-beam ratio** (slenderness; typically 5.5 to 8.0) - $B/T$ → **Beam-to-draft ratio** (typically 2.0 to 3.5) - $D/T$ → **Depth-to-draft ratio** (typically 1.1 to 1.5) - $C_B$ → **Block coefficient** - $C_P$ → **Prismatic coefficient** - $C_M$ → **Midship section coefficient** ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Design Module > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formulas** — Memorize these for KTU University Exam. They appear frequently in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Displacement** | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes (t) | Mass of water displaced | | 2 | **Deadweight (DWT)** | $DWT = \Delta - LWT$ | tonnes | Total variable load | | 3 | **Block Coefficient** | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Hull fullness indicator | | 4 | **Prismatic Coefficient** | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}}$ | dimensionless | Distribution of volume along L | | 5 | **Midship Coefficient** | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Cross-section fullness | | 6 | **Waterplane Coefficient** | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L_{PP} \cdot B}$ | dimensionless | Loading area efficiency | | 7 | **Froude Number** | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{PP}}}$ | dimensionless | Wave-making similarity | | 8 | **EHP–THP Relation** | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ (metric HP) | kW / HP | Resistance × speed | | 9 | **Propulsive Coeff.** | $\eta_D = \dfrac{EHP}{SHP}$ | dimensionless | Hull + propeller efficiency | | 10 | **Required SHP** | $SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_D}$ | kW / HP | Brake power needed | | 11 | **Reserve Buoyancy** | $RB = 100 \cdot \dfrac{V_{aboveWL}}{\nabla}$ | % | Safety against sinking | | 12 | **GM (Transverse)** | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | metres | Initial stability lever | | 13 | **BM** | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | metres | Stiffness of waterplane | | 14 | **Freeboard** | $FB = D - T$ | metres | Vertical safety margin | | 15 | **Slenderness Ratio** | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{PP}}{\nabla^{1/3}}$ | dimensionless | Hull slenderness | ## 2.4 Coefficients — Real-World Physical Meaning > [!IMPORTANT] > - **$C_B$ close to 1.0** → Full hull (tankers, bulkers) — high capacity, lower speed. > - **$C_B$ close to 0.5** → Fine hull (frigates, container ships) — low resistance, high speed. > - **$C_P$ close to 1.0** → Parallel middle body (efficient for cargo). > - **$C_P$ close to 0.55** → Fine ends (warships, sailing yachts). ## 2.5 Design Constraints — Regulatory Framework A naval architect's design must simultaneously satisfy: - **Load Line Convention** — Minimum freeboard based on $L$, type, and zone. - **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** — Subdivision, life-saving, fire protection. - **MARPOL** — Pollution prevention (oily water, sewage, garbage). - **Classification Society Rules** — Scantlings (plate thickness, frame spacing). - **Flag State & Port State** — Local operational requirements. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Displacement from Hull Geometry We begin from first principles. Archimedes' principle states that a floating body displaces a volume of water whose weight equals the body weight. **Starting statement (given):** The hull encloses a submerged volume $\nabla$ (cubic metres). **Step 1 — Express the buoyancy force.** $$ F_B = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \nabla $$ Where $\rho_w$ is the **density of seawater (typically 1.025 t/m³)** and $g$ is gravitational acceleration. **Step 2 — Equate buoyancy to weight at equilibrium.** $$ W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \nabla $$ **Step 3 — Convert to mass units (tonnes) by dividing by $g$.** $$ \Delta = \rho_w \cdot \nabla $$ **Step 4 — Substitute sea water density.** $$ \Delta = 1.025 \cdot \nabla $$ This is the **fundamental displacement equation** used in every design office worldwide. ## 3.2 Derivation of Block Coefficient from Geometry **Starting statement (definition):** Block coefficient $C_B$ is the ratio of the actual submerged volume to the volume of a rectangular box enclosing the waterline length, beam, and draft. **Step 1 — Write the volume of the bounding box.** $$ \nabla_{box} = L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T $$ **Step 2 — Write the displacement volume definition.** $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $C_B$.** $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ **Step 4 — Derive the relationship between $C_B$, $C_P$, and $C_M$.** By definition: $$ C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} \quad \text{and} \quad C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} $$ Multiplying the two: $$ C_P \cdot C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ **Step 5 — Equate to the box relation.** $$ C_B = C_P \cdot C_M $$ This identity is the **most-tested relationship** in KTU Part A questions on form coefficients. ## 3.3 Derivation of EHP → SHP Relationship **Step 1 — Define Effective Horsepower (EHP).** $$ EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} \quad (\text{metric HP, with } R_T \text{ in kgf and } V \text{ in m/s}) $$ In SI units: $$ EHP = R_T \cdot V \quad (\text{Watts, when } R_T \text{ in N and } V \text{ in m/s}) $$ **Step 2 — Define Shaft Horsepower (SHP).** SHP is the power delivered to the propeller shaft by the main engine. **Step 3 — Define Propulsive Coefficient.** $$ \eta_D = \frac{EHP}{SHP} $$ This bundles together **hull efficiency ($\eta_H$), propeller efficiency ($\eta_O$), and rotational efficiency ($\eta_R$)**: $$ \eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R $$ **Step 4 — Solve for required SHP.** $$ SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} $$ Typical values: $\eta_D \approx 0.55$ to $0.65$ for single-screw merchant vessels. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Examination Style > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A bulk carrier has $L_{PP} = 180$ m, $B = 28$ m, $T = 10.5$ m, and $C_B = 0.78$. Compute (a) Displacement, (b) Wetted Surface Area using $S = 1.7 \cdot L \cdot T + \nabla / T$ (Schneekluth approximation). **Step 1 — Compute displaced volume.** $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T $$ $$ \nabla = 0.78 \cdot 180 \cdot 28 \cdot 10.5 $$ $$ \nabla = 0.78 \cdot 52920 = 41,277.6 \ \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Compute displacement in tonnes.** $$ \Delta = 1.025 \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot 41277.6 = 42,309.5 \ \text{t} $$ **Step 3 — Compute wetted surface area.** $$ S = 1.7 \cdot L \cdot T + \frac{\nabla}{T} $$ $$ S = 1.7 \cdot 180 \cdot 10.5 + \frac{41277.6}{10.5} $$ $$ S = 3213 + 3931.2 = 7144.2 \ \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Sanity check.** For a bulk carrier at $C_B = 0.78$, these values fall in the expected commercial band ($\nabla$ typically 30,000–60,000 m³ for Panamax class). ✔ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Coefficient Trade-Off Table | Hull Type | Typical $C_B$ | Typical $C_P$ | Typical $C_M$ | Speed (knots) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Ultra Large Tanker (VLCC) | 0.82 – 0.85 | 0.82 – 0.85 | 0.99 | 15 – 16 | | Bulk Carrier | 0.78 – 0.82 | 0.78 – 0.82 | 0.98 | 13 – 15 | | Container Ship | 0.55 – 0.68 | 0.62 – 0.75 | 0.92 | 20 – 25 | | General Cargo | 0.60 – 0.72 | 0.65 – 0.78 | 0.94 | 14 – 18 | | Frigate / Corvette | 0.45 – 0.55 | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.85 | 25 – 35 | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Ship Design Spiral — Mermaid Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Step A: Mission Statement
Cargo, Route, Speed, Capacity] --> nodeB[Step B: Estimate Principal Dimensions
Lpp, B, D, T] nodeB --> nodeC[Step C: Compute Displacement
Delta equals rho times Nab] nodeC --> nodeD[Step D: Form Coefficients
Cb, Cp, Cm, Cwp] nodeD --> nodeE[Step E: Resistance Estimate
Holtrop or Hollenbach] nodeE --> nodeF[Step F: Powering
SHP equals EHP over etaD] nodeF --> nodeG[Step G: Stability Check
GM, GZ, Range] nodeG --> nodeH[Step H: Structural Scantlings
Classification Rules] nodeH --> nodeI{Step I: Pass All Checks?} nodeI -- No --> nodeB nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ[Step J: Freeze GA, Lines Plan, Specs] nodeJ --> nodeK[Step K: Detail Design and Production] ``` ## 4.2 Hull Form Geometry — Lines Plan Components ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Profile View
Sheer Plan - Side View] --> nodeC[Lines Plan Integration] nodeB[Body Plan
Transverse Sections] --> nodeC nodeD[Half Breadth Plan
Horizontal Sections] --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeE[3D Hull Form
Reconstructed Geometry] nodeC --> nodeF[Hydrostatic Calculations] nodeC --> nodeG[Stability Cross Curves] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Design Constraint Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Owner Mission Statement] --> nodeB[Conceptual Design] nodeB --> nodeC[Preliminary Design] nodeC --> nodeD[Detailed Design] nodeD --> nodeE[Production] nodeA --> constraintA[Commercial Constraint
Cost and ROI] nodeA --> constraintB[Statutory Constraint
SOLAS MARPOL Loadline] nodeA --> constraintC[Classification Constraint
IACS Society Rules] nodeA --> constraintD[Physical Constraint
Hydrostatics and Waves] constraintA --> nodeB constraintB --> nodeB constraintC --> nodeB constraintD --> nodeB ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Power Transmission Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Main Engine] -->|SHP| nodeB[Gearbox and Shaft Line] nodeB -->|DHP| nodeC[Propeller] nodeC -->|THP| nodeD[Thrust on Hull] nodeD -->|Overcomes| nodeE[Total Resistance RT] nodeE -->|Yields| nodeF[Vessel Speed V] nodeG[Propulsive Efficiency etaD] -.-> nodeB nodeG -.-> nodeC ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Office Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Marketing and Tendering Dept] -->|Inquiries| nodeB[Basic Design Office] nodeB -->|Specs and GA| nodeC[Contract and Project Dept] nodeC -->|Signed Contract| nodeD[Detailed Design Office] nodeD -->|Drawing Set| nodeE[Production Planning] nodeE -->|Cutting Plans| nodeF[Workshop and Yard] nodeF -->|As-built Feedback| nodeD nodeB -.->|Hydrostatic Inputs| nodeG[Hydrodynamic Section] nodeB -.->|Weight Inputs| nodeH[Weight Control Section] nodeB -.->|Cost Inputs| nodeI[Cost Estimation Section] nodeG -.-> nodeB nodeH -.-> nodeB nodeI -.-> nodeB ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Block Coefficient and explain its significance in ship design.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Block coefficient $C_B$ is defined as the ratio of the displaced volume $\nabla$ to the product of length between perpendiculars $L_{PP}$, breadth $B$, and draft $T$. $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ **Significance:** It is the single most important indicator of hull fullness. A high $C_B$ (≥ 0.80) indicates a full-bodied hull suitable for tankers and bulk carriers carrying cargo at low speeds. A low $C_B$ (≤ 0.55) indicates a fine hull suited for fast vessels like container ships and naval combatants. For merchant ships, $C_B$ typically ranges from 0.55 to 0.85. ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **What is the Design Spiral? Why is ship design described as iterative?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Design Spiral is a graphical representation proposed by **D.G.M. Watson and J.A. Gillmer** that shows the sequence of activities in ship design — moving from the owner's mission statement through displacement estimation, resistance calculation, stability checking, and structural design in a spiral fashion. It is iterative because each parameter depends on others: changing the length affects displacement, which affects resistance, which affects power, which affects weight, which again affects displacement. Hence, the designer must circle back repeatedly until all constraints are satisfied. The spiral grows outward representing increasing refinement and detail until the design converges to a final solution. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU ESE Part B carries **Module Internal Choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below. Students answer EITHER set. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **various phases of ship design** in detail. What are the deliverables of each phase? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A container ship has $L_{PP} = 250$ m, $B = 32$ m, $T = 12$ m, $C_B = 0.62$. The ship's design speed is 24 knots. Calculate the **(i) Displacement volume**, **(ii) Displacement in tonnes**, and **(iii) Froude Number**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Introduction (1 mark):** Ship design is divided into four phases — Conceptual, Preliminary (Contract), Detailed (Functional), and Production Design. **Phase 1 — Conceptual Design (2 marks):** The owner states the operational requirement. The naval architect prepares 2–3 alternative designs with estimated principal dimensions, displacement, speed, and approximate cost. Deliverables: Feasibility Report, Concept Sketches. **Phase 2 — Preliminary / Contract Design (2 marks):** Selected concept is refined. General Arrangement, Lines Plan, Hydrostatics, Powering Estimate, Stability Booklet, and Specifications are produced. Deliverables: Contract Drawings, Specification Book, Pricing. **Phase 3 — Detailed Design (1 mark):** Structural drawings, system diagrams (piping, electrical, HVAC), workshop fabrication drawings. Deliverables: Full Drawing Set, Bill of Materials. **Phase 4 — Production Design (1 mark):** Cutting plans, assembly sequences, work instructions. Deliverables: Production Plan. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $L_{PP} = 250$ m, $B = 32$ m, $T = 12$ m, $C_B = 0.62$, $V = 24$ knots. **(i) Displacement Volume (2 marks):** $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T $$ $$ \nabla = 0.62 \cdot 250 \cdot 32 \cdot 12 $$ $$ \nabla = 0.62 \cdot 96000 $$ $$ \nabla = 59{,}520 \ \text{m}^3 $$ **(ii) Displacement in Tonnes (2 marks):** $$ \Delta = \rho_w \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot 59520 $$ $$ \Delta = 60{,}983 \ \text{t} $$ **(iii) Froude Number (3 marks):** Convert speed to m/s: $$ V = 24 \ \text{knots} = 24 \cdot 0.5144 = 12.346 \ \text{m/s} $$ Apply Froude formula: $$ F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{PP}}} = \frac{12.346}{\sqrt{9.81 \cdot 250}} $$ $$ F_n = \frac{12.346}{\sqrt{2452.5}} = \frac{12.346}{49.52} $$ $$ F_n = 0.249 $$ **Result:** $F_n \approx 0.25$ indicates the vessel is operating in the **displacement–planing transition regime**, typical of fast container ships. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **(a)** Define the **principal form coefficients** ($C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$) and derive the relationship between them. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a bulk carrier, the displacement is 45,000 tonnes at a draft of 11 m. If the block coefficient is 0.80 and $L/B = 6.5$, calculate the **(i) Length between perpendiculars**, **(ii) Breadth**, and **(iii) Wetted Surface Area** using Schneekluth's formula $S = 1.7 \cdot L \cdot T + \nabla / T$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Definitions (3 marks):** **Block Coefficient $C_B$:** $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ It compares the displaced volume to the bounding rectangular box. **Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$:** $$ C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} $$ It indicates how the volume is distributed along the length. A high $C_P$ (≥ 0.80) means parallel middle body; a low $C_P$ (≤ 0.60) means fine ends. **Midship Section Coefficient $C_M$:** $$ C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} $$ It is the fullness of the maximum cross-section. Typically 0.90–0.99 for merchant vessels. **Derivation of Relationship (4 marks):** Starting from $C_P$: $$ C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} $$ Multiply both sides by $C_M$: $$ C_P \cdot C_M = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} \cdot \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} $$ Cancel $A_M$: $$ C_P \cdot C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ The right-hand side is, by definition, $C_B$: $$ C_B = C_P \cdot C_M $$ This is the **master identity** among form coefficients. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $\Delta = 45000$ t, $T = 11$ m, $C_B = 0.80$, $L/B = 6.5$. **Step 1 — Compute displaced volume.** $$ \nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_w} = \frac{45000}{1.025} = 43{,}902.4 \ \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Use $C_B$ definition.** $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} \Rightarrow L \cdot B = \frac{\nabla}{C_B \cdot T} $$ $$ L \cdot B = \frac{43902.4}{0.80 \cdot 11} = \frac{43902.4}{8.8} = 4989.0 \ \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Apply $L/B = 6.5$.** $$ L = 6.5 \cdot B $$ Substituting: $$ 6.5 \cdot B^2 = 4989.0 $$ $$ B^2 = 767.5 $$ $$ B = 27.7 \ \text{m} $$ **Step 4 — Compute $L$.** $$ L = 6.5 \cdot 27.7 = 180.1 \ \text{m} \approx 180 \ \text{m} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Wetted Surface Area (3 marks).** $$ S = 1.7 \cdot L \cdot T + \frac{\nabla}{T} $$ $$ S = 1.7 \cdot 180 \cdot 11 + \frac{43902.4}{11} $$ $$ S = 3366 + 3991.1 $$ $$ S = 7357.1 \ \text{m}^2 $$ **Final Answers:** - **(i) $L_{PP} \approx 180$ m** - **(ii) $B \approx 27.7$ m** - **(iii) $S \approx 7357$ m²** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the 1.025 factor** for seawater density in displacement calculations — instant loss of 1 mark. > 2. **Unit mismatch in Froude Number** — must convert knots to m/s. Mixing up units is the #1 reason students get $F_n$ wrong. > 3. **Confusing $\nabla$ (volume) with $\Delta$ (mass)** — examiners differentiate sharply between m³ and tonnes. > 4. **Skipping units in final answers** — every numerical answer must carry units (m, m³, t, knots). > 5. **Writing $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ without derivation** — Examiner expects to see at least two algebraic lines for full marks. > 6. **Confusing $L_{OA}$ (Length Overall) with $L_{PP}$ (Length Between Perpendiculars)** — Lines Plan uses $L_{PP}$, GA drawing uses $L_{OA}$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Design** - **Design Phases (4):** Conceptual → Preliminary → Detailed → Production. - **Design Spiral:** Watson–Gillmer iterative concept. Centre = mission; Outer = finalized GA. - **Four Design Constraints:** Physical, Statutory, Classification, Commercial. - **Six Principal Dimensions:** $L_{PP}$, $B$, $D$, $T$, plus form coefficients. - **Master Identity:** $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ — must be memorized. - **Block Coefficient $C_B$:** Hull fullness indicator; 0.55 (fast) to 0.85 (full). - **Displacement:** $\Delta = 1.025 \cdot \nabla$ (tonnes, using seawater). - **Froude Number:** $F_n = V / \sqrt{g \cdot L_{PP}}$ — governs wave-making resistance. - **EHP / SHP / DHP / THP hierarchy:** $THP \geq EHP \geq DHP \geq SHP$ with efficiencies in between. - **Propulsive Coefficient:** $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R \approx 0.55$–$0.65$. - **GM Formula:** $GM = KB + BM - KG$ — basic stability check. - **Freeboard:** $FB = D - T$ — safety against sinking. - **Wetted Surface:** Schneekluth $S = 1.7LT + \nabla / T$ — used in friction resistance. - **Classification:** IACS societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, BV, ABS) write the structural rules. - **Regulatory Conventions:** SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, COLREG, STCW. - **Units convention:** Knots → multiply by 0.5144 to get m/s. - **Sea water density:** Use **1.025 t/m³**, not 1.0. - **Lines Plan:** Three views — Profile (Sheer), Body Plan, Half Breadth Plan.

topics.title: Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ship Production** in naval architecture refers to the systematic, industrial-scale sequence of operations through which a designed vessel is transformed from engineering drawings, raw steel plates, and structural sections into a fully outfitted, seaworthy ship ready for delivery. It encompasses every activity from material procurement, cutting, forming, sub-assembly, block erection, joining (welding), launching, and final outfitting to trials.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OENST724)** syllabus framework, the "Production" module covers the **modernized industrial methods** used in shipyards — primarily moving away from the obsolete "berth-building" technique to advanced **modular / block construction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2: Production**
> The module focuses on **Construction Methods (Longitudinal, Transverse, Isometric)**, **Sub-assembly & Block Construction**, **Shipyard Layout**, and **Production Planning** as they apply to modern steel-hulled merchant vessels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of building a ship the way you would build a giant **flat-pack IKEA furniture set** — but on an industrial scale and using **welded steel** instead of wood and screws.

**Real-world analogy:**
- **Old Method (Berth Construction)**: Imagine building a car from scratch directly on the driveway, working from the ground up. Slow, cramped, and weather-dependent.
- **Modern Method (Block Construction)**: Imagine manufacturing each major piece of the car (chassis, doors, roof, engine bay) as a **separate pre-finished module in different workshops**, and then assembling them on a final assembly line — fast, parallel work, weather-protected, and quality-controlled.

This shift from sequential on-berth work to **parallel pre-fabrication in workshops** is the heart of modern ship production.

### 1.3 Key Production Metrics (Bolded for KTU emphasis)

- **DWT (Deadweight Tonnage)** — Cargo-carrying capacity in tonnes.
- **LDT (Lightweight Deadweight Tonnage)** — Total weight of the empty steel structure of the ship.
- **CGT (Compensated Gross Tonnage)** — Standard industry productivity measure; 1 CGT ≈ **production output of a standard ship volume unit**.
- **Man-Hours per CGT** — A yard's efficiency indicator (lower = more productive).
- **Steel Conversion Factor** — Ratio of fabricated steel weight to raw steel purchased (typically **0.85 to 0.90** due to wastage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Shipyard Productivity Benchmark (2024):**
> A modern Korean/Chinese mega-yard completes approximately **30–50 CGT per worker per year**, whereas traditional European yards produce **8–15 CGT per worker per year**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Classical Hull Construction Methods

The way a ship is assembled depends on which structural member is erected **first**. This gives us three classical methods:

#### 2.1.1 Longitudinal Method (Longitudinal Over-Transverse)
- **Logic**: Longitudinal girders (stringers, longitudinal stiffeners) are erected **before** the transverse frames (ribs).
- **Why it exists**: Modern container ships and tankers have very long, fine hull forms where longitudinal strength is critical.
- **How it works**:
  1. The **keel plate and bottom longitudinals** are laid first.
  2. **Bottom shell plates** are fitted over them.
  3. **Side shell plates** with their longitudinals come next.
  4. Transverse frames are fitted last as secondary members.
- **Use case**: Bulk carriers, tankers, very long ships.

#### 2.1.2 Transverse Method (Transverse Over-Longitudinal)
- **Logic**: Transverse frames (ribs) are erected first; longitudinals are added later.
- **Use case**: Smaller vessels, fishing boats, naval auxiliaries, older designs.

#### 2.1.3 Isometric Method
- **Logic**: Both transverse and longitudinal members are erected **simultaneously and progressively** from the keel upwards.
- **Use case**: Mid-sized vessels, modern frigates.

### 2.2 Modern Block Construction (Modular Construction) — The Industrial Standard

This is **the most exam-relevant concept** in this module.

**Definition**: The hull is divided into **pre-defined blocks** (typically 15–25 m long, 15–25 m wide, weighing 100–600 tonnes each). Each block is fully fabricated, welded, painted, and partially outfitted in a workshop, then lifted by gantry crane onto the building dock/b slip for final joining.

**Typical Block Classification:**

| Block Type | Location on Ship | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Double Bottom Block** | Bottom of hull | Holds ballast, supports cargo |
| **Side Shell Block** | Port & Starboard sides | Watertight hull envelope |
| **Deck Block** | Top of hull | Supports deck cargo / superstructure |
| **Transverse Ring** | Full hull cross-section | Links side shells & bottom |
| **Superstructure Block** | Above main deck | Houses bridge, accommodation |
| **Engine Room Module** | Aft section | Pre-piped, pre-wired power plant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Concept — "Pre-Construction Equipping" (PCE) or "Pre-Outfitting":**
> Piping, cable trays, machinery foundations, and insulation are installed **inside the block while it is still at ground level in the workshop** — this saves massive amounts of work-at-height later.

### 2.3 Shipyard Layout — Functional Zoning

A modern shipyard is divided into **six functional zones** working in a logical flow:

1. **Steel Stockyard** — Raw plate and section storage.
2. **Steel Processing Shop** — Shot blasting, cutting, forming (press brake, line heating).
3. **Panel Fabrication Shop** — Sub-assemblies of plates + stiffeners.
4. **Block Assembly Shop** — Sub-blocks → Mega-blocks.
5. **Erection Berth / Building Dock** — Final joining of mega-blocks.
6. **Outfitting Quay** — Final painting, equipment installation, trials.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Block Weight Estimate** | $W_b = \rho_s \cdot V_b \cdot k_f$ | tonnes | Crane capacity planning |
| **Steel Conversion Factor** | $\eta_{sc} = \dfrac{W_{fab}}{W_{raw}}$ | dimensionless (0.85–0.90) | Material procurement budget |
| **Production Rate** | $P_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{blocks}}{T_{cycle}}$ | blocks/day | Yard throughput planning |
| **Man-Hours per Tonne** | $M_{ht} = \dfrac{H_{total}}{W_{steel}}$ | hours/tonne | Labor cost estimation |
| **CGT Productivity** | $\eta_{CGT} = \dfrac{CGT_{out}}{H_{total}}$ | CGT/worker-year | International yard benchmarking |
| **Launching Force (Slipway)** | $F_L = W \cdot \sin\theta + \mu \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta$ | kN | Slipway design |
| **Welding Heat Input** | $E = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | kJ/mm | Weld quality control |
| **Cutting Oxygen Consumption** | $V_{O_2} = k_c \cdot t_{plate}$ | m³/m of cut | Gas cost estimation |

**Where:**
- $\rho_s$ = density of ship steel = **7.85 t/m³**
- $V_b$ = block volume
- $k_f$ = fabrication factor (typically **1.05–1.15**, accounts for weld metal + stiffeners)
- $W$ = ship weight at launch
- $\theta$ = slipway angle
- $\mu$ = friction coefficient (~**0.03 with grease, 0.15 dry**)
- $V, I$ = arc voltage (V) and current (A)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/min)
- $k_c$ = oxygen cutting constant (≈ **0.08 m³/m per mm thickness**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility**: The **CGT metric** is the global currency of shipbuilding. South Korean mega-yards dominate world order books partly because they achieve ~**30–50 CGT/worker/year**, nearly double the global average.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Block Erection Cycle Time

**Problem Context:** A shipyard plans to erect **N = 60 blocks** for a Panamax bulk carrier. The gantry crane has a **single-shift operation, 8 hours/day, 5 days/week**. Each block requires: **lifting (1.5 h), alignment (2.0 h), tack-welding (3.5 h), and full welding (8.0 h)**. Determine the **minimum erection cycle time** and total project duration.

**Given:**
- $N = 60$ blocks
- Crane shift: 8 h/day
- Work week: 5 days/week
- Per-block time: $T_{lift} = 1.5\,\text{h}$, $T_{align} = 2.0\,\text{h}$, $T_{tack} = 3.5\,\text{h}$, $T_{weld} = 8.0\,\text{h}$

**Step 1 — Total work hours per block.**

$$
T_{block} = T_{lift} + T_{align} + T_{tack} + T_{weld}
$$

$$
T_{block} = 1.5 + 2.0 + 3.5 + 8.0
$$

$$
T_{block} = 15.0\ \text{hours/block}
$$

**Step 2 — Total crane hours required for entire hull.**

$$
H_{total} = N \cdot T_{block}
$$

$$
H_{total} = 60 \cdot 15.0
$$

$$
H_{total} = 900\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert crane hours to working days.**

$$
D_{work} = \dfrac{H_{total}}{H_{day}} = \dfrac{900}{8}
$$

$$
D_{work} = 112.5\ \text{working days}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert working days to calendar weeks.**

$$
W_{cal} = \dfrac{D_{work}}{5} = \dfrac{112.5}{5}
$$

$$
W_{cal} = 22.5\ \text{calendar weeks} \approx 5.2\ \text{months}
$$

**Step 5 — Add contingency & parallel pre-outfitting buffer.**

A standard shipyard contingency is **+15%** for rework, weather, and crane maintenance:

$$
W_{final} = W_{cal} \cdot (1 + k_{cont})
$$

$$
W_{final} = 22.5 \cdot 1.15
$$

$$
W_{final} \approx 25.9\ \text{calendar weeks} \approx 6.0\ \text{months}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is *only* the hull erection phase. Total ship build time (steel cutting → delivery) is typically **10–14 months for a Panamax bulk carrier** in a modern yard. Erection is just one-third of the total timeline.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

| Parameter | Longitudinal Method | Transverse Method | Isometric Method | Block Construction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed of Build** | Slow | Slowest | Medium | **Fastest** |
| **Structural Strength** | **Best (long. continuous)** | Adequate | Good | Excellent |
| **Suitability for Long Ships** | **Yes** | No | Limited | **Yes** |
| **Outfitting Work at Height** | High | High | High | **Minimized (PCE)** |
| **Weather Sensitivity** | High | High | High | **Low (workshop-based)** |
| **Capital Cost (Cranes)** | Low | Low | Low | **Very High** |
| **Work Parallelism** | Low | Low | Medium | **Very High** |
| **Modern Use** | Rare | Traditional | Naval | **Industry Standard** |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Steel Conversion Factor Calculation

**Problem:** A shipyard purchases **10,000 tonnes** of raw steel plates and sections. After cutting, edge preparation, and fabrication waste, the **finished fabricated steel weight** delivered to the erection berth is **8,650 tonnes**. Compute the steel conversion factor and identify the loss source.

$$
\eta_{sc} = \dfrac{W_{fab}}{W_{raw}} = \dfrac{8650}{10000}
$$

$$
\eta_{sc} = 0.865\ \text{or}\ 86.5\ \%
$$

**Loss source analysis:**

$$
W_{loss} = W_{raw} - W_{fab} = 10000 - 8650 = 1350\ \text{tonnes}
$$

Typical loss breakdown:

- **Cutting kerf & edge trim** ≈ 4 % → 400 t
- **Plate nesting inefficiency** ≈ 5 % → 500 t
- **Scrap (rejected plates, distortion)** ≈ 3 % → 300 t
- **Welding spatter & shrinkage** ≈ 1.5 % → 150 t

$$
\text{Total} = 4\% + 5\% + 3\% + 1.5\% = 13.5\% \quad \checkmark
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Shipyard Functional Block-Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Steel Stockyard] --> A2[Shot Blasting and Priming Shop]
    A2 --> A3[Cutting Shop NC Plasma and Oxy Fuel]
    A3 --> A4[Forming Shop Press Brake and Line Heating]
    A4 --> A5[Panel Fabrication Shop T Panels and Flat Panels]
    A5 --> A6[Sub Assembly Shop Small Stiffened Plates]
    A6 --> A7[Block Assembly Shop Mega Blocks 100 to 600 t]
    A7 --> A8[Pre Outfitting Zone Pipes Cables Insulation]
    A8 --> A9[Block Painting Shop]
    A9 --> A10[Erection Berth or Building Dock]
    A10 --> A11[Joinery of Mega Blocks Full Welding]
    A11 --> A12[Launching via Slipway or Floating Dock]
    A12 --> A13[Outfitting Quay Final Equipment]
    A13 --> A14[Sea Trials and Delivery]

    style A1 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A7 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A10 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A12 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A14 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Hierarchy Architecture — Hull Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Complete Ship Hull] --> H2[Mega Block Aft]
    H1 --> H3[Mega Block Midship]
    H1 --> H4[Mega Block Forward]
    H1 --> H5[Superstructure Module]

    H2 --> S1[Double Bottom Block]
    H2 --> S2[Side Shell Block Port]
    H2 --> S3[Side Shell Block Starboard]
    H2 --> S4[Deck Block]

    S1 --> P1[Stiffened Plate Panel]
    S1 --> P2[Flat Plate Panel]
    S1 --> P3[T Section Stiffener]

    H3 --> S5[Engine Room Module]
    S5 --> P4[Pre Piped Engine Foundation]
    S5 --> P5[Pre Cabled Control Console]
    S5 --> P6[Pre Insulated Bulkhead]

    style H1 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H2 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H3 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H4 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S1 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Construction Method Decision Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the L over B ratio of the ship?}
    Q1 -->|Greater than 6.0| M1[Use Longitudinal Method]
    Q1 -->|Between 5.0 and 6.0| M2[Use Isometric Method]
    Q1 -->|Less than 5.0| M3[Use Transverse Method]

    M1 --> F1[Add Block Construction Overlay]
    M2 --> F1
    M3 --> F1

    F1 --> F2{Modern Yard with Gantry Crane over 600 t?}
    F2 -->|Yes| F3[Full Block Construction Adopted]
    F2 -->|No| F4[Sub Assembly and Section Construction]

    style Q1 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F3 fill:#22c55e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> In exam answers, always draw the **shipyard material flow as a left-to-right block diagram**. Examiners award **2–3 marks** just for a clean, labeled flow diagram of the shipyard layout.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, OENST724]**
*Define **block construction** in shipbuilding. List **any four** advantages it offers over the traditional berth construction method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Block construction is a modern ship production method in which the hull is divided into a number of pre-defined large structural units (blocks) of 100–600 tonnes. Each block is fabricated, welded, painted, and pre-outfitted in a workshop, and later lifted and joined on the building dock.

**Advantages (0.375 each × 4 = 1.5 marks):**
1. **Parallel work** — multiple blocks fabricated simultaneously, drastically reducing total build time.
2. **Pre-outfitting (PCE)** — piping, cables, and insulation fitted at ground level inside the block, eliminating work-at-height.
3. **Improved quality** — workshop environment allows better welding, NDT, and dimensional control.
4. **Weather independence** — most work happens indoors, reducing delays from rain/wind.
5. **Higher productivity** — man-hours per tonne drop by 30–50% versus berth construction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, OENST724]**
*Differentiate between **longitudinal method** and **transverse method** of hull construction. State **one** ship type best suited for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Longitudinal Method | Transverse Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Member erected first** | Longitudinal girders (stringers) | Transverse frames (ribs) |
| **Structural behavior** | Continuous longitudinal strength | Distributed transverse stiffness |
| **Best suited vessel** | Long, fine ships — Bulk carriers, Tankers | Short, bluff ships — Tugs, Fishing vessels |

(1 mark for correct definition of each + 0.5 mark for ship type example = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Construction Methods & Block Production**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **isometric method of hull construction**. List **three advantages** and **two limitations**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The isometric method involves erecting **both transverse frames and longitudinal stiffeners simultaneously and progressively** from the keel plate upwards, working outward and upward on both sides of the centerline at the same time.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Keel plate is laid on the building slip blocks.
2. Bottom shell plating is fitted on either side.
3. Bottom longitudinals and bottom transverse frames are erected together.
4. Side shell plates, with their own longitudinals and transverse frames, are progressively raised.
5. Deck plating, sheer strake, and deck longitudinals are fitted last.
6. The work proceeds **in a balanced, mirror-image fashion** about the centerline to avoid hull distortion.

**Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
- Balanced loading of the slipway → less hull distortion.
- Faster than pure longitudinal or transverse method.
- Suitable for medium-length vessels (frigates, offshore supply vessels).

**Limitations (0.5 mark each):**
- Requires more skilled labor coordination on the berth.
- Less suited to very large or very long ships.

---

**(b)** Describe **modern block construction** in detail. Explain the **concept of pre-construction equipping (PCE)** and its impact on production efficiency. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Construction Workflow (3 marks):**
1. Hull is engineered and divided into **60–150 blocks** using 3D CAD nesting.
2. Steel plates are cut, formed, and welded into **panels** in workshops.
3. Panels are joined into **sub-assemblies**, then into **blocks** of 100–600 t.
4. Blocks are pre-painted, transported by **Self-Propelled Modular Transporters (SPMTs)** to the erection berth.
5. Mega-blocks are lifted by **gantry cranes (800–1200 t capacity)** and welded together.
6. Final outfitting, sea trials, and delivery.

**Pre-Construction Equipping (PCE) — 2 marks:**
PCE means installing **piping, electrical cabling, HVAC ducts, machinery foundations, insulation, and outfit** items *inside the block while it is still at workshop level*. This is done because:
- Work at ground level is faster, safer, and more accurate.
- Multiple trades can work in parallel.
- Final joining (welding) and outfitting no longer compete for the same space on the berth.

**Impact on Production Efficiency — 2 marks:**
- Reduces **total man-hours by 30–40%** compared to traditional berth outfitting.
- Shortens **build period by 25–35%**.
- Improves **weld quality** (less repair work after erection).
- Enables **year-round production** (workshop is enclosed, climate-controlled).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Shipyard Layout & Production Planning**

**(a)** Draw a **block diagram of a modern shipyard layout** showing the **flow of materials** from raw steel stockyard to sea trials. List the **functions of any four zones**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Student should draw a linear/sequential flow as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Zone Functions (4 marks — 1 each, any four):**
1. **Steel Stockyard** — Storage of raw plates, sections, and pipes.
2. **Steel Processing Shop** — Shot blasting, NC cutting, edge preparation.
3. **Panel Shop** — Fabrication of stiffened panels and T-panels.
4. **Block Assembly Shop** — Joining of panels into 3D blocks; heavy welding.
5. **Pre-Outfitting Zone** — Installation of machinery, piping, and cables inside blocks.
6. **Erection Berth** — Joining of mega-blocks using gantry crane.
7. **Outfitting Quay** — Final painting, equipment installation, mooring trials.

---

**(b)** A shipyard has ordered **12,000 tonnes** of raw steel for a newbuild. The expected **steel conversion factor is 0.88**. The yard operates **300 working days/year**, with **8 hours/day** per worker, and employs **500 workers** for the steel phase.
**(i)** Calculate the **finished fabricated steel weight**.
**(ii)** Estimate the **total man-hours available per year**.
**(iii)** Calculate the **man-hours per tonne of fabricated steel** (productivity index). **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Finished fabricated weight (2 marks):**

$$
W_{fab} = \eta_{sc} \cdot W_{raw}
$$

$$
W_{fab} = 0.88 \cdot 12000
$$

$$
W_{fab} = 10560\ \text{tonnes}
$$

**(ii) Total man-hours per year (2 marks):**

$$
H_{total} = N_{workers} \cdot D_{work} \cdot H_{day}
$$

$$
H_{total} = 500 \cdot 300 \cdot 8
$$

$$
H_{total} = 1{,}200{,}000\ \text{man-hours/year}
$$

**(iii) Man-hours per tonne (productivity index) (3 marks):**

$$
M_{ht} = \dfrac{H_{total}}{W_{fab}} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000}{10560}
$$

$$
M_{ht} \approx 113.6\ \text{man-hours/tonne}
$$

> **Bench-mark note:** Modern yards target **80–120 man-hours/tonne**. A value of 113.6 places this yard in the **competitive tier**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "block" and "section":** A *section* is a smaller sub-assembly (e.g., a transverse ring); a *block* is a much larger, 3D, fully-fabricated unit. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the shipyard flow diagram** in Q on "shipyard layout" — a clean diagram alone earns **2–3 marks**, even with sparse text.
> 3. **Mixing up units:** Welding heat input is in **kJ/mm**, NOT J/mm. Man-hours are **per worker-year**, not per worker-day.
> 4. **Ignoring the steel conversion factor** in numerical problems — this is a **must-mention assumption** in any LDT/man-hour calculation.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the assumption** that work is single-shift; for multi-shift problems, students must divide crane hours by the *effective* number of shifts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Three classical methods**: Longitudinal, Transverse, Isometric — know the **first member erected** in each.
- ✅ **Block construction** is the **modern industrial standard** for any ship > 10,000 DWT.
- ✅ **PCE (Pre-Construction Equipping)** is the most important efficiency driver in modern ship production.
- ✅ **Steel density** $\rho_s = 7.85\ \text{t/m}^3$ — memorize this constant.
- ✅ **Steel conversion factor** typical range: **0.85 – 0.90**.
- ✅ **CGT** is the global productivity metric; **man-hours per CGT** is the universal yard benchmark.
- ✅ **Welding heat input** $E = \dfrac{60 V I}{v}$ — used for weld procedure qualification.
- ✅ Shipyard flow: **Stockyard → Processing → Panel Shop → Block Shop → Erection Berth → Outfitting → Trials**.
- ✅ Always draw a **labeled flow diagram** in any layout question.
- ✅ Final answers should always carry **units** and a **benchmarking note** ("modern yard target: …") for examiner goodwill.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Quality in Ship Construction

**Quality** in ship construction is formally defined as the **conformance of a ship, its components, and its fabrication processes to specified design standards, classification society rules, statutory regulations, and contractual requirements**. It is a multidimensional measure encompassing structural integrity, dimensional accuracy, material soundness, weld integrity, surface finish, and seaworthiness over the vessel's operational life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Quality in shipbuilding refers to the totality of characteristics of an entity (ship, system, or component) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs — encompassing design quality, build quality, and operational quality throughout the vessel lifecycle.*

### The Three Pillars of Shipbuilding Quality

| Pillar | Focus Area | Key Stakeholders |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| **Design Quality** | Hydrostatics, structural strength, stability | Naval Architect, Classification Society |
| **Build Quality** | Material, fabrication, welding, outfitting | Shipyard, QA/QC Department |
| **Operational Quality** | Sea-keeping, reliability, maintainability | Shipowner, Crew, Flag State |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Quality as a "Health Check" for Ships

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine building a ship is like baking a multi-tier wedding cake for a 1,000-guest banquet. **Quality** is the guarantee that every layer (hull, deck, superstructure) is the right height, every icing line (weld seam) is smooth and unbroken, every ingredient (steel plate) is fresh and graded, and the entire cake can survive being transported across town without collapsing. Just as the baker follows recipes (Classification Rules), inspects ingredients (Material Testing), checks the oven temperature (Process Control), and gets a final tasting (Sea Trials), a shipyard follows a **Quality Management System** from steel cutting to delivery.

## 1.3 Hierarchy of Quality Standards in Shipbuilding

$$\text{Ship Quality} = f(\text{Statutory Rules} + \text{Classification Rules} + \text{Owner's Specs} + \text{ISO Standards})$$

The international maritime quality framework rests on four layers:

1. **Statutory Rules** — Mandated by the **Flag State** (e.g., SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line Conventions administered by the IMO). These are **non-negotiable legal requirements** with **0% tolerance** for non-compliance.
2. **Classification Society Rules** — Issued by independent bodies such as **Lloyd's Register (LR), American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), DNV, Bureau Veritas (BV), and Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)**. Compliance grants the vessel its **Class Certificate**, essential for insurance and port entry.
3. **Owner's Specification** — Contractual extras beyond class and statutory requirements (e.g., extra coating thickness, premium accommodation finishes).
4. **ISO 9001 / IACS QSCS Standards** — Process-level quality management applicable to the shipyard itself as an organization.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** *"SLIC-I"* — **S**tatutory, **L**loyd/Class, **I**SO, **C**ontract (Owner's Spec).

## 1.4 Quality vs. Safety vs. Reliability — Critical Distinctions

> [!WARNING]
> Students often use these terms interchangeably in exams — examiners deduct marks. Memorize the precise distinctions below.

| Term | Precise Meaning | Example in Ships |
|------|----------------|------------------|
| **Quality** | Degree of conformance to specification | A plate meets Class A steel grade |
| **Safety** | Freedom from unacceptable risk of harm | Watertight integrity of bulkhead |
| **Reliability** | Probability of performing without failure for a given time | Main engine MTBF ≥ 30,000 hrs |
| **Durability** | Ability to withstand wear over service life | Coating lasts 5 years before recoating |

## 1.5 Historical Context: Why Quality Standards Exist in Shipbuilding

The **Titanic disaster (1912)** was a watershed moment. Inadequate quality control on rivets and steel plates contributed to the hull failure. This directly led to the establishment of stronger **SOLAS conventions** (1914 onward) and the strengthening of classification society oversight. The **Torrey Canyon (1967)** oil spill triggered enhanced quality in pollution-prevention systems. Modern ships follow an unbroken chain of lessons-learned codified into quality regulations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Quality Management System (QMS) — Theoretical Framework

A **Quality Management System** in a modern shipyard is built upon the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) Deming Cycle**, formalized under **ISO 9001:2015**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of PDCA in Ship Construction

1. **PLAN** — The naval architect and production engineer define the *quality plan*: welding procedures (WPS), inspection test plans (ITP), material lists, dimensional tolerances, and acceptance criteria derived from Class Rules.
2. **DO** — The shipyard executes the plan: steel cutting, forming, sub-assembly, block erection, welding, and outfitting. Every worker follows documented procedures.
3. **CHECK** — Inspectors (yard QC + Class Surveyor + Owner Rep) verify conformance using visual inspection, dimensional surveys, NDT, and hydrostatic tests.
4. **ACT** — Non-conformities (NCRs) are raised, root-cause analyzed (e.g., 5-Why, Fishbone), corrective actions implemented, and the lessons fed back into the next planning cycle.

## 2.2 Classification Societies — The Quality Gatekeepers

A **Classification Society** is an independent, non-governmental organization that establishes and applies technical standards related to the design, construction, and survey of ships. They do not regulate — they **certify**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**: Founded 1969. The 12 member societies (ABS, BV, CCS, CRS, DNV, IRS, KR, LR, NK, PRS, RINA, RS) collectively cover **~90% of world tonnage**.

### Tier-1 Classification Societies (most relevant for KTU)

| Society | Abbreviation | Origin | Key Strength |
|---------|--------------|--------|--------------|
| **Lloyd's Register** | LR | UK (1760) | Oldest, global acceptance |
| **American Bureau of Shipping** | ABS | USA (1862) | Gulf of Mexico, US Navy |
| **Det Norske Veritas** | DNV | Norway (1864) | Offshore, Arctic ships |
| **Bureau Veritas** | BV | France (1828) | LNG, cruise ships |
| **Indian Register of Shipping** | IRS | India (1975) | Indian-flag vessels |

## 2.3 Quality Control (QC) vs. Quality Assurance (QA)

> [!NOTE]
> **QC** is *product-oriented* — inspecting the finished product. **QA** is *process-oriented* — ensuring the process that creates the product is capable.

- **QC Activities**: Visual inspection, dimensional check, NDT, hydrostatic test, tilting test, shaft alignment check.
- **QA Activities**: WPS qualification, welder qualification, procedure audits, calibration of instruments, document control, training records.

In a shipyard:
$$\text{Total Quality} = \text{QA (Process Right)} + \text{QC (Product Right)}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all key formulas and metrics used in quality-based numerical problems for ship construction exams. **Memorize these.**

| # | Concept / Metric | Formula or Definition | Units | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|------------------|----------------------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Acceptance Quality Level (AQL)** | Maximum acceptable % defective in a sampling plan | % | Sampling plans |
| 2 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | Weld tolerance |
| 3 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | ppm | Six Sigma |
| 4 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units inspected}}$ | % | Block erection |
| 5 | **Rolled Steel Yield Strength Req.** | $\sigma_y \geq \dfrac{k \cdot L}{\lambda}$ (for column buckling) | MPa | Plate acceptance |
| 6 | **Weld Quality — Toe Angle** | $\theta = 30°$–$60°$ for full-strength butt weld | degrees | WPS |
| 7 | **Repair Rate** | $\text{Repair Rate} = \dfrac{\text{NCRs raised}}{\text{Total welds}} \times 100\%$ | % | Yard KPI |
| 8 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | \$/ship | TQM |
| 9 | **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Equipment quality |
| 10 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | Engine quality |
| 11 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless | Operational quality |
| 12 | **IACS Common Structural Rules** | Scantlings per CSR-BR / CSR-BC | t, mm | Hull quality |

**Legend:** USL = Upper Spec Limit, LSL = Lower Spec Limit, $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = standard deviation, $\lambda$ = failure rate, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair.

## 2.5 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Methods in Shipbuilding

| NDT Method | Principle | Typical Application | Defects Detected |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Visual Testing (VT)** | Direct eye / borescope | Welds, surfaces | Cracks, undercut, porosity |
| **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | X-ray / Gamma ray | Butt welds | Internal porosity, slag, lack of fusion |
| **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | High-frequency sound | Plate, welds | Laminations, embedded defects |
| **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** | Magnetic flux leakage | Ferritic welds | Surface & near-surface cracks |
| **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT)** | Capillary action | Non-ferrous welds | Surface cracks only |
| **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** | Electromagnetic induction | Tube inspection | Surface cracks, wall thinning |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The quality framework ensures:
- **Insurance eligibility** — No Class = No insurance = Cannot trade.
- **Port State Control (PSC) clearance** — Sub-standard ships are detained (Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU).
- **Crew safety** — Quality prevents catastrophic structural failure at sea.
- **Asset value** — A well-maintained, Class-approved vessel retains **70–80%** of new-build value over 15 years vs. **30–40%** for poorly maintained ones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) Applied to Plate Thickness

**Engineering Scenario:** A shipyard rolls **12 mm thick Grade A steel plates** for hull plating. The specification requires **$11.5 \text{ mm} \leq t \leq 12.5 \text{ mm}$**. After measuring 100 plates, the mean thickness is **$\mu = 11.95 \text{ mm}$** with standard deviation **$\sigma = 0.12 \text{ mm}$**. Determine the process capability.

### Step 1: Identify the Specification Limits

The Lower Specification Limit (LSL) is the minimum acceptable thickness:
$$\text{LSL} = 11.5 \text{ mm}$$

The Upper Specification Limit (USL) is the maximum acceptable thickness:
$$\text{USL} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Apply the $C_{pk}$ Formula

The process capability index is defined as:
$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

### Step 3: Compute the Upper Capability Term

We substitute the values for the upper capability:
$$\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.5 - 11.95}{3 \times 0.12}$$

Performing the numerator first:
$$12.5 - 11.95 = 0.55$$

Computing the denominator:
$$3 \times 0.12 = 0.36$$

Dividing the numerator by the denominator:
$$\frac{0.55}{0.36} = 1.5277...$$

### Step 4: Compute the Lower Capability Term

Substituting the values for the lower capability:
$$\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{11.95 - 11.5}{3 \times 0.12}$$

Numerator:
$$11.95 - 11.5 = 0.45$$

Denominator (same as before):
$$3 \times 0.12 = 0.36$$

Division:
$$\frac{0.45}{0.36} = 1.2500$$

### Step 5: Take the Minimum Value

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.5277,\ 1.2500)$$

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = 1.25}$$

### Step 6: Interpret the Result

A $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is considered **capable** for shipbuilding. Our value of **1.25** is **marginally capable** — the process is biased toward the lower specification limit (thinner plates), which is **critical for corrosion allowance**. The yard should re-center the rolling mill to bring $\mu$ closer to the nominal 12.0 mm.

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation Benchmark:** $C_{pk} < 1.00$ = Inadequate | $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ = Marginal | $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ = Capable | $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ = Excellent (Six Sigma level).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Rolled Steel Plate Scantling Acceptance (Hull Girder Strength)

**Scenario:** Verify that a steel plate proposed for the strength deck of a 180 m bulk carrier meets the minimum yield strength requirement under axial compressive buckling per **IACS CSR-BC**.

### Step 1: Identify the Geometric Parameters

For a strength deck plate between two transverse webs:
- Plate length (between stiffeners): $l = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- Plate breadth: $b = 0.8 \text{ m}$
- Plate thickness: $t = 0.020 \text{ m}$ (20 mm)
- Slenderness ratio: $\lambda = \dfrac{l}{t}$

### Step 2: Compute the Slenderness Ratio

$$\lambda = \frac{l}{t} = \frac{2.5}{0.020} = 125$$

### Step 3: Apply the Euler-Based Critical Buckling Stress

For a plate simply supported on all four edges, the critical buckling stress is:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

where:
- $E = 2.10 \times 10^{5} \text{ MPa}$ (Young's modulus for ship steel)
- $\nu = 0.30$ (Poisson's ratio)
- $k = 4.0$ (plate buckling coefficient, simply supported)

### Step 4: Substitute the Values

First, compute the term inside the brackets:
$$\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.020}{0.8}\right)^2 = (0.025)^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

Compute the material denominator:
$$12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2) = 12 \cdot (1 - 0.09) = 12 \times 0.91 = 10.92$$

Compute the constant:
$$k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E = 4.0 \times (3.1416)^2 \times (2.10 \times 10^5)$$

$$= 4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 2.10 \times 10^5$$

$$= 4.0 \times 2.0726 \times 10^6$$

$$= 8.290 \times 10^6 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Critical Buckling Stress

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{8.290 \times 10^6}{10.92} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 7.592 \times 10^5 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 474.5 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 6: Determine the Required Minimum Yield Strength

Using a safety factor $\eta = 1.50$ against buckling:
$$\sigma_{y,\min} = \eta \cdot \sigma_{cr} = 1.50 \times 474.5$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{y,\min} = 711.7 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Step 7: Practical Acceptance Decision

Since standard **Grade A steel has $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$** and **Grade AH36 has $\sigma_y = 355 \text{ MPa}$**, the proposed 20 mm plate **does NOT meet the buckling-based strength requirement**. The shipyard must either:
- Increase plate thickness to **~30 mm** (Grade A), OR
- Upgrade material to **Grade DH36 or EH36** ($\sigma_y = 355 \text{ MPa}$) AND increase thickness to 25 mm.

This demonstrates how quality assessment drives design iteration in shipbuilding.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sampling Plan Application (AQL)

**Scenario:** A shipyard receives a lot of **500 weld electrodes**. Use **ISO 2859-1** sampling with **AQL = 1.0%, General Inspection Level II**.

| Lot Size | Sample Size | Accept (Ac) | Reject (Re) |
|----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| 281–500 | 50 | 1 | 2 |

If 1 defective electrode is found in 50 sampled, **Accept**.
If 2 or more are found, **Reject the lot**.

This illustrates statistical quality control in shipbuilding material acceptance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
