<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Quality Assurance and Quality Control. Differentiate between them with a shipbuilding example.**

**Model Answer:**
Quality Assurance (QA) is a **process-oriented** systematic activity implemented within the quality management system to ensure that the *process* is capable of producing conforming products. Quality Control (QC) is a **product-oriented** activity that involves inspection, testing, and measurement of the *finished product* against specified requirements.

| Aspect | QA | QC |
|--------|----|----|
| Focus | Process | Product |
| Timing | Before and during production | After production step |
| Example in shipbuilding | Approving a Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) and qualifying welders before block erection begins | Performing ultrasonic testing on a completed butt weld in the strength deck |

**Exam Tip:** Always mention *when* the activity happens. QA is **proactive**; QC is **reactive**. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the role of a Classification Society in ship construction? Name any two international classification societies.**

**Model Answer:**
A Classification Society is an **independent, non-governmental organization** that establishes and applies technical standards for the design, construction, and survey of ships. Its primary role is to verify that a vessel meets the society's rules for **structural integrity, safety, and seaworthiness**, thereby issuing a **Class Certificate** which is mandatory for insurance, registration, and commercial operation.

Key functions include:
- Approval of design drawings
- Survey during construction (Keel Laid, Hull, Machinery, Electrical)
- Setting rules for materials and welding
- Periodic surveys during service life

**Two societies:** **Lloyd's Register (LR)** and **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)**. *(Other acceptable answers: DNV, BV, IRS, KR, NK, RINA.)* **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A `(a)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) cycle as applied to quality management in a shipyard. Illustrate with an example of welding quality control of a hull plate butt joint. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **PDCA cycle**, also called the **Deming Cycle**, is a four-stage iterative framework for continuous improvement of quality, formalized under ISO 9001:2015.

**Stage 1 — PLAN:** The shipyard's QA department formulates a Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) per **AWS D1.1 / IACS UR W17**. The plan includes joint preparation, electrode grade (e.g., E7018), preheat temperature (≥ 100°C for thick plates), welding parameters, and acceptance criteria (Class rules).

**Stage 2 — DO:** The qualified welder executes the butt weld on the hull plate using the WPS. Parameters like current (200–250 A), voltage (24–28 V), and travel speed are logged on the weld traveler.

**Stage 3 — CHECK:** The QC inspector performs Visual Testing (VT) first. If the surface is acceptable, Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) — typically 100% Ultrasonic Testing (UT) for critical hull seams — is carried out by a qualified NDT Level II technician. Results are compared to acceptance criteria in the WPS.

**Stage 4 — ACT:** If defects are found, a **Non-Conformity Report (NCR)** is raised, the weld is repaired or cut out, and the root cause is analyzed using the **5-Why** method. Lessons learned are fed back into updated WPS for the next joint, completing the cycle.

**Key Outcome:** Through repeated PDCA cycles, the weld defect rate drops, productivity rises, and the ship achieves **first-class classification** with minimal rework. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question A `(b)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(b) Describe any four Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods used in ship construction. State the type of defect each method detects and one typical application. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | NDT Method | Principle | Defect Detected | Typical Ship Application |
|---|-----------|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Visual Testing (VT)** | Direct observation or with aids (magnifier, borescope) | Surface cracks, undercut, porosity, dimensional errors | Initial inspection of every weld before NDT |
| 2 | **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | Penetration by X-ray or Ir-192 gamma rays recorded on film/digital detector | Internal volumetric defects: porosity, slag inclusion, lack of fusion, root cracks | Butt welds in strength deck and side shell |
| 3 | **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | High-frequency sound waves reflected from internal interfaces | Embedded laminations, delaminations, sidewall fusion defects, through-thickness cracks | Plate lamination check, post-weld inspection of thick sections |
| 4 | **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** | Magnetic flux leakage at surface/near-surface discontinuities | Surface and sub-surface cracks in ferromagnetic materials | Toe cracks in fillet welds on mild steel hull components |
| 5 | **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT)** | Capillary action of penetrant dye into surface-breaking defects | Surface cracks only, on non-ferrous or non-magnetic materials | Crack detection on austenitic stainless steel pipe welds |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU answers, drawing a small schematic (even in text form) of each method earns extra marks.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `(a)` `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Set]`

**(a) Explain the role of IACS (International Association of Classification Societies). List any six member societies and discuss the importance of Common Structural Rules (CSR). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**IACS** was founded in **1969** as a non-governmental body to coordinate the activities of classification societies and to develop unified technical standards. Today, IACS represents **12 member societies** that classify more than **90% of world commercial tonnage**.

**Six Member Societies:**
1. Lloyd's Register (LR) — UK
2. American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) — USA
3. Det Norske Veritas (DNV) — Norway
4. Bureau Veritas (BV) — France
5. Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) — India
6. ClassNK (NK) — Japan *(Also acceptable: RINA, KR, CCS, RS, PRS, CRS)*

**Importance of Common Structural Rules (CSR):**
- **CSR-BC (Bulk Carriers)** and **CSR-BC & OT (Oil Tankers)** harmonize hull scantling rules across IACS societies, eliminating design discrepancies.
- They provide **uniform safety levels** for tankers and bulkers, especially addressing structural failures like the **MOL Comfort (2013)** and the **Prem Divya (2013)** casualties.
- They specify enhanced **yield strength requirements**, **corrosion addition**, and **fatigue assessment** using common spectral wave parameters.
- For shipyards, CSR simplifies multi-class design, reduces engineering rework, and ensures the vessel is acceptable to any IACS society without redrawing.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `(b)` `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Set]`

**(b) A shipyard produces steel plates with target thickness 12 mm. The specification allows 11.5 mm to 12.5 mm. From a sample of 100 plates, the mean thickness is 11.95 mm and the standard deviation is 0.12 mm. Calculate the process capability index Cpk and comment on whether the process is suitable. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Lower Specification Limit: $\text{LSL} = 11.5 \text{ mm}$
- Upper Specification Limit: $\text{USL} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$
- Process Mean: $\mu = 11.95 \text{ mm}$
- Process Standard Deviation: $\sigma = 0.12 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**
$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Upper Capability:**
$$\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.5 - 11.95}{3 \times 0.12} = \frac{0.55}{0.36} = 1.528$$

**Step 3 — Lower Capability:**
$$\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{11.95 - 11.5}{3 \times 0.12} = \frac{0.45}{0.36} = 1.250$$

**Step 4 — Take the minimum:**
$$C_{pk} = \min(1.528,\ 1.250) = 1.25$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
A $C_{pk}$ value of **1.25** is **marginally capable** (target ≥ 1.33 for shipbuilding). The process mean is **shifted toward the LSL**, meaning plates are running slightly *thin*. This is critical because insufficient plate thickness reduces corrosion allowance and structural safety margin.

**Recommendation:** Re-center the rolling mill to shift $\mu$ from 11.95 mm to 12.00 mm, and reduce $\sigma$ through improved process control, to achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.

**(7 marks — full working essential for marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Quality Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing QA with QC** in Part A → 1-mark deduction each time. Always say *"QA = process; QC = product."*
> 2. **Missing units in Cpk / DPMO calculations** → Examiner deducts ½ mark.
> 3. **Naming only one classification society** when the question asks "any two" → Loss of 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation step** after a Cpk calculation → Always close with *"this means the process is / is not capable because..."*
> 5. **Writing 'IMO is a classification society'** → Incorrect! IMO is a UN regulatory body, **not** a classification society.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Quality** = conformance to design, class, statutory, and contractual specifications.
- **Three pillars:** Design Quality, Build Quality, Operational Quality.
- **SLIC-I hierarchy:** Statutory → Lloyd/Class → ISO → Contract.
- **QA** is process-oriented and **proactive**; **QC** is product-oriented and **reactive**.
- **Classification Societies (IACS members):** LR, ABS, DNV, BV, IRS, NK (plus 6 others).
- **IACS** was founded in **1969** and covers **>90% of world tonnage**.
- **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** standardize scantlings for bulk carriers (CSR-BC) and tankers (CSR-OT).
- **PDCA Deming Cycle** = Plan → Do → Check → Act — the heart of continuous quality improvement.
- **Key NDT methods:** VT, RT, UT, MT, PT, ET — each with distinct defect-detection capability.
- **Cpk** < 1.00 = inadequate; 1.00–1.33 = marginal; 1.33–1.67 = capable; ≥ 1.67 = excellent.
- **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
- **MTBF and Availability** are core metrics for operational quality of marine machinery.
- **Class Certificate** is mandatory for insurance, registration, and port entry — *"No Class = No Trade."*
- **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** and **WPQ (Welder Performance Qualification)** are foundational QA documents in any shipyard.
- **NCR (Non-Conformity Report)** is raised for any deviation; root-cause analysis (5-Why / Fishbone) follows.
- **SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line** are the three statutory pillars — administered by Flag State under IMO conventions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Management Commercial Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Management Commercial Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Material Management Commercial Activities** refers to the integrated set of commercial, administrative, and logistical functions carried out within a shipyard that govern the **procurement, inspection, storage, accounting, and disbursement** of raw materials, components, and outfit items required for the construction, outfitting, and repair of ships. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, this domain sits at the intersection of **production engineering, marine commerce, and supply-chain governance**, ensuring that the right material reaches the right workstation at the right cost, quantity, and quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> Material management in shipbuilding is not merely "buying steel." It is the *commercial orchestration* of **tendering → vendor selection → inspection → warehousing → issue-to-production → reconciliation**, governed by maritime classification society rules (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS) and yard-specific ERP systems.

The commercial activities encompass:

1. **Tendering and Bidding** – inviting quotations from vendors.
2. **Pre-qualification of Vendors** – assessing technical and financial capability.
3. **Purchase Order (PO) and Contract Management** – legally binding commercial transactions.
4. **Inspection and Certification** – classification society witnessed material tests.
5. **Inventory Control** – Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), ABC analysis, lead-time analysis.
6. **Stores Accounting** – FIFO, LIFO, weighted average costing.
7. **Vendor Payments and Reconciliation** – milestone-based disbursement.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Shipyard as a Wedding Planner**
> Imagine planning a large Indian wedding. You need a venue (steel), caterers (outfit items), decorators (machinery), invitations (documentation), and transport (logistics). The wedding planner must:
> - Decide *what* to buy (material specification).
> - Choose *whom* to buy from (vendor selection).
> - Negotiate the *price* (commercial terms).
> - Ensure *quality* (inspection).
> - Store items *safely* until needed (warehousing).
> - Pay on time to maintain goodwill (vendor payment).
>
> A shipyard performs the **exact same choreography** but on a scale of thousands of tonnes of steel, millions of components, and a delivery window spanning **18–36 months**. *Material Management Commercial Activities is the "wedding planner's playbook" of the shipyard.*

### 1.3 Key Terminology – Bold Constants and Metrics

The following are the **standard commercial metrics** used universally in shipyards:

| Parameter | Standard Reference Value |
|---|---|
| Lead Time (Steel Plates) | **45–90 days** |
| Safety Stock Factor (k) | **1.65** (95% service level) |
| Re-order Level (ROL) | **Maximum Consumption × Lead Time** |
| EOQ Model | **Harris (1913) classical formula** |
| Inventory Carrying Cost | **18–25 % per annum of item value** |
| Vendor Rating Index Threshold | **≥ 0.85** for grade-A vendor |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Why this topic matters in KTU exams:** Questions from this module test your understanding of *commercial procedures* (procurement cycle, tender documents, INCOTERMS) and *quantitative tools* (EOQ, lead-time analysis). Expect at least one **14-mark descriptive question** and one **3-mark direct definition**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Material Management Commercial Activities

The commercial lifecycle of shipyard material can be broken down into five logically sequenced pillars:

#### Pillar 1 – Indent and Requisition
- The **Production Planning Department** raises a *Material Requisition Note (MRN)* based on the *Bill of Materials (BoM)* extracted from the ship's specification.
- The MRN is forwarded to the **Purchase Department** which converts it into a *Purchase Requisition (PR)*.
- **Why this step?** It separates *technical need* from *commercial sourcing*.

#### Pillar 2 – Vendor Enquiry and Tendering
- A **Request for Quotation (RFQ)** is floated to pre-qualified vendors.
- Three commercial formats are common:
  1. **Open Tender** – publicly advertised (used for capital items).
  2. **Limited Tender** – sent to 3–5 known vendors (used for standard steel).
  3. **Single Tender / Nomination** – proprietary or proprietary-grade items.
- Vendors return a **quotation** containing unit price, delivery period, payment terms, and validity.

#### Pillar 3 – Comparative Statement and Purchase Order
- The Purchase Department prepares a **Comparative Statement (CS)** tabulating:
  - Price
  - Delivery
  - Payment terms
  - Past performance
- The **Lowest Cost Responsive Bidder (LCRB)** is selected, and a *Purchase Order (PO)* is issued.
- **PO is a legally binding contract** under the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

#### Pillar 4 – Inspection, Receipt, and Classification Certification
- Material is received at the **Receiving Bay** of the yard.
- Classification society surveyors (e.g., IRS) witness tests such as:
  - Tensile test
  - Charpy impact test
  - Chemical analysis
- On satisfactory testing, a **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** is cross-verified.
- Only after certification is material moved to the **stockyard** with a unique *Material Code*.

#### Pillar 5 – Issue to Production and Reconciliation
- Material is issued to the shop floor against a *Material Issue Note (MIN)*.
- The cost is debited to the **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** account of the specific ship under construction.
- Unused/returned material is credited back via a *Material Return Note (MRN)*.
- **End-of-month reconciliation** matches actual consumption with planned BoM.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula a KTU student must know for this topic. **Memorize these verbatim.**

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | D = Annual demand (units), S = Ordering cost per order (₹), H = Holding cost per unit per year (₹) | Minimize total inventory cost |
| Re-order Level (ROL) | $ROL = (D_{\max} \times L)$ | $D_{\max}$ = Maximum daily consumption, L = Lead time (days) | When to place next order |
| Safety Stock (SS) | $SS = k \times \sigma_{dL}$ | k = Service-level factor (**1.65** for 95 %), $\sigma_{dL}$ = Std. dev. of demand during lead time | Buffer against variability |
| ABC Analysis (Cumulative Value) | $V_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} (P_j \times Q_j)$ | $P_j$ = Unit price, $Q_j$ = Annual quantity of item j | Classify items A/B/C |
| Total Inventory Cost (TIC) | $TIC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | Q = Order quantity | Verify EOQ optimum |
| Payment Discount Effective Rate | $r_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{d}{1-d} \times \dfrac{360}{n}$ | d = Discount %, n = Net period minus discount period (days) | Vendor payment terms |
| Vendor Rating Index (VRI) | $VRI = 0.4Q + 0.3D + 0.2P + 0.1R$ | Q = Quality score, D = Delivery score, P = Price score, R = Response score (out of 1) | Vendor grading |

**Boundary conditions and assumptions:**
- EOQ assumes **constant demand rate**, **instant replenishment**, and **no stock-out cost**.
- ABC Analysis threshold: **A-items ≈ 10–20 % of SKUs but 70–80 % of value**.
- INCOTERMS 2020 governs all international shipbuilding procurements (most relevant: **FOB, CIF, EXW, DDP**).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Indian Shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE)** implement SAP-MM and Oracle SCM modules for real-time procurement.
- The **EOQ concept** determines optimal ordering frequency for high-volume consumables (electrodes, paint, rivets).
- **ABC analysis** ensures that expensive items (main engine spares, propeller forgings) get the tightest inventory control.
- **INCOTERMS** define risk transfer between yard and vendor — critical for imported main engines and shaft forgings.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-world linkage:** The Ministry of Ports, Shipping & Waterways (India) mandates that all public-sector shipyards follow **General Financial Rules (GFR) 2017** for procurement above ₹25 lakh.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

The EOQ formula is one of the most frequently tested quantitative topics in KTU examinations. We now derive it rigorously from first principles.

#### Step 1 – Define Total Annual Cost

The **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** per year is the sum of two components:

$$
TIC(Q) = \text{Ordering Cost} + \text{Holding Cost}
$$

Where:
- **Ordering Cost** = $\dfrac{D}{Q} \times S$ (number of orders per year × cost per order)
- **Holding Cost** = $\dfrac{Q}{2} \times H$ (average inventory × holding cost per unit per year)

#### Step 2 – Express TIC as a Function of Order Quantity Q

Combining the two components:

$$
TIC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H
$$

**Textual interpretation:** Every time we order, we pay a fixed setup cost S. If we order more, we order less frequently, so ordering cost decreases. But holding cost increases because average inventory rises. There exists an optimum Q where the total is minimum.

#### Step 3 – Differentiate TIC with respect to Q

To find the minimum, take the first derivative and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{d(TIC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

#### Step 4 – Solve for Q

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^{2}}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $Q^{2}$:

$$
\frac{H \cdot Q^{2}}{2} = DS
$$

Solving for $Q^{2}$:

$$
Q^{2} = \frac{2DS}{H}
$$

Taking the positive square root (since Q > 0):

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
$$

This is the **classical EOQ formula**, also called the **Wilson-Harris formula** (Ford W. Harris, 1913).

#### Step 5 – Verify the Second-Order Condition

The second derivative is:

$$
\frac{d^{2}(TIC)}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2DS}{Q^{3}} > 0
$$

Since the second derivative is positive, the critical point is indeed a **minimum**, confirming that EOQ minimizes total cost.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A shipyard consumes **10,000 tonnes of shipbuilding steel plates annually**. The ordering cost per order is **₹15,000** and the holding cost is **₹3,000 per tonne per year**. Determine (a) the EOQ, (b) the optimal number of orders per year, (c) the minimum total cost, and (d) the time between orders.

**Given:**
- $D = 10{,}000$ tonnes/year
- $S = 15{,}000$ ₹/order
- $H = 3{,}000$ ₹/tonne/year

**Part (a) – EOQ:**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 15{,}000}{3{,}000}}
$$

Computing numerator:

$$
2 \times 10{,}000 \times 15{,}000 = 300{,}000{,}000
$$

Dividing by denominator:

$$
\frac{300{,}000{,}000}{3{,}000} = 100{,}000
$$

Square root:

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{100{,}000} \approx 316.23 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Part (b) – Optimal number of orders per year (N*):**

$$
N^{*} = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{10{,}000}{316.23} \approx 31.62 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Part (c) – Minimum Total Inventory Cost:**

$$
TIC_{\min} = \frac{D}{EOQ}S + \frac{EOQ}{2}H
$$

$$
TIC_{\min} = 31.62 \times 15{,}000 + \frac{316.23}{2} \times 3{,}000
$$

$$
TIC_{\min} = 474{,}300 + 474{,}345 \approx 9{,}48{,}645 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Part (d) – Time between orders (TBO):**

$$
TBO = \frac{\text{Working days per year}}{N^{*}}
$$

Assuming **300 working days/year**:

$$
TBO = \frac{300}{31.62} \approx 9.49 \text{ days}
$$

### 3.3 Re-order Level and Safety Stock Derivation

$$
ROL = (D_{\max} \times L) + SS
$$

$$
SS = k \times \sigma_{dL}
$$

Where $\sigma_{dL}$ is the standard deviation of demand *during* the lead-time window. For a **95 % service level**, $k = 1.65$; for **99 %**, $k = 2.33$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to add Safety Stock to the basic ROL. Examiners specifically check whether ROL = $D_{\max} \times L$ alone OR $D_{\max} \times L + SS$. Both forms are accepted, but full-marks require the SS term with k-value justification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram – End-to-End Material Management Cycle

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the complete commercial lifecycle of shipyard material, from indent to reconciliation. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid v10+ parser safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Production Planning Dept] -->|Raises MRN| A2[Material Requisition Note]
    A2 -->|Converted to PR| B1[Purchase Department]
    B1 -->|Floats RFQ| B2[Vendor Enquiry Cell]
    B2 -->|Open or Limited Tender| C1[Vendor Quotation Receipt]
    C1 -->|Tabulated into CS| C2[Comparative Statement]
    C2 -->|Selects LCRB| D1[Purchase Order Issued]
    D1 -->|Contract Binding| D2[Vendor Manufacturing]
    D2 -->|Dispatch with MTC| E1[Receiving Bay at Yard]
    E1 -->|Classification Witness| E2[Quality Inspection]
    E2 -->|Passed| F1[Stockyard Storage]
    E2 -->|Rejected| F2[Rejection and Return]
    F1 -->|MIN raised| G1[Issue to Production Shopfloor]
    G1 -->|Debited to WIP| G2[Ship Construction Account]
    G2 -->|MRN for surplus| H1[Return to Stores]
    G2 -->|End of Month| H2[Inventory Reconciliation]
    H2 -->|Feedback to| A1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram – Hierarchical Organization of a Shipyard's Commercial Department

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CM[Chief General Manager Materials] --> D1[Purchase Manager]
    CM --> D2[Stores Manager]
    CM --> D3[Inspection Manager]
    CM --> D4[Vendor Development Cell]
    D1 --> S1[Tendering Section]
    D1 --> S2[Order Follow-up Section]
    D1 --> S3[Import and Customs Section]
    D2 --> T1[Receipt Bay]
    D2 --> T2[Stockyard]
    D2 --> T3[Issue Section]
    D3 --> U1[Mechanical Test Lab]
    D3 --> U2[Chemical Lab]
    D3 --> U3[Classification Coordination]
    D4 --> V1[Vendor Registration]
    D4 --> V2[Vendor Rating and Audits]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram – ABC Inventory Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Annual Consumption Value of Each Item] --> P2[Sort Items in Descending Order]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Cumulative Value Percent]
    P3 --> Q1{Cumulative Percent Less Than 80 Percent}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[CLASS A ITEMS - Tight Control]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Between 80 and 95 Percent}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[CLASS B ITEMS - Moderate Control]
    Q2 -->|No| R3[CLASS C ITEMS - Loose Control]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in an exam:** In KTU 14-mark answers, **always insert at least one Mermaid-style or hand-drawn block diagram**. Examiners explicitly allocate **1–2 marks** for visual representation under "presentation skills."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

Define **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**. State the assumptions made in deriving the EOQ formula.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **EOQ** is the order quantity that minimizes the total annual inventory cost, comprising ordering cost and holding cost. Mathematically,
>
> $$
> EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
> $$
>
> where D = annual demand, S = ordering cost per order, H = holding cost per unit per year.
>
> **Assumptions (any three for full marks):**
> 1. Demand rate is **known and constant** throughout the year.
> 2. Lead time is **constant and known**.
> 3. Replenishment is **instantaneous** (infinite production rate).
> 4. **No stock-outs** are permitted.
> 5. Ordering cost and holding cost are **independent** of order quantity.

*(Marks: Definition = 1, Formula = 1, Assumptions = 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

What is **ABC analysis** in inventory management? Why is it called the "**Always Better Control**" technique?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **ABC analysis** is an inventory classification technique based on the **Pareto principle (80/20 rule)**, where items are grouped by their annual consumption value:
> - **A-items:** ~10–20 % of SKUs, ~70–80 % of value – **tight control**.
> - **B-items:** ~20–30 % of SKUs, ~15–25 % of value – **moderate control**.
> - **C-items:** ~50–70 % of SKUs, ~5–10 % of value – **loose control**.
>
> It is called "**Always Better Control**" because applying strict control measures uniformly to all items is uneconomical; ABC analysis directs managerial attention *selectively* to high-value items, thereby improving inventory control efficiency.

*(Marks: Definition = 1, Classification with values = 1, Naming logic = 1)*

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert (Q3 and Q4):**
> - Do **NOT** write only a bullet list. Examiners expect **prose explanation with at least one diagram**.
> - For numerical sub-parts, **always show formula → substitution → final answer with units**. Marks are split as: Formula = 1, Substitution = 1, Answer = 1.
> - Avoid generic statements like "it is important." Use **yard-specific** terminology: "main engine," "shell plate," "classification society," etc.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **procurement cycle** of a shipyard. List the key documents involved at each stage with their purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard requires **8,000 welding electrodes per month**. The ordering cost is **₹1,200 per order** and the holding cost is **₹40 per electrode per year**. Calculate the **EOQ**, **number of orders per year**, and **time between consecutive orders**. Assume 300 working days/year. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a) – Procurement Cycle

The procurement cycle of a shipyard is the **end-to-end sequence** by which a material requirement is converted into a stocked, certified, and accounted-for inventory item. It consists of **seven interlinked stages**:

1. **Indent Generation (Material Requisition Note):** The Production Planning Department raises an indent based on the *Bill of Materials* of the ship under construction. The MRN specifies the *item description, grade, quantity, delivery date, and ship number*. **(1 mark)**

2. **Purchase Requisition (PR):** The Purchase Department converts the MRN into a PR, attaching technical specifications and budget code. **(1 mark)**

3. **Vendor Enquiry and Tender:** A Request for Quotation (RFQ) is sent to pre-qualified vendors via Open, Limited, or Single Tender route. Vendors return sealed quotations within the bid validity period. **(1 mark)**

4. **Comparative Statement and Negotiation:** The Purchase Department tabulates quotations into a Comparative Statement showing price, delivery, payment terms, and past performance. Negotiation (if any) is conducted with the Lowest Cost Responsive Bidder. **(1 mark)**

5. **Purchase Order (PO) Issuance:** A legally binding PO is issued with the agreed commercial terms. The PO includes *price, quantity, delivery schedule, inspection clause, payment terms, penalty clause, and force majeure*. **(1 mark)**

6. **Inspection, Receipt, and Storage:** Material is received at the yard, inspected by the Quality department in the presence of classification society surveyors (e.g., IRS), and moved to the stockyard with a unique material code. **(1 mark)**

7. **Issue, Consumption, and Reconciliation:** Material is issued to production against a Material Issue Note, debited to the ship's WIP account, and reconciled at month-end. **(1 mark)**

> *(Diagrammatic representation adds 1 bonus mark for presentation.)*

#### Model Solution – Part (b) – EOQ Calculation

**Given:**
- Monthly demand = 8,000 electrodes
- Annual demand $D = 8{,}000 \times 12 = 96{,}000$ electrodes/year
- Ordering cost $S = ₹1{,}200$ per order
- Holding cost $H = ₹40$ per electrode per year
- Working days = 300/year

**Step 1 – Apply EOQ formula:**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 96{,}000 \times 1{,}200}{40}}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute numerator:**

$$
2 \times 96{,}000 \times 1{,}200 = 230{,}400{,}000
$$

**Step 3 – Divide by H:**

$$
\frac{230{,}400{,}000}{40} = 5{,}760{,}000
$$

**Step 4 – Take square root:**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{5{,}760{,}000} \approx 2{,}400 \text{ electrodes per order}
$$

**Step 5 – Number of orders per year (N*):**

$$
N^{*} = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{96{,}000}{2{,}400} = 40 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 6 – Time between orders (TBO):**

$$
TBO = \frac{300}{40} = 7.5 \text{ days}
$$

**Final Answer:** EOQ ≈ **2,400 electrodes**, **40 orders per year**, time between orders ≈ **7.5 days**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Discuss the **importance of vendor rating** in shipbuilding material management. Describe any **one quantitative vendor rating model** with formula and weightage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard receives a quotation from a vendor offering a **2/10 net 30** payment term. The bank rate of interest is **12 % per annum**. Should the yard accept the discount? Show calculations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a) – Vendor Rating

Vendor rating is the **systematic evaluation of a vendor's performance** over multiple purchase cycles. It is essential because:
- Shipbuilding material is **safety-critical** (hull steel, life-saving appliances).
- Late delivery of even one item can **stall an entire ship block**.
- Quality rejection leads to **re-work and demurrage costs**.

A standard quantitative model is the **Ly's Vendor Rating Model** (R. N. Ly, 1970s):

$$
VRI = 0.4Q + 0.3D + 0.2P + 0.1R
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Quality score (ratio of lots accepted to lots inspected, max = 1)
- $D$ = Delivery score (ratio of lots on-time to total lots, max = 1)
- $P$ = Price score (ratio of lowest quotation to vendor's quotation, max = 1)
- $R$ = Response score (rating of vendor's responsiveness, max = 1)

**Weightage rationale:** Quality (40 %) and Delivery (30 %) receive the highest weights because they directly impact ship safety and schedule. Price (20 %) is weighted lower than quality because cheapest is not always best in shipbuilding. Response (10 %) reflects vendor's commercial engagement.

**Threshold:** VRI ≥ **0.85** qualifies a vendor as **Grade-A**; 0.70–0.85 is **Grade-B**; < 0.70 is **Grade-C** (debarred).

#### Model Solution – Part (b) – Payment Discount Decision

**Given:**
- Discount terms: **2/10 net 30**
- Discount $d = 2\% = 0.02$
- Discount period = 10 days, Net period = 30 days
- $n = 30 - 10 = 20$ days (extra days credit forfeited by paying early)
- Bank rate $r = 12\% = 0.12$ per annum

**Step 1 – Apply effective annual rate formula:**

$$
r_{\text{eff}} = \frac{d}{1-d} \times \frac{360}{n}
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute values:**

$$
r_{\text{eff}} = \frac{0.02}{1 - 0.02} \times \frac{360}{20}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute each term:**

$$
\frac{0.02}{0.98} \approx 0.02041
$$

$$
\frac{360}{20} = 18
$$

**Step 4 – Multiply:**

$$
r_{\text{eff}} = 0.02041 \times 18 \approx 0.3673 \text{ or } 36.73\%
$$

**Step 5 – Decision:**

Since the **effective annualized discount (36.73 %) is greater than the borrowing cost (12 %)**, the yard should **accept the discount and pay on Day 10** rather than on Day 30.

> **Interpretation:** By paying 20 days early, the yard effectively earns a 36.73 % annualized return on the early payment — far above the bank's 12 % lending rate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 – Material Management Commercial Activities:**

- ✅ **Procurement cycle has 7 stages:** MRN → PR → RFQ → CS → PO → Inspection → Issue/Reconciliation.
- ✅ **Three tender routes:** Open, Limited, Single (Nomination). Use **Limited Tender** for standard steel and **Open Tender** for capital items.
- ✅ **EOQ formula:** $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ — derived by setting $\dfrac{d(TIC)}{dQ} = 0$.
- ✅ **EOQ assumptions:** constant demand, no stock-outs, infinite replenishment rate, constant lead time.
- ✅ **Re-order Level:** $ROL = D_{\max} \times L + SS$; never forget the Safety Stock term in exams.
- ✅ **Safety Stock factor k = 1.65** for 95 % service level; **k = 2.33** for 99 %.
- ✅ **ABC analysis:** A-items = 10–20 % SKUs but 70–80 % value; **Always Better Control**.
- ✅ **Vendor Rating Index (VRI):** $0.4Q + 0.3D + 0.2P + 0.1R$; Grade-A threshold = 0.85.
- ✅ **INCOTERMS 2020:** FOB (Free on Board), CIF (Cost, Insurance, Freight), EXW (Ex-Works), DDP (Delivered Duty Paid). Classify risk transfer carefully.
- ✅ **Payment discount:** If $r_{\text{eff}} >$ bank rate, **accept the discount**.
- ✅ **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** must accompany every batch of shipbuilding steel.
- ✅ **Classification societies (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS)** witness material tests and certify compliance.
- ✅ **Inventory carrying cost** = 18–25 % per annum of item value — use this in cost estimation problems.
- ✅ **Lead time** for shipbuilding steel = 45–90 days; for main engine = 9–12 months.
- ✅ **Always draw at least one block diagram** in 14-mark answers for presentation marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, allocate roughly **2 marks for a diagram**, **2 marks for formulas/equations**, and **10 marks for structured prose explanation**. Use **headings + sub-points** to make your answer visually scannable to the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Resource
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Human Resource

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Human Resource (HR)** in the context of ship construction refers to the **total pool of manpower** — including engineers, supervisors, skilled tradesmen, semi-skilled workers, and unskilled laborers — that is organised, trained, deployed, and managed to execute the design, fabrication, assembly, outfitting, and repair of ships and floating structures within a shipyard or marine construction facility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Worthy):**
> *"Human Resource in shipbuilding is the integrated management of the workforce — encompassing recruitment, training, classification, productivity optimisation, safety, welfare, and skill development — required to translate naval architectural designs into a fully operational seagoing vessel."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hospital operating theatre**. A surgeon cannot operate alone — they need an anesthetist, nurses, technicians, and support staff, each with a defined role. The success of the surgery depends not just on the surgeon but on the **coordination of the entire team**.

A shipyard works the same way. The **Naval Architect** is the "surgeon" who designs the ship, but the actual cutting, welding, fitting, painting, electrical work, and pipe work is done by hundreds of **specialised workers**. Human Resource Management (HRM) is the system that ensures every worker is in the right place, at the right time, with the right skills, and the right safety equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Shipyard is Like a Symphony Orchestra**
> - The **Conductor** = Project Manager / Production Engineer
> - **Section Leaders** = Foremen / Supervisors (Fitters, Welders, Electricians)
> - **Musicians** = Skilled Tradesmen
> - **Apprentices** = Trainees / Semi-skilled workers
> - **Stage Crew** = Unskilled laborers / Helpers
> The orchestra (shipyard) can only perform the symphony (build the ship) when every section is trained, timed, and coordinated perfectly.

## 1.3 Why Human Resource is a Critical Module in KTU Ship Construction

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Labour-intensive industry** | Shipbuilding is one of the most labour-intensive heavy industries, with labour costs forming **20–40%** of total production cost. |
| **Safety-critical environment** | Shipyards involve high-risk operations (working at heights, confined spaces, hot work, heavy lifting). |
| **Skilled trade dependency** | Quality of welds, pipe joints, and electrical work directly determines vessel seaworthiness. |
| **International standards** | Regulated by **ILO** (International Labour Organization), **IMO STCW**, and **ISO 45001** (Occupational Health & Safety). |
| **Productivity driver** | Human efficiency directly affects project delivery time and cost competitiveness. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used in Shipyard HR Planning:**
> - **Man-hours per Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT):** typically **30–80 man-hours/CGT** for modern yards.
> - **Worker Density:** **0.5–1.5 workers per m² of building berth area**.
> - **Skilled-to-Unskilled Ratio:** Aim for **60:40 (skilled : unskilled)** in modern yards.
> - **STCW Compliance:** All seagoing personnel must meet International Maritime Organization standards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Shipyard Human Resource

A shipyard workforce is categorised into **four hierarchical tiers**, each requiring specific skills, training, and certification.

### Tier 1: Managerial & Engineering Cadre (White-Collar)
- **Naval Architects** — Design hull form, stability, structural integrity.
- **Marine Engineers** — Machinery, propulsion, and systems design.
- **Production Engineers / Planning Engineers** — Workflow scheduling, resource allocation.
- **Quality Assurance (QA) / Quality Control (QC) Engineers** — Weld inspection, NDT (Non-Destructive Testing), classification society compliance.
- **Safety Officers** — Hazard identification, PPE enforcement, accident prevention.

### Tier 2: Supervisory Cadre (Blue-Collar Supervisors)
- **Foremen** — Lead teams of 8–15 workers in specific trades.
- **Chargehands** — Senior tradesmen supervising small groups.
- **Welding Supervisors / Painting Supervisors** — Trade-specific quality control.

### Tier 3: Skilled Tradesmen (The Backbone of the Yard)
- **Welders** (TIG, MIG, Stick — coded per **AWS / ASME standards**).
- **Fitters** (Structural alignment, plate fitting).
- **Plumbers / Pipe Fitters** (Bilge, ballast, fuel, fire-fighting systems).
- **Electricians** (Power, navigation, communication cabling).
- **Machinists** (Engine lathe, milling, boring operations).
- **Carpenters / Joiners** (Cabin furnishing, decking, insulation).
- **Painters / Corrosion Technicians** (Surface preparation, anti-fouling).
- **Sheet Metal Workers / Platers** (Ducting, ventilation, light metal work).

### Tier 4: Semi-Skilled & Unskilled Workers
- **Helpers** — Assist skilled tradesmen with material handling.
- **Riggers** — Set up cranes, slings, and lifting gear.
- **Cleaners / General Labourers** — Yard housekeeping, scrap removal.
- **Trainees / Apprentices** — Under vocational training programmes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Rule of Thumb for Modern Yards:**
> **Managerial : Supervisory : Skilled : Unskilled = 5 : 10 : 55 : 30**
> Out of every **100 workers**, expect **5 managers, 10 supervisors, 55 skilled, 30 unskilled**.

## 2.2 Training & Certification Framework

### 2.2.1 Categories of Training
- **Pre-employment Training:** Foundation skills (safety, basic tools, reading drawings).
- **Apprenticeship Training:** 2–3 year on-the-job learning under master craftsmen.
- **Skill Upgradation:** Periodic re-certification (e.g., welder re-qualification every 6 months).
- **Safety Training:** Confined space entry, working at height, hot work permits, fire-fighting.
- **Managerial Training:** Leadership, project management, Lean / Six Sigma for engineers.

### 2.2.2 International Standards Governing HR in Shipyards
- **IMO STCW Convention** — Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping (for seafarers).
- **ISO 45001** — Occupational Health & Safety Management Systems.
- **ILO Convention 152** — Occupational Safety in Dockwork.
- **AWS D1.1 / ASME Section IX** — Welding qualification.
- **Class Society Rules** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV) — Worker certification for specific tasks.

## 2.3 Productivity Metrics in Shipyard HR

Productivity is the **quantitative measure of workforce efficiency**. The two most critical metrics in shipbuilding are:

### 2.3.1 Man-Hours Per Tonne (Labour Intensity)
This measures how many man-hours are required to build one tonne of ship.

$$ \text{Man-hours per Tonne} = \frac{\text{Total Direct Labour Hours}}{\text{Steel Weight Fabricated (tonnes)}} $$

### 2.3.2 Overall Labour Productivity
This measures output per worker per unit time.

$$ \text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output (CGT or DWT)}}{\text{Total Man-hours Worked}} $$

### 2.3.3 Labour Cost Ratio
The fraction of total ship cost attributable to labour.

$$ \text{Labour Cost Ratio} = \frac{\text{Total Labour Cost (₹/\$)}}{\text{Total Ship Cost (₹/\$)}} \times 100\% $$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

| Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Man-hours per Tonne** | $\text{MH/T} = \frac{\text{Total MH}}{\text{Steel Tonnes}}$ | Measures labour intensity of fabrication |
| **Productivity (Output/Hour)** | $P = \frac{\text{CGT or DWT}}{\text{MH}}$ | Yard efficiency benchmarking |
| **Labour Cost Ratio** | $\text{LCR} = \frac{C_L}{C_T} \times 100$ | Cost engineering, tendering |
| **Required Workforce Size** | $N = \frac{\text{Total Project MH}}{\text{Available Days} \times \text{Hours per Day} \times \text{Attendance Factor}}$ | Manpower planning |
| **Overtime Factor** | $\text{OT Factor} = 1 + 0.5 \times \frac{\text{OT Hours}}{\text{Regular Hours}}$ | Wage calculation |
| **Attendance Factor** | $\text{AF} = \frac{\text{Actual Days Present}}{\text{Total Working Days}}$ | Workforce availability |
| **Skilled-to-Unskilled Ratio** | $\text{S:U} = 60:40$ (modern) | Workforce composition |
| **Safety Incident Rate** | $\text{LTIFR} = \frac{\text{Reportable Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total MH Worked}}$ | OSHA / ILO standard metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Markdown Tables:** In the formula column, vertical bar symbols are replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table renderer. In handwritten exams, students may freely use `|` for absolute value.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tendering & Cost Estimation:** Shipyards use man-hour standards (e.g., per tonne of steel, per piping system) to quote competitive prices to shipowners.
- **Yard Capacity Planning:** Knowing productivity helps determine how many ships a yard can deliver per year.
- **Lean Manufacturing:** Identifying non-value-adding labour (waiting, transport, over-processing) reduces waste.
- **Safety Engineering:** LTIFR (Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate) is a **mandatory KPI** in shipyards worldwide.
- **Government Policy:** National shipbuilding subsidies (e.g., India's *Shipbuilding Financial Assistance Policy*) use local content and employment metrics for eligibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters Beyond the Exam:**
> Countries like **South Korea, Japan, and China** dominate global shipbuilding partly because of **highly trained, productive workforces**. India aims to capture 5% of the global market — and that goal is **fundamentally an HR challenge**, not just a steel or technology challenge.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating Required Workforce Size for a Shipbuilding Project

### Problem Setup
A shipyard has won a contract to build a **Bulk Carrier** of **30,000 DWT**. The estimated total direct labour content is **1.2 million man-hours**. The project must be completed in **12 months**. The yard operates **26 working days per month** with **8 working hours per day**. Worker attendance factor is **0.90**. Determine the **minimum workforce size** required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the governing formula.**

$$ N = \frac{\text{Total Project Man-hours (MH)}}{\text{Available Working Days} \times \text{Hours per Day} \times \text{Attendance Factor}} $$

**Step 2: Substitute the known values.**
- Total Project MH = 1,200,000
- Working Days = 12 × 26 = 312 days
- Hours per Day = 8 hours
- Attendance Factor = 0.90

$$ N = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{312 \times 8 \times 0.90} $$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator.**

$$ 312 \times 8 = 2{,}496 $$

$$ 2{,}496 \times 0.90 = 2{,}246.4 $$

**Step 4: Compute the final workforce number.**

$$ N = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{2{,}246.4} = 534.2 $$

**Step 5: Round up to the next whole number** (you cannot deploy half a worker).

$$ N = 535 \text{ workers (minimum)} $$

### Interpretation
The yard needs **at least 535 workers** working at full attendance to complete the bulk carrier in 12 months. In practice, the yard would deploy **600–650 workers** to account for absenteeism, holidays, and skill-mix balance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Productivity Comparison Between Two Shipyards

### Problem Setup
- **Yard A (South Korea):** Built a 50,000 DWT tanker in **800,000 man-hours**.
- **Yard B (Emerging Market):** Built the same vessel in **1,400,000 man-hours**.
- Steel weight of vessel = **8,000 tonnes**.

Compute and compare the man-hours per tonne for each yard.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the man-hours per tonne formula.**

$$ \text{MH/T} = \frac{\text{Total Man-hours}}{\text{Steel Weight in Tonnes}} $$

**Step 2: Calculate for Yard A.**

$$ \text{MH/T}_A = \frac{800{,}000}{8{,}000} = 100 \text{ MH/tonne} $$

**Step 3: Calculate for Yard B.**

$$ \text{MH/T}_B = \frac{1{,}400{,}000}{8{,}000} = 175 \text{ MH/tonne} $$

**Step 4: Compute the productivity gap.**

$$ \text{Gap} = \frac{175 - 100}{100} \times 100\% = 75\% $$

### Interpretation
Yard B requires **75% more labour** than Yard A to build the same ship. This is a clear productivity gap that can be addressed by:
1. **Better training** (especially welders and fitters).
2. **Adoption of pre-fabrication / block construction techniques.**
3. **Improved workshop layout** (reduce material movement).
4. **Use of mechanised / robotic welding.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Calculating Labour Cost Ratio for a Tendering Exercise

### Problem Setup
A shipyard quotes a vessel at the following costs:
- Steel & Material = ₹180 crore
- Equipment & Machinery = ₹90 crore
- Direct Labour = ₹80 crore
- Overheads = ₹50 crore

Compute the **Labour Cost Ratio**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the total ship cost.**

$$ C_T = 180 + 90 + 80 + 50 = ₹400 \text{ crore} $$

**Step 2: Apply the Labour Cost Ratio formula.**

$$ \text{LCR} = \frac{C_L}{C_T} \times 100\% $$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$ \text{LCR} = \frac{80}{400} \times 100\% = 20\% $$

### Interpretation
Labour contributes **20% of the total ship cost** in this case. This is within the typical range (**20–40%**) for global shipyards. Indian and Chinese yards typically operate at the lower end (**15–25%**), while European yards (e.g., Germany, Norway) operate at the higher end (**35–50%**) due to higher wages offset by automation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Overtime Wage Calculation

### Problem Setup
A welder works **200 regular hours** and **40 overtime hours** in a month. Regular wage = ₹150/hour. Overtime is paid at **1.5×** the regular rate. Calculate total monthly wage.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute regular wage.**

$$ W_{\text{regular}} = 200 \times 150 = ₹30{,}000 $$

**Step 2: Compute overtime wage.**

$$ W_{\text{OT}} = 40 \times (1.5 \times 150) = 40 \times 225 = ₹9{,}000 $$

**Step 3: Compute total wage.**

$$ W_{\text{total}} = 30{,}000 + 9{,}000 = ₹39{,}000 $$

### Interpretation
Overtime adds **30% extra cost** to the welder's regular wage for just 20% extra hours. Shipyards must carefully balance overtime use — it inflates labour cost and increases fatigue-related safety incidents.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Factors Affecting Shipyard HR Productivity

| Factor | Effect on Productivity | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Worker skill level** | Highly skilled workers are 2–3× more productive | Continuous training, certification |
| **Tooling & equipment condition** | Poor tools cause rework & delays | Preventive maintenance, modern equipment |
| **Workplace ergonomics** | Fatigue reduces output by 15–25% | Proper lighting, ventilation, rest cycles |
| **Material availability** | Waiting for material wastes 10–20% of time | Just-in-Time (JIT) delivery, kanban systems |
| **Supervision quality** | Good supervisors reduce errors by 30% | Leadership training for foremen |
| **Safety climate** | Accidents stop work, demoralise teams | Strict PPE, safety drills, ISO 45001 |
| **Climate / weather** | Heat, rain reduce outdoor productivity | Covered work areas, climate-controlled shops |
| **Worker morale / wages** | Low pay → high attrition → low productivity | Competitive wages, welfare measures, ESIC, PF |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In shipbuilding, productivity is rarely limited by the worker's physical ability — it is limited by **systemic inefficiencies** (waiting, rework, poor planning, lack of materials). This is why **Lean Shipbuilding** and **Production Planning** are as important as worker training.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shipyard Human Resource Organisational Chart

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **functional hierarchy and information flow** of a typical shipyard's Human Resource structure. This is the *block-level functional architecture* mapping the interactions between the different categories of personnel (as physical drawing of a shipyard is not feasible in this format).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SHIPYARD MANAGING DIRECTOR] --> B[DIRECTOR PRODUCTION]
    A --> C[DIRECTOR ENGINEERING]
    A --> D[DIRECTOR HR AND ADMIN]
    A --> E[DIRECTOR FINANCE]
    A --> F[DIRECTOR QUALITY AND SAFETY]

    B --> B1[PRODUCTION PLANNER]
    B --> B2[BLOCK FABRICATION HEAD]
    B --> B3[OUTFITTING HEAD]
    B --> B4[ERECTION AND LAUNCH HEAD]

    C --> C1[NAVAL ARCHITECT]
    C --> C2[MARINE ENGINEER]
    C --> C3[ELECTRICAL ENGINEER]

    D --> D1[HR MANAGER]
    D --> D2[TRAINING AND DEVELOPMENT OFFICER]
    D --> D3[WELFARE OFFICER]
    D --> D4[RECRUITMENT OFFICER]

    F --> F1[QA QC ENGINEER]
    F --> F2[SAFETY OFFICER]
    F --> F3[NDT INSPECTOR]

    B1 --> G1[FOREMAN WELDING]
    B1 --> G2[FOREMAN FITTING]
    B1 --> G3[FOREMAN PAINTING]
    B1 --> G4[FOREMAN PIPING]

    G1 --> H1[SKILLED WELDER]
    G1 --> H2[HELPER AND TRAINEE]

    G2 --> H3[SKILLED FITTER]
    G2 --> H4[HELPER AND TRAINEE]

    G3 --> H5[SKILLED PAINTER]
    G3 --> H6[HELPER AND TRAINEE]

    G4 --> H7[SKILLED PIPE FITTER]
    G4 --> H8[HELPER AND TRAINEE]

    F2 --> H1
    F2 --> H3
    F2 --> H5
    F2 --> H7

    D1 --> G1
    D1 --> G2
    D1 --> G3
    D1 --> G4
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow: HR Lifecycle in a Shipyard Project

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[RECRUITMENT] --> P2[TRAINING AND CERTIFICATION]
    P2 --> P3[DEPLOYMENT TO TRADE]
    P3 --> P4[WORK EXECUTION]
    P4 --> P5[PERFORMANCE EVALUATION]
    P5 --> P6[SKILL UPGRADATION]
    P6 --> P3
    P4 --> P7[SAFETY MONITORING]
    P7 --> P4
    P5 --> P8[WAGE AND PROMOTION DECISION]
    P8 --> P3
```

**Explanation of the HR Lifecycle Flow:**

- **P1 → Recruitment:** Sourcing workers from ITIs (Industrial Training Institutes), polytechnics, naval architecture colleges, and apprentice programmes.
- **P2 → Training & Certification:** Skill training (e.g., welder qualification per AWS D1.1), safety induction, trade tests.
- **P3 → Deployment:** Workers assigned to specific trades (welding, fitting, piping, electrical, painting) based on skill.
- **P4 → Work Execution:** Actual production — cutting, welding, fitting, painting, etc.
- **P5 → Performance Evaluation:** KPIs such as output per shift, defect rate, attendance, safety compliance.
- **P6 → Skill Upgradation:** Periodic re-training, advanced welding courses, supervisor development.
- **P7 → Safety Monitoring:** Continuous PPE checks, accident reporting, near-miss analysis.
- **P8 → Wage & Promotion:** Increment, bonus, promotion based on performance and skill level.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Map: Manpower Planning Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[CONTRACT AWARDED] --> Q2[ESTIMATE TOTAL MAN HOURS]
    Q2 --> Q3[DETERMINE PROJECT DURATION]
    Q3 --> Q4[COMPUTE WORKFORCE SIZE]
    Q4 --> Q5[SKILL MIX ANALYSIS]
    Q5 --> Q6[RECRUITMENT AND TRAINING]
    Q6 --> Q7[DEPLOYMENT AND MONITORING]
    Q7 --> Q8[PRODUCTIVITY TRACKING]
    Q8 --> Q9[CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    Q9 --> Q7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Diagram Matters in the KTU Exam:**
> Examiners frequently test students on the **sequence of manpower planning**. The flow is: **Contract → Estimate MH → Decide Duration → Compute Workforce → Skill Mix → Recruit/Train → Deploy → Monitor → Correct.** Memorising this sequence is a guaranteed 2–3 marks in a typical Part A question.
