# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Human Resource in the context of ship construction. List any four categories of shipyard workers.**

**Model Answer:**

Human Resource in ship construction refers to the entire workforce involved in the design, fabrication, assembly, outfitting, and repair of ships — including engineers, supervisors, skilled tradesmen, semi-skilled workers, and unskilled laborers.

**Four categories of shipyard workers:**
1. **Managerial & Engineering Cadre** — Naval Architects, Marine Engineers, Production Engineers.
2. **Supervisory Cadre** — Foremen, Chargehands, Welding Supervisors.
3. **Skilled Tradesmen** — Welders, Fitters, Pipe Fitters, Electricians, Machinists.
4. **Semi-Skilled / Unskilled** — Helpers, Riggers, Cleaners, Apprentices.

*(Word limit: ~80 words. Mention hierarchical role.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by 'Man-hours per Tonne' in shipbuilding productivity measurement? What is the typical range for modern shipyards?**

**Model Answer:**

Man-hours per Tonne (MH/T) is a standard productivity metric in shipbuilding that measures the **total direct labour hours required to fabricate one tonne of steel** in a ship.

$$ \text{MH/T} = \frac{\text{Total Direct Man-hours}}{\text{Steel Weight (tonnes)}} $$

**Typical Range:**
- **Modern automated yards (South Korea, Japan):** 30–80 MH/tonne.
- **Semi-automated yards (China, India):** 100–150 MH/tonne.
- **Traditional / small yards:** 200–300 MH/tonne.

Lower values indicate higher productivity and competitiveness.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the classification of shipyard human resource into different categories. Discuss the role and responsibilities of skilled tradesmen in ship construction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard has to fabricate 5,000 tonnes of steel for a new vessel in 8 months. The yard operates 25 days per month, 8 hours per day. Worker attendance factor is 0.85. Calculate the minimum workforce required. Also compute the man-hours per tonne. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** What are the various training programmes required for shipyard workers? Explain the role of international standards (STCW, ISO 45001) in workforce certification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two shipyards build identical 40,000 DWT bulk carriers. Yard X uses 900,000 man-hours, Yard Y uses 1,500,000 man-hours. The vessel's steel weight is 9,000 tonnes. Compute and compare the man-hours per tonne and the productivity ratio. Suggest two methods to improve Yard Y's productivity. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification of Shipyard HR

The shipyard workforce is classified into **four hierarchical tiers:**

**1. Managerial & Engineering Cadre (White-Collar, ~5% of workforce):**
- Naval Architects: design hull form, stability, structural drawings.
- Marine Engineers: design propulsion, auxiliary systems.
- Production / Planning Engineers: schedule workflows.
- QA/QC Engineers: ensure compliance with class society rules.
- Safety Officers: enforce health and safety standards.

**2. Supervisory Cadre (~10% of workforce):**
- Foremen lead teams of 8–15 workers in specific trades.
- Welding Supervisors ensure weld quality as per WPS (Welding Procedure Specification).
- Chargehands assist foremen and act as senior tradesmen.

**3. Skilled Tradesmen (~55% of workforce) — The Backbone:**
- **Welders:** Join steel plates and sections. Code-certified per AWS D1.1 / ASME IX.
- **Fitters:** Align structural members, check dimensional accuracy.
- **Pipe Fitters:** Install bilge, ballast, fuel, fire-fighting, and sanitary systems.
- **Electricians:** Install power, lighting, navigation, and communication cables.
- **Machinists:** Operate lathes, milling, drilling machines for engine parts.
- **Painters / Corrosion Technicians:** Surface prep, primer, anti-fouling application.

**4. Semi-Skilled & Unskilled Workers (~30% of workforce):**
- Helpers, Riggers, Cleaners, Apprentices.
- Assist skilled workers with material handling, scaffolding, and housekeeping.

**Role & Responsibilities of Skilled Tradesmen:**
- Execute the **actual transformation of raw materials into ship components.**
- Interpret **engineering drawings** and work instructions.
- Maintain **quality standards** set by QA/QC and classification societies.
- Follow **safety protocols** (PPE, hot work permits, lock-out-tag-out).
- Provide **apprenticeship training** to trainees.
- Report **defects, near-misses, and process improvements** to supervisors.

---

#### Part (b) — Workforce Size & Man-Hours per Tonne Calculation

**Given:**
- Steel to fabricate = 5,000 tonnes
- Project duration = 8 months
- Working days = 25 days/month
- Hours per day = 8 hours
- Attendance factor = 0.85

**Step 1: Calculate total available hours per worker.**

$$ \text{Available Hours per Worker} = 8 \times 25 \times 8 = 1{,}600 \text{ hours} $$

**Step 2: Apply the workforce formula.**

$$ N = \frac{\text{Total Project Man-hours}}{\text{Available Days} \times \text{Hours per Day} \times \text{AF}} $$

First, we need total project man-hours. Using industry average of **120 MH/tonne** (semi-automated yard):

$$ \text{Total MH} = 5{,}000 \times 120 = 600{,}000 \text{ MH} $$

**Step 3: Substitute and solve.**

$$ N = \frac{600{,}000}{8 \times 25 \times 8 \times 0.85} $$

$$ N = \frac{600{,}000}{1{,}360} = 441.2 $$

$$ N \approx 442 \text{ workers (minimum)} $$

**Step 4: Man-hours per tonne (using the assumed 120 MH/tonne):**

$$ \text{MH/T} = \frac{600{,}000}{5{,}000} = 120 \text{ MH/tonne} $$

**Final Answer:** The yard requires a **minimum of 442 workers**, and the labour intensity is **120 man-hours per tonne** (consistent with semi-automated Indian yards).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to multiply the attendance factor. This inflates the workforce estimate by 15% — examiners deduct **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Students confuse "man-hours per tonne" with "man-hours per ship" — these are different metrics. Always state units clearly.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Rounding down instead of up. You cannot have 0.2 of a worker — always **round up** to the next whole number.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Not clearly stating assumptions (e.g., MH/tonne baseline). Examiners reward stated assumptions.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Training Programmes & International Standards

**Training Programmes Required:**

**1. Pre-Employment / Induction Training:**
- Yard safety rules, PPE usage, emergency procedures.
- Basic tool handling, reading engineering drawings.
- Duration: 1–2 weeks.

**2. Apprenticeship / Vocational Training:**
- Conducted via **Industrial Training Institutes (ITIs)** and **polytechnics**.
- Trades: Welder, Fitter, Turner, Electrician, Painter, Pipe Fitter.
- Duration: 2–3 years (Trade Apprentice Act, 1961 in India).

**3. Trade-Specific Skill Training:**
- Welder qualification per **AWS D1.1, ASME Section IX**.
- NDT training (UT, RT, MPI, DPI) for inspectors.
- Pipe fitting per **ASME B31.1** (power piping) and class rules.

**4. Safety Training:**
- Working at heights, confined space entry, hot work permits.
- Fire-fighting, first aid, hazard communication (GHS).
- OSHA / ISO 45001 aligned.

**5. Skill Upgradation & Re-certification:**
- Welder re-qualification every 6 months.
- New technology training (e.g., robotic welding, CNC plasma cutting).
- Supervisor / foreman development programmes.

**6. Managerial / Leadership Training:**
- Production planning, Lean / Six Sigma, project management.
- Cross-functional training for engineers.

**Role of International Standards:**

- **IMO STCW (Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping):**
  - Governs the training and certification of **seafarers** (engine room, deck officers).
  - Mandatory for any seafarer serving on internationally trading ships.
  - Covers fire-fighting, first aid, survival craft, security awareness.

- **ISO 45001 (Occupational Health & Safety Management Systems):**
  - Replaces OHSAS 18001.
  - Sets requirements for systematic safety management in shipyards.
  - Emphasises leadership, worker participation, risk-based thinking.
  - Reduces accidents, LTIFR, and improves worker morale.

- **Other Standards:**
  - **AWS D1.1** — Structural welding code (welder qualification).
  - **ASME Section IX** — Welding and brazing qualifications.
  - **Class Society Rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) — Worker certification for specific shipboard tasks.

---

#### Part (b) — Productivity Comparison of Two Yards

**Given:**
- Vessel: 40,000 DWT bulk carrier
- Steel weight: 9,000 tonnes
- Yard X man-hours: 900,000 MH
- Yard Y man-hours: 1,500,000 MH

**Step 1: Calculate MH/tonne for Yard X.**

$$ \text{MH/T}_X = \frac{900{,}000}{9{,}000} = 100 \text{ MH/tonne} $$

**Step 2: Calculate MH/tonne for Yard Y.**

$$ \text{MH/T}_Y = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{9{,}000} = 166.67 \text{ MH/tonne} $$

**Step 3: Calculate the productivity ratio (Yard X : Yard Y).**

$$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{100}{166.67} = 0.60 $$

This means Yard X is **1/0.60 = 1.67 times more productive** than Yard Y. Equivalently, Yard Y is **66.67% less productive** than Yard X.

**Step 4: Identify the productivity gap in absolute terms.**

$$ \Delta \text{MH/tonne} = 166.67 - 100 = 66.67 \text{ MH/tonne} $$

This means Yard Y wastes an extra **66.67 man-hours for every tonne of steel** — translating to **6,00,000 extra man-hours** for the entire vessel.

**Step 5: Two methods to improve Yard Y's productivity:**

**Method 1: Skill Upgradation & Welder Re-certification:**
Yard Y likely has a high proportion of semi-skilled workers. Investing in **welder training (AWS D1.1 certification)**, **NDT training**, and **fitter training** will reduce rework and increase output per worker. A well-trained welder produces 2–3× more defect-free welds per shift than an untrained one.

**Method 2: Adoption of Pre-fabrication and Block Construction:**
Yard Y is likely building the ship in a "stick" fashion on the slipway. Switching to **block construction** (building large 3D assemblies in workshops with cranes, then joining them on the berth) will:
- Shift work to covered, mechanised shops (improving productivity by 30–50%).
- Reduce work at height and weather-related delays.
- Enable parallel work streams, compressing the project schedule.

**Additional suggestions** *(for extra marks)*: Lean manufacturing implementation, automated cutting/welding, better material flow, ergonomic workstation design, performance-linked wages.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **DWT** (Deadweight Tonnage) with **CGT** (Compensated Gross Tonnage). DWT measures carrying capacity; CGT measures work content. For productivity calculations, **CGT is the preferred metric**, but steel-tonnage is acceptable.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Not stating which yard is more productive. Always end with a **clear conclusion** — "Yard X is 1.67× more productive than Yard Y."
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Vague improvement suggestions like "train workers better." Examiners want **specific, actionable** methods (e.g., *"AWS D1.1 welder re-certification programme"*).
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Calculation errors in ratio — remember productivity is **inversely proportional** to MH/tonne.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION — Pin This in Your Mind Before the Exam:**

**Core Definitions:**
- **Human Resource** = total workforce (managerial + supervisory + skilled + unskilled) in ship construction.
- **Man-hours per Tonne (MH/T)** = Total MH ÷ Steel weight — measures labour intensity.
- **Productivity** = Output (CGT/DWT) ÷ Man-hours.
- **LTIFR** = (Injuries × 10⁶) ÷ Man-hours — safety KPI.
- **Attendance Factor** = Actual days present ÷ Total working days (~0.85–0.95 typical).

**Workforce Composition Rule (Modern Yard):**
- Managerial : Supervisory : Skilled : Unskilled = **5 : 10 : 55 : 30**
- Skilled : Unskilled = **60 : 40** (target ratio).

**Productivity Benchmarks (MH/tonne of steel):**
- World-class yards (S. Korea, Japan): **30–80 MH/T**
- Mid-tier (China, India): **100–150 MH/T**
- Traditional yards: **200–300 MH/T**

**Key International Standards (Must-Memorise):**
- **STCW** (IMO) — seafarer certification.
- **ISO 45001** — shipyard OHS management.
- **AWS D1.1** — structural welding code.
- **ASME Section IX** — welder qualification.

**HR Lifecycle:** Recruit → Train → Deploy → Work → Evaluate → Upgrade → (loop back to Deploy).

**Manpower Planning Sequence:** Contract → Estimate MH → Duration → Workforce Size → Skill Mix → Recruit/Train → Deploy → Monitor → Corrective Action.

**Cost Engineering Note:** Labour cost is typically **20–40% of total ship cost**. Overtime costs 1.5× regular wage.

**Safety Mantra:** *"In shipbuilding, an unsafe yard is an unproductive yard."* LTIFR target for world-class yards: **< 1.0 per million man-hours**.

**One-Line Exam Punch Lines (Use These for Full Marks):**
1. *"A shipyard is a symphony — and HR management is the conductor."*
2. *"Skilled tradesmen form the backbone of any shipyard — comprising ~55% of total workforce."*
3. *"Lower MH/tonne = higher productivity = greater global competitiveness."*
4. *"Manpower planning is a closed-loop cycle: plan → deploy → monitor → correct → re-plan."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Control (QC)** in ship construction is a systematic, planned set of operational techniques and inspection activities used to fulfill, verify, and document quality requirements for materials, fabricated components, and the final vessel, ensuring compliance with the rules of classification societies, the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), and the contract specifications between the shipyard and the owner.

According to the **ISO 8402:1994** (superseded by ISO 9000:2015) terminology, Quality Control is defined as *"the operational techniques and activities that are used to fulfill requirements for quality."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, QC is treated as an **operational engineering discipline** that bridges design intent (engineering drawings, class rules) and physical realization (welded plates, assembled blocks, launched vessel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> Quality Control in shipbuilding covers the framework of standards, inspection methods, statistical process control, classification society surveys, and documentation that collectively ensure a vessel meets safety, seaworthiness, and performance criteria.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a large commercial batch of biscuits for a national brand:
- **Quality Assurance (QA)** is the *recipe book* — it documents the standards, ingredients, oven temperature, and procedures.
- **Quality Control (QC)** is the *chef constantly tasting, weighing, and inspecting* the biscuits during baking to make sure they are not burnt, undercooked, or misshapen.

In shipbuilding, the **"recipe"** is the class-approved drawing set and the shipbuilding specification. The **"tasting chef"** is the QC inspector with ultrasonic gauges, micrometers, dye-penetrant spray cans, and documentation pads. Without QC, even a perfect design can become a defective ship due to a single bad weld.

## 1.3 Key Stakeholders and Standard-Making Bodies

| Stakeholder | Role |
|---|---|
| **Classification Societies** (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas, IRS, KR) | Establish technical rules for hull, machinery, and safety. Conduct statutory surveys. |
| **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** | Provides generic QMS standards (e.g., **ISO 9001:2015**). |
| **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** | Harmonizes rules across major class societies. |
| **Flag State / Maritime Administration** | Enforces statutory conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line). |
| **Shipowner** | Imposes additional commercial/performance specifications. |
| **Shipyard QA/QC Department** | Executes day-to-day inspection and documentation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric in ship QC:** A modern shipyard is expected to maintain a weld defect rate below **1.5%** during block fabrication, with first-pass radiographic (RT) film acceptance above **96%** to be competitive in international markets.

## 1.4 Quality Control vs. Quality Assurance

| Aspect | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process-oriented (prevent defects) | Product-oriented (detect defects) |
| **Timing** | Before and during production | During and after production |
| **Tools** | Audits, procedures, training | Inspection, testing, measurement |
| **Goal** | Build quality *in* | Inspect quality *out* |
| **Documentation** | Quality Manual, Procedures | Inspection reports, test records |

> [!TIP]
> A simple rule of thumb for exams: **QA = Prevention, QC = Detection.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillars of Quality Control in Ship Construction

A robust QC system in a shipyard rests on **seven interlinked pillars**:

1. **Material Certification and Traceability** — every steel plate, profile, and consumable must carry a **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** traceable to heat number, batch, and chemical composition.
2. **Welding Quality Control** — qualification of welders, approval of Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS), and inspection of welds via NDT.
3. **Dimensional and Geometric Control** — checking tolerances on plate thickness, block dimensions, line alignment, and shaft alignment.
4. **Surface Preparation and Coating Control** — verifying blast cleaning grades (Sa 2.5 per **ISO 8501-1**), coating thickness (DFT — Dry Film Thickness), and adhesion.
5. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — Visual, Ultrasonic, Radiographic, Magnetic Particle, Dye Penetrant, Eddy Current.
6. **Documentation and Records** — Inspection and Test Plans (ITP), Quality Control Plans (QCP), hold/witness point records.
7. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — using control charts and capability indices to monitor fabrication processes.

## 2.2 The Inspection and Test Plan (ITP) Framework

The **ITP** is the master document that sequences all inspection activities throughout a shipbuilding project. It typically contains:

- **Item / Stage** — what is being inspected (e.g., shell plate, butt weld, machinery foundation).
- **Inspection Activity** — visual, dimensional, NDT, performance test.
- **Acceptance Criteria** — class rule reference, tolerance band, drawing note.
- **Code** — refers to the procedure document.
- **Hold Point (H)** — work *must stop* until the inspector releases the item.
- **Witness Point (W)** — inspector *may attend*; work may continue if not present (with prior notice).
- **Surveillance (S)** — routine periodic checks.
- **Records** — what document is generated (e.g., RT film, dimensional report).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hold Points are legally binding under IACS UR Z17** — the ship cannot proceed to the next stage until the class surveyor has stamped the relevant stage.

## 2.3 Statistical Process Control (SPC) — The Mathematics of Quality

SPC uses control charts to distinguish between **common-cause (random) variation** and **special-cause (assignable) variation**.

### 2.3.1 Control Limits for X-bar and R Charts

For a subgroup of size *n* with mean $\bar{X}$ and range *R*:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$$

$$UCL_R = D_4 \cdot \bar{R}$$

$$LCL_R = D_3 \cdot \bar{R}$$

Where:
- $\bar{\bar{X}}$ is the grand average of subgroup means.
- $\bar{R}$ is the average of subgroup ranges.
- $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ are tabulated control chart constants depending on subgroup size *n*.

### 2.3.2 Process Capability Indices

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

Where:
- $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit
- $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit
- $\mu$ = process mean
- $\sigma$ = process standard deviation

A **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is the industrial minimum; **$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$** indicates a Six-Sigma-capable process.

### 2.3.3 Six Sigma Defect Rate

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}} \times 10^6$$

$$Y = \text{Yield} = e^{-\text{DPMO}/10^6} \text{ (Poisson approximation)}$$

A Six-Sigma process allows only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.

## 2.4 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Methods

| Method | Principle | Typical Use in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual Testing (VT)** | Direct eye inspection, borescopes | Weld surface, surface prep, alignment |
| **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | High-frequency sound waves | Plate lamination, weld thickness, corrosion mapping |
| **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | X-rays / Gamma rays (Ir-192, Co-60) | Butt weld internal defects (porosity, inclusions, lack of fusion) |
| **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** | Magnetic flux leakage | Surface/near-surface cracks in ferromagnetic welds |
| **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT)** | Capillary action of penetrant | Surface cracks in non-magnetic materials (stainless, aluminum) |
| **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** | Electromagnetic induction | Surface cracks, conductivity measurement, tube inspection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance criteria** for welds in ships are governed by **IACS UR W11 / W13 / W17** and the class-specific weld quality standards. For example, ABS and DNV categorize defects into three levels: *acceptable, repairable, and rejectable.*

## 2.5 Welding QC — The Heart of Ship Fabrication

Welding consumes about **30–40%** of shipyard man-hours and is the single largest source of quality concerns. The QC framework follows:

1. **Welder Qualification** — every welder must pass an approval test on a representative joint per **ISO 9606-1** or **ASME Section IX**.
2. **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** — qualified per **ISO 15614-1** or **ASME Section IX**, covering base material, filler, preheat, interpass, and PWHT.
3. **Production Welding** — executed per WPS, monitored by welding coordinators (IWT/IWE per **IIW** standards).
4. **Weld Inspection** — 100% VT + selected NDT (typically **10–20% UT/RT** for butt welds in primary structure).
5. **Defect Repair** — documented repair procedures, re-inspection, traceability.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Control limit (X-bar chart) | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | same as measurement | SPC monitoring |
| 2 | Lower control limit | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | same as measurement | SPC monitoring |
| 3 | Range chart UCL | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | same as measurement | SPC monitoring |
| 4 | Process capability | $C_p = (USL - LSL) / (6\sigma)$ | dimensionless | Process fitness |
| 5 | Adjusted capability | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | Process centering |
| 6 | Defects per million | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{N} \times 10^6$ | defects/M-opp | Six-Sigma |
| 7 | Coating DFT measurement | $\text{Mean DFT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | microns ($\mu$m) | Corrosion control |
| 8 | Tolerance stack-up (linear) | $\Delta_{total} = \sum \vert \Delta_i \vert$ | mm | Dimensional QC |
| 9 | Acceptance number (sampling) | Based on **AQL** from **ISO 2859-1** | dimensionless | Lot inspection |
| 10 | Weld heat input | $E = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | WPS control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant values $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ for subgroup size $n=5$ are **0.577, 0, 2.114** respectively. For $n=4$: **0.729, 0, 2.282**.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Classification surveys** (Annual, Special, Continuous) are legally mandated under the SOLAS Convention and the Load Line Convention. Without proper QC records, a ship can be **detained** at any port worldwide.
- **Oil major vetting** (SIRE inspections) demands perfect QC documentation; a single missing weld record can blacklist a vessel from major charterers.
- **Hull insurance premiums** drop significantly for yards with demonstrated QC track records (low insurance *loss ratios*).
- **Offshore wind and naval vessels** demand QC traceability up to 25–30 years of operational life.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $3\sigma$ Defines Control Limits

The theoretical foundation of SPC rests on the Central Limit Theorem. For any normally distributed process, approximately:
- **68.27%** of measurements fall within $\mu \pm 1\sigma$
- **95.45%** fall within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$
- **99.73%** fall within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$

**Step-by-step derivation of the X-bar chart factor $A_2$:**

Let the subgroup size be $n$. The standard error of the subgroup mean is:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

By definition, the **Upper Control Limit** of the X-bar chart is set at 3 standard errors above the central line (process mean):

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Since the within-subgroup standard deviation $\sigma$ is not known directly, it is estimated from the average range $\bar{R}$ using the relationship:

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant depending on subgroup size $n$. Substituting:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} \cdot \bar{R}$$

Defining the **X-bar chart constant**:

$$A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

We obtain the standard textbook formula:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$$

For $n = 5$: $d_2 = 2.326$, hence $A_2 = 3 / (2.326 \times \sqrt{5}) = 0.577$, which matches the standard table.

> This derivation is frequently asked in KTU University Examinations under "Process Capability and SPC" derivations.

## 3.2 Worked Example: SPC Application in Plate Cutting

A shipyard measures the **plate length** of 25 cut plates (5 subgroups of size $n = 5$) to monitor the accuracy of its CNC cutting machine. The nominal target is **12,000 mm** with a tolerance of $\pm 5$ mm. The collected data is:

| Subgroup | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ | $X_5$ | $\bar{X}$ | $R$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12001 | 12003 | 11999 | 12002 | 12000 | 12001.0 | 4 |
| 2 | 12002 | 12000 | 12001 | 12003 | 11999 | 12001.0 | 4 |
| 3 | 11998 | 12001 | 12002 | 12000 | 12004 | 12001.0 | 6 |
| 4 | 12003 | 12000 | 12001 | 12002 | 11999 | 12001.0 | 4 |
| 5 | 12001 | 12002 | 12000 | 12003 | 11999 | 12001.0 | 4 |

**Step 1: Compute grand mean and average range.**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{12001.0 + 12001.0 + 12001.0 + 12001.0 + 12001.0}{5} = 12001.0 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{4 + 4 + 6 + 4 + 4}{5} = 4.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: For $n = 5$, retrieve chart constants from SPC table:**

$$A_2 = 0.577, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.114$$

**Step 3: Compute control limits for the X-bar chart.**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 12001.0 + (0.577 \times 4.4) = 12001.0 + 2.539 = 12003.54 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 12001.0 - 2.539 = 11998.46 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute control limits for the R chart.**

$$UCL_R = 2.114 \times 4.4 = 9.30 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_R = 0 \times 4.4 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Process capability assessment.**

Estimated process standard deviation:

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{4.4}{2.326} = 1.892 \text{ mm}$$

Capability index:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{12005 - 11995}{6 \times 1.892} = \frac{10}{11.352} = 0.881$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{12005 - 12001}{3 \times 1.892}, \frac{12001 - 11995}{3 \times 1.892}\right) = \min(0.704, 1.056) = 0.704$$

**Step 6: Interpretation.**

- Since $C_p < 1.0$, the process variation **exceeds the specification width**. The process is **not capable**.
- Since $C_{pk} = 0.704 < 1.0$, the process is **neither capable nor centered**.
- Recommendation: investigate sources of variation (cutting torch wear, plate preheating, operator skill) and re-center the machine to the target of 12,000 mm.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Acceptance Sampling by Attributes

A shipyard receives a lot of **2000 welding electrodes** for a critical structural weld. The contract specifies **AQL = 1.0%** (Acceptable Quality Level) for general inspection per **ISO 2859-1**.

**Step 1: Determine sample size code letter.**

For lot size 2001–3200, General Inspection Level II, the code letter from Table I of ISO 2859-1 is **K**.

**Step 2: Determine sample size and acceptance number.**

For code letter K, AQL = 1.0, the sample size $n = 80$, acceptance number $Ac = 2$, rejection number $Re = 3$.

**Step 3: Decision rule.**

- Randomly sample 80 electrodes.
- If defects $\leq 2$, **accept** the lot.
- If defects $\geq 3$, **reject** the lot.

This protects the yard against a "consumer's risk" of $\beta \approx 10\%$ at a process quality of approximately 4.5% defective (LTPD).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which NDT

| Defect Type | Material | Location | Best NDT Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface crack | Steel | Weld cap | MT or PT |
| Sub-surface crack | Steel | Weld root | UT |
| Porosity cluster | Steel | Butt weld | RT |
| Lamination | Steel plate | Through-thickness | UT |
| Lack of penetration | Steel | Butt joint | RT or UT |
| Crack in stainless | Stainless steel | Hull fitting weld | PT |
| Corrosion under coating | Steel | Ballast tank | UT thickness gauging |
| Fatigue crack initiation | Aluminum | Superstructure weld | ET (Eddy Current) |

> [!TIP]
> Always start with **Visual Testing (VT)** — it is the cheapest, fastest, and catches **70–80%** of all weld defects before any expensive NDT is deployed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Quality Control System in a Shipyard

The following block diagram illustrates how information and material flow through the QC system from contract signature to vessel delivery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Contract and Class Rules] --> B[Quality Plan QP]
    B --> C[Inspection and Test Plan ITP]
    C --> D[Material Receipt Inspection]
    D --> E[Cutting and Forming Stage]
    E --> F[Sub-Assembly Welding]
    F --> G[Block Fabrication]
    G --> H[NDT Inspection UT RT MT PT]
    H --> I{QC Acceptance Decision}
    I -->|Accept| J[Move to Ere Berth]
    I -->|Reject| K[Repair and Re inspect]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Erection on Slipway]
    L --> M[Launch]
    M --> N[Outfitting Stage]
    N --> O[Sea Trials]
    O --> P[Class Survey and Delivery]
    P --> Q[Final QC Documentation Dossier]

    subgraph Documentation
        R[Mill Test Certificates]
        S[WPS and PQR Records]
        T[Welder Qualifications]
        U[NDT Reports]
        V[As Built Drawings]
    end

    R --> D
    S --> F
    T --> F
    U --> H
    V --> Q
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Non-Destructive Testing Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Defect Suspected] --> Q1{Defect Location?}
    Q1 -->|Surface| Q2{Material is Magnetic?}
    Q1 -->|Sub-surface| Q3{Material Thickness?}
    Q1 -->|Through-thickness| UT[Use Ultrasonic Testing UT]

    Q2 -->|Yes| MT[Use Magnetic Particle Testing MT]
    Q2 -->|No| PT[Use Dye Penetrant Testing PT]

    Q3 -->|Less than 25 mm| RT[Use Radiographic Testing RT]
    Q3 -->|Greater than 25 mm| UT2[Use Ultrasonic Testing UT]

    MT --> Doc[Document on NDT Report]
    PT --> Doc
    RT --> Doc
    UT --> Doc
    UT2 --> Doc
    Doc --> Decision{Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    Decision -->|Yes| Release[Release for Next Stage]
    Decision -->|No| Repair[Mark and Repair]
    Repair --> Reinspect[Re-inspect per WPS]
    Reinspect --> Doc
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy Diagram: Quality Management Documentation

```mermaid
graph TD
    QM[Quality Manual Top Level] --> QP[Quality Procedures]
    QP --> WI[Work Instructions]
    QP --> ITP[Inspection and Test Plan]
    ITP --> QCP[Quality Control Plan per Item]
    QCP --> Rec[Records and Reports]
    Rec --> MTC[Mill Test Certificates]
    Rec --> WeldRec[Weld Inspection Records]
    Rec --> NDTR[NDT Reports]
    Rec --> Dim[Dimensional Reports]
    Rec --> Coat[Coating Inspection Reports]

    QM --> ISO[ISO 9001 2015 Compliance]
    ITP --> Class[Class Society Rules]
    QCP --> Spec[Project Specification]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: SPC Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Process[Shipyard Process e.g. Welding] --> Sensor[Measurement Gauges and NDT]
    Sensor --> Data[Data Collection on Subgroups]
    Data --> Chart[Control Chart X-bar R]
    Chart --> Analysis[Statistical Analysis]
    Analysis --> Decision{In Control?}
    Decision -->|Yes| Continue[Continue Process]
    Decision -->|No| Action[Identify Root Cause]
    Action --> Process
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Quality Assurance** and **Quality Control** in ship construction.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Process-oriented | Product-oriented |
| Approach | Preventive | Detective |
| Stage | Pre-production and ongoing | During and post-production |
| Tools | Procedures, audits, training | Inspection, testing, measurement |
| Documentation | Quality Manual, Procedures | Inspection reports, NDT films |
| Responsibility | Entire shipyard | QC department and class surveyors |

In shipbuilding, QA establishes the *framework* (e.g., WPS, ITP) while QC *executes* the inspections (e.g., VT, UT, RT) to verify compliance.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is an **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)**? List any four columns typically included in it.

**Model Answer:**

An **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** is a master matrix document prepared before the start of a shipbuilding project that lists every inspection activity, the responsible party, the acceptance criteria, the relevant code/standard, and the hold/witness points throughout the construction sequence.

**Four typical columns of an ITP:**

1. **Item / Stage of Construction** — e.g., shell plate, butt weld, shaft alignment.
2. **Inspection Activity** — e.g., visual, dimensional, ultrasonic, radiographic.
3. **Acceptance Criteria** — e.g., class rule reference, drawing tolerance, weld quality grade.
4. **Hold / Witness / Surveillance (H/W/S)** — defines the engagement level of the class surveyor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students describe ITP as merely a *checklist*. In reality, it is a **legally binding contract document** between the yard, owner, and classification society. Failing to honour a Hold Point (H) is a major non-conformity that can result in rework of the entire block.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** 

**(a)** Explain the **various Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods** used in ship construction with their suitability for different defect types. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A shipyard monitors the **shell plate thickness** during production. The target is 22.0 mm. Subgroup data (in mm) of size $n = 4$ for 5 subgroups is:

| Subgroup | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 22.1 | 21.9 | 22.0 | 22.2 |
| 2 | 22.3 | 21.8 | 22.1 | 22.0 |
| 3 | 21.9 | 22.2 | 22.0 | 21.8 |
| 4 | 22.0 | 22.1 | 21.9 | 22.2 |
| 5 | 22.2 | 22.0 | 21.8 | 22.1 |

Specification limits are $USL = 23.0$ mm and $LSL = 21.0$ mm. Determine the **X-bar and R control limits** and assess the **process capability** ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$). *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) NDT Methods in Ship Construction (7 marks):**

1. **Visual Testing (VT):** Direct visual examination using gauges, magnifying glasses, and borescopes. Most common first-level inspection. Catches surface defects like undercut, overlap, surface cracks, and dimensional errors. *Suitability: all welds, all stages.*

2. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT):** Uses high-frequency sound waves (1–10 MHz) injected via a probe. Defects reflect the sound and create echoes. *Suitability: sub-surface defects, lamination in plates, weld root defects, thickness gauging, corrosion mapping in ballast tanks.*

3. **Radiographic Testing (RT):** Uses X-rays or gamma rays (Ir-192, Co-60). A film is placed opposite the source. Defects appear as dark spots on the developed film. *Suitability: internal defects in butt welds — porosity, slag inclusions, lack of fusion, root porosity.*

4. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT):** The part is magnetized and fine iron particles are sprinkled. Defects cause flux leakage and particle accumulation. *Suitability: surface and near-surface cracks in ferromagnetic materials (carbon steel, low-alloy steel welds).*

5. **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT):** A coloured or fluorescent penetrant is applied, drawn into surface defects by capillary action, and a developer reveals them. *Suitability: surface cracks in non-magnetic materials (stainless steel, aluminium, copper-nickel).*

6. **Eddy Current Testing (ET):** Uses electromagnetic induction. *Suitability: surface defects in non-ferrous materials, tube inspection in heat exchangers, conductivity measurement for material sorting.*

**(b) Solution to SPC Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute subgroup means and ranges.**

| Subgroup | $\bar{X}$ | $R$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(22.1+21.9+22.0+22.2)/4 = 22.05$ | $22.2 - 21.9 = 0.3$ |
| 2 | $(22.3+21.8+22.1+22.0)/4 = 22.05$ | $22.3 - 21.8 = 0.5$ |
| 3 | $(21.9+22.2+22.0+21.8)/4 = 21.975$ | $22.2 - 21.8 = 0.4$ |
| 4 | $(22.0+22.1+21.9+22.2)/4 = 22.05$ | $22.2 - 21.9 = 0.3$ |
| 5 | $(22.2+22.0+21.8+22.1)/4 = 22.025$ | $22.2 - 21.8 = 0.4$ |

**Step 2: Grand mean and average range.**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{22.05 + 22.05 + 21.975 + 22.05 + 22.025}{5} = 22.03 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{0.3 + 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.4}{5} = 0.38 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Retrieve constants for $n = 4$:**

$$A_2 = 0.729, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.282, \quad d_2 = 2.059$$

**Step 4: Control limits for X-bar chart.**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 22.03 + (0.729 \times 0.38) = 22.03 + 0.277 = 22.307 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 22.03 - 0.277 = 21.753 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Control limits for R chart.**

$$UCL_R = 2.282 \times 0.38 = 0.867 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_R = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Process capability assessment.**

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.38}{2.059} = 0.1846 \text{ mm}$$

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{23.0 - 21.0}{6 \times 0.1846} = \frac{2.0}{1.1076} = 1.806$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{23.0 - 22.03}{3 \times 0.1846}, \frac{22.03 - 21.0}{3 \times 0.1846}\right) = \min(1.752, 1.859) = 1.752$$

**Step 7: Interpretation.**

- All subgroup means (22.05, 22.05, 21.975, 22.05, 22.025) fall **within the control limits** (21.753, 22.307). The process is **in statistical control**.
- $C_p = 1.806 > 1.33$ — the process is **highly capable** in terms of spread.
- $C_{pk} = 1.752 > 1.33$ — the process is **centered and capable**.
- The plate-cutting process is **acceptable** for producing 22.0 mm shell plates.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Classification Societies** in quality control of ships. Explain the types of surveys they conduct. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **welding quality control procedure** in shipbuilding. List the documents required under IACS UR Z17. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Role of Classification Societies in QC (7 marks):**

**Classification societies** are non-governmental organizations that establish and apply technical standards for the design, construction, and survey of ships. Major societies include **Lloyd's Register (LR), DNV, American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), Bureau Veritas (BV), Indian Register of Shipping (IRS), Korean Register (KR), ClassNK (Japan), and RINA (Italy).** The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** harmonizes their rules.

**Roles:**

1. **Rule Development:** Publish detailed rules covering hull girder strength, plate scantlings, weld quality, machinery, electrical systems, and stability.
2. **Plan Approval:** Review and approve all design drawings, calculations, and material specifications *before* construction begins.
3. **Survey During Construction:** Class surveyors attend the shipyard, witness tests, sign off hold points, and verify compliance.
4. **Certification:** Issue the **Classification Certificate**, which is essentially the ship's "fitness passport."
5. **Continuous Survey:** Conduct surveys throughout the ship's life to ensure it remains in class.

**Types of Class Surveys:**

| Survey Type | Description | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Keel Laying Survey** | Verify line, scantlings, and steel grade at start | Once at build |
| **Annual Survey (AS)** | General examination of hull, machinery, equipment | Every year |
| **Intermediate Survey (IS)** | More detailed than AS, includes internal spaces | Within 2.5–5 years |
| **Special Survey (SS)** | Very thorough, dry-docking, thickness gauging | Every 5 years |
| **Continuous Survey (CS)** | Annual rolling survey of specific items | Alternative to SS |
| **Bottom Survey / Drydock** | Hull, propeller, rudder, sea valves inspection | Within SS cycle |
| **Class Survey after Damage** | After grounding, collision, fire | Event-triggered |

**(b) Welding Quality Control Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Base Material Verification.** Confirm steel grade (e.g., AH36, DH36) from **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)**, check for surface defects, dimensional tolerance, and traceability of heat number.

**Step 2: Welder Qualification.** Every welder must be qualified for the specific joint type, position, and material per **ISO 9606-1** or **ASME Section IX**. A valid welder qualification certificate must be on file.

**Step 3: WPS and PQR.** A **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** is qualified by a **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)** per **ISO 15614-1**. The WPS defines filler metal, shielding gas, preheat temperature, interpass temperature, heat input, and PWHT requirements.

**Step 4: Production Welding.** Welding is performed strictly as per the WPS by qualified welders. The **welding coordinator** (IWT/IWE certified per IIW) supervises the operations.

**Step 5: Visual Inspection (VT).** 100% of welds are visually inspected for surface defects (cracks, undercut, porosity, profile).

**Step 6: NDT.** Selected welds undergo **UT, RT, MT, or PT** based on the ITP. Critical welds (e.g., longitudinal strength deck welds) may require **100% UT + 100% RT**.

**Step 7: Documentation and Traceability.** All records are retained for the life of the ship.

**Documents Required under IACS UR Z17:**

1. Quality Plan (QP)
2. Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)
3. Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS) with supporting PQR
4. Welder qualification certificates
5. Material certificates (Mill Test Certificates)
6. NDT procedure specifications
7. NDT reports and radiographic films
8. Non-conformance reports (NCR)
9. Repair procedures and re-inspection records
10. As-built drawings and dimensional reports
11. Calibration records of measuring equipment
12. Welding coordinator's qualification certificate

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> When answering welding QC questions, students often **omit the welding coordinator's role (IWT/IWE)** and **forget to mention PWHT (Post Weld Heat Treatment)** when discussing WPS. A perfect 7-mark answer must include the IACS UR Z17 documentation list **explicitly** and reference the **ISO 9606-1 / ISO 15614-1** standards.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **QC is detection-oriented; QA is prevention-oriented** — distinguish clearly in 3-mark questions.
- The **ITP** is the master document of QC, with **Hold (H), Witness (W), Surveillance (S)** points.
- **IACS UR Z17** governs procedural requirements for shipbuilding quality.
- **Welder qualification (ISO 9606-1)** and **WPS qualification (ISO 15614-1)** are non-negotiable.
- The **six major NDT methods** are VT, UT, RT, MT, PT, and ET — know the application of each.
- **SPC formulas** most frequently tested: $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$, $C_p$, $C_{pk}$.
- For $n=4$: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$, $d_2 = 2.059$.
- For $n=5$: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $d_2 = 2.326$.
- A process with **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is industrially acceptable; **$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$** is world-class.
- **Classification societies** conduct: Annual, Intermediate, Special, Continuous, and Bottom surveys.
- **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** ensures material traceability by **heat number** to the steelmaker.
- **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** of marine coatings is measured per **ISO 19840**.
- **Surface preparation standard** for marine coating is **ISO 8501-1** (Sa 2.5 for most shipbuilding applications).
- The **three pillars of welding QC**: qualified welder + qualified procedure + verified inspection.
- **Six-Sigma target** in modern shipyards: **3.4 DPMO** (defects per million opportunities).
- **Common student mistake:** confusing *AQL* (Acceptable Quality Level) with LTPD — AQL is the producer's risk point, not the consumer's risk.
- **Document retention:** ship QC records must be kept for the **entire operational life** of the vessel (often 25–30 years).

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Quality Assurance (QA)** in ship construction is defined as the *planned and systematic set of activities implemented within the Quality Management System (QMS)* that can be *demonstrated to provide confidence* that a product or service will satisfy given requirements for quality. It is a **proactive, process-oriented** discipline embedded across the entire shipbuilding lifecycle — from design approval, steel procurement, fabrication, outfitting, and commissioning to sea trials and delivery.

According to **ISO 9000:2015**, Quality Assurance is *"part of quality management focused on providing confidence that quality requirements will be fulfilled."* In the maritime domain, this confidence is enforced not only by shipyards but also by independent third parties such as **Classification Societies** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas) and regulatory bodies like the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**.

$$ \text{QA} = f(\text{Process Planning}, \text{Standardization}, \text{Documentation}, \text{Verification}, \text{Continuous Improvement}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QA vs QC — A Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU)**
> - **Quality Assurance (QA)**: Process-focused, preventive, system-wide. Answers *"Are we building the ship the right way?"*
> - **Quality Control (QC)**: Product-focused, detective, inspection-based. Answers *"Did we build the ship right?"*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Building a Ship is Like Baking a Cake — But 100x Larger, Stronger, and Deadlier if Faulty"**

Imagine you are running a massive bakery producing 500 wedding cakes a day. You cannot taste-test every cake after baking. Instead, you:

1. **Write down the recipe perfectly** (Standard Operating Procedures / Welding Procedure Specifications).
2. **Train your bakers rigorously** (Workmanship standards / Welder qualification tests).
3. **Check the oven temperature every hour** (Process audits / In-process inspection).
4. **Taste a slice from random cakes** (Sampling inspection / Non-Destructive Testing).
5. **Stamp the cake box with a guarantee seal** (Classification society certification).

That entire *system* — from recipe to seal — is **Quality Assurance**. Quality Control is *only* the tasting step. In shipbuilding, a single defective weld in a hull plate can cause a vessel to split in half mid-ocean, killing dozens. Hence, the QA system is non-negotiable.

## 1.3 Key Stakeholders in Shipbuilding QA

| Stakeholder | Role in QA |
|---|---|
| **Shipyard QA Department** | Implements and audits internal QMS |
| **Classification Society** | Independent verification (e.g., LR, DNV, ABS, BV) |
| **Flag State Administration** | Enforces statutory regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL) |
| **IACS** | Harmonizes class rules globally |
| **Marine Warranty Surveyor (MWS)** | Insures cargo and vessel integrity during transport |
| **Owner / Charterer Representative** | Verifies compliance with build specification |
| **Regulatory Bodies (IMO)** | Sets international maritime safety standards |

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Highlight)

- **Acceptable Weld Defect Rate**: ≤ **2%** by radiographic testing (per IACS UR W17)
- **Steel Plate Impact Test Temperature**: -**20 °C** for hull structural plates (per Common Structural Rules)
- **NDT Coverage for Butt Welds**: **100%** radiographic or ultrasonic
- **Paint Dry Film Thickness (DFT)**: typically **200–350 μm** for anti-corrosive systems

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillars of a Shipyard Quality Management System

A shipyard's QMS rests on **seven interdependent pillars**, each corresponding to a clause in **ISO 9001:2015**:

1. **Context of the Organization** — Understanding internal/external issues (shipowner demands, class rules, market trends).
2. **Leadership** — Top management commitment (yard director signs off on QA policy).
3. **Planning** — Risk-based thinking, quality objectives (e.g., reduce weld rework by 15%).
4. **Support** — Competent workforce, calibrated equipment, documented information.
5. **Operation** — Production controls, type approval, process qualification.
6. **Performance Evaluation** — Internal audits, management review, KPIs.
7. **Improvement** — CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Actions), root cause analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**: Every shipbuilding yard of repute must hold an **ISO 9001:2015 certification**, and its welding operations must comply with **ISO 3834** (Quality requirements for fusion welding of metallic materials).

## 2.2 ISO 3834 Quality Levels for Welding

| Part | Title | Application in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 3834-2** | Comprehensive Quality Requirements | Warships, LNG carriers, critical hull sections |
| **ISO 3834-3** | Standard Quality Requirements | Commercial cargo ships, superstructure |
| **ISO 3834-4** | Elementary Quality Requirements | Non-critical outfitting items, furniture |

## 2.3 The QA Documentation Hierarchy

A robust shipyard maintains a multi-tiered document pyramid:

- **Level 1 — Quality Manual**: The yard's QA policy and objectives.
- **Level 2 — Procedures**: Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS), NDT procedures, Painting procedures.
- **Level 3 — Work Instructions**: Step-by-step shop floor guidance.
- **Level 4 — Records**: Inspection reports, test certificates, traceability sheets.

## 2.4 Key Formulas & Acceptance Criteria (KTU Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Standard / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| $DPS$ | $DPS = \dfrac{A_{defect}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage ($\%$) | Defect Percentage Score in welds |
| $RT$ | $RT = \dfrac{N_{pass}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage ($\%$) | Radiographic Testing pass rate |
| $WPAR$ | $WPAR = \dfrac{N_{rework}}{N_{total\,welds}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage ($\%$) | Weld Process Acceptance Rate |
| $Z$ | $Z = 27 \cdot J_{min} \cdot e^{\lambda T}$ | Joules, $T$ in °C | Charpy V-notch impact energy criterion |
| $\sigma_{allow}$ | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{FS}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Allowable stress, $FS$ = Factor of Safety (typically 1.5–2.5) |
| $DFT$ | $DFT = WFT \times \dfrac{Solids\%}{100}$ | Micrometers ($\mu m$) | Dry Film Thickness from Wet Film |
| $AQL$ | Lot acceptance sampling threshold | Percentage ($\%$) | Acceptable Quality Level in sampling plans |
| $C_p$ | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Process Capability Index |
| $C_{pk}$ | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Dimensionless | Process Capability (centred) |
| $RPN$ | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Score (1–1000) | Risk Priority Number in FMEA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $C_{pk}$ matters in shipbuilding**: Steel plate thickness tolerances must be held tightly. A yard aims for $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (industry minimum), and $\geq 1.67$ for world-class performance.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Quality Assurance in ship construction is not bureaucratic overhead — it is the **backbone of seaworthiness**. Every bolted connection, every welded seam, every painted surface is traceable. Without QA:

- A **bulk carrier** could suffer catastrophic hull girder failure (see *MV Derbyshire* sinking, 1980).
- An **LNG carrier** could leak cryogenic cargo.
- A **naval vessel** could fail battle damage tolerance.

The QA system ensures that when a surveyor audits a yard, *every* component has a documented genesis — from raw steel certificate (3.1/3.2 mill cert) to final sea trial report.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) for Plate Cutting Tolerance

**Problem:** A shipyard's automated plasma cutting line produces hull plates with a nominal thickness specification of $20 \pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$. Quality control records over a 30-day production window show:

- Sample mean: $\mu = 19.95 \text{ mm}$
- Sample standard deviation: $\sigma = 0.12 \text{ mm}$

Calculate the process capability indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Is the process capable at the industry standard of $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the specification limits.**

The tolerance is $20 \pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$, therefore:

$$
USL = 20 + 0.5 = 20.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
LSL = 20 - 0.5 = 19.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the process spread (numerator).**

$$
USL - LSL = 20.5 - 19.5 = 1.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $C_p$ (process capability, ignoring centring).**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{1.0}{6 \times 0.12} = \frac{1.0}{0.72} = 1.389
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $C_{pk}$ (accounting for centring error).**

The process mean $\mu = 19.95 \text{ mm}$ is offset from the target $T = 20.0 \text{ mm}$ by $0.05 \text{ mm}$.

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{20.5 - 19.95}{3 \times 0.12},\; \frac{19.95 - 19.5}{3 \times 0.12} \right)
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{0.55}{0.36},\; \frac{0.45}{0.36} \right) = \min(1.528,\; 1.250)
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{pk} = 1.250}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

- $C_p = 1.389$ → Process is **statistically capable** in terms of spread.
- $C_{pk} = 1.250$ → Process is **NOT centred**. It is below the industry threshold of **1.33**.

**Engineering action required:** Recalibrate the plasma cutter to shift the mean toward $20.0 \text{ mm}$ to recover centring. Without this, approximately **6,210 ppm (parts per million)** of plates will fall outside the LSL — a **non-conformance** that triggers a class society NCR (Non-Conformance Report).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**: $C_p$ alone is **misleading** — it tells you the *spread* is fine but ignores whether the process is *aimed at the target*. Always quote $C_{pk}$ in your answers.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Weld Defect Percentage Score (DPS)

**Problem:** During ultrasonic testing of 240 butt welds in the bottom shell plating, the following defects are found:
- 4 welds with slag inclusions
- 2 welds with porosity
- 1 weld with lack of penetration
- 1 weld with under-cut exceeding tolerance

Calculate the **DPS** and decide whether the lot meets the **IACS UR W17** threshold of $\text{DPS} \leq 2\%$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Count total defective welds.**

$$
N_{defect} = 4 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 8
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DPS formula.**

$$
DPS = \frac{N_{defect}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{240} \times 100\%
$$

$$
DPS = 0.0333 \times 100\% = 3.33\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with IACS threshold.**

$$
3.33\% > 2.00\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NON-COMPLIANT}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering decision.**

- The 8 defective welds must be **repaired** (gouged out and re-welded).
- The repaired welds must be **re-tested** (100% NDT coverage on the repair zone).
- The welder(s) responsible may require **re-qualification** under **ISO 9606-1**.
- A **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** is triggered — was it procedural (WPS), human (welder), or material (plate chemistry)?

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Mistake**: Adding the percentages of individual defects. You must count **distinct defective units**, not sum their relative percentages.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Dry Film Thickness (DFT) from Wet Film

**Problem:** A yard applies an epoxy anti-corrosive coating with a wet film thickness of **250 μm**. The coating technical data sheet specifies **Solids by Volume = 60%**. Calculate the expected DFT and compare with the class requirement of **200 μm minimum**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the formula.**

$$
DFT = WFT \times \frac{\text{Solids}\%}{100}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
DFT = 250 \times \frac{60}{100} = 250 \times 0.60
$$

$$
DFT = 150 \text{ μm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with class requirement.**

$$
150 \text{ μm} < 200 \text{ μm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NON-COMPLIANT}
$$

**Step 4 — Required WFT to achieve 200 μm DFT.**

$$
WFT_{required} = \frac{DFT_{target}}{\text{Solids}\%} \times 100 = \frac{200}{60} \times 100 = 333.3 \text{ μm}
$$

The painter must apply at least **333 μm** wet film, or apply a **second coat**.


# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) in the context of ship construction. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Quality Assurance (QA) is a *proactive, process-oriented* management approach that establishes the system, procedures, and standards to *prevent* defects from occurring in ship construction. It covers the entire shipbuilding workflow — from design approval to sea trials — and aims to build quality *into* the product. Examples: (i) qualification of welding procedures per ISO 15614, (ii) welder performance qualification per ISO 9606-1.

Quality Control (QC) is a *reactive, product-oriented* activity that *detects* defects in the finished or in-process ship structure through inspection and testing. Examples: (i) ultrasonic testing of butt welds, (ii) dimensional inspection of fabricated hull blocks against the lofting template.

QA answers *"Are we building the ship the right way?"* while QC answers *"Did we build the ship right?"*

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall**: Writing only definitions without giving *ship-specific* examples costs 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of a Classification Society in shipbuilding quality assurance? Name any two classification societies.**

**Model Answer:**
A Classification Society is an *independent, non-governmental* organization that establishes and applies technical rules for the *design, construction, and survey* of ships to ensure their structural integrity and operational reliability. In QA, the classification society:

1. Approves the ship's design drawings and scantlings.
2. Reviews the shipyard's quality system during the *Newbuilding Approval* process.
3. Conducts *stage surveys* (e.g., keel laying, launch, dock, sea trial).
4. Issues the *Classification Certificate* and the *Statutory Certificates* on behalf of the flag state.

**Two classification societies:** Lloyd's Register (LR) and Det Norske Veritas (DNV). Other valid answers: American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), Bureau Veritas (BV), ClassNK, RINA, Korean Register (KR), Indian Register of Shipping (IRS).

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the seven quality management principles of ISO 9001:2015 and apply any three of them to a shipbuilding context. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The seven quality management principles of ISO 9001:2015 are:

1. **Customer Focus** — The primary focus is to meet customer (shipowner) requirements and exceed their expectations. *Shipbuilding application*: The shipyard conducts contract review meetings to ensure the build specification precisely reflects the owner's operational profile (e.g., route, cargo, speed).

2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction. *Shipbuilding application*: The Yard Director and QA Manager jointly set quality KPIs such as *"reduce hull weld rework by 20% YoY"* and communicate them through toolbox talks.

3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered people are essential. *Shipbuilding application*: Welders, fitters, and painters undergo qualification tests and continuous upskilling; their work is signed off only after individual traceability is recorded in the QMS database.

4. **Process Approach** — Desired results are achieved more efficiently when activities are managed as interrelated processes. *Shipbuilding application*: The hull erection process is decomposed into pre-erection, block joining, and post-erection phases, each with KPIs, input/output definitions, and feedback loops.

5. **Improvement** — Continuous improvement is a permanent objective. *Shipbuilding application*: The yard uses the **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) to refine its blasting and painting process after each class society surface preparation audit.

6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Decisions based on data analysis. *Shipbuilding application*: Defect density per block is tracked monthly; statistical process control (SPC) charts are used to identify trends before they escalate to non-conformities.

7. **Relationship Management** — Managing relationships with interested parties (suppliers, class, flag). *Shipbuilding application*: The yard maintains Approved Supplier Lists (ASL) and conducts annual supplier audits for steel mills, paint manufacturers, and engine makers.

*(Any three principles explained with shipbuilding context suffice for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Describe the structure and role of IACS (International Association of Classification Societies). How does IACS influence quality standards in ship construction? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Structure of IACS:**
The International Association of Classification Societies (IACS) was founded in 1968 and is headquartered in **London, UK**. It is an association of **12 member classification societies**, including LR, DNV, ABS, BV, ClassNK, RINA, KR, IRS, CRS, PRS, RSR, and TL.

IACS operates through:
- A **General Assembly** (highest decision-making body).
- A **Council** with rotating Chairmanship.
- **Technical Working Groups**, **Quality Systems Certification Committee (QSCS)**, **Panel on Verification and Certification**, and **Permanent Secretariat**.

**Role of IACS in Quality Assurance:**

1. **Harmonization of Rules** — Through *Unified Requirements (URs)*, IACS ensures that all member societies apply consistent technical standards. For example, **UR W17** governs weld defect acceptance criteria, and **UR S21** covers hull girder strength.

2. **Quality Systems Certification** — IACS certifies that each member society's own QA procedures meet **QSCS standards** — a form of *meta-QA* ensuring that the certifiers themselves are competent.

3. **Transfer of Class** — Standardized procedures allow a ship built under one class to be smoothly transferred to another, reducing duplicate surveys.

4. **Statutory Role** — IACS works closely with the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and acts as a recognized organization (RO) representing flag states for issuing statutory certificates.

5. **Continuous Improvement** — IACS publishes annual reports on quality, safety, and environmental performance benchmarks across the industry.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) List the different Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods used in ship construction. Explain Ultrasonic Testing (UT) and Radiographic Testing (RT) in detail with their advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**NDT Methods in Ship Construction:**

1. Visual Testing (VT)
2. Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)
3. Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)
4. Ultrasonic Testing (UT)
5. Radiographic Testing (RT)
6. Eddy Current Testing (ET)
7. Time of Flight Diffraction (TOFD)
8. Phased Array UT (PAUT)
9. Alternating Current Field Measurement (ACFM)
10. Positive Material Identification (PMI)

---

**Ultrasonic Testing (UT):**

- **Principle**: High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) are introduced into the weld using a piezo-electric transducer. The waves reflect from internal flaws and are displayed on a cathode ray oscilloscope (CRO) or digital screen.
- **Procedure**: The surface is cleaned, a couplant (gel) is applied, and the probe is moved along the weld in a zig-zag pattern per **IACS UR W34**.
- **Advantages**:
  - Detects *both* surface and sub-surface flaws.
  - Highly sensitive to planar defects (cracks, lack of fusion).
  - Portable, safe (no radiation hazard), fast.
  - Suitable for thick plates (> 50 mm).
- **Limitations**:
  - Requires skilled, certified operators (Level II per ISO 9712 / EN 473).
  - Surface preparation is critical — rough or painted surfaces interfere.
  - Difficult on austenitic stainless and dissimilar welds.

**Radiographic Testing (RT):**

- **Principle**: Gamma rays (Ir-192) or X-rays are passed through the weld onto a film or digital detector. Flaws appear as dark spots due to differential absorption.
- **Procedure**: The radiation source is placed on one side and the film cassette on the other; exposure time depends on thickness per **IACS UR W17**.
- **Advantages**:
  - Provides a *permanent visual record* on film.
  - Excellent for volumetric defects (porosity, slag, tungsten inclusions).
  - Can evaluate internal geometry of the flaw.
- **Limitations**:
  - **Radiation hazard** — requires strict controlled area, dosimetry, and licensed radiographers.
  - Slower and more expensive than UT.
  - Less effective on thick sections and on welds with high thermal mass.
  - Cannot detect tight laminar cracks oriented parallel to the beam.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students confuse *surface* vs *volumetric* defects. RT excels at volumetric; UT excels at planar (crack-type). Mentioning this distinction earns bonus marks.

---

**(b) What is a Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)? Explain the procedure to qualify a WPS as per ISO 15614-1. Why is welder qualification (ISO 9606-1) separately required? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Welding Procedure Specification (WPS):**
A WPS is a *documented procedure* that provides all the necessary technical details for performing a specific weld on a specific material, in a specific joint configuration. It is the *recipe* for welding, derived from a successfully tested Procedure Qualification Record (PQR).

Typical WPS content:
- Base metal grade and thickness range
- Joint type and preparation
- Filler metal classification
- Shielding gas composition
- Preheat and interpass temperatures
- Welding positions (1G, 2G, 3G, 4G, etc.)
- Electrical parameters (current, voltage, travel speed)
- Heat input calculation: $H = \dfrac{60 \times V \times I}{1000 \times S}$ in kJ/mm

---

**WPS Qualification Procedure per ISO 15614-1:**

1. **Preliminary Step**: Identify the joint, material, and welding process.
2. **PQR Preparation**: Weld a test coupon under *proposed* parameters.
3. **Non-Destructive Testing**: Subject the test coupon to VT, UT, and/or RT.
4. **Destructive Testing**:
   - **Transverse tensile test** (2 specimens) — must meet base metal strength.
   - **Bend test** (2 root + 2 face) — must show ≥ 3 mm bend radius without cracks.
   - **Charpy V-notch impact test** at the design temperature — must meet $27 \text{ J}$ minimum.
   - **Macro-examination** to verify fusion and absence of defects.
5. **PQR Documentation**: All measured values are recorded in the PQR.
6. **WPS Issuance**: The WPS is issued within the ranges validated by the PQR.
7. **Range of Qualification**: ISO 15614-1 defines what *thickness ranges* and *material groups* the qualified WPS covers.

---

**Why Welder Qualification is Separately Required (ISO 9606-1):**

A WPS qualifies the *procedure*, but not the *human*. A welder's skill must be independently demonstrated because:

1. **Manual Skill Variability** — Two welders following the same WPS will produce different weld bead profiles. The welder's *dexterity, posture, and arc control* are human factors.
2. **Welding Position Competence** — A welder qualified in 1G (flat) cannot weld in 3G (vertical) without re-qualification.
3. **Material Specificity** — A welder qualified on carbon steel may not be qualified on stainless or high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steel.
4. **Re-qualification Period** — Validity typically **6 months to 2 years** depending on continuity of work; otherwise the welder must be re-tested.
5. **Legal and Insurance Defensibility** — In the event of a casualty, the *class society* and *flag state* will require valid welder certificates for traceability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Conflating WPS qualification (procedure) with welder qualification (person). The WPS can be perfect but if the welder is unqualified, the weld is **invalid** under class rules.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — KTU 2024 Exam Sprint**

- ✅ **QA = process-oriented (prevention)**; **QC = product-oriented (detection)**. Remember the analogy of the bakery.
- ✅ Every modern shipyard holds **ISO 9001:2015** certification; welding operations follow **ISO 3834** (parts 2/3/4).
- ✅ **IACS** = 12 member societies; issues *Unified Requirements (URs)*; certifies member societies' own QA (QSCS).
- ✅ **Classification Societies**: LR, DNV, ABS, BV, ClassNK, RINA, KR, IRS — name any two for a 3-mark question.
- ✅ **Documentation Hierarchy**: Quality Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records.
- ✅ **Key Formulas** to memorize:
  - $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$
  - $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$
  - $DPS = \dfrac{N_{defect}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$
  - $DFT = WFT \times \dfrac{\text{Solids}\%}{100}$
  - $H = \dfrac{60 V I}{1000 S}$ (Heat input in kJ/mm)
- ✅ **NDT Methods**: VT, MT, DPT, UT, RT, ET, TOFD, PAUT, ACFM, PMI. Know UT and RT in depth.
- ✅ **WPS (ISO 15614-1)** qualifies the *procedure*; **Welder Qualification (ISO 9606-1)** qualifies the *person*. Both are mandatory.
- ✅ **Charpy V-notch** minimum energy at design temperature is typically **27 J** for hull plates.
- ✅ **IACS UR W17** sets the weld defect acceptance threshold; default DPS ≤ **2%**.
- ✅ **CAPA** = Corrective and Preventive Action; driven by NCRs and RCA (5-Why or Fishbone).
- ✅ **Process Capability** benchmark: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (industry minimum), $\geq 1.67$ (world-class).
- ✅ **Steel traceability** must be ensured via **3.1 / 3.2 Mill Certificates** (EN 10204) — common viva question.
- ✅ **Paint DFT** must be verified using calibrated gauges at a frequency per the painting procedure (e.g., every 10 m²).
- ✅ Remember the **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) for continuous improvement — frequently asked in 3-mark questions.
- ✅ **Statutory vs Class** certificates: Class = structural integrity; Statutory = SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line (issued by Class on behalf of Flag State).

**Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE!** 🚢⚓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accuracy Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accuracy Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Accuracy Control in Ship Construction

**Accuracy Control** in shipbuilding refers to the systematic engineering methodology employed to ensure that every fabricated structural component, sub-assembly, block, and the final integrated hull conforms to the prescribed dimensional tolerances, geometric forms, and positional alignments as defined in the ship's **Lines Plan**, **Shell Expansion Plan**, and **Constructional Detail Drawings** issued by the Naval Architect.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, accuracy control is treated as an integrated quality assurance discipline that governs the permissible deviations in:

- **Plate cutting and forming tolerances**
- **Welding-induced distortions**
- **Block dimensional integrity**
- **Hull line fairness and station accuracy**
- **Propulsion shaft alignment**
- **Stem and stern post fabrication precision**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Accuracy Control is the bridge between **theoretical design geometry** (Lines Plan) and **physical as-built geometry** (Actual Hull). Without it, the ship will not float on its intended waterline, will experience uneven stress distribution, and will fail classification society inspections (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Tailor's Precision Principle":**
> Imagine commissioning a bespoke suit from a master tailor. The tailor first takes 27 individual measurements (chest, waist, sleeve length, shoulder pitch, etc.) with a tolerance of ±2 mm. Now scale this concept up to a ship — except instead of 27 measurements, the Naval Architect defines **tens of thousands of dimensional control points** across a steel structure weighing thousands of tonnes, fabricated across multiple workshops, and assembled over 18–24 months.
>
> Just as the tailor uses a measuring tape, the shipbuilder uses **laser total stations, theodolites, optical plummets, and 3D coordinate measuring systems** to verify that every plate, stiffener, and panel is positioned within its allowable tolerance window. Accuracy Control is the "measuring tape and quality inspector" of shipbuilding — without it, components made in Shop A will not fit components made in Shop B.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Tolerances (KTU Reference Values)

The following bold-listed metrics are universally accepted shipbuilding tolerances as per classification society rules:

| Parameter | Typical Tolerance |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Hull length overall (LOA) | **±0.1% of Lpp** (between perpendiculars) |
| Block length dimension | **±3 mm to ±5 mm** |
| Plate flatness (1 m × 1 m panel) | **±2 mm** |
| Welding angular distortion | **±1° to ±3°** per joint |
| Hull breadth (molded) | **±10 mm** |
| Depth (molded) | **±15 mm** |
| Shaft alignment offset | **≤ 0.10 mm/m** |
| Block diagonal squareness | **±10 mm over 20 m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These tolerance values are **non-negotiable** under KTU examination context — examiners expect students to cite specific numeric ranges for accuracy control parameters.

---

### 1.4 The Five Pillars of Shipyard Accuracy Control

Accuracy Control in modern shipyards is built upon five inter-dependent pillars:

1. **Lofting Accuracy** — the digital/physical templating that defines the master geometry.
2. **Cutting & Forming Accuracy** — controlling thermal and mechanical deformation during plate profiling.
3. **Welding Accuracy** — minimizing heat-input-induced distortions (angular, longitudinal, transverse bowing).
4. **Block Assembly Accuracy** — dimensional control of pre-assembled 3D units before erection on the slipway.
5. **Erection & Launching Accuracy** — final hull line integrity on the building berth.

These five pillars collectively ensure that the **as-built hull geometry matches the as-designed Lines Plan** within the IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) tolerance envelope.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Accuracy Control

Accuracy control is fundamentally a **tolerance stack-up management discipline**. Every ship consists of approximately:

- **200 to 400 steel blocks** (depending on vessel size)
- **3,000 to 8,000 tonnes of steel** (medium-sized vessel)
- **20,000 to 50,000 individual plate parts** (cut from nesting plans)
- **~150 km of weld seams** in a single hull

Each of these elements carries an inherent dimensional deviation. Accuracy Control exists to **prevent the accumulation (stack-up)** of these small deviations into unacceptably large cumulative errors.

### 2.2 Sources of Dimensional Deviations

The deviations in ship construction arise from **five primary sources**:

| Source | Mechanism | Typical Magnitude |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Material Inherent** | Mill tolerance on plates (length, width, thickness, camber) | **±2 to ±5 mm** |
| **Cutting Process** | Oxy-fuel, plasma, laser kerf variation | **±1 to ±3 mm** |
| **Cold Forming** | Springback after line heating or roller bending | **±2 to ±10 mm** |
| **Welding Distortion** | Heat input causes shrinkage | **±5 to ±50 mm cumulative** |
| **Erection Sag** | Gravity deflection of large blocks during lift | **±10 to ±30 mm** |

### 2.3 Welding Distortion — The Dominant Deviation Source

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Critical Focus Area:** Welding distortion accounts for **60–70% of all dimensional deviations** in a steel hull. Students must understand its four modes:

The four modes of welding distortion are:

1. **Transverse Shrinkage (⊥ to weld)** — Causes plates to narrow perpendicular to the weld line.
2. **Longitudinal Shrinkage (∥ to weld)** — Causes the welded assembly to shorten along the weld direction.
3. **Angular Distortion (Bending)** — Rotation of plates about the weld axis due to uneven through-thickness heating.
4. **Buckling Distortion** — Out-of-plane waviness in thin panels due to compressive residual stress.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are the **must-know equations** for accuracy control calculations in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
