- **Step 1 — Establish the Baseline Plan.** Before a single plate is cut, the planning department issues a **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** which dictates *what* must be done, *when*, and *by whom*. This baseline is the "ruler" against which all future measurements are made.
- **Step 2 — Decompose Work into Measurable Units.** A ship is decomposed into **blocks** (typically 200–800 tonnes each), **units** (assemblies), **parts**, and **outfitting zones**. Each of these becomes a measurable "package."
- **Step 3 — Measure Actual Physical Progress.** Each block is given a *% complete* based on its physical state: 0% at steel cutting → 25% at panel fabrication → 50% at sub-block assembly → 75% at block erection → 100% at outfitting completion.
- **Step 4 — Compare Actual vs. Planned (Variance Analysis).** The difference between *Planned Progress* and *Actual Progress* at any date is called the **Schedule Variance (SV)**. A negative SV indicates slippage.
- **Step 5 — Forecast and Control.** Using the trend so far, the yard **forecasts the new completion date and cost**, and triggers corrective action (additional labour, overtime, resequencing, design freeze acceleration).

### 2.2 Key Methods of Progress Monitoring

Six methods are universally accepted in shipbuilding:

- **Method A: Milestone Tracking** – binary (done/not-done) markers like *Keel Laying*, *Launching*, *Sea Trials*, *Delivery*.
- **Method B: Unit / Block Completion Count** – tracks number of blocks fully erected.
- **Method C: S-Curve Analysis** – plots cumulative progress (typically cumulative tonnes of steel erected) against time, producing the characteristic 'S' shape.
- **Method D: Earned Value Management (EVM)** – integrates scope, schedule, and cost into three values: PV, EV, AC.
- **Method E: PERT / CPM Tracking** – critical path floats are recalculated weekly to identify slipping activities.
- **Method F: Computer-Integrated Systems** – ERP/MES software like *FORAN*, *TRIBON*, *AVEVA Marine*, or *SmartShip* automate data collection from CNC machines and bar-coding of parts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In modern Korean and Japanese mega-yards (Hyundai Heavy Industries, Samsung Heavy Industries, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries), progress monitoring is fully digitized. Every steel plate carries an RFID tag. As it moves through the workshop, gates automatically log its location. The progress dashboard refreshes every hour. This level of granularity would have been impossible in the 1970s when progress was tracked using chalkboards.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Unit | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical Progress (P) | $P = \dfrac{W_{done}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Weight-based or block-based ratio |
| Earned Value (EV) | $EV = \%Complete \times BAC$ | currency | Monetary value of work done |
| Planned Value (PV) | $PV = \%Planned \times BAC$ | currency | Monetary value of work scheduled |
| Actual Cost (AC) | $AC = \sum(\text{actual expenditure})$ | currency | Real money spent so far |
| Schedule Variance (SV) | $SV = EV - PV$ | currency | Positive = ahead, Negative = behind |
| Cost Variance (CV) | $CV = EV - AC$ | currency | Positive = under budget |
| Schedule Performance Index (SPI) | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | dimensionless | $>\!1$ ahead, $<\!1$ behind |
| Cost Performance Index (CPI) | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | dimensionless | $>\!1$ under budget, $<\!1$ over |
| Productivity Index (PI) | $PI = \dfrac{SMH}{AMH}$ | dimensionless | Standard MH / Actual MH |
| Steel Erected Progress | $S_{prog} = \dfrac{T_{erected}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Hull structural completion |
| Estimate at Completion (EAC) | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | currency | Forecasted total cost |
| Variance at Completion (VAC) | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | currency | Budget surplus or overrun |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When using *Physical Progress*, students often confuse **tonnage-based progress** with **block-count progress**. A ship may be 80% complete by block-count but only 55% complete by tonnage — this happens when early blocks are small and later blocks are large. Always state the basis.

### 2.4 The Concept of an S-Curve

The S-Curve is the **single most important visual tool in shipbuilding progress monitoring**. It arises because shipbuilding work follows a natural lifecycle:

- **Slow start** (design, nesting, first-cut plates).
- **Rapid middle phase** (peak block erection).
- **Tapering end** (outfitting, commissioning, finishing).

This cumulative growth pattern mathematically approximates a **logistic curve** or a **cubic polynomial**.

A second S-curve (the *Planned S-Curve*) is overlaid on the *Actual S-Curve*. The **horizontal gap** between them at any time $t$ is the *schedule slip in days*, and the **vertical gap** is the *progress slip in tonnes or percentage*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Physical Progress from Block Status

In a ship broken into $N$ blocks, each block $i$ has a planned weight $w_i$ and an actual completion percentage $\alpha_i$ (where $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq 1$).

The **weighted average physical progress** is derived as follows.

Let total steel weight $W_{total}$ be the sum of all block weights:

$$W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i$$

This is the total work to be done, expressed in tonnes.

Let the weight of completed portion in block $i$ be:

$$w_{i,done} = \alpha_i \cdot w_i$$

The total completed weight is:

$$W_{done} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \cdot w_i$$

Therefore, the physical progress percentage is:

$$P = \frac{W_{done}}{W_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \cdot w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i} \times 100$$

This is the **weight-averaged block progress**, the most accurate indicator of hull construction status.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Steel Erection Progress

Consider a ship broken into 5 blocks with the following status reported by the foreman:

| Block $i$ | Planned Weight $w_i$ (tonnes) | Completion $\alpha_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 800 | 1.00 |
| 2 | 1200 | 1.00 |
| 3 | 1500 | 0.60 |
| 4 | 1100 | 0.20 |
| 5 | 900 | 0.00 |

**Step 1:** Compute numerator and denominator.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} \alpha_i w_i = (1.00 \cdot 800) + (1.00 \cdot 1200) + (0.60 \cdot 1500) + (0.20 \cdot 1100) + (0.00 \cdot 900)$$

$$= 800 + 1200 + 900 + 220 + 0 = 3120 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 800 + 1200 + 1500 + 1100 + 900 = 5500 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2:** Compute physical progress.

$$P = \frac{3120}{5500} \times 100 = 56.73\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> If you had used a simple block-count method ($\frac{2}{5} \times 100 = 40\%$), the figure would be misleadingly low. Weight-averaging is therefore the *correct* method in shipbuilding.

### 3.3 Derivation: Forecasted Completion Date from Trend (Linear Slippage)

Assume at day $t_{now}$, actual progress is $P_a$ and planned progress is $P_p$. Total duration is $T$ days. The original planned completion was at $T$.

The slippage in progress is:

$$\Delta P = P_p - P_a \quad \text{(positive if behind schedule)}$$

Assuming the slippage rate remains constant, the additional days required beyond $T$ are:

$$\Delta t = \Delta P \cdot \frac{T}{P_p}$$

The **forecasted completion date** is then:

$$T_{forecast} = T + \Delta t$$

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Forecast Slippage

Given: $T = 540$ days, $P_p = 60\%$, $P_a = 50\%$, current day $t = 300$.

Compute slippage:

$$\Delta P = 60 - 50 = 10\%$$

Compute additional days:

$$\Delta t = 10 \times \frac{540}{60} = 90 \text{ days}$$

Forecasted completion:

$$T_{forecast} = 540 + 90 = 630 \text{ days}$$

The ship is forecasted to be delivered **90 days late** unless corrective action is taken.

### 3.5 Derivation: Earned Value Indices

Let $BAC$ = Budget at Completion (total sanctioned budget for the project).
Let $\%Complete = P$ (decimal).
Let $\%Planned = P_p$ (decimal).

The three EVM pillars are defined as:

$$EV = P \times BAC$$

$$PV = P_p \times BAC$$

$$AC = \text{actual cumulative cost incurred}$$

The two performance indices become:

$$SPI = \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{P \times BAC}{P_p \times BAC} = \frac{P}{P_p}$$

$$CPI = \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{P \times BAC}{AC}$$

If $SPI = 0.85$, the project is achieving **only 85 paise of work for every 1 rupee of work scheduled** — i.e., it is lagging.

### 3.6 Worked EVM Example

Given a hull-and-outfitting contract with $BAC = 200$ Crore INR. At the end of month 8 of an 18-month project:

- Planned cumulative completion: $P_p = 40\%$
- Actual cumulative completion: $P_a = 32\%$
- Actual cumulative cost: $AC = 75$ Crore

Compute PV:

$$PV = 0.40 \times 200 = 80 \text{ Crore}$$

Compute EV:

$$EV = 0.32 \times 200 = 64 \text{ Crore}$$

Compute SV:

$$SV = 64 - 80 = -16 \text{ Crore (behind schedule)}$$

Compute CV:

$$CV = 64 - 75 = -11 \text{ Crore (over budget)}$$

Compute SPI:

$$SPI = \frac{64}{80} = 0.80$$

Compute CPI:

$$CPI = \frac{64}{75} = 0.853$$

Compute EAC (forecasted total cost):

$$EAC = \frac{200}{0.853} = 234.47 \text{ Crore}$$

Compute VAC (forecasted overrun):

$$VAC = 200 - 234.47 = -34.47 \text{ Crore}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Interpretation: The project is forecast to **overshoot budget by ~34 Crore** and is **20% behind schedule** in terms of work efficiency. Management must immediately intervene.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Progress Monitoring Closed-Loop Flow

The following block diagram illustrates the **closed-loop control logic** that every shipyard's production planning department follows. Note that the system is *cyclic* — output of one cycle becomes input of the next.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Baseline Production Plan and Schedule] --> nodeB[Decompose Ship into Blocks and Zones]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Assign Weights and Standard Man-Hours]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Execute Production on Shop Floor]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Collect Actual Data from Shop Floor]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Actual Progress Percentage]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compare with Planned Progress]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Is Variance within Tolerance?}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Continue Current Plan]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Diagnose Root Cause of Deviation]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Issue Corrective Action Notice]
    nodeK --> nodeD
    nodeG --> nodeL[Update S-Curve and EVM Reports]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Forecast New Completion Date and Cost]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Management Review Meeting]
    nodeN --> nodeD
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Progress Measurement Levels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[Level 1: Overall Project Milestones] --> nodeB1[Level 2: Stage Completion Hull Outfitting Painting]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[Level 3: Block and Zone Status]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[Level 4: Sub Block and Unit Status]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE1[Level 5: Part and Piece Status]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF1[Daily Shop Floor Activity Logs]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of an Integrated Yard Monitoring System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeShop[Shop Floor Execution Layer] --> nodeData[Data Acquisition RFID Barcode Manual Entry]
    nodeData --> nodeMES[Manufacturing Execution System MES]
    nodeMES --> nodeDB[Centralized Project Database]
    nodeDB --> nodeEVM[Earned Value Engine]
    nodeDB --> nodeSCurve[S Curve Generator]
    nodeDB --> nodeCritical[Critical Path Analyzer]
    nodeEVM --> nodeDash[Management Dashboard]
    nodeSCurve --> nodeDash
    nodeCritical --> nodeDash
    nodeDash --> nodeReport[Daily Weekly Monthly Reports]
    nodeReport --> nodeMgmt[Senior Management Decision Support]
```

### 4.4 Progress Status Classification Matrix (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS0[Status Zero Not Started] --> nodeS25[Status 25 Steel Cut and Panel Fabricated]
    nodeS25 --> nodeS50[Status 50 Sub Block Assembled]
    nodeS50 --> nodeS75[Status 75 Block Erected on Slipway]
    nodeS75 --> nodeS90[Status 90 Outfitting and Painting Done]
    nodeS90 --> nodeS100[Status 100 Final Block Acceptance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Mapping for KTU Exam:** If a question asks "explain how block-level progress is monitored", reproduce the four status diagrams above and explicitly state that each status is a physical milestone visible on the shop floor, making it auditable by a quality surveyor.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
Define **Progress Monitoring** in the context of ship construction. List any **two methods** used for monitoring progress in a modern shipyard.

**Model Answer:**
Progress Monitoring is the systematic process of measuring, recording, and comparing actual construction activities against the pre-established baseline production plan, in order to detect deviations and trigger corrective action.
Two methods commonly used are:
1. **S-Curve Analysis** – plots cumulative progress (e.g., tonnes of steel erected) against time.
2. **Earned Value Management (EVM)** – integrates scope, schedule, and cost into three values: Planned Value, Earned Value, and Actual Cost, from which variances and performance indices are derived.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
What is the significance of the **S-Curve** in shipbuilding progress monitoring? Why does it have an "S" shape?

**Model Answer:**
The S-Curve is a graphical representation of cumulative progress (steel weight, man-hours, or cost) plotted against time. Its characteristic "S" shape arises because shipbuilding progress is slow at the start (design, first-cut steel, panel fabrication), accelerates during the middle phase (peak block erection on the slipway), and tapers off near the end (outfitting, commissioning, sea trials). It allows shipyard management to visually compare **planned** vs. **actual** cumulative progress and detect slippage early.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** A bulk carrier is divided into **6 blocks** with the following data. Compute the **weight-averaged physical progress** of hull construction. *(7 Marks)*

| Block | Planned Weight (tonnes) | Completion Status |
|---|---|---|
| A | 950 | Fully erected |
| B | 1100 | Fully erected |
| C | 1400 | 80% complete |
| D | 1250 | 50% complete |
| E | 1000 | 20% complete |
| F | 800 | Not started |

**(b)** With reference to the above project, if the **total project duration is 600 days** and at day **300**, the planned progress was **55%** but the actual progress was **42%**, compute the **forecasted additional delay** and the **new estimated delivery date**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Weight-averaged physical progress:

Numerator:
$$\sum \alpha_i w_i = (1.00)(950) + (1.00)(1100) + (0.80)(1400) + (0.50)(1250) + (0.20)(1000) + (0)(800)$$
$$= 950 + 1100 + 1120 + 625 + 200 + 0 = 3995 \text{ tonnes}$$

Denominator:
$$\sum w_i = 950 + 1100 + 1400 + 1250 + 1000 + 800 = 6500 \text{ tonnes}$$

Physical progress:
$$P = \frac{3995}{6500} \times 100 = 61.46\%$$

**(b)** Forecasted additional delay:

Progress slippage:
$$\Delta P = 55 - 42 = 13\%$$

Additional days required:
$$\Delta t = \Delta P \times \frac{T}{P_p} = 13 \times \frac{600}{55} = 141.82 \text{ days} \approx 142 \text{ days}$$

New estimated delivery date:
$$T_{forecast} = 600 + 142 = 742 \text{ days from project start}$$

The vessel is forecast to be delivered **142 days late** unless the slippage is arrested.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Earned Value Management (EVM)** in shipbuilding projects. Define the terms **PV, EV, AC, SV, CV, SPI, and CPI**, with their formulas. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** For a naval vessel project, the sanctioned **Budget at Completion (BAC) is ₹500 Crore**. At the end of month 12 of a 24-month schedule, the planned progress is **50%**, the actual progress is **38%**, and the actual cumulative cost incurred is **₹220 Crore**. Compute:
- (i) PV, EV, AC
- (ii) SV and CV
- (iii) SPI and CPI
- (iv) EAC and VAC
Comment on the health of the project. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory:**

- **PV (Planned Value)** = value of work scheduled to be done = $P_p \times BAC$.
- **EV (Earned Value)** = value of work actually completed = $P_a \times BAC$.
- **AC (Actual Cost)** = actual money spent so far.
- **SV (Schedule Variance)** = $EV - PV$; positive means ahead of schedule.
- **CV (Cost Variance)** = $EV - AC$; positive means under budget.
- **SPI (Schedule Performance Index)** = $EV/PV$; $> 1$ is good.
- **CPI (Cost Performance Index)** = $EV/AC$; $> 1$ is good.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $BAC = 500$ Crore, $P_p = 0.50$, $P_a = 0.38$, $AC = 220$ Crore.

(i) PV, EV, AC:
$$PV = 0.50 \times 500 = 250 \text{ Crore}$$
$$EV = 0.38 \times 500 = 190 \text{ Crore}$$
$$AC = 220 \text{ Crore (given)}$$

(ii) SV, CV:
$$SV = 190 - 250 = -60 \text{ Crore (behind schedule)}$$
$$CV = 190 - 220 = -30 \text{ Crore (over budget)}$$

(iii) SPI, CPI:
$$SPI = \frac{190}{250} = 0.76$$
$$CPI = \frac{190}{220} = 0.864$$

(iv) EAC, VAC:
$$EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{500}{0.864} = 578.70 \text{ Crore}$$
$$VAC = 500 - 578.70 = -78.70 \text{ Crore (forecast overrun)}$$

**Comment:** The project is in **critical condition**. It is **24% behind schedule** ($SPI = 0.76$) and **13.6% over budget efficiency** ($CPI = 0.864$). Forecasted final overrun is **₹78.70 Crore**. Management must initiate cost-control measures, accelerate critical path activities, and possibly invoke liquidated-damages clauses on subcontractors.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls (verified against KTU valuation patterns):**
> 1. **Mixing % in decimal vs. percentage.** Writing $P = 0.38$ instead of $38\%$ in $EV = P \times BAC$ gives wrong answer by a factor of 100. Always state units.
> 2. **Using block-count progress instead of weight-averaged progress.** Examiners specifically test whether the student understands the difference.
> 3. **Confusing SV with CV.** SV relates to *time*; CV relates to *cost*. Writing $SV = EV - AC$ is a 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Forgetting to state units** (tonnes, crore, days) in the final answer — KTU deducts **0.5 mark** per missing unit.
> 5. **Skipping the verbal interpretation** of SPI and CPI. KTU awards a **1-mark bonus** for correctly stating "SPI = 0.76 means we are achieving 76 paise of work for every 1 rupee scheduled."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Progress Monitoring** is a continuous, closed-loop process — Plan → Execute → Measure → Compare → Control.
- The **five key activities** are: Measurement, Evaluation, Reporting, Forecasting, Corrective Action.
- The **six standard methods** are: Milestone Tracking, Block/Unit Counting, S-Curve, EVM, PERT/CPM, Computerized MES.
- **Physical Progress Formula:** $P = \frac{\sum \alpha_i w_i}{\sum w_i} \times 100$ — **always** use weight-averaged, not block-count.
- **Earned Value Formula:** $EV = \%Complete \times BAC$ — the cornerstone of EVM.
- **Schedule Variance:** $SV = EV - PV$ — negative means *behind*.
- **Cost Variance:** $CV = EV - AC$ — negative means *over*.
- **SPI** is schedule efficiency; **CPI** is cost efficiency — both should be $> 1$ for a healthy project.
- The **S-Curve** shape is a consequence of the shipbuilding lifecycle: slow start, rapid middle, tapering end.
- **Forecasted delay formula:** $\Delta t = \Delta P \times \dfrac{T}{P_p}$.
- **EAC formula:** $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$.
- Modern yards use **RFID, barcoding, and MES** for real-time, automated progress monitoring.
- Always **state the basis** of progress (tonnage, block-count, man-hours, or cost) before quoting a percentage.
- **Three dimensions** are always monitored simultaneously: Time, Cost, Scope — the *Iron Triangle*.
- **Weight-averaged progress** is the most accurate hull-progress indicator for KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PERT Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** PERT Charts ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique)** is a probabilistic project management tool developed by the U.S. Navy in 1958 (for the Polaris submarine missile program) used to plan, schedule, and coordinate the activities involved in completing a project. In ship construction, PERT charts visually represent the sequence of shipbuilding tasks (steel cutting, hull erection, outfitting, launching, sea trials) using a network of nodes and arrows, where each activity is assigned **three time estimates** to account for uncertainty in shipyard operations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** PERT is fundamentally a *probabilistic* model — unlike CPM (Critical Path Method), it does NOT rely on a single deterministic activity duration. It uses a Beta distribution assumption to model uncertainty, which is highly relevant to ship construction where weather, supply chain delays, and welding rates introduce variability. ### The Three Time Estimates | Symbol | Name | Meaning | Shipbuilding Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **$t_o$** | Optimistic Time | Best-case duration under ideal conditions | Hull plates cut without errors | | **$t_m$** | Most Likely Time | Realistic duration under normal working conditions | Typical block erection rate at the yard | | **$t_p$** | Pessimistic Time | Worst-case duration under adverse conditions | Delays due to monsoon, late steel delivery | ### Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🛳️ > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Captain's Voyage Plan"** > Imagine a ship captain planning a voyage from Kochi to Marmugao. The captain doesn't trust a single ETA because the sea is unpredictable. So the captain calculates **three ETAs**: > - *Optimistic*: "If winds are perfect and currents favor us — **5 days**." > - *Most Likely*: "Based on average weather — **7 days**." > - *Pessimistic*: "If a storm hits — **14 days**." > > The captain then weights these into a single **Expected Voyage Time** and asks: *"What is the probability I arrive by Tuesday?"* — this is exactly what PERT does for shipbuilding projects! ### Core Terminology Map - **Event (Node)**: A milestone representing the start or completion of one or more activities (e.g., "Keel Laid", "Hull Launched"). - **Activity (Arrow)**: A time-consuming task that consumes resources (man-hours, steel, welding rods). - **Dummy Activity**: A dashed arrow representing logical dependency without consuming time. - **Critical Path**: The longest-duration path through the network; determines the **minimum project duration**. - **Slack/Float**: The time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project. - **TE / TL**: Earliest and Latest event times. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Fundamental Assumptions of PERT 1. The project can be broken down into a finite set of mutually exclusive activities. 2. Each activity has **three independent time estimates**: $t_o$, $t_m$, $t_p$. 3. Activity durations follow a **Beta Probability Distribution** (the rationale: Beta is bounded by optimistic and pessimistic limits, and is unimodal like most real-world tasks). 4. The critical path is statistically determined — it is the path with the highest probability of being the bottleneck. 5. Activity times are **statistically independent** of one another. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Step 1 — Network Construction (Arrow-on-Activity / AOA)** - Each node is numbered ($1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$). - Activities are represented as directed arrows from event $i$ to event $j$. - The numbering convention: head event number $>$ tail event number (i.e., $j > i$). **Step 2 — Forward Pass (Earliest Event Time, $T_E$)** - Start at the first node: $T_E(1) = 0$. - For each subsequent node $j$: $$T_E(j) = \max_i \{T_E(i) + t_{ij}\}$$ where $t_{ij}$ is the expected duration of activity $(i \to j)$. **Step 3 — Backward Pass (Latest Event Time, $T_L$)** - For the last node $n$: $T_L(n) = T_E(n) = T_{project}$. - For each preceding node $i$: $$T_L(i) = \min_j \{T_L(j) - t_{ij}\}$$ **Step 4 — Slack Calculation** $$\text{Slack}(i) = T_L(i) - T_E(i)$$ - Slack = 0 → Critical Event → Critical Path. **Step 5 — Variance Propagation** The variance of a path is the **sum of variances** of all activities on that path (since activities are assumed independent): $$\sigma^2_{path} = \sum_{k \in \text{path}} \sigma^2_k$$ ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize Before Exam** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Expected Time** | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | Days / Weeks | | 2 | **Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ | (Days)² | | 3 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}$ | Days | | 4 | **Project Variance (Critical Path)** | $\sigma^2_{cp} = \sum \sigma^2_{critical}$ | (Days)² | | 5 | **Project Standard Deviation** | $\sigma_{cp} = \sqrt{\sigma^2_{cp}}$ | Days | | 6 | **Z-Value (Probability)** | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{cp}}$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | **Earliest Event Time** | $T_E(j) = \max\{T_E(i) + t_{ij}\}$ | Days | | 8 | **Latest Event Time** | $T_L(i) = \min\{T_L(j) - t_{ij}\}$ | Days | | 9 | **Slack** | $S_i = T_L(i) - T_E(i)$ | Days | | 10 | **Probability of Completion** | $P(T \leq T_s) = \Phi(Z)$ | Read from Standard Normal Table | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In shipyards (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock), PERT is used to monitor mega-block construction, scheduling outfitting alongside hull erection, and managing sea-trial readiness. The probabilistic Z-value helps the project manager quote **delivery dates with a confidence percentage** to shipowners. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: Expected Time ($t_e$) from Beta Distribution PERT assumes the activity duration follows a Beta distribution with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ chosen such that: - Mode of Beta = Most Likely Time = $t_m$ - Range bounded by $[t_o, t_p]$ The **mean of the Beta distribution** is given by: $$\mu = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}$$ Under the PERT simplification (assuming $\alpha + \beta = 6$, which forces a specific shape), the mean becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} t_e &= \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6} \\ \\ &\text{Logic: The Beta mean is a weighted average where } t_m \text{ (most likely)} \\ &\text{receives 4× weight, and the extremes } t_o, t_p \text{ each receive 1× weight.} \\ &\text{This reflects that real activities usually cluster near } t_m. \end{aligned} $$ ### Derivation 2: Variance of a PERT Activity The variance of the Beta distribution under the PERT assumption is: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{\alpha \beta}{[(\alpha + \beta)]^2 (\alpha + \beta + 1)}$$ For the PERT approximation ($\alpha = 1 + 4\frac{t_m - t_o}{t_p - t_o}$, $\beta = 1 + 4\frac{t_p - t_m}{t_p - t_o}$), the simplified result used in practice is: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma^2 &= \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2 \\ \\ &\text{Logic: The standard deviation is approximated as one-sixth of the spread} \\ &\text{between optimistic and pessimistic times. This is a direct consequence of} \\ &\text{the empirical rule: for any unimodal distribution, roughly 99.7\% of values} \\ &\text{fall within } \pm 3\sigma \text{ of the mean, so } \sigma \approx \frac{\text{range}}{6}. \end{aligned} $$ ### Derivation 3: Project Variance (Path Sum) For a critical path containing activities $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_k$ with variances $\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \ldots, \sigma_k^2$, the total path variance is: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma^2_{path} &= \text{Var}(t_1 + t_2 + \cdots + t_k) \\ &= \text{Var}(t_1) + \text{Var}(t_2) + \cdots + \text{Var}(t_k) \\ &\quad + 2\sum_{i 0, \text{ the} \\ &\text{schedule has buffer; if } Z < 0, \text{ the deadline is aggressive.} \end{aligned} $$ The probability $P(T \leq T_s) = \Phi(Z)$ is read from the **Standard Normal Cumulative Distribution Table**. ### Worked Numerical Example — Ship Hull Construction **Problem:** A shipyard plans a hull erection project with the following activities: | Activity | Description | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1-2 | Steel Cutting | 4 | 6 | 14 | | 1-3 | Plate Preparation | 2 | 4 | 6 | | 2-4 | Block Fabrication | 6 | 8 | 16 | | 3-4 | Sub-assembly | 3 | 5 | 7 | | 4-5 | Hull Erection & Launch | 5 | 7 | 15 | **Step 1: Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$ for each activity.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Activity 1-2: } t_e &= \frac{4 + 4(6) + 14}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{1-2} &= \left(\frac{14-4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = 2.78 \\ \\ \text{Activity 1-3: } t_e &= \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{1-3} &= \left(\frac{6-2}{6}\right)^2 = 0.44 \\ \\ \text{Activity 2-4: } t_e &= \frac{6 + 4(8) + 16}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{2-4} &= \left(\frac{16-6}{6}\right)^2 = 2.78 \\ \\ \text{Activity 3-4: } t_e &= \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{3-4} &= \left(\frac{7-3}{6}\right)^2 = 0.44 \\ \\ \text{Activity 4-5: } t_e &= \frac{5 + 4(7) + 15}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{4-5} &= \left(\frac{15-5}{6}\right)^2 = 2.78 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Identify All Paths.** - Path A: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5$ → $7 + 9 + 8 = 24$ days - Path B: $1 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5$ → $4 + 5 + 8 = 17$ days **Step 3: Identify the Critical Path.** The longest path = **Path A (1-2-4-5) with 24 days** → This is the **Critical Path**. **Step 4: Compute Project Variance and Standard Deviation.** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma^2_{cp} &= \sigma^2_{1-2} + \sigma^2_{2-4} + \sigma^2_{4-5} \\ &= 2.78 + 2.78 + 2.78 = 8.34 \\ \\ \sigma_{cp} &= \sqrt{8.34} = 2.89 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Probability of Completion within $T_s = 26$ days.** $$ \begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{26 - 24}{2.89} = \frac{2}{2.89} = 0.69 \\ \\ P(T \leq 26) &= \Phi(0.69) \approx 0.7549 \approx 75.5\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation for Shipyard Manager:** There is a **75.5% probability** of delivering the hull within 26 days. If the shipowner demands **90% confidence**, we must extend the deadline: > $Z_{0.90} = 1.28$, so $T_s = 24 + 1.28 \times 2.89 = 27.7 \approx 28$ days. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: PERT Network for Ship Hull Construction (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart LR node1(["NODE 1
Project Start"]) node2(["NODE 2
Steel Cut Complete"]) node3(["NODE 3
Plates Ready"]) node4(["NODE 4
Blocks Assembled"]) node5(["NODE 5
Hull Launched"]) node1 -- "A: Steel Cutting
te=7, s2=2.78" --> node2 node1 -- "B: Plate Prep
te=4, s2=0.44" --> node3 node2 -- "C: Block Fab
te=9, s2=2.78" --> node4 node3 -- "D: Sub-assembly
te=5, s2=0.44" --> node4 node4 -- "E: Hull Erection
te=8, s2=2.78" --> node5 style node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style node5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style node4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Nodes are EVENTS (milestones), arrows are ACTIVITIES. The red-shaded node 4 lies on the **Critical Path**. Labels show activity name, expected time ($t_e$), and variance ($\sigma^2$). ### Diagram 2: PERT Calculation Workflow (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["Define Shipyard Project Scope"]) block1["STEP 1: Activity Breakdown
List all shipbuilding tasks"] block2["STEP 2: Estimate Times
Collect to, tm, tp from foremen"] block3["STEP 3: Compute Expected Time
te = to + 4 tm + tp divided by 6"] block4["STEP 4: Compute Variance
sigma squared = tp minus to divided by 6, squared"] block5["STEP 5: Build Network
Draw nodes and arrows"] block6["STEP 6: Forward and Backward Pass
Compute TE and TL for each event"] block7["STEP 7: Identify Critical Path
Path with zero slack, longest duration"] block8["STEP 8: Compute Project Sigma
Square root of sum of variances on CP"] block9["STEP 9: Probability Analysis
Z value then Normal Table lookup"] finish(["Output: Completion Probability Curve"]) start --> block1 --> block2 --> block3 --> block4 --> block5 --> block6 --> block7 --> block8 --> block9 --> finish style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style finish fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style block7 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style block9 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ### Diagram 3: PERT vs CPM Comparison Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A(["Project Management Tools"]) --> B["PERT
Probabilistic"] A --> C["CPM
Deterministic"] B --> B1["Three time estimates to, tm, tp"] B --> B2["Beta distribution model"] B --> B3["Used for R and D, new ship prototypes"] B --> B4["Output: Probability of completion"] C --> C1["Single time estimate"] C --> C2["Normal distribution model"] C --> C3["Used for repetitive shipbuilding tasks"] C --> C4["Output: Critical path and float"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define PERT. State any two advantages of using PERT in ship construction projects.* **Model Answer:** PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) is a probabilistic project scheduling and control technique that uses a network of activities and events with three time estimates (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic) to determine the expected project duration and probability of completion within a scheduled deadline. **Advantages in Ship Construction:** 1. **Handles Uncertainty:** Shipbuilding involves variable factors like weather, weld quality, and supply delays. PERT's probabilistic model captures this uncertainty better than deterministic CPM. 2. **Probability-Based Deadlines:** Allows the shipyard manager to quote delivery dates with quantified confidence levels (e.g., "85% probability of launch by Q3"). 3. **Critical Activity Identification:** Helps focus monitoring efforts on activities that are most likely to delay the project. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Write the formula for expected time in PERT. Why is the most likely time weighted by 4?* **Model Answer:** The expected time formula is: $$t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$$ The most likely time $t_m$ is weighted by 4 because PERT assumes the activity duration follows a Beta distribution where $t_m$ corresponds to the **mode** (peak) of the distribution. Empirical studies of repetitive industrial tasks (including shipyard operations) show that activity durations cluster most densely near the most likely value, justifying a 4× weighting. The optimistic and pessimistic times, being extreme scenarios, receive only 1× weight each. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **(a)** A ship repair project has the following activities with three time estimates (in days): | Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1-2 | 3 | 6 | 15 | | 1-3 | 2 | 3 | 4 | | 2-4 | 6 | 7 | 14 | | 3-4 | 4 | 8 | 12 | | 2-5 | 5 | 8 | 17 | | 4-5 | 3 | 5 | 7 | Compute the expected time and variance for each activity. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Activity 1-2: } t_e &= \frac{3 + 4(6) + 15}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{1-2} &= \left(\frac{15-3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{12}{6}\right)^2 = 4.00 \\ \\ \text{Activity 1-3: } t_e &= \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{1-3} &= \left(\frac{4-2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{6}\right)^2 = 0.11 \\ \\ \text{Activity 2-4: } t_e &= \frac{6 + 4(7) + 14}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{2-4} &= \left(\frac{14-6}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = 1.78 \\ \\ \text{Activity 3-4: } t_e &= \frac{4 + 4(8) + 12}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{3-4} &= \left(\frac{12-4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = 1.78 \\ \\ \text{Activity 2-5: } t_e &= \frac{5 + 4(8) + 17}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{2-5} &= \left(\frac{17-5}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{12}{6}\right)^2 = 4.00 \\ \\ \text{Activity 4-5: } t_e &= \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days} \\ \sigma^2_{4-5} &= \left(\frac{7-3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.44 \end{aligned} $$ **(b)** Determine the critical path, expected project duration, project variance, and the probability of completing the project in 26 days. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify all paths from node 1 to node 5.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Path 1: 1-2-4-5} &\rightarrow 7 + 8 + 5 = 20 \text{ days} \\ \text{Path 2: 1-3-4-5} &\rightarrow 3 + 8 + 5 = 16 \text{ days} \\ \text{Path 3: 1-2-5} &\rightarrow 7 + 9 = 16 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Critical Path.** The longest path = **1-2-4-5 = 20 days** → **Critical Path** Expected Project Duration $T_e = 20$ days. **Step 3: Project Variance and Standard Deviation.** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma^2_{cp} &= \sigma^2_{1-2} + \sigma^2_{2-4} + \sigma^2_{4-5} \\ &= 4.00 + 1.78 + 0.44 = 6.22 \\ \sigma_{cp} &= \sqrt{6.22} = 2.49 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Probability of Completion in 26 days.** $$ \begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{cp}} = \frac{26 - 20}{2.49} = \frac{6}{2.49} = 2.41 \\ P(T \leq 26) &= \Phi(2.41) \approx 0.9920 = 99.20\% \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** There is a **99.2% probability** of completing the project within 26 days. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **(a)** Compare PERT and CPM. State four points of difference. Why is PERT preferred for ship construction R&D projects? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Aspect | PERT | CPM | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Time Estimates | Three ($t_o$, $t_m$, $t_p$) | Single deterministic | | Distribution Model | Beta distribution | Normal/Point estimate | | Focus | Probability of completion | Time-cost trade-off | | Best For | R&D, prototypes, non-repetitive | Repetitive construction | | Crashing | Not applicable | Applicable | | Output | Probability curve | Critical path with floats | **Why PERT is preferred for ship R&D:** 1. **High Uncertainty:** New vessel designs involve unfamiliar technologies (e.g., LNG fuel systems) where activity durations cannot be predicted accurately. 2. **Probabilistic Insight:** Management needs to know the *risk* of missing deadlines, not just the critical path. 3. **Stakeholder Communication:** Shipowners understand probability-based delivery commitments better than fixed dates. 4. **First-of-a-Kind Projects:** Where historical data is unavailable, the three-estimate approach leverages expert judgment. **(b)** A ship outfitting project has a critical path with expected duration 45 days and variance 9. The shipowner promises delivery in 50 days. Calculate: (i) Probability of meeting the deadline, (ii) Deadline for 95% confidence. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Given: $T_e = 45$ days, $\sigma^2 = 9$, so $\sigma = 3$ days. **(i) Probability of meeting $T_s = 50$ days:** $$ \begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{50 - 45}{3} = \frac{5}{3} = 1.67 \\ P(T \leq 50) &= \Phi(1.67) \approx 0.9525 = 95.25\% \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Deadline for 95% confidence:** For 95% probability, $Z_{0.95} = 1.645$. $$ \begin{aligned} T_s &= T_e + Z_{0.95} \times \sigma \\ &= 45 + 1.645 \times 3 \\ &= 45 + 4.935 = 49.94 \approx 50 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The current 50-day deadline already gives ~95% confidence. To improve to a safer margin, the yard should quote 51 days ($Z \approx 2.0$, $P \approx 97.7\%$). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Variance summation mistake:** Students often add standard deviations instead of variances along the critical path. *Always add variances, then take square root at the end.* > 2. **Wrong path selected as critical:** Always verify the critical path AFTER computing all $t_e$ values. The longest $t_e$ sum determines it. > 3. **Forgetting to use $T_e$ of critical path:** When computing $Z$, the numerator uses $T_e$ of the **critical path** (not the average of all paths). > 4. **Incorrect $Z$-table reading:** $Z = -0.5$ corresponds to ~30.85%, NOT 69.15%. Negative $Z$ = deadline is BEFORE expected completion. > 5. **Omitting units:** Always mention days/weeks for time and dimensionless for $Z$. > 6. **Not showing the three-estimate calculation explicitly:** Marks are awarded for the formula $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$, not just the final number. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam** - ✅ **PERT = Probabilistic; CPM = Deterministic.** Know the contrast cold. - ✅ **Expected Time Formula:** $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$ — the *single most tested formula*. - ✅ **Variance Formula:** $\sigma^2 = [(t_p - t_o)/6]^2$ — easy marks lost here. - ✅ **Critical Path = Longest $t_e$ path** (NOT the path with highest variance). - ✅ **Project $\sigma$ = Square root of SUM of variances** on critical path (variances add, not $\sigma$'s). - ✅ **Z-value:** $Z = (T_s - T_e)/\sigma_{cp}$. Negative $Z$ → low probability of meeting deadline. - ✅ **Standard Normal Table:** $Z = 0 \to 50\%$, $Z = 1.645 \to 95\%$, $Z = 2.33 \to 99\%$. - ✅ **Three time estimates** must always be estimated by the *person doing the work* (welder, fitter, foreman), not by management alone. - ✅ **Dummy activities** are needed only for logical dependencies, not time consumption. - ✅ **Slack = 0** ⟹ Event is on the critical path. - ✅ **Real-world applications:** Hull construction, ship repair scheduling, dry-dock planning, sea-trial preparation. - ✅ **Limitation:** PERT assumes activity independence — a weakness when shipyard activities are tightly resource-coupled (e.g., one crane shared between blocks).

topics.title: Cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Ship Construction**, **Cutting** is formally defined as the controlled separation of steel plates, sections, and rolled profiles into desired geometric shapes and sizes, forming the foundational raw parts (called *pieces* or *parts*) of the ship's hull and superstructure. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OENST724)** syllabus, cutting is the second critical stage of the *Hull Steel Fabrication Sequence*, succeeding *Material Reception & Storage* and preceding *Cleaning, Edge Preparation, and Assembly*.

Cutting processes are broadly classified by the **physical principle of material removal / separation**:

- **Thermal (Hot) Cutting** — using heat to melt or oxidize the metal.
- **Mechanical (Cold) Cutting** — using shear force, abrasion, or impact.
- **Hybrid Cutting** — combining thermal and mechanical action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cutting in shipbuilding is NOT the same as machining. Here, the goal is **rapid separation of large steel plates (typically 6 mm to 100 mm thick)** with acceptable edge quality for subsequent welding. Precision in millimeters, not microns, is the tolerance window.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **tailor cutting a large piece of cloth** to stitch a shirt. Before cutting, the tailor:

1. **Marks** the cloth using chalk and a template (paper pattern).
2. **Chooses the right tool** — scissors for thin cotton, a heavy blade for denim, or a hot wire for synthetic fabric.
3. **Cuts along the marked lines**, taking care not to fray the edges.

Shipyard cutting is **exactly the same**, but with **steel instead of cloth** and **oxy-fuel torches, plasma beams, or water jets instead of scissors**. The "tailor" is the **plater / fabricator**, the "chalk lines" are **scribed templates**, and the "shirt pieces" are **ship hull plates** that will later be welded together to form the ship's body.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Shipyard Cutting

- **Plate thickness range** in modern merchant ships: **6 mm to 80 mm** (mild steel, grade A, B, D, E, AH, DH, EH per IACS standards).
- **Typical cutting tolerance**: **±2 mm** for primary hull plates.
- **Edge squareness tolerance**: **±1°** maximum deviation from perpendicular.
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)**: must be **< 2 mm** for class-approved cuts.
- **Bevel angle for welding edges**: typically **30° ± 5°** (single-V or double-V groove).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Classification Reference:** Most shipbuilding cutting operations follow **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** UR S21, S22, and **ISO 9013** standards for thermal cutting quality grades.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cutting Processes in Ship Construction

### A. Thermal (Hot) Cutting Methods

**1. Oxy-Fuel Gas Cutting (OFG) — Most Common in Shipyards**
- **Principle**: Uses a fuel gas (acetylene, propane, methane, or LPG) combusting in pure oxygen to produce a flame (~3,200 °C). A separate high-purity oxygen jet oxidizes (burns) the preheated steel into iron oxide slag, which is blown away.
- **Process Steps**:
  1. Plate surface cleaned (rust, mill scale, paint removed).
  2. Edge preheated to ignition temperature (~870 °C).
  3. Cutting oxygen jet strikes the surface → iron oxidizes → exothermic reaction sustains cut.
  4. Slag is ejected downward by oxygen pressure.
- **Suitable for**: Carbon steel and low-alloy steel **up to 300 mm thickness**.
- **Limitation**: Cannot cut stainless steel, copper, or aluminum (oxide layer blocks oxidation).

**2. Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC)**
- **Principle**: A gas (air, nitrogen, argon, or oxygen) is ionized into a high-temperature plasma jet (~20,000–30,000 °C) that melts and blows away metal.
- **Used for**: Stainless steel, aluminum, and high-tensile steel plates.
- **Advantages**: Faster than OFG on thin plates (< 25 mm), narrower kerf, cleaner cut.

**3. Laser Cutting**
- **Principle**: A focused high-power laser beam (CO₂ or fiber laser) melts/vaporizes the metal.
- **Used for**: Thin plates (< 20 mm), high-precision components.
- **Limitation**: Expensive; not economical for thick hull plates.

**4. Water Jet Cutting (Abrasive Water Jet — AWJ)**
- **Principle**: High-pressure water (up to 4,000 bar) mixed with garnet abrasive cuts the metal.
- **Advantage**: No HAZ, no thermal distortion. Used for special alloys and non-ferrous metals.

### B. Mechanical (Cold) Cutting Methods

**1. Shearing (Guillotine & Alligator Shears)**
- **Principle**: Two blades apply opposing shear stress exceeding the material's ultimate shear strength.
- **Used for**: Plates up to **25 mm thickness**, straight cuts.
- **Limitation**: Produces deformed (cambered) edges on thick plates.

**2. Punching & Nibbling**
- **Principle**: Punch-and-die set shears small slugs out of plate.
- **Used for**: Holes in stiffeners, brackets.

**3. Sawing (Band Saw, Circular Saw)**
- **Used for**: Cutting rolled sections (angles, channels, beams).

**4. Cold Saw / Abrasive Disc Cutting**
- **Used for**: Pipes and small sections.

### C. Edge Preparation (Beveling)

After cutting, edges to be welded must be **beveled** (chamfered) to provide a V-groove for weld metal penetration. Methods include:
- Flame beveling (oxy-fuel).
- Plasma beveling.
- Mechanical edge milling.

## 2.2 Why Cutting is Critical in Ship Construction

- **Hull Accuracy**: A ship is built from thousands of individual plates. A 2 mm error per cut, repeated 5,000 times, results in **10 meters of cumulative dimensional drift**.
- **Weld Quality**: Poor cut edges → poor weld penetration → structural weakness → class society rejection.
- **Material Economy**: Optimal nesting (arrangement of parts on a plate) reduces scrap from **15–20%** down to **5–8%**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat required to raise plate to ignition temp | J (Joules) |
| 2 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Rate of heat input during preheating | W (Watts) |
| 3 | $v_c = \dfrac{L}{t_c}$ | Cutting speed (plate length $\div$ cut time) | mm/min |
| 4 | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = k \cdot t \cdot v_c$ | Oxygen consumption rate (k $\approx$ empirical factor 150–250 L/m for steel) | L/min |
| 5 | $P_{O_2} = \dfrac{F_{cut}}{A_{nozzle}}$ | Cutting oxygen pressure (force $\div$ nozzle area) | bar (MPa) |
| 6 | $R_{kerf} = \dfrac{V_{removed}}{V_{plate}}$ | Kerf ratio (typical $\vert$ $R$ $\vert$ = 0.02–0.05) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{total}}$ | Thermal efficiency of oxy-fuel cut ($\approx$ 0.40–0.70) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta x = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal distortion ($\alpha$ = thermal expansion coeff. for steel $\approx 12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$) | mm |
| 9 | $Nesting Efficiency = \dfrac{A_{used}}{A_{stock}} \times 100\%$ | Material utilization rate (target $\geq$ 85%) | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems, you will most commonly be asked to calculate **cutting time** or **oxygen consumption** for a given plate using the standard shipyard empirical equations above.

## 2.4 Heat Input Calculation — Conceptual Derivation

The **Heat Input (HI)** during thermal cutting determines the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** width and plate distortion.

$$HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v_c}$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = process efficiency (0.6 for OFG, 0.8 for plasma)
- $V$ = arc voltage (volts) — only for arc-based cutting
- $I$ = current (amperes) — only for arc-based cutting
- $v_c$ = cutting speed (mm/min)

For pure **oxy-fuel cutting** (no arc), the heat input is governed by combustion enthalpy:

$$HI_{OFG} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot HHV_{fuel}}{v_c}$$

Where $HHV_{fuel}$ is the **Higher Heating Value** of the fuel gas:
- Acetylene ($C_2H_2$): **48 MJ/kg**
- Propane ($C_3H_8$): **46 MJ/kg**
- Methane ($CH_4$): **55 MJ/kg**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cutting Time for a Rectangular Plate (KTU Frequent Question)

**Problem Statement:** A shipyard plate measuring **4,000 mm × 2,000 mm × 20 mm** (mild steel) is to be cut into 4 equal rectangular pieces using oxy-fuel cutting. The cutting speed for 20 mm plate is **400 mm/min**. The plate must be cut twice along its length and once along its width. Calculate the **total cutting time** including a **15% allowance** for preheating, slag removal, and part handling.

### Given Data
- Plate dimensions: $L = 4000$ mm, $W = 2000$ mm, $t = 20$ mm
- Cutting speed: $v_c = 400$ mm/min
- Allowance factor: $A = 15\% = 0.15$
- Number of cuts: $n_{cuts} = 3$ (2 longitudinal + 1 transverse)

### Step 1: Calculate Total Cut Length

The total length to be cut is the sum of all cut paths.

$$L_{total} = n_{length} \cdot W + n_{width} \cdot L$$

$$L_{total} = 2 \times 2000 + 1 \times 4000$$

$$L_{total} = 4000 + 4000 = 8000 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Base Cutting Time

Cutting time is total cut length divided by cutting speed.

$$t_{base} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{v_c}$$

$$t_{base} = \dfrac{8000 \text{ mm}}{400 \text{ mm/min}}$$

$$t_{base} = 20 \text{ minutes}$$

### Step 3: Apply Allowance Factor

The total time including the 15% allowance for setup and handling:

$$t_{total} = t_{base} \times (1 + A)$$

$$t_{total} = 20 \times (1 + 0.15)$$

$$t_{total} = 20 \times 1.15$$

$$\boxed{t_{total} = 23 \text{ minutes}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Oxygen Consumption Calculation

**Problem Statement:** For the above plate, calculate the **total oxygen consumption** for cutting. Use empirical factor $k = 180$ L of oxygen per meter of cut length for 20 mm mild steel.

### Step 1: Identify Variables
- Empirical oxygen factor: $k = 180$ L/m
- Total cut length: $L_{total} = 8$ m

### Step 2: Apply Oxygen Consumption Formula

$$V_{O_2,total} = k \cdot L_{total}$$

$$V_{O_2,total} = 180 \text{ L/m} \times 8 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{V_{O_2,total} = 1440 \text{ Liters}}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Cost Implication (Optional)

If oxygen cost is ₹15 per cubic meter (industrial grade):

$$\text{Cost} = V_{O_2,total} \times \text{Unit Price} = 1.44 \text{ m}^3 \times 15 = \text{₹}21.60$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** A mid-size shipyard may consume **50,000–80,000 m³** of oxygen per year just for cutting. Oxygen logistics are a major operational cost driver.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Required to Preheat a Steel Plate

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the **heat required** to preheat a 20 mm × 1000 mm × 1000 mm steel plate from 25 °C (room temp) to 870 °C (iron ignition temperature). Given: density of steel $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, specific heat $c_p = 490$ J/(kg·K).

### Step 1: Calculate Mass of the Plate

$$V_{plate} = L \times W \times t = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.020 = 0.020 \text{ m}^3$$

$$m = \rho \cdot V_{plate} = 7850 \times 0.020$$

$$m = 157 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Temperature Difference

$$\Delta T = T_{ignition} - T_{ambient} = 870 - 25$$

$$\Delta T = 845 \text{ °C} = 845 \text{ K}$$

### Step 3: Apply Sensible Heat Formula

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q = 157 \times 490 \times 845$$

$$Q = 65{,}007{,}850 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 65.01 \text{ MJ}}$$

### Step 4: Verification — Energy Content Comparison

Energy in 1 liter of acetylene (HHV ≈ 48 MJ/L) would suffice for:

$$N = \dfrac{Q}{HHV_{acetylene}} = \dfrac{65.01}{48} \approx 1.35 \text{ L of acetylene per m}^2$$

This matches real shipyard data of **~1.0–1.5 L of acetylene per square meter of 20 mm plate**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Nesting Efficiency & Material Saving

**Problem Statement:** A stock plate of 4000 × 2000 mm (8 m²) is used to cut 5 small brackets, each 800 × 400 mm. Calculate **(a)** total area of parts, **(b)** the minimum theoretical plate area required, and **(c)** the nesting efficiency if 0.5 m² of waste remains.

### Step 1: Calculate Area of Parts

$$A_{part} = 0.8 \times 0.4 = 0.32 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{total parts} = 5 \times 0.32 = 1.60 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2: Theoretical Minimum Plate Area

$$A_{min} = A_{total parts} = 1.60 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3: Nesting Efficiency

$$\eta_{nest} = \dfrac{A_{used}}{A_{stock}} \times 100\%$$

Where $A_{used} = A_{total parts} + A_{acceptable waste} = 1.60 + 0.50 = 2.10$ m²

$$\eta_{nest} = \dfrac{2.10}{8.00} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{nest} = 26.25\%}$$

This shows a **poor nesting** — the plate is oversized. In practice, **CNC nesting software** packs hundreds of small parts to achieve $\geq 85\%$ efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Shipyard Cutting Methods

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Shipyard Cutting Methods] --> B[Thermal Hot Cutting]
    A --> C[Mechanical Cold Cutting]
    A --> D[Hybrid Cutting]

    B --> B1[Oxy-Fuel Gas OFG]
    B --> B2[Plasma Arc PAC]
    B --> B3[Laser Cutting]
    B --> B4[Water Jet AWJ]

    B1 --> B1a[Oxy-Acetylene]
    B1 --> B1b[Oxy-Propane]
    B1 --> B1c[Oxy-Methane]

    C --> C1[Shearing Guillotine]
    C --> C2[Punching and Nibbling]
    C --> C3[Sawing]
    C --> C4[Abrasive Disc]

    D --> D1[Plasma Assisted Mechanical]
    D --> D2[Laser Assisted Mechanical]

    B1 --> E[Preheating Phase]
    E --> F[Ignition 870 C]
    F --> G[Oxidation Reaction]
    G --> H[Slag Removal]
    H --> I[Finished Cut Edge]

    C1 --> J[Straight Cut Only]
    J --> K[Cambered Edge Deformation]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Oxy-Fuel Cutting (OFG) Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Ship Steel Plate Received]) --> Clean[Step 1: Surface Cleaning]
    Clean --> Mark[Step 2: Marking using Templates]
    Mark --> Set[Step 3: Setup of Cutting Torch and Gas Pressure]
    Set --> Pre[Step 4: Preheating to 870 C]
    Pre --> Ignite{Step 5: Ignition Achieved?}
    Ignite -->|No| Pre
    Ignite -->|Yes| Cut[Step 6: Cutting Oxygen Jet Activated]
    Cut --> Travel[Torch Travels Along Marked Path]
    Travel --> Check[Step 7: Quality Inspection]
    Check --> Pass{Dimension and Edge OK?}
    Pass -->|No| Rework[Rework or Recycle]
    Pass -->|Yes| Bevel[Step 8: Beveling if Required]
    Bevel --> Store[Step 9: Storage in Assembly Area]
    Rework --> Store
    Store --> End([Ready for Next Fabrication Stage])
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — CNC Cutting Machine Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    CAD[CAD File Hull Lines] --> CAM[Nesting Software]
    CAM --> CNC[CNC Controller Unit]
    CNC --> DriveX[X Axis Drive Motor]
    CNC --> DriveY[Y Axis Drive Motor]
    CNC --> DriveZ[Z Axis Drive Motor]
    DriveX --> Gantry[Gantry and Bridge]
    DriveY --> Gantry
    Gantry --> Torch[Cutting Torch Holder]
    DriveZ --> Torch
    Torch --> Gas1[Oxygen Cylinder Bank]
    Torch --> Gas2[Acetylene or Propane Cylinder]
    Gas1 --> Reg[Pressure Regulators]
    Gas2 --> Reg
    Reg --> Torch
    Torch --> Plate[Steel Plate on Cutting Bed]
    Plate --> Slat[Slat Table for Slag Collection]
    Plate --> Sensor[Edge Tracking Sensor]
    Sensor --> CNC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Shipyard Reality:** Over **90% of cutting in new shipyards** is done using **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) plasma or oxy-fuel machines** with automatic nesting, ensuring high dimensional accuracy and material economy.

## 4.4 Comparative Selection Matrix — Which Cutting Method to Use?

| Plate Thickness | Recommended Method | Typical Speed | Edge Quality |
|:----------------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|:------------:|
| $\leq$ 6 mm | Laser or Shearing | Very High | Excellent |
| 6–25 mm | Plasma or OFG | High | Good |
| 25–50 mm | Oxy-Fuel Gas (OFC) | Medium | Good |
| 50–100 mm | OFC + Multi-Torch | Low | Acceptable |
| $\geq$ 100 mm | OFC + Edge Planer | Very Low | Rough |
| Stainless Steel | Plasma or Laser | Medium | Excellent |
| Aluminum | Plasma or Water Jet | Medium | Excellent |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Cutting" as applied to ship construction. List any four common methods of cutting steel plates used in shipyards.**

**Model Answer:**

Cutting in ship construction refers to the process of separating steel plates and sections into required shapes and sizes using mechanical, thermal, or combined methods to prepare them for subsequent welding and assembly operations.

**Four common methods of cutting steel plates:**

1. **Oxy-Fuel Gas Cutting (OFG)** — Most widely used; employs combustion of fuel gas with oxygen.
2. **Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC)** — Uses ionized gas plasma at very high temperature.
3. **Shearing (Guillotine Cutting)** — Mechanical cold cutting using two opposing blades.
4. **Laser Cutting** — Uses focused high-energy laser beam for precision cuts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *cutting* with *machining*. Remember — **cutting in shipyards = plate separation**, NOT metal removal for tolerance/finish like in CNC machining.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain why oxy-fuel gas cutting is not suitable for cutting stainless steel or aluminum plates in ship construction.**

**Model Answer:**

Oxy-fuel gas cutting relies on the **exothermic oxidation reaction** of iron with oxygen. The cutting oxygen jet oxidizes the heated metal, and the molten oxide slag is blown away by gas pressure.

For stainless steel and aluminum:

1. **Stainless Steel**: Contains chromium which forms a **refractory chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃) layer** on the surface. This layer has a melting point **higher than the base metal**, preventing the oxygen jet from reaching the unoxidized metal underneath. The cut either stops or produces a rough, slag-filled edge.

2. **Aluminum**: Has a **very thin, tenacious aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) layer** that melts at ~2050 °C — far above aluminum's own melting point (660 °C). Also, aluminum's high thermal conductivity dissipates heat too fast to maintain ignition temperature.

**Conclusion**: For these metals, **Plasma Arc Cutting or Laser Cutting** are preferred alternatives.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q: With neat sketches, describe the Oxy-Fuel Gas Cutting (OFG) process used in shipbuilding. Also explain the functions of (a) preheating oxygen, (b) cutting oxygen, and (c) the cutting nozzle.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
Oxy-Fuel Gas Cutting (OFG) is the most widely used thermal cutting process in ship construction. It employs a fuel gas (acetylene, propane, methane, or LPG) combusting with oxygen to produce a high-temperature flame that preheats the steel to its ignition temperature (~870 °C). A separate stream of high-purity cutting oxygen is then introduced through a central jet in the nozzle to oxidize (burn) the steel into molten iron oxide (FeO/Fe₂O₃), which is ejected as slag.

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

The process consists of the following sequential steps:

1. **Plate Preparation**: The steel plate surface is cleaned of rust, mill scale, paint, and oil to ensure uniform preheating.
2. **Preheating**: The fuel gas + preheating oxygen mixture is ignited at the nozzle tip to produce a flame (~3,200 °C in oxy-acetylene). This flame preheats the steel surface to its ignition temperature.
3. **Cutting Oxygen Activation**: Once ignition temperature is reached, the cutting oxygen valve is opened. A high-velocity oxygen jet strikes the preheated spot, initiating an **exothermic oxidation reaction**:
$$Fe + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow FeO + \text{Heat (Heat of Oxidation} \approx 7.4 \text{ MJ/kg)}$$
4. **Slag Ejection**: The molten iron oxide (slag) is blown downward by the cutting oxygen jet, leaving a clean kerf.
5. **Travel**: The torch is moved along the marked line at a controlled **cutting speed**, sustaining the oxidation process.

**Functions of Key Components (5 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **(a) Preheating Oxygen** | Mixed with fuel gas to produce a hot flame (≈3,200 °C) that raises the steel surface to its ignition temperature (~870 °C). |
| **(b) Cutting Oxygen** | A high-purity, high-velocity oxygen jet that oxidizes the preheated steel into iron oxide and ejects the molten slag, performing the actual cut. |
| **(c) Cutting Nozzle** | A precision-machined brass nozzle with separate orifices — outer ring for preheating flames and central bore for cutting oxygen jet. The nozzle geometry (orifice diameter) determines kerf width and oxygen pressure requirement. |

**Diagram (2 marks):**
A neat labelled sketch of an oxy-fuel cutting torch showing:
- Fuel gas inlet
- Preheating oxygen inlet
- Cutting oxygen inlet
- Nozzle assembly
- Kerf and slag formation on plate

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly score zero by writing only the torch diagram without explaining the **chemistry of oxidation**. KTU examiners specifically look for the **Fe + O₂ → FeO** reaction and the **role of slag ejection** in achieving a clean cut.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain with a neat sketch the Plasma Arc Cutting process. List its advantages and limitations compared to oxy-fuel gas cutting. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A mild steel plate of dimensions 3000 mm × 1500 mm × 15 mm is to be cut into three equal parts along its length using oxy-fuel cutting. Calculate (i) the total cut length, (ii) the cutting time if cutting speed is 350 mm/min, and (iii) the total oxygen consumed using empirical factor k = 160 L/m. Assume 10% allowance for preheating and handling. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

### Part (a) — Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC) (7 Marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
Plasma Arc Cutting uses an **electric arc** struck between an electrode (tungsten) and the workpiece (or between two electrodes) inside a constricted nozzle. A gas (air, nitrogen, oxygen, or argon-hydrogen) is forced through this arc, becoming ionized into a **fourth state of matter — plasma** — with temperatures of **20,000–30,000 °C**. This plasma jet melts the metal and blows away the molten material.

**Sketch Description (2 marks):**
A labelled sketch must show:
- Tungsten electrode (cathode)
- Copper constricting nozzle (anode)
- Plasma gas inlet
- Arc column inside nozzle
- Plasma jet emerging from nozzle orifice
- Workpiece (steel plate)
- Molten metal and gas flow downward

**Advantages over OFG (2 marks):**
1. Cuts **all electrically conductive metals** — stainless steel, aluminum, copper, high-tensile steel.
2. **Faster on thin plates** (< 25 mm) with narrower kerf and less slag.
3. **Better edge quality** — smaller HAZ, less distortion.
4. Easier to automate with CNC.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Higher equipment cost than OFG.
2. Limited plate thickness (typically up to 50 mm economically).
3. Produces **noise, UV radiation, and fumes** — requires fume extraction and operator PPE.

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Plate dimensions: $L = 3000$ mm, $W = 1500$ mm, $t = 15$ mm
- Cutting speed: $v_c = 350$ mm/min
- Oxygen factor: $k = 160$ L/m
- Allowance: $A = 10\% = 0.10$
- Number of longitudinal cuts: $n = 2$ (to divide plate into 3 equal parts)

**(i) Total Cut Length (2 marks):**

$$L_{total} = n \times W = 2 \times 1500$$

$$L_{total} = 3000 \text{ mm} = 3 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Cutting Time (3 marks):**

Base time:
$$t_{base} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{v_c} = \dfrac{3000}{350} = 8.571 \text{ min}$$

Total time with 10% allowance:
$$t_{total} = t_{base} \times (1 + A) = 8.571 \times 1.10$$

$$\boxed{t_{total} \approx 9.43 \text{ minutes}}$$

**(iii) Oxygen Consumed (2 marks):**

$$V_{O_2} = k \times L_{total} = 160 \text{ L/m} \times 3 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{V_{O_2} = 480 \text{ Liters}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Numerical Problems:**
> 1. Forgetting to **convert mm to m** when using the empirical oxygen factor (always check units!).
> 2. Counting cuts incorrectly — to divide a plate into 3 parts you need **only 2 cuts**, not 3.
> 3. Forgetting to **add the percentage allowance** to base time in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cutting** in ship construction = controlled separation of steel plates into usable hull parts, NOT machining.
- **Oxy-Fuel Gas (OFG) cutting** is the most common method; works only on carbon/low-alloy steels due to reliance on iron oxidation.
- The **ignition temperature of steel** is approximately **870 °C** — a key exam value.
- **Three sequential steps** of OFG: **Preheating → Ignition → Oxidation + Slag ejection**.
- **Plasma Arc Cutting** is used for stainless steel, aluminum, and high-tensile steels.
- **Shearing** is limited to plates $\leq$ 25 mm and produces cambered edges.
- **Standard plate thickness range** in merchant shipbuilding: **6 mm to 80 mm**.
- **Nesting efficiency target**: $\geq$ **85%** to minimize material wastage.
- **Cutting speed formula**: $v_c = L / t_c$ — most common in numerical questions.
- **Oxygen consumption formula**: $V_{O_2} = k \cdot L$ where $k$ is empirical (typically 100–300 L/m).
- **Always remember to convert mm to m** when applying empirical factors in numericals.
- **Edge preparation (beveling)** typically produces a **30° V-groove** for subsequent welding.
- **Modern shipyards use CNC cutting machines** with automatic nesting for accuracy and economy.
- **Class societies (IACS, IRS, DNV, ABS, LR)** have strict standards (UR S21, ISO 9013) for cut quality.
- **Safety**: Cutting produces UV radiation, fumes (NO₂, CO), molten slag, and noise — PPE and fume extraction are mandatory.
- **The three oxy-fuel cutting parameters to remember**: **fuel gas, preheating oxygen, cutting oxygen** — each plays a distinct role.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending and Welding of Steel Plates and Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bending and Welding of Steel Plates and Frames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Steel Plate Bending in Ship Construction

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Steel plate bending is a fundamental forming process in shipbuilding wherein flat rolled steel plates (typically of grades **AH36**, **DH36**, **EH36**, or ordinary mild steel per classification society rules) are plastically deformed along one or more axes to produce curved shells, decks, bulkheads, and other hull components. The process exploits the ductility of steel beyond its elastic limit, producing permanent set without fracture.

**Key Metrics Used in Industry:**
- **Plate thickness range:** **3 mm to 100 mm** for ship hull plates
- **Minimum bending radius (R):** Typically **R ≥ 2t to 3t** for transverse cold bending (where *t* = plate thickness)
- **Standard mill plate size:** Up to **5 m × 20 m**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Ship plates are bent to develop the curvature of the hull — transverse curvature (to form the round of the bilge and sheer) and longitudinal curvature (to form the camber and rise of the floor). These bends are non-developable surfaces approximated by developable patches.

### 1.2 Frame Bending (Section Bending)

**Formal Definition:**
Frame bending refers to the plastic deformation of rolled steel sections (such as **T-bars, L-angles, HP-bulb plates, flat bars, and channels**) used as longitudinal and transverse frames in a ship's structure. Frames must be curved to follow the hull's geometry and welded into position to form the ship's skeleton (the *framing system*).

**Common Frame Sections in Shipbuilding:**
- **T-Sections** (web + flange) — for floors, longitudinals
- **Bulb Plates** — for side stringers
- **L-Angles** — for bracket connections
- **Flat Bars** — for light stiffeners

### 1.3 Welding in Ship Construction

**Formal Definition:**
Welding is a **fusion joining process** used to permanently fuse steel plates and frames by localized melting of the parent metal (with or without filler wire) to form a metallurgical bond. In shipbuilding, **arc welding** (specifically **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)**, **Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)**, and **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)**) dominates, replacing older **riveting** methods since the 1940s.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Plate Bending as "Origami for Steel":**
> Imagine you have a thick sheet of construction paper. You cannot just push it into a curved tube without creases — it folds sharply. But steel is special: under sufficient force, it *flows plastically* (the atoms slip past each other along crystal planes) and adopts a smooth curve without cracking. **Cold bending** is like bending the paper with your hands, while **hot bending** is like warming the paper with a hairdryer first to make it pliable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Welding as "Steel Glue that Melts":**
> Ordinary glue sticks two surfaces together externally. Welding, however, *melts* the two steel pieces at the joint so the liquid metal pools together and freezes into one continuous piece — the joint is often **stronger than the parent metal** itself. A welder's arc is essentially a controlled, miniature lightning bolt of **~6,000 °C**.

### 1.4 Classification of Bending Methods

| Method | Temperature | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold Bending (Press Braking)** | Room Temp | Plates up to ~25 mm, small curvature |
| **Cold Bending (Rolling)** | Room Temp | Large radius bends, cylindrical shells |
| **Hot Bending** | 900 °C – 1000 °C (red heat) | Heavy plates >25 mm, tight radii |
| **Line Heating (Flame Bending)** | Localized 600 °C – 900 °C | Complex 3D curves (bow, stern) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cold Bending of Plates — Operational Logic

**Step-by-Step Process Logic:**

1. **Loading Stage:** The plate is positioned over a die (V-die or U-die) in a press brake. A punch descends with force **F**.
2. **Elastic Deformation:** Initial stress is below yield strength **σy**. Strain is recoverable.
3. **Plastic Deformation:** Stress exceeds **σy** (≈ **250 MPa** for mild steel). The plate yields and bends plastically.
4. **Springback:** Upon punch release, the plate recovers elastically by an angle **Δθ**, increasing the bend radius.
5. **Final Geometry:** The actual bend radius is **R = R_die + t/2 + springback compensation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters in KTU answers:** Examiners reward students who mention that **springback** must be over-bent (over-bending angle ≈ 1°–3° for steel) to achieve the design radius. Forgetting springback is the #1 reason real fabricators scrap plates.

### 2.2 Minimum Bending Radius (Why and How)

**The "Why":** Beyond a certain tight radius, the outer fibers of the plate exceed the ultimate tensile strength **σu** and crack.

**The "How":** For a plate of thickness *t* bent to inner radius *R*, the outer fiber strain is:
- $\varepsilon_{outer} = \dfrac{t}{2R + t}$

Setting this equal to the allowable strain (≈ **25%** for mild steel, **17%** for AH36):
- $R_{min} = \dfrac{t}{2\varepsilon_{allow}} - \dfrac{t}{2}$

For AH36 with $\varepsilon_{allow} = 0.17$:
- $R_{min} \approx 2.4\,t$

This matches the **classification society rule** of **R ≥ 2.5t** for transverse bending.

### 2.3 Hot Bending and Line Heating — Why It Works

When steel is heated above its **upper critical temperature (Ac3 ≈ 723 °C for eutectoid steel)**, the BCC ferrite transforms to FCC austenite, which is **~30% more ductile**. Thermal expansion also produces a *bulge*; when water-quenched (or air-cooled in line heating), this bulge contracts more than the surrounding cool zone, producing a **shrinkage-induced bend**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Line Heating is the cornerstone of modern shipyard plate forming.** Robotic line heaters can induce 3D compound curvature by precisely controlling torch speed, heat input, and cooling pattern.

### 2.4 Welding Process Selection Logic

**Decision Flow:**
1. **Position** (1G, 2G, 3G, 4G per ASME) → Determines whether to use **SMAW** (versatile) or **SAW** (flat only).
2. **Plate thickness** → Thin (<6 mm): **GMAW/FCAW**; Thick (>20 mm): **SAW or Electroslag**.
3. **Speed required** → **FCAW** deposits **2–4 kg/hr**; **SAW** deposits **up to 15 kg/hr**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $R_{min} = \dfrac{t}{2\varepsilon_{allow}} - \dfrac{t}{2}$ | Minimum bending radius before cracking | mm |
| $\varepsilon_{outer} = \dfrac{t}{2R + t}$ | Outer fiber strain in a bend | dimensionless |
| $\Delta R = \dfrac{\sigma_y \cdot R^2}{E \cdot t}$ | Springback radius increase (small deflection) | mm |
| $\theta_{springback} = \dfrac{M_y \cdot L}{E \cdot I}$ | Springback angle in elastic recovery | radians |
| $t_t = 0.707 \cdot h$ | Fillet weld throat thickness (*h* = leg size) | mm |
| $t_e = t$ | Effective throat for full-penetration butt weld | mm |
| $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{0.6 \sigma_y}{FS}$ | Allowable weld stress (AWS-D1.1, FS = safety factor) | MPa |
| $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{t}$ | Heat input in welding (V = voltage, I = current, t = time) | **kJ/mm** |
| $P = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{R^2}$ | Press brake bending force approximation | **N** |
| $R = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot D^2}{h} + \dfrac{h}{2}$ | Rolled plate radius from chord *D* and rise *h* | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The heat input equation $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{t}$ (in **kJ/mm**) is the single most regulated parameter in shipbuilding welds because excessive heat input causes **HAZ softening, distortion, and reduced Charpy impact toughness** at low temperatures (critical for Arctic-class vessels).

### 2.6 Weld Types in Shipbuilding

| **Weld Type** | **Symbol** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Square Butt** | `\|\|` | Plates < 6 mm |
| **Single-V Butt** | `V` | Plates 6–25 mm |
| **Double-V Butt** | `X` | Plates > 25 mm (both sides welded) |
| **Single Bevel Butt** | `>` | One-sided access only |
| **Fillet (T-joint)** | `Δ` | Frame-to-plate connections |
| **Slot/Pocket Weld** | `▢` | Light stiffeners |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Springback in Plate Bending

**Given:** A plate of thickness *t* is bent elasto-plastically over a die of radius $R_d$, then released. Find the final radius *R*.

**Step 1 — Define the loading geometry:**
During bending, the neutral axis (mid-plane) has radius $R_d + t/2$. The outer fiber has radius $R_d + t$, and the inner fiber has radius $R_d$.

**Step 2 — Apply curvature-strain relationship:**
The outer fiber strain (at the moment of bending, while still under load) is:

$$
\varepsilon_{load} = \dfrac{(R_d + t) - (R_d + t/2)}{R_d + t/2} = \dfrac{t/2}{R_d + t/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the elastic and plastic components:**
Beyond yield, the strain splits into elastic (recoverable) and plastic (permanent) parts. Using the standard assumption that upon unloading, the **bending moment M = σy · Z** (plastic moment capacity) is released by an elastic reverse curvature:

$$
\Delta\kappa = \dfrac{M}{E \cdot I} = \dfrac{\sigma_y \cdot (bt^2/4)}{E \cdot (bt^3/12)} = \dfrac{3\sigma_y}{E \cdot t}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert curvature change to radius change:**
Since curvature $\kappa = 1/R$, for small changes:

$$
\Delta\left(\dfrac{1}{R}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta R}{R^2}
$$

Setting magnitudes equal:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta R}{R_d^2} \approx \dfrac{3\sigma_y}{E \cdot t}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for springback radius:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta R = \dfrac{3\sigma_y \cdot R_d^2}{E \cdot t}}
$$

**Interpretation for KTU:** For **mild steel** with $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 200$ GPa, $t = 10$ mm, $R_d = 30$ mm:

$$
\Delta R = \dfrac{3 \cdot 250 \cdot 30^2}{200{,}000 \cdot 10} = \dfrac{675{,}000}{2{,}000{,}000} = 0.3375 \text{ mm}
$$

The springback is small here, but for **thin, high-strength plates** (e.g., $t = 3$ mm) the effect becomes very large, requiring **over-bending** or **bottoming** the punch.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Throat Thickness of a Fillet Weld

**Given:** A fillet weld with equal leg length *h* (an isosceles right triangle in cross-section).

**Step 1 — Draw the geometry:**
The fusion face of the weld is the hypotenuse. The throat is the **perpendicular distance** from the root of the joint to the hypotenuse face.

**Step 2 — Apply Pythagoras to half the triangle:**
