Consider the right triangle with hypotenuse $h$ and two legs of length $h/\sqrt{2}$. The altitude from the right angle to the hypotenuse is:

$$
t_t = \sqrt{h^2 - \left(\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2} = \sqrt{h^2 - \dfrac{h^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{h^2}{2}} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
\boxed{t_t = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,h}
$$

**KTU Significance:** The shear stress in a fillet weld is checked as:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \cdot h \cdot L} \leq \tau_{allow} = \dfrac{0.4 \sigma_y}{FS}
$$

This is the design equation examiners love to test for **bracket-to-plate** and **frame-to-shell** connections.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Plate Rolling Radius from Sagitta Method

**Given:** A plate of length *L* is rolled to produce a chord *D* and a rise (sagitta) *h*. Derive the curvature radius.

**Step 1 — Geometric setup:**
Two right triangles share the radius line from the center of curvature to each chord endpoint. Each has:
- Hypotenuse = **R** (radius)
- One leg = **R − h** (from center to chord)
- Other leg = **D/2** (half-chord)

**Step 2 — Apply Pythagorean theorem:**

$$
R^2 = \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^2 + (R - h)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the right side:**

$$
R^2 = \dfrac{D^2}{4} + R^2 - 2Rh + h^2
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $R^2$ from both sides:**

$$
0 = \dfrac{D^2}{4} - 2Rh + h^2
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for *R*:**

$$
2Rh = \dfrac{D^2}{4} + h^2
$$

$$
\boxed{R = \dfrac{D^2}{8h} + \dfrac{h}{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use:** Shipyard quality inspectors measure **sagitta** with a template and chord tape to verify the rolled plate matches the required hull curvature (typically **R = 10 m to 50 m** for side shell plating).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Weld Defects vs. Causes vs. Remedies

| **Defect** | **Primary Cause** | **Remedy in Shipyard** | **Standard Reference** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Porosity** | Contaminants (rust, oil, moisture) | Grind clean, re-weld with **low-hydrogen electrode** (e.g., E7018) | AWS D1.1 |
| **Undercut** | Excessive current / wrong angle | Reduce current, adjust torch angle to **15°–30° drag** | AWS D1.1 |
| **Lack of Fusion** | Low heat input / dirty surface | Increase current, grind to clean metal | AWS D1.1 |
| **Crack (Transverse)** | High restraint + hydrogen | Pre-heat to **75–150 °C**, use **low-hydrogen process** | IACS S21 |
| **Distortion** | Uneven heating/cooling | Use **balanced welding sequence**, **backstep technique** | IACS S21 |
| **Lamellar Tearing** | Through-thickness strain in T-joints | Use **Z-direction steel** (S275/Z35) | EN 10164 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plate Bending Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flat Steel Plate] --> B{Thickness Check}
    B -->|t less than 25 mm| C[Cold Bending Path]
    B -->|t greater than 25 mm| D[Hot Bending Path]
    C --> C1[Press Brake]
    C --> C2[Three-Roll Pyramid Bending]
    C --> C3[Four-Roll Bending]
    D --> D1[Line Heating - Robotic Torch]
    D --> D2[Oven Heating then Rolling]
    D --> D3[Induction Heating]
    C1 --> E[Quality Check - Sagitta Template]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Approved Curved Plate]
    E -->|Rejects| G[Scrap or Re-bend]
    F --> H[Ship Block Assembly]
```

### 4.2 Welding Process Selection — Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Required Joint] --> B{Position}
    B -->|Flat 1G or 2G| C[SAW Submerged Arc Welding]
    B -->|All Positions| D[SMAW or FCAW]
    B -->|Vertical 3G| E[SMAW - Cellulosic Electrode]
    A --> F{Plate Thickness}
    F -->|less than 6 mm| G[GMAW MIG]
    F -->|6 to 25 mm| H[FCAW Flux Cored]
    F -->|greater than 25 mm| I[SAW or Electroslag]
    C --> J[Final Weld Inspection]
    D --> J
    E --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{NDT Method}
    K -->|Visual| L[VT - 100 percent]
    K -->|Surface| M[MPT or DPT]
    K -->|Sub-surface| N[UT or RT]
    L --> O[Ship Hull Section]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.3 Weld Types in Hull Joints — Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Butt Weld - Shell to Shell] --> A1[Full Penetration X Groove]
    A2[Fillet Weld - Frame to Plate] --> B[Standard 70 deg Leg]
    A3[T Lap Weld - Stiffener] --> C[Light Duty Fillet]
    A4[Plug or Slot Weld] --> D[Light Stiffener to Plate]
    A5[Edge Weld - Bulkhead to Deck] --> E[T Joint Fillet]
    A1 --> F[Strength Critical]
    B --> G[Stiffener Connection]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> F
```

### 4.4 Frame Bending — Process Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rolled Section] --> B{Section Size}
    B -->|Small T less than 100 mm| C[Cold Bending - Pyramid Rolls]
    B -->|Large HP Bulb or T| D[Hot Bending - Furnace at 900 C]
    C --> E[Check Radius vs Template]
    D --> E
    E -->|OK| F[Fit to Hull Block]
    E -->|Reject| G[Heat Straighten or Scrap]
    F --> H[Tack Weld]
    H --> I[Full Welding]
    I --> J[NDT and Release]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each node uses alphanumeric IDs (e.g., `A`, `C1`, `J`) per KTU visualization guidelines. Mermaid renders the text labels as plain uppercase strings for maximum clarity during board-exam revision.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**
*What is line heating? Why is it preferred for 3D-curved ship hull plates?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Line heating is a thermal forming process in which a controlled oxy-fuel torch heats a narrow strip of steel plate to **600–900 °C** and the surrounding cooler water-sprayed or air-cooled zone constrains the contraction, producing a **local shrinkage-induced bend**. (1 mark)
It is preferred for 3D-curved plates (e.g., bulbous bow, transom stern) because: (a) the torch path can be programmed in CNC patterns to induce **compound curvature** in both longitudinal and transverse directions simultaneously; (b) it handles **heavy plates** (up to 100 mm) without massive press equipment; (c) the heating and cooling cycle allows **fine, iterative correction** of curvature by template measurement. (2 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State the significance of throat thickness in a fillet weld. Write the relationship between throat and leg size.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The throat thickness ($t_t$) is the **smallest cross-sectional dimension** of a fillet weld, measured perpendicular to the fusion face. It is the **effective load-carrying area** resisting shear forces along the weld. (2 marks)
For an equal-leg fillet weld, the relationship is: $t_t = 0.707 \times h$ (where $h$ = leg length). (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **cold bending of steel plates** using a press brake. Discuss the role of **springback** and how it is compensated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **mild steel plate** of thickness **t = 12 mm** is bent over a die of radius **R_d = 60 mm** in a press brake. Given $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, calculate the **springback in radius** $\Delta R$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Cold Bending in a Press Brake — Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Definition:** Cold bending is plastic deformation of a plate at **ambient temperature** using a press brake. (1 mark)
- **Components:** Upper punch (male tool), lower V-die, hydraulic or mechanical ram, backgauge. (1 mark)
- **Process:** Plate placed on die → punch descends → plate elastically then plastically deforms → bend formed → punch retracts. (1 mark)
- **V-die opening rule:** Opening = **6t to 12t** for mild steel (smaller t-multiple → tighter radius). (1 mark)
- **Springback:** Upon unloading, the plate recovers elastically. The radius increases by $\Delta R = 3\sigma_y R_d^2 / (E \cdot t)$. The bend angle opens by **~1°–3°** depending on $R/t$ ratio. (1 mark)
- **Compensation methods:** (i) **Over-bending** (bending past the target angle by the springback amount); (ii) **Bottoming the punch** (pressing the plate fully into the die bottom to negate elastic recovery); (iii) **Compensated die geometry** with reduced radius. (1 mark)
- **Sketch:** Should show V-die, punch, plate, and angle indication. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the springback formula:**

$$
\Delta R = \dfrac{3 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot R_d^2}{E \cdot t}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (with units):**

$$
\Delta R = \dfrac{3 \cdot 250 \text{ N/mm}^2 \cdot (60 \text{ mm})^2}{2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \cdot 12 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3 \cdot 250 \cdot 3600 = 2{,}700{,}000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 10^5 \cdot 12 = 2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute springback:**

$$
\Delta R = \dfrac{2.7 \times 10^6}{2.4 \times 10^6} = 1.125 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — State the final answer:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta R = 1.125 \text{ mm}}
$$

The die radius should be reduced by **1.125 mm** (i.e., use a die of radius **~58.9 mm**) to achieve the target 60 mm radius after springback. (1 mark)

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **line heating method** for forming curved hull plates. State the variables that control the curvature produced. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **double-V butt weld** is to be made in a **20 mm thick AH36 plate** with a **leg preparation of 60°** and a **root gap of 2 mm**. Calculate: (i) the **total cross-sectional area of the weld metal**; (ii) the **weight of weld metal required per metre run** if the density of weld metal is **7850 kg/m³**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Line Heating (7 marks):**

- **Definition:** A thermal forming technique using a moving oxy-fuel torch to heat a narrow line (typically **15–25 mm wide**) on a plate; the heated zone expands and is constrained by surrounding cool metal. On cooling, the heated zone shrinks more than the surroundings, inducing a **bend toward the heated side**. (2 marks)
- **Process sequence:** Mark torch path using NC template → heat strip → water-spray or air-quench → measure sagitta against template → repeat for compound curvature. (2 marks)
- **Controlling variables:** (i) **Heat input** $Q = VI/t$ in **kJ/mm**; (ii) **Torch speed** (typical **5–30 mm/s**); (iii) **Track spacing** for multiple parallel lines; (iv) **Plate temperature** reached; (v) **Cooling rate** (water vs. air); (vi) **Plate thickness**; (vii) **Steel grade** (carbon equivalent). (3 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Geometry of one V-groove (one side of double-V):**

The cross-section of a single V-groove is an isosceles triangle with:
- Depth of groove = $(20 - 2)/2 = 9$ mm (excluding 2 mm root face plus root gap absorbed) → simpler: use full V depth **h = 10 mm** (symmetric, with **2 mm root gap** spread over both sides → each side depth = $(20-2)/2 = 9$ mm, with included angle 60°)
- Included angle: **60°** → half-angle = 30°
- Width at top of one V = $2 \cdot 9 \cdot \tan(30°) = 2 \cdot 9 \cdot 0.5774 = 10.39$ mm

**Step 2 — Area of one V-groove cross-section:**

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 10.39 \cdot 9 = 46.78 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Area of root gap (2 mm wide × depth in one side):**

Root gap is a small rectangle of **2 mm × 9 mm** (one side's share):

$$
A_{gap} = 2 \cdot 9 = 18 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Total weld area (both sides + root):**

$$
A_{total} = 2 \cdot (46.78 + 18) = 2 \cdot 64.78 = 129.56 \text{ mm}^2
$$

(Alternatively, by a direct formula: $A = 2 \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 2h \cdot \tan(\alpha/2) \cdot h\right] + 2 \cdot g \cdot h = 2 h^2 \tan(\alpha/2) + 2gh$, with $h=9$, $\alpha=60°$, $g=2$: $A = 2(81)(0.577) + 2(2)(9) = 93.5 + 36 = 129.5$ mm²) ✓

**Step 5 — Convert to m² and compute volume per metre:**

$$
A = 129.56 \text{ mm}^2 = 129.56 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V = A \cdot L = 129.56 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1 = 1.2956 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per metre}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute weight:**

$$
W = \rho \cdot V = 7850 \cdot 1.2956 \times 10^{-4} = 1.017 \text{ kg/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{weld} = 129.56 \text{ mm}^2, \quad W = 1.017 \text{ kg/m run}}
$$

(1 mark for units check, 1 mark for final boxed answer)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams (Topic: Bending & Welding):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Loss of **0.5–1 mark** per question. Always write "**mm**", "**MPa**", "**kg/m**" alongside numbers.
> 2. **Wrong V-groove area formula** — Students often use only $A = \frac{1}{2}bh$ and **forget the root-gap contribution** ($\approx 14\%$ of total). This costs **1.5–2 marks** on a 7-mark problem.
> 3. **Confusing throat thickness with leg size** — Throat is **0.707 × leg**, not equal to leg. This is a classic MCQ trap.
> 4. **Not mentioning springback** in cold-bending answers — Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for omitting this phenomenon.
> 5. **Sketch missing labels** — In 7-mark theory questions, sketches must be **labelled** (die, punch, plate, radius). Unlabelled sketches get only **0.5 of 2 marks** allotted.
> 6. **Wrong welding-process choice** — Don't write "use SAW for overhead welding" — it physically cannot be used in vertical/overhead positions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Bending Methods:** Cold bending (press brake, rolling) for thin plates; **hot bending** (>900 °C) and **line heating** for thick plates and 3D shapes.
- **Springback Formula:** $\Delta R = \dfrac{3\sigma_y R_d^2}{E t}$ — always mention in cold-bending answers.
- **Minimum Bending Radius:** $R_{min} \geq 2.5t$ for AH36 per classification rules; derived from outer-fiber strain limit (~17%).
- **Line Heating:** Robotic torch + water/air cooling = precise compound curvature; controlled by **heat input** (kJ/mm), **torch speed**, and **track spacing**.
- **Welding Processes:** SMAW (versatile), FCAW (high-deposition), SAW (high-speed, flat only), GMAW (thin plates).
- **Fillet Weld Throat:** $t_t = 0.707 \times h$ (leg size).
- **Butt Weld Throat:** $t_t = t$ (full penetration).
- **Heat Input:** $Q = \dfrac{VI}{t}$ in **kJ/mm** — regulated to control HAZ properties.
- **Common Defects:** Porosity, undercut, lack of fusion, cracks, lamellar tearing — know cause + remedy.
- **Weld Inspection:** VT (100%), MPT/DPT (surface), UT/RT (sub-surface).
- **Frame Bending:** T-sections and bulb plates bent cold (small) or hot (large); checked against hull templates.
- **Classification Society Rules:** Always refer to **IACS S21** (welding) and **IACS S11** (plates) in design answers.
- **Sketch Discipline:** Every 7-mark theory question in KTU demands a **labelled diagram** (die, punch, weld symbol, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fabrication of Component Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fabrication of Component Parts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fabrication of Component Parts** in shipbuilding refers to the systematic transformation of raw shipbuilding steel (plates, sections, and bars received from the rolling mill or steel stockyard) into accurately shaped, dimensioned, and edge-prepared structural members ready for sub-assembly and final welding on the berth or in the panel line.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, *fabrication* is treated as the **pre-assembly stage** that lies between *steel preparation* (cutting, marking) and *block construction* (joining of sub-assemblies into units). It includes all shop-floor operations such as **straightening, rolling (line heating/cold forming), bending, edge preparation (beveling), and pre-fabrication of stiffeners, brackets, and curved plates**.

### 1.2 Component Parts of a Ship — Overview

A modern steel-hulled ship is essentially a **three-dimensional welded space frame** composed of:

| Component Part | Typical Thickness | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| Shell Plating | 10 – 40 mm | Watertight outer hull envelope |
| Deck Plating | 8 – 25 mm | Horizontal strength + weather deck |
| Bulkhead Plating | 6 – 20 mm | Transverse/longitudinal subdivision |
| Stiffeners (Flat Bars, Bulb Plates) | 6 – 25 mm (web) | Local panel stiffening |
| Longitudinals & Girders | 8 – 30 mm | Longitudinal strength members |
| Brackets, Braces, Stays | 8 – 20 mm | Triangulation and load transfer |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel Origami" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are building a giant 3-D puzzle from a flat sheet of steel.** First you flatten the steel into manageable flat plates (raw material), then you cut them into pieces (marking & cutting), next you bend some pieces into L, T, or curved shapes (rolling & bending), and finally you smooth their edges so two pieces can be glued (welded) seamlessly together. This entire "cut → bend → edge-prep" workflow is exactly what **fabrication of component parts** means — it is the **"shaping workshop"** of a shipyard before any large-scale assembly begins.

### 1.4 Standard Shipbuilding Grades Referenced

- **Grade A, B, D, E** (as per IACS / ASTM A131) for general hull.
- **Grade AH32, DH36, EH36, AH40** for higher-strength applications.
- Yield strengths typically **235 MPa (mild)** to **390 MPa (high-tensile)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR)** and **KTU 2024 Module 2**, fabrication tolerances for plate flatness, section straightness, and bevel angle are non-negotiable quality checkpoints.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Component Fabrication

The fabrication workflow can be decomposed into a logical sequence:

1. **Receiving & Identification** — Plates arrive with rolling-mill markings, heat numbers, and certificates. Each plate is logged into the **Material Identification System (MIS)**.
2. **Straightening** — Plates and sections may be warped from rolling/transport. They are flattened using:
   - Cold straightening (rolling press, gag press)
   - Hot straightening (line heating with oxy-acetylene torch)
3. **Marking & Cutting** — Using templates (formerly wooden, now CNC/laser) plates are marked with center lines, weld joint positions, and cut to shape using:
   - Cold: shearing, punching, nibbling, plasma, laser, water-jet
   - Hot: oxy-fuel cutting (manual, photo-electric, CNC)
4. **Forming (Rolling & Bending)** — Plates and sections are deformed plastically into curved/angled shapes.
5. **Edge Preparation** — Plate edges are beveled (e.g., single-V, double-V, J-groove, K-groove) for full-penetration welds.

### 2.2 Rolling (Plate Bending) — The Physics

Rolling is a **three-roller pyramid-type bending** operation. The plate passes between rolls that apply a **transverse bending moment** along the roll axis. Plastic deformation is achieved by **exceeding the yield stress** in the outer fibres.

**Key principle:** Radius of curvature $(R)$ is inversely proportional to roll offset $(h)$:

$$R \;\propto\; \frac{1}{h}$$

A *smaller* roll displacement produces a *larger* (gentler) bend radius; a *larger* displacement produces a *tighter* (sharper) curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the plate curve?** When the top roll pushes down, the upper fibres of the plate are compressed (shorter) and the lower fibres are stretched (longer). This differential length is what locks the curvature in the steel permanently.

### 2.3 Edge Preparation — Why It Matters

For full-penetration butt welds, the **root gap (G)** and **bevel angle (α)** must allow the welding arc and molten filler metal to reach the root of the joint. Standard shipbuilding bevels:

- **Single-V (most common, 50–60° included angle)**
- **Double-V** for plates > 20 mm
- **J-groove / U-groove** for thick plates — saves weld metal
- **Square butt** only for thin plates ≤ 6 mm

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bend Allowance (BA) | $BA = (R + k \cdot t)\,\theta$ | mm | $R$ = neutral axis radius, $k$ = 0.33 (mild steel) / 0.5, $t$ = thickness, $\theta$ = bend angle in radians |
| 2 | Outer fibre stretch | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{(R + t)\,\theta - R\,\theta}{R\,\theta} = \dfrac{t}{R}$ | dimensionless | Used to check cold-forming limits (≤ 5% per IACS) |
| 3 | Cold-bending min. radius | $R_{min} = 20\,t$ (transverse), $R_{min} = 50\,t$ (longitudinal) | mm | Empirical shipyard rule |
| 4 | Plate area | $A = L \times B$ | $m^2$ | Used for nesting/cutting plans |
| 5 | Plate mass | $m = L \cdot B \cdot t \cdot \rho$ | kg | $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³ |
| 6 | Shrinkage allowance | $S_a = 0.001 \times L$ per weld pass | mm | Empirical allowance for weld shrinkage |
| 7 | Roll pressure (approx.) | $P \approx \dfrac{\sigma_y \cdot L \cdot t^{2}}{2R}$ | N | For pyramid rolling estimate |
| 8 | Bevel cross-section area | $A_{bevel} = t^{2}\,\tan(\alpha/2) - G\cdot t$ | mm² | Used to estimate weld volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** The factor **k = 0.33** for the K-factor (neutral axis offset) for soft mild steel, and **k = 0.5** for harder high-tensile grades. Memorize this — it appears in almost every fabrication numerical.

### 2.5 Why Fabrication Engineering Matters in Real Shipyards

- A 1° angular error in a 20 m longitudinal translates to a **35 cm misalignment** at the far end.
- A 5 mm overshoot in plate curvature cannot be corrected after welding — the panel must be scrapped.
- Accurate fabrication directly determines **welding distortion**, **fair-line quality** of the hull, and ultimately **EEDI / EEXI compliance** through hydrodynamically smooth lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bend Allowance (BA)

The neutral axis of a plate during bending lies neither at the centre nor at the inner surface, but at a fraction **k·t** from the inner surface (where k is the K-factor).

**Step 1 — Length of neutral axis after bending:**

$$L_{neutral} = (R + k \cdot t)\;\theta$$

**Step 2 — Define Bend Allowance (the flat blank length required to produce the bent shape):**

$$BA \;\equiv\; L_{neutral}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$BA = (R + k \cdot t)\,\theta$$

For mild steel, **k ≈ 0.33**, so:

$$BA = \left(R + \tfrac{t}{3}\right)\,\theta$$

> This is the flat blank length the cutter must allocate on the original plate.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Edge-Strain Check Before Cold Rolling

**Problem (KTU typical 14-mark style):**
A shipyard intends to cold-roll a **mild-steel plate of thickness t = 18 mm** to a radius **R = 360 mm** along its transverse direction. Determine whether the operation is permissible under the IACS 5% cold-strain rule. If not, by how much must R be increased so the strain becomes exactly 4%?

**Step 1 — State the cold-strain rule:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{t}{R} \le 0.05$$

**Step 2 — Compute the actual strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{18}{360} = 0.05 = 5\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the 5% limit:**

$$\varepsilon = 5\% \quad \text{is exactly at the limit — borderline acceptable}$$

**Step 4 — Find R for exactly 4% strain:**

$$R_{new} = \frac{t}{0.04} = \frac{18}{0.04} = 450 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Increase in radius:**

$$\Delta R = 450 - 360 = 90 \text{ mm}$$

> **Answer:** The plate is *just* at the limit; safer practice is to raise R to **450 mm**, i.e. increase the roll radius by **90 mm**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Bend Allowance for an Angle Bracket

**Problem:** A 12 mm thick bracket is to be bent through **60°** around an inner bend radius of **80 mm**. Compute the required bend allowance for mild steel (k = 0.33).

**Step 1 — Convert angle to radians:**

$$\theta = 60° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.0472 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into bend-allowance formula:**

$$BA = \left(R + \tfrac{t}{3}\right)\theta$$

$$BA = \left(80 + \tfrac{12}{3}\right)\times 1.0472$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerically:**

$$BA = (80 + 4)\times 1.0472 = 84 \times 1.0472$$

$$BA \approx 87.96 \text{ mm}$$

> **Answer:** The flat blank must be cut approximately **88 mm** longer along the neutral axis to allow for the bend.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Forming Methods

| Method | Temperature | Min. Radius (vs t) | Accuracy | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pyramid Roll (cold) | Ambient | 20 – 50 t | High (±1 mm/m) | Shell, deck plating |
| Press Brake | Ambient | 2 – 4 t | Very high | Stiffeners, brackets |
| Line Heating | ~600 – 750 °C | < 10 t possible | Medium | Double-curvature hull plates |
| Hot Forming | > 900 °C | 1 – 2 t | Lower | Heavy forgings |
| Flame Bending | Localized heating | 10 – 30 t | Operator-dependent | Repairs |

---

### 3.5 Tolerances (KTU 2024 / IACS Common Structural Rules — Typical)

$$\Delta L_{plate} = \pm \dfrac{L}{1000} \text{ mm}, \quad L \le 10 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta R_{curved\;plate} = \pm 0.001\,R \text{ or } \pm 5 \text{ mm (whichever is greater)}$$

$$\alpha_{bevel} \pm 5°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These tolerances are the **valuation checklist** markers — examiners award full marks only when the student states the *formula* **and** the *allowable tolerance range*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow: Fabrication Workflow in a Modern Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Steel Stockyard] --> B[Receiving & MIS Logging]
    B --> C[Straightening Cold or Hot]
    C --> D[Surface Shot Blasting Sa 2.5]
    D --> E[Marking CNC Templates]
    E --> F[Cutting Plasma Oxy-fuel Laser]
    F --> G[Edge Preparation Beveling]
    G --> H{Rolling Required}
    H -- Yes --> I[Cold or Hot Forming]
    H -- No --> J[Stiffener Pre-fab]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Dimensional QC Check]
    K --> L[Transfer to Panel Line]
    L --> M[Sub Assembly Welding]
    M --> N[Block Erection on Berth]
```

---

### 4.2 Schematic: Three-Roll Pyramid Bending Machine (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PyramidRoll [Three Roll Pyramid Bending]
        R1[Bottom Rear Roll Fixed] --- R2[Top Roll Adjustable]
        R2 --- R3[Bottom Front Roll Adjustable]
        P[Plate Infeed] --> R1
        R1 --> R2
        R2 --> R3
        R3 --> O[Curved Plate Outlet]
    end
    HY[Hydraulic Actuator] -. Adjusts gap .-> R2
    HY -. Adjusts gap .-> R3
    CTRL[Operator Console] --> HY
    CTRL --> S[Encoder Sensors]
    S --> CTRL
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Typical Edge Bevel Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Square Butt] --> B[Single V Groove 50 to 60 deg]
    B --> C[Double V Groove for t greater 20 mm]
    C --> D[J Groove Saves Weld Metal]
    D --> E[K Groove Thick Plates]
    E --> F[X Groove Heavy Sections]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The exact included angle for a Single-V groove in shipbuilding is normally **50° – 60°**; a wider angle (≥ 60°) is preferred when **GMAW / FCAW** robots are used because it improves side-wall fusion.

---

### 4.4 Schematic: Plate Nesting & Cutting Plan (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PL[Parent Plate 12 m x 3 m] --> N1[Nest Part A Shell Plate]
    PL --> N2[Nest Part B Deck Plate]
    PL --> N3[Nest Part C Longitudinal]
    PL --> N4[Nest Part D Bracket]
    N1 --> CUT[CNC Plasma Cut]
    N2 --> CUT
    N3 --> CUT
    N4 --> CUT
    CUT --> Q[Quality Check Dimensions]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Fabrication of Component Parts" as applied in shipbuilding. List any four operations involved.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for def + 0.5 × 4 for ops):**

Fabrication of component parts is the shop-floor stage where raw shipbuilding steel (plates, sections, bars) is processed into accurately shaped, dimensioned, and edge-prepared members ready for assembly and welding. The four principal operations are:
1. **Straightening** of plates/sections,
2. **Cutting and marking** to required shapes,
3. **Forming (rolling/bending)** to curved or angled profiles, and
4. **Edge preparation (beveling)** for full-penetration welds.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is edge preparation essential before butt-welding two ship plates? Name any two standard bevel profiles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 + 1 + 0.5 × 2):**

Edge preparation ensures that the welding arc and molten filler metal can penetrate **fully to the root** of the joint, producing a **100% joint efficiency** as required by classification societies. Without it, lack of penetration and slag inclusions form defects that drastically reduce fatigue life.

Standard bevel profiles:
1. **Single-V groove** (50°–60° included angle — most common).
2. **Double-V groove** (used for plates thicker than 20 mm to reduce total weld volume).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write the definition of *fabrication* but forget to mention the **dimensional-tolerance link to IACS CSR rules**. Marks are specifically reserved for naming the **standard, the tolerance value, and the inspection method**. Skipping any of these three forfeits up to 3 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Forming and Bending*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **three-roll pyramid plate-bending machine**. State the relationship between roll displacement and bend radius. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 16 mm thick mild-steel plate (k = 0.33) is to be cold-rolled to an inner radius of 320 mm through an angle of 90°. Determine (i) the bend allowance and (ii) whether the operation complies with the IACS 5% cold-strain rule. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks**

A three-roll pyramid machine has **two bottom rolls** (fixed rear + adjustable front) and **one top roll** (vertically adjustable). The plate is inserted between the rolls and the top roll is lowered, while the bottom-front roll is raised, generating a **transverse bending moment**.

The bending curvature is set by adjusting the **relative displacement (h)** between the rolls:

$$R = \dfrac{L_{roll}^{2}}{2h} \quad \text{(approximate for small h)}$$

where $L_{roll}$ is the centre-to-centre distance between the lower rolls. *Thus, the bend radius R is inversely proportional to the roll displacement h.* Hydraulic actuators drive the rolls; digital encoders feed back the curvature.

*(Marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for principle, 2 for R-h relationship, 1 for finishing sentence.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Convert angle to radians:**

$$\theta = 90° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.5708 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Bend allowance:**

$$BA = \left(R + \tfrac{t}{3}\right)\theta = \left(320 + \tfrac{16}{3}\right) \times 1.5708$$

$$BA = (320 + 5.33) \times 1.5708 = 325.33 \times 1.5708$$

$$BA \approx 511.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Cold-strain check:**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{t}{R} = \dfrac{16}{320} = 0.05 = 5\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare with IACS limit:**

$$\varepsilon = 5\% \le 5\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{Borderline acceptable, but safer practice is hot forming.}$$

> **Answer:** Bend allowance ≈ **511 mm**; strain exactly **5%**, so the operation is at the IACS permissible limit.

*(Marks: 1 for θ, 2 for BA calculation, 2 for strain calculation, 1 for the comparison + conclusion, 1 for clean presentation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Edge Preparation and Tolerances*

**(a)** Explain with sketches the different types of **edge bevels** used in shipbuilding. State the criteria for selecting between single-V and double-V grooves. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A shell plate of dimensions **12 m × 3 m × 20 mm** is to be cut from Grade A mild-steel stock. Compute (i) the mass of the plate ($\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³) and (ii) the total weld shrinkage allowance if the plate will receive **two parallel butt welds** (assume shrinkage factor 1 mm/m of weld). *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks**

The principal bevels are:

1. **Square butt** — no bevel; used only for $t \le 6$ mm.
2. **Single-V groove** — most common; included angle 50°–60°, root gap 2–3 mm; preferred for plates 6–20 mm.
3. **Double-V groove** — symmetrical V from both sides; halves the total weld volume; used for $t > 20$ mm where distortion control is critical.
4. **J-groove** — single-bevel J-shape; economizes weld metal; used in thick plate.
5. **K-groove** — combination of single-V and J; for very thick sections.
6. **X-groove** — symmetrical double-bevel; for heavy castings/weldments.

**Selection criterion:** Use **single-V** when plate thickness $\le 20$ mm and back-gouging access is available; switch to **double-V** when $t > 20$ mm to reduce angular distortion and total weld metal deposition.

*(Marks: 3 for listing + sketches, 2 for selection logic, 1 for criteria, 1 for neatness.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Plate volume:**

$$V = L \times B \times t = 12 \times 3 \times 0.020 = 0.72 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Plate mass:**

$$m = V \cdot \rho = 0.72 \times 7850$$

$$m = 5652 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Weld lengths (two parallel butt welds, full length of plate):**

$$L_{weld} = 2 \times 12 = 24 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Shrinkage allowance (1 mm/m of weld):**

$$S_a = 1 \text{ mm/m} \times 24 \text{ m} = 24 \text{ mm}$$

> **Answer:** Plate mass = **5652 kg**; total shrinkage allowance = **24 mm** to be compensated in the cut plan.

*(Marks: 1 each for V, m, weld lengths, shrinkage — plus 2 for final answer + conclusion, 1 for presentation.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12 Golden Points You MUST Memorize for Module 2:**

- ⭐ **Fabrication** = the cut → form → bevel workflow *before* assembly.
- ⭐ Standard shipbuilding steel grades: **A, B, D, E** (mild) and **AH32, DH36, EH36** (HT).
- ⭐ $\rho_{steel}$ for mass calc = **7850 kg/m³**.
- ⭐ Three-roll pyramid bending: **R ∝ 1/h** (radius ∝ inverse of roll gap).
- ⭐ **Bend Allowance:** $BA = (R + k\cdot t)\,\theta$, with **k = 0.33** (mild) or **0.5** (HT).
- ⭐ IACS cold-strain limit: **ε = t/R ≤ 5%** for transverse, **≤ 2%** longitudinal.
- ⭐ Standard Single-V bevel angle: **50° – 60°**, root gap **2–3 mm**.
- ⭐ Double-V bevel mandatory when **t > 20 mm**.
- ⭐ Plate length tolerance: **± L/1000 mm** (max 10 m).
- ⭐ Shrinkage allowance empirical factor: **~1 mm per metre of weld**.
- ⭐ Line heating used for **double-curvature plates**; cold rolling for **single-curvature**.
- ⭐ All fabricated dimensions must conform to **IACS CSR tolerance table** before release to the panel line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Month Strategy Tip:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, Module 2 frequently appears as a **14-mark Part B question on either (a) rolling/bending calculations or (b) edge-preparation classification.** Practise *both* numerical problem patterns above; one of them will appear in your paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cutting Processes and Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cutting Processes and Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cutting Processes** in ship construction refer to the controlled separation of metal plates, sections, and profiles into required geometric shapes and sizes using thermal, mechanical, or chemical energy sources. These processes form the **first major fabrication stage** in a shipyard, directly influencing dimensional accuracy, material utilization, and downstream welding productivity.

In the context of the **2024 KTU syllabus** for *OENST724 - Basics of Ship Construction*, cutting is classified under **Module 2: Shipyard Fabrication Processes**, and is considered a prerequisite operation that determines the **fit-up tolerance**, **weld volume**, and ultimately the **structural integrity** of the hull.

## 1.2 Classification of Cutting Processes

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Exam Favourite**: Ship construction cutting processes are broadly categorized as **Thermal Cutting** and **Mechanical Cutting**.

| Category | Sub-Process | Energy Source | Typical Shipyard Use |
|----------|-------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Thermal | Oxy-Fuel Cutting (OFC) | Chemical + Combustion | Mild steel plates ≥ 6 mm |
| Thermal | Plasma Arc Cutting | Electrical + Gas | Stainless steel, aluminium |
| Thermal | Laser Cutting | Coherent Light | Thin plates, intricate profiles |
| Thermal | Water Jet Cutting | High-pressure water + abrasive | Non-thermal-sensitive alloys |
| Mechanical | Shearing | Mechanical force | Plates ≤ 25 mm straight cuts |
| Mechanical | Sawing | Rotary blade | Sections, bars, pipes |
| Mechanical | Nibbling | Punching action | Openings, light plates |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Explanation)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Cutting a Cake"**: Imagine you want to shape a cake into the hull profile of a ship. You could:
> 1. **Slice with a knife** (mechanical/shearing) — works on soft, thin layers, leaves a clean edge.
> 2. **Burn through with a hot wire** (oxy-fuel/plasma) — works on thick, hard material, leaves a heat-affected zone (HAZ).
> 3. **Melt with a focused sun-ray** (laser) — precise but slow on thick material.
>
> A shipyard engineer chooses the "knife" based on **plate thickness, material type, edge quality requirement, and production volume** — exactly like choosing the right cake-cutting tool!

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Cutting

The following **standard shipyard metrics** are governed by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS):

- **Kerf Width** (typical value: **1.5 – 5 mm** depending on process)
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** width
- **Cutting Speed (mm/min)** = function of thickness
- **Edge Squareness (degrees)** = should be ≤ **1°** deviation for Class approval

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **IACS UR Z17** and **ISO 9013**, the permissible **cut surface quality** is graded from **Quality Level 1 (roughest)** to **Quality Level 4 (finest)** — every KTU question on cutting almost always references this grading implicitly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Oxy-Fuel Cutting (OFC) — Step-by-Step Logic

OFC is the **workhorse process** in any steel shipyard. It works on a simple three-stage principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why OFC works only on steel?** Because iron oxide (FeO) melts at a temperature **lower** than pure iron, allowing continuous oxidation to "burn" through the plate.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Pre-heat Stage**: The steel plate is pre-heated to its **ignition temperature (~870 °C)** using an oxy-acetylene or oxy-propane flame.
2. **Oxidation Stage**: A high-purity **cutting oxygen jet** is introduced. Iron reacts exothermically:
   $$\text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{FeO} + \text{Heat}$$
3. **Blow-out Stage**: The molten iron oxide (FeO slag) is blown away by the kinetic energy of the oxygen jet, producing a clean kerf.

## 2.2 Plasma Arc Cutting — Operational Mechanism

A **constricted electric arc** ionizes a gas (air, N₂, Ar-H₂) into a **plasma state** at temperatures exceeding **20,000 °C**. The high-velocity plasma jet melts and ejects the metal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Plasma cutting is **not dependent on oxidation**, so it cuts **stainless steel, aluminium, and copper alloys** — materials where OFC fails.

## 2.3 Mechanical Cutting — Shearing Mechanics

Shearing works on the **shear failure principle** where two opposing blades (upper and lower) apply a force exceeding the **ultimate shear strength** of the plate. The cut occurs along the **shear plane** between the blades.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the table below** — these equations appear in nearly every KTU numerical on cutting.

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Cutting Speed (OFC) | $v_c = \dfrac{k}{t}$ | mm/min | $k$ = constant (≈ 250 for mild steel), $t$ = thickness (mm) |
| Heat Input per unit length | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $v$ = travel speed |
| Oxygen Consumption (OFC) | $Q_{O_2} = c \cdot t \cdot v_c$ | m³/hr | $c$ = specific oxygen factor |
| Kerf Width (OFC) | $K_w \approx 1.5 + 0.02 \cdot t$ | mm | Empirical relation |
| Shear Force | $F = \tau_u \cdot A = \tau_u \cdot L \cdot t$ | N | $\tau_u$ = ultimate shear stress |
| Laser Cutting Power | $P = \dfrac{E}{t \cdot v_c}$ | kW | $E$ = specific energy (J/mm³) |
| Plate Utilization | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful area}}{\text{Total plate area}} \times 100$ | % | Aims for ≥ **85 %** in nesting |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a real **Kochi Shipyard** or **Cochin Shipyard** scenario, the choice between OFC and plasma cutting has direct cost implications:

- **OFC**: ₹ 2 – 4 per metre cut (low cost, slow on stainless steel)
- **Plasma**: ₹ 8 – 15 per metre cut (versatile, higher precision)
- **Laser**: ₹ 20 – 40 per metre cut (premium finish, high capex)

For a **Panamax bulk carrier** with ~ **5,000 tonnes of steel** requiring ~ **25 km of cutting**, the process selection can swing project economics by **₹ 10–20 lakhs**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cutting Speed as a Function of Plate Thickness (OFC)

We will derive the **inverse relationship** between cutting speed and plate thickness from first principles using heat-balance logic.

### Step 1 — Define the heat required to cut a unit length

Let $t$ = plate thickness (mm) and $v_c$ = cutting speed (mm/min). Consider a strip of length $L = v_c$ (i.e., material cut in 1 minute).

The volume of metal removed per minute:
$$V_m = v_c \cdot t \cdot K_w$$

where $K_w$ is the kerf width.

### Step 2 — Energy needed to raise metal to ignition + melt + slag

The total specific energy required per unit volume is $E_s$ (J/mm³), which is an experimentally determined constant for the material. So total heat required per minute:
$$Q_{req} = E_s \cdot V_m = E_s \cdot v_c \cdot t \cdot K_w$$

### Step 3 — Heat supplied by the oxy-fuel flame

Let $P_{in}$ be the effective heat input rate (J/min) from the flame acting on the cut zone. For a steady-state cut:
$$P_{in} = Q_{req}$$

Substituting:
$$P_{in} = E_s \cdot v_c \cdot t \cdot K_w$$

### Step 4 — Solve for cutting speed

Rearranging the equation to isolate $v_c$:
$$v_c = \dfrac{P_{in}}{E_s \cdot t \cdot K_w}$$

### Step 5 — Recognize the inverse-thickness form

Since $P_{in}$, $E_s$, and $K_w$ are approximately constant for a given nozzle and material, we can define a **constant**:
$$k = \dfrac{P_{in}}{E_s \cdot K_w}$$

This reduces the equation to the standard shipyard form:
$$v_c = \dfrac{k}{t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning**: Doubling the plate thickness **halves** the cutting speed. This is why cutting **50 mm plate** is significantly slower than **25 mm plate** — a concept frequently tested in KTU numericals.

## 3.2 Derivation: Oxygen Consumption for OFC

The oxygen jet must perform **three jobs**: pre-heat the kerf, oxidize the iron, and eject the slag.

### Step 1 — Empirical volumetric oxygen per metre cut

Define $q_o$ = oxygen volume (m³) required to cut 1 metre of plate of thickness $t$ (mm). Empirically:
$$q_o = \alpha + \beta \cdot t$$

where $\alpha$ = base oxygen (for ignition) and $\beta$ = slope constant.

### Step 2 — Oxygen consumption per hour

For cutting speed $v_c$ (mm/min) and length per hour $= 60 \cdot v_c$ (mm/hr) = $0.06 \cdot v_c$ (m/hr):

$$Q_{O_2} = q_o \cdot (0.06 \cdot v_c)$$

Substituting:
$$Q_{O_2} = (\alpha + \beta \cdot t) \cdot 0.06 \cdot v_c$$

Using $v_c = k / t$:
$$Q_{O_2} = 0.06 \cdot k \cdot (\alpha + \beta \cdot t) \cdot \dfrac{1}{t}$$

### Step 3 — Final simplified form

$$\boxed{Q_{O_2} = 0.06 \cdot k \cdot \left(\dfrac{\alpha}{t} + \beta\right)}$$

This equation reveals that **oxygen consumption per hour increases as thickness decreases** — because more metres must be cut to compensate for the lower kerf volume.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection for Shipbuilding Materials

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix in any KTU "Compare and Contrast" question.**

| Material | Plate Thickness | Recommended Process | Reason |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------|--------|
| Mild Steel (MS) | ≤ 6 mm | Shearing / Plasma | Fast, no heat distortion |
| Mild Steel (MS) | 6 – 100 mm | **OFC** | Economical, reliable |
| Mild Steel (MS) | > 100 mm | OFC (multiple passes) | Only feasible thermal method |
| Stainless Steel | Any | Plasma / Laser | OFC fails (Cr-oxide blocks oxidation) |
| Aluminium | Any | Plasma | OFC fails (Al-oxide refractory) |
| Copper / Brass | Any | Plasma (high current) | High thermal conductivity defeats OFC |
| High-tensile steel (AH36, DH36) | 10 – 50 mm | Plasma (precision) | Low HAZ preserves mechanical properties |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU Style)

**Problem**: Calculate the cutting speed and oxygen consumption for a **20 mm thick mild steel plate** using OFC. Given: $k = 300$ (mm²/min), $\alpha = 0.05$ m³/m, $\beta = 0.012$ m³/m·mm.

**Solution:**

Cutting speed:
$$v_c = \dfrac{k}{t} = \dfrac{300}{20} = 15 \text{ mm/min}$$

Oxygen consumption per metre:
$$q_o = \alpha + \beta \cdot t = 0.05 + 0.012 \cdot 20 = 0.05 + 0.24 = 0.29 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$$

Oxygen per hour:
$$Q_{O_2} = 0.06 \cdot v_c \cdot q_o = 0.06 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.29 = 0.261 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer**: $v_c = 15$ mm/min, $Q_{O_2} = 0.261$ m³/hr. Always quote units in KTU numericals!

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Oxy-Fuel Cutting Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steel Plate Input] --> B[Surface Cleaning and Marking]
    B --> C[Pre-heat Flame Application]
    C --> D{Temperature Reached 870 C?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Cutting Oxygen Jet Activated]
    E --> F[Iron Oxidation Reaction Fe plus O2]
    F --> G[Slag Formation and Ejection]
    G --> H[Kerf Produced]
    H --> I[Quality Inspection per ISO 9013]
    I --> J{Quality Acceptable?}
    J -- No --> K[Re-cut or Grind]
    J -- Yes --> L[Plate Ready for Next Fabrication Stage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above **functional flow** mirrors the exact sequence followed on a CNC flame-cutting table in any Indian shipyard (e.g., HSL Vishakapatnam, GRSE Kolkata).

## 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram: Thermal Cutting Process Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Material and Thickness Decision] --> N{Material Type}
    N -- Mild Steel --> O{Thickness}
    N -- Stainless Steel --> P[Plasma Arc Cutting]
    N -- Aluminium --> P
    N -- Thin Plate Under 6 mm --> Q[Shearing]

    O -- 6 to 100 mm --> R[OFC Selected]
    O -- Above 100 mm --> S[OFC Multi Pass]
    O -- Precision Required --> T[Laser Cutting]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Shipyard Cutting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Steel Stock Yard] --> B1[Nesting and CAD Marking]
    B1 --> C1[Plate Edge Preparation]
    C1 --> D1[Cutting Process Selection]
    D1 --> E1[CNC Flame or Plasma Cutting]
    E1 --> F1[Edge Grinding and Beveling]
    F1 --> G1[Transfer to Assembly Bay]

    style A1 fill:#f9e79f
    style G1 fill:#a9dfbf
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Components of an Oxy-Fuel Torch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Torch Body] --> B0[Pre-heat Oxygen Inlet]
    A0 --> C0[Fuel Gas Inlet Acetylene or Propane]
    A0 --> D0[Cutting Oxygen Lever and Nozzle]
    A0 --> E0[Mixing Chamber]
    A0 --> F0[Tip Nozzle]
    E0 --> F0
    B0 --> E0
    C0 --> E0
    D0 --> F0
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip**: When asked to "draw and label an OFC torch" in KTU, replace the mermaid block with a hand-drawn schematic on your answer sheet showing the **pre-heat ring** and **central cutting jet** — both must be visible for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define oxy-fuel cutting. State two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

Oxy-fuel cutting (OFC) is a thermal cutting process in which a high-purity oxygen jet is used to oxidize and remove metal by means of an exothermic chemical reaction, after the base metal has been pre-heated to its ignition temperature (~870 °C for steel).

**Advantages:**
1. Low equipment cost and high reliability for thick mild-steel plates.
2. Suitable for plates up to **300 mm** thickness using multi-pass techniques.

**Limitation:**
1. Cannot cut **stainless steel, cast iron, or non-ferrous metals** due to refractory oxide formation.

*(3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is kerf? How is it compensated in shipyard nesting?**

**Model Answer:**

Kerf is the **width of metal removed** during the cutting process, typically **1.5 – 5 mm** for OFC. It is compensated in **nesting software** (e.g., ShipConstructor, AutoNEST) by offsetting the part profile inward by half the kerf width on each side, ensuring that the **cut part achieves the nominal dimension**.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: Students commonly lose marks by (i) skipping neat diagrams, (ii) not writing the governing chemical reaction in OFC, (iii) forgetting units in numericals, and (iv) confusing plasma with laser cutting — always specify the **energy source**!

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **oxy-fuel cutting** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **chemistry** involved and explain why OFC is restricted to mild steel. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **15 mm thick mild steel plate** is to be cut at a speed of **20 mm/min** using OFC. Calculate the **oxygen consumption per hour**, given that oxygen required per metre of cut is $q_o = 0.08 + 0.015 \cdot t$ m³/m. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a):

**Working Principle** (refer to SECTION_2.1 for full theory):

1. **Pre-heating**: Mild steel plate is heated to **870 °C** using a pre-heat flame (oxy-acetylene or oxy-propane).
2. **Cutting oxygen jet**: High-purity oxygen is injected through a central nozzle.
3. **Oxidation reaction**:
$$\text{Fe} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{FeO} + \text{Heat } (\approx 250 \text{ kJ/mol})$$
4. **Slag ejection**: Molten FeO is blown out by the kinetic energy of the oxygen jet.
5. **Result**: A clean kerf is produced and the plate is severed.

**Why restricted to mild steel?**

For OFC to work, the **metal oxide must melt at a temperature lower than the parent metal** and must be **fluid enough to be ejected**. In mild steel, FeO melts at ~**1370 °C**, lower than iron (~1538 °C), enabling continuous cutting. In **stainless steel**, chromium forms **Cr₂O₃** which melts at ~**2300 °C** (refractory) and blocks further oxidation — OFC fails. Similarly, **aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃)** is highly refractory. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (b):

Given: $t = 15$ mm, $v_c = 20$ mm/min.

**Step 1**: Compute $q_o$:
$$q_o = 0.08 + 0.015 \cdot 15 = 0.08 + 0.225 = 0.305 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$$

**Step 2**: Length cut per hour:
$$L_{hr} = v_c \cdot 60 = 20 \cdot 60 = 1200 \text{ mm/hr} = 1.2 \text{ m/hr}$$

**Step 3**: Oxygen consumption per hour:
$$Q_{O_2} = q_o \cdot L_{hr} = 0.305 \cdot 1.2 = 0.366 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{O_2} = 0.366 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

*(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare **oxy-fuel cutting, plasma arc cutting, and laser cutting** with respect to energy source, applicable materials, edge quality, and plate thickness range. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A shipyard must cut **500 plates of 25 mm thick mild steel**, each measuring **2 m × 1.5 m**. Calculate the **total oxygen consumption** if the cutting speed is $v_c = k/t$ where $k = 350$ mm²/min, and oxygen per metre is $q_o = 0.05 + 0.018 \cdot t$ m³/m. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a):

| Feature | Oxy-Fuel Cutting | Plasma Arc Cutting | Laser Cutting |
|---------|------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Energy Source | Chemical oxidation of Fe | Constricted electric arc | Coherent light beam |
| Temperature | ~3,200 °C | ~20,000 °C | ~10,000 °C (surface) |
| Material | Mild steel only | All metals (SS, Al, Cu) | Thin sheets, all metals |
| Thickness Range | 6 – 300 mm | 0.5 – 50 mm | 0.1 – 25 mm |
| Edge Quality | ISO 9013 Level 2–3 | ISO 9013 Level 3–4 | ISO 9013 Level 4 |
| HAZ | Large | Moderate | Smallest |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

*(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1**: Cutting speed:
$$v_c = \dfrac{350}{25} = 14 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 2**: Oxygen per metre:
$$q_o = 0.05 + 0.018 \cdot 25 = 0.05 + 0.45 = 0.50 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$$

**Step 3**: Length cut per hour:
$$L_{hr} = 14 \cdot 60 = 840 \text{ mm/hr} = 0.84 \text{ m/hr}$$

**Step 4**: Perimeter of one plate:
$$P = 2 \cdot (2 + 1.5) = 7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5**: Total perimeter for 500 plates:
$$P_{total} = 500 \cdot 7 = 3500 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6**: Total oxygen consumption:
$$Q_{total} = q_o \cdot P_{total} = 0.50 \cdot 3500 = 1750 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 7**: Add hourly oxygen rate for context:
$$Q_{hr} = q_o \cdot 0.84 = 0.50 \cdot 0.84 = 0.42 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 1750 \text{ m}^3 \text{ of oxygen}}$$

*(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam!**

- **OFC requires three conditions**: (i) ignition temperature < melting point of oxide, (ii) oxide melting point < parent metal, (iii) exothermic oxidation reaction.
- **Cutting speed inversely proportional to thickness**: $v_c = k/t$.
- **Plasma cutting** uses ionized gas at ~**20,000 °C** — works on all conductive metals.
- **Laser cutting** offers the **smallest HAZ** and **highest precision** but limited thickness (< 25 mm).
- **Kerf width** must be compensated during **CAD nesting**.
- **Quality grading**: ISO 9013 — Levels **1 to 4** (4 = finest).
- **Plate utilization (nesting efficiency)** should target **≥ 85 %** for economical shipbuilding.
- **Standard HAZ tolerance** as per IACS UR Z17 for critical hull cuts.
- **OFC fails** on stainless steel, cast iron, copper, aluminium — use **plasma or laser** instead.
- **Numerical units** — always carry units through every step in KTU numericals.
- **Mnemonic for cutting processes**: **"OPLW-SN"** — Oxy-fuel, Plasma, Laser, Water-jet, Shearing, Nibbling.

---

> [!TIP]
> **End of Module 2 Notes**. Cross-link these notes with Module 3 (Welding & Fabrication) — the kerf and HAZ knowledge built here directly influences weld volume calculations later. All the best for your KTU ESE!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Description of the Various Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Description of the Various Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Naval Architecture and Ship Construction**, the term **"Various Machines"** refers to the comprehensive suite of mechanical, electrical, and thermal prime movers and auxiliary systems installed on board a vessel. These machines are responsible for **propulsion, power generation, cargo handling, navigation support, and habitability services**. The principal classification, as per KTU 2024 syllabus, divides shipboard machinery into:

1. **Main Propulsion Machinery** – Prime movers driving the propeller.
2. **Auxiliary Machinery** – Supporting systems for power generation, fluid handling, HVAC, and safety.
3. **Boilers and Steam Generators** – Energy conversion equipment.
4. **Transmission Machinery** – Shafting, reduction gears, couplings, and bearings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The 2024 scheme emphasizes a *general descriptive understanding* of these machines — their working principle, layout, application in ships, and relative advantages. Numerical deep-dives are secondary; descriptive clarity is primary for scoring marks.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body:
- The **main engine** is the **heart**, pumping energy (in the form of rotation) throughout the ship.
- The **boilers/fuel system** is the **digestive system**, converting raw fuel into usable energy.
- The **auxiliary pumps and generators** are the **kidneys and lungs**, filtering, circulating, and refreshing vital fluids and air.
- The **shaft and propeller** are the **legs**, providing actual locomotion through water.

Just as a human body needs all organs to function in harmony, a ship requires careful integration of all these machines to ensure **seaworthiness, efficiency, and safety**. The Engine Room of a modern merchant vessel is therefore often called the **"Heart of the Ship."**

### Key Standard Metrics

- **Shaft Power (P):** Measured in **kW** or **MW**.
- **Service Speed:** Typical merchant ships operate at **15–25 knots**.
- **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** Modern marine diesels ≈ **170 g/kWh**.
- **Propulsive Efficiency (ηP):** Usually **0.60 – 0.70** for single-screw ships.
- **Overall Thermal Efficiency:** Diesel plant ≈ **0.50**, Steam plant ≈ **0.30**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Remember:**
> - **BHP (Brake Horse Power)** – Power developed by the engine at the crankshaft.
> - **SHP (Shaft Horse Power)** – Power delivered to the propeller shaft.
> - **EHP (Effective Horse Power)** – Power actually required to tow the hull through water.
> - **DHP (Delivered Horse Power)** – Power reaching the propeller after transmission losses.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Marine Prime Movers

The selection of a main propulsion system depends on **vessel type, size, speed, range, and operational economics**. The major categories are:

#### A. Internal Combustion (Diesel) Engines
- **Working Principle:** Combustion of fuel inside a closed cylinder converts chemical energy to mechanical rotation via reciprocating motion.
- **Types Used in Ships:**
  - **Slow-speed (2-stroke) marine diesels** – 60–250 RPM, directly coupled to propeller.
  - **Medium-speed (4-stroke) diesels** – 350–1000 RPM, coupled via reduction gearbox.
  - **High-speed diesels** – Used in small craft, fast patrol boats.
- **Advantages:** Highest thermal efficiency, no separate boiler, quick start, low fuel cost (heavy fuel oil).
- **Disadvantages:** High initial cost, large L × B × H footprint for slow-speed units.
- **Application:** Almost all modern merchant vessels (tankers, bulk carriers, container ships).

#### B. Steam Turbines
- **Working Principle:** High-pressure steam expands over turbine blades, converting thermal energy into rotational energy.
- **System Components:** Oil-fired boilers → Superheater → HP Turbine → LP Turbine → Condenser → Feed pumps → Back to boiler.
- **Advantages:** Smooth, vibration-free operation; very high power output possible (used in aircraft carriers and large LNG carriers).
- **Disadvantages:** Low thermal efficiency (~30%), complex auxiliary systems, slow response.
- **Application:** Large warships, nuclear submarines, very large LNG carriers.

#### C. Gas Turbines
- **Working Principle:** Compressed air mixed with fuel burns in a combustion chamber; hot gases drive turbine blades.
- **Advantages:** Compact, lightweight, high power-to-weight ratio, rapid start-up.
- **Disadvantages:** High fuel consumption, sensitive to salt-air ingestion.
- **Application:** Naval frigates, destroyers, fast attack craft (CODOG, COGAG, CODLAG configurations).

#### D. Nuclear Propulsion
- **Working Principle:** Heat from nuclear fission in a reactor generates steam that drives turbines.
- **Advantages:** Virtually unlimited range, no air intake needed (submarines), high sustained power.
- **Disadvantages:** Very high capital cost, stringent safety regulations, radiation shielding.
- **Application:** Aircraft carriers, submarines, icebreakers.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "D-S-G-N":** **D**iesel, **S**team, **G**as, **N**uclear — the four prime mover families.

---

### 2.2 Auxiliary Machinery Systems

Auxiliary machinery, although secondary in propulsion, is **critical for safety and habitability**. Key systems include:

| Auxiliary System | Function | Typical Capacity |
|---|---|---|
| **Bilge Pumps** | Remove accumulated water from hull bottom | 100–400 m³/hr |
| **Ballast Pumps** | Fill/empty ballast tanks for stability | 500–2500 m³/hr |
| **Fire Pumps** | Supply seawater for fire-fighting | 50–150 m³/hr at 10 bar |
| **Cooling Pumps** | Circulate seawater/freshwater for heat exchangers | Variable |
| **Air Compressors** | Generate starting air (30 bar) and control air (7 bar) | 30–100 m³/hr |
| **Diesel Generators** | Electrical power for ship's services | 0.5–5 MW per set |
| **Boilers (Auxiliary)** | Provide steam for heating, fuel oil heating, laundry | 1–10 tonnes/hr |
| **Steering Gear** | Turn rudder for ship course control | Up to 1000 kNm torque |
| **Bow Thrusters** | Lateral propulsion at low speeds | 1–3 MW |

---

### 2.3 Reduction Gears and Shafting

Since most modern high-speed diesel engines rotate at **500–1000 RPM** while propellers prefer **80–200 RPM**, **reduction gears** are mandatory. The most common is the **double-reduction helical gearbox**.

**Shafting layout (typical):**
> Engine → Flexible Coupling → Reduction Gear → Thrust Bearing → Intermediate Shaft → Tail Shaft → Propeller

The **thrust bearing** transmits the propeller's thrust force into the ship's hull structure.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE Module 2 questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shaft Power (P)} &= 2\pi N T / 60 \quad \text{[in Watts when N is RPM and T is N·m]} \\
\text{Effective Horse Power (EHP)} &= R_T \times V_S / 75 \quad \text{(in CGS, Cv)} \\
\text{Propulsive Coefficient (PC)} &= \text{EHP} / \text{SHP} \\
\text{Overall Efficiency} (\eta_O) &= \text{EHP} / \text{BHP} = \eta_H \times \eta_S \times \eta_R \times \eta_T \times \eta_G \\
\text{Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)} &= m_{\text{fuel}} / P_{\text{shaft}} \quad \text{[g/kWh]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = rotational speed (RPM)
- $T$ = torque (N·m)
- $R_T$ = total resistance (N)
- $V_S$ = ship speed (m/s)
- $\eta_H, \eta_S, \eta_R, \eta_T, \eta_G$ = hull, screw, relative rotative, transmission, gear efficiencies

**Typical Efficiencies Table:**

| Component | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Hull Efficiency | $\eta_H$ | 1.0 – 1.2 |
| Propeller (Screw) Efficiency | $\eta_S$ | 0.55 – 0.70 |
| Relative Rotative Efficiency | $\eta_R$ | 0.95 – 1.05 |
| Transmission Efficiency | $\eta_T$ | 0.95 – 0.99 |
| Gearbox Efficiency | $\eta_G$ | 0.95 – 0.98 |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
These formulas allow Naval Architects to size engines correctly. For example, a 300,000 DWT VLCC needs approximately **25–30 MW of Brake Power** at service speed. Over-design wastes fuel; under-design causes engine overload and failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shaft Power from Torque and RPM

The **shaft power** transmitted by a rotating shaft is the product of angular velocity and torque.

**Step 1 — Define Angular Velocity:**
Angular velocity $\omega$ is the rotational speed in radians per second.

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical Power Definition:**
Mechanical power is the rate of doing work. For a rotating shaft, work done per revolution equals torque × angular displacement ($2\pi$).

$$
W_{\text{per rev}} = T \times 2\pi
$$

**Step 3 — Power per Second:**
If the shaft rotates $N$ times per minute, then rotations per second are $N/60$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= W_{\text{per rev}} \times \text{revs/sec} \\
P &= (T \times 2\pi) \times \left(\frac{N}{60}\right) \\
P &= \frac{2\pi N T}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Unit Conversion:**
With $N$ in RPM and $T$ in N·m, $P$ comes out in **Watts (W)**.

$$
P \text{[W]} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{where } N \text{[RPM]}, T \text{[N·m]}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical Engineering Form:**
Dividing by 1000 gives kW; multiplying by 1.341 gives Horsepower (HP).

$$
P \text{[kW]} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** A 2-stroke marine diesel running at 90 RPM and developing 3.2 MN·m torque gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{2\pi \times 90 \times 3.2 \times 10^6}{60} \\
P &= \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 90 \times 3.2 \times 10^6}{60} \\
P &= 30.16 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \\
P &\approx 30.16 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the size of engines installed on large container ships (e.g., 14-cylinder MAN B&W 14G95ME-C).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Propulsive Coefficient Relationship

The **overall propulsive chain** maps fuel energy to useful hull motion:

$$
\text{Fuel Energy} \rightarrow \text{Engine BHP} \rightarrow \text{SHP} \rightarrow \text{DHP} \rightarrow \text{EHP} \rightarrow \text{Towing Power}
$$

Each transition has an associated efficiency:

$$
\text{EHP} = \text{BHP} \times \eta_G \times \eta_T \times \eta_R \times \eta_S \times \eta_H
$$

Where the individual terms are:
- $\eta_G$ = Reduction gear efficiency (mechanical losses in gearing)
- $\eta_T$ = Stern tube bearing friction efficiency
- $\eta_R$ = Rotative efficiency (accounts for non-uniform wake at propeller)
- $\eta_S$ = Propeller open-water efficiency
- $\eta_H$ = Hull efficiency (wake + thrust deduction combined)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the Chain:** Each "stage" represents an energy conversion. Efficiency values less than 1.0 mean losses; values greater than 1.0 (possible only for $\eta_H$) indicate the propeller recovers rotational energy in the wake field.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Prime Movers

| Parameter | Diesel Engine | Steam Turbine | Gas Turbine | Nuclear |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Efficiency | **45–55%** | 28–35% | 30–38% | 25–30% |
| Power-to-Weight Ratio | Moderate | Low | **Very High** | Moderate |
| Specific Fuel Consumption | **Low** | High | Highest | Negligible (Uranium) |
| Starting Time | Quick | Slow | Quick | Hours |
| Maintenance | Moderate | High | Moderate | Very High |
| Capital Cost | High | Moderate | Moderate | **Very High** |
| Noise & Vibration | Moderate | Low | High | Moderate |
| Typical Application | Merchant ships | Warships, LNG | Naval frigates | Carriers, submarines |
| Fuel Type | HFO, Diesel, LNG | Fuel Oil | Aviation Kerosene | Enriched Uranium |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question Pattern:** "Compare steam turbines and diesel engines as marine prime movers." Use the matrix above as your comparative backbone.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Marine Propulsion Power Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how energy flows from fuel bunkers to the propeller, including all conversion stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["FUEL OIL BUNKERS"] --> node2["FUEL TREATMENT\nAND PURIFIERS"]
    node2 --> node3["MAIN ENGINE\nDIESEL OR TURBINE"]
    node3 --> node4["REDUCTION GEARBOX"]
    node4 --> node5["THRUST BEARING"]
    node5 --> node6["INTERMEDIATE SHAFT"]
    node6 --> node7["TAIL SHAFT AND\nSTERN TUBE"]
    node7 --> node8["PROPELLER"]
    node3 --> nodeA["EXHAUST GAS\nAND HEAT"]
    node8 --> node9["WAKE AND THRUST\nFORCE ON HULL"]
    node9 --> node10["SHIP FORWARD MOTION"]
    node3 --> nodeB["GENERATORS\nELECTRICAL POWER"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["AUXILIARY PUMPS\nCOMPRESSORS\nBOILERS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["HVAC AND\nSHIP SERVICES"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **horizontal arrow chain** (node1 → node2 → ... → node10) represents the **propulsion energy chain**.
- The **downward branch** (node3 → nodeA) shows **wasted energy** as exhaust heat.
- The **downward branch** (node3 → nodeB → nodeC → nodeD) represents the **electrical and auxiliary power distribution**.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Arrangement of Auxiliary Machinery

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeSG["MAIN SWITCHBOARD ROOM"]
        nodeDG1["DIESEL GENERATOR 1"]
        nodeDG2["DIESEL GENERATOR 2"]
        nodeDG3["EMERGENCY GENERATOR"]
    end
    subgraph nodeER["ENGINE ROOM"]
        nodeP1["BILGE PUMP"]
        nodeP2["BALLAST PUMP"]
        nodeP3["COOLING PUMP"]
        nodeP4["FIRE PUMP"]
        nodeP5["AIR COMPRESSOR"]
        nodeP6["AUXILIARY BOILER"]
    end
    subgraph nodeDK["DECK MACHINERY"]
        nodeM1["STEERING GEAR"]
        nodeM2["BOW THRUSTER"]
        nodeM3["MOORING WINCH"]
    end
    nodeDG1 --> nodeBUS["MAIN BUS BAR"]
    nodeDG2 --> nodeBUS
    nodeDG3 --> nodeBUS
    nodeBUS --> nodeP1
    nodeBUS --> nodeP2
    nodeBUS --> nodeP3
    nodeBUS --> nodeM1
    nodeBUS --> nodeM2
    nodeBUS --> nodeM6["HVAC AND LIGHTING"]
```

**Diagram Legend:**
- The rectangular subgraphs represent **physical compartments** within the ship.
- Arrows denote **electrical power flow** (480V or 6.6kV AC distribution).
- The **emergency generator** is normally off and starts automatically on main power failure.

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Engine Room Cross-Section (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeUS["UPPER DECK"]
        nodeFL["FUNNEL AND UPTAKES"]
    end
    subgraph nodePL["PLATFORM DECK"]
        nodeME["MAIN ENGINE"]
        nodeRG["REDUCTION GEAR"]
        nodeGB["GENERATOR ENGINES"]
    end
    subgraph nodeLL["LOWER PLATFORM"]
        nodeSH["SHAFT AND THRUST BLOCK"]
        nodePUMPS["PUMPS AND AUXILIARIES"]
    end
    subgraph nodeBL["BILGE LEVEL"]
        nodeTS["TANK TOP AND BILGE"]
    end
    nodeME --> nodeRG
    nodeRG --> nodeSH
    nodeFL -.EXHAUST.-> nodeME
    nodeGB --> nodePUMPS
    nodePUMPS --> nodeTS
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks each — Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **Brake Horse Power (BHP)** and **Effective Horse Power (EHP)**. How are they related?

**Model Answer:**
- **BHP** is the power actually developed by the engine at its crankshaft, measured by a brake or dynamometer. It represents the total mechanical power output before transmission losses.
- **EHP** is the useful power required to tow the ship's hull through water at a given speed. It depends on the total resistance ($R_T$) and ship speed ($V_S$).

$$
\text{BHP} = \frac{EHP}{\eta_O} \quad ; \quad \text{EHP} = \frac{R_T \times V_S}{75} \text{ (metric HP)}
$$

The ratio of EHP to BHP is the **overall propulsive efficiency** $\eta_O$, which is typically **0.45 to 0.60** for single-screw merchant ships.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
List any **four types of auxiliary machinery** found in the engine room of a merchant ship.

**Model Answer (any four):**
1. **Bilge Pumps** – Remove water accumulation from hull bottom.
2. **Fire Pumps** – Supply seawater at high pressure for fire-fighting.
3. **Air Compressors** – Generate starting air (30 bar) and control air (7 bar).
4. **Diesel Generators** – Provide electrical power for ship's services.
5. **Cooling Fresh Water Pumps** – Circulate coolant through main engine heat exchangers.
6. **Steering Gear** – Operate the rudder for course control.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **general arrangement of main propulsion machinery** on board a modern merchant ship. Identify the major components and their functions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **steam turbines and diesel engines** as marine prime movers under the following heads: thermal efficiency, weight, starting time, maintenance, and application.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Main Propulsion Machinery Arrangement:**

A typical merchant ship propulsion system (single-screw, diesel-driven) consists of the following components in sequence:

1. **Fuel Oil System:** Heavy fuel oil is stored in bunker tanks, purified, heated to ~150°C, and pumped to the engine at ~6–10 bar pressure.
2. **Main Engine:** A 2-stroke, slow-speed marine diesel directly coupled to the propeller. Cylinder lubrication, cooling water, and scavenge air are provided through auxiliary systems.
3. **Shafting System:** Includes the thrust bearing (transmits propeller thrust to hull), intermediate shaft, and tail shaft passing through the stern tube.
4. **Propeller:** Typically a 4–5 bladed fixed-pitch propeller, mounted on the tail shaft inside the aperture.

> *Block diagram reference:* See Section 4.1 of these notes.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Diesel Engine | Steam Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal Efficiency | **45–55%** | 28–35% |
| Weight | Moderate | High (boilers add weight) |
| Starting Time | Quick (seconds) | Slow (minutes to hours) |
| Maintenance | Moderate | High (boiler tubes, turbines) |
| Application | Merchant ships | Warships, very large LNG |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **working principle of a reduction gearbox** in a marine propulsion system. Why is it necessary in medium-speed diesel installations?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **function and types of pumps** used in the engine room. Give at least three examples with their specific applications.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Reduction Gearbox:**

A reduction gearbox is a mechanical assembly of helical gears that **reduces the high rotational speed of a medium-speed diesel engine** (typically 500–1000 RPM) to a propeller-friendly **80–120 RPM**. It also reverses the direction of rotation (ahead/astern).

- **Types:** Single-reduction (one stage), double-reduction (two stages).
- **Necessity:**
  1. Diesel engines are most efficient at 400–1000 RPM; propellers need 80–200 RPM.
  2. Direct drive would cause cavitation and inefficient thrust generation.
  3. Reversal without gearbox would require a bulky marine gearbox with separate ahead/astern pinions.

**(b) Pumps in Engine Room:**

1. **Centrifugal Pumps:** Used for seawater cooling, ballast, and bilge. Operate on rotational impeller principle; high flow, low pressure.
2. **Positive Displacement Pumps (Reciprocating/Rotary):** Used for fuel oil and lubrication oil transfer; provide high pressure at low flow.
3. **Gear Pumps:** Used for lube oil circulation; fixed displacement and reliable flow rate.

**Applications:**
- **Bilge pump:** Removes water from machinery spaces.
- **Ballast pump:** Adjusts ship trim by filling/emptying ballast tanks.
- **Fire pump:** Provides high-pressure seawater to fire main.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 2 (Various Machines):**
> 1. **Confusing BHP/SHP/EHP/DHP.** Many students interchange these in formulas. Remember: BHP ≥ SHP ≥ DHP ≥ EHP.
> 2. **Skipping the block diagram in "general arrangement" questions.** A diagram carries **2–3 marks** by itself. Always draw one, even if rough.
> 3. **Naming the prime mover without explaining working principle.** Examiners expect *how* it works, not just *what* it is.
> 4. **Forgetting units.** Always mention kW or MW when stating power; mark loss if omitted.
> 5. **Writing generic answers about "turbines" without specifying marine application.** Mention marine-specific terms: HP/LP turbine stages, reheat cycles, marine boilers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Prime Movers:** Diesel, Steam, Gas, Nuclear — mnemonic **D-S-G-N**.
- **Power Hierarchy:** BHP → SHP → DHP → EHP (each progressively smaller).
- **Slow-speed 2-stroke diesels** dominate merchant ship propulsion today.
- **Reduction gearboxes** are essential for medium and high-speed engines.
- **Auxiliary machinery** includes pumps, compressors, generators, boilers, and steering gear.
- **Hull efficiency $\eta_H$** can exceed 1.0 due to wake recovery.
- **Propulsive efficiency** = EHP/SHP, typically 0.55–0.70.
- **Shaft power formula** (high-yield): $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ (Watts).
- **Nuclear propulsion** offers unlimited range but at huge capital cost.
- **Always draw block diagrams** for "arrangement" type questions — easy marks.
- **Steering gear** is a critical auxiliary for SOLAS compliance.
- **Bow thrusters** improve maneuverability in ports.
- **Reduction gears** also provide ahead/astern direction reversal.
- **Thrust bearing** transfers propeller force into the hull — not just a support bearing.
- **Air compressors** provide both starting air (30 bar) and control air (7 bar).
- **Marine boilers** now often use exhaust gas heat recovery (EGHR) for fuel economy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photoelectric and NC Control Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Photoelectric and NC Control Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Photoelectric Devices — Formal Definition
A **photoelectric device** is an optoelectronic transducer that converts incident light energy (photons) into an electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance change) by exploiting the **photoelectric effect** — the emission of electrons from a material when illuminated by electromagnetic radiation of sufficient frequency. In ship construction, these devices form the backbone of non-contact sensing, position detection, safety alarming, and precision measurement systems aboard vessels and in shipyards.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Photoelectric devices in shipbuilding are not studied for their solid-state physics, but for their **engineering function** — *sensing, counting, positioning, flame detection, and level monitoring* — both on the ship and in the fabrication workshop.

**Classification by Operational Mode:**
- **Photoemissive devices** — Photons liberate electrons from a cathode into vacuum/gas (Phototubes, Photomultipliers).
- **Photoconductive devices** — Light alters the electrical resistance of a semiconductor (LDR — Light Dependent Resistor, Photodiodes in reverse bias).
- **Photovoltaic devices** — Light generates a voltage across a p-n junction (Solar cells, Photodiodes in zero bias).
