- **Photo-junction devices** — Light controls current flow across a junction (Phototransistors, Photodarlingtons).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **photoelectric sensor** as a *silent electronic "watchman"*. Imagine a security guard standing in a corridor who cannot see but has a pressure pad. When a person (light beam) breaks the pad, the guard raises an alarm. The photoelectric sensor does the same — it projects an invisible infrared beam; when a hull plate, a cargo crane hook, or a passing sailor interrupts the beam, the receiver triggers a relay. **No contact, no moving parts, no wear — just photons doing the job.**

### 1.3 NC Control Devices — Formal Definition
A **Numerical Control (NC) device** is a machine tool or automated system whose motions and operations are directed by **alphanumeric coded instructions** (typically G-codes and M-codes) stored on a punched tape, magnetic disk, or computer memory. The **Controller** interprets these instructions and drives servo motors along linear/rotary axes to perform cutting, welding, drilling, or forming operations with sub-millimetre precision — indispensable in modern shipyards for **plate cutting, profile burning, pipe bending, and propeller machining**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Distinction — NC vs CNC vs DNC:**
> - **NC (Numerical Control):** Hard-wired logic; instructions from punched tape. *Obsolete in modern yards.*
> - **CNC (Computer Numerical Control):** Microprocessor-based; soft-wired; editable programs; closed-loop feedback.
> - **DNC (Direct Numerical Control):** A central host computer directly controls a network of CNC machines — the standard in large shipyards.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy for NC
Imagine teaching a robot apprentice to cut a complex ship plate by **writing a recipe in numbers** — *"Move right 500 mm, lower torch, ignite, travel along path ABC at 800 mm/min, lift, extinguish"*. The CNC reads this recipe, calculates the exact motor rotations, and produces identical plates day after day, year after year, with no fatigue, no error, and no lunch break. **The "NC" is the language; the "Device" is the executor.**

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metric:** Modern marine CNC plate-cutting machines operate with a **positional accuracy of ±0.1 mm** over a working envelope of **up to 30 m × 8 m** — sufficient to cut a complete deck plate in a single setup.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of Photoelectric Devices — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Photon Incidence**
Light of frequency $\nu$ strikes a photosensitive surface. Each photon carries discrete energy $E = h\nu$, where $h$ is **Planck's constant = $6.626 \times 10^{-34} \,\text{J}\cdot\text{s}$**.

**Step 2 — Electron Emission (Einstein's Photoelectric Equation)**
If $h\nu$ exceeds the work function $\phi$ of the material, an electron is ejected with maximum kinetic energy given by:

$$E_{k,\max} = h\nu - \phi$$

- If $h\nu < \phi$ → **No emission**, regardless of light intensity.
- This proves the **quantum nature of light**, confirmed by Einstein (1905).

**Step 3 — Threshold Frequency & Wavelength**
The minimum frequency required to liberate an electron is:

$$\nu_0 = \frac{\phi}{h}$$

The corresponding **threshold wavelength** (for visible/IR work) is:

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{\nu_0} = \frac{hc}{\phi} = \frac{1240 \,\text{nm}\cdot\text{eV}}{\phi \,(\text{in eV})}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8 \,\text{m/s}$ is the speed of light.

**Step 4 — Signal Conditioning**
The tiny photo-generated current (typically $\mu$A to mA) is amplified, conditioned, and used to drive a relay, a counter, or a 4–20 mA industrial signal for the ship's **Integrated Automation System (IAS)**.

### 2.2 Types of Photoelectric Sensors Used in Shipyards

| **Type** | **Operating Mode** | **Typical Shipyard/Ship Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Through-beam (Retro-reflective)** | Separate emitter + receiver across a gap | Crane collision prevention, dock-door safety |
| **Diffuse reflective** | Emitter and receiver in one housing | Hull-plate edge detection, level sensing in ballast tanks |
| **Retro-reflective with polarising filter** | Beam reflected from a corner-cube prism | Engine room door interlocks, conveyor counting |
| **Fiber-optic photoelectric** | Light guided through fiber to hostile zone | Boiler flame monitoring (with UV/IR sensing tube) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Flame Scanner:** A UV-sensitive phototube detects ultraviolet emission (185–260 nm) from the boiler flame. Loss of flame signal triggers an immediate **fuel shut-off** — a critical safety function governed by **SOLAS Reg. II-2**.

### 2.3 Working Principle of NC/CNC Devices

**Step 1 — Part Program Input**
The part programmer writes a sequence of codes:
- **G-codes** → Geometry (G00 rapid, G01 linear, G02 clockwise arc, G03 counter-clockwise arc)
- **M-codes** → Miscellaneous functions (M03 spindle on, M05 spindle stop, M08 coolant on)
- **Coordinate words** → X, Y, Z positions; F for feed rate; S for spindle speed.

**Step 2 — Interpretation by MCU**
The **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** decodes each block, computes tool-path geometry (via **interpolation** — linear, circular, or helical), and generates **reference commands** for each axis.

**Step 3 — Servo Drive**
A **servo amplifier** receives the command, compares it with the **feedback** from an encoder/linear scale, and drives a **servo motor** (typically AC brushless) to reduce the error (closed-loop control).

**Step 4 — Tool/Machine Action**
The cutting tool, plasma torch, oxy-fuel nozzle, or welding head moves precisely to remove/add material as programmed.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation** | **Meaning** | **Typical Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E = h\nu$ | Photon energy | J, eV | Photoelectric device selection |
| $E_{k,\max} = h\nu - \phi$ | Einstein's photoelectric equation | J, eV | Threshold calculation |
| $\phi = h\nu_0 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_0}$ | Work function | J, eV | Material selection (Cs, Na, K) |
| $I_p = R_\lambda \cdot \Phi_e$ | Photo-current relation | A, A/W, W | Sensor calibration |
| $N = \dfrac{v_f}{f_z \cdot z}$ | Spindle speed formula | rpm, mm/min, mm/tooth | CNC milling/drilling |
| $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | Feed rate formula | mm/min | NC programming |
| $T_{machining} = \dfrac{L + L_{approach} + L_{overtravel}}{v_f}$ | Cycle time | min, mm | Production planning |
| $R_{resolution} = \dfrac{L_{full\,scale}}{2^n}$ | Encoder resolution | mm/pulse | Position feedback accuracy |
| $A_s = a_x \cdot a_y \cdot a_z$ | 3-axis working envelope | m³ | Machine selection in shipyard |

> **Note:** In the table above, $\Phi_e$ = incident radiant flux, $R_\lambda$ = spectral responsivity, $f_z$ = feed per tooth, $z$ = number of teeth, $L$ = cut length, $n$ = encoder bits.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Marine Field

- **Photoelectric:** Automated plate-edge detection on the cutting line; smoke detection in accommodation blocks; optical shaft RPM pick-up; cargo hold level indication; paint-belt speed monitoring in dry dock.
- **NC/CNC:** Plasma/oxy-fuel profile cutting of hull plates; robotic welding of stiffener-to-plate seams; CNC machining of propeller blades (5-axis); pipe-bending for engine room pipework; automated shot-blasting path planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Einstein's Photoelectric Equation from Energy Conservation

Einstein assumed that light travels as discrete quanta (photons) of energy $E = h\nu$. When a single photon is absorbed by a single electron inside the photosensitive material:

**Step 1 — Total energy input to the electron:**
The electron absorbs the entire photon energy. So the total energy available to the electron is $h\nu$.

**Step 2 — Energy required to escape the surface:**
The electron, while still inside the metal, is bound by an energy $\phi$ (the **work function**). To escape into vacuum, it must overcome this binding.

**Step 3 — Energy left after escape:**
By the **Law of Conservation of Energy**, the energy left with the ejected electron as kinetic energy is the difference between the absorbed energy and the work required to escape:

$$E_{k,\max} = h\nu - \phi$$

This is the famous Einstein photoelectric equation. The "max" subscript indicates the kinetic energy in the case where the electron is liberated from the **surface** (no internal energy loss).

### 3.2 Derivation of Stopping Potential and Threshold Wavelength

A **stopping potential** $V_s$ is the reverse voltage at which the most energetic photoelectrons are just brought to rest. Therefore:

$$eV_s = E_{k,\max}$$

Substituting Einstein's equation:

$$eV_s = h\nu - \phi$$

Rearranging for $V_s$:

$$V_s = \frac{h}{e}\nu - \frac{\phi}{e}$$

This is a **straight line** when $V_s$ is plotted against $\nu$ — the famous experimental verification of Einstein's theory.
- **Slope of the line** = $h/e$ → used historically to determine Planck's constant.
- **Intercept on the $\nu$-axis** = $\phi/h$ = $\nu_0$ (threshold frequency).

The threshold wavelength is obtained by setting $V_s = 0$ (just enough energy, no excess kinetic energy):

$$h\nu_0 = \phi \quad\Rightarrow\quad \nu_0 = \frac{\phi}{h}$$

Converting frequency to wavelength using $c = \nu\lambda$:

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{\nu_0} = \frac{hc}{\phi}$$

Using $hc = 1240 \,\text{nm}\cdot\text{eV}$:

$$\lambda_0 \,\text{(nm)} = \frac{1240}{\phi \,\text{(eV)}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Threshold Wavelength of Caesium

**Given:** A photoelectric sensor uses a Caesium (Cs) photocathode. Work function of Cs = $\phi = 1.9 \,\text{eV}$.
**Find:** Threshold wavelength $\lambda_0$.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{1240 \,\text{nm}\cdot\text{eV}}{\phi}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{1240}{1.9}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$\lambda_0 \approx 652.6 \,\text{nm}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:** The sensor is sensitive to light of wavelength $\leq 652.6$ nm (i.e., visible red and shorter). For shipboard flame scanners, this is appropriate since combustion flames emit strongly in the 400–600 nm band.

### 3.4 Worked Example — NC Plate-Cutting Cycle Time

**Given:** A CNC plasma cutter is profiling a 4 m × 3 m rectangular plate.
- Cut path length per perimeter = $2(4 + 3) = 14 \,\text{m}$.
- Feed rate $v_f = 2000 \,\text{mm/min}$.
- Approach + overtravel allowance = 200 mm.
**Find:** Cycle time (ignition only, no piercing time).

**Step 1 — Total travel:**

$$L_{total} = 14000 + 200 = 14200 \,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Cycle time formula:**

$$T = \frac{L_{total}}{v_f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$T = \frac{14200}{2000} = 7.1 \,\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to minutes-seconds:** $T \approx 7 \,\text{min}\, 6 \,\text{s}$.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Photoelectric vs NC Device Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Photoelectric Device** | **NC/CNC Device** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary input** | Light (photon flux) | Numerical coded instructions |
| **Primary output** | Electrical signal (V, mA) | Mechanical motion / process action |
| **Core physics** | Quantum photoemission / photoconduction | Electromechanical servo control |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 0.5\%$ of set-point | $\pm 0.01$–$0.1$ mm positioning |
| **Response time** | $\mu$s to ms | s to min (per program block) |
| **Marine application** | Detection, alarm, level, RPM | Cutting, welding, bending, machining |
| **Failure mode** | Lens contamination, ageing of emitter | Tool wear, servo drift, encoder fault |
| **Maintenance** | Periodic lens cleaning, alignment | Calibration, lubrication, software update |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Shipboard Photoelectric Flame Scanner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Boiler Flame UV Emission] --> B[Quartz Lens and Fiber Optic Guide]
    B --> C[UV Phototube]
    C --> D[Amplifier and Signal Conditioner]
    D --> E[Comparator with Reference]
    E --> F[Flame Present Relay]
    E --> G[Flame Loss Timer]
    G --> H[Fuel Shut Off Solenoid]
    F --> I[Alarm and Indicator Panel]
    H --> I
    D --> J[4 to 20 mA Signal to IAS]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** UV photons from the boiler flame are focused by a quartz lens into a fiber-optic bundle, which carries them safely to the phototube. The amplified current is compared with a reference; if flame is lost for more than 1 second (typical), the **fuel shut-off solenoid** closes — a SOLAS-mandated safety chain.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — CNC Plate-Cutting Machine Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Operator Console HMI] --> B[Machine Control Unit MCU]
    B --> C[Interpreter and Interpolator]
    C --> D[X Axis Servo Drive]
    C --> E[Y Axis Servo Drive]
    C --> F[Z Axis Servo Drive]
    D --> G[X Servo Motor and Ballscrew]
    E --> H[Y Servo Motor and Rack Pinion]
    F --> I[Z Servo Motor and Lifter]
    G --> J[Linear Encoder Feedback]
    H --> K[Linear Encoder Feedback]
    I --> L[Rotary Encoder Feedback]
    J --> B
    K --> B
    L --> B
    B --> M[Plasma or Oxy Fuel Torch]
    B --> N[Height Control Sensor Photoelectric]
    N --> I
    M --> O[Hull Plate]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A closed-loop CNC cutting machine. The operator enters a part program via HMI. The MCU interprets each block, the interpolator generates smooth tool paths, and the servo drives move the gantry (X and Y) and the torch height (Z). Photoelectric sensors provide **torch-to-plate height feedback** (typically 3–8 mm arc gap for plasma). Encoders return actual position for error correction.

### 4.3 Working Envelope of a Typical Marine CNC Profile Cutter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Longitudinal Travel X axis 0 to 30000 mm] --> B[Transverse Travel Y axis 0 to 8000 mm]
    B --> C[Z axis Lift 0 to 250 mm]
    C --> D[Plasma or Oxy Fuel Torch]
    D --> E[Plate Support and Slag Bed]
```

### 4.4 Signal Conditioning Chain for a Photoelectric Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source IR LED] --> B[Target Object]
    B --> C[Photodiode Receiver]
    C --> D[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    D --> E[Band Pass Filter]
    E --> F[Comparator]
    F --> G[Schmitt Trigger]
    G --> H[Output Transistor Open Collector]
    H --> I[PLC Counter or Relay]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Modulated IR light is reflected from a target; the photodiode converts it to current; the transimpedance amplifier converts it to voltage; a band-pass filter rejects ambient light; a comparator and Schmitt trigger produce clean digital edges; the output transistor switches a PLC input for counting (e.g., conveyor bottles, plates, or cargo pieces).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the photoelectric effect. State Einstein's photoelectric equation and explain the significance of the work function.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **photoelectric effect** is the phenomenon of emission of electrons from a metal surface when electromagnetic radiation of suitable frequency falls on it. Einstein's equation is:

$$E_{k,\max} = h\nu - \phi$$

where $h\nu$ is the incident photon energy and $\phi$ is the **work function** — the minimum energy required to liberate an electron from the surface. The work function is significant because: (i) it determines the **threshold frequency** $\nu_0 = \phi/h$, below which no emission occurs regardless of intensity, and (ii) it governs the choice of photocathode material for shipboard sensors (e.g., Cs for visible-red, GaAs for IR).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between NC, CNC, and DNC systems in 5 points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No | NC | CNC | DNC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hard-wired logic | Microprocessor-based | Central host + network of CNC |
| 2 | Punched tape input | Memory/USB input | Direct communication |
| 3 | No feedback (open loop) | Closed-loop with encoder | Real-time program editing |
| 4 | Difficult to edit | Easy editing at console | Multi-machine management |
| 5 | Obsolete in modern yards | Standard in shipyards | Used in large shipyards |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Choice]**
*(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **photoelectric flame scanner** used in a marine boiler. Discuss its role in **SOLAS safety compliance**. (7 marks)
*(b)* Describe the **functional architecture of a CNC plate-cutting machine** used in a shipyard. Explain the role of the MCU, interpolator, servo drives, and feedback encoders. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Photoelectric Flame Scanner (7 marks)**

A marine boiler must shut off its fuel supply within ~1 second of flame failure, or a dangerous **unburnt fuel accumulation** and subsequent explosion can occur. SOLAS Reg. II-2/4 mandates this.

**Block description:**
1. **Flame as source** — Combustion emits UV (185–260 nm), visible, and IR.
2. **Sight tube + quartz/quartz-glass lens** — Focuses flame radiation onto the sensor.
3. **UV phototube** — A photocathode (e.g., Cs-Te, MgF₂ window) emits electrons only when UV photons above threshold strike it.
4. **Amplifier** — Boosts microampere-level photo-current.
5. **Comparator** — Compares signal with reference; if signal > threshold → "flame ON" relay energised.
6. **Flame-failure timer** — If signal absent for > 1 s → trip.
7. **Fuel shut-off solenoid** — Closes the oil/gas valve; burner management system logs the trip.

**Types in marine use:** UV scanner, IR scanner (lead-sulphide cell), visible-light photo-resistor (CdS). UV is preferred for oil-fired boilers (soot does not emit UV); IR is preferred for gas turbines.

**(b) CNC Plate-Cutter Architecture (7 marks)**

A modern shipyard's profile-burning machine is a **gantry-type CNC cutter** with two longitudinal rails (X-axis, up to 30 m) and a traversing bridge (Y-axis, up to 8 m) carrying one or two torches (Z-axis, 250 mm lift).

**Functional blocks:**
1. **HMI/Operator Console** — For part-program loading, simulation, and overrides.
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** — Heart of the CNC; runs the interpreter and interpolator.
3. **Interpreter** — Decodes G/M-codes into axis commands.
4. **Interpolator** — Generates intermediate points between the start and end of a cut (linear, circular, or helical interpolation) at the **BLU (Basic Length Unit)** of 0.001 mm.
5. **Servo Amplifiers** — Receive position command, compare with encoder feedback, and drive the servo motors.
6. **Servo Motors** — AC brushless type, coupled via **ballscrews** (X) or **rack-and-pinion** (Y) to move the gantry.
7. **Feedback Encoders** — Linear scales on each axis report actual position back to the MCU; closed-loop control maintains accuracy.
8. **Torch Height Control** — A photoelectric (capacitive/arc-voltage) sensor keeps the plasma or oxy-fuel nozzle at constant standoff distance from the plate.
9. **Plasma/Oxy-fuel Power Source** — Provides the cutting energy; controlled via M-codes (M07 ignite, M08 coolant, etc.).
10. **Dust/slag extraction & safety interlocks** — Comply with factory safety norms.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model]**
*(a)* Derive **Einstein's photoelectric equation** from energy conservation. Hence obtain the expression for the **threshold wavelength** in terms of work function. (7 marks)
*(b)* A shipyard uses a CNC milling machine to machine a **ship propeller blade blank**. The tool has 4 teeth, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.2$ mm/tooth, and the recommended cutting speed is $v_c = 80$ m/min. The cutter diameter is 100 mm. Calculate: (i) the spindle speed $N$, (ii) the feed rate $v_f$, and (iii) the cycle time for a cut length of 500 mm. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

- **Assumption:** Light consists of discrete photons, each with energy $E = h\nu$. *(1 mark)*
- A single photon is absorbed by a single electron. *(1 mark)*
- The electron uses part of the photon energy to overcome the work function $\phi$ and the rest appears as kinetic energy. *(2 marks)*
- Energy conservation gives $h\nu = \phi + E_{k,\max}$. Rearranging:

$$E_{k,\max} = h\nu - \phi \quad \text{(Einstein's equation)} \quad (1 \,\text{mark})$$

- For just emission (zero kinetic energy), $h\nu_0 = \phi$, so $\nu_0 = \phi/h$.
- Converting via $c = \nu\lambda$:

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{\nu_0} = \frac{hc}{\phi} \quad (2 \,\text{marks})$$

Using $hc = 1240 \,\text{nm}\cdot\text{eV}$:

$$\lambda_0 \,\text{(nm)} = \frac{1240}{\phi \,\text{(eV)}}$$

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $z = 4$ teeth, $f_z = 0.2$ mm/tooth, $v_c = 80$ m/min, $D = 100$ mm, $L = 500$ mm.

**(i) Spindle speed:**

$$N = \frac{v_c}{\pi D} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{\pi \times 100} = \frac{80000}{314.159}$$

$$N \approx 254.6 \,\text{rpm} \quad (3 \,\text{marks})$$

**(ii) Feed rate:**

$$v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.2 \times 4 \times 254.6 = 203.7 \,\text{mm/min}$$

$$v_f \approx 204 \,\text{mm/min} \quad (2 \,\text{marks})$$

**(iii) Cycle time:**

$$T = \frac{L}{v_f} = \frac{500}{203.7} = 2.455 \,\text{min}$$

$$T \approx 2 \,\text{min}\, 27 \,\text{s} \quad (2 \,\text{marks})$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $N$ calculation:** Always convert $v_c$ from m/min to mm/min by multiplying by 1000, and keep $D$ in mm. A common error is using $D$ in cm and losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Photoelectric derivation:** Students often write $E = h\nu$ but forget the *energy conservation statement* — examiners specifically look for the phrase *"The energy of the absorbed photon equals the work function plus the kinetic energy of the emitted electron"*.
> 3. **Threshold frequency confusion:** $\nu_0$ is the **minimum frequency for emission**, not the frequency of the incident light. Don't interchange.
> 4. **Confusing $\phi$ and $\phi_e$:** $\phi$ is the work function in J or eV; do not confuse with electric potential $\phi_e$ (V).
> 5. **NC vs CNC vs DNC:** Examiners deduct marks if you state "NC and CNC are the same" — they are **not**, especially regarding feedback and editability.
> 6. **CNC feed direction:** Feed is *per tooth per revolution*; the total feed per revolution is $f_z \times z$, and the *table* feed rate is $f_z \times z \times N$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- **Photoelectric effect** is the quantum emission of electrons from a surface illuminated by light of frequency $\nu \geq \nu_0$.
- **Einstein's equation:** $E_{k,\max} = h\nu - \phi$ — universally tested.
- **Threshold frequency:** $\nu_0 = \phi/h$; **Threshold wavelength:** $\lambda_0 = hc/\phi = 1240/\phi_{eV}$ (in nm).
- **Work function values** (in eV) to remember: Cs = 1.9, K = 2.3, Na = 2.75, Cu = 4.7, W = 4.5.
- **Photoelectric device classifications:** Photoemissive, Photoconductive, Photovoltaic, Photo-junction — know one shipboard use of each.
- **Marine flame scanner** → UV phototube → trip within 1 s → fuel shut-off (SOLAS).
- **NC** = punched-tape, hard-wired, no feedback; **CNC** = microprocessor, closed-loop, editable; **DNC** = networked host + multiple CNCs.
- **MCU** interprets the part program; **Interpolator** generates intermediate points; **Servo** drives the motor; **Encoder** closes the loop.
- **Key NC formulas:** $N = v_c / (\pi D)$, $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$, $T = L / v_f$.
- **CNC accuracy in shipyards:** ±0.1 mm typical; working envelopes up to 30 m × 8 m for plate cutters.
- **Maintenance cue:** Photoelectric sensors fail most often due to **lens contamination**; CNC machines fail most often due to **encoder/ballscrew wear** — both relevant to shipyard maintenance planning.
- **Always use consistent units** (mm, rpm, mm/min) — KTU evaluators are strict about unit marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Edge Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Edge Preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge Preparation (Definition per AWS / ISO 9692-1)**
> *Edge preparation* is the mechanical, thermal, or combined process of shaping the edges of metal plates, sections, or pipes to a specific geometric profile (bevel angle, root face, root gap, and land) prior to welding, in order to facilitate complete fusion, ensure full penetration of the weld metal into the joint, and produce a sound metallurgical and mechanical bond between the parent members.

In the context of **ship construction**, edge preparation is performed on hull plates, sheer strakes, keel plates, bulkhead plates, deck plates, and stiffener flanges/webs before they are assembled for arc welding. The IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) and classification societies such as **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)**, **LR (Lloyd's Register)**, **ABS**, **DNV**, and **BV** strictly govern edge preparation tolerances under their welding rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Society Context for KTU**
> Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OENST724)**, edge preparation is treated as a foundational pre-welding fabrication activity under Module 2 (Fabrication & Welding Technology). It directly links to subsequent modules on **welding processes (SMAW, SAW, GMAW, FCAW)** and **weld defects (lack of penetration, lack of fusion)**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

The following terms form the vocabulary backbone of edge preparation and are frequently asked in KTU exams:

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Root Face (Land)** | The unmachined / unflanged portion of the prepared edge that touches the opposing plate. Typically **1.5 mm to 3 mm**. |
| **Root Gap** | The shortest distance between the two prepared plate edges before welding. Typically **1 mm to 4 mm**. |
| **Bevel Angle** | The included angle of the prepared groove on a single side. |
| **Groove Angle (Total Angle)** | The total included angle of the groove (e.g., 60° single-V, 90° double-V). |
| **Bevel** | An angled edge preparation on ONE side only. |
| **J-Groove** | A curved (concave) edge preparation on one side, used for thicker plates. |
| **U-Groove** | A double-curved edge preparation offering deeper penetration with less weld metal. |
| **Root** | The deepest point of the prepared groove where the two plates meet. |
| **Toe** | The junction between the prepared face (bevel) and the original plate surface. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Two-Bread Sandwich"**
> Imagine trying to glue two thick slices of bread together with cheese in the middle. If the bread edges are flat and pressed together, glue only sticks to the outer surface — the inner contact is weak (this is like a **square butt joint** on a thin plate, which is strong enough for *thin* material).
> 
> Now imagine you slice each bread piece at a 30° angle before pressing them together. The contact area becomes a large "V" filled with cheese. Glue penetrates deep into this V and locks the pieces together far more strongly. This is exactly what a **Single-V groove** does for welding — the bevel increases the contact surface area for molten weld metal to fuse with.
> 
> For very thick "bread" (thick plates ≥ 25 mm), you would bevel BOTH sides (a **Double-V** or **X-groove**) — this is like making a strong joint without having to fill one huge V with too much cheese (weld metal), saving filler material and reducing distortion.

This intuitive picture directly translates to the engineering rule of thumb:
- **Thin plate (≤ 6 mm)** → Square butt (no preparation needed).
- **Medium plate (6–25 mm)** → Single-V or Single-bevel.
- **Thick plate (> 25 mm)** → Double-V (X), Double-U, or Double-J.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Edge Preparation is Necessary — The Engineering Rationale

Edge preparation is **not arbitrary**; it is governed by metallurgy, weldability, and economics. The four driving reasons are:

1. **Ensuring Full Penetration** — A square butt on thick plate leaves a central unfused region (cold lap). Beveling opens a path for the arc and molten metal to reach the root.
2. **Reducing Total Weld Metal Volume** — Counter-intuitively, the *volume* of filler required to fill a properly beveled joint is *less* than filling a square butt on thick plate (after considering the unfused root defect in the square butt).
3. **Minimizing HAZ (Heat Affected Zone)** — Controlled root gap + bevel angle allows lower heat input welding, preserving base metal toughness (critical for LTTE / EH36 shipbuilding steel).
4. **Accessibility for the Arc** — The groove geometry must allow the welding electrode / torch to reach the root without short-circuiting against sidewalls.

### 2.2 Classification of Edge Preparations

Edge preparations fall under two master categories (per **ISO 9692-1** and **AWS A2.4**):

#### A. No Preparation (Square Butt)
- Used when plate thickness *t* ≤ **6 mm** for shipbuilding (some standards allow up to 8 mm for low-stress locations).
- Edges are sheared or machined square.
- A permanent backing or root run is required.

#### B. Prepared Edges (Groove Welds)
Prepared edges are further classified by **shape** and **sidedness**:

**By Shape:**
- **Bevel** (single flat inclined face)
- **V-Groove** (two opposing bevels forming a V)
- **U-Groove** (two opposing concave faces)
- **J-Groove** (one concave face, one square)
- **Compound / Combination** (e.g., J + Bevel)

**By Sidedness:**
- **Single-Sided** (preparation on one side only)
- **Double-Sided** (preparation on both sides; commonly X, K, double-U)

### 2.3 Standard Edge Profiles Used in Shipbuilding

The following table consolidates the **standard profiles** prescribed by **IACS S21 / S22** and widely used in Indian shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE, HSL):

| Profile | Sketch (Cross-section) | Typical Bevel Angle θ | Root Face (mm) | Root Gap (mm) | Typical Plate Thickness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Square Butt | \|⌐ ⌐\| | 0° | Full thickness | 0–2 | ≤ 6 mm |
| Single-V | V-shape | 50°–60° | 1.5–3 | 2–3 | 6 – 25 mm |
| Single-Bevel | Half-V | 45°–50° | 1.5–3 | 2–3 | 6 – 16 mm |
| Double-V (X) | X-shape | 60°–90° (each side) | 1.5–2 | 2–3 | > 25 mm |
| Double-Bevel (K) | K-shape | 45°–50° (each side) | 1.5–2 | 2–3 | 16 – 40 mm |
| Single-U | U on one side | 8°–15° (sidewall) | 1.5–2 | 2–3 | > 20 mm |
| Double-U | U on both sides | 8°–15° (each) | 1.5–2 | 2–3 | > 40 mm |
| Single-J | J curve one side | — | 1.5–2 | 2–3 | > 16 mm |
| Double-J | J on both sides | — | 1.5–2 | 2–3 | > 30 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The most frequently asked edge profile in KTU exams is the **Single-V Groove** for its numerical dimensioning and weld metal volume calculations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Edge Preparation Geometry

The following formulas are essential for KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical problems.

**Notation used in all formulas below:**
- $t$ = plate thickness (mm)
- $\theta$ = total groove angle (degrees)
- $b$ = root gap (mm)
- $c$ = root face / land (mm)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of weld metal (mm²) — *per unit length of weld*

| # | Joint Type | Cross-Sectional Area of Weld Metal | Penetration Check |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Square Butt (full penetration assumed) | $A = t \cdot b$ | Only valid for $t \leq 6$ mm |
| 2 | Single-V Groove | $A = t \cdot b + \dfrac{(t-c)^2}{2} \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot 2 / 2$ → simplifies to $A = (t-c)^2 \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) + t \cdot b$ | Full penetration guaranteed |
| 3 | Single-Bevel | $A = (t-c)^2 \tan(\theta) / 2 + t \cdot b$ | Requires back-gouging for full penetration |
| 4 | Double-V (X) | $A = 2 \cdot \left[\dfrac{(t/2 - c)^2}{2}\right] \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) + t \cdot b$ | Full penetration guaranteed |
| 5 | Double-Bevel (K) | $A = (t/2 - c)^2 \tan(\theta) + t \cdot b$ | Requires back-gouging |
| 6 | Single-U Groove | $A = \dfrac{(t-c)^2}{2}\tan(\theta) + (t-c) \cdot R_{\text{root}} + t \cdot b$ | Limited use in shipbuilding (costly) |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Rule:** All absolute-value and division bars in formulas are written as `\vert` or `\mid` (never as a bare `\vert` pipe) to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real Shipyards

- **Weld Metal Savings:** A Double-V (X-groove) on a 30 mm plate can save up to **35–40 %** weld metal compared with a Single-V, because each side only fills half the depth.
- **Distortion Control:** Symmetric Double-V / Double-Bevel profiles balance thermal shrinkage on both faces of the plate, minimizing **angular distortion** and **bowing** — critical for maintaining hull-plate flatness tolerances (±2 mm over 2 m, per IACS UR S21).
- **Welding Process Selection:**
  - Single-V → commonly **SMAW** (Shielded Metal Arc Welding) and **FCAW**.
  - Double-V (X) → **SAW** (Submerged Arc Welding) for the bulk passes.
  - Single-U / Double-U → preferred for **GMAW / mechanized welding** of thicker plates.
- **Cost Implication:** Machining a U-groove costs **3–5×** more than flame/bevel cutting a V-groove. Hence V-grooves dominate shipyard economics; U-grooves appear only in pressure vessels and critical nozzle attachments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of Weld Metal in a Single-V Groove

**Objective:** To derive the closed-form expression for the weld-metal cross-sectional area of a Single-V groove joint per unit length of weld.

**Given:**
- Plate thickness = $t$
- Root gap = $b$
- Root face = $c$
- Total groove angle = $\theta$

**Geometric Model:** Consider the Single-V groove as the superposition of **two regions**:

1. A **rectangular** strip of width $b$ spanning the entire thickness $t$.
2. A **triangular** region above the root face on each side, bounded by the root face (length $t - c$) and the inclined bevel face.

**Step 1 — Identify the rectangular component:**

The rectangular region is a simple rectangle of height $t$ and width $b$:

$$
A_{\text{rect}} = t \cdot b
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the triangular component (one side):**

On one side of the V, the bevel face rises from the root-face tip to the plate top surface. The horizontal projection of this face is:

$$
x = (t - c) \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

The vertical projection is the root face length itself: $h = t - c$.

Hence the area of ONE triangular side is:

$$
A_{\text{tri, one}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (t - c) \cdot (t - c) \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
A_{\text{tri, one}} = \frac{1}{2}(t - c)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Combine both triangular sides (Single-V has 2 sides):**

$$
A_{\text{tri, total}} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}(t - c)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
A_{\text{tri, total}} = (t - c)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the rectangular and triangular components:**

$$
A_{\text{total}} = A_{\text{rect}} + A_{\text{tri, total}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_{\text{Single-V}} = (t - c)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + t \cdot b\,}
$$

This is the standard KTU-formula Single-V weld-metal cross-section.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cross-Sectional Area of Weld Metal in a Double-V (X) Groove

**Given:** Same symbols, but the groove is symmetric with two opposing V's meeting at the plate mid-thickness.

**Step 1 — Effective thickness per side:**
Each side now only fills half the depth, so the effective bevel depth is $\frac{t}{2} - c$ (root face still on each side):

$$
h_{\text{eff}} = \frac{t}{2} - c
$$

**Step 2 — Triangular area per side:**

$$
A_{\text{tri, one}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{t}{2} - c\right)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Two sides × two V's:**

$$
A_{\text{tri, total}} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{t}{2} - c\right)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
A_{\text{tri, total}} = \left(\frac{t}{2} - c\right)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Rectangular root-gap contribution (same as Single-V):**

$$
A_{\text{rect}} = t \cdot b
$$

**Step 5 — Final expression:**

$$
\boxed{\,A_{\text{Double-V}} = \left(\frac{t}{2} - c\right)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + t \cdot b\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Insight:** Since $(t/2 - c) < (t - c)$ for the same plate thickness, the Double-V groove deposits *significantly less* weld metal than a Single-V — exactly the reason shipyards prefer it for thick plates.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimal Bevel Angle for Minimum Weld Volume

**Objective:** Show that for a fixed plate thickness and root gap, the bevel angle that minimizes weld volume lies in the 50°–60° range.

**Step 1 — Start with Single-V formula (excluding constant $t \cdot b$):**

$$
V(\theta) = (t - c)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\theta$:**

$$
\frac{dV}{d\theta} = (t - c)^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\sec^{2}\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

Since $\sec^{2}(\theta/2) \geq 1$ and $(t - c)^2 \geq 0$, the derivative is always positive.

**Step 3 — Interpret:**
$V(\theta)$ is a **monotonically increasing** function of $\theta$. Therefore, the smallest practical angle gives the least weld volume.

**Step 4 — Practical lower bound:**
The minimum bevel angle is constrained by **arc accessibility** and **sidewall fusion**. Empirical and ISO/AWS standards fix the lower limit at **≈ 50°** for shipbuilding.

Therefore, the optimal practical range is:

$$
\boxed{\,50^{\circ} \leq \theta_{\text{opt}} \leq 60^{\circ}\,}
$$

This matches the **IACS S21** recommended groove angle for ship hull welding.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Problem:** A Single-V butt joint is to be made on **20 mm** mild-steel ship plate. Root face = **2 mm**, root gap = **2 mm**, total groove angle = **60°**. Calculate the cross-sectional weld-metal area per mm of weld length.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (t - c)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + t \cdot b \\
&= (20 - 2)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{60^{\circ}}{2}\right) + (20 \times 2) \\
&= (18)^{2} \tan(30^{\circ}) + 40 \\
&= 324 \times 0.5774 + 40 \\
&= 187.07 + 40 \\
&= 227.07 \ \text{mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $A \approx 227.07 \ \text{mm}^{2}$ per mm weld length.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Edge Profile

The matrix below is a **high-yield KTU exam table**. Use it to choose the right preparation based on plate thickness.

| Plate Thickness (mm) | Recommended Profile | Bevel Angle | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\leq 6$ | Square Butt | 0° | No edge prep needed; welding from one side. |
| 6 – 12 | Single-Bevel | 45°–50° | One-sided prep; back-gouge root. |
| 12 – 25 | Single-V | 50°–60° | Most common shipbuilding joint. |
| 25 – 40 | Double-Bevel (K) | 45°–50° each | Saves weld metal; back-gouge. |
| 40 – 60 | Double-V (X) | 60° each | Bulk welding via SAW. |
| > 60 | Double-U | 8°–15° sidewall | Heavy plate, nuclear / submarine hulls. |
| Pipe (all sizes) | Single-V or Single-J | 60° (V), 8°–15° (J) | As per ASME B31.1 / B31.3. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Edge Preparation Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block represents the **decision flow** a shipyard fabricator follows to select an edge-preparation profile.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Plate for Welding] --> B{Plate Thickness t in mm}
    B -->|t le 6| C[Square Butt Joint]
    B -->|6 lt t le 25| D{Single-Sided Access?}
    B -->|t gt 25| E{Symmetric Distortion Control Needed?}
    D -->|Yes| F[Single-V Groove]
    D -->|No| G[Single-Bevel with Back-Gouge]
    E -->|Yes| H[Double-V X Groove]
    E -->|No| I[Double-Bevel K Groove]
    C --> J{Welding Process}
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -->|Manual| K[SMAW or FCAW]
    J -->|Mechanized| L[SAW or GMAW]
    K --> M[Inspect per IACS UR S21]
    L --> M
    M --> N{Defects Found?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Repair / Re-prep]
    N -->|No| P[Approved Weld]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Edge Preparation Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph InputPhase[Input Stage]
        nodeA[Raw Ship Plate EH36] --> nodeB[Plate Surface Cleaning]
        nodeB --> nodeC[Plate Marking and Nesting]
    end

    subgraph PrepPhase[Edge Preparation Stage]
        nodeC --> nodeD[Thermal Cutting Oxy Fuel]
        nodeC --> nodeE[Plasma Arc Cutting]
        nodeC --> nodeF[Mechanical Machining]
        nodeD --> nodeG[Bevel Inspection]
        nodeE --> nodeG
        nodeF --> nodeG
    end

    subgraph QCPhase[Quality Control Stage]
        nodeG --> nodeH{Dimensional Check OK?}
        nodeH -->|No| nodeI[Re-cut / Re-machine]
        nodeI --> nodeG
        nodeH -->|Yes| nodeJ[NDE Visual PT MT]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage[Assembly Stage]
        nodeJ --> nodeK[Fit-up Tacking]
        nodeK --> nodeL[Root Run Welding]
        nodeL --> nodeM[Cap Pass Welding]
        nodeM --> nodeN[Final NDE UT RT]
    end
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description — Single-V Groove Cross-Section

A Single-V Groove cross-section (for KTU diagram questions) consists of:

1. Two parent plates of thickness **t**, placed vertically with a **root gap b** between them.
2. Each plate edge cut at an angle of **θ/2** from vertical, so the included angle between the two bevel faces is **θ**.
3. A small **root face c** left at the bottom of each bevel, which contacts the opposing plate across the root gap.
4. The V-shaped cavity is filled with weld metal in **multiple passes**: a root pass, hot/fill passes, and a final cap pass.

### 4.4 Schematic Description — Double-V (X) Cross-Section

For thick plates (t > 25 mm), the groove is cut from **both sides**. The two V's meet at approximately the plate mid-thickness. The **unbeveled root region** is preserved on each side as the root face. Welding proceeds alternately from both sides to balance heat input, minimizing distortion.

### 4.5 Mermaid Block — Welding Symbol for Edge Preparation (AWS A2.4 Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Line] --> B[Arrow Side Symbol Bevel]
    A --> C[Other Side Symbol]
    A --> D[Tail Specifications]
    B --> E[Groove Angle 60 deg]
    B --> F[Root Face 2 mm]
    B --> G[Root Gap 2 mm]
    B --> H[Groove Depth if any]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define edge preparation. List any four standard edge profiles used in shipbuilding.*

**Model Answer:**
Edge preparation is the process of shaping plate edges to a specific geometric profile (bevel angle, root face, root gap) prior to welding, ensuring complete fusion and full penetration of the weld metal.

Four standard profiles:
1. Square Butt
2. Single-V Groove
3. Double-V (X) Groove
4. Single-Bevel / K-Groove

(Any four from the classification table; full marks require both definition and profile names.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the function of the root face in an edge preparation?*

**Model Answer:**
The root face (land) is the small unmachined portion of the prepared edge that:
1. Acts as a **supporting shelf** for the root run weld metal.
2. Prevents the arc from burning through the root.
3. Maintains a consistent **root gap** during fit-up.
4. Typical size: **1.5 – 3 mm** in shipbuilding applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students commonly lose 2–4 marks on this topic for the following reasons:
> 1. **Confusing root face with root gap** — examiners mark this as a fundamental error.
> 2. **Wrong formula application** — using the Single-V formula for a Double-V problem (or vice versa) is a *show-stopper* error.
> 3. **Missing units** in the final numerical answer (e.g., writing `227` instead of `227.07 mm²`).
> 4. **Not mentioning classification society standards** (IACS S21, AWS A2.4) in descriptive answers — losing 2 application-level marks.
> 5. **Forgetting back-gouging** in single-bevel / K-groove answers when full penetration is required.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Choice A)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *(Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme)*

**(a)** *With neat sketches, explain the following edge-preparation profiles used in shipbuilding: (i) Single-V Groove, (ii) Double-V (X) Groove, (iii) Single-Bevel Groove. Mention typical bevel angles, root face, and root gap values for each.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Single-V Groove:**
- **Sketch:** Two plates of thickness *t* with opposing 30° bevels (total angle 60°).
- **Bevel Angle (θ):** 50°–60°
- **Root Face (c):** 1.5–3 mm
- **Root Gap (b):** 2–3 mm
- **Use:** General shipbuilding for plate thickness 6–25 mm.
- **Formula:** $A = (t - c)^{2} \tan(\theta/2) + t \cdot b$

**(ii) Double-V (X) Groove:**
- **Sketch:** Two opposing V-grooves cut from both faces of the plate.
- **Bevel Angle (θ each side):** 60° (total per side); typically 60°–90°.
- **Root Face (c):** 1.5–2 mm per side
- **Root Gap (b):** 2–3 mm
- **Use:** Plates thicker than 25 mm; reduces weld volume by ~35–40 % vs Single-V.
- **Formula:** $A = ((t/2 - c)^{2}) \tan(\theta/2) + t \cdot b$

**(iii) Single-Bevel Groove:**
- **Sketch:** Only one plate is beveled; the other remains square.
- **Bevel Angle (θ):** 45°–50°
- **Root Face (c):** 1.5–3 mm
- **Root Gap (b):** 2–3 mm
- **Use:** When only one side is accessible (e.g., internal bulkhead to shell plating).
- **Note:** Requires **back-gouging** from the back side for full penetration.

---

**(b)** *A Single-V butt joint is to be welded on a 25 mm thick EH36 ship plate. Root face = 2 mm, root gap = 3 mm, total groove angle = 60°. Calculate: (i) cross-sectional area of weld metal per unit length, (ii) total weld volume for a 1.2 m long weld, (iii) compare the result with a Double-V joint of the same dimensions and comment on weld-metal saving.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Single-V Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{SV}} &= (t - c)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + t \cdot b \\
&= (25 - 2)^{2} \tan(30^{\circ}) + (25 \times 3) \\
&= 23^{2} \times 0.5774 + 75 \\
&= 529 \times 0.5774 + 75 \\
&= 305.44 + 75 \\
&= 380.44 \ \text{mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Weld Volume for L = 1.2 m = 1200 mm:**

$$
V_{\text{SV}} = A \cdot L = 380.44 \times 1200 = 456{,}528 \ \text{mm}^{3} \approx 456.5 \ \text{cm}^{3}
$$

**(iii) Double-V Cross-Sectional Area (Same dimensions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{DV}} &= \left(\frac{t}{2} - c\right)^{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + t \cdot b \\
&= \left(\frac{25}{2} - 2\right)^{2} \tan(30^{\circ}) + (25 \times 3) \\
&= (10.5)^{2} \times 0.5774 + 75 \\
&= 110.25 \times 0.5774 + 75 \\
&= 63.66 + 75 \\
&= 138.66 \ \text{mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Weld Volume (Double-V):**

$$
V_{\text{DV}} = 138.66 \times 1200 = 166{,}392 \ \text{mm}^{3} \approx 166.4 \ \text{cm}^{3}
$$

**Weld-Metal Saving:**

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{456.5 - 166.4}{456.5} \times 100 \approx 63.6 \%
$$

**Comment:** Double-V groove saves approximately **63.6 %** weld metal compared with Single-V on a 25 mm plate, justifying its use for thicker plates in shipbuilding.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme)*

**(a)** *Explain the role of edge preparation in ship welding. List and sketch any five standard edge-preparation profiles with their typical application ranges.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Edge Preparation:**
1. Ensures **full penetration** of weld metal.
2. Reduces weld metal volume in thick plates.
3. Minimizes Heat Affected Zone (HAZ).
4. Provides arc accessibility to the root.
5. Improves mechanical strength and fatigue life of joints.

**Five Standard Profiles (with sketches):**

| Profile | Sketch Description | Application Range |
|---|---|---|
| Square Butt | Two flat edges; no bevel | Plates ≤ 6 mm |
| Single-V | V-shape with θ = 60° | Plates 6 – 25 mm |
| Single-Bevel | One-side angle, other side flat | Plates 6 – 16 mm (one-sided access) |
| Double-V (X) | V on both sides | Plates > 25 mm |
| Single-U | Concave U-shape on one side | Plates > 20 mm |

(Sketches must show the cross-section with root gap, root face, and bevel angle.)

---

**(b)** *For a Double-Bevel (K-groove) joint on a 30 mm thick plate with bevel angle 50° on each side, root face 2 mm per side, root gap 2 mm, calculate (i) the cross-sectional weld-metal area per mm length, (ii) total weld metal volume for a 2 m weld, (iii) percentage weld saving versus an equivalent Square Butt joint.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Double-Bevel (K) Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{K}} &= \left(\frac{t}{2} - c\right)^{2} \tan(\theta) + t \cdot b \\
&= (15 - 2)^{2} \tan(50^{\circ}) + (30 \times 2) \\
&= 13^{2} \times 1.1918 + 60 \\
&= 169 \times 1.1918 + 60 \\
&= 201.41 + 60 \\
&= 261.41 \ \text{mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Weld Volume for L = 2 m = 2000 mm:**

$$
V_{\text{K}} = 261.41 \times 2000 = 522{,}820 \ \text{mm}^{3} \approx 522.82 \ \text{cm}^{3}
$$

**(iii) Equivalent Square Butt (theoretical — not normally used):**

$$
A_{\text{SB}} = t \cdot b = 30 \times 2 = 60 \ \text{mm}^{2}
$$

(Note: Square butt on 30 mm plate would not achieve full penetration; the comparison shows that K-groove requires **more** weld metal but achieves full penetration, which the square butt cannot.)

$$
V_{\text{SB}} = 60 \times 2000 = 120{,}000 \ \text{mm}^{3}
$$

$$
\% \text{ Increase (K vs SB)} = \frac{522.82 - 120}{120} \times 100 \approx 335.7 \%
$$

**Conclusion:** Although the K-groove uses more weld metal than a (theoretical) square butt, it is the **only** configuration that achieves full penetration on a 30 mm plate — the 335 % extra is the engineering cost of integrity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- [x] **Edge preparation** = shaping plate edges for full-penetration welding.
- [x] **Root face (c)** = 1.5 – 3 mm; **Root gap (b)** = 1 – 4 mm; **Bevel angle (θ)** = 45° – 90°.
- [x] **Square Butt** → *t* ≤ 6 mm only.
- [x] **Single-V** → *t* = 6 – 25 mm; **θ** = 50° – 60°.
- [x] **Double-V (X)** → *t* > 25 mm; saves ~35–65 % weld metal.
- [x] **Single-Bevel / K-Groove** → requires **back-gouging**.
- [x] **Master Formula (Single-V):** $A = (t-c)^2 \tan(\theta/2) + t \cdot b$
- [x] **Master Formula (Double-V):** $A = (t/2 - c)^2 \tan(\theta/2) + t \cdot b$
- [x] **U-Groove** → expensive but saves weld metal in thick plates (> 60 mm).
- [x] **Classification standards:** IACS UR S21, AWS A2.4, ISO 9692-1, IRS Rules.
- [x] **Symmetric profiles** (X, K, Double-U) **reduce angular distortion** by balancing heat input.
- [x] **V-groove volume** is **monotonically increasing** with θ — minimum practical θ ≈ 50°.
- [x] Always specify **units (mm², mm³, cm³)** in numerical answers.
- [x] Always mention **back-gouging** when single-sided preparations are used in service-critical joints.
- [x] **Welding symbol** for edge preparation per AWS A2.4 must show groove angle, root face, root gap, and groove depth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending of Rolled and Built Up Sections - Control of the Bending Process
Automation of Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automation of Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automation of Bending** in ship construction refers to the integration of **Computer-Numerically Controlled (CNC) systems**, **robotic manipulators**, **servo-hydraulic actuation**, and **closed-loop sensor feedback** to perform the plastic deformation of shipbuilding steel plates, sections, and stiffeners into compound-curved hull geometries without continuous manual intervention.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, automation of bending encompasses four primary sub-processes:

1. **Plate Bending Automation** — cold forming of flat plates into cylindrical or conical shells (e.g., shell plating, bilge strakes).
2. **Section Bending Automation** — cold/hot forming of rolled sections such as angles, T-bars, channels, and HP-bulb profiles for longitudinal stiffeners.
3. **Line Heating Automation** — controlled application of oxy-fuel or induction heat along a defined path on the plate to induce shrinkage bending (replaces traditional hand line heating).
4. **Profile Bending Automation** — roller-bending of profiles to match the curvature of decks, sheer strakes, and transverse frames.

The unifying philosophy of automation is **geometric repeatability, dimensional accuracy (typically ±1 mm per meter for modern yard tolerances), reduction of skilled-labour dependency, and ergonomic safety** in compliance with **ISO 9001 / IACS S21 / S22** shipbuilding quality standards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Robotic Origami of Steel"**
>
> Imagine you are trying to fold a thick, springy stainless-steel sheet into the exact shape of a ship's bow. A human worker would hammer, heat, and press it by feel and experience. **Automation of bending is like giving that worker a robotic arm equipped with thermal cameras, a digital twin of the hull, and a precise CNC controller.** The robot "knows" the exact curvature of every plate, calculates the heat input or roller position, and executes the bend to within a millimetre — and does it identically for the next 1,000 plates on the production line.

For a B.Tech student new to shipbuilding, think of the hull as a **3D jigsaw puzzle of curved metal**. Each puzzle piece must be bent precisely so the pieces fit together. Older shipyards bent plates manually (skilled artisans using hammers, waterbags, and torches). Modern automated yards use:

- **CNC press brakes** (vertical bending)
- **Three-roll pyramid bending machines** (cylindrical rolling)
- **Robotic line-heating carriages** (thermal bending)
- **Multi-axis section benders** (profile rolling)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition | Typical Shipyard Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Plate thickness (t)** | Nominal steel plate thickness | **6 mm – 80 mm** |
| **Bending radius (R)** | Inner radius of bend | ≥ **2t** for cold bending |
| **Spring-back angle (Δθ)** | Elastic recovery after unloading | **1° – 5°** |
| **Yield strength (σ_y)** | Mild shipbuilding steel | **235 MPa** (Grade A) |
| **Young's modulus (E)** | Steel elastic modulus | **210 GPa** |
| **Thermal expansion coeff. (α)** | Carbon steel | **11.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** |
| **Line-heat temperature** | Oxy-fuel torch | **600 °C – 900 °C** |
| **Hull tolerance** | As-built vs. design | **±1 mm/m** (modern yard) |
| **CO₂ reduction target** | IACS / EU yard directive | **≥ 30 %** vs manual |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme strongly emphasises the *transition from craft-based to Industry 4.0 / Shipyard 4.0 bending*, linking it to digital twins, IoT-enabled plate tracking, and AI-based bend prediction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bending Processes

### 2.1.1 By Temperature Regime

1. **Cold Bending** (T < 0.4 T_melt ≈ 600 °C for steel)
   - Performed at ambient temperature.
   - Uses mechanical force only (press, roller, roll bender).
   - Plate thickness typically **≤ 25 mm** for ship plates.
   - Requires **over-bending** to compensate for spring-back.

2. **Warm Bending** (600 °C – 900 °C)
   - Intermediate temperature; reduces spring-back.
   - Common for **higher-strength steels (AH36, DH36, EH36)** to avoid cold-cracking.

3. **Hot Bending** (> 900 °C)
   - Above the recrystallisation temperature.
   - Used for **thick plates (> 40 mm)** and heavily curved sections.
   - In automation → **induction heating + water cooling** replaces flame torch.

### 2.1.2 By Loading Mode

| Mode | Equipment | Output Geometry |
|---|---|---|
| **Three-point bending** | Press brake, press | Single curvature (cylindrical) |
| **Roll bending** | 3-roll / 4-roll pyramid bender | Cylindrical / conical shells |
| **Line heating** | Robotic torch / induction coil | Compound curvature (3D shape) |
| **Stretch forming** | Hydraulic stretcher | Long, gentle curves (sheer line) |
| **Die forming** | Matched dies | Repetitive standard shapes (brackets) |

## 2.2 Cold Bending Mechanics — Theory of Plastic Deformation

When a flat plate of thickness **t** is bent about a radius **R**, the outer fibre is stretched and the inner fibre is compressed. The **neutral axis** shifts towards the centre of curvature by an amount:

$$y_0 = \frac{R \, \ln(R_c / R_t)}{R_c / R_t - 1}$$

Where:
- $R_c$ = radius of compression (inner) surface
- $R_t$ = radius of tension (outer) surface
- For thin plates ($t \ll R$), $y_0 \approx t/2$ (i.e., mid-surface).

### 2.2.1 Spring-Back Compensation

When the bending moment is released, the elastic strain energy recovers a fraction of the imposed curvature. The **spring-back angle** is:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{M_p \, L}{E \, I_e}$$

Where:
- $M_p$ = plastic moment capacity = $\sigma_y \, Z_p$ (Z_p is the plastic section modulus)
- $L$ = developed (arc) length of the bend
- $E$ = Young's modulus = **210 GPa**
- $I_e$ = second moment of area (elastic)

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Engineering Insight:** In automated CNC press-brakes, the controller **pre-loads** the punch by an over-bend angle $\theta_{over} = \theta_{design} + \Delta\theta$. This eliminates spring-back error at the output, which is a hallmark of closed-loop automation.

## 2.3 Roll Bending (3-Roll Pyramid Bender) — Kinematics

A pyramid roll bender has two bottom rolls (fixed) and one top roll (adjustable). The plate passes between them, and the **top roll displacement δ** sets the curvature.

$$R = \frac{a^2 + \delta^2}{2\delta}$$

Where:
- $a$ = half the centre-to-centre distance of the bottom rolls
- $\delta$ = vertical displacement of the top roll
- $R$ = resulting bend radius (centreline)

For **multi-pass conical bending** (where $R$ varies along the length), modern CNC benders tilt the side rolls and feed the plate in a controlled helical path — this is purely a **CNC automation** capability unavailable to manual benders.

## 2.4 Line Heating (Thermal Bending) — Automation Theory

This is the **single most shipbuilding-specific** bending process and the strongest area of automation. The principle:

> Heat a narrow strip on one side of the plate → that strip tries to expand → is restrained by the cooler surrounding metal → produces **compressive plastic strain on heating** → on cooling, the strip shrinks → **bends the plate toward the heated side**.

### 2.4.1 Curvature Equation (Fundamental)

The induced curvature $\kappa$ from line heating is given by the **thermal bending formula**:

$$\kappa = \frac{\alpha \, \Delta T}{t}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion = **11.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**
- $\Delta T$ = average temperature rise of the heated strip above ambient
- $t$ = plate thickness
- $\kappa = 1 / R$ = curvature

This is derived from the **assumption of uniform through-thickness heating**; in practice a **temperature gradient** exists and the relation becomes:

$$\kappa = \frac{\alpha}{t} \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} \frac{T(y) - T_{ref}}{T_{ref}} \, dy$$

This integral form is what **automated controllers solve in real-time** using embedded thermal cameras.

### 2.4.2 Heat-Input Control

The **Rosenthal solution** for a moving point heat source on a semi-infinite plate gives peak temperature at the heat centre:

$$T_{peak} = \frac{2 \, Q}{\pi \, k \, \rho \, c_p} \cdot \frac{1}{v}$$

Simplified (line-source on thin plate):

$$T_{peak} \approx \frac{q}{2 \, \rho \, c_p \, t \, \sqrt{\pi \, \kappa_t \, \tau}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = heat input rate (W)
- $q$ = linear heat input (W/m)
- $v$ = torch travel speed (m/s)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity = **45 W/m·K**
- $\rho$ = density = **7850 kg/m³**
- $c_p$ = specific heat = **490 J/kg·K**
- $\kappa_t$ = thermal diffusivity
- $\tau$ = effective heating time at a point

### 2.4.3 Automation Architecture of Line Heating

Modern automated line-heating systems integrate:

1. **Path planning** — from 3D hull CAD model, heating lines are generated offline.
2. **Robotic carriage / gantry** — carries the torch with **6-DOF (or 7-DOF)** accuracy.
3. **Pyrometer / IR-camera feedback** — measures real-time $T_{peak}$.
4. **Adaptive controller** — adjusts $q$ and $v$ to maintain target temperature.
5. **Shape inspection post-cool** — laser scanners compare actual vs. target hull geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> A typical Japanese/Korean shipyard uses **3-axis gantry robots with induction heating** at a rate of **10–20 m²/hour** of accurately shaped plating — roughly **3× faster than skilled manual line-heaters** with superior tolerance.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\kappa = \alpha \, \Delta T / t$ | Curvature from line heating | m⁻¹ |
| 2 | $R = a^2 + \delta^2 / (2\delta)$ | 3-roll bender radius | m |
| 3 | $BA = \pi \, (R + K_t \, t) \, \theta / 180$ | Bend allowance (arc length on neutral axis) | mm |
| 4 | $R_{min} = K \, t$ | Min cold-bend radius ($K \approx 1$–$3$ for steel) | mm |
| 5 | $\Delta\theta = M_p \, L / (E \, I_e)$ | Spring-back angle | rad |
| 6 | $y_0 = R \ln(R_c / R_t) / (R_c / R_t - 1)$ | Neutral-axis shift | mm |
| 7 | $T_{peak} \approx q / (2 \rho c_p t \sqrt{\pi \kappa_t \tau})$ | Peak temperature (line heat) | °C |
| 8 | $M_p = \sigma_y \, Z_p$ | Plastic moment capacity | N·m |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_\theta = \alpha \, \Delta T - \sigma_y / E$ | Effective plastic strain on heating | — |
| 10 | $P_{press} = \sigma_y \, L \, t$ | Press brake force (approx.) | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where to use $\vert$ vs $\mid$ in exam scripts:** Always write absolute values as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ — never `|x|` — to keep markdown tables and LaTeX legible. KTU examiners prefer explicit LaTeX.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Line-Heating Curvature (Uniform Heat Strip)

**Goal:** Derive $\kappa = \alpha \, \Delta T / t$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Free Thermal Expansion (Unrestrained)

If a strip of length $L_0$ is heated uniformly by $\Delta T$ with no restraint, it expands to:

$$L = L_0 \, (1 + \alpha \, \Delta T)$$

The unrestrained thermal strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \, \Delta T$$

### Step 2 — Introduce Restraining Surrounding Plate

The surrounding (cooler) plate restrains this expansion. Mechanically, the heated strip is forced back to the original length $L_0$. This imposes a **compressive mechanical strain**:

$$\varepsilon_{mech} = - \alpha \, \Delta T$$

### Step 3 — Above Yield → Plastic Strain

If $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon_{mech}$ exceeds the yield stress $\sigma_y$, plastic deformation occurs. After cooling ($\Delta T \to 0$), the thermal strain vanishes, but the **plastic strain remains**:

$$\varepsilon_{plastic} = - \alpha \, \Delta T \quad \text{(compressive, residual)}$$

### Step 4 — Geometric Curvature from Differential Strain

For a plate of thickness $t$, place the heated surface at $y = +t/2$ and the cool surface at $y = -t/2$. The curvature $\kappa$ of the mid-surface arises from the **strain gradient**:

$$\kappa = \frac{\varepsilon_{top} - \varepsilon_{bottom}}{t}$$

After cooling, the top (heated) surface has residual plastic strain $-\alpha \Delta T$, and the bottom is unstrained ($\varepsilon = 0$). Therefore:

$$\kappa = \frac{-\alpha \, \Delta T - 0}{t} = \frac{\alpha \, \Delta T}{t}$$

$$\boxed{\kappa = \frac{\alpha \, \Delta T}{t}}$$

The sign convention indicates the plate curves **toward the heated side**, exactly as observed in shipyards.

## 3.2 Derivation of Bend Allowance (for Press-Brake Programming)

**Goal:** Compute the developed arc length $L$ along the neutral axis of a bend.

### Step 1 — Define Bend Geometry

Consider a plate of thickness $t$ bent through an angle $\theta$ (degrees) about an **inner bend radius** $R_i$. The neutral-axis radius is:

$$R_n = R_i + K_t \, t$$

Where $K_t$ is the **K-factor** (position of neutral axis as a fraction of thickness, $0 < K_t < 0.5$). For ship steel, $K_t \approx 0.33$ under typical plastic bending.

### Step 2 — Arc-Length on Neutral Axis

$$L = \frac{\pi \, R_n \, \theta}{180}$$

$$\boxed{L = \frac{\pi \, (R_i + K_t \, t) \, \theta}{180}}$$

This is the **exact length of material** that must be fed into the press-brake to produce the design angle $\theta$ after spring-back.

### Step 3 — Compensation for Spring-Back

The CNC controller actually programs an **over-angle** $\theta_{prog}$:

$$\theta_{prog} = \theta + \Delta\theta$$

Where $\Delta\theta$ is calculated by:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{M_p \, L}{E \, I_e} = \frac{\sigma_y \, Z_p \, L}{E \, I_e}$$

For a rectangular cross-section plate of width $b$, $Z_p = b t^2 / 4$ and $I_e = b t^3 / 12$. Therefore:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\sigma_y \, b \, t^2 \, L}{4 \, E \, (b t^3 / 12)} = \frac{3 \, \sigma_y \, L}{E \, t}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\theta = \frac{3 \, \sigma_y \, L}{E \, t}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (Worked)

**Problem:** A **12 mm thick** mild-steel plate (**σ_y = 235 MPa**, **E = 210 GPa**) of width **1500 mm** is bent to **90°** over an inner radius of **24 mm**. Compute (i) the bend allowance, (ii) the spring-back angle, (iii) the programmed over-angle.

**Solution (i) — Bend Allowance:**
Using $K_t = 0.33$:

$$R_n = R_i + K_t \, t = 24 + 0.33 \times 12 = 24 + 3.96 = 27.96 \text{ mm}$$

$$L = \frac{\pi \times 27.96 \times 90}{180} = 43.93 \text{ mm}$$

**Solution (ii) — Spring-Back:**

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{3 \times 235 \times 10^6 \times 43.93 \times 10^{-3}}{210 \times 10^9 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Step-by-step:

Numerator: $3 \times 235 \times 10^6 = 7.05 \times 10^8$; multiplied by $43.93 \times 10^{-3}$ gives $\approx 3.097 \times 10^7$.

Denominator: $210 \times 10^9 \times 12 \times 10^{-3} = 2.52 \times 10^9$.

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{3.097 \times 10^7}{2.52 \times 10^9} = 0.01229 \text{ rad}$$

Convert to degrees:

$$\Delta\theta_{deg} = 0.01229 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.704°$$

**Solution (iii) — Programmed Angle:**

$$\theta_{prog} = 90° + 0.704° = 90.704°$$

## 3.3 Derivation of 3-Roll Bending Radius

**Goal:** Show $R = (a^2 + \delta^2) / (2\delta)$ geometrically.

### Step 1 — Geometry of Contact

Consider two bottom rolls separated by $2a$ (centre-to-centre). The top roll pushes down by $\delta$ from the level of the bottom-roll tops.

### Step 2 — Curvature from Two-Point Contact

The plate touches the bottom rolls at two points and the top roll at one point (mid-span). The mid-point deflection $\delta$ corresponds to a circular arc. Using the **sagitta (versine) formula**:

$$R = \frac{a^2}{2\delta} + \frac{\delta}{2} = \frac{a^2 + \delta^2}{2\delta}$$

For small $\delta \ll a$:

$$R \approx \frac{a^2}{2\delta}$$

This shows that **small displacements produce large radii** (gentle curves) and large displacements produce tight curves.

### Step 3 — Sensitivity Check

$$\frac{dR}{d\delta} = -\frac{a^2 - \delta^2}{2\delta^2} \approx -\frac{a^2}{2\delta^2}$$

The negative sign means: increasing $\delta$ **decreases** $R$ (tighter bend). The sensitivity is **non-linear**, which is why CNC automation with **real-time laser gauging** of $R$ is essential.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Line Heating

**Problem:** A **20 mm thick** mild-steel deck plate is to be curved to a radius of **8 m** by line heating. Compute the required temperature rise (uniform through thickness assumption).

**Solution:**

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Rearranging $\kappa = \alpha \Delta T / t$:

$$\Delta T = \frac{\kappa \, t}{\alpha} = \frac{0.125 \times 0.020}{11.6 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{11.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 215.5 \text{ °C}$$

Therefore, heating the strip to **~215 °C** above ambient (i.e., about **235 °C absolute plate temperature**) will produce an **8 m radius** — well within the range achievable by **automated oxy-fuel or induction heating systems**.
