# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

### Question 1
**Define automation of bending in ship construction. List any two advantages of automated line heating over traditional hand line heating.**

**Model Answer:**
Automation of bending in ship construction is the use of CNC-controlled machines, robotic manipulators, and sensor-based feedback loops to perform plastic deformation of steel plates and sections into the curved shapes required for the ship hull, replacing manual hammer/torch work.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Dimensional accuracy:** Automated systems achieve ±1 mm/m tolerance vs. ±5 mm/m for manual line heating.
2. **Productivity:** Robotic line-heating works at 10–20 m²/hr, approximately three times faster than skilled manual operators.
3. (Bonus) **Ergonomics / safety:** No exposure of workers to heat, fumes, and noise.

---

### Question 2
**State the thermal-bending curvature formula used in automated line heating and explain the role of temperature rise (ΔT) and plate thickness (t).**

**Model Answer:**
The thermal-bending curvature is given by $\kappa = \alpha \Delta T / t$, where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion of steel (≈ 11.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C).

- **ΔT:** Higher temperature rise produces greater shrinkage strain on cooling, hence tighter (smaller-radius) curvature. The relationship is **linear**.
- **t:** Thicker plates require proportionally higher temperature to achieve the same curvature, because the bending strain distributes over more material. Doubling the plate thickness doubles the required ΔT for the same radius.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern, ESE Module Internal Choice]**

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a **CNC 3-roll pyramid plate bending machine**. Derive the relation between the top-roll displacement and the resulting bend radius. A steel plate of width 1500 mm and thickness 16 mm is bent between two bottom rolls placed 600 mm apart (centre-to-centre). If the top roll is pressed down by 35 mm, determine the bend radius produced. Assume $K_t = 0.33$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **automation architecture** of a modern robotic line-heating system used in shipbuilding. Include a block diagram and discuss the role of IR pyrometer feedback.

#### Solution to (a):

**Working Principle:** In a 3-roll pyramid bender, the plate passes between two fixed bottom rolls and one adjustable top roll. The downward displacement of the top roll imposes a curvature. Multiple passes or pre-formed end-clamps allow cylindrical, conical, and reverse-curvature shapes.

**Derivation:** As shown in Section 3.3,

$$R = \frac{a^2 + \delta^2}{2\delta}$$

**Numerical Evaluation:**
Given: $2a = 600$ mm → $a = 300$ mm; $\delta = 35$ mm.

$$R = \frac{(300)^2 + (35)^2}{2 \times 35} = \frac{90000 + 1225}{70} = \frac{91225}{70} = 1303.2 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 1.303 \text{ m}}$$

Check for minimum-radius rule: $R_{min} = 3 \times t = 48$ mm; 1303 mm >> 48 mm ⇒ **safe cold bend.**

#### Solution to (b):

A modern robotic line-heating system consists of:

1. **CAD input & path planning** — Hull CAD model → heat line patterns extracted → tool-path generated offline.
2. **6-DOF robotic arm / gantry** — Carries the induction coil (or oxy-fuel torch) with sub-mm repeatability.
3. **Heating source** — Either **induction coil** (clean, fast, no flame) or **oxy-fuel torch** (traditional but automated).
4. **Real-time IR pyrometer / thermal camera** — Measures the surface temperature field during heating.
5. **Adaptive CNC controller** — Adjusts heat input $q$ and travel speed $v$ to keep the peak temperature at the design value.
6. **Closed-loop feedback** — If $T_{measured} > T_{target}$, controller reduces $q$ or increases $v$, and vice versa.
7. **Post-process inspection** — Laser scanner compares actual plate to CAD; deviation triggers re-bending.

**Role of IR Pyrometer:** Provides **non-contact, real-time** temperature measurement across the heated strip. Used as the **feedback signal** in the closed loop. Typical accuracy: ±10 °C at 800 °C. Modern systems use **multi-point pyrometer arrays** to capture the temperature gradient through thickness indirectly.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with necessary derivation the principle of **thermal bending by line heating**. A 25 mm thick steel plate is to be curved to a radius of 5 m. Compute the temperature rise required, given $\alpha = 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C. Discuss how the temperature can be achieved and controlled in an automated system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **cold bending, warm bending, and hot bending** in terms of process parameters, equipment, plate thickness range, and suitability for automation. Mention the standards (e.g., IACS S21) governing such operations in modern shipyards.

#### Solution to (a):

**Principle:** Heat applied to one side of the plate causes local thermal expansion. The surrounding cooler material restrains this expansion, generating compressive plastic strain. On cooling, the heated zone shrinks, bending the plate towards the heated side.

**Derivation:** As shown in Section 3.1,

$$\kappa = \frac{\alpha \, \Delta T}{t}$$

**Numerical Evaluation:**
Given: $t = 25$ mm = 0.025 m; $R = 5$ m → $\kappa = 1/5 = 0.2$ m⁻¹.

$$\Delta T = \frac{\kappa \, t}{\alpha} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.025}{11.6 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{11.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 431.03 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T \approx 431 \text{ °C}}$$

To achieve this from ambient (say 25 °C), the heated strip must reach ~456 °C — within the capability of **oxy-fuel** or **induction heating** systems.

**Control in Automation:** The CNC controller monitors real-time temperature using **IR pyrometers** and modulates **heat input rate** $Q$ and **travel speed** $v$ to maintain the target temperature profile. PID controllers or model-predictive control (MPC) algorithms are common.

#### Solution to (b):

| Parameter | Cold Bending | Warm Bending | Hot Bending |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | < 600 °C | 600 – 900 °C | > 900 °C |
| **Equipment** | Press brake, 3-roll bender | Induction heater + press | Furnace + die press |
| **Plate thickness** | ≤ 25 mm | 25–40 mm | > 40 mm |
| **Automation level** | Very high (CNC press brake) | Medium-high (induction + robot) | Medium (furnace handling) |
| **Spring-back** | Significant (over-bend needed) | Low | Negligible |
| **Surface quality** | Excellent | Good (oxide scale) | Moderate |
| **Suitable steels** | Mild (Grade A) | AH/DH 36 | EH36, thick plates |

**Governing Standards:**
- **IACS S21** — Hull form tolerances and bending accuracy.
- **IACS S22** — Welding and forming procedures for high-strength steels.
- **ISO 9001:2015** — Quality management system for bending processes.
- **AWS D1.1 / D1.5** — Forming procedures for bridges & marine structures (reference).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas (KTU Board Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Units error in $\Delta T$ calculation:** Mixing $t$ in mm and $R$ in m without unit conversion. Always convert to SI (metres) before substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting spring-back compensation:** A student who quotes the design angle directly into the press-brake program without $\Delta\theta$ loses **2 marks minimum** in any 14-mark question.
> 3. **Missing closed-loop explanation:** In questions on automation architecture, examiners award marks specifically for mentioning *sensor → controller → actuator* feedback. Drawing the loop is essential.
> 4. **Confusing $R$ (centreline) vs $R_i$ (inner radius):** The bend allowance formula requires **neutral-axis** radius, not inner or centreline radius unless explicitly stated.
> 5. **Ignoring $\alpha$ value for the correct material:** Mild steel $\alpha = 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C is the standard. Using the wrong constant costs marks.
> 6. **Not relating theory to ship context:** KTU 2024 examiners award marks for **application statements** — e.g., "this is used for bilge strakes" or "this tolerance satisfies IACS S21". Don't write in generic mechanical-engineering language.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Automation of Bending**

**Core Concepts**
- Automation = CAD/CAM + robotic actuators + sensor feedback + adaptive control.
- The **thermal-bending curvature equation** $\kappa = \alpha \Delta T / t$ is the single most-tested formula.
- **Cold bending** is governed by plastic deformation; **spring-back** must be compensated.
- **Roll bending** radius follows $R = (a^2 + \delta^2) / (2\delta)$.
- **Press-brake bend allowance** uses neutral-axis radius: $L = \pi (R_i + K_t t) \theta / 180$.

**Automation-Specific**
- **Closed-loop control** distinguishes automation from mechanisation.
- **IR pyrometer / thermal camera** is the temperature sensor of choice for line heating.
- **Digital twins** link CAD hull model → bend program → real-time quality control.
- **Induction heating** is replacing oxy-fuel for cleaner, faster, and safer automation.

**Standards & Tolerances**
- Hull plate tolerance: **±1 mm/m** (modern automated yard).
- Min cold-bend radius: **R ≥ 2t** (mild steel); **R ≥ 3t** (high-strength steel).
- Line-heat peak temperature: **600–900 °C**.
- Material: shipbuilding Grade A / AH36 / DH36 / EH36.

**Numerical Knobs**
- $\alpha$ for steel = **11.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**
- $E$ for steel = **210 GPa**
- $\sigma_y$ for Grade A = **235 MPa**
- Plate thickness range = **6 mm – 80 mm**

**One-Sentence Definition to Memorise:**
> *Automation of bending is the CNC-controlled, sensor-guided plastic deformation of steel plates and sections into compound-curved hull geometries with sub-millimetre repeatability, replacing manual hammer/torch craft with robotic line-heating and roll-bending cells.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plate Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plate Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In ship structural engineering, a **plate** is a flat, thin structural element whose thickness ($h$) is small compared to its in-plane dimensions (length $a$ and breadth $b$). **Plate bending** refers to the transverse deflection and associated flexural stresses induced in such a plate when it is loaded perpendicular to its middle surface by transverse loads such as hydrostatic water pressure, cargo loads, or sea-wave slamming forces.

Mathematically, the out-of-plane deflection $w(x, y, t)$ of the plate's middle surface is governed by the **biharmonic (Kirchhoff–Love) plate equation**:

$$D \nabla^{4} w = q(x, y, t)$$

where:
- $D = \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12(1 - \nu^{2})}$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate (in $\text{N·m}$),
- $E$ is Young's modulus (typically **$\mathbf{2.1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{MPa}}$** for shipbuilding steel),
- $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio (typically **$\mathbf{0.30}$** for steel),
- $h$ is plate thickness,
- $q$ is the distributed transverse load (Pa),
- $\nabla^{4} = \dfrac{\partial^{4}}{\partial x^{4}} + 2\dfrac{\partial^{4}}{\partial x^{2}\partial y^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{4}}{\partial y^{4}}$ is the biharmonic operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Plate bending is fundamental to the strength assessment of hull plating, deck plating, bulkheads, and superstructure panels. Classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS) prescribe plate-thickness rules derived from plate-bending theory.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a **kitchen steel tray** (a thin plate) when you press your palm down on it — it bends, the centre sags, and the edges curl up. Now compare it to a **thick wooden chopping board** — it barely bends at all under the same load. The difference lies in the **flexural rigidity $D$**, which scales with the **cube of thickness $h^{3}$**. Double the thickness, and the plate becomes **eight times stiffer** in bending.

In a ship, the hull plating behaves exactly like that tray. The **sea water** on one side and the **internal cargo or air** on the other exert a pressure difference (often called **head pressure** or **external pressure**) that makes the plate bend between the supporting frames and stiffeners. The frames are the "hands holding the tray from below" — they support the plate edges and prevent excessive sagging.

> [!NOTE]
> A useful everyday analogy: imagine a **bookshelf shelf loaded with books**. The wooden shelf is a plate. It bends under the weight of the books. If you add a **central stiffener (a wooden batten glued underneath)**, the same shelf carries far more weight because the bending is reduced — this is the principle behind **stiffened plating** in ships.

### 1.3 Why Plate Bending Matters in Ships

Ships are essentially **large box-girder structures** made of steel plating supported by a framework of longitudinals, transverses, frames, and girders. Almost every structural element in the ship is a **plate (possibly stiffened) acting in bending**:

- **Bottom shell plating** → bends under hydrostatic pressure and slamming loads.
- **Side shell plating** → bends under external water pressure.
- **Deck plating** → bends under cargo, machinery, or weather loads.
- **Bulkhead plating** → bends under head of liquid in tanks (sloshing, filling).
- **Superstructure side plating** → bends under wind and accommodation loads.

Hence, the ability to predict **deflection, bending stress, and shear force** in plates is a cornerstone of ship structural analysis and is a direct KTU 2024 Scheme learning outcome.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants to Memorise (for KTU numericals):**
> - $E_{\text{steel}} = 2.1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/mm}^{2}$
> - $\nu_{\text{steel}} = 0.30$
> - Density of sea water $\rho = 1.025\ \text{t/m}^{3}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$
> - Hydrostatic pressure: $p = \rho g h$ (in kPa when $h$ is in metres)

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Kirchhoff–Love Plate Theory — Underlying Assumptions

The classical plate-bending theory rests on the following assumptions, all of which are valid for ship plating because the plate thickness is **much smaller** than its in-plane dimensions (typically $h/a \le 1/100$):

1. The **straight line normal to the undeformed middle surface** remains **straight, inextensible, and normal** to the deformed middle surface after bending (no transverse shear deformation).
2. The **middle surface does not stretch** in the plane (no membrane action considered in pure bending).
3. **Plane sections originally normal to the middle surface remain plane** but rotate by a small angle.
4. The plate material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's law applies).
5. **Deflections are small** compared to plate thickness — geometric linearity is assumed.
6. The plate is **initially flat** and unloaded transverse stresses are negligible.

> [!NOTE]
> For very thin plates under high compressive in-plane loads, the plate may undergo **buckling**, and the above linear theory must be replaced by **large-deflection (von Kármán) plate theory** — important for ultra-thin plate panels between closely spaced stiffeners.

### 2.2 Internal Force Resultants

The plate-bending stress resultants per unit length (so that the actual stress = resultants × stress-couple arms) are derived from integration of the bending stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ through the thickness $h$. The standard result is:

- Bending moments per unit length:

$$M_{x} = -D\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} + \nu \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\right)$$

$$M_{y} = -D\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}} + \nu \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\right)$$

- Twisting moment per unit length:

$$M_{xy} = -D(1 - \nu)\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\, \partial y}$$

- Effective transverse shear forces per unit length (from the equilibrium of an element):

$$Q_{x} = -D \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\nabla^{2} w\right)$$

$$Q_{y} = -D \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\nabla^{2} w\right)$$

- **Reactive (Kirchhoff) edge force** at a boundary $x = \text{const}$:

$$V_{x} = Q_{x} + \frac{\partial M_{xy}}{\partial y}$$

These four bending/twisting moments and two shear forces completely characterise the bending state of the plate.

### 2.3 Maximum Bending Stress

The extreme fibre bending stresses occur at $z = \pm h/2$ and are:

$$\sigma_{x,\max} = \pm \frac{6 M_{x}}{h^{2}} = \pm \frac{6 D}{h^{2}}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} + \nu \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\right)$$

$$\sigma_{y,\max} = \pm \frac{6 M_{y}}{h^{2}}$$

The factor $6/h^{2}$ reduces to the familiar $1/Z$ where $Z = h^{2}/6$ is the section modulus per unit width.

### 2.4 Aspect Ratio, Slenderness, and Plate Classification

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Ship Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aspect ratio | $\lambda = a/b$ | Span-to-width ratio of supported panel | $1$ to $3$ |
| Slenderness (width-thickness) ratio | $\beta = b/h$ | Width divided by thickness | $40$ to $150$ |
| Span-thickness ratio | $s = a/h$ | Span divided by thickness | $50$ to $400$ |

- **Long plate** $\Rightarrow \lambda \ge 2$ — bending is essentially one-way (cylindrical bending).
- **Square plate** $\Rightarrow \lambda = 1$ — full two-way bending.
- **Wide plate** $\Rightarrow \lambda \le 0.5$ — behaviour approaches a beam-strip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The term "aspect ratio" sometimes refers to $a/b$ and sometimes to $b/a$ in different texts. Always check the convention by referring to the support distance (span) first. For a plate between two frames, the span is the **smaller** spacing.

### 2.5 Edge / Boundary Conditions

Real ship plates are supported along their edges by frames, longitudinals, or bulkheads. The three classical edge conditions are:

| Edge Condition | Deflection $w$ | Slope $\partial w/\partial n$ | Bending Moment $M_{n}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clamped (built-in / fixed)** | $0$ | $0$ | Free (non-zero) |
| **Simply supported (pinned)** | $0$ | Free (non-zero) | $0$ |
| **Free edge** | Free | Free | $0$ + $V = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ship practice:** A plate edge welded to a deep frame is best modelled as **clamped**. A plate edge welded to a flat-bar stiffener with no torsional rigidity is best modelled as **simply supported**. A free edge occurs at a hatch opening or a free deck edge.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory equations for the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).** Memorise these and their physical meaning.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12(1 - \nu^{2})}$ | Flexural rigidity per unit width | $\text{N·m}$ |
| 2 | $D \nabla^{4} w = q$ | Plate-bending governing PDE | — |
| 3 | $M_{x} = -D\!\left(w_{,xx} + \nu w_{,yy}\right)$ | Bending moment per unit width about $y$-axis | $\text{N·m/m}$ |
| 4 | $M_{y} = -D\!\left(w_{,yy} + \nu w_{,xx}\right)$ | Bending moment per unit width about $x$-axis | $\text{N·m/m}$ |
| 5 | $M_{xy} = -D(1 - \nu) w_{,xy}$ | Twisting moment per unit width | $\text{N·m/m}$ |
| 6 | $Q_{x} = -D\,\partial_{x}(\nabla^{2} w)$ | Effective shear per unit width in $x$ | $\text{N/m}$ |
| 7 | $V_{x} = Q_{x} + \partial_{y} M_{xy}$ | Kirchhoff edge reaction | $\text{N/m}$ |
| 8 | $w_{\max} = \alpha_{w}\dfrac{q a^{4}}{D}$ | Centre deflection of SS rectangular plate | m |
| 9 | $M_{\max} = \alpha_{m}\, q a^{2}$ | Max. moment of SS rectangular plate | $\text{N·m/m}$ |
| 10 | $\sigma_{x,\max} = \dfrac{6 M_{x}}{h^{2}}$ | Extreme fibre stress | Pa |
| 11 | $p_{\text{hydro}} = \rho g H$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $H$ | Pa |
| 12 | $t_{\min} = s\sqrt{\dfrac{p}{k\,\sigma_{\text{allow}}}}$ | Empirical plate thickness rule (s = span factor) | m |
| 13 | $\lambda = a/b$ | Aspect ratio | — |
| 14 | $\beta = b/h$ | Slenderness (width-thickness) ratio | — |
| 15 | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{k\, \pi^{2} D}{b^{2} h}$ | Elastic buckling pressure | Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> The coefficients $\alpha_{w}$ and $\alpha_{m}$ depend on the **aspect ratio $a/b$ and edge conditions**. Their numerical values are tabulated in standard texts (Timoshenko & Woinowsky-Krieger, *Theory of Plates and Shells*). For the KTU ESE, the lecturer will typically provide a small table or expect you to recall the most common cases ($a/b = 1.0$ or $a/b = 1.5$).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Classification-society rules** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, IRS) express minimum required plate thickness as $t = s \cdot \sqrt{p / (C \cdot \sigma_{\text{allow}})}$ where $C$ is a coefficient that accounts for aspect ratio and edge fixity — directly derived from plate-bending theory.
- **Finite-Element ship structural analysis** (e.g., using **MAESTRO**, **SESAM**, **NAPA**, or **ANSYS**) idealises the hull as an assembly of **shell elements (plate elements)** whose stiffness matrix comes from the very plate-bending equation shown above.
- **Slamming and whipping** assessments use the dynamic plate-bending equation $D \nabla^{4} w + \rho h \ddot{w} = q(t)$ to predict transient response.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Plate-Bending Equation $D \nabla^{4} w = q$

We derive this carefully because it is the single most important equation in the topic and frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions.

**Step 1 — Strain–displacement relations (kinematics).**

By the Kirchhoff hypothesis, a point at height $z$ above the middle surface has displacements:

$$u(x, y, z) = -z\,\frac{\partial w}{\partial x},\qquad v(x, y, z) = -z\,\frac{\partial w}{\partial y},\qquad w(x, y, z) = w(x, y)$$

The normal strain components are therefore:

$$\varepsilon_{x} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -z\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} = -z\, w_{,xx}$$

$$\varepsilon_{y} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -z\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}} = -z\, w_{,yy}$$

The shear strain is:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = -2z\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\,\partial y} = -2z\, w_{,xy}$$

**Step 2 — Stress–strain relations (constitutive law for isotropic plane stress).**

The plate is in a state of plane stress ($\sigma_{z} = 0$) and the material is linear elastic:

$$\sigma_{x} = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^{2}}\left(\varepsilon_{x} + \nu \varepsilon_{y}\right) = -\frac{E\, z}{1 - \nu^{2}}\!\left(w_{,xx} + \nu\, w_{,yy}\right)$$

$$\sigma_{y} = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^{2}}\left(\varepsilon_{y} + \nu \varepsilon_{x}\right) = -\frac{E\, z}{1 - \nu^{2}}\!\left(w_{,yy} + \nu\, w_{,xx}\right)$$

$$\tau_{xy} = G\,\gamma_{xy} = -\frac{E\, z}{1 + \nu}\, w_{,xy} = -\frac{E\, z}{1 - \nu^{2}}\,(1 - \nu)\, w_{,xy}$$

(using $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$).

**Step 3 — Define bending and twisting moment resultants.**

Integrating the stress distributions through the thickness from $z = -h/2$ to $z = +h/2$:

$$M_{x} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_{x}\, z\, dz = -\frac{E}{1 - \nu^{2}}\!\left(w_{,xx} + \nu\, w_{,yy}\right)\!\int_{-h/2}^{h/2} z^{2}\, dz$$

The integral of $z^{2}$ is the second moment of area per unit width $I = h^{3}/12$. Substituting:

$$M_{x} = -D\!\left(w_{,xx} + \nu\, w_{,yy}\right),\quad D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1 - \nu^{2})}$$

By identical procedure:

$$M_{y} = -D\!\left(w_{,yy} + \nu\, w_{,xx}\right)$$

$$M_{xy} = -D(1 - \nu)\, w_{,xy}$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium of an infinitesimal plate element.**

Consider a plate element of sides $dx, dy$. The moments on the $x$-face vary, producing a net moment about the $y$-axis, and adding the load $q$ on the top, summing moments yields the **two moment-equilibrium equations**:

$$\frac{\partial M_{x}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial M_{xy}}{\partial y} = Q_{x}$$

$$\frac{\partial M_{y}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial M_{xy}}{\partial x} = Q_{y}$$

**Step 5 — Force equilibrium in the $z$-direction.**

Summing forces perpendicular to the plate:

$$\frac{\partial Q_{x}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial Q_{y}}{\partial y} + q = 0$$

**Step 6 — Eliminate the shears by substitution.**

Substitute the moment expressions (Step 3) into Step 4, then the resulting $Q_{x}, Q_{y}$ into Step 5:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} M_{x}}{\partial x^{2}} + 2\,\frac{\partial^{2} M_{xy}}{\partial x\,\partial y} + \frac{\partial^{2} M_{y}}{\partial y^{2}} = -q$$

Now substitute $M_{x}, M_{y}, M_{xy}$:

$$-D\!\left(w_{,xxxx} + 2 w_{,xxyy} + w_{,yyyy}\right) = -q$$

$$\boxed{\,D\!\left(\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + 2\,\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2}\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}}\right) = q\,}$$

which is the **Kirchhoff–Love plate-bending equation** in compact form:

$$D \nabla^{4} w = q \quad \text{where} \quad \nabla^{4} = \frac{\partial^{4}}{\partial x^{4}} + 2\frac{\partial^{4}}{\partial x^{2}\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4}}{\partial y^{4}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** This derivation in six steps is a guaranteed 14-mark question. The marker expects the three classical laws (kinematics, constitutive, equilibrium) to be invoked — leaving out any one will cost 4–5 marks.

### 3.2 Closed-Form Solution: Navier Solution for a Simply Supported Rectangular Plate Under Uniform Load

This is the classic KTU 14-mark application problem. Given a rectangular plate $0 \le x \le a$, $0 \le y \le b$, **simply supported on all four edges** and loaded by a uniform pressure $q$ (e.g., hydrostatic water head), find the deflection $w(x, y)$.

**Step 1 — Boundary conditions (simply supported).**

$$w = 0 \ \text{on all four edges (no deflection at support)}$$

$$M_{x} = 0 \ \text{at}\ x = 0, a \quad\Rightarrow\quad w_{,xx} + \nu\, w_{,yy} = 0 \ \text{at}\ x = 0, a$$

$$M_{y} = 0 \ \text{at}\ y = 0, b \quad\Rightarrow\quad w_{,yy} + \nu\, w_{,xx} = 0 \ \text{at}\ y = 0, b$$

**Step 2 — Use a double sine series (Navier's method) for $q$ and $w$.**

Expand the uniform load as:

$$q(x, y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} q_{mn} \sin\!\frac{m\pi x}{a}\sin\!\frac{m\pi y}{b}$$

where the double Fourier coefficient for a uniform load is:

$$q_{mn} = \frac{4 q}{mn\pi^{2}}\quad \text{for odd } m, n;\quad q_{mn} = 0 \ \text{for even } m \text{ or } n$$

Assume a similar expansion for $w$:

$$w(x, y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} W_{mn} \sin\!\frac{m\pi x}{a}\sin\!\frac{n\pi y}{b}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the plate equation.**

Each term of the double series satisfies $\nabla^{4}\!\left[\sin\frac{m\pi x}{a}\sin\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right] = \pi^{4}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{\!2}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{a}\sin\frac{n\pi y}{b}$

Substituting and equating coefficients of the independent modes:

$$D\, \pi^{4}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{\!2} W_{mn} = q_{mn}$$

$$W_{mn} = \frac{4 q}{D \pi^{6} m n}\cdot\frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \dfrac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{\!2}}\quad (\text{odd } m, n)$$

**Step 4 — Maximum centre deflection (at $x = a/2$, $y = b/2$).**

Substituting $x = a/2,\ y = b/2$ so that $\sin(m\pi/2)\sin(n\pi/2) = (\pm 1)(\pm 1)$ and only odd $m, n$ survive, and the coefficients of alternating sign sum (with all positive values since $W_{mn} > 0$ and the sines are $\pm 1$):

$$w_{\max} = \frac{4 q}{\pi^{6} D}\sum_{m=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{(m+n)/2 - 1}}{m n\!\left(\dfrac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \dfrac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{\!2}}$$

For a **square plate** ($a = b$), the series converges rapidly and the leading term ($m = n = 1$) gives an excellent approximation:

$$w_{\max} \approx \frac{4 q\, a^{4}}{D \pi^{6}}\cdot\frac{1}{1 \cdot 1 \cdot (2/a^{2})^{2}} = \frac{4 q\, a^{4}}{D \pi^{6}}\cdot\frac{a^{4}}{4} = \frac{q\, a^{4}}{D \pi^{6}} \cdot a^{4}$$

Carrying out the full numerical series (Timoshenko's tables), the exact result is:

$$\boxed{\,w_{\max,\,a=b} = 0.00406\,\frac{q a^{4}}{D}\,}$$

For a **rectangular plate** with $a > b$, define $\lambda = a/b$. The general result is tabulated as:

$$w_{\max} = \alpha_{w}(\lambda)\,\frac{q a^{4}}{D}$$

| Aspect ratio $a/b$ | $1.0$ | $1.5$ | $2.0$ | $\infty$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha_{w}$ | $0.00406$ | $0.00772$ | $0.01013$ | $0.01302$ |
| $\alpha_{m}$ (max. moment coefficient) | $0.0479$ | $0.0812$ | $0.1017$ | $0.1250$ |

The last column ($a/b \to \infty$) corresponds to **cylindrical bending of a long strip**, recovering the simple beam formula $w_{\max} = 5qa^{4}/(384 D) = 0.01302\,qa^{4}/D$. ✓

**Step 5 — Maximum bending moment and stress.**

The maximum bending moment per unit width is:

$$M_{\max} = \alpha_{m}(\lambda)\, q\, a^{2}$$

The maximum bending stress in the plate is:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{6 M_{\max}}{h^{2}} = \frac{6 \alpha_{m}\, q\, a^{2}}{h^{2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** If $a/b \to \infty$, the plate behaves like a beam of span $a$ loaded by $q$ per unit length (because the load is now $q$ per unit area × unit width = $q$ per unit length). The simple beam maximum moment is $M = qa^{2}/8 = 0.125\,qa^{2}$, which matches $\alpha_{m}(\infty) = 0.1250$. ✓

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem (KTU Model):** A bottom-shell plate panel in a ship has dimensions $a = 1.2\ \text{m}$ (longitudinal span between floors) by $b = 0.8\ \text{m}$ (transverse span between longitudinals), thickness $h = 12\ \text{mm}$. The plate is made of mild steel ($E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{MPa}$, $\nu = 0.30$) and is subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure $q = 50\ \text{kPa}$ from the sea. Assuming all four edges are **simply supported**, find:
> (a) the flexural rigidity $D$,
> (b) the maximum centre deflection $w_{\max}$,
> (c) the maximum bending moment per unit width $M_{\max}$,
> (d) the maximum bending stress $\sigma_{\max}$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Flexural rigidity.**

$$D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1 - \nu^{2})} = \frac{2.1\times 10^{11}\ \text{Pa}\times (0.012)^{3}\ \text{m}^{3}}{12(1 - 0.3^{2})}$$

Numerator: $2.1\times 10^{11}\times 1.728\times 10^{-6} = 3.6288\times 10^{5}\ \text{N·m}$
Denominator: $12(1 - 0.09) = 12\times 0.91 = 10.92$

$$D = \frac{3.6288\times 10^{5}}{10.92} = 3.323\times 10^{4}\ \text{N·m} = 33.23\ \text{kN·m}$$

**(b) Maximum centre deflection.**

Aspect ratio: $\lambda = a/b = 1.2/0.8 = 1.5$. From the table, $\alpha_{w}(1.5) = 0.00772$.

$$w_{\max} = 0.00772 \times \frac{q a^{4}}{D} = 0.00772 \times \frac{50\,000\ \text{Pa}\times (1.2)^{4}\ \text{m}^{4}}{3.323\times 10^{4}\ \text{N·m}}$$

$1.2^{4} = 2.0736$, so $q a^{4} = 50\,000 \times 2.0736 = 1.0368\times 10^{5}\ \text{N·m}^{3}$.

$$w_{\max} = 0.00772 \times \frac{1.0368\times 10^{5}}{3.323\times 10^{4}} = 0.00772 \times 3.120 = 0.0241\ \text{m} = 24.1\ \text{mm}$$

**(c) Maximum bending moment.**

$\alpha_{m}(1.5) = 0.0812$.

$$M_{\max} = 0.0812 \times q a^{2} = 0.0812 \times 50\,000 \times 1.44 = 5.846 \times 10^{3}\ \text{N·m/m} = 5.85\ \text{kN·m/m}$$

**(d) Maximum bending stress.**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{6 M_{\max}}{h^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 5846\ \text{N·m/m}}{(0.012\ \text{m})^{2}} = \frac{35\,076}{1.44\times 10^{-4}} = 2.436\times 10^{8}\ \text{Pa} = 243.6\ \text{MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Validation Pitfall:** The resulting $\sigma_{\max} = 243.6\ \text{MPa}$ **exceeds the yield stress of mild steel (≈ 235 MPa)**. This means the chosen plate is **undersized**! In a real KTU answer, the student should comment on this — full marks require the **engineering interpretation** ("plate is inadequate; thickness must be increased to at least 13 mm to satisfy $\sigma \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$").

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Plate Behaviour for Common Boundary Conditions

| Edge Conditions | $w_{\max}$ Coefficient $\alpha_{w}$ | $M_{\max}$ Coefficient $\alpha_{m}$ | Deflection-to-Span Ratio $w_{\max}/a$ at $q = 10\,\text{kPa}, a = 1\,\text{m}, h = 10\,\text{mm}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| SS on all 4 edges ($a = b$) | $0.00406$ | $0.0479$ | $0.0042$ |
| SS on all 4 edges ($a/b = 2$) | $0.01013$ | $0.1017$ | $0.0104$ |
| Clamped on all 4 edges ($a = b$) | $0.00126$ | $0.0229$ | $0.0013$ |
| Two opposite edges SS, two free ($a = b$) | $0.01302$ | $0.1250$ | $0.0134$ |
| Cantilever (two opposite edges clamped, two free) | $0.12500$ | $0.5000$ | $0.1280$ |

> [!TIP]
> The matrix shows the **dramatic effect of edge fixity**: a clamped square plate deflects only **one-third** of a simply supported one. In ship design, this is the reason deep frames and girders are preferred — they "clamp" the plate edges and reduce deflection and stress.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Plate Bending Analysis Workflow

This flowchart represents the **engineering workflow** a naval architect follows to solve a plate-bending problem, from identifying the panel to designing the stiffening scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Ship Plate Panel Identified]) --> stepB[Step 1: Gather Geometric Inputs a, b, h]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Identify Edge Conditions Clamped / SS / Free]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Determine Transverse Load q Pressure, Cargo, Wave]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Compute Flexural Rigidity D = Eh cubed over 12 times 1 minus nu squared]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Solve Plate Bending Equation D Laplacian to the fourth w equals q]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Extract Field Quantities w, Mx, My, sigma]
    stepG --> stepH{Step 7: Check sigma max versus sigma allow}
    stepH -- OK --> stepI[Step 8: Compute Deflection w versus span ratio]
    stepH -- Exceeds --> stepJ[Step 9: Increase Plate Thickness h]
    stepI --> stepK{Step 10: w within serviceability limit}
    stepK -- OK --> stepL[Step 11: Design OK]
    stepK -- Exceeds --> stepM[Step 12: Add Stiffeners to Reduce Effective Span]
    stepJ --> stepE
    stepM --> stepL
    stepL --> endN([End: Finalised Plate Stiffening Plan])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** Begin at `startA` with the plate panel. After each computational block (`stepB` through `stepG`), a decision diamond (`stepH`, `stepK`) checks against KTU/class-rule limits. The loop `stepJ → stepE` represents **iterative design refinement** — exactly the iterative logic a classification-society rule (e.g., IRS Pt.3 Ch.2) implements when it asks you to recalculate thickness until $\sigma \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$.

### 4.2 Schematic Representation of Plate-Bending Internal Forces

The following diagram depicts the **internal bending and twisting moments and transverse shear forces** acting on a small plate element. Although it cannot be drawn photographically, the **Mermaid block-diagram** captures the directional sense and naming convention of each stress resultant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    elemA["Plate Element dx by dy"] --> mdir["Mx acts on x-face, bending about y-axis"]
    elemA --> mdir2["My acts on y-face, bending about x-axis"]
    elemA --> mdir3["Mxy acts on both faces, twisting"]
    elemA --> sdir["Qx on x-face, transverse shear"]
    elemA --> sdir2["Qy on y-face, transverse shear"]
    elemA --> qdir["q distributed load on top face"]
    mdir --> stress["sigma x max = 6 Mx over h squared"]
    mdir2 --> stress2["sigma y max = 6 My over h squared"]
    mdir3 --> stress3["tau xy max = 6 Mxy over h squared"]
    sdir --> shear["Reactive edge force Vx = Qx plus partial derivative Mxy with respect to y"]
    sdir2 --> shear2["Reactive edge force Vy = Qy plus partial derivative Mxy with respect to x"]
    qdir --> eqbm["Equilibrium: partial Qx over partial x plus partial Qy over partial y plus q equals zero"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading hint:** In the KTU exam, when you are asked to "sketch the internal forces on a plate element", you only need to show the **six arrows** (two $M_x$, two $M_y$, four $M_{xy}$, two $Q_x$, two $Q_y$) on a small square — labelled clockwise/anticlockwise consistently. The exact schematic in the Mermaid block above is the formal reference.

### 4.3 Physical Picture: Plate Bending in a Ship Hull

Although Mermaid cannot render a 3-D hull, the **block-level interaction map** below summarises the dominant physical picture: the hull plate is loaded by sea pressure and supported by an orthogonal grillage of frames and longitudinals.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sea["Sea Water: Hydrostatic Pressure p = rho g H"] -->|Acts outward| hullplating["Hull Plate: 2D Plate Element"]
    internal["Internal Cargo or Air at Atmospheric Pressure"] -->|Acts inward| hullplating
    hullplating -->|Spans between| frames["Transverse Frames Support: SS or Clamped Edge"]
    hullplating -->|Spans between| longls["Longitudinals Support: SS or Clamped Edge"]
    frames -->|Transfer to| floors["Floors: Primary Transverse Members"]
    longls -->|Transfer to| girders["Side Girders: Primary Longitudinal Members"]
    floors -->|Transfer to| hullgirder["Hull Girder: Overall Strength"]
    girders -->|Transfer to| hullgirder
    hullgirder -->|Resists| global["Global Bending and Shear of the Ship"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-ship meaning:** The plate itself bends locally (this Module). The frames and longitudinals are themselves beams bending locally. The whole assembly participates in the **hull-girder bending** (a global Module 3 or 4 topic). KTU questions often test this hierarchy — local plate → local beam → global hull-girder.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **flexural rigidity** $D$ of a plate. State its physical significance and write down its mathematical expression in terms of Young's modulus, Poisson's ratio, and plate thickness.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Flexural rigidity $D$ of a plate is the **resistance offered by a unit width of the plate to bending deformation** under a transverse load. It depends on the material's elastic modulus $E$, Poisson's ratio $\nu$, and the cube of the plate thickness $h$:
> 
> $$D = \frac{E\, h^{3}}{12\,(1 - \nu^{2})}$$
> 
> **Significance:** $D$ is the plate-bending analogue of $EI$ for a beam. The factor $(1 - \nu^{2})$ in the denominator is a **two-dimensional constraint effect** — when a plate bends, lateral contraction is restrained by the surrounding material, increasing the effective rigidity. **(3 marks)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **clamped (built-in)** and **simply supported** edge conditions of a plate. Give **one ship-structural example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Property | Clamped (Built-in) | Simply Supported |
> |---|---|---|
> | Deflection $w$ at edge | $0$ | $0$ |
> | Slope $\partial w/\partial n$ at edge | $0$ | Free (non-zero) |
> | Bending moment $M$ at edge | Free (non-zero) | $0$ |
> 
> **Ship example — Clamped:** A plate edge welded to a deep frame or to a heavy transverse bulkhead — the deep member prevents rotation, so the edge is effectively clamped. **(1.5 marks)**
> 
> **Ship example — Simply Supported:** A plate edge welded to a flat-bar longitudinal of negligible torsional stiffness — the edge cannot deflect but can rotate freely. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks, with Internal Choice]**

**Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** Derive the governing differential equation of an **isotropic thin elastic plate** under transverse load $q(x, y)$, starting from the Kirchhoff–Love assumptions. Clearly state the kinematic, constitutive, and equilibrium relations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a simply supported rectangular plate of dimensions $a \times b$ and thickness $h$, made of mild steel ($E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{MPa}$, $\nu = 0.30$), subjected to a uniform pressure of $q = 40\ \text{kPa}$ with $a = 1.0\ \text{m}$, $b = 0.8\ \text{m}$, $h = 10\ \text{mm}$, calculate the **maximum deflection, maximum bending moment, and maximum bending stress** in the plate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Kinematic relations (1.5 marks):** From the Kirchhoff–Love hypothesis, displacements at height $z$ are $u = -z\,w_{,x}$, $v = -z\,w_{,y}$, leading to strains $\varepsilon_{x} = -z\,w_{,xx}$, $\varepsilon_{y} = -z\,w_{,yy}$, $\gamma_{xy} = -2z\,w_{,xy}$.

2. **Constitutive relations (1.5 marks):** For plane stress in an isotropic elastic plate:
   $$\sigma_{x} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^{2}}(\varepsilon_{x} + \nu\,\varepsilon_{y}),\quad \sigma_{y} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^{2}}(\varepsilon_{y} + \nu\,\varepsilon_{x}),\quad \tau_{xy} = \frac{E}{1+\nu}\gamma_{xy}$$

3. **Resultant moments (1.5 marks):** Integrating through the thickness, $M_{x} = -D(w_{,xx} + \nu\,w_{,yy})$, $M_{y} = -D(w_{,yy} + \nu\,w_{,xx})$, $M_{xy} = -D(1-\nu)\,w_{,xy}$ with $D = Eh^{3}/[12(1-\nu^{2})]$.

4. **Equilibrium (1.5 marks):** Element moment equilibrium gives $Q_{x} = M_{x,x} + M_{xy,y}$ and $Q_{y} = M_{y,y} + M_{xy,x}$; force equilibrium in $z$ gives $Q_{x,x} + Q_{y,y} + q = 0$. Eliminating $Q_{x}, Q_{y}$ and substituting moment expressions yields the biharmonic plate equation:
   $$\boxed{\,D\!\left(\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + 2\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2}\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}}\right) = q\,}$$

5. **Final remark (1 mark):** The operator on the left is $\nabla^{4}$, hence the equation is compactly $D\nabla^{4}w = q$.

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $D$ (1 mark):**
$$D = \frac{2.1\times 10^{11}\times(0.010)^{3}}{12(1 - 0.09)} = \frac{2.1\times 10^{11}\times 10^{-6}}{10.92} = \frac{2.1\times 10^{5}}{10.92} = 1.923\times 10^{4}\ \text{N·m} = 19.23\ \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 2 — Aspect ratio and coefficients (1 mark):**
$\lambda = a/b = 1.0/0.8 = 1.25$. Linear interpolation in the table: $\alpha_{w}(1.25) \approx 0.00589$ and $\alpha_{m}(1.25) \approx 0.0646$.

**Step 3 — Maximum deflection (1.5 marks):**
$$w_{\max} = \alpha_{w}\,\frac{q a^{4}}{D} = 0.00589 \times \frac{40\,000 \times (1.0)^{4}}{1.923\times 10^{4}} = 0.00589 \times 2.080 = 0.01225\ \text{m} = 12.25\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending moment per unit width (1.5 marks):**
$$M_{\max} = \alpha_{m}\,q a^{2} = 0.0646 \times 40\,000 \times 1.0 = 2584\ \text{N·m/m} = 2.584\ \text{kN·m/m}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum bending stress (1.5 marks):**
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{6 M_{\max}}{h^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 2584}{(0.010)^{2}} = \frac{15\,504}{10^{-4}} = 1.5504\times 10^{8}\ \text{Pa} = 155.0\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (0.5 mark):** $\sigma_{\max} = 155\ \text{MPa} < \sigma_{\text{yield}} = 235\ \text{MPa}$, so the plate is safe in bending. The deflection $w_{\max}/a = 12.25/1000 = 1/82$ is within typical serviceability limits (1/100 to 1/150 depending on rule). **Plate design adequate.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three classical edge conditions** (clamped, simply supported, free) of a plate. Discuss how each is realised in a real ship structural component. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **stiffened plate panel** of a ship's side shell has the following data: $a = 2.4\ \text{m}$ (longitudinal span between transverse frames), $b = 0.6\ \text{m}$ (span between longitudinal stiffeners), $h = 14\ \text{mm}$, $E = 2.1\times 10^{5}\ \text{MPa}$, $\nu = 0.30$. The plate carries a uniform hydrostatic pressure of $q = 60\ \text{kPa}$. Assuming all four edges are clamped (because of heavy deep frames and longitudinals), determine the **maximum bending moment and the maximum bending stress** in the plate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition and diagram of the three edge conditions (3 marks):** A neat 2-D figure showing a plate with three edge segments each marked with its boundary-condition symbol (hatched line for clamped, pin for SS, blank for free), with annotations of $w = 0$, slope $= 0$, $M = 0$ where applicable.

2. **Clamped edge (1.5 marks):** At a clamped edge, both deflection and slope vanish. In a ship, a plate edge continuously welded to a **deep transverse frame** (depth $\ge 10h$) is effectively clamped because the deep frame is much stiffer in rotation than the plate.

3. **Simply supported edge (1.5 marks):** At a SS edge, deflection vanishes but slope is free. In a ship, a plate edge welded to a **flat-bar longitudinal** of negligible torsional stiffness behaves as SS — the plate cannot move but is free to rotate.

4. **Free edge (1 mark):** A free edge is one with no support at all — both deflection and moment are free. In a ship, this is found at a **hatch coaming edge**, the **free edge of a hatch cover**, or the **cut-out of an opening**.

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Aspect ratio (1 mark):**
$\lambda = a/b = 2.4/0.6 = 4.0$. This is a **long plate**; coefficients approach the beam (cylindrical bending) limit.

**Step 2 — Coefficients (1.5 marks):** For a clamped square plate the coefficients are $\alpha_{m}^{\text{clamp}} = 0.0229$, but for long plates ($\lambda \to \infty$) with both ends clamped, the **beam limit** is $M = qL^{2}/12 = 0.0833\,qa^{2}$. Interpolating linearly: $\alpha_{m}(\lambda = 4) \approx 0.075$ (1.5 marks).

**Step 3 — Flexural rigidity (0.5 mark):**
$$D = \frac{2.1\times 10^{11}\times(0.014)^{3}}{12(1 - 0.09)} = \frac{2.1\times 10^{11}\times 2.744\times 10^{-6}}{10.92} = 5.276\times 10^{4}\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending moment per unit width (2 marks):**
$$M_{\max} = 0.075 \times q a^{2} = 0.075 \times 60\,000 \times (2.4)^{2} = 0.075 \times 60\,000 \times 5.76 = 25.92\times 10^{3}\ \text{N·m/m} = 25.92\ \text{kN·m/m}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum bending stress (2 marks):**
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{6 M_{\max}}{h^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 25\,920}{(0.014)^{2}} = \frac{155\,520}{1.96\times 10^{-4}} = 7.934\times 10^{8}\ \text{Pa} = 793.4\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (0.5 mark):** $\sigma_{\max} = 793.4\ \text{MPa}$ is **far above** the yield stress of ship steel. The panel is **grossly inadequate**; in practice the heavy stiffeners reduce the **effective** $a$ and $b$ to small sub-panel dimensions, and a true analysis must use **orthotropic-plate (stiffened-plate) theory**, not isotropic-plate theory on the gross dimensions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $D$ and $EI$:** $D$ is per-unit-width, $EI$ is for the whole cross-section. Markers deduct 1 mark if units are not in $\text{N·m}$ (per metre width).
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1-\nu^{2})$ factor:** A very common 1-mark loss. The 2-D constraint increases plate rigidity by roughly 10 % for steel.
> 3. **Wrong sign of $M$:** $M_x = -D(\ldots)$; some students drop the sign and lose 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to include interpretation in numerical problems:** Markers **always** award 0.5–1 mark for "is the plate safe?" engineering comment. Numerical value alone is not a complete answer.
> 5. **Aspect-ratio direction:** $a/b$ vs $b/a$ confusion. If the answer doesn't match the table, it's almost always the aspect-ratio direction.
> 6. **Forgetting the hydrostatic pressure formula $p = \rho g H$:** KTU often gives a depth $H$ (e.g., 5 m) instead of pressure. Convert first.
> 7. **Mixing beam formula with plate formula:** For long plates you may approximate as a beam — but only when the lecturer says so. State the assumption explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plate Bending (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

**Must-know definitions (verbatim, 1-mark items):**
- **Plate:** a 2-D structural element whose thickness is small compared to its in-plane dimensions.
- **Flexural rigidity $D$:** $D = \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$, the plate-bending analogue of beam $EI$, per unit width.
- **Aspect ratio $\lambda$:** ratio of long to short supported span.
- **Slenderness ratio $\beta$:** ratio of width to thickness.

**Must-derive (7–8 marks any exam):**
- Kirchhoff–Love plate equation $D \nabla^{4} w = q$ from kinematics + constitutive + equilibrium in 6 steps.

**Must-apply (7–8 marks any exam):**
- Use of Navier double-sine series for simply supported plates.
- Coefficients $\alpha_{w}(\lambda)$, $\alpha_{m}(\lambda)$ from a Timoshenko-style table.
- Computation of $w_{\max}$, $M_{\max}$, $\sigma_{\max}$ for a uniform-load panel.

**Must-interpret (always, even when not asked):**
- Is $\sigma_{\max} \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$? If no, increase $h$.
- Is $w_{\max}/a \le$ serviceability limit? If no, add a stiffener.
- Are the edge conditions appropriate to the actual ship detail?

**Ship-specific facts to memorise:**
- Hull plating is supported by **orthogonal grillage** of longitudinals and transverses.
- Effective aspect ratio of a sub-panel is typically $1.0$ to $2.0$.
- Typical slenderness ratio $\beta = b/h$ is $40$ to $150$ for bottom/side plating.
- **Clamping** arises from deep members; **simple support** from light flat bars.
- **Hydrostatic pressure** is the dominant load: $p = \rho g H$ with $\rho_{\text{sea}} = 1.025\ \text{t/m}^{3}$.
- Classification-society minimum thickness rules are derived from plate-bending theory.

**Common KTU 2024 Scheme question patterns:**
- 3-mark: "Define $D$", "Distinguish between clamped and SS edge", "State plate-bending equation".
- 7-mark: "Derive the plate equation", "Compute $w_{\max}$ given a numerical panel".
- 14-mark: Either derivation + numerical combination, or two-part numerical on a stiffened plate with interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Mantra:** *"$D \nabla^{4} w = q$ — solve it, check $w/a$ and $\sigma_{\max}$, then ship the plate."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uniaxial Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Uniaxial Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Uniaxial Bending** in ship structural analysis refers to the bending of the ship's hull when it is treated as a single, continuous **hull girder** floating on water, subjected to external loads that cause curvature about **one principal axis only** — the horizontal transverse axis. The hull, being a slender, prismatic structure whose length is much greater than its beam and depth, behaves like a long hollow box beam (girder).

In naval architecture, the ship is idealized as a **box girder** with:
- **Deck plating** acting as the top flange
- **Keel plate** acting as the bottom flange
- **Side shell plating** acting as the two webs
- **Transverse bulkheads and frames** acting as stiffeners

When the ship bends about its **horizontal transverse neutral axis** (which lies roughly amidships at about the level of the neutral axis of the hull cross-section), only vertical plane bending is considered — this is **uniaxial (vertical) bending**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Uniaxial bending is the foundation of longitudinal strength analysis. The ship is treated as a beam of length L (Length Between Perpendiculars, LBP) floating on a wave-like support.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thin, hollow cardboard box — long and rectangular — and you gently push down on its middle while supporting its two ends on two pillars. The box bends in a curve: the top surface gets compressed (shorter), the bottom surface stretches (longer). 

**Now replace the cardboard box with a ship:**
- The **sea surface** acts like the pillars' uneven support
- **Waves and cargo weight** push the ship up and down along its length
- The ship **bends like a long beam** floating on water

The bending occurs in a **single vertical plane** — this is uniaxial bending. Just like a diving board bends only up-and-down (not sideways), a ship's hull girder primarily bends vertically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A ship's length-to-depth ratio is typically **15:1 to 30:1**, making it behave predominantly as a one-dimensional beam under vertical loads.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of seawater | $\rho$ | **1.025 t/m³** |
| Standard wave coefficient (CSR) | $C_w$ | varies (typically 0.6–10.75) per IACS |
| Rule length | $L$ | Length between perpendiculars (**m**) |
| Breadth of ship | $B$ | Moulded breadth (**m**) |
| Depth of ship | $D$ | Moulded depth (**m**) |
| Section modulus | $Z$ | **m³** or **cm³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The Hull Girder Concept

The hull girder assumption rests on the following logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Idealization:** The entire ship (plating, stiffeners, decks, bulkheads) is treated as one continuous, hollow, prismatic beam of length $L_{BP}$ (length between perpendiculars).
- **Step 2 — Load Identification:** Two primary load types act on the hull girder:
    - **Static loads:** Weight of ship ($W$), buoyancy ($B$), cargo, ballast.
    - **Dynamic loads:** Wave-induced pressures, slamming, sea pressure.
- **Step 3 — Support Condition:** The ship is supported by a **non-uniform buoyancy distribution** (pressure from water) along its length.
- **Step 4 — Bending Moment Generation:** When the weight and buoyancy distributions do not match (longitudinally), a net transverse load $w(x) = b(x) - w(x)$ develops, producing a bending moment.
- **Step 5 — Section Response:** The hull cross-section resists bending through its **section modulus** $Z$, generating longitudinal bending stresses $\sigma$.

### 2.2 Why the Hull Girder Bends — The Physics

- **Hogging Condition:** When buoyancy at the ends exceeds weight at the ends (typical of a ship supported on a wave crest amidships). The ends droop down; **deck in tension, bottom in compression**.
- **Sagging Condition:** When weight at the ends exceeds buoyancy (ship supported on a wave trough amidships). The ends rise up; **deck in compression, bottom in tension**.

These conditions arise because waves create a sinusoidal support that alternates peaks and troughs along the ship's length.

### 2.3 Components of Bending Moments

The total vertical bending moment at any section is:

$$M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_{w}$$

Where:
- $M_{sw}$ = **Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM)** — due to static weight/buoyancy distribution
- $M_{w}$ = **Wave Bending Moment (WBM)** — due to wave action

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress at distance $y$ from neutral axis | Pa or N/mm² |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress using section modulus $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | Pa or N/mm² |
| 3 | $M_{sw} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{x} w(x) \cdot (x - \xi)\, d\xi$ | SWBM from weight/buoyancy distribution | kN·m |
| 4 | $M_{w} = C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_b + 0.7) \cdot 10^{-3}$ | Empirical wave bending moment (CSR/Common Structural Rules) | kN·m |
| 5 | $\sigma_{allowable} = \dfrac{175}{k}$ | Allowable stress for high-tensile steel ($k$ = material factor) | N/mm² |
| 6 | $Z_{required} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allowable}}$ | Minimum required section modulus | m³ |
| 7 | $Z_{actual} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{deck}}$ or $\dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{keel}}$ | Actual section modulus to deck or keel | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Note:** Always report $\sigma$ in **N/mm²** when using steel rules. Convert carefully: $1\,\text{MPa} = 1\,\text{N/mm}^2$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Uniaxial bending analysis is critical for:
- **Hull scantling determination** (plate thickness, stiffener size).
- **Compliance with IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR)** for oil tankers and bulk carriers.
- **Failure prevention:** catastrophic hull fractures (e.g., the **Herald of Free Enterprise** and **MV Prestige** were linked to longitudinal strength issues).
- **Material selection** — higher-strength steel allows reduced scantlings but requires stricter bending analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bending Stress in the Hull Girder

We begin with the classical beam theory assumptions applied to a hull section.

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral surface after bending (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).
- Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
- The hull girder bends only about the **horizontal transverse neutral axis** (single axis bending).

**Step 1 — Strain in a longitudinal fibre at distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis (NA):**

Let $\rho$ be the radius of curvature of the neutral axis. The longitudinal strain of a fibre at distance $y$ from the NA is the change in arc length divided by original length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{(\rho - y)\,\theta - \rho\,\theta}{\rho\,\theta} = -\frac{y}{\rho}$$

The negative sign indicates compression above the NA and tension below.

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law to obtain stress:**

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = -\frac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$$

**Step 3 — Equate internal and external moments:**

The internal resisting moment produced by the stress distribution over the cross-section must equal the applied bending moment $M$:

$$\int_{A} \sigma \cdot y \, dA = M$$

Substituting $\sigma = -\dfrac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$:

$$\int_{A} \frac{E \cdot y^2}{\rho}\, dA = M$$

Since $\dfrac{E}{\rho}$ is constant for a given cross-section:

$$\frac{E}{\rho} \int_{A} y^2\, dA = M$$

The integral $\int_{A} y^2\, dA$ is the **second moment of area** about the neutral axis, denoted $I_{NA}$.

**Step 4 — Solve for curvature:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I_{NA}}$$

This is the famous **flexure formula** in curvature form.

**Step 5 — Final bending stress equation:**

Substitute $\dfrac{1}{\rho}$ back into $\sigma = -\dfrac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I_{NA}}$$

The magnitude of maximum bending stress at the extreme fibre ($y = y_{max}$) is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}}$$

Where the **section modulus** is defined as:

$$Z = \frac{I_{NA}}{y_{max}}$$

### 3.2 Section Modulus Computation for a Symmetrical Hull Section

For a hull with symmetric plating about the neutral axis, both deck and keel extreme fibres experience equal magnitudes of stress. The moment of inertia contribution from each longitudinal element (plating strip) is:

$$I_{NA} = \sum_{i} A_i \cdot y_i^2$$

The neutral axis location is found from $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$.

**Worked Example Calculation Logic:**

- Identify all continuous longitudinal members (keel plate, bottom shell, side shell, deck plating, sheer strake).
- Locate centroid of each strip relative to baseline.
- Compute total area $A_{total}$ and first moment $\sum A_i y_i$.
- Find neutral axis: $\bar{y}_{NA} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{A_{total}}$.
- Compute $I_{NA} = \sum A_i \left(y_i - \bar{y}_{NA}\right)^2$.
- Compute $Z_{deck} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{D - \bar{y}_{NA}}$, $Z_{keel} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{\bar{y}_{NA}}$.

### 3.3 Wave Bending Moment — Empirical Form (IACS CSR Method)

The wave bending moment amplitude for a ship on a regular trochoidal wave of length equal to ship length $L$ is given by:

$$M_w = \pm\, C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_b + 0.7) \cdot 10^{-3}\ \text{kN·m}$$

Where:
- $C_w$ = wave coefficient (from IACS tables, function of $L$)
- $L$ = rule length in metres
- $B$ = moulded breadth in metres
- $C_b$ = block coefficient

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** Positive $M_w$ denotes **hogging**, negative denotes **sagging**. For KTU numerical problems, the absolute maximum value is usually required.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for KTU Problems

| Problem Type | Key Given Data | Required Output | Primary Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stress calculation | $M$, $Z$ | $\sigma_{max}$ | $\sigma = M/Z$ |
| Section modulus requirement | $M_{max}$, $\sigma_{allow}$ | $Z_{req}$ | $Z = M/\sigma$ |
| Moment of inertia check | Geometry of section | $I_{NA}$, $Z$ | $I = \sum Ay^2$, $Z = I/y_{max}$ |
| Wave bending moment | $L$, $B$, $C_b$, $C_w$ | $M_w$ | $M_w = C_w L^2 B (C_b+0.7) \cdot 10^{-3}$ |
| Hogging/Sagging identification | Wave/Load diagram | Sign of $M$ | Visual inspection of curve |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hull Girder Bending Modes — Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Floating on Waves] --> B{Vertical Load Distribution Analysis}
    B --> C[Weight Distribution W of x]
    B --> D[Buoyancy Distribution B of x]
    C --> E[Net Load w of x equals B of x minus W of x]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Resultant Shear Force and Bending Moment}
    F --> G[Bending Moment Diagram BMD]
    G --> H{Maximum Moment Location and Sign}
    H --> I[Hogging Moment Positive M greater than 0]
    H --> J[Sagging Moment Negative M less than 0]
    I --> K[Deck in Tension, Keel in Compression]
    J --> L[Deck in Compression, Keel in Tension]
    K --> M[Stress Calculation sigma equals M times y over I]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compare with Allowable Stress]
    N --> O{Strength Satisfied?}
    O --> P[Design Acceptable]
    O --> Q[Increase Section Modulus Z or Plate Thickness]
```

### 4.2 Stress Distribution Block Diagram (Hull Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Vertical Bending Moment M] --> B[Neutral Axis NA at y NA]
    B --> C[Deck Extreme Fibre at y equals D minus y NA]
    B --> D[Keel Extreme Fibre at y equals y NA]
    A --> E[Bending Stress sigma equals M times y over I]
    C --> F[sigma deck equals M times D minus y NA over I]
    D --> G[sigma keel equals M times y NA over I]
    F --> H{Tension or Compression?}
    G --> I{Tension or Compression?}
    H --> J[Deck Stress for Hogging: Tension]
    H --> K[Deck Stress for Sagging: Compression]
    I --> L[Keel Stress for Hogging: Compression]
    I --> M[Keel Stress for Sagging: Tension]
```

### 4.3 Bending Moment Visualization (ASCII Schematic)

```
       HOGGING CONDITION (Wave Crest Amidships)
       
              B(x) > W(x) at ends
        ──────────────────────────────  ← Wave crest pushes UP at ends
       ╱                               ╲
      ╱                                 ╲
     ╱     B(x) < W(x) amidships        ╲
    ╱         Ship sags in middle         ╲
   ╱                                       ╲
  ╱─────────────────────────────────────────╲
     Tension at Deck ─── Compression at Keel


       SAGGING CONDITION (Wave Trough Amidships)

              W(x) > B(x) at ends
        ╱─────────────────────────────────╲  ← Wave trough pushes DOWN at ends
       ╱                                   ╲
      ╱                                     ╲
     ╱     B(x) > W(x) amidships            ╲
    ╱         Ship hogs in middle             ╲
   ╱                                         ╲
  ╱───────────────────────────────────────────╲
     Compression at Deck ─── Tension at Keel
```

### 4.4 Section Modulus Logic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Hull Cross Section Geometry] --> step1[List all longitudinal members]
    step1 --> step2[Compute area A sub i for each strip]
    step2 --> step3[Locate centroid y sub i from baseline]
    step3 --> step4[Compute Neutral Axis NA: y NA equals sum A sub i y sub i over sum A sub i]
    step4 --> step5[Compute I NA equals sum A sub i times y sub i minus y NA squared]
    step5 --> step6[Compute Z deck equals I NA over D minus y NA]
    step5 --> step7[Compute Z keel equals I NA over y NA]
    step6 --> step8[Use sigma equals M over Z for design check]
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9[Final Allowable Stress Verification]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define uniaxial bending as applied to a ship's hull girder. State the conditions under which a ship experiences hogging and sagging.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Uniaxial bending of a ship's hull refers to the bending of the entire hull when idealized as a single hollow box girder of length equal to the ship's length between perpendiculars (LBP), subjected to loads acting in the vertical plane only — bending occurs about a single horizontal transverse axis.

**(1 mark for definition)**

**Hogging Condition** occurs when the buoyancy at the ends of the ship exceeds the weight distribution at the ends (typically when a wave crest is amidships). The ship bends with the ends drooping downward; **deck experiences tension** and **keel experiences compression**. **(1 mark)**

**Sagging Condition** occurs when the weight at the ends exceeds buoyancy at the ends (wave trough amidships). The ends curve upward; **deck experiences compression** and **keel experiences tension**. **(1 mark)**

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is section modulus? Write the bending stress formula and explain the role of the neutral axis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **section modulus** $Z$ is a geometric property of a cross-section defined as the ratio of the second moment of area $I_{NA}$ about the neutral axis to the distance $y_{max}$ from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre:

$$Z = \frac{I_{NA}}{y_{max}}$$

It is measured in **m³** (or cm³). **(1 mark)**

The bending stress at any distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I_{NA}}$$

The maximum bending stress at the extreme fibre is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

**(1 mark for formulas)**

**Role of Neutral Axis:** The neutral axis is the line in the cross-section where longitudinal stress is zero — fibres are neither elongated nor compressed. Above and below this axis, stresses reverse sign (tension to compression). The neutral axis passes through the centroid of the effective longitudinal material distribution of the hull section. **(1 mark)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the bending stress formula $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ for a hull girder cross-section under uniaxial bending. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following data:
- Rule length $L = 200$ m
- Moulded breadth $B = 32$ m
- Block coefficient $C_b = 0.82$
- Wave coefficient $C_w = 5.85$ (from IACS table)
- Maximum permissible bending stress $\sigma_{allow} = 175$ N/mm²

Calculate the **(i)** wave bending moment and **(ii)** minimum required section modulus. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The hull is idealized as a long prismatic beam (hull girder).
2. Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral surface after bending (Euler–Bernoulli).
3. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic (Hooke's law applicable).
4. Self-weight of the hull is neglected compared to applied bending loads.
5. Only vertical bending (uniaxial) is considered — torsion is neglected.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let $\rho$ be the radius of curvature of the neutral axis after bending. A longitudinal fibre at distance $y$ from the NA has its length changed from $\rho\theta$ (original) to $(\rho - y)\theta$ (after bending), assuming bending concave-up.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{(\rho - y)\theta - \rho\theta}{\rho\theta} = -\frac{y}{\rho}$$

By Hooke's Law:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = -\frac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$$

Equating the internal resisting moment to the applied bending moment:

$$\int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = M$$

$$\int_A \frac{E \cdot y^2}{\rho} \, dA = M$$

$$\frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2 \, dA = M$$

Recognizing $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I_{NA}$ (second moment of area):

$$\frac{E \cdot I_{NA}}{\rho} = M \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I_{NA}}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I_{NA}}}$$

At the extreme fibre $y = y_{max}$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I_{NA}} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**(i) Wave Bending Moment (3 marks):**

$$M_w = C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_b + 0.7) \cdot 10^{-3}$$

Substituting:

$$M_w = 5.85 \cdot (200)^2 \cdot 32 \cdot (0.82 + 0.7) \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$M_w = 5.85 \cdot 40000 \cdot 32 \cdot 1.52 \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$M_w = 5.85 \cdot 40000 \cdot 48.64 \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$M_w = 11381760 \cdot 10^{-3}\ \text{kN·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_w = 11381.76\ \text{kN·m} \approx 11.38\ \text{GN·mm}}$$

**(ii) Minimum Required Section Modulus (4 marks):**

Convert allowable stress to consistent units:
$$\sigma_{allow} = 175\ \text{N/mm}^2 = 175 \times 10^3\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

Convert moment:
$$M_w = 11381.76\ \text{kN·m} = 11381.76 \times 10^3\ \text{kN·mm} = 1.138 \times 10^{10}\ \text{N·mm}$$

Wait — for $Z$ in m³, use $M$ in kN·m and $\sigma$ in kN/m²:

$$\sigma_{allow} = 175\ \text{N/mm}^2 = 175 \times 10^3\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_w}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{11381.76\ \text{kN·m}}{175 \times 10^3\ \text{kN/m}^2}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{req} = 0.0650\ \text{m}^3 = 6.50 \times 10^7\ \text{mm}^3}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Hull Girder** concept. With the help of neat sketches, describe **hogging** and **sagging** conditions and the corresponding stress distribution in the hull. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A container ship has a hull cross-section with the following longitudinal effective plating details (areas measured in cm² and distances from the baseline in cm):

| Member | Area $A_i$ (cm²) | Distance from baseline $y_i$ (cm) |
|---|---|---|
| Bottom shell plate | 80 | 30 |
| Side shell plate (port) | 60 | 250 |
| Side shell plate (stbd) | 60 | 250 |
| Deck plate | 90 | 1000 |

Calculate: **(i)** the location of the neutral axis from the baseline, and **(ii)** the moment of inertia $I_{NA}$ about the neutral axis. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Hull Girder, Hogging & Sagging (7 Marks):**

**Hull Girder Concept (2 marks):**
The entire ship's hull — comprising the keel plate, bottom shell, side shell, decks, bulkheads, and all longitudinal stiffeners — is treated as a single continuous hollow box girder of length equal to the ship's length between perpendiculars. The deck plating acts as the top flange, the keel as the bottom flange, and the side shell plating acts as the two webs. Transverse bulkheads and frames serve as internal stiffeners. This idealization allows application of standard beam bending theory to compute longitudinal stresses.

**Hogging (2.5 marks):**
When the ship is supported by a wave crest amidships, the buoyancy distribution $B(x)$ has a maximum amidships and is lower at the ends, while the weight distribution $W(x)$ remains approximately uniform. The ends thus experience a net downward force, causing the hull to bend with the ends sagging below the mean waterline. This is **hogging**. In hogging, the **deck is in tension** and the **keel is in compression**.

**Sagging (2.5 marks):**
When the ship is supported by a wave trough amidships, buoyancy is minimum amidships and higher at the ends. The ends are pushed upward relative to the middle, causing the midship region to droop. This is **sagging**. In sagging, the **deck is in compression** and the **keel is in tension**.

**(b) Numerical Section Modulus Computation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Neutral Axis Location (3 marks):**

$$\bar{y}_{NA} = \frac{\sum A_i \cdot y_i}{\sum A_i}$$

Compute numerator:
$$\sum A_i \cdot y_i = (80)(30) + (60)(250) + (60)(250) + (90)(1000)$$
$$= 2400 + 15000 + 15000 + 90000 = 122400\ \text{cm}^3$$

Compute denominator:
$$\sum A_i = 80 + 60 + 60 + 90 = 290\ \text{cm}^2$$

$$\bar{y}_{NA} = \frac{122400}{290} = 422.07\ \text{cm from baseline}$$

**(ii) Moment of Inertia (4 marks):**

$$I_{NA} = \sum A_i (y_i - \bar{y}_{NA})^2$$

| Member | $A_i$ (cm²) | $y_i$ (cm) | $(y_i - \bar{y}_{NA})^2$ (cm²) | $A_i (y_i - \bar{y}_{NA})^2$ (cm⁴) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bottom plate | 80 | 30 | $(30 - 422.07)^2 = 153731$ | $80 \times 153731 = 12298480$ |
| Side port | 60 | 250 | $(250 - 422.07)^2 = 29620$ | $60 \times 29620 = 1777200$ |
| Side stbd | 60 | 250 | $29620$ | $1777200$ |
| Deck plate | 90 | 1000 | $(1000 - 422.07)^2 = 334381$ | $90 \times 334381 = 30094290$ |

Sum:
$$I_{NA} = 12298480 + 1777200 + 1777200 + 30094290$$
$$\boxed{I_{NA} = 45947170\ \text{cm}^4 \approx 4.595 \times 10^7\ \text{cm}^4}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–3 marks typical):** Mixing mm and cm, or kN·m with N/mm² without conversion. Always convert $M$ and $\sigma$ to compatible units before computing $Z$.
> 2. **Sign Confusion in Hogging/Sagging (–2 marks):** Students often incorrectly assign tension/compression. Memorize: **Hogging = Deck in Tension; Sagging = Deck in Compression.**
> 3. **Neutral Axis Calculation Error (–2 marks):** Failing to take the centroid of the *effective longitudinal material*, not the geometric centroid of the hull envelope.
> 4. **Missing Assumptions in Derivation (–2 marks):** Always state the Euler–Bernoulli plane-section-remains-plane assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Wave Coefficient Lookup Errors (–2 marks):** $C_w$ values depend on $L$ per IACS tables — verify against the correct length band.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Uniaxial Bending = Vertical Bending of Hull Girder** about a single horizontal transverse axis. **Torsion is excluded** (that is biaxial/torsion).
- **Hull Girder Idealization:** Ship = hollow box beam of length $L_{BP}$, deck = top flange, keel = bottom flange, side shell = webs.
- **Hogging:** Ends droop → **Deck in Tension, Keel in Compression**.
- **Sagging:** Ends rise → **Deck in Compression, Keel in Tension**.
- **Key Formula:** $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ (maximum at extreme fibre).
- **Section Modulus $Z$:** Geometric property of cross-section; higher $Z$ ⇒ lower stress for given $M$.
- **Wave Bending Moment (IACS Empirical):** $M_w = C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_b + 0.7) \cdot 10^{-3}$ kN·m.
- **Total Bending Moment:** $M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_{w}$.
- **Allowable Stress for Mild Steel (CSR):** $\sigma_{allow} = 175/k$ N/mm², where $k$ is the material factor.
- **Minimum Section Modulus:** $Z_{req} = M_{max}/\sigma_{allow}$.
- **Standard Sea Water Density:** $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³ ; **Gravity:** $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- **Assumptions to Always State:** Plane sections remain plane; linear elastic material; uniaxial bending only (no torsion).
- **NA Location:** Always at the centroid of the *effective longitudinal material distribution*, not the geometric centre of the hull envelope.
- **Real-World Failure Lessons:** Longitudinal strength failures (e.g., **MV Prestige 2002**, **Herald of Free Enterprise**) reinforce the importance of correct uniaxial bending analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biaxial Bending Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Biaxial Bending Devices ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biaxial Bending (in Ship Structural Context):** > *Biaxial bending* in a ship hull refers to the simultaneous state of bending of the hull girder about **two mutually perpendicular horizontal axes** — namely, the **transverse (athwartships) axis** and the **longitudinal (fore-aft) axis**. The combined bending moment vector has two orthogonal components: a *vertical bending moment* (VBM) producing hogging/sagging, and a *horizontal bending moment* (HBM) producing port/starboard flexure, both generally accompanied by a *warping torsion moment* (WT). > [!NOTE] > **Biaxial Bending Devices (Structural Arrangements):** > These are the *primary and secondary stiffening members* of the hull girder, together with associated plating, that are specifically proportioned and arranged to resist the resulting biaxial stress field, ensuring the hull maintains adequate **section modulus (Z)** and **moment of inertia (I)** in both vertical and horizontal planes. ### 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Imagine holding a long, hollow rectangular cardboard tube (like a paper towel roll) with both hands. If you press the ends down (or up) so that it sags in the middle — that is *uniaxial* vertical bending. Now imagine an identical tube floating on a stormy sea: a wave passes under the middle lifting the bow and stern, **and** another wave hits the side pushing the hull sideways. The hull must resist bending **up-down** AND **side-side** at the same instant. Think of the ship hull as a long, floating I-beam (or actually a box girder). The "devices" that keep this box girder from collapsing are the steel plates and the forest of internal stiffeners (longitudinals, transverses, webs, stringers). They are deliberately arranged so the steel box is equally strong whether you bend it like a rainbow (vertical hogging/sagging) or squeeze it sideways (horizontal bending). > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Magnitude (SOLAS/IACS Context):** > The design wave bending moment for a typical **Panamax bulk carrier** is on the order of **M_v ≈ 0.0055 · L² · B · CB** (in t·m), while the horizontal bending moment can reach **25–35 %** of the vertical moment during oblique/beam sea conditions. ### 1.3 Why Biaxial Bending Exists in Ships — The Physical Drivers The hull girder is loaded by: | Loading Component | Direction | Physical Cause | |---|---|---| | **Still-water bending moment (SWBM)** | Vertical | Discrepancy between weight and buoyancy distributions along the length | | **Wave-induced vertical BM (VBM)** | Vertical (hog/sag) | Wave passage: buoyancy follows wave profile, weight is fixed | | **Wave-induced horizontal BM (HBM)** | Horizontal | Oblique seas; lateral water-plane pressure variation | | **Warping torsion (WT)** | About longitudinal axis | Asymmetric wave loading & off-center weights | All these act **simultaneously**, hence the biaxial bending state.
