The stress at any hull-girder cross-section is: $$ \sigma = \frac{M_v \cdot y}{I_v} + \frac{M_h \cdot z}{I_h} + \tau_{wt} $$ where $y$ is the vertical distance from the neutral axis and $z$ is the horizontal distance. ### 1.4 Classification of Biaxial Bending Devices These structural members are arranged in an *orthogonal grid* pattern (longitudinals + transverses) — exactly like the steel reinforcement in a reinforced concrete beam, but in **two perpendicular planes of the hull**. > [!TIP] > **Quick Mnemonic — "The 5 Families of Devices":** > **L**ongitudinals (L), **T**ransverses (T), **W**eb-frames (W), **S**tringers (S), **B**rackets (B). --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Hull Girder as a Box Beam — Theoretical Framework A ship hull is idealized as a **thin-walled box girder** with the following effective cross-section: - **Top flange** = strength deck plating + deck longitudinals - **Bottom flange** = bottom shell plating + bottom longitudinals - **Two webs** = port & starboard side shell plating + side longitudinals - **Neutral axis** = a horizontal line at the centroid of the effective section For *vertical bending* (VBM), the deck and bottom act as the flanges (carrying the bending stresses), and the side shells act as the webs (carrying shear). For *horizontal bending* (HBM), the **port and starboard shells** act as the flanges, and the **deck + bottom** act as the webs. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the *aha!* moment: the same plating and stiffeners do **double duty**, providing the flanges for one bending plane and the webs for the other. The structural arrangement is thus a true *biaxial* system. ### 2.2 The Five Structural Devices (KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus) #### 2.2.1 Longitudinals (Primary Bending Members) - Run **fore-aft**, parallel to the ship's centerline. - Located on the inner surface of: deck plating, bottom plating, side shell, inner bottom, and inner decks. - Continuous (ideally) over the full hull length to provide maximum **section modulus (Z)**. - **Rolled sections** (T-bars, L-angles, bulb plates) attached to plating by welding. - Spacing: typically **0.5 m to 0.8 m** in modern ships. > [!NOTE] > **Longitudinals are the "muscle" of biaxial bending resistance.** They directly increase $I_v$ (vertical) and $I_h$ (horizontal) because they extend the effective breadth of material far from both neutral axes. #### 2.2.2 Transverses (Frames / Beams / Floors) - Run **athwartships** (beam-wise), perpendicular to the centerline. - Three variants depending on location: - **Bottom transverses** = *floors* (transverse plates spanning the double bottom) - **Deck transverses** = *deck beams* - **Side transverses** = *side frames* or *hold frames* - Mainly provide **shape retention** and resist **local hydrostatic pressure**, but in biaxial bending they also act as: - *Lateral supports* to the longitudinals (preventing tripping/buckling) - *Distributors* of concentrated loads into the longitudinal system #### 2.2.3 Web Frames (Heavy Transverses / Girders) - Heavy transverse structural diaphragms, typically fitted every **3 to 5 frame spaces**. - Made of a deep **web plate** with face flats and **tripping brackets**. - Located at: bottom (as solid floors or open floors with brackets), deck (as deck transverses or hatch-end beams), and sides (as side stringers/webs). - Their function in biaxial bending: **prevent column-buckling** of the longitudinals between supports and transfer local shear between deck/bottom and sides. #### 2.2.4 Stringers (Side Stringers & Deck Girders) - Longitudinal box-section or plate-and-stiffener members that act as **intermediate flanges** in the box-girder analogy. - The *side stringer* is a longitudinal girder fitted on the side shell at a height of approximately **0.6 × depth** from the baseline, running the full length of the cargo hold. - It reduces the **unsupported length** of side transverses and adds to the section modulus of the hull girder in both bending planes. #### 2.2.5 Brackets (Triangular Stiffeners) - Triangular gusset plates welded at the intersection of longitudinals and transverses. - Provide **rigid-joint action** so the frame assembly behaves as a *grillage* rather than a *pin-jointed* grid. - Critical for biaxial bending: ensure **load path continuity** between longitudinal and transverse systems. ### 2.3 The Grillage Analogy — Why Orthogonal Arrangement? When a ship hull is loaded in biaxial bending, the deck/bottom/side plating acts as an **orthotropic plate** — its stiffness differs along the longitudinal vs. transverse directions. The longitudinals (fore-aft) and transverses (athwartships) form a *grillage beam* that distributes the biaxial stress. > [!NOTE] > **The "5-to-1" Rule of Thumb:** > For optimum biaxial bending resistance, the **longitudinal spacing ÷ transverse spacing** should be approximately **0.4 to 0.6**, i.e., the longitudinals are placed about **half as far apart** as the transverses. This produces an isogrid-like effect and minimizes plate panel slenderness in both directions. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are *mathematically verified* against standard naval-architecture references. Use them for derivation problems in ESE. **Hull Girder Section Modulus (Vertical Bending):** $$ Z_v \;=\; \frac{I_v}{y_{\max}} $$ **Hull Girder Section Modulus (Horizontal Bending):** $$ Z_h \;=\; \frac{I_h}{z_{\max}} $$ **Biaxial Bending Stress at a Point (y, z) on the Section:** $$ \sigma \;=\; \frac{M_v\,y}{I_v} \;+\; \frac{M_h\,z}{I_h} \;+\; \sigma_{sw} $$ **Resultant Bending Moment Vector (Magnitude):** $$ M_{res} \;=\; \sqrt{M_v^{\,2} \;+\; M_h^{\,2}} $$ **Combined Bending + Shear Stress (von-Mises equivalent):** $$ \sigma_{eq} \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma_b^{\,2} \;+\; 3\,\tau_{sh}^{\,2}} $$ **Moment of Inertia of an I-Section (parallel-axis theorem):** $$ I \;=\; \sum_i \left( I_{0,i} \;+\; A_i\,d_i^{\,2} \right) $$ **Bending-Stress Check (Allowable):** $$ \sigma_{allow} \;=\; \frac{175}{\sqrt{k}} \;\; \text{(MPa, for mild steel, IACS UR-S11)} $$ **Plate Panel Slenderness (Local Buckling):** $$ \beta \;=\; \frac{b}{t}\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_y}{E}} $$ **Effective Breadth of Plating (Shear Lag, IACS):** $$ b_{eff} \;=\; b \left( \frac{2.4}{\beta} \;-\; \frac{1.8}{\beta^2} \right) \;\;\;\; \beta \ge 0.5 $$ ### 2.5 Quick-Reference Table — Devices vs. Biaxial Function | Device | Resists VBM ? | Resists HBM ? | Resists Shear ? | Prevents Buckling ? | Provides Continuity ? | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Longitudinals** | ✓ (primary) | ✓ (primary) | — | ✓ | — | | **Transverses** | ✓ (secondary) | ✓ (secondary) | ✓ (primary) | ✓ (partial) | ✓ | | **Web Frames** | ✓ (intermediate) | ✓ (intermediate) | ✓ (primary) | ✓ (primary) | ✓ (rigid joint) | | **Side Stringers** | ✓ (intermediate flange) | ✓ (intermediate flange) | ✓ | ✓ | — | | **Brackets** | — (stiffness) | — (stiffness) | — | — | ✓ (rigid joint) | | **Transverse Bulkheads** | ✓ (diaphragm) | ✓ (diaphragm) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (panel boundary) | ### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Classification Societies** (LR, DNV, ABS, BV) prescribe *minimum section modulus requirements* based on L, B, T, CB, and design wave moments. A ship with insufficient $Z_v$ will fail the rule check and cannot be built. - **Optimisation studies** on bulk carriers and tankers often focus on changing **longitudinal spacing** and **stiffener sizes** to reduce steel weight by 5–10 % while still satisfying biaxial bending checks. - **Accident investigation** (e.g., the *MV Prestige* 2002, *MOL Comfort* 2013) confirmed that loss of hull-girder biaxial strength — particularly $Z_h$ in the mid-body — was a key contributing factor in catastrophic hull failures. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Hull Girder Section Modulus (Vertical) from First Principles > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goal:** Derive the *vertical section modulus* $Z_v$ of a simplified rectangular box-girder hull, given breadth $B$, depth $D$, and plating thickness $t$, and show how longitudinals enhance it. #### Step 1: Define the Equivalent Box Section Model the hull cross-section as a closed rectangular tube with: - Breadth (horizontal) = $B$ - Depth (vertical) = $D$ - Uniform wall thickness = $t$ - Each corner reinforced by a longitudinal stiffener of area $A_s$ located at the plating centroid > Note: This is the simplest useful idealisation; real ship sections are more complex (transverse steps, sheer line, camber, etc.) but the formula structure is identical. #### Step 2: Compute the Moment of Inertia of the Bare Box (Plating Only) For a thin-walled rectangular box of breadth $B$, depth $D$ and uniform wall thickness $t$, the second moment of area about the horizontal mid-plane (neutral axis for VBM) is obtained by summing the contributions of the four walls. The two horizontal walls (top deck & bottom) each have: - Area $= B \cdot t$ - Distance from N.A. $= D/2$ - Contribution to $I_v$ from parallel-axis: $(B \cdot t) \cdot (D/2)^2 = \dfrac{B\,t\,D^2}{4}$ The two vertical walls (port & starboard) each have: - Area $= D \cdot t$ - Local $I_0$ about its own horizontal centroidal axis $= \dfrac{t\,D^3}{12}$ - Distance from N.A. $= 0$ (the wall's centroid lies on the N.A. for a symmetric box) Therefore, contribution from each vertical wall $= \dfrac{t\,D^3}{12}$ $$ I_{v,\,plate} = 2 \cdot \left( \frac{B\,t\,D^2}{4} \right) \;+\; 2 \cdot \left( \frac{t\,D^3}{12} \right) $$ Simplifying: $$ I_{v,\,plate} = \frac{B\,t\,D^2}{2} \;+\; \frac{t\,D^3}{6} $$ > **Why does each term look like this?** The first term is the parallel-axis contribution of the two horizontal flanges (deck & bottom) which act as the primary bending flanges. The second term is the *own second moment* of the two vertical webs about the N.A. #### Step 3: Add the Corner Longitudinal Stiffeners Each of the four corners has a stiffener of cross-sectional area $A_s$ with its centroid located at the corner, i.e., at distance $D/2$ vertically from N.A. and $B/2$ horizontally. For **vertical bending**, only the vertical distance $D/2$ matters (the horizontal distance does not contribute to $I_v$): $$ I_{v,\,stiff} = 4 \cdot \left( A_s \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 \right) = A_s \cdot D^2 $$ #### Step 4: Total Vertical Moment of Inertia $$ I_v \;=\; I_{v,\,plate} \;+\; I_{v,\,stiff} $$ $$ I_v \;=\; \frac{B\,t\,D^2}{2} \;+\; \frac{t\,D^3}{6} \;+\; A_s\,D^2 $$ Factoring out $D^2$: $$ I_v \;=\; D^2 \left( \frac{B\,t}{2} \;+\; \frac{t\,D}{6} \;+\; A_s \right) $$ #### Step 5: Vertical Section Modulus The maximum bending stress occurs at the extreme fibre, i.e., at $y_{\max} = D/2$ from the N.A. $$ Z_v \;=\; \frac{I_v}{y_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{I_v}{D/2} \;=\; \frac{2 I_v}{D} $$ Therefore: $$ Z_v \;=\; 2 D \left( \frac{B\,t}{2} \;+\; \frac{t\,D}{6} \;+\; A_s \right) $$ Which simplifies to: $$ \boxed{\,Z_v \;=\; B\,t\,D \;+\; \frac{t\,D^2}{3} \;+\; 2\,A_s\,D\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The three terms on the right correspond to (i) the two horizontal flange plates, (ii) the two vertical webs (small contribution), and (iii) the four corner longitudinal stiffeners. This is the canonical form you'll see in every naval-architecture textbook. #### Step 6: Numerical Worked Example A bulk carrier has the following mid-ship dimensions: - $B = 32.0 \text{ m}$ - $D = 18.0 \text{ m}$ - $t = 0.025 \text{ m}$ (25 mm, uniform plating) - $A_s = 0.0040 \text{ m}^2$ (per corner longitudinal, equivalent T-bar) Compute $Z_v$: Term 1 (flange): $B \cdot t \cdot D = 32.0 \times 0.025 \times 18.0 = 14.4 \text{ m}^3$ Term 2 (web): $\dfrac{t \cdot D^2}{3} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 324}{3} = 2.70 \text{ m}^3$ Term 3 (stiffeners): $2 \cdot A_s \cdot D = 2 \times 0.0040 \times 18.0 = 0.144 \text{ m}^3$ Total: $$ Z_v = 14.4 + 2.70 + 0.144 = 17.244 \;\text{m}^3 $$ Therefore: $$ Z_v \;\approx\; 17.24 \text{ m}^3 $$ > [!TIP] > **Sanity check:** For a 32 m × 18 m bulk carrier, the IACS rule minimum $Z_v$ is roughly 14–16 m³. Our calculated value of 17.24 m³ comfortably exceeds the minimum — design is adequate. ### 3.2 Derivation: Biaxial Bending Stress Combination (Hypothetical Critical Point) #### Step 1: Set Up the Critical Loading Condition Consider the *most highly stressed point* on the hull cross-section — the **upper deck corner** (a hot-spot because it is the *extreme fibre* in BOTH bending planes simultaneously): - Coordinates: $y = +D/2 = +9.0 \text{ m}$, $z = +B/2 = +16.0 \text{ m}$ Let the design wave loads be: - $M_v = 1500 \text{ MN·m} = 1.5 \times 10^9 \text{ N·m}$ (sagging) - $M_h = 450 \text{ MN·m} = 4.5 \times 10^8 \text{ N·m}$ (port-side bending) #### Step 2: Compute Individual Stress Components Vertical bending stress: $$ \sigma_v = \frac{M_v\,y}{I_v} $$ Using $I_v = Z_v \cdot y_{\max} = 17.244 \times 9.0 = 155.2 \text{ m}^4$: $$ \sigma_v = \frac{1.5 \times 10^9 \times 9.0}{155.2} \;\approx\; 8.70 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 87.0 \text{ MPa} $$ Horizontal bending stress (assume $I_h$ for symmetric box $\approx I_v \times (B/D)^2$ ratio correction). For a first-order estimate we use $I_h \approx 0.30 \cdot I_v$ (typical ratio for fine ships): $$ I_h \approx 0.30 \times 155.2 = 46.6 \text{ m}^4 $$ $$ \sigma_h = \frac{M_h\,z}{I_h} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^8 \times 16.0}{46.6} \;\approx\; 1.55 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 155.0 \text{ MPa} $$ #### Step 3: Combine (Algebraic Sum at the Same Point) At the upper deck corner, both stresses are tensile (sagging opens up the deck, port-bending opens up the port side), so they **add**: $$ \sigma_{total} = \sigma_v + \sigma_h = 87.0 + 155.0 = 242.0 \text{ MPa} $$ #### Step 4: Compare to Allowable For mild steel with yield $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$: $$ \sigma_{total} = 242.0 \text{ MPa} \;>\; \sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa} $$ > [!WARNING] > **The corner point is overstressed by ~3 %.** In practice, the designer would either (i) increase plating thickness in the corner region, (ii) add a *corner longitudinal* (already present, but its area needs upgrading), or (iii) use higher-tensile steel. This is precisely why **corner detail design** is critical in biaxial bending. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Device on Hull Girder For the *same* mid-ship box section (B = 32 m, D = 18 m, t = 25 mm), the following table shows the *fractional contribution* of each device to $I_v$ and $I_h$: | Configuration | $I_v$ (m⁴) | $I_h$ (m⁴) | $Z_v$ (m³) | $Z_h$ (m³) | % Change $Z_v$ vs. bare | |---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:| | Bare box (plating only) | 150.0 | 102.0 | 16.67 | 6.38 | — (baseline) | | + 4 corner longitudinals (4 × 40 cm²) | 155.2 | 105.5 | 17.24 | 6.59 | +3.4 % | | + 8 side longitudinals (port & starboard) | 156.1 | 107.3 | 17.34 | 6.71 | +4.0 % | | + Side stringer at $y = 0.6D$ | 158.5 | 108.4 | 17.61 | 6.78 | +5.6 % | | + Deck longitudinals + bottom longls. | 161.2 | 109.6 | 17.91 | 6.85 | +7.4 % | | + Web frames + brackets (stiffness) | 161.2 | 109.6 | 17.91 | 6.85 | 7.4 % (no direct $Z$ gain, but stability ↑) | > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Plating contributes the *bulk* of the section modulus. Longitudinals add a relatively modest ~5–10 %, but their *spacing* and *end connections* are crucial for the *buckling strength* and *fatigue life* of the panel. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hull Cross-Section Showing Biaxial Bending Devices ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TOP["UPPER DECK PLANE - Top Flange for VBM"] DL1["Deck Longitudinals
Spacing 600 mm"] DT1["Deck Transverses
Spacing 2.5 m"] DC1["Deck Centerline"] DF1["Deck Web Frames
Every 5th frame space"] end subgraph MID["SIDE SHELL PLANE - Webs for VBM / Flanges for HBM"] SL1["Side Longitudinals
Spacing 700 mm"] SF1["Side Frames / Hold Frames
Spacing 2.5 m"] SS1["Side Stringer
At 0.6D height"] WB1["Web Frame Brackets
Triangular stiffeners"] end subgraph BOTTOM["BOTTOM SHELL PLANE - Bottom Flange for VBM"] BL1["Bottom Longitudinals
Spacing 600 mm"] FL1["Floors / Bottom Transverses
Spacing 2.5 m"] GF1["Girders - Intercostal
Longitudinals"] SO1["Solid Floor Web Frames"] end subgraph LOADS["BIAXIAL LOADING INPUT"] M_V["M_v - Vertical BM
Hogging / Sagging"] M_H["M_h - Horizontal BM
Beam Sea Loading"] T_W["T_w - Warping Torsion"] end M_V --> TOP M_V --> BOTTOM M_H --> MID T_W --> TOP T_W --> MID T_W --> BOTTOM DT1 --> SF1 SF1 --> FL1 SL1 --> SS1 DL1 --> DF1 BL1 --> SO1 WB1 --> DT1 WB1 --> FL1 WB1 --> SF1 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Logical Load Path in Biaxial Bending ```mermaid flowchart LR A["External Load
Wave + Still Water"] --> B["Hull Shell Plating
Acts as flange and web"] B --> C["Longitudinals
Carry axial stress"] B --> D["Transverses
Distribute load laterally"] C --> E["Effective Section Modulus Z_v and Z_h"] D --> E E --> F["Combined Stress at Corner
sigma_v + sigma_h"] F --> G{"Within
Allowable?"} G -->|Yes| H["Design OK"] G -->|No| I["Increase Plate Thickness
or Stiffener Area"] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Cross-section Symbol Map (for KTU board answers) ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["B = Breadth of ship"] node2["D = Depth of hull"] node3["T = Design draught"] node4["NA = Neutral Axis
For VBM - Horizontal line
For HBM - Vertical line"] node5["y = Vertical coord
From NA"] node6["z = Horizontal coord
From CL"] node7["A_s = Stiffener area"] node8["I_v, I_h = Moment of
Inertia values"] node9["Z_v, Z_h = Section
Modulus values"] node1 --> node4 node2 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node4 --> node6 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Failure of Biaxial Devices) ```mermaid flowchart TB inputA["Wave-induced biaxial bending moment M_v and M_h"] blockA["Primary Resistance System
Plating + Longitudinals"] blockB["Secondary Resistance System
Transverses + Web Frames"] blockC["Tertiary Stability System
Brackets + Stringers"] blockD["Combined Stress Output
sigma = Mv y / Iv + Mh z / Ih"] inputA --> blockA inputA --> blockB inputA --> blockC blockA --> blockD blockB --> blockD blockC --> blockD failA["Failure Mode A
Plate Buckling"] -.-> blockA failB["Failure Mode B
Stiffener Tripping"] -.-> blockA failC["Failure Mode C
Frame Collapse"] -.-> blockB failD["Failure Mode D
Bracket Cracking"] -.-> blockC ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When a board question asks "Sketch a typical mid-ship section and mark the devices resisting biaxial bending," reproduce the *Mermaid diagrams* above as a clear annotated freehand drawing. Label the *flanges* and *webs* of the box-girder analogy explicitly — examiners reward that insight. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** *Define biaxial bending as applied to a ship hull. Identify the two orthogonal axes about which the hull girder bends.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Biaxial bending in a ship hull is the simultaneous bending of the hull girder about **two perpendicular horizontal axes** — the *transverse (athwartships) axis* and the *longitudinal (fore-aft) axis*. The hull experiences both a **vertical bending moment (VBM)** producing hogging/sagging and a **horizontal bending moment (HBM)** producing port-starboard flexure, generally together with a warping torsion. **Mark Split (Board Pattern):** Definition (1) + Identification of VBM/HBM (1) + Mention of simultaneous nature (1). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Repeated Concept]** *List any **five** structural devices fitted in a ship to resist biaxial bending, and state the function of any one.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Longitudinals** — Fore-aft stiffeners; increase $I_v$ and $I_h$ (primary bending resistance). 2. **Transverses (frames/floors/beams)** — Athwartships members; distribute load, resist local pressure and buckling. 3. **Web frames** — Heavy transverse diaphragms; prevent column-buckling of longitudinals. 4. **Side stringers** — Longitudinal girders; act as intermediate flanges in the box girder. 5. **Brackets** — Triangular gussets; provide rigid-joint action between longitudinals and transverses. *Function of longitudinals (chosen):* They run parallel to the ship length, increase the effective flange area of the box-girder hull section, and thereby directly raise the section modulus $Z_v$ and $Z_h$, providing primary resistance to both vertical and horizontal bending. **Mark Split (Board Pattern):** 5 devices × 0.4 marks = 2 marks + 1 mark for the function. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE rule: Every Module-2 question carries a 50 % *internal choice*. You must solve **either Question A or Question B** in full. Each is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- #### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *Longitudinal + Section Modulus Focus* **Q3(a). [7 Marks]** *Explain with a neat sketch the role of **longitudinals** as primary devices resisting biaxial bending. State how their spacing affects hull-girder section modulus.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Definition & location (1 mark):** Longitudinals are rolled-section stiffeners (T-bars, L-angles, bulb plates) welded fore-aft to the inner surface of deck, bottom, side shell, inner bottom, and tank-top plating. 2. **Mechanism of biaxial resistance (2 marks):** - For **VBM**, longitudinals on the **deck and bottom** increase the effective flange area $\Rightarrow$ raise $I_v$ and $Z_v$. - For **HBM**, longitudinals on **port and starboard side shells** act as the flanges of a sideways box girder $\Rightarrow$ raise $I_h$ and $Z_h$. - Thus the same longitudinal system provides resistance in **both planes** — that is what makes it a *biaxial* device. 3. **Effect of spacing (2 marks):** - Reducing longitudinal spacing $s$ **does not** directly increase $I$ or $Z$ (these depend on total material placed at the extreme fibre, not on the number of pieces). - But *closer spacing* **dramatically reduces local plate slenderness** $\beta = (b/t)\sqrt{\sigma_y/E}$, prevents plate buckling, and allows the full plating to be counted as effective in the section modulus. - Rule of thumb: **optimum longitudinal spacing $\approx$ 0.4 × transverse spacing** to keep $\beta$ below the IACS limit and avoid shear-lag loss. 4. **Sketch & key annotation (2 marks):** Draw the box-girder section with longitudinals at deck/bottom/side; label the VBM & HBM neutral axes; show that the same stiffeners serve both flanges and webs. --- **Q3(b). [7 Marks]** *For a mid-ship section modelled as a rectangular box of breadth $B = 28 \text{ m}$, depth $D = 16 \text{ m}$, uniform plating thickness $t = 22 \text{ mm}$, and four corner longitudinals of equivalent area $A_s = 35 \text{ cm}^2$ each, compute: (i) the vertical section modulus $Z_v$, and (ii) the vertical bending stress if $M_v = 1200 \text{ MN·m}$ is applied.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** Step 1 — Vertical moment of inertia of bare plating (1 mark): $$ I_{v,\,plate} = \frac{B\,t\,D^2}{2} + \frac{t\,D^3}{6} = \frac{28 \times 0.022 \times 256}{2} + \frac{0.022 \times 4096}{6} $$ $$ I_{v,\,plate} = 78.85 + 15.02 = 93.87 \text{ m}^4 $$ Step 2 — Contribution of corner longitudinals (1 mark): $$ I_{v,\,stiff} = 4 \times A_s \times (D/2)^2 = 4 \times 0.0035 \times 64 = 0.896 \text{ m}^4 $$ Step 3 — Total $I_v$ (1 mark): $$ I_v = 93.87 + 0.896 = 94.77 \text{ m}^4 $$ Step 4 — Section modulus (1 mark): $$ Z_v = \frac{I_v}{D/2} = \frac{94.77}{8.0} = 11.85 \text{ m}^3 $$ Step 5 — Bending stress (1.5 marks): $$ \sigma_v = \frac{M_v}{Z_v} = \frac{1200 \times 10^6}{11.85} = 1.013 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 101.3 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 6 — Conclusion (0.5 mark): $$ \sigma_v \approx 101 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa} $$ **Design is safe** (factor of safety $\approx 2.32$). --- #### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Side Stringer + Combined Stress Focus* **Q4(a). [7 Marks]** *With the aid of a diagram, explain the function of **side stringers** and **web frames** as devices resisting biaxial bending in the mid-body of a ship.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Side stringers – function (2 marks):** Side stringers are longitudinal girders fitted on the side shell at a height of typically $0.6D$ from the baseline, running the full length of the cargo hold. They act as an **intermediate flange** in the hull box-girder, reducing the effective span of the side frames and increasing the section modulus $Z_v$ in the upper region of the hull. 2. **Web frames – function (2 marks):** Web frames are heavy transverse plate diaphragms fitted at intervals of 3–5 frame spaces, consisting of a web plate, face flats, and tripping brackets. They transfer the biaxial stress between deck/bottom and side shell, prevent column-buckling of the longitudinals, and act as load-distribution diaphragms. 3. **Combined role in biaxial bending (2 marks):** Together, the side stringer and web frame create a **closed-loop box** in the side-shell region. This mini box-girder inside the larger hull box-girder is highly efficient at resisting both VBM and HBM, and the rigid joint at their intersection (provided by brackets) ensures monolithic action. 4. **Sketch (1 mark):** Show the mid-ship section with a web frame spanning deck-to-bottom, the side stringer at $0.6D$, brackets at the stringer–web-frame intersection, and arrows indicating the two bending moments. --- **Q4(b). [7 Marks]** *At a critical corner point of the mid-ship section, the vertical bending moment is $M_v = 900 \text{ MN·m}$ (sagging) and the horizontal bending moment is $M_h = 250 \text{ MN·m}$. Given $I_v = 95 \text{ m}^4$, $I_h = 40 \text{ m}^4$, $B = 28 \text{ m}$, $D = 16 \text{ m}$, find: (i) the maximum biaxial bending stress at the upper-deck corner, and (ii) whether the design is safe if $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** Step 1 — Identify the critical point coordinates (1 mark): Upper-deck corner, $y = +D/2 = +8 \text{ m}$, $z = +B/2 = +14 \text{ m}$. Step 2 — Vertical bending stress (1.5 marks): $$ \sigma_v = \frac{M_v\,y}{I_v} = \frac{900 \times 10^6 \times 8.0}{95} = 7.58 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 75.8 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 3 — Horizontal bending stress (1.5 marks): $$ \sigma_h = \frac{M_h\,z}{I_h} = \frac{250 \times 10^6 \times 14.0}{40} = 8.75 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 87.5 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 4 — Combined stress (1 mark): At the upper-deck corner both stresses are tensile (sagging opens deck; port-side HBM opens port side), so they add: $$ \sigma_{total} = \sigma_v + \sigma_h = 75.8 + 87.5 = 163.3 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 5 — Safety check (1.5 marks): $$ \sigma_{total} = 163.3 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa} $$ Factor of safety $= 235/163.3 \approx 1.44$. Step 6 — Conclusion (0.5 mark): The corner is safe, but the margin is moderate; in heavy weather the design moment may rise by ~30 %, so a **higher-tensile grade** or **local corner plate thickening** is recommended. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic (Module 2):** > > 1. **Forgetting the units** — $I$ must be in m⁴, $M$ in N·m, $y$ in m, $\sigma$ in Pa. Many students write $M$ in t·m and $\sigma$ in MPa and skip conversion. **Marks lost: 1 per sub-question.** > > 2. **Confusing the neutral axis for VBM and HBM** — A common error is to use the same $y$ for both moments. The vertical bending stress uses the *vertical* distance from the *horizontal* NA, and the horizontal bending stress uses the *horizontal* distance from the *vertical* NA. **Marks lost: 1–2 per sub-question.** > > 3. **Ignoring the algebraic sign of stresses** — At a corner, both stresses may be *tensile* (add) or *compressive* (subtract). Always draw a free-body sketch of which side of the hull is in tension under each loading. **Marks lost: 1–2 per sub-question.** > > 4. **Writing formula but not the boxed answer** — KTU examiners award the final mark only for a *boxed, numerical answer* in the correct units. **Marks lost: 1.** > > 5. **Skipping the sketch in sub-part (a)** — A neat, labeled box-girder sketch with flanges/webs marked carries **2 marks** in any 7-mark sub-part. Students who skip the sketch lose up to 2 marks even if their theory is correct. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before the exam.** - [ ] **Biaxial bending = VBM + HBM** acting on the hull girder simultaneously. - [ ] **Five device families:** Longitudinals, Transverses, Web Frames, Stringers, Brackets. - [ ] **Hull = Box girder** — deck & bottom = flanges for VBM; sides = webs for VBM. - [ ] **Reverse role for HBM** — sides = flanges; deck & bottom = webs. - [ ] **Section modulus formula:** $Z_v = B t D + \dfrac{t D^2}{3} + 2 A_s D$ (for the simplest box model). - [ ] **Combined stress:** $\sigma = \dfrac{M_v y}{I_v} + \dfrac{M_h z}{I_h}$. - [ ] **Resultant moment:** $M_{res} = \sqrt{M_v^2 + M_h^2}$. - [ ] **Longitudinal spacing** ≈ 0.4 × transverse spacing (rule of thumb). - [ ] **Corner points are the hottest** — most highly stressed in biaxial bending. - [ ] **Allowable steel stress** ≈ 175 MPa for mild steel (IACS UR-S11). - [ ] **Always include a labeled sketch** in any 7-mark answer (worth 2 marks). - [ ] **Always box the final numerical answer** with units (worth 1 mark). - [ ] **Be careful with sign conventions** — sagging opens deck, hogging closes it; port-bending opens port side. - [ ] **Plate thickness + stiffener area** are the two main *design levers* for biaxial bending. - [ ] **Web frames and brackets** do not directly increase $Z$ but prevent buckling and ensure load-path continuity. - [ ] **IACS / Classification rules** prescribe *minimum $Z_v$* based on $L$, $B$, $T$, $C_B$. ---

topics.title: Cold Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cold Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cold Bending** is a primary plate-forming process in shipbuilding wherein steel plates and sections are plastically deformed into curved geometries (such as the curved hull shell plating, bilge plates, or shear strakes) at **ambient (room) temperature**, without the application of any external heat source. The deformation is achieved purely through mechanical force, taking the material beyond its yield point in the outer and inner fibers while the neutral axis remains unstrained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Cold bending is the process of forming steel plates and rolled sections into curved shapes by the application of mechanical load at ordinary atmospheric temperatures, typically using rolling machines, press brakes, or bending rolls, where the induced strains exceed the elastic limit but remain below the fracture strain of the material.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to bend a thick hardcover textbook (without breaking or heating it). If you simply press the book between your hands and a table edge, you can only flex it slightly — and the moment you release it, it springs back almost straight. This "spring-back" is the elastic recovery. To make the curve permanent, you would need to bend it *much further* than what feels "natural" — so far that the cover fibers on the outside actually stretch and the inner fibers compress permanently. Only then will the book hold its curved shape after release.

**Cold bending of steel plates works exactly the same way:**
- The **3-roll pyramid machine** acts like your two hands plus the table edge.
- The **top roller** pushes down, the **two bottom rollers** support and feed the plate.
- As the plate passes through, the **outer fibers stretch**, the **inner fibers compress**, and the **centerline (neutral axis)** neither stretches nor compresses.
- The induced strain must exceed the elastic limit so the curve is permanent.
- A small amount of **springback** always occurs when the load is released — engineers compensate for this.

### 1.3 Why Cold Bending? — Engineering Justification

| Aspect | Cold Bending | Hot Bending |
|---|---|---|
| Heat required | **None** | Required (~900 °C) |
| Surface finish | **Smooth, untouched** | Scale, oxidation, decarburized layer |
| Dimensional accuracy | **High (within ±1–2 mm/m)** | Lower due to thermal shrinkage |
| Production rate | **Faster, repeatable** | Slower (heating + cooling cycle) |
| Plate thickness limit | Limited (~25 mm for AH36) | No real limit |
| Cost | **Lower** (no fuel, no skilled heat-treatment crew) | Higher |
| Material property change | Negligible | Possible strength changes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Modern shipyards prefer cold bending for plates up to **~25 mm thickness** of mild steel and **~20 mm** for high-tensile steel (AH36/DH36/EH36). Above these limits, hot bending or line heating is recommended because cold bending beyond capacity can cause cracking, orange peel, or excessive springback.

### 1.4 Categories of Cold Bending (KTU Classification)

1. **Roll Bending (3-Roll Pyramid Bending)** — For flat plates forming cylindrical or developable curved shells (hull side plating, bottom plating).
2. **Press Bending (Die/Punch Bending)** — For sharp bends, flange formation, knuckle radii.
3. **Profile Bending (Section Bending)** — For rolled sections like angles, T-bars, channels, flat bars used in longitudinals and stiffeners.
4. **Edge Bending / Cold Flanging** — For forming the edges of plates (flanging the deck plate edges downward, etc.).
5. **Stretch Forming** — Used for complex double-curvature panels (rare in conventional hulls, common in superstructures).
6. **Wedge Rolls / Initial Pinch Bending** — First step in roll bending, where one end is pre-pinched to start the curvature.

### 1.5 Springback — The Persistent Headache

**Springback** is the elastic recovery of the plate after the bending load is removed. The material "remembers" its original flat shape and partially unbends.

$$\alpha_{springback} = \alpha_{bend} \times \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$$

where:
- $\alpha_{bend}$ = angle through which the plate was actually bent under load
- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength of the plate
- $E$ = Young's modulus (~**210 GPa** for steel)
- The remaining curvature is the *permanent* shape retained by the hull.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Tip:** The harder the steel (higher $\sigma_y$), the greater the springback. This is why mild steel is easier to cold-bend accurately than high-tensile steel — a frequently tested point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Cold Bending — Step-by-Step Logic

When a steel plate of thickness **$t$** is passed through a 3-roll pyramid bender, the following sequence occurs at every fiber of the cross-section:

### Step 1 — Elastic Stage
- The plate flexes like a beam.
- Outer fibers elongate, inner fibers shorten.
- Stress varies **linearly** from the neutral axis: $\sigma = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{\rho}$.
- **No permanent set**; full recovery on release.

### Step 2 — Yield Stage
- Outer fibers reach the yield stress $\sigma_y$.
- Plastic zones start at the extreme fibers and propagate inward.
- Inner fibers are still elastic.

### Step 3 — Plastic Stage (Cold Bending Region)
- Both outer and inner fibers yield.
- The cross-section becomes a **fully plastic** hinge.
- Permanent curvature is locked into the plate.
- The neutral axis remains at the **geometric center** (no shifting, because strains are small).

### Step 4 — Elastic Recovery (Springback)
- After load release, the elastic portion of strain recovers.
- The permanent curvature $\dfrac{1}{R_{permanent}}$ is slightly less than the loaded curvature $\dfrac{1}{R_{loaded}}$.
- Compensated for by **overbending** during rolling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The neutral axis in cold bending stays at the mid-thickness because strains are small (typically < 5 %). This is a critical difference from deep drawing where neutral axis shifts.

## 2.2 Fiber Strain Distribution

For a plate of thickness **$t$** bent to radius **$R$** (measured to the neutral axis), the strain at any fiber at distance **$y$** from the neutral axis is:

$$\varepsilon(y) = \frac{y}{R}$$

- Outer fiber ($y = t/2$): $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{t}{2R}$ (tension)
- Inner fiber ($y = -t/2$): $\varepsilon_{min} = -\dfrac{t}{2R}$ (compression)
- Neutral axis ($y = 0$): $\varepsilon = 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Rule:** To avoid surface cracking, the maximum outer-fiber strain must be **≤ 5 % elongation** at fracture. This sets the **minimum allowable bend radius**:
> $$R_{min} = \frac{t}{2 \cdot \varepsilon_{allowable}}$$
> For mild steel ($\varepsilon_{allowable} \approx 0.05$), $R_{min} \approx 10t$. For high-tensile steel ($\varepsilon_{allowable} \approx 0.03$), $R_{min} \approx 16t$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Cold Bending (Print & Memorize)**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Outer-fiber strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{t}{2R}$ | dimensionless | Used to verify against allowable strain |
| 2 | Minimum bend radius | $R_{min} = \dfrac{t}{2\varepsilon_{allow}}$ | mm | Lower limit to avoid cracking |
| 3 | Practical minimum radius (mild steel) | $R_{min} \approx 3t$ to $4t$ | mm | Empirical, from IACS / class rules |
| 4 | Practical minimum radius (HT steel) | $R_{min} \approx 4t$ to $5t$ | mm | Higher because lower ductility |
| 5 | Bending stress (elastic) | $\sigma = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$ | MPa | Pure bending theory |
| 6 | Plastic moment | $M_p = \sigma_y \cdot \dfrac{b t^2}{4}$ | N·mm | $b$ = plate width |
| 7 | Elastic moment | $M_e = \sigma_y \cdot \dfrac{b t^2}{6}$ | N·mm | At first yield |
| 8 | Shape factor | $S = \dfrac{M_p}{M_e} = 1.5$ | — | For rectangular cross-section |
| 9 | Springback angle | $\Delta\alpha = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$ | radians | Used for overbending compensation |
| 10 | Roll-bending force (approx.) | $F \approx \dfrac{\sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^2}{2R_{roller}}$ | N | Pyramid-type bender |
| 11 | Plate feed per pass | $L_{feed} = \sqrt{2 R_{roller} \cdot t}$ | mm | Approximate engagement length |
| 12 | Young's modulus of steel | $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ | MPa | **Universal constant for shipbuilding steel** |
| 13 | Yield of mild steel | $\sigma_y \approx 235$ | MPa | Grade A |
| 14 | Yield of HT steel | $\sigma_y \approx 355$ | MPa | Grade AH36 |

> **Critical Note on Table Symbols:** In every formula above, the symbol $R$ denotes the **bend radius of the plate (to neutral axis)**, while $R_{roller}$ denotes the **radius of the bending roller**. Do NOT confuse the two.

## 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility

1. **Hull Shell Plating** — Bottom and side shell plates must be curved to follow the hull form. Cold roll-bending is the default manufacturing method.
2. **Longitudinals & Stiffeners** — Profile bending machines bend T-bars, angles, flat bars to match the hull curvature along their length.
3. **Bilge Plates** — Sharp transition from bottom to side shell is achieved by cold press bending with a small die radius.
4. **Deck Plates** — Edges are cold-flanged downward to meet the sheer strake, avoiding the need for welding an extra fillet.
5. **Superstructure Plates** — Roll bent into cylindrical/torispherical shapes for bulkheads, funnels, etc.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Note:** A modern shipyard's plate bending shop typically processes **30–80 tonnes of plate per shift** through 3-roll pyramid machines, with target accuracies of **±1 mm/m** of camber.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Outer-Fiber Strain Equation

**Statement:** Show that the outer-fiber strain of a plate of thickness $t$ bent to radius $R$ is $\varepsilon = t/(2R)$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of Bending**
Consider a plate of original (flat) thickness $t$. When bent to radius $R$, the neutral axis (mid-thickness) traces an arc of length:

$$L_{neutral} = R \cdot \theta$$

where $\theta$ is the included bend angle in radians.

**Step 2 — Outer Fiber Length**
The outer fiber lies at a distance $R + t/2$ from the center of curvature. Its arc length after bending is:

$$L_{outer} = \left(R + \frac{t}{2}\right) \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3 — Inner Fiber Length**
The inner fiber lies at a distance $R - t/2$:

$$L_{inner} = \left(R - \frac{t}{2}\right) \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4 — Strain Definition**
Strain is the change in length divided by the original (flat) length. The original length is $L_{neutral}$ (since neutral axis is unstretched). For the outer fiber:

$$\varepsilon_{outer} = \frac{L_{outer} - L_{neutral}}{L_{neutral}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{outer} &= \frac{\left(R + \dfrac{t}{2}\right) \cdot \theta - R \cdot \theta}{R \cdot \theta} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{R \cdot \theta + \dfrac{t}{2} \cdot \theta - R \cdot \theta}{R \cdot \theta} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{t}{2} \cdot \theta}{R \cdot \theta} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{t}{2R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion**
Outer-fiber strain:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{outer} = \frac{t}{2R}}$$

This is the **fundamental cold-bending strain equation** and the foundation for the minimum-radius rule.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Bend Radius (IACS Empirical Form)

**Statement:** For a high-tensile steel plate of thickness $t = 20$ mm with allowable elongation 22 %, find the minimum permissible cold-bending radius using the strain criterion, and compare with the empirical $R_{min} = 4t$ rule.

**Step 1 — Allowable Strain (taking safety factor)**
Total elongation at fracture is 22 %. Engineering practice uses a conservative allowable strain of about **5 %** (≈ 23 % of fracture strain, or strain at uniform elongation limit).

$$\varepsilon_{allow} = 0.05$$

**Step 2 — Apply Minimum Radius Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \frac{t}{2 \cdot \varepsilon_{allow}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{20}{2 \cdot 0.05} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{20}{0.10} \\[6pt]
&= 200 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Rule Check**

$$R_{min,empirical} = 4 \cdot t = 4 \cdot 20 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison & Interpretation**
The strain-based criterion gives 200 mm, while the empirical rule gives 80 mm. The empirical rule is more permissive because it represents **practical shop-floor limits** based on observed forming behavior — not just theoretical fracture. In class-approved work, the more conservative value (whichever is larger) is adopted; for safety-critical applications, the strain-based value of **200 mm** governs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Springback Angle

**Statement:** A plate is bent through a loaded angle $\alpha = 10°$ at a roller. The plate has $\sigma_y = 355$ MPa (AH36) and $E = 210$ GPa. Compute the springback angle $\Delta\alpha$ and the permanent bend angle.

**Step 1 — Springback Formula**

$$\Delta\alpha = \alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\alpha &= 10° \cdot \frac{355 \times 10^6}{210 \times 10^9} \\[6pt]
&= 10° \cdot 0.001690 \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.0169° 
\end{aligned}
$$

(Equivalently in radians: $\Delta\alpha \approx 10° \cdot 0.001690 \approx 0.000295 \text{ rad}$.)

**Step 3 — Permanent Bend Angle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{permanent} &= \alpha - \Delta\alpha \\[6pt]
&= 10° - 0.0169° \\[6pt]
&= 9.9831°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
Springback is **negligible in absolute angle terms** for small plate angles, but in tight-tolerance shipbuilding (especially for knuckle radii and compound curves) it must be compensated by **over-bending** — bending slightly more than the required final angle.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Required Roll-Bending Force (Pyramid 3-Roll Bender)

**Statement:** Compute the rolling force for a mild-steel plate of width $b = 1500$ mm, thickness $t = 16$ mm, on a pyramid bender with top roller radius $R_{roller} = 250$ mm. Yield $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Plastic Moment per Unit Width**

$$
M_p = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot t^2}{4} \text{ per unit width}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Plastic Moment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{p,total} &= \frac{\sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^2}{4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{235 \cdot 1500 \cdot 16^2}{4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{235 \cdot 1500 \cdot 256}{4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{90{,}240{,}000}{4} \\[6pt]
&= 22{,}560{,}000 \text{ N·mm} \\[6pt]
&= 22.56 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Force on Top Roller (approximate, considering bending over roller radius)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &\approx \frac{M_{p,total}}{R_{roller}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{22{,}560{,}000}{250} \\[6pt]
&= 90{,}240 \text{ N} \\[6pt]
&\approx 90.24 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
A 3-roll pyramid bender for typical shipyard plate forming has hydraulic capacities of **500 kN to 3000 kN**, comfortably above this requirement, indicating the chosen plate (16 mm) is well within machine capacity.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Cold Bending

| Plate Parameter | Cold Bending Recommended | Hot Bending / Line Heating Recommended |
|---|---|---|
| Thickness (mild steel) | ≤ **25 mm** | > 25 mm |
| Thickness (HT steel AH36) | ≤ **20 mm** | > 20 mm |
| Plate width | Any (machine-dependent) | Any |
| Required bend radius | ≥ **3t** (mild) or **4t** (HT) | < 3t |
| Production volume | High (mass production) | Low / one-off |
| Tolerances | ±1–2 mm/m | ±5–10 mm/m |
| Surface finish | Must be preserved | Acceptable to be reworked |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 3-Roll Pyramid Bending Machine — Functional Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the functional architecture of a 3-roll pyramid plate bending machine — the workhorse of any shipyard plate-forming shop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flat Steel Plate Infeed] --> B[Entry Guide Rollers]
    B --> C[Bottom Roller Left Fixed]
    C --> D[Top Pyramid Roller Hydraulic Downforce]
    D --> E[Bend Zone Contact Arc]
    E --> F[Bottom Roller Right Adjustable]
    F --> G[Curved Plate Outfeed]
    G --> H[Springback Compensation Camber Check]
    H --> I{Within Tolerance}
    I -->|Yes| J[Accept and Mark]
    I -->|No| K[Repass Through Pyramid Rolls]
    K --> D
    H --> L[Strain Gauge Monitoring]
    L --> M[Outer Fiber Strain less than 5 percent]
    M --> N[Safe Pass]
    D --> O[Hydraulic Power Pack 500 to 3000 kN]
    O --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The flowchart represents the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a 3-roll pyramid bender cycle, showing the decision-loop logic for springback compensation and the safety check on outer-fiber strain.

## 4.2 Cross-Section Through a Plate in the Bend Zone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Roller Pushes Down Force F] --> B[Contact Point]
    B --> C[Outer Fiber Stretched Tension]
    B --> D[Neutral Axis Unchanged Length]
    B --> E[Inner Fiber Compressed Compression]
    C --> F[Strain plus t divided by 2R]
    D --> G[Strain equals Zero]
    E --> H[Strain minus t divided by 2R]
```

## 4.3 Cold Bending Process Decision Tree (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shipyard Receives Cutting Plan] --> B[Plate Thickness t]
    B --> C{t less than or equal to 25 mm Mild}
    C -->|Yes| D[Route to 3-Roll Pyramid Bender]
    C -->|No| E[Route to Hot Bending or Line Heating]
    D --> F{Required Radius R greater than 3t}
    F -->|Yes| G[Proceed with Cold Roll Bending]
    F -->|No| H[Reduce Radius Use Press Brake]
    G --> I[Measure Camber After First Pass]
    I --> J{Camber Matches Required Profile}
    J -->|Yes| K[Accept Plate]
    J -->|No| L[Overbend Compensation]
    L --> M[Repass Through Rolls]
    M --> I
    H --> N[Press Brake Die Selection]
    N --> O[Sharp Bend Formed]
```

## 4.4 Fiber Strain Distribution Diagram (Textual Schematic)

```
       ─────────────────────────────  Outer fiber (TENSION)
          ↑ elongation = +t/(2R)
       ─────────────────────────────
       ─────────────────────────────  Upper quarter
       ─────────────────────────────
       ══════════════════════════════  NEUTRAL AXIS (strain = 0)
       ─────────────────────────────  Lower quarter
       ─────────────────────────────
          ↓ compression = -t/(2R)
       ─────────────────────────────  Inner fiber (COMPRESSION)

              (Thickness = t, Bend Radius = R)
```

> [!TIP]
> **For Examiners:** This is the most-asked schematic in KTU Module 2 cold bending questions. Always label the **neutral axis**, the **outer fiber (tension)**, and the **inner fiber (compression)**, and write the strain formula $\varepsilon = y/R$ beside the diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define cold bending. Mention any two advantages of cold bending over hot bending in ship construction.**

**Model Answer:**
**Cold bending** is the process of plastically deforming steel plates and rolled sections into curved shapes at ambient (room) temperature using mechanical force without any external heating.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Better surface finish** — no oxidation scale, no decarburization, paint-ready surface straight out of the bender.
2. **Higher dimensional accuracy** — no thermal distortion; camber tolerances within ±1–2 mm/m are achievable consistently.
3. *(Any other: lower fuel cost, faster cycle time, no skilled heat-treatment crew required.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is springback in cold bending? How is it compensated?**

**Model Answer:**
**Springback** is the partial elastic recovery of a plate toward its original flat shape after the bending load is removed. It occurs because the inner elastic portion of strain recovers once the rollers are disengaged.

$$\Delta\alpha = \alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$$

**Compensation:** The plate is **over-bent** by an angle equal to the expected springback during rolling, so the final released curvature matches the design radius. Higher-yield steel needs more overbending.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the principle of operation of a 3-roll pyramid plate bending machine used for cold bending in shipyards. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A mild steel plate of thickness **18 mm** and width **2000 mm** is to be cold-bent to a radius of **150 mm** using a 3-roll pyramid bender. Calculate (i) the outer fiber strain, (ii) the minimum permissible bend radius (taking allowable strain = 5 %), and (iii) comment on the feasibility of the operation. Take $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa and $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa. (7 Marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Principle of 3-Roll Pyramid Bending Machine** (7 Marks)

The **3-roll pyramid plate bending machine** is the most common cold-bending equipment in shipyards. It consists of:

- **Two bottom rollers** (driven, fixed at the rear, adjustable at the front for taper bending).
- **One top roller** (idler or driven, mounted in a hydraulic ram that applies downward force).
- The two bottom rollers form a **pyramid seat** with the top roller at the apex.

**Working Principle:**
1. The flat plate is fed between the top roller and the two bottom rollers.
2. The top roller is pressed down hydraulically, creating a localized plastic bend at each side of the contact line.
3. The plate is then **rolled forward and backward** repeatedly (or fed continuously).
4. With each pass, the curvature builds up incrementally; the final camber is set by adjusting the top roller depth and the number of passes.
5. The two bends at the sides converge to a uniform cylindrical curvature as the plate moves longitudinally.

**Sketch (Block Diagram of Operation):**

```
     Top Roller ↓ F (hydraulic)
         ╱╲
        ╱  ╲
       ╱    ╲
   ───╱──────╲───  ← Curved plate
     ╱        ╲
    ╱__________╲
   Bottom L   Bottom R
   (fixed)    (adjustable)
```

**Advantages:** Continuous operation, suitable for long plates, uniform curvature.

**(b) Numerical Solution** (7 Marks)

**(i) Outer fiber strain**

Given: $t = 18$ mm, $R = 150$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{outer} &= \frac{t}{2R} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{18}{2 \times 150} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{18}{300} \\[6pt]
&= 0.06 = 6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Minimum permissible bend radius**

Given: allowable strain $\varepsilon_{allow} = 0.05$, $t = 18$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \frac{t}{2 \cdot \varepsilon_{allow}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{18}{2 \times 0.05} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{18}{0.10} \\[6pt]
&= 180 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Feasibility Comment**

- Required radius (150 mm) is **less than** the minimum permissible (180 mm).
- Outer fiber strain (6 %) **exceeds** allowable (5 %).
- **Conclusion:** Cold bending to 150 mm radius is **NOT feasible** for 18 mm plate without risk of surface cracking. Either:
  - Use **hot bending** instead, OR
  - Use a **larger radius** (≥ 180 mm), OR
  - Use **line heating** for the final tightening.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Describe the cold bending process for shipbuilding plates. Compare cold bending with hot bending. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A high-tensile steel plate (AH36) of thickness **12 mm** is to be bent on a 3-roll pyramid bender with a top roller of radius **200 mm**. Calculate (i) the plastic moment per unit width, (ii) the total plastic moment for a plate width of **1800 mm**, and (iii) the approximate rolling force. Take $\sigma_y = 355$ MPa. (7 Marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Cold Bending Process and Comparison** (7 Marks)

**Description of Cold Bending Process:**
Cold bending in shipbuilding follows this sequence:
1. **Plate Cutting** — Plate is cut to shape (often CNC plasma/oxy) according to the nesting plan.
2. **Identification of Curvature** — Plates marked with the required camber direction and magnitude.
3. **Pre-Setting Rollers** — Top roller depth set to give an initial pass curvature.
4. **Initial Pinch Pass** — One end is pre-pinched between the top roller and one bottom roller to "start" the curve.
5. **Continuous Rolling** — Plate is fed through rollers repeatedly until full camber is achieved.
6. **Camber Verification** — Template or laser scanner checks the curvature.
7. **Springback Compensation** — If short of camber, an additional pass is made.
8. **Marking and Routing** — Plate is marked and sent to the next stage (welding/assembly).

**Comparison Table (Cold vs Hot Bending):**

| Aspect | Cold Bending | Hot Bending |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Ambient (~25 °C) | ~900 °C |
| Equipment | Roll bender / press brake | Furnace + roll bender |
| Surface finish | Smooth | Oxidized scale |
| Accuracy | High (±1 mm/m) | Lower (±5 mm/m) |
| Speed | Fast cycle | Slow (heat + cool) |
| Material change | None | Possible HAZ effects |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Thickness | ≤25 mm (mild) | Any |

**(b) Numerical Solution** (7 Marks)

**(i) Plastic moment per unit width**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_p &= \frac{\sigma_y \cdot t^2}{4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{355 \cdot 12^2}{4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{355 \cdot 144}{4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{51{,}120}{4} \\[6pt]
&= 12{,}780 \text{ N·mm/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total plastic moment for $b = 1800$ mm**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_p &= m_p \cdot b \\[6pt]
&= 12{,}780 \cdot 1800 \\[6pt]
&= 23{,}004{,}000 \text{ N·mm} \\[6pt]
&= 23.0 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Approximate rolling force (top roller radius = 200 mm)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &\approx \frac{M_p}{R_{roller}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{23{,}004{,}000}{200} \\[6pt]
&= 115{,}020 \text{ N} \\[6pt]
&\approx 115 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** A 115 kN rolling force is well within the capacity of typical shipyard pyramid benders (500–3000 kN), so the operation is feasible.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $R$ (plate bend radius) with $R_{roller}$ (machine roller radius)** — they are NOT the same. The plate is bent to a much larger radius than the roller itself.
> 2. **Forgetting to halve $t$** — strain is $t/(2R)$, not $t/R$. Half the batch gets this wrong.
> 3. **Wrong springback direction** — springback means the plate unbends on release, so you must OVERBEND, not underbend.
> 4. **Using $E = 2 \times 10^5$ GPa** — wrong unit. $E$ for steel is **$2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa = $2.1 \times 10^{11}$ N/m²**. Pick one and stick to it.
> 5. **Comparing cold vs hot on thickness alone** — cold bending is preferred for **small to medium thicknesses** with high accuracy requirements; hot bending for very thick plates and tight radii. Always state BOTH criteria.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch** — a neat labeled diagram of the 3-roll pyramid bender or the fiber strain distribution earns 2–3 easy marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cold Bending** = plastic deformation of steel plates/sections at room temperature using mechanical force (no heat).
- **Preferred for plate thickness:** ≤ **25 mm** (mild steel) or ≤ **20 mm** (HT steel AH36).
- **Equipment:** 3-roll pyramid bender (most common), press brake, profile bender, cold flanger.
- **Fundamental strain equation:** $\varepsilon_{outer} = \dfrac{t}{2R}$
- **Minimum bend radius (strain-based):** $R_{min} = \dfrac{t}{2\varepsilon_{allow}}$
- **Empirical rule:** $R_{min} \approx 3t$ (mild), $\approx 4t$ (HT steel).
- **Springback:** $\Delta\alpha = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$ — compensated by **overbending**.
- **Plastic moment per unit width:** $m_p = \dfrac{\sigma_y t^2}{4}$
- **Rolling force (approx.):** $F \approx \dfrac{M_p}{R_{roller}}$
- **Neutral axis** remains at mid-thickness (small strains).
- **Outer fiber = tension**, **inner fiber = compression**, both **must yield** for permanent bend.
- **Advantages over hot bending:** better finish, higher accuracy, faster, cheaper.
- **Limitations:** thickness limit, cracking at very small radii, greater springback in HT steel.
- **Key constant:** $E_{steel} = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa (always).
- **Safety criterion:** outer-fiber strain ≤ 5 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Takeaway for the Exam Hall:** *Cold bending is governed by outer-fiber strain $\varepsilon = t/(2R)$; keep it below 5 % by ensuring $R \geq t/(0.10) = 10t$, and compensate springback by overbending.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat-Line Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat-Line Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Heat-Line Bending** is a specialised plate-forming technique employed in shipbuilding in which a narrow, straight zone (a "heat line") along a plate or section is subjected to controlled oxy-acetylene flame heating, causing **localised thermal expansion** on the heated surface. Upon subsequent cooling, the heated fibres undergo **plastic contraction** (shrinkage), which forces the plate to develop a permanent, uniform angular bend along the heated line. The technique is used to introduce deliberate knuckled or sharply-curved transitions in plating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Heat-Line Bending** is classified under *Mechanical / Thermal Forming of Plates* and is distinct from **Line Heating** (which produces smooth curves) and **Cold Bending** (mechanical roller/press bending). Heat-line bending specifically generates a *discrete bend along a line* rather than a continuous curve.

**Key Terminology**:
- **Heat Line**: The narrow straight path traversed by the torch on the plate surface (typically **15 mm – 25 mm** wide).
- **Knuckle**: The angular bend produced at the heated line.
- **Heating Speed (v)**: Rate of torch traverse, generally **3–8 m/hr** for mild steel plates.
- **Plate Thickness (t)**: Standard shipbuilding plate thickness ranges from **6 mm to 40 mm**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a flat, stiff **paper card** and passing a candle flame slowly along a straight line on its surface. The heated side of the card tries to expand, but the cooler side restrains it. As the flame moves on and the heated zone cools, that strip of paper shrinks, **pulling the card into a permanent crease or fold** along that line. The same principle governs heat-line bending of steel — only the temperatures are far higher (around **600 °C – 750 °C**, below the steel's melting point) and the material is mild steel plate.

> [!NOTE]
> The steel is **NOT melted**. It is heated to a temperature where the yield stress drops dramatically, allowing plastic flow to occur on cooling.

### 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Material commonly used**: Mild steel (ASTM A36 / Grade A) with yield strength around **250 MPa**.
- **Heating temperature range**: **600 °C to 750 °C** (cherry-red to dull-red heat).
- **Bend angle achievable in single pass**: up to **15°** in standard plates.
- **Cooling medium**: Ambient air, water spray, or forced air — water cooling produces sharper knuckle angles.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physics — "Why Does the Plate Bend?"

When a localised strip of a steel plate is heated:

1. The heated fibres **expand** because of thermal expansion (governed by the coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha_t$).
2. The surrounding cold material **restrains this expansion**, creating **compressive thermal stresses** in the heated zone.
3. When the temperature exceeds the steel's creep / yield threshold, the heated material **plastically deforms (upset)**.
4. On cooling, the heated strip wants to return to its original length, but now it has **less material** (due to plastic upset) → it **shrinks**.
5. This shrinkage generates a **tensile force** on the top (heated) surface and a **compressive force** on the bottom surface → the plate curls, producing a **bend along the line**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 – Marking**: The bend line is marked on the plate using chalk lines or scribed templates from the **loft** (ship's full-scale drawing floor).
- **Step 2 – Pre-positioning**: The plate is placed with the heated surface (the side that will shorten) facing **upward** if the desired knuckle points **downward**, and vice versa.
- **Step 3 – Heating Pass**: The oxy-acetylene torch is moved in a single, straight pass along the heat line. The torch is held at a distance giving a **soft, broad flame** (excess acetylene to avoid oxidation).
- **Step 4 – Cooling**: After heating, cooling is achieved by:
  - **Air cooling**: Slow, produces gentle knuckle (~3°–5°).
  - **Water quenching**: Fast, produces sharper knuckle (up to **15°**).
- **Step 5 – Inspection**: Bend angle measured with protractor or template.

### 2.3 The "Triangle Method" — Estimating Heat Input

The classical shipyard rule of thumb for predicting the bend angle uses the **shrinkage triangle**:

The plate is conceptually divided into a triangular region near the heat line. The volume of the upset (compressed) material on heating, and its shrinkage on cooling, determines the final angle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------------------|--------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Linear thermal expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | $\alpha_t \approx 12 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ C$ for steel |
| 2 | Free thermal strain | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha_t \cdot \Delta T$ | dimensionless | Heating-induced |
| 3 | Plastic strain after cooling | $\varepsilon_p = \alpha_t \cdot \Delta T - \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$ | dimensionless | Elastic recovery subtracted |
| 4 | Shrinkage on cooling | $\Delta s = \alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | At constant stress-free state |
| 5 | Bend angle (small angle approx.) | $\theta \approx \dfrac{\Delta s}{c}$ | radians | $c$ = distance of heat line from neutral axis |
| 6 | Bend angle (degrees) | $\theta^\circ = \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta s}{c}$ | degrees | Final KTU-usable form |
| 7 | Heating speed (rule of thumb) | $v = \dfrac{k}{t}$ | m/hr | $t$ = thickness in mm, $k \approx 20$ |
| 8 | Plate thickness limit | $t \le 40$ mm | mm | Above this, press bending preferred |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams**: When writing $\vert \Delta L \vert$ or absolute-value expressions in tables, always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX — never the bare vertical pipe — to avoid breaking the markdown table rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- Used to form **knuckled bottom plating** in single-bottom ships.
- Forms **stern frame gusset plates** with sharp transitions.
- Creates **sponsons, bulwark plates, and hatch coaming** returns.
- Critical in **prefabrication yards** for accelerating modular construction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bend-Angle Equation

We will derive the relationship between the **thermal shrinkage on the heated line** and the resulting **bend angle $\theta$** of the plate.

#### Step 1 — Thermal Expansion of the Heat Strip

Consider a strip of length $L$ along the heat line. When heated by $\Delta T$ above ambient, its free expansion would be:

$$
\Delta L_{free} = \alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

Here, $\alpha_t$ is the **coefficient of linear thermal expansion** of mild steel, and $\Delta T$ is the temperature rise.

#### Step 2 — Constrained Expansion → Compressive Stress

In reality, the surrounding plate prevents this free expansion. The strip is compressed by an amount equal to its free expansion. The induced compressive elastic strain is:

$$
\varepsilon_{el} = \alpha_t \cdot \Delta T
$$

The corresponding compressive stress (using Hooke's Law, with $E$ = Young's modulus) is:

$$
\sigma_c = E \cdot \varepsilon_{el} = E \cdot \alpha_t \cdot \Delta T
$$

#### Step 3 — Plastic Upset (Yielding)

If $\sigma_c$ exceeds the **yield stress $\sigma_y$** of steel at the elevated temperature, the material yields and undergoes **plastic compressive flow (upsetting)**. The plastic strain accumulated is:

$$
\varepsilon_p = \alpha_t \cdot \Delta T - \frac{\sigma_y}{E}
$$

The first term is the total thermal strain; the second is the elastic strain that is recovered when the load is removed. The remainder is the **permanent plastic shortening**.

#### Step 4 — Cooling and Net Shrinkage

When the heat source is removed, the strip cools back to ambient. Its free length now is its **plastically shortened** length. As it cools, it wants to contract by:

$$
\Delta s = \alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

But, since the compressive elastic strain is gone, the **net residual shrinkage** relative to the original plate is:

$$
\Delta s = \alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

#### Step 5 — Conversion of Shrinkage to Bend Angle

The shrinkage occurs on the **top (heated) surface** of the plate. The bottom surface does not shrink. This length mismatch forces the plate to bend.

Let $c$ be the **distance from the heat line to the neutral axis** of the plate cross-section (approximately the mid-thickness for symmetric bending, so $c \approx t/2$).

The bend angle in radians, for small angles, is given by arc length / radius:

$$
\theta = \frac{\Delta s}{c}
$$

Substituting $\Delta s = \alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ and $c = t/2$:

$$
\theta = \frac{\alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T}{t/2} = \frac{2 \alpha_t L \Delta T}{t}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\theta^\circ = \frac{180}{\pi} \cdot \frac{2 \alpha_t L \Delta T}{t}
$$

#### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given**:
- Steel plate thickness: $t = 20$ mm
- Length of heat line: $L = 2$ m = **2000 mm**
- Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 600$ °C
- $\alpha_t = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ C$
- Distance of heat line from neutral axis: $c = t/2 = 10$ mm

**Step A — Calculate free thermal expansion**:

$$
\Delta L_{free} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000 \times 600
$$

$$
\Delta L_{free} = 14.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step B — Calculate bend angle in radians**:

$$
\theta = \frac{\Delta s}{c} = \frac{14.4}{10} = 1.44 \text{ rad}
$$

Since this is unrealistically large, we recognise the **small-angle approximation** is invalid. We re-evaluate: actually, the plastic upset is what remains — the **elastically recovered portion is removed**. The effective plastic shrinkage is much smaller.

**Step C — Apply plastic strain correction** (assuming $\sigma_y / E \approx 0.001$ at 600 °C, since yield drops sharply at elevated temperature):

$$
\varepsilon_p = (12 \times 10^{-6} \times 600) - 0.001 = 0.0072 - 0.001 = 0.0062
$$

$$
\Delta s = \varepsilon_p \cdot L = 0.0062 \times 2000 = 12.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step D — Bend angle in radians**:

$$
\theta = \frac{12.4}{10} = 1.24 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step E — Convert to degrees**:

$$
\theta^\circ = 1.24 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 71.0^\circ
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The single-pass theoretical maximum is closer to **~15°** in practice because (a) only part of the heat is concentrated along the line, (b) the yield drop is not as severe as assumed, and (c) bending distributes further strain. The KTU examiner will expect the **idealised formula** with the $\Delta s$ in terms of plastic strain.

#### Final Working Equation (for KTU Answer Sheets)

$$
\boxed{\;\theta^\circ = \frac{180}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_{eff}}{c}\;}
$$

where $\Delta T_{eff}$ is the **effective plastic-flow temperature rise** above the steel's hot-yield threshold (typically **300 °C – 500 °C** for mild steel in shipyard conditions).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plate Received from Stockyard] --> B[Mark Heat Line from Loft Template]
    B --> C[Position Plate on Heat-Line Bending Bay]
    C --> D{Pre-heat Check on Torch Flame Profile}
    D --> E[Adjust Oxygen-Acetylene Ratio: Slightly Carburising]
    E --> F[Traverse Torch Along Heat Line at Controlled Speed 3 to 8 m per hr]
    F --> G{Plate Thickness Greater than 25 mm}
    G -- Yes --> H[Water Quench Immediately Behind Flame]
    G -- No --> I[Air Cool Naturally]
    H --> J[Measure Bend Angle with Protractor]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Angle Within Tolerance Plus or Minus 1 degree}
    K -- Yes --> L[Plate Released to Next Stage]
    K -- No --> M[Re-heat or Reverse Bend]
    M --> F
    L --> N[End: Plate Ready for Assembly]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Heat-Line Bending

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputSystem["INPUT SYSTEM"]
        L1[Loft Templates]
        L2[Plate Thickness and Grade Data]
    end

    subgraph ProcessSystem["PROCESS SYSTEM"]
        P1[Torch Heating Unit]
        P2[Heat Line Traverse Mechanism]
        P3[Cooling System: Air or Water]
    end

    subgraph OutputSystem["OUTPUT SYSTEM"]
        O1[Knuckled Plate]
        O2[Inspection Gauge]
    end

    L1 --> P1
    L2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Plate Cross-Section (Before & After Bending)

**Before heating** (cross-section, side view):

```
+-----------------------+   <- top surface (unmarked)
|                       |
|        PLATE          |   <- thickness t
|                       |
+-----------------------+   <- bottom surface
```

**After heating top surface and cooling**:

```
+-----------------------+   <- top surface (shortened by Δs)
 \                     \
  \                     \   <- heat line at top
   \                     \
    +---------------------+  <- bottom surface (original length)
```

The top is shorter than the bottom → plate knuckled **upward** (towards the heated side).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define heat-line bending. State two shipboard applications where it is preferred over cold bending.*

**Model Answer**:
Heat-line bending is a thermal plate-forming process in which a localised straight zone on a plate is heated by an oxy-acetylene flame; the subsequent shrinkage on cooling produces a permanent angular bend (knuckle) along that line.

**Applications**:
1. **Knuckled bottom plating** of single-bottom ships — sharp transitions cannot be rolled.
2. **Bulwark plates and hatch coaming returns** — small angular changes without rolling equipment.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for two applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the effect of water quenching versus air cooling on the bend angle produced by heat-line bending?*

**Model Answer**:
Water quenching **traps the plastic strain** by rapidly cooling the heated zone, preventing recovery, and produces a **larger, sharper bend angle** (up to ~15°). Air cooling allows partial elastic recovery, producing a **gentler, smaller bend angle** (~3°–5°).

*(3 marks: 1 for stating the effect, 1 for each cooling method's outcome)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, adapted]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **heat-line bending process** for ship plates. State clearly the role of the heating temperature and the torch traverse speed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

1. **Process Description** (3 marks):
   - A heat line is marked on the plate surface using a lofted template.
   - The plate is positioned with sufficient clear space on both sides of the heat line.
   - An oxy-acetylene torch (excess acetylene — soft flame) is traversed once along the heat line.
   - The heat input causes plastic upset on the heated surface; cooling causes shrinkage.
   - Plate develops a permanent angular bend (knuckle) along the heated line.

2. **Role of Temperature** (2 marks):
   - Must be raised to **600 °C – 750 °C** (below melting) so the steel yields locally.
   - Higher temperature → larger plastic strain → larger bend angle (within limits).

3. **Role of Traverse Speed** (2 marks):
   - Slower speed → more heat input per unit length → greater plastic upset → larger bend.
   - Typical range: **3–8 m/hr** for ship plates.
   - Rule of thumb: $v \approx k / t$ where $k \approx 20$ and $t$ is plate thickness in mm.

---

**(b)** A mild-steel plate of thickness **25 mm** is subjected to heat-line bending. The length of the heat line is **3 m**. The heating temperature rise is **650 °C**. Calculate the **bend angle** produced. Take $\alpha_t = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ C$, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, and assume the elastic recovery is $\sigma_y / E = 0.0012$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1** — Free thermal expansion:
$$
\Delta L_{free} = \alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 3000 \times 650 = 23.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2** — Plastic strain after elastic recovery:
$$
\varepsilon_p = \alpha_t \cdot \Delta T - \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = 0.0078 - 0.0012 = 0.0066
$$

**Step 3** — Net shrinkage of heat line:
$$
\Delta s = \varepsilon_p \cdot L = 0.0066 \times 3000 = 19.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4** — Distance of heat line from neutral axis:
$$
c = t / 2 = 25 / 2 = 12.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5** — Bend angle in radians:
$$
\theta = \frac{\Delta s}{c} = \frac{19.8}{12.5} = 1.584 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 6** — Convert to degrees:
$$
\theta^\circ = 1.584 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 90.76^\circ
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: A 90° bend from a *single pass* is physically unrealistic. KTU examiners will **deduct 1 mark** if students do **not** comment that the practical single-pass maximum is **~15°** and the excess theoretical value indicates the need for **multi-pass heating** or **press-bending assistance**. Always add a "practical commentary" line in your numerical answers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **"triangle method"** used in shipyards to estimate the bend angle in heat-line bending. How is the location of the heat line on the plate determined from the loft? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

1. **Triangle Method Concept** (4 marks):
   - The plate is conceptually divided into a triangular wedge adjacent to the heat line.
   - The volume of the wedge that becomes plastically upset determines the shrinkage.
   - For a triangle of base $b$ and height $h$ (both related to plate dimensions and desired knuckle), the bend angle is estimated as:
   $$
   \theta \approx \frac{b}{h} \text{ (radians, small angle)}
   $$
   - The method is empirical, calibrated against yard experience tables.

2. **Heat Line Location from Loft** (3 marks):
   - The ship's hull form is **lofted** full-scale on the mould loft floor.
   - Lines of intersection (knuckle lines) are marked on the developed plate shapes.
   - The heat line is **transferred directly** from the loft drawing to the plate by scribing or template.

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast **heat-line bending** with **cold bending** and **line heating**. State the specific situations in which each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

| Parameter | Heat-Line Bending | Cold Bending | Line Heating |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Heat source** | Oxy-acetylene flame | None (mechanical) | Oxy-acetylene flame |
| **Result** | Sharp angular knuckle | Smooth curve by rollers/press | Smooth compound curve |
| **Plate thickness** | Up to 25–40 mm | Up to 50 mm | Up to 50 mm |
| **Equipment cost** | Low (torch) | High (press, rolls) | Low (torch) |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate (±1°) | High (±0.5°) | Moderate |
| **Best for** | Discreet knuckles, returns | Hull plates with uniform curvature | Hull plates with varying curvature |

**Preferred situations** (3 marks):
- **Heat-line bending**: knuckled bottom plates, hatch coamings, bulwark returns.
- **Cold bending**: large-radius hull plates where a press/roll is available.
- **Line heating**: complex 3D-curved plates (bow, stern, sheer line transitions).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Areas in Heat-Line Bending Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the plastic-strain correction** (subtracting $\sigma_y / E$) — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up heat-line bending with line heating** in definitions — examiners look for the *angular knuckle* signature.
> 3. **Not converting radians to degrees** in numerical problems.
> 4. **Failing to comment on practical limits** (single-pass max ~15°). Always conclude numerical answers with a "practical feasibility" remark.
> 5. **Using wrong units for $\alpha_t$** — must be per °C, not per °F or per K without conversion.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Heat-line bending** = oxy-acetylene heating of a *narrow straight line* → permanent angular knuckle on cooling.
- **NOT** the same as **line heating** (which produces *smooth curves*).
- **Heating temperature**: 600 °C – 750 °C (below melting point ~1500 °C for steel).
