- **Bend angle formula**: $\theta^\circ = (180/\pi) \cdot (\alpha_t \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_{eff}) / c$.
- **Plastic strain correction**: subtract $\sigma_y / E$ from total thermal strain.
- **Water cooling** → sharp knuckle (up to 15°); **air cooling** → gentle knuckle (3°–5°).
- **Plate thickness limit**: ~40 mm; beyond this, prefer press bending.
- **Torch traverse speed**: $v \approx 20 / t$ m/hr (yard rule of thumb).
- **Applications**: knuckled bottom plates, hatch coamings, bulwark returns, stern gussets.
- **Lofting** is essential for accurate heat-line marking.
- **Safety**: ensure flame is *slightly carburising* (excess acetylene) to avoid scale/oxidation.
- **Inspection**: bend angle measured with protractor/template within **±1°** tolerance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly of Ship's Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assembly of Ship's Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assembly of Ship's Structure** is the systematic, sequential process of joining individual steel plates, sections, stiffeners, and sub-components into progressively larger structural units (panels → sub-assemblies → blocks → grand blocks → final ship) to form the complete hull, superstructure, and outfitted vessel, carried out in compliance with classification society rules, shipyard production standards, and structural integrity requirements.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic covers the **phased construction methodology** that transforms raw steel plates (received from the steel stockyard) into a fully functional seagoing vessel. The process is governed by three governing principles:
1. **Progressive Enlargement** — small parts are joined to form larger ones.
2. **Maximum Pre-Outfitting** — outfit items are fitted *before* final joining, to reduce post-erection work at height.
3. **Welding Sequence Integrity** — to minimize distortion, residual stress, and shrinkage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a Giant Steel LEGO Ship:**
> Imagine building a massive 3D puzzle where each "LEGO brick" is actually a welded steel plate or stiffened panel. You cannot build the whole ship at once, just as you cannot fit a full ship in a small workshop. So, the shipyard does it backwards: it first builds **tiny pieces (panels)** on the floor, then joins panels to form **walls and floors (blocks)**, then stands these blocks upright on the slipway, welds them together, and finally "climbs" the blocks to form the complete 3D hull — just like assembling a multi-story building, but the building will *float and sail*.

The assembly hierarchy is conceptually similar to:
- **Steel Plate** → a single sheet of paper
- **Panel** → a stiffened card (paper + straws glued on)
- **Sub-Assembly** → a small box made of stiffened cards
- **Block** → a room (with walls, floor, and ceiling) made of multiple boxes
- **Module/Grand Block** → an entire floor or wing of a building
- **Ship** → the fully completed, furnished, floating building

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Benchmarks in Modern Ship Assembly:**
> - **Block Weight Range:** Typically **50 tonnes to 600 tonnes** per block, with mega-blocks reaching up to **1200 tonnes** using Goliath/gantry cranes.
> - **Welding Efficiency Standard:** Minimum **85%** radiographic (RT) weld quality acceptance in critical hull regions (per IACS UR Z17).
> - **Plate Thickness Range:** Hull plating typically **6 mm to 100 mm**; high-tensile steel (AH36, DH36, EH36) is standard for thicker sections.
> - **Block Tolerance Limit:** ±**3 mm to ±5 mm** for block-to-block alignment on the slipway.
> - **Pre-Outfitting Target:** Modern yards aim for **70% to 85%** outfitting completion *before* block erection.

## 1.4 Significance in KTU 2024 Syllabus

This topic is a **high-weightage** subject within Module 3 because:
- It directly links to the *Course Outcomes (CO3)* — "Illustrate the construction methodology of ships."
- It forms the foundation for understanding **modern modular shipbuilding**, **shipyard layout planning**, and **welding technology**.
- It appears frequently in KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) as a **Part B (14 marks)** question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Hierarchy of Ship Structure Assembly

The assembly of a ship's structure follows a **strictly hierarchical, bottom-up sequence**. Each level inherits the precision and quality of the level below it.

### **Stage 1: Steel Preparation & Primary Processing**
- Raw steel plates and profiles arrive at the stockyard.
- Plates are **shot-blasted**, **primed**, and **cut** (gas, plasma, or laser cutting).
- Edges are beveled (edge preparation) for butt-welded joints.
- Marking of lines (waterlines, buttock lines, section lines) using **line marking tables** and **phototemplates**.

### **Stage 2: Panel Fabrication (Sub-Unit Stage)**
- A **panel** is the smallest welded assembly unit, typically consisting of a **plate + longitudinal stiffeners** (T-bars, flat bars, L-angles, or bulb plates).
- Panels are fabricated on **panel lines** using:
  - Automatic **submerged arc welding (SAW)** machines
  - **Friction stir welding (FSW)** for aluminum superstructures
  - **One-side welding** (often using ceramic backing) for double-bottom plates
- **Curved panels** (for sheer, camber, or bilge regions) are formed on **plate bending rolls** (3-roll or 4-roll pyramid type).
- Panels are typically **12 m × 3 m** for hull bottom, and **10 m × 2 m** for side shell.

### **Stage 3: Sub-Assembly (3D Mini-Block)**
- Panels are rotated, mated, and welded together to form **3D sub-assemblies** such as:
  - **Double bottom units** (inner bottom plate + floors + girders + bottom plate)
  - **Transverse ring sub-assemblies** (floor + tank-side brackets + side shell frame)
  - **Deck panels with supporting transverses**
- This stage is performed on **sub-assembly jigs** with strict dimensional control.

### **Stage 4: Block Assembly (The Crucial Stage)**
- Sub-assemblies are joined to form **blocks** — the most critical pre-erection units.
- A typical **block** includes:
  - Hull side, bottom, and deck plating
  - Transverse bulkheads
  - Longitudinal girders, web frames
  - Pre-installed **piping, machinery seats, cable trays, and ventilation ducts** (pre-outfitting)
  - Sometimes even **machinery units** like main engines and generators (in advanced yards)
- Block sizes are dictated by:
  - Yard **crane capacity** (e.g., 600 t, 1000 t gantry cranes)
  - **Transportability** (road, rail, waterway)
  - **Welding access** and **block stability**

### **Stage 5: Grand Block / Final Erection (On the Slipway or in Dry Dock)**
- Blocks are **erected** (lifted) onto the building berth (slipway or dry dock).
- Adjacent blocks are aligned, clamped, and **welded together** at the **block-to-block joints**.
- The hull grows progressively from keel-up, then continues with superstructure blocks.
- Final stages: shaft alignment, propeller fitting, sea trials, and delivery.

## 2.2 Classification of Assembly Methods

| **Method** | **Description** | **Modern Relevance** | **KTU Exam Priority** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Piece-Small (Stern-to-Stern)** | Each plate and frame is fitted individually on the slipway. | Obsolete — used in 19th/early 20th century. | Low |
| **Section / Unit Method** | Hull is divided into 2-3 large sections built separately, then joined. | Used for repair/conversion. | Medium |
| **Block Construction** | Hull is divided into 30–200+ blocks built in workshops, then erected. | **Industry standard worldwide.** | **High** |
| **Modular / Zonal Construction** | Blocks are grouped into functional zones (engine room zone, bow zone, accommodation zone). | Used in LNG, naval, and cruise ships. | **High** |
| **Mega-Block / Grand Block** | Entire hull sections (e.g., 1/4 of the ship) are built in covered halls and floated/rolled to the berth. | Modern mega-yards (South Korea, China). | Medium |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although ship assembly is largely descriptive, certain quantitative formulas are tested in KTU exams. They relate to **hull form coefficients**, **tonnage**, and **block weight/erection calculations**.

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)** | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Dimensionless | Indicates hull fullness; typical cargo ship $C_B$ = 0.65 to 0.85. |
| **Midship Section Coefficient ($C_M$)** | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Dimensionless | Indicates fullness of the cross-section. |
| **Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)** | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$ | Dimensionless | Indicates longitudinal fullness distribution. |
| **Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_WP$)** | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_W}{L \cdot B}$ | Dimensionless | Affects ship roll and added resistance. |
| **Displacement ($\nabla$)** | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | m³ | Volume of water displaced by the hull. |
| **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** | $DWT = \nabla_{loaded} - \nabla_{light}$ | tonnes | Total cargo + fuel + stores + crew + ballast capacity. |
| **Light Ship Weight (LSW)** | $LSW = W_{steel} + W_{outfit} + W_{machinery}$ | tonnes | Weight of the empty ship. |
| **Block Erection Time Estimate** | $T_{erect} = \dfrac{N_{blocks} \cdot t_{weld}}{N_{teams} \cdot \eta}$ | days | Approximate scheduling parameter. |
| **Welding Heat Input (HI)** | $HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S} \times 0.06$ | kJ/mm | Critical for HTS plates to control HAZ softening. |
| **Block Tolerance Build-up** | $\delta_{total} = \sqrt{\sum \delta_i^2}$ | mm | RSS (Root-Sum-Square) tolerance accumulation. |

**Notation Key:**
- $L$ = Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP), $B$ = Breadth, $T$ = Draught
- $\nabla$ = Displacement volume, $A_M$ = Midship section area, $A_W$ = Waterplane area
- $V$ = Voltage, $I$ = Current, $S$ = Travel speed (welding parameters)
- $\eta$ = Welding efficiency factor (typically 0.5 to 0.7)

## 2.4 Why This Stage Matters in Real Engineering

> [!TIP]
> The choice of assembly method directly affects:
> - **Construction Time:** Modular construction can cut build time by 30–50% compared to piece-small methods.
> - **Cost:** Pre-outfitting reduces work-at-height costs (scaffolding) by up to 60%.
> - **Quality:** Workshop welding (in covered halls) is far more consistent than open-berth welding (no wind, rain, or cold-induced defects).
> - **Safety:** Block erection reduces manual work at dangerous heights, lowering accident rates.
> - **Welding Distortion Control:** Pre-fabrication in flat panels allows symmetrical welding sequences, minimizing plate distortion.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Block Tolerance Build-Up Using RSS Method

In ship assembly, tolerances from each sub-stage accumulate. The **Root-Sum-Square (RSS)** method is universally used because worst-case addition of tolerances is too conservative and unrealistic for statistically independent manufacturing errors.

### **Step 1: Identify Individual Tolerances**

Suppose we have **four sequential stages**, each with its own dimensional tolerance:
- $\delta_1$ = Plate cutting tolerance = **±2 mm**
- $\delta_2$ = Panel welding distortion = **±3 mm**
- $\delta_3$ = Sub-assembly jig alignment = **±2 mm**
- $\delta_4$ = Block-to-block fit-up = **±1 mm**

### **Step 2: Apply the RSS Formula**

The total accumulated tolerance is:

$$
\delta_{total} = \sqrt{\delta_1^2 + \delta_2^2 + \delta_3^2 + \delta_4^2}
$$

This is valid because manufacturing errors at each stage are *statistically independent* and *randomly distributed* (Gaussian).

### **Step 3: Substitute the Values**

$$
\delta_{total} = \sqrt{(2)^2 + (3)^2 + (2)^2 + (1)^2}
$$

### **Step 4: Compute the Squares**

$$
\delta_{total} = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 4 + 1}
$$

### **Step 5: Sum the Squares**

$$
\delta_{total} = \sqrt{18}
$$

### **Step 6: Take the Square Root**

$$
\delta_{total} \approx 4.24 \text{ mm}
$$

### **Step 7: Compare with Worst-Case Method**

If we had naively added the tolerances (worst-case method):

$$
\delta_{worst} = 2 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

This is almost **double** the realistic RSS value, demonstrating why RSS is preferred in tolerance budgeting.

### **Step 8: Final Conclusion**

$$
\boxed{\delta_{total} \approx 4.24 \text{ mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \delta_{worst} = 8 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 4.24 mm accumulated tolerance over 4 stages is well within the **±5 mm KTU/Classification society block acceptance limit**, confirming the design feasibility. However, the welding distortion (3 mm) is the dominant contributor — indicating that *welding process control* is the most critical quality bottleneck.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Block Weight Estimation for Crane Selection

A shipyard plans to build a **double-bottom block** of a Panamax bulk carrier. The block dimensions are:
- **Length** = 24 m
- **Breadth** = 8 m (full bottom width)
- **Height** = 2 m
- **Steel density** ($\rho_{steel}$) = **7850 kg/m³**
- **Average plate thickness** in the block = **18 mm** (accounting for varying thicknesses of bottom plate, floors, girders, inner bottom)
- **Total steel volume fraction** (since the block is hollow and stiffened) = **0.25** (i.e., 25% of the gross block volume is solid steel)

### **Step 1: Calculate the Gross Block Volume**

$$
V_{gross} = L \times B \times H
$$

$$
V_{gross} = 24 \times 8 \times 2 = 384 \text{ m}^3
$$

### **Step 2: Calculate the Effective Steel Volume**

$$
V_{steel} = V_{gross} \times 0.25
$$

$$
V_{steel} = 384 \times 0.25 = 96 \text{ m}^3
$$

### **Step 3: Convert Steel Volume to Weight**

$$
W_{block} = V_{steel} \times \rho_{steel}
$$

$$
W_{block} = 96 \times 7850 = 753{,}600 \text{ kg}
$$

### **Step 4: Convert to Tonnes**

$$
W_{block} = 753{,}600 \div 1000 = 753.6 \text{ tonnes}
$$

### **Step 5: Add Outfit Weight Allowance (15% for pre-outfitting items)**

$$
W_{outfit} = 0.15 \times 753.6 = 113.04 \text{ tonnes}
$$

### **Step 6: Calculate Total Lift Weight**

$$
W_{total} = W_{block} + W_{outfit}
$$

$$
W_{total} = 753.6 + 113.04
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{total} \approx 866.64 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

### **Step 7: Crane Selection**

> [!TIP]
> With a **safety factor of 1.25**, the required crane capacity is:
> $C_{crane} = 866.64 \times 1.25 \approx 1083$ tonnes
>
> This is beyond most conventional gantry cranes, so the yard would either: (a) use a **1000 t + 200 t tandem lift**, (b) subdivide the block, or (c) build a **smaller, lighter block** to fit a 600 t crane. This is a classic KTU-style **design trade-off problem**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Assembly Methods

| **Parameter** | **Piece-Small** | **Block Method** | **Modular (Zonal)** | **Mega-Block** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Build Cycle Time** | 18–36 months | 8–14 months | 6–12 months | 4–8 months |
| **Yard Investment** | Very Low | High (cranes) | Very High | Extremely High |
| **Welding Quality** | Poor (outdoor) | Good (workshop) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Pre-Outfitting %** | 5–10% | 50–65% | 70–85% | 80–90% |
| **Work-at-Height Risk** | Very High | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| **Labor Productivity** | 10–20 t/man-year | 30–60 t/man-year | 60–100 t/man-year | 100–200 t/man-year |
| **Typical User** | Small traditional yards | Most commercial yards | Korean/European yards | Mega-yards (Daewoo, Hyundai) |
| **KTU 2024 Exam Weightage** | Low | **Very High** | **High** | Medium |

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Welding Sequence Within a Block

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP1 as Fitter 1
    participant OP2 as Fitter 2
    participant W1 as Welder 1
    participant W2 as Welder 2
    participant QC as Quality Inspector

    OP1->>OP1: Stage A: Fit Bottom Plate to Jig
    OP2->>OP2: Stage B: Position Transverse Floors
    W1->>W1: Stage C: Tack Weld Floors to Bottom Plate
    OP1->>OP1: Stage D: Lift Inner Bottom Plate
    W2->>W2: Stage E: Tack Weld Inner Bottom
    W1->>W1: Stage F: Full Butt Weld Floor to Plate
    W2->>W2: Stage G: Full Butt Weld Inner Bottom Seams
    QC->>QC: Stage H: Visual and NDT Inspection
    QC-->>W1: Approve / Rework
    Note over W1,W2: Welding proceeds symmetrically from midship outward to control distortion
```

**Interpretation:**
This sequence diagram captures the **fitter-welder-inspector cycle** inside a typical block. Note the **symmetrical welding principle** — welders work from the center outward (or in balanced pairs) to **minimize thermal distortion** and residual stress, a concept frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison of Block vs. Modular Construction

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Traditional Block Method
    TB1[Block 1: Bow] --> TS1[Slipway Erection]
    TB2[Block 2: Midship] --> TS1
    TB3[Block 3: Stern] --> TS1
    end

    subgraph Modern Modular Method
    MB1[Module 1: Accommodation Block] --> MS1[Pre-Outfitted at Yard Hall]
    MB2[Module 2: Engine Room Block] --> MS1
    MB3[Module 3: Cargo Hold Block] --> MS1
    MB4[Module 4: Bow Module] --> MS1
    MS1 --> MS2[Float-On or Lift-On to Ship]
    end

    style TS1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style MS2 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600
```

**Interpretation:**
The traditional block method builds ~30–60 small blocks and erects them sequentially on a slipway. The modern modular method pre-assembles 4–6 *complete functional modules* (each can be 1000+ tonnes) under cover, and installs them as near-complete systems — dramatically reducing the on-berth workload.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define "block construction" in shipbuilding and list any four advantages over the traditional piece-small method.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Block construction is a modern shipbuilding methodology in which the hull is divided into a number of large, prefabricated three-dimensional structural units (called *blocks*). These blocks are fabricated, welded, and pre-outfitted in workshops, and then transported to the building berth where they are erected and joined together to form the complete ship.
>
> **Advantages over piece-small method (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks, but write at least 4):**
> 1. **Reduced build time** — parallel work in workshops and on the slipway.
> 2. **Improved welding quality** — workshop conditions (no wind, controlled temperature) reduce defects.
> 3. **Higher pre-outfitting percentage** — outfit items installed at floor level, reducing high-altitude work.
> 4. **Lower labor cost and improved safety** — ergonomic, ground-level work reduces accidents.
> 5. **Better dimensional control** — assembly jigs ensure accurate block fit-up.
> 6. **Higher productivity** (tonnes per man-year) than piece-small methods.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is "pre-outfitting" in ship construction? Why is it preferred over post-erection outfitting?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Pre-outfitting is the process of installing outfit items (piping, HVAC ducts, cable trays, machinery seats, insulation, flooring, etc.) into a ship block or sub-assembly *before* it is lifted and erected onto the slipway.
>
> **Why preferred (1.5 marks):**
> 1. Work is performed at **ground level**, improving worker safety and productivity.
> 2. It **reduces work-at-height**, lowering the need for costly scaffolding.
> 3. It enables **parallel workflows** — outfitting proceeds in the shop while structural work continues on the berth.
> 4. It **shortens the overall build cycle** and improves first-time quality (less rework after launch).

---

## 📝 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **hierarchical stages of ship structure assembly**, from the steel stockyard to the launching stage. Include sketches/flowchart and the operations carried out at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **block construction method** in modern shipbuilding. Compare it with the **piece-small method** under the following heads: (i) build cycle time, (ii) welding quality, (iii) pre-outfitting percentage, (iv) labor productivity, and (v) cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks**

The assembly of a ship's structure proceeds through **five hierarchical levels**:

**Level 1: Steel Stockyard and Plate Preparation (1 mark)**
- Raw steel plates (mild steel, AH36, DH36) and rolled sections (L, T, bulb, flat bars) are received.
- Plates are **shot-blasted** to remove mill scale and rust.
- **Cutting** is done by NC gas/plasma/laser cutting machines based on nesting software output.
- **Edge preparation** (beveling) for butt welds using beveling machines.
- Plates are **primed** with anti-corrosive shop primer.

**Level 2: Panel Fabrication (1.5 marks)**
- Plates and stiffeners are assembled on **panel lines** using jigs.
- Longitudinally stiffened panels are produced by **automatic SAW** or **electroslag welding**.
- Curved panels are formed on **plate rolls** (3-roll pyramid or 4-roll).
- Typical panel size: 12 m × 3 m for bottom, 10 m × 2 m for side shell.
- Panels are flipped and back-welded as required.

**Level 3: Sub-Assembly (1.5 marks)**
- Panels are joined to form 3D sub-units like **double-bottom units, transverse rings, and deck plates with supporting beams**.
- Performed on **sub-assembly jigs** with accurate fit-up.
- Tolerances are tightly controlled (±2 to ±3 mm).

**Level 4: Block Assembly (2 marks)**
- Sub-assemblies are joined to form **blocks** — large 3D hull units.
- Includes pre-installed **machinery foundations, piping, ventilation, and cable trays** (pre-outfitting).
- Block weights typically range from **50 to 600 tonnes**.
- Painting and insulation may be applied before erection.

**Level 5: Grand Block Erection and Final Joining (1 mark)**
- Blocks are lifted by gantry/goliath cranes and erected on the **building berth (slipway or dry dock)**.
- Block-to-block alignment is done using **hydraulic jacks, dogs, and strongbacks**.
- Final welding at **block-to-block joints** is performed.
- Superstructure blocks are added.
- Hull is launched (by sliding, gravity, or float-out depending on berth type).
- **Fitting out** continues at the outfit quay, followed by **sea trials** and **delivery**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table: Block vs. Piece-Small Method (5 × 1 = 5 marks)**

| **Parameter** | **Piece-Small Method** | **Block Construction** |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Build Cycle Time** | 18–36 months | 6–14 months |
| **(ii) Welding Quality** | Poor (outdoor, weather-affected) | Excellent (workshop, controlled environment) |
| **(iii) Pre-Outfitting %** | 5–10% (mostly after launch) | 50–85% (in workshop) |
| **(iv) Labor Productivity** | 10–20 tonnes/man-year | 40–100 tonnes/man-year |
| **(v) Cost** | Lower initial investment; higher per-ton labor cost | Higher initial investment (cranes, shops); lower per-ton cost |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
The block construction method is the **industry standard worldwide** for building commercial ships, naval vessels, and offshore structures. It delivers **faster build cycles, superior quality, and better worker safety** than the obsolete piece-small method. The high initial capital investment in gantry cranes and assembly halls is justified by the substantial reduction in build time, labor cost, and rework. Modern mega-yards have further evolved the block method into **modular/zonal construction** for even higher efficiency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **modern modular (zonal) construction method** used in large shipyards. What are the typical **zones** into which a ship is divided, and how does this method improve productivity? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard is planning to fabricate a **side shell block** with the following details:
- Block length: 20 m
- Block height: 8 m
- Block depth (transverse): 2 m
- Average plate thickness: 16 mm
- Steel density: 7850 kg/m³
- Solid steel fraction in the block: 0.22
- Pre-outfitting allowance: 12% of the bare block weight

Calculate:
(i) The bare steel weight of the block.
(ii) The pre-outfitting weight.
(iii) The total lift weight.
(iv) The required crane capacity, assuming a **safety factor of 1.25**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition of Modular Construction (1.5 marks):**
Modular (or zonal) construction is an advanced form of block construction in which the entire ship is divided into **a small number of large, functionally complete modules** (typically 4 to 8). Each module is a self-contained unit — sometimes spanning multiple decks, including fully fitted-out accommodation, machinery spaces, or cargo blocks — built in covered assembly halls and transported to the building berth as nearly complete systems.

**Typical Zones in a Ship (3 marks):**
A modern ship is typically divided into zones such as:
1. **Bow Module** — bulbous bow, forepeak tank, anchor handling, mooring equipment.
2. **Cargo Zone Module** — holds, hatch covers, cargo piping, ballast systems.
3. **Engine Room Module** — main engine, generators, separators, lubricating oil system, exhaust piping.
4. **Accommodation Module** — cabins, galley, mess, hospital, navigation bridge — fully outfitted with furniture, HVAC, and electrics.
5. **Stern Module** — rudder trunk, propeller shaft tunnel, steering gear.
6. **Superstructure Module** — wheelhouse, communication antennas, masts.
7. **LNG/Cryogenic Module** (for LNG carriers) — independent tank containment systems.
8. **Funnel Module** — exhaust uptakes and engine casing.

**Productivity Improvements (2.5 marks):**
1. **Parallel Production:** All modules are built simultaneously in different halls, compressing the overall build time by 30–50%.
2. **Maximum Pre-Outfitting:** Up to 80–90% outfitting is done in the workshop, including painting, insulation, flooring, and even furniture.
3. **Reduced On-Berth Work:** Final erection involves only block-to-module and module-to-module welding, minimizing work-at-height.
4. **Improved Quality:** All critical welding and outfitting is done in controlled environments.
5. **Workforce Efficiency:** Workers operate in ergonomic, well-lit halls, increasing output by 2–3× compared to open berths.
6. **Safety:** Fewer working-at-height operations drastically reduce accident rates.

**Example Yards:** Hyundai Heavy Industries, Daewoo Shipbuilding & Marine Engineering, Samsung Heavy Industries (South Korea); Meyer Turku (Finland); Fincantieri (Italy).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks**

**Given Data:**
- $L = 20$ m, $H = 8$ m, $D = 2$ m
- $t_{avg} = 16$ mm = 0.016 m
- $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³
- Solid steel fraction = 0.22
- Pre-outfitting allowance = 12% of bare weight
- Safety factor (SF) = 1.25

**Step (i): Gross Block Volume (0.5 mark)**

$$
V_{gross} = L \times H \times D = 20 \times 8 \times 2 = 320 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step (ii): Effective Steel Volume (0.5 mark)**

$$
V_{steel} = V_{gross} \times 0.22 = 320 \times 0.22 = 70.4 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step (iii): Bare Steel Weight (1 mark)**

$$
W_{bare} = V_{steel} \times \rho_{steel} = 70.4 \times 7850 = 552{,}640 \text{ kg} = 552.64 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step (iv): Pre-Outfitting Weight (1 mark)**

$$
W_{outfit} = 0.12 \times 552.64 = 66.32 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step (v): Total Lift Weight (1 mark)**

$$
W_{total} = W_{bare} + W_{outfit} = 552.64 + 66.32 = 618.96 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step (vi): Required Crane Capacity (1 mark)**

$$
C_{crane} = W_{total} \times SF = 618.96 \times 1.25
$$

$$
C_{crane} = 773.7 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step (vii): Final Answer (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{C_{crane} \approx 774 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The shipyard would need a gantry crane of at least 800-tonne capacity (next standard size above 774 t) to safely lift this side shell block.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Panel, Sub-Assembly, and Block:** Many students write "panel = block = sub-assembly." The KTU examiner expects a **clear hierarchical distinction**. *Panel = 2D plate + stiffeners*; *Sub-assembly = small 3D unit*; *Block = large 3D unit (≥50 t)*. Mixing them up = **−2 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Skipping the Pre-Outfitting Concept:** If a question asks about modern block construction and you *don't* mention pre-outfitting, you lose 2–3 marks. This is a **favorite KTU sub-topic**.
>
> 3. **Numerical Unit Errors:** In tolerance and block weight problems, students often forget to convert mm to m, or kg to tonnes. Always show unit conversions explicitly.
>
> 4. **Wrong Tolerance Formula:** Students sometimes use **arithmetic sum** of tolerances instead of **RSS**. KTU expects you to justify the use of RSS (statistical independence of errors). Using the wrong formula = **−1.5 marks**.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Safety Factors in Crane Problems:** Always apply the safety factor (typically 1.25 or stated in the problem). Skipping it is a **−1 mark** deduction.
>
> 6. **No Diagram in Descriptive Answers:** For a 7-mark descriptive question, a **neatly labeled flow diagram** is mandatory. Without it, expect a **−1 to −2 mark** deduction.
>
> 7. **Writing "Block Construction" Without Examples:** Always cite at least **one example yard** (e.g., Hyundai, Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock) to show application awareness.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Assembly of Ship's Structure:**

- **Assembly is hierarchical:** Plate → Panel → Sub-Assembly → Block → Grand Block → Ship. **Memorize this exact order.**
- **Block construction** is the **dominant modern method** — know its definition, advantages, and limitations.
- **Modular / zonal construction** is the **advanced evolution** — mention zones (bow, engine room, accommodation, stern, superstructure).
- **Pre-outfitting** = installing machinery, piping, and outfit items *before* block erection. It improves **safety, quality, and speed**.
- **Key tolerances** are controlled using the **RSS (Root-Sum-Square) method**, not arithmetic addition.
- **Block weights** typically range from **50 t to 600 t**, with mega-blocks up to **1200 t**.
- **Crane capacity** calculation: $C_{crane} = W_{total} \times SF$ (with SF = 1.25).
- **Plate rolling** is done on **3-roll pyramid or 4-roll plate bending machines**.
- **Welding methods** for panel/block fabrication: **SAW, FCAW, GMAW, FSW** (for aluminum).
- **Hull form coefficients** ($C_B$, $C_M$, $C_P$, $C_{WP}$) are related by: $C_P = C_B / C_M$.
- **Displacement:** $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$.
- **Welding heat input** formula: $HI = \frac{V \cdot I}{S} \times 0.06$ (in kJ/mm) — critical for **high-tensile steel** (HTS) plates to avoid HAZ softening.
- **Symmetrical welding sequence** (from center outward) minimizes **distortion and residual stress**.
- **Modern mega-yards** use **covered assembly halls, gantry cranes (600–1000 t), and digital twin technology** for block fabrication.
- **Block-to-block alignment** uses **hydraulic jacks, dogs, strong-backs, and laser alignment systems**.
- **Launching methods:** gravitational side launch, end launch, float-out (from dry dock), and ship-lift.
- **Always draw a labeled diagram** in any 7-mark or 14-mark descriptive answer — KTU examiners reward visual clarity.
- **Cite at least one classification society** (e.g., **IRS, LR, DNV, BV, ABS**) in answers related to welding/assembly standards.
- **IACS UR Z17** is the universal standard for **hull welding quality** — know it.
- **For 14-mark questions**, the structure should be: **Definition → Stages → Diagram → Comparison → Conclusion → Example Yard**.

---

> **END OF KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 NOTES**
> **Module 3 — Assembly of Ship's Structure | OENST724 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub-Assemblies - Built Up T Bars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sub-Assemblies - Built Up T Bars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Built-Up T Bar:** A structural sub-assembly fabricated by permanently joining (usually by **continuous fillet welding**) a **flat web plate** and a **flat flange plate/bar** at right angles, such that the longitudinal axis of the flange coincides with the centerline of the web, producing a cross-section that geometrically resembles the alphabet **"T"**. In ship construction, built-up T bars are the most widely used **secondary stiffeners** (longitudinals, transverses, stiffeners on decks, sides, bottoms, and bulkheads) when the required section modulus cannot be economically obtained from standard rolled T-sections.

**Nomenclature of the Two Components:**

| Component | Geometric Form | Primary Function | Orientation in T |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Web Plate** | Flat rectangular plate of thickness $t_w$ and depth $h$ | Resists **shear force** and provides the major part of the **section modulus** | Vertical (stem of the T) |
| **Flange Plate / Flat Bar** | Flat rectangular plate of thickness $t_f$ and width $b$ | Resists **bending stress** (compression/tension fibre) | Horizontal (top arm of the T) |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Diving Board:**
> Imagine a long, narrow **diving board** fixed at one end to a swimming pool deck. The board must support a person standing at its free end without sagging or snapping.
> - The **top horizontal layer of wood** (the *flange*) is what your foot presses on — it carries the **direct push/pull (axial) stress**.
> - The **vertical web of wood** below it (the *web*) is what **keeps the top layer from buckling** and carries the **vertical sliding (shear) force** down to the support.
> - When you glue (weld) these two pieces of wood at right angles, you get a **T-shaped cross-section** that is dramatically stiffer and lighter than a single thick plank of the same total weight.
> - A ship hull behaves identically: the **shell plating** acts like a wide thin plank, and welding a **T-bar stiffener** to it prevents the plating from buckling under bending or water pressure. The T-bar is the engineer's "reinforced diving board."

### 1.3 Why "Built-Up" Instead of Rolled T-Sections?

Standard **rolled steel T-sections** (cut from I-beams or hot-rolled) are limited by mill availability. Shipbuilding requires:

- **Custom section moduli** matching the design bending stress
- **Asymmetric proportions** (very deep webs with narrow flanges, or vice versa)
- **Lightweight minimum-scantling compliance** with classification society rules (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, BV, ABS)
- **Reduced waste** — plates are stocked in standard sizes; building a T from them uses material efficiently

**Standard rolled T-bar sizes** (kept for reference per IRS Rules):
> The web depth $h$ typically ranges from **80 mm to 400 mm** and flange width $b$ from **40 mm to 200 mm**, with thicknesses $t_w$ and $t_f$ of **5 mm to 16 mm**.

Built-up T bars therefore dominate modern ship superstructure, deck, and bulkhead stiffening.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Built-Up T Bar Resists Load

When a built-up T bar is welded to a ship plate (deck, side shell, bulkhead) and the plate is loaded (e.g., by water pressure, cargo load, or hull bending), the load travels through the section as follows:

1. **Bending stress** ($\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$) is highest at the **outer fibre** of the flange (the tip of the T-arm). The flange's wide, thin geometry places maximum material exactly where bending stress is greatest — this is the **principle of efficient material distribution**.

2. **Shear stress** ($\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot t}$) is maximum at the **neutral axis** of the section, which lies inside the web. The web's tall, deep geometry resists this vertical shear effectively.

3. **Buckling resistance** of the flange in compression is provided partly by its own thickness $t_f$ and partly by the lateral support from the welded connection to the plating (acting as a continuous **spring support**).

4. **Stress flow** is transferred from the plating → fillet weld → web/flange interface → distributed throughout the T-section.

### 2.2 Core Design Philosophy — Why the T-Shape?

- Material is concentrated **as far as possible from the neutral axis**, maximising the **second moment of area** $I$ for the least weight.
- The T-section is **open**, allowing easy inspection, painting, and welding from one side (critical for shipyard practicality).
- It can be **continuous** through transverse frames (passing over them), unlike angle-bar stiffeners which must be cut at every frame.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Section Property Formulas for a Built-Up T Bar**
> (Web: depth $h$, thickness $t_w$ | Flange: width $b$, thickness $t_f$)

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Web area | $A_w = h \cdot t_w$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Rectangular |
| 2 | Flange area | $A_f = b \cdot t_f$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Rectangular |
| 3 | Total area | $A_T = A_w + A_f$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Sum of components |
| 4 | Centroid from web bottom (web only) | $\bar{y}_w = \dfrac{h}{2}$ | mm | For isolated web |
| 5 | Centroid from web bottom (flange only) | $\bar{y}_f = h + \dfrac{t_f}{2}$ | mm | For isolated flange |
| 6 | Overall centroid from web bottom | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{A_w \cdot \bar{y}_w + A_f \cdot \bar{y}_f}{A_T}$ | mm | Reference axis = bottom of web |
| 7 | Self $I$ of web about its own centroid | $I_{w,\text{self}} = \dfrac{t_w \cdot h^{3}}{12}$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | Standard rectangle formula |
| 8 | Self $I$ of flange about its own centroid | $I_{f,\text{self}} = \dfrac{t_f \cdot b^{3}}{12}$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | Standard rectangle formula |
| 9 | Parallel axis shift — web | $d_w = \bar{y} - \bar{y}_w$ | mm | Distance from web centroid to NA |
| 10 | Parallel axis shift — flange | $d_f = \bar{y}_f - \bar{y}$ | mm | Distance from flange centroid to NA |
| 11 | Total $I$ about neutral axis | $I_{NA} = I_{w,\text{self}} + A_w d_w^{2} + I_{f,\text{self}} + A_f d_f^{2}$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | **Master equation** |
| 12 | Distance to extreme fibre (top) | $y_{\max} = (h + t_f) - \bar{y}$ | mm | Top of flange |
| 13 | Distance to extreme fibre (bottom) | $y_{\min} = \bar{y}$ | mm | Bottom of web |
| 14 | Effective depth for modulus | $y_{\text{eff}} = \max(y_{\max}, y_{\min})$ | mm | Use the larger value |
| 15 | Section modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{\text{eff}}}$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | $Z = I / c$ |
| 16 | Radius of gyration | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{NA}}{A_T}}$ | mm | For slenderness / buckling check |
| 17 | Plastic section modulus (T) | $Z_p = A_w \cdot \dfrac{h}{2} + 2 \cdot A_f \cdot d_f'$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Used in limit-state / ultimate strength |
| 18 | Minimum section modulus (IRS Rule) | $Z_{\min} = c \cdot L^{2} \cdot s$ | $\text{cm}^3$ | $c$ = coefficient, $L$ = span (m), $s$ = spacing (m) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention Alert (KTU Valuation):** Always measure $\bar{y}$ from the **bottom of the web** (the free edge) — *not* from the top — unless the question explicitly specifies otherwise. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for ambiguous reference axes.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Longitudinal stiffeners** on ship decks, bottoms, and sides are predominantly built-up T bars.
- **Transverse frames** in double-bottom tanks, bulkheads, and engine-room platforms use built-up T bars.
- **Superstructure side scuttles and window surrounds** often incorporate small built-up T-stiffeners.
- **Bridge decks and offshore platform decks** use scaled-up versions of the same logic.
- Classification societies (IRS Pt.3 Ch.4, Lloyd's SSC, DNV Pt.3 Ch.4) publish **minimum section modulus tables** based on the formula in row 18 above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Location of the Neutral Axis (Centroid)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** Locate the neutral axis $\bar{y}$ of a built-up T bar measured from the bottom of the web.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

The neutral axis of a built-up section is found using the **first moment of area theorem**:

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i \cdot \bar{y}_i}{\sum A_i}
$$

where the summation runs over every individual plate element of the section.

**Step 1 — Identify the two elements:**

- Web (rectangle of height $h$, thickness $t_w$)
- Flange (rectangle of width $b$, thickness $t_f$)

**Step 2 — Compute individual areas:**

$$
A_w = h \cdot t_w \qquad \text{and} \qquad A_f = b \cdot t_f
$$

**Step 3 — Locate individual centroids** (measured from the bottom of the web, which is taken as the reference datum):

$$
\bar{y}_w = \frac{h}{2} \qquad \text{(mid-height of web)}
$$

$$
\bar{y}_f = h + \frac{t_f}{2} \qquad \text{(web height + half flange thickness)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the centroid formula:**

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{A_w \cdot \bar{y}_w + A_f \cdot \bar{y}_f}{A_w + A_f}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{(h \cdot t_w)\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right) + (b \cdot t_f)\left(h + \dfrac{t_f}{2}\right)}{h \cdot t_w + b \cdot t_f}
$$

**Final Working Expression:**

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{\dfrac{t_w h^{2}}{2} + b \cdot t_f \left(h + \dfrac{t_f}{2}\right)}{t_w h + b \cdot t_f}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Second Moment of Area about the Neutral Axis

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** Derive the moment of inertia $I_{NA}$ of the built-up T bar about its own neutral axis.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

The **parallel axis theorem** (Steiner's theorem) is used for each off-centre element:

$$
I = I_{\text{self}} + A \cdot d^{2}
$$

where $d$ is the perpendicular distance from the element's own centroid to the neutral axis of the whole section.

**Step 1 — Self moment of inertia of the web** (about its own horizontal centroidal axis):

$$
I_{w,\text{self}} = \frac{t_w \cdot h^{3}}{12}
$$

**Step 2 — Self moment of inertia of the flange** (about its own horizontal centroidal axis):

$$
I_{f,\text{self}} = \frac{t_f \cdot b^{3}}{12}
$$

**Step 3 — Distance from the web centroid to the overall neutral axis:**

$$
d_w = \bar{y} - \bar{y}_w = \bar{y} - \frac{h}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Distance from the flange centroid to the overall neutral axis:**

$$
d_f = \bar{y}_f - \bar{y} = \left(h + \frac{t_f}{2}\right) - \bar{y}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the parallel axis theorem to each element:**

For the web:

$$
I_w = I_{w,\text{self}} + A_w \cdot d_w^{2} = \frac{t_w h^{3}}{12} + (h \cdot t_w)\left(\bar{y} - \frac{h}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

For the flange:

$$
I_f = I_{f,\text{self}} + A_f \cdot d_f^{2} = \frac{t_f b^{3}}{12} + (b \cdot t_f)\left(h + \frac{t_f}{2} - \bar{y}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum the contributions:**

$$
I_{NA} = I_w + I_f
$$

**Final Master Expression:**

$$
I_{NA} = \frac{t_w h^{3}}{12} + h t_w \left(\bar{y} - \frac{h}{2}\right)^{2} + \frac{t_f b^{3}}{12} + b t_f \left(h + \frac{t_f}{2} - \bar{y}\right)^{2}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A built-up T bar consists of a web plate **200 mm deep × 8 mm thick** and a flange plate **100 mm wide × 10 mm thick**, welded at the top of the web. Calculate:
> (a) The position of the neutral axis from the bottom of the web.
> (b) The moment of inertia about the neutral axis.
> (c) The section modulus.

**Given Data:**

$$
h = 200\ \text{mm}, \quad t_w = 8\ \text{mm}, \quad b = 100\ \text{mm}, \quad t_f = 10\ \text{mm}
$$

#### Part (a) — Neutral Axis Position

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$
A_w = 200 \times 8 = 1600\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
A_f = 100 \times 10 = 1000\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
A_T = 1600 + 1000 = 2600\ \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Individual centroidal distances (from web bottom):**

$$
\bar{y}_w = \frac{200}{2} = 100\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\bar{y}_f = 200 + \frac{10}{2} = 205\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the centroid formula:**

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{(1600)(100) + (1000)(205)}{2600}
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{160000 + 205000}{2600} = \dfrac{365000}{2600}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{y} = 140.38\ \text{mm from bottom of web}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Moment of Inertia

**Step 1 — Self inertias:**

$$
I_{w,\text{self}} = \frac{8 \times 200^{3}}{12} = \frac{8 \times 8{,}000{,}000}{12} = 5{,}333{,}333.33\ \text{mm}^4
$$

$$
I_{f,\text{self}} = \frac{10 \times 100^{3}}{12} = \frac{10 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{12} = 833{,}333.33\ \text{mm}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Parallel axis distances:**

$$
d_w = 140.38 - 100 = 40.38\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
d_f = 205 - 140.38 = 64.62\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Parallel axis terms:**

$$
A_w d_w^{2} = 1600 \times (40.38)^{2} = 1600 \times 1630.54 = 2{,}608{,}872\ \text{mm}^4
$$

$$
A_f d_f^{2} = 1000 \times (64.62)^{2} = 1000 \times 4175.74 = 4{,}175{,}744\ \text{mm}^4
$$

**Step 4 — Sum all contributions:**

$$
I_{NA} = 5{,}333{,}333.33 + 2{,}608{,}872 + 833{,}333.33 + 4{,}175{,}744
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{NA} = 12{,}951{,}283\ \text{mm}^4 \approx 1.295 \times 10^{7}\ \text{mm}^4}
$$

#### Part (c) — Section Modulus

**Step 1 — Extreme fibre distances:**

$$
y_{\text{bottom}} = \bar{y} = 140.38\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
y_{\text{top}} = (h + t_f) - \bar{y} = 210 - 140.38 = 69.62\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the larger value** (governing fibre for section modulus):

$$
y_{\text{eff}} = 140.38\ \text{mm} \quad \text{(bottom fibre governs)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Z$:**

$$
Z = \frac{I_{NA}}{y_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{12{,}951{,}283}{140.38}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z \approx 92{,}257\ \text{mm}^3 \approx 92.26\ \text{cm}^3}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why T is Better Than Flat Bar

| Property | Flat Bar (Plate alone) | Built-Up T Bar | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material area | $b \cdot t$ | $h t_w + b t_f$ | $\approx$ same (for equal weight design) |
| Depth available | $t$ | $h + t_f$ | $\approx 20\times$ |
| Moment of inertia | $\dfrac{b t^{3}}{12}$ | $I_{NA}$ (calculated above) | $\approx 100\times$ |
| Section modulus | $\dfrac{b t^{2}}{6}$ | $Z = I_{NA}/y_{\text{eff}}$ | $\approx 30\text{–}50\times$ |
| Bending stiffness | Very low | Very high | Drastic |
| Application | Only for lightly loaded members | All major ship stiffeners | Universal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of the analysis:** A small addition of steel in the form of a T-flange **multiplies the section modulus by an order of magnitude** without proportional weight penalty — this is the fundamental engineering reason T-bars dominate ship structural design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Built-Up T Bar Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["FLANGE PLATE"]:::flange -->|continuous fillet weld| B["WEB PLATE"]:::web
    B -->|welded to| C["SHELL OR DECK PLATING"]:::plate
    C --> D["STRESS FROM LOAD"]:::load
    D -->|bending| E["FLANGE carries axial push pull"]:::stress
    D -->|shear| F["WEB carries vertical shear"]:::stress
    D -->|compression| G["FLANGE tip can buckle"]:::buckling
    H["INSPECTION and PAINTING ACCESS"]:::access --- A
    H --- B
    I["GUSSET or SCALLOP at frame crossings"]:::detail --- B

    classDef flange fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A06000,color:#000
    classDef web fill:#A0D0FF,stroke:#003F7F,color:#000
    classDef plate fill:#D0D0D0,stroke:#404040,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#FFB0B0,stroke:#7F0000,color:#000
    classDef stress fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006020,color:#000
    classDef buckling fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#7F0020,color:#000
    classDef access fill:#F0E0FF,stroke:#400080,color:#000
    classDef detail fill:#FFF0C0,stroke:#806000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Section Property Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["INPUT h, tw, b, tf"]:::in --> P2["Aw = h x tw"]
    P2 --> P3["Af = b x tf"]
    P3 --> P4["AT = Aw + Af"]
    P4 --> P5["yw = h / 2"]
    P5 --> P6["yf = h + tf / 2"]
    P6 --> P7["ybar = Aw yw + Af yf over AT"]:::calc
    P7 --> P8["dw = ybar - yw"]
    P8 --> P9["df = yf - ybar"]
    P9 --> P10["Iself web = tw h cubed over 12"]
    P10 --> P11["Iself flange = tf b cubed over 12"]
    P11 --> P12["INA = Iself web + Aw dw squared + Iself flange + Af df squared"]:::calc
    P12 --> P13["y eff = max of ybar and h plus tf minus ybar"]
    P13 --> P14["Z = INA divided by y eff"]:::calc
    P14 --> P15["OUTPUT ybar, INA, Z"]:::out

    classDef in fill:#C0E0FF,stroke:#003070,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#FFE080,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#C0FFC0,stroke:#006020,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Flow Through the T Bar

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["EXTERNAL LOAD on plating"]:::ext --> S2["Distributed to T Bar via welds"]:::weld
    S2 --> S3a["Top of flange: max COMPRESSION or TENSION"]:::top
    S2 --> S3b["Neutral axis: max SHEAR stress"]:::mid
    S2 --> S3c["Bottom of web: opposite axial stress"]:::bot
    S3a --> S4a["sigma = M y over I"]:::eq
    S3b --> S4b["tau = V Q over I t"]:::eq
    S3c --> S4c["sigma = M y over I"]:::eq
    S4a --> S5["Section modulus Z governs design"]:::design
    S4b --> S5
    S4c --> S5

    classDef ext fill:#FFD0D0,stroke:#800000,color:#000
    classDef weld fill:#FFE0A0,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#FF8080,stroke:#400000,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#FFFF80,stroke:#606000,color:#000
    classDef bot fill:#80C0FF,stroke:#003060,color:#000
    classDef eq fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#202080,color:#000
    classDef design fill:#80FF80,stroke:#006000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic of the Cross-Section (for quick reference)

```
            ←—— b = 100 mm ——→
            ┌────────────────┐  ↑
            │   FLANGE       │  │ t_f = 10 mm
            │   t_f = 10 mm  │  ↓
    ────────┤────────────────├────── ← Top datum (y = h + t_f = 210 mm)
            │                │
            │   WEB          │
            │   t_w = 8 mm   │  ↑
            │                │  │ h = 200 mm
            │                │  ↓
            │                │
            │                │
    ────────┤                ├────── ← Bottom datum (y = 0)
            └────────────────┘
                (h × t_w)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define a built-up T bar. List its two main components and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **built-up T bar** is a structural section fabricated by welding a flat web plate to a flat flange plate at right angles, producing a "T"-shaped cross-section. It is used as a stiffener in ship structures (decks, sides, bulkheads).

The two components are:

1. **Web plate** — the vertical stem of the T; resists **shear force** and provides the major contribution to the depth of the section.
2. **Flange plate (flat bar)** — the horizontal arm of the T; resists **bending stress** at the extreme fibre and provides the bulk of the section modulus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks distribution:** Definition = 1, Components = 1, Functions = 1.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Why are built-up T bars preferred over standard rolled T-sections in modern shipbuilding?**

**Model Answer (3 key points):**

1. **Size flexibility** — Rolled T-sections are limited to mill-standard dimensions, whereas built-up T bars can be fabricated to any web depth, flange width, and thickness required by the structural design.
2. **Optimised weight** — Built-up sections are tailored to meet the *exact* section modulus specified by classification society rules, avoiding material wastage.
3. **Continuous length** — Built-up T bars can be welded from multiple plates to run continuously through several frames, unlike rolled bars which are cut to stock lengths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks distribution:** 1 mark per valid point (any 3 points).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module Internal Choice — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part = **7 marks**. Total = 14 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction of a built-up T bar. State any **four** applications of built-up T bars in ship construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A built-up T bar has a web plate **250 mm deep × 10 mm thick** and a flange plate **120 mm wide × 12 mm thick**, welded at the top of the web. Calculate the position of the neutral axis from the bottom of the web, the moment of inertia about the neutral axis, and the section modulus. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction & Applications (7 marks)

**Construction (4 marks):**

A built-up T bar is fabricated in a shipyard by the following process:

1. **Material preparation** — A web plate (typically 5–16 mm thickness) is cut to the required depth and length. A flange plate (flat bar) is cut to the required width and length.
2. **Edge preparation** — The contact edges of both plates are prepared (typically square-edged for thin plates, bevelled for thicker plates > 12 mm) to ensure full penetration of the weld.
3. **Assembly / tacking** — The flange plate is placed on a flat welding jig, and the web plate is erected vertically on the centerline of the flange. The assembly is tacked at intervals of about 300–500 mm.
4. **Continuous fillet welding** — A continuous **fillet weld** is deposited along both sides of the web–flange junction. Weld leg size is typically **0.7 × thinner plate** per IRS rules.
5. **Inspection** — The welds are visually inspected and, where required, subjected to non-destructive testing (ultrasonic or magnetic particle).

**Sketch (must be drawn in the answer booklet):**

```
   ←————— b —————→
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │                  │  ← Flange (t_f)
   ├──────────────────┤
   │                  │
   │                  │
   │   Web (t_w)      │
   │                  │
   │                  │
   │                  │
   │                  │
   │                  │
   │                  │
   └──────────────────┘
       ↑     ↑
     weld  weld
   (both sides)
```

**Four Applications in Ship Construction (3 marks — 1 mark each, any four):**

1. **Longitudinal deck stiffeners** on weather decks, platform decks, and tank tops.
2. **Side shell longitudinals** in single-skin and double-skin ship side structures.
3. **Bottom longitudinals** in double-bottom construction.
4. **Transverse bulkhead stiffeners** for increased buckling resistance under sloshing loads.
5. **Superstructure side framing** for windows, scuttles, and accommodation spaces.
6. **Engine-room and pump-room platforms** for supporting machinery loads.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $h = 250\ \text{mm}$, $t_w = 10\ \text{mm}$, $b = 120\ \text{mm}$, $t_f = 12\ \text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Areas (1 mark):**

$$
A_w = 250 \times 10 = 2500\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
A_f = 120 \times 12 = 1440\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
A_T = 2500 + 1440 = 3940\ \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Centroidal distances (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{y}_w = \frac{250}{2} = 125\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\bar{y}_f = 250 + \frac{12}{2} = 256\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Neutral axis position (1.5 marks):**

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{(2500)(125) + (1440)(256)}{3940}
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{312500 + 368640}{3940} = \dfrac{681140}{3940}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{y} = 172.88\ \text{mm from bottom of web}}
$$

**Step 4 — Distances from overall neutral axis (1 mark):**

$$
d_w = 172.88 - 125 = 47.88\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
d_f = 256 - 172.88 = 83.12\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Self moments of inertia (1 mark):**

$$
I_{w,\text{self}} = \frac{10 \times 250^{3}}{12} = \frac{10 \times 15{,}625{,}000}{12} = 13{,}020{,}833.33\ \text{mm}^4
$$

$$
I_{f,\text{self}} = \frac{12 \times 120^{3}}{12} = 1{,}728{,}000\ \text{mm}^4
$$

**Step 6 — Parallel axis terms (1 mark):**

$$
A_w d_w^{2} = 2500 \times (47.88)^{2} = 2500 \times 2292.49 = 5{,}731{,}232\ \text{mm}^4
$$

$$
A_f d_f^{2} = 1440 \times (83.12)^{2} = 1440 \times 6908.93 = 9{,}948{,}859\ \text{mm}^4
$$

**Step 7 — Total moment of inertia (0.5 mark):**

$$
I_{NA} = 13{,}020{,}833.33 + 5{,}731{,}232 + 1{,}728{,}000 + 9{,}948{,}859
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{NA} = 30{,}428{,}924\ \text{mm}^4 \approx 3.043 \times 10^{7}\ \text{mm}^4}
$$

**Step 8 — Section modulus (1 mark):**

$$
y_{\text{top}} = (250 + 12) - 172.88 = 89.12\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
y_{\text{bottom}} = 172.88\ \text{mm} \quad \text{(governs)}
$$

$$
Z = \frac{30{,}428{,}924}{172.88}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z \approx 176{,}023\ \text{mm}^3 \approx 176.02\ \text{cm}^3}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Compare built-up T bars with **standard rolled T-sections** and **flat bars** as stiffeners in shipbuilding. Discuss the relative merits and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship side longitudinal is to be designed as a built-up T bar with the following dimensions: web **300 mm deep × 12 mm thick**, flange **150 mm wide × 14 mm thick**. Calculate: (i) the section modulus, (ii) the moment of inertia, and (iii) the radius of gyration of the section about its neutral axis. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (Key Steps)

#### Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (write this in the answer booklet):**

| Parameter | Flat Bar | Rolled T-Section | Built-Up T Bar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cross-section | Rectangular | Hot-rolled T | Welded plate T |
| Depth | Very small | Limited by mill | Customisable |
| Section modulus | Very low | Moderate | Very high (optimised) |
| Material efficiency | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Cost | Cheapest | Higher | Moderate |
| Availability | Universal | Standard sizes only | Fabricated on demand |
| Welded to plating | Difficult (needs edge weld) | Easy | Easy |
| Use in shipbuilding | Limited to light members | Moderate use | Dominant (90%+) |

**Merits of built-up T bars:** high section modulus, customisable, lightweight, easy to weld.
**Limitations:** requires welding (skilled labour, NDT), distortion control needed, longer fabrication time.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $h = 300\ \text{mm}$, $t_w = 12\ \text{mm}$, $b = 150\ \text{mm}$, $t_f = 14\ \text{mm}$

**Areas:**

$$
A_w = 300 \times 12 = 3600\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
A_f = 150 \times 14 = 2100\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
A_T = 5700\ \text{mm}^2
$$

**Centroidal distances:**

$$
\bar{y}_w = 150\ \text{mm}, \qquad \bar{y}_f = 300 + 7 = 307\ \text{mm}
$$

**Neutral axis:**

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{(3600)(150) + (2100)(307)}{5700} = \dfrac{540000 + 644700}{5700} = \dfrac{1184700}{5700}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{y} = 207.84\ \text{mm}}
$$

**Distances to NA:**

$$
d_w = 207.84 - 150 = 57.84\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
d_f = 307 - 207.84 = 99.16\ \text{mm}
$$

**Self inertias:**

$$
I_{w,\text{self}} = \frac{12 \times 300^{3}}{12} = 27{,}000{,}000\ \text{mm}^4
$$

$$
I_{f,\text{self}} = \frac{14 \times 150^{3}}{12} = \frac{14 \times 3{,}375{,}000}{12} = 3{,}937{,}500\ \text{mm}^4
$$

**Parallel axis contributions:**

$$
A_w d_w^{2} = 3600 \times (57.84)^{2} = 3600 \times 3345.47 = 12{,}043{,}692\ \text{mm}^4
$$

$$
A_f d_f^{2} = 2100 \times (99.16)^{2} = 2100 \times 9832.71 = 20{,}648{,}691\ \text{mm}^4
$$

**(i) Moment of inertia:**

$$
I_{NA} = 27{,}000{,}000 + 12{,}043{,}692 + 3{,}937{,}500 + 20{,}648{,}691
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{NA} = 63{,}629{,}883\ \text{mm}^4 \approx 6.363 \times 10^{7}\ \text{mm}^4}
$$

**(ii) Section modulus:**

$$
y_{\text{eff}} = \max(207.84,\ 314 - 207.84) = \max(207.84,\ 106.16) = 207.84\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
Z = \frac{63{,}629{,}883}{207.84}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z \approx 306{,}144\ \text{mm}^3 \approx 306.14\ \text{cm}^3}
$$

**(iii) Radius of gyration:**

$$
k = \sqrt{\frac{I_{NA}}{A_T}} = \sqrt{\frac{63{,}629{,}883}{5700}} = \sqrt{11163.14}
$$

$$
\boxed{k \approx 105.66\ \text{mm}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong reference axis** — Measuring centroid from the **top** of the flange instead of the **bottom** of the web. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the parallel axis term** $A \cdot d^{2}$ — students often use only $I_{\text{self}}$ and forget the Steiner contribution. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing cm and mm without conversion. Always stay in **mm** for the entire calculation. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Wrong extreme fibre** — using $h + t_f$ as $y_{\text{eff}}$ instead of computing **both** top and bottom distances from the NA. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Missing sketch in descriptive answers** — Part (a) of any 14-mark question must include a **neat labelled sketch**. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 6. **Decimal precision** — KTU examiners expect answers to **2 decimal places** for distances and **whole numbers** for areas. Avoid 5+ decimal places.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Built-Up T Bars (Module 3)**
>
> - A built-up T bar = **welded web plate + flange plate**; used as ship stiffener.
> - **Web** = vertical, resists **shear**; **Flange** = horizontal, resists **bending stress** at extreme fibre.
> - Preferred over rolled T-sections because of **size flexibility, weight optimisation, and continuous length**.
> - **Centroid** measured from the **bottom of the web** (datum line).
> - **Master equations** to memorise:
>   * $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i \bar{y}_i}{\sum A_i}$
>   * $I_{NA} = \sum \left( I_{\text{self},i} + A_i d_i^{2} \right)$
>   * $Z = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{\text{eff}}}$
>   * $k = \sqrt{I_{NA} / A_T}$
> - The **governing extreme fibre** is whichever is **farther** from the neutral axis (top or bottom).
> - For typical ship T-bars, $\bar{y}$ usually lies in the **lower half of the web**, so the **bottom fibre** generally governs $Z$.
> - Welds are **continuous double-sided fillet welds**; leg size = **0.7 × thinner plate** per IRS rules.
> - Built-up T bars are used for **deck longitudinals, side shell longitudinals, bottom longitudinals, bulkhead stiffeners, and superstructure framing**.
> - Always check **classification society minimum section modulus** ($Z_{\min} = c L^{2} s$) before finalising the design.
> - In KTU 2024 exams: **3-mark** questions test definition and applications; **14-mark** questions test the **calculation sequence** plus a **sketched description**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Web Frames # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Web Frame (Transverse Web):** A web frame is a specially deepened, heavy transverse structural member of a ship's hull, fabricated from a plate web with flanged edges, designed to provide enhanced transverse strength and rigidity in regions subjected to concentrated loads, high shear forces, or significant bending moments. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (OENST724 – Basics of Ship Construction), web frames are classified as **primary transverse stiffening members** that supplement the standard frame system. They are formally recognized by classification societies (Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, IRS) as load-bearing structures that must satisfy minimum section modulus and moment of inertia requirements. **Key Structural Identifiers:** - **Depth Ratio:** Web frame depth is typically **2.5 to 4 times** the depth of an ordinary frame. - **Material Form:** Rolled steel plate (web) + flat bar or bulb flange (face plate/flange). - **Standard Spacing:** Every 3rd, 4th, or 5th frame space (i.e., 2.4 m to 4.0 m typical). - **Standard Location:** Engine room, boiler room, forepeak, afterpeak, and under deckhouses. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rib Cage Reinforcement" Model Imagine a human rib cage. The regular ribs are thin and numerous — they protect the body but cannot withstand heavy blows. However, in specific places (the sternum, the back), there are **thicker, more rigid bones** that provide extra strength where it is most needed. A web frame is exactly this — the **"reinforced rib"** of a ship. While ordinary frames spread across the hull like rib bones, web frames are strategically placed at stress hotspots to carry concentrated loads that ordinary frames simply cannot handle alone. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):** Web frames, together with transverses and stringers, form the **transverse framing system** of a ship. Their primary engineering role is to **transfer localized deck loads and hull pressures to the shell plating and bottom structure**, preventing panel buckling and ensuring global hull integrity. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Web frame depth (D) | **400 to 1200** | mm | | Web plate thickness (t) | **8 to 20** | mm | | Face plate breadth (b_f) | **80 to 200** | mm | | Face plate thickness (t_f) | **10 to 25** | mm | | Frame spacing (s) | **600 to 800** | mm (ordinary) | | Web frame spacing (S) | **2400 to 4000** | mm | | Section modulus (Z) | **100 to 600** | cm³ | | Yield stress (σ_y) | **235 to 355** | MPa | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Web Frames Are Needed The decision to install web frames is governed by the **load path principle** in ship structural design. The logic chain is: 1. **Local Pressure Loading:** Deck cargo, heavy machinery, sea pressure on shell, and tank fluid loads create localized high-intensity forces. 2. **Ordinary Frame Insufficiency:** Standard frames (typically 75×75×8 mm T-bars or angles) have low section modulus and cannot resist these concentrated loads without excessive deflection. 3. **Reinforcement Trigger:** When the calculated required section modulus exceeds ordinary frame capacity, **web frames are introduced** at strategic spacings. 4. **Load Transfer Pathway:** Web frames act as deep girders that span between the shell plating and the deck/inner bottom, transferring loads to the **primary supporting structure** (transverses, girders, pillars). 5. **Stiffened Panel Behaviour:** The web frame stiffens the shell/deck plating, reducing the unsupported span of plate panels and **preventing buckling** under compressive or shear loads. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Structural Breakdown - **Web Plate (Vertical Plate):** The deep vertical plate that forms the "web" of the I-section. Carries shear force primarily. - **Face Plate (Flange):** A horizontal flat bar welded to the upper/lower edge of the web plate. Resists bending stress (tension/compression). - **End Brackets / Gussets:** Triangular plates welded at the top and bottom of the web frame to distribute load into the deck and bottom plating. - **Lightening Holes (Optional):** Circular or oval cut-outs in the web plate to reduce weight, provided stress concentrations are adequately compensated by surrounding plate. - **Stiffeners on Web:** Vertical or horizontal stiffeners welded to the web plate to prevent the web itself from buckling under shear (web crippling). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations for KTU University Exam (Part A & Part B).** Critical unit consistency: keep dimensions in **mm** for direct mm³/ mm⁴ output, or convert to **cm** for cm³/cm⁴. ### Table 2.1: Web Frame Section Property Formulas | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Moment of Inertia (web only) | $I_{w} = \dfrac{D \cdot t^{3}}{12}$ | mm⁴ | Contribution of the vertical web plate alone | | 2 | Moment of Inertia (flange) | $I_{f} = \dfrac{b_{f} \cdot t_{f}^{3}}{12} + b_{f} \cdot t_{f} \cdot y^{2}$ | mm⁴ | Parallel axis theorem applied to flange | | 3 | Total Moment of Inertia | $I_{total} = I_{w} + I_{f}$ | mm⁴ | Sum of contributions (about N.A.) | | 4 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I_{total}}{c}$ | mm³ | Where $c$ = distance to extreme fiber | | 5 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | MPa | Maximum bending stress check | | 6 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A_{web}} = \dfrac{V}{D \cdot t}$ | MPa | Average shear in web | | 7 | Web Frame Depth (Rule) | $D = k \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot S}$ | mm | Empirical rule (L=span, S=spacing) | | 8 | Load on Web Frame | $w = p \cdot S$ | N/mm | Pressure × spacing | | 9 | Bending Moment | $M = \dfrac{w \cdot L^{2}}{8}$ | N·mm | For simply-supported case | | 10 | Required Section Modulus | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{perm}}$ | mm³ | Design check formula | > Where: > - $D$ = depth of web plate (mm) > - $t$ = web plate thickness (mm) > - $b_{f}$ = breadth of face plate (mm) > - $t_{f}$ = face plate thickness (mm) > - $y$ = distance from N.A. to centroid of flange (mm) > - $L$ = unsupported span of web frame (mm) > - $S$ = web frame spacing (mm) > - $p$ = design pressure (MPa = N/mm²) > - $M$ = maximum bending moment > - $V$ = maximum shear force > - $\sigma_{perm}$ = permissible bending stress (typically **0.6 σ_y**) > - $k$ = empirical constant (typically 2.5 to 3.5 depending on classification society) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In commercial shipbuilding, web frames are the **silent load-bearers** in the following scenarios: - **VLCC Engine Rooms:** The 80–120 MW main engine transmits thousands of kN of force to the hull foundation; web frames under the engine bear this load. - **Bulk Carrier Holds:** Cargo impact loads (ore dropping from grabs) are absorbed by web frames at alternate frames. - **Naval Warships:** Web frames support magazine and weapon mounting structures where blast/shock loads are critical. - **Offshore Supply Vessels:** Deck-mounted drilling equipment loads are routed through deep web frames to the hull. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Section Modulus of an I-Type Web Frame ### Given A web frame consists of a vertical web plate of depth **D = 800 mm** and thickness **t = 12 mm**, with a single face plate of breadth **b_f = 150 mm** and thickness **t_f = 15 mm** attached at the top edge. The neutral axis (N.A.) is assumed at the centroid of the web (since flange area is small compared to web depth). ### Step 1: Identify the geometric components The web frame cross-section has two parts: - **Web plate (rectangle):** Depth $D$, thickness $t$ - **Face plate (rectangle):** Breadth $b_{f}$, thickness $t_{f}$ ### Step 2: Calculate the Moment of Inertia of the Web Plate The web plate is treated as a rectangle bending about its horizontal centroidal axis. $$ I_{w} = \frac{D \cdot t^{3}}{12} $$ Substituting the values: $$ I_{w} = \frac{800 \times 12^{3}}{12} $$ $$ I_{w} = \frac{800 \times 1728}{12} $$ $$ I_{w} = \frac{1382400}{12} $$ $$ I_{w} = 115200 \;\; \text{mm}^{4} $$ ### Step 3: Calculate the Moment of Inertia of the Face Plate Using the **parallel axis theorem**, the moment of inertia of the face plate about the neutral axis of the combined section is: $$ I_{f} = \frac{b_{f} \cdot t_{f}^{3}}{12} + b_{f} \cdot t_{f} \cdot y^{2} $$ Where $y$ is the distance from the N.A. of the combined section to the centroid of the face plate. Approximating $y \approx D/2$ (when flange area is small): $$ y = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{800}{2} = 400 \;\; \text{mm} $$ Now compute $I_{f}$: $$ I_{f} = \frac{150 \times 15^{3}}{12} + 150 \times 15 \times 400^{2} $$ $$ I_{f} = \frac{150 \times 3375}{12} + 2250 \times 160000 $$ $$ I_{f} = \frac{506250}{12} + 360000000 $$ $$ I_{f} = 42187.5 + 360000000 $$ $$ I_{f} = 360042187.5 \;\; \text{mm}^{4} $$ ### Step 4: Total Moment of Inertia $$ I_{total} = I_{w} + I_{f} $$ $$ I_{total} = 115200 + 360042187.5 $$ $$ I_{total} = 360157387.5 \;\; \text{mm}^{4} $$ ### Step 5: Section Modulus The distance from the N.A. to the extreme fiber of the flange is: $$ c = \frac{D}{2} + t_{f} = 400 + 15 = 415 \;\; \text{mm} $$ Therefore: $$ Z = \frac{I_{total}}{c} $$ $$ Z = \frac{360157387.5}{415} $$ $$ Z = 867849.61 \;\; \text{mm}^{3} \approx 867.85 \;\; \text{cm}^{3} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The flange contributes **99.97%** of the total moment of inertia. The web alone contributes a negligible amount (0.03%). This proves the engineering principle: **flanges carry bending, web carries shear** in an I-section. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Verification of Required Web Frame Depth (Rule Formula) ### Given - Span of web frame between supports: $L = 6000$ mm - Spacing of web frames: $S = 3000$ mm - Design pressure on supported plating: $p = 0.05$ MPa = 0.05 N/mm² - Permissible bending stress: $\sigma_{perm} = 0.6 \times 235 = 141$ MPa (for mild steel) - Empirical constant: $k = 3.0$ ### Step 1: Load per unit length on the web frame $$ w = p \cdot S $$ $$ w = 0.05 \times 3000 = 150 \;\; \text{N/mm} $$ ### Step 2: Maximum bending moment (simply supported) $$ M = \frac{w \cdot L^{2}}{8} $$ $$ M = \frac{150 \times 6000^{2}}{8} $$ $$ M = \frac{150 \times 36000000}{8} $$ $$ M = \frac{5.4 \times 10^{9}}{8} $$ $$ M = 6.75 \times 10^{8} \;\; \text{N·mm} $$ ### Step 3: Required section modulus $$ Z_{req} = \frac{M}{\sigma_{perm}} $$ $$ Z_{req} = \frac{6.75 \times 10^{8}}{141} $$ $$ Z_{req} = 4.787 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{mm}^{3} = 4787 \;\; \text{cm}^{3} $$ ### Step 4: Empirical rule depth $$ D = k \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot S} $$ $$ D = 3.0 \times \sqrt{6000 \times 3000} $$ $$ D = 3.0 \times \sqrt{18000000} $$ $$ D = 3.0 \times 4242.64 $$ $$ D \approx 12728 \;\; \text{mm} \;\; \text{(unrealistic, hence rule modified in practice)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Interpretation:** The empirical rule $D = k\sqrt{LS}$ gives an **upper bound estimate**. Classification society rules (e.g., Lloyd's Register Pt.3, Ch.4) use **direct section modulus formulas** with pressure coefficients. The empirical constant $k$ is usually **2.5** for tank top regions and **3.5** for side shell regions. Always cross-check with the relevant rule book. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Web Frame Sizing Decision Table | Condition | Decision | Action | |---|---|---| | Ordinary frame Z < Z_required | **Install web frame** | Increase depth by 2.5–4× | | Web frame span > 6 m | **Provide central support** | Add pillar or partial bulkhead | | Engine room, boiler room | **Mandatory** | Every 3rd–4th frame | | High cyclic load area | **Add face plate both sides** | Doubled stiffening | | Corrosive tank boundary | **Increase corrosion addition** | Add 1–2 mm to thickness | | Web frame meets bulkhead | **Provide gusset plate** | Weld heavy fillet | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Web Frame Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Ship Hull Section] --> B[Transverse Framing System] B --> C[Ordinary Frame
Spacing 600-800mm] B --> D[Web Frame
Spacing 2400-4000mm] B --> E[Tank Side Transverse] D --> F[Web Plate
Vertical Steel Plate] D --> G[Face Plate
Horizontal Flange] D --> H[End Brackets
Top and Bottom] D --> I[Lightening Holes
Weight Reduction] D --> J[Web Stiffeners
Buckling Prevention] F --> K[Load Transfer Pathway] G --> K H --> K K --> L[Shell Plating] K --> M[Deck Plating] K --> N[Inner Bottom] L --> O[Sea Pressure Reaction] M --> P[Deck Load Reaction] N --> Q[Cargo Pressure Reaction] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Web Frame Load Path Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Applied Pressure
p N/mm²] --> M1[Load per unit length
w = p x S] M1 --> B1[Bending Moment
M = wL²/8] M1 --> S1[Shear Force
V = wL/2] B1 --> Z1[Required Section Modulus
Z = M/sigma_perm] S1 --> T1[Shear Stress Check
tau = V/Aweb] Z1 --> D1[Select Web Frame
D, t, bf, tf] D1 --> C1[Check Buckling
Web and Flange] C1 --> Pass{Safe?} Pass -->|Yes| OK[Web Frame Approved] Pass -->|No| Rev[Revise Dimensions] Rev --> D1 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Section Detail (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CrossSection[Web Frame Cross Section] direction TB TopFlange[Face Plate Top
bf x tf] WebPlate[Web Plate
D x t] BotFlange[Face Plate Bottom
bf x tf] TopFlange --- WebPlate WebPlate --- BotFlange end Stiff1[Vertical Stiffener] --> WebPlate Stiff2[Horizontal Stiffener] --> WebPlate Hole1[Lightening Hole 1] -.-> WebPlate Hole2[Lightening Hole 2] -.-> WebPlate ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Since physical 3D drawings of ship structural members are complex, these Mermaid block diagrams represent the **functional load-transfer architecture** and **component hierarchy** of a web frame assembly. The actual fabricated member is a single I-shaped rolled/welded plate girder spanning vertically between the deck and the bottom. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Define a web frame. Why is it called a "web" frame and not an ordinary frame?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A web frame is a specially deepened transverse structural member in a ship's hull, made from a vertical plate web with horizontal flanges, used to provide additional strength in regions of concentrated loads. It is called a "web" frame because the **main load-carrying element is a deep vertical plate (the "web")** of an I-section girder, as opposed to an ordinary frame which is a simple angle or T-bar. The web provides the **shear-carrying depth** required to resist heavy transverse loads, which an ordinary small frame cannot. **(Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for terminology, 1 mark for distinguishing from ordinary frame.)** ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: List any four locations in a ship where web frames are mandatorily provided.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Engine room and boiler room** — to support heavy machinery foundations. 2. **Fore peak and after peak** — high slamming and shear stress zones. 3. **Under deckhouses** — concentrated superstructure loads. 4. **At bulkhead intersections** — to distribute high shear at structural discontinuities. 5. (Optional) **In way of heavy cargo holds** (bulk carriers) or **drilling rigs** (offshore vessels). **(Distribution: 0.75 marks each for any four correctly stated locations.)** --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice A]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the constructional details of a typical web frame, including its components and end connections. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A web frame in the engine room of a ship has the following dimensions: web depth D = 900 mm, web thickness t = 14 mm, face plate breadth b_f = 180 mm, face plate thickness t_f = 18 mm. Calculate the moment of inertia and section modulus of the section.
