- $k_1$ = material constant (≈ 0.0015 for mild steel).

**Substituting the heat input formula:**

$$\alpha = \dfrac{k_1}{t^{1.5}} \cdot \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$$

For SAW with $\eta = 0.85$, $V = 32$ V, $I = 600$ A, $v = 400$ mm/min, and $t = 20$ mm:

**Step 1:** Compute heat input per mm:
$$H = \dfrac{0.85 \times 32 \times 600}{400 \times 1000} = \dfrac{16320}{400000} = 0.0408 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute distortion:
$$\alpha = \dfrac{0.0015 \times 0.0408}{20^{1.5}} = \dfrac{6.12 \times 10^{-5}}{89.44} = 6.84 \times 10^{-7} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to degrees:
$$\alpha = 6.84 \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 3.92 \times 10^{-5} \text{ degrees}$$

This is negligible — confirming why SAW on a panel line gives nearly distortion-free panels.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Panel Mass Calculation

**Problem:** A flat bottom panel measures 12 m × 8 m. Plate thickness is 18 mm. Five T-bar stiffeners (each 9 kg/m) run along the 12 m direction. Calculate the total panel mass.

**Step 1:** Plate mass
$$m_{\text{plate}} = \rho \cdot A \cdot t = 7850 \times (12 \times 8) \times 0.018$$
$$m_{\text{plate}} = 7850 \times 96 \times 0.018 = 13{,}564.8 \text{ kg} \approx 13.56 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2:** Stiffener mass
$$m_{\text{stiff}} = 9 \text{ kg/m} \times 12 \text{ m} \times 5 = 540 \text{ kg} = 0.54 \text{ t}$$

**Step 3:** Total mass
$$m_{\text{total}} = 13.56 + 0.54 = 14.10 \text{ t}$$

This 14-tonne panel is well within the 5–25 t handling envelope of a typical yard gantry crane.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Flat Panel Line — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **9-stage production workflow** of a flat section prefabrication line. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters per the safeguards.


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define *flat section* in shipbuilding. List any four types of flat sections.

**Model Answer:**

A **flat section** (or **plating panel**) is a two-dimensional, low-curvature structural unit of the ship's hull, fabricated in the workshop as a discrete sub-assembly before being erected on the slipway. It consists of a parent plate, stiffeners, and edge attachments.

Four types of flat sections are:
1. **Bottom shell panel** (keel plate, bilge, flat of bottom).
2. **Side shell panel** (amidships vertical hull).
3. **Deck panel** (strength deck and platform decks).
4. **Transverse/longitudinal bulkhead panel** (collision, engine room, cargo hold bulkheads).

*(Alternative: Superstructure side panels, girder panels, hatch coaming panels.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
State any **three advantages** of prefabricating flat sections in a shipyard.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Improved productivity:** Welding is done in shop conditions in the down-hand position, increasing deposition rate and reducing defect rates.
2. **Reduced construction time:** Parallel fabrication of multiple panels shortens overall shipbuilding cycle.
3. **Better quality control:** Workshop jigs, mechanized lines, and NDT access ensure higher dimensional accuracy and weld integrity.
4. *Weather independence:* Indoor fabrication is unaffected by rain, wind, or cold.
5. *Ergonomic safety:* Workers operate at floor level rather than at height on the slipway.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **9-stage production flow** of a typical **flat panel line** in a modern shipyard. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **advantages and limitations** of prefabricating flat sections. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

The 9-stage flow of a flat panel line is:

| Stage | Operation | Equipment Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steel storage & issue | Overhead gantry crane, magnetic chuck |
| 2 | Surface preparation | Shot blasting machine, primer booth |
| 3 | Marking & nesting | CNC marking table |
| 4 | Cutting | Plasma/oxy-fuel/laser cutting machine |
| 5 | Edge beveling | 3-torch beveling carriage |
| 6 | Plate turning | Hydraulic turnover rolls |
| 7 | Stiffener fit-up & tack | Welding jig, magnetic clamps, tack welder |
| 8 | Main welding | SAW twin-arc, FCAW, GMAW stations |
| 9 | Inspection & dispatch | NDT lab, painting booth, dispatch trailer |

*(See Section 4.1 block diagram for the visual flow.)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Advantages:**
- Higher productivity and welding speed.
- Consistent quality via jigs and mechanization.
- Reduced hull erection time.
- Better working conditions for welders.
- Lower overall cost per tonne of steel.

**Limitations:**
- High initial investment in workshop infrastructure.
- Heavy panels require powerful gantry cranes.
- Transport of large panels from shop to berth is complex.
- Not suitable for sharply curved sections (bow, stern, bilge sphere).

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Actual Pattern]

**(a)** Describe the **classification of flat sections** in a ship with neat sketches (use a labelled block diagram if sketches are not possible). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **bottom shell panel** measures 15 m × 10 m with a plate thickness of 20 mm. Six T-stiffeners (mass 12 kg/m) run along the 15 m direction. Calculate the **total mass of the panel** and the **heat input per unit length** if SAW is performed at $V = 30$ V, $I = 700$ A, travel speed $v = 450$ mm/min, with thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

Flat sections are classified as:
1. **Bottom shell panels** — keel, bilge, flat of bottom (thickest plates, up to 50 mm).
2. **Side shell panels** — port and starboard, built in pairs.
3. **Deck panels** — strength deck, platform decks, stringer plates.
4. **Bulkhead panels** — transverse (collision, engine room), longitudinal (in tankers).
5. **Superstructure panels** — accommodation, bridge wings, mast houses.

Each panel typically consists of a **parent plate**, **stiffeners** (T-bars, L-bars, flat bars), **edge laps/butt straps**, and **local cut-outs**.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Plate mass:**

$$m_{\text{plate}} = \rho \cdot A \cdot t = 7850 \times (15 \times 10) \times 0.020$$
$$m_{\text{plate}} = 7850 \times 150 \times 0.020 = 23{,}550 \text{ kg} = 23.55 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2 — Stiffener mass:**

$$m_{\text{stiff}} = 12 \text{ kg/m} \times 15 \text{ m} \times 6 = 1080 \text{ kg} = 1.08 \text{ t}$$

**Step 3 — Total mass:**

$$m_{\text{total}} = 23.55 + 1.08 = 24.63 \text{ t}$$

**Step 4 — Heat input per unit length:**

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 30 \times 700}{450 \times 1000}$$

$$H = \dfrac{17{,}850}{450{,}000} = 0.0397 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

This heat input is well within the **0.5–2.0 kJ/mm** typical range for SAW, indicating acceptable HAZ characteristics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "flat section" with "block construction":** Flat sections are *panels* (sub-blocks). A block is a 3-D assembly of panels. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting units in heat input:** Always express in kJ/mm. Writing J/mm or kJ/m without unit conversion loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the rework loop:** When asked for the "production flow", students often list only the forward path. The quality-rework feedback loop is a *bonus mark* area.
> 4. **Using wrong density:** Plate mass calculations MUST use $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for steel. Using 7800 or 8000 costs 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the beveling stage:** Many students jump from cutting to welding, skipping the **edge preparation** stage, which is a critical step for groove welds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** Flat section = a low-curvature workshop-built plating panel with parent plate, stiffeners, and edge attachments.
- **Five major types:** Bottom, side, deck, bulkhead, superstructure.
- **Nine production stages:** Storage → Blasting → Marking → Cutting → Beveling → Turning → Fit-up → Welding → Inspection & Dispatch.
- **Key equipment:** CNC marking table, plasma/oxy-fuel cutter, 3-torch beveler, SAW twin-arc station, NDT lab, gantry crane.
- **Critical parameters:** Panel mass (5–25 t), heat input ($\le 2$ kJ/mm for SAW), bevel angle (30°–45°), flatness tolerance ($\pm 2$ mm/m).
- **Core formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ (heat input in kJ/mm).
- **Core formula:** $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot t$ (plate mass).
- **Core formula:** $N_{\text{opt}} \propto (L \cdot B)^{0.583}$ (optimum panel count).
- **Major advantages:** Productivity, quality, weather-independence, ergonomics, parallel production.
- **Major limitations:** High capital cost, requires heavy lifting, unsuitable for sharply curved sections.
- **Key welding process:** Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) is the workhorse of panel lines.
- **Quality checks:** Visual, dimensional, UT, RT, MPI as applicable; rework loop must be illustrated.
- **Modern trend:** Automated robotic panel lines (e.g., Hyundai HI's "Smart Yard") with vision-based fit-up and AI-driven welding parameter control.
- **Exam tip:** Always include the **block diagram** in long answers — it is a high-yield, marks-rich component.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-Assembly of Biaxial Stiffened Panels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pre-Assembly of Biaxial Stiffened Panels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Biaxial Stiffened Panel** is a primary load-bearing ship structural element consisting of a thin plating reinforced by a grid of orthogonal stiffeners — *longitudinal* (running fore-aft, parallel to the principal stress) and *transverse* (running athwartships, perpendicular to the principal stress). The combined plate–stiffener system resists in-plane (membrane) and out-of-plane (buckling) loads typical of ship hulls, decks, bulkheads, and superstructures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A biaxial stiffened panel is a planar plate structure in which the parent plate is stiffened in **two perpendicular directions**, forming an integrated grillage. In ship construction, the **pre-assembly** refers to the *workshop fabrication* of this panel as a discrete sub-assembly *before* it is lifted and welded into the final block/ship.

**Pre-Assembly** of biaxial stiffened panels is a modular shipbuilding strategy in which:
1. The plating is first cut, cleaned, and laid flat on a jig/shop floor.
2. Orthogonal stiffeners (T-bars, flat bars, bulb plates, or angle bars) are fitted, tacked, and welded to the plate.
3. The completed panel is **non-destructively tested (NDT)** and dimensionally checked.
4. The unit is then transported and erected as a single welded assembly in the block-construction phase.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **biaxial stiffened panel** as the *skeleton-and-skin* of a freight shipping crate:
- The **plywood sheet** is the plate.
- The **wooden battens** glued across the sheet in two directions form the stiffener grillage.
- Pre-assembling the entire reinforced sheet on a workshop bench — *before* nailing it onto the larger crate — is exactly the same logic shipbuilders use: **build it small, build it right, then lift it into place**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pre-Assemble?**
> - **Quality control** in a controlled workshop environment (better lighting, stable jigs, easier NDT access).
> - **Productivity boost** through parallel workflow (panels built while other blocks are being erected).
> - **Distortion control** since welding flat panels is far easier to manage than welding in vertical ship sections.
> - **Safety** — most welding is done at ground level, not at height.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Material Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Plate yield stress (σ_y) | **235 MPa** (Grade A) to **390 MPa** (Grade AH36) | Mild steel dominates merchant ships |
| Young's Modulus (E) | **205,000 MPa** (2.05 × 10⁵ N/mm²) | For shipbuilding steel |
| Poisson's ratio (ν) | **0.30** | Isotropic steel assumption |
| Typical plate thickness (t) | **6 mm – 25 mm** | Hull / deck / bulkhead plating |
| Stiffener spacing (b) | **500 mm – 900 mm** | Depends on design pressure |
| Stiffener types | T-bar, bulb plate, flat bar, L-angle | DNV / LR / ABS rule sets |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic of Biaxial Stiffening — Why Two Directions?

A uniaxially stiffened plate (stiffeners in **one** direction only) buckles in long, sweeping waves between stiffeners. By adding stiffeners in the **transverse direction**, the panel is divided into smaller sub-panels, each of which has a **higher critical buckling load**.

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step:

1. **Longitudinal stiffeners (primary)** carry the dominant in-plane compressive load (e.g., hull girder bending stress) and prevent *column-like* buckling of the plate.
2. **Transverse stiffeners (secondary)** subdivide the longitudinal span, reducing the unsupported length of both the plate and the longitudinal stiffener.
3. **The plate itself** acts as a shear membrane, transferring load between stiffeners (a concept formalized by the **orthotropic plate theory** of Timoshenko and von Kármán).
4. **Cross joints (stiffener intersections)** are typically designed with *scallops* or *collars* to reduce fatigue hot-spots while maintaining shear transfer.
5. **Pre-assembly sequencing** ensures the welding heat input is distributed symmetrically to minimize angular and bowing distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Biaxial stiffening is the *backbone* of naval architecture. Without it, ship hulls would buckle at 5–10% of their designed load. With it, steel usage drops by **30–40%** for the same stiffness, dramatically reducing displacement and improving fuel efficiency.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{cr}^{p} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{c}\,\pi^{2}\,E}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ | **Ideal plate buckling stress** (compressive) | Plate between stiffeners |
| 2 | $\sigma_{cr}^{s} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,E}{\lambda^{2}}$ | **Euler column buckling** of stiffener | Slender stiffener web check |
| 3 | $\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \sigma_{y}\left[1 - \dfrac{\sigma_{y}\,\lambda^{2}}{4\pi^{2}\,E}\right]$ | **Johnson–Ostenfeld correction** | Intermediate stiffener column |
| 4 | $\lambda_{0} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\pi^{2}\,E}{\sigma_{y}}}$ | **Slenderness limit** between Euler and Johnson | λ₀ ≈ 100 for mild steel |
| 5 | $\dfrac{b_{eff}}{b} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_{a}}}$ | **Effective width** (Faulkner / von Kármán) | Post-buckling reserve |
| 6 | $k_{c} \;=\; 4.0$ (for long simply-supported plates, m=1) | **Buckling coefficient** | Uniaxial compression, $a/b \to \infty$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{vM} \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma_{x}^{2} + \sigma_{y}^{2} - \sigma_{x}\sigma_{y} + 3\tau_{xy}^{2}}$ | **Von Mises yield criterion** | Biaxial stress check |
| 8 | $\delta_{weld} \;=\; \dfrac{0.2 \cdot A_{w}}{t^{2}}$ | **Angular distortion estimate** (DW formula) | Distortion control in pre-assembly |

> Where:
> - $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $t$ = plate thickness, $b$ = smaller panel dimension.
> - $a$ = larger panel dimension, $m$ = number of buckling half-waves.
> - $\sigma_{y}$ = yield stress, $\lambda$ = slenderness ratio ($L/r$).
> - $\sigma_{a}$ = applied stress, $A_{w}$ = weld throat area, $\delta_{weld}$ = angular distortion.

## 2.3 Pre-Assembly Workflow — Conceptual Stages

1. **Lofting & Nesting:** Plate profiles and stiffener cut lengths are generated from 3D ship model and nested to minimize waste.
2. **Plate Preparation:** Edge cleaning, marking of stiffener lines, pre-cambering (if required).
3. **Stiffener Fitting:** T-bars/bulb plates are placed in jigs, clamped, and tack-welded.
4. **Welding:** Typically **CO₂ / MAG** (GMAW) or **SAW** for thick sections, using **back-step** or **skip** sequences to control distortion.
5. **Distortion Control:** Clamps, strongbacks, water-cooling, and symmetric weld sequencing.
6. **NDT & Inspection:** Visual, dimensional, **UT** (ultrasonic), **MPI** (magnetic particle), or **DPI** (dye penetrant) per classification society rules.
7. **Marking & Handling:** Panel is marked with block number, orientation, and lifting points for transport to the erection shop.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Critical Buckling Stress of a Simply-Supported Plate (Uniaxial Compression)

We derive the classical **Bryan-Rihm plate-buckling formula** for the panel between stiffeners in a biaxial stiffened plate.

**Step 1: Governing Differential Equation**

For a thin elastic plate under in-plane compressive load $N_x$ per unit length, the governing equation (in terms of lateral deflection $w$) is:

$$D \nabla^{4} w \;+\; N_{x}\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 2: Plate Flexural Rigidity**

$$D \;=\; \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})}$$

**Explanation:** $D$ captures the plate's bending stiffness per unit width. The factor $(1-\nu^{2})$ corrects for the Poisson restraint that stiffens the plate laterally.

**Step 3: Trial Double-Sine Solution**

For a simply-supported rectangular plate of dimensions $a \times b$, the lateral deflection is assumed as:

$$w(x,y) \;=\; \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_{mn}\,\sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

**Explanation:** The sine function satisfies the simply-supported boundary conditions ($w = 0$ and $M = 0$ at edges).

**Step 4: Substituting the Trial Function**

Substituting into Step 1 and applying the Galerkin / Rayleigh-Ritz orthogonality conditions, one obtains the critical load for the $(m,n)$-th mode:

$$N_{x,cr}^{(m,n)} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,D}{b^{2}}\!\left(m\,\dfrac{b}{a} + n^{2}\,\dfrac{a}{m\,b}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 5: Minimum Critical Load**

Minimizing with respect to $m$ (number of half-waves in the load direction) by setting $\partial N_{x,cr}/\partial m = 0$ gives:

$$m \;=\; a\,\sqrt{\dfrac{n^{2}}{a^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{?}} \;\;\;\text{(optimum integer $m$)}$$

For minimum (i.e., $n=1$ in the y-direction and optimum $m$ in the x-direction), the **buckling coefficient** becomes:

$$k_{c} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{m\,b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{m\,b}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 6: Critical Buckling Stress**

Dividing the critical load $N_{x,cr}$ by the plate thickness $t$:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{cr}^{p} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{c}\,\pi^{2}\,E}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})}\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}\;}$$

**Step 7: Special Case — Long Plate ($a/b \to \infty$)**

For a long panel (typical in ship hulls), $m$ becomes large and $a/(mb) \to 0$, hence:

$$k_{c} \;\to\; 4.0$$

This is the standard result used in classification society rules for **plate panel** between stiffeners.

**Step 8: Worked Numerical Example**

> **Problem:** A ship hull plate has $t = 12$ mm, $b = 600$ mm, $a = 3000$ mm, $E = 205{,}000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa. Find the plate buckling stress.

**Solution:**

$$\dfrac{a}{b} \;=\; \dfrac{3000}{600} \;=\; 5.0$$

The optimum number of half-waves: $m \approx a/b = 5$ (since $a/(mb) \to 1/\sqrt{k_c}$ minimization).

$$k_{c} \;=\; \left(5 \cdot \dfrac{600}{3000} + \dfrac{3000}{5\cdot 600}\right)^{2} \;=\; (1.0 + 1.0)^{2} \;=\; 4.0$$

$$\sigma_{cr}^{p} \;=\; \dfrac{4.0 \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot 205{,}000}{12\,(1 - 0.30^{2})}\left(\dfrac{12}{600}\right)^{2}$$

Numerator: $4.0 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 205{,}000 = 8{,}092{,}072$ MPa.

Denominator: $12 \cdot (1 - 0.09) = 12 \cdot 0.91 = 10.92$.

$$\sigma_{cr}^{p} \;=\; \dfrac{8{,}092{,}072}{10.92} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{50}\right)^{2} \;=\; 740{,}940 \cdot 0.0004 \;=\; 296.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** $\sigma_{cr}^{p} \approx 296.4$ MPa, which exceeds the yield stress of 235 MPa. This means the plate **will yield before it buckles** — a stocky panel suitable for hull bottom plating. The actual design stress would be governed by the **yield criterion** (or the classification society's allowable stress).

## 3.2 Stiffener Column Buckling — Euler vs. Johnson–Ostenfeld

**Step 1: Slenderness Ratio**

$$\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{L_{e}}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{L_{e}}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$

where $L_e$ is the effective length (depending on end conditions), $I$ is the moment of inertia of the stiffener plus effective plate flange, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area.

**Step 2: Limiting Slenderness**

$$\lambda_{0} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi^{2}E}{\sigma_{y}}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 205{,}000}{235}} \;\approx\; 131.4$$

For $\lambda \leq 0.2\,\lambda_0$ (very stocky), the stiffener yields directly.

**Step 3: Euler Region ($\lambda \geq \lambda_0$)**

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}E}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Johnson–Ostenfeld (Intermediate, $\lambda < \lambda_0$)**

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \sigma_{y}\left[1 - \dfrac{\sigma_{y}\,\lambda^{2}}{4\pi^{2}E}\right]$$

**Explanation:** This is a parabolic transition curve that smoothly joins the Euler curve at $\lambda = \lambda_0$ and the yield plateau at $\lambda = 0$. It accounts for plastic deformation in intermediate columns, which is the *practical* case for most ship stiffeners.

## 3.3 Effective Plate Width (Post-Buckling Reserve)

When a plate panel **does** buckle (slender case, $\sigma_a > \sigma_{cr}$), it does **not** collapse immediately. Stress redistributes to the edges. The **effective width** concept of von Kármán simplifies this:

$$\dfrac{b_{eff}}{b} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_{a}}} \quad \text{(Faulkner, for } \sigma_a/\sigma_{cr} > 0.5\text{)}$$

The effective cross-section (stiffener + effective plate flange) is then used to compute the column buckling of the stiffener. This is the basis of most **DNV / LR / ABS** rules for stiffened panels.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Biaxial Stiffened Panel — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Parent Plate Element"] --> B["Plate Panel Subunit 1"]
    A --> C["Plate Panel Subunit 2"]
    A --> D["Plate Panel Subunit 3"]
    A --> E["Plate Panel Subunit 4"]
    F["Longitudinal Stiffener T Bar"] --> B
    F --> C
    F --> D
    F --> E
    G["Transverse Stiffener T Bar"] --> B
    G --> C
    G --> D
    G --> E
    B --> H["Load Transfer Node"]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    H --> I["Grillage Boundary Support"]
```

> **Reading guide:** The four plate subunits are created by the intersection of one longitudinal stiffener (`F`) and one transverse stiffener (`G`). Each subunit is a simply-supported plate panel that transfers in-plane loads back to the stiffeners, which then carry the cumulative load to the grillage boundary.

## 4.2 Pre-Assembly Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Lofting and Nesting"] --> S2["Plate Cutting and Edge Prep"]
    S2 --> S3["Plate Laying on Jig"]
    S3 --> S4["Fitting Longitudinal Stiffeners"]
    S4 --> S5["Fitting Transverse Stiffeners"]
    S5 --> S6["Tack Welding"]
    S6 --> S7["Full Welding Sequence Skip or Back Step"]
    S7 --> S8["Distortion Monitoring"]
    S8 --> S9{"NDT Pass"}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10["Marking Lifting Points"]
    S9 -- No --> S11["Repair Reweld"]
    S11 --> S8
    S10 --> S12["Transport to Block Erection"]
```

> **Reading guide:** This block-level flow tracks the workshop fabrication. The decision diamond at S9 enforces quality control — only NDT-passed panels are released to the next stage. The **skip / back-step welding sequence** at S7 is critical for distortion control.

## 4.3 Pre-Assembly Workshop — Functional Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph YARD["Shipyard Layout"]
        direction TB
        Z1["Lofting Bay"]
        Z2["Steel Stockyard"]
        Z3["Panel Pre Assembly Shop"]
        Z4["NDT and Inspection Bay"]
        Z5["Block Assembly Area"]
        Z6["Dry Dock or Slipway"]
    end
    Z1 --> Z3
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z3 --> Z4
    Z4 --> Z5
    Z5 --> Z6
```

> **Reading guide:** The arrow sequence is the **logistic flow** of a biaxial stiffened panel from raw steel to ship launch site. The *pre-assembly shop* (Z3) is where the biaxial stiffened panel is fully built before being transferred to block assembly (Z5).

## 4.4 Schematic — Biaxial Stiffened Panel Cross-Section (ASCII Representation)

```
  ________________________________________________________
  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |   <-- Plate (t = 12 mm)
  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
  ========================================================   <-- Transverse stiffener
  ___________|___________________|_____________________|___
  |   ___    |     ___          |      ___             |
  |  |   |   |    |   |         |     |   |            |   <-- Longitudinal stiffeners
  |  | T |   |    | T |         |     | T |            |     (T-bars or bulb plates)
  |  |___|   |    |___|         |     |___|            |
  ========================================================
  b1          b2                 b3         b4
  <---------  a  ---------------->
  (b = b1 = b2 = b3 = b4 = panel width between stiffeners)
```

> **Reading guide:** This is a top-view schematic. The horizontal lines are **transverse stiffeners**; the vertical T-shaped bars are **longitudinal stiffeners**. The small spacing $b$ between two adjacent longitudinal stiffeners defines the **plate panel** for which the buckling stress is computed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define a biaxial stiffened panel and state two advantages of stiffening a plate in two perpendicular directions.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A biaxial stiffened panel is a structural plate element reinforced by stiffeners arranged in two perpendicular directions — typically longitudinal (primary) and transverse (secondary), forming a grillage.
>
> **Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
> 1. The plate is subdivided into smaller sub-panels, each with a **higher critical buckling stress** (since $\sigma_{cr} \propto (t/b)^2$ and $b$ is reduced).
> 2. The unsupported length of longitudinal stiffeners is reduced by transverse stiffeners, **increasing their column buckling resistance**.
> 3. Stress distribution becomes more uniform, reducing peak stress and improving fatigue life.

---

### Question 2
**Q: List any three quality-control checks performed during the pre-assembly of a biaxial stiffened panel.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Dimensional inspection:** Plate flatness, stiffener spacing, and squareness using templates, laser scanners, or total stations.
> 2. **Weld inspection:** Visual (VT), ultrasonic (UT), magnetic particle (MPI), or dye penetrant (DPI) testing of all fillet welds between stiffeners and plate.
> 3. **Distortion check:** Measurement of angular and bowing distortion using the formula $\delta = 0.2\,A_w/t^2$ as a guideline, and corrective heat-straightening if exceeded.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option I
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **pre-assembly process** of a biaxial stiffened panel in a modern shipyard. List the major operations in sequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biaxially stiffened hull panel has the following data:
- Plate: $a = 2400$ mm, $b = 600$ mm, $t = 10$ mm
- Material: Mild steel, $E = 205{,}000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa
- Loading: Uniaxial compression along the longer edge

**Calculate:**
- (i) The **buckling coefficient** $k_c$ assuming $a/b = 4$.
- (ii) The **critical plate buckling stress** $\sigma_{cr}^{p}$.
- (iii) The **slenderness ratio** of the panel and comment on whether the plate yields or buckles first. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Pre-Assembly Process (7 marks):**

The pre-assembly of a biaxial stiffened panel involves the following steps:

1. **Lofting and Nesting (1 mark):** Plate and stiffener shapes are generated from the 3D ship model. Plates are nested on the steel stock sheet to minimize wastage (typical nesting efficiency: 85–90%).

2. **Cutting and Edge Preparation (0.5 mark):** Plates are CNC-cut, edges are bevelled for butt welds, and stiffener cut-lengths are produced (typically with allowances for shrinkage ≈ 1 mm per joint).

3. **Plate Laying on Jig (0.5 mark):** The plate is positioned on a flat jig with reference lines marked. Strongbacks may be tack-welded to control camber.

4. **Fitting of Stiffeners (1.5 marks):**
   - **Longitudinal stiffeners** are placed first along pre-marked lines, checked for straightness, and temporarily clamped.
   - **Transverse stiffeners** are then placed orthogonally, with their position relative to longitudinal stiffeners verified. Cross joints are fitted with scallops or collars (typically $r \geq 25$ mm or per class rules) to reduce fatigue hot-spots.

5. **Tack Welding and Full Welding (1 mark):** Stiffeners are tack-welded (typical tack: 50 mm long, 4 mm leg, 200–300 mm pitch), then fully welded using **skip welding** or **back-step welding** sequence to minimize angular distortion. Weld leg size is typically $a_w = 0.7 t_{\min}$.

6. **Distortion Control and NDT (1 mark):** Distortion is measured against allowable limits (typical: 0.5°–1° angular, 1% span bowing). NDT (UT, MPI) is conducted on critical welds. Repair and heat-straightening are performed if defects are found.

7. **Marking and Handling (0.5 mark):** Panel is marked with block number, orientation arrow, lifting points, and centre-of-gravity. Crane lift plan is prepared before moving to block assembly.

> *(Sketch: refer to Section 4.4 schematic.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $a = 2400$ mm, $b = 600$ mm, $t = 10$ mm, $E = 205{,}000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa, uniaxial compression along the 2400 mm edge.

**(i) Buckling Coefficient (2 marks):**

For a simply-supported plate, the optimum $m$ is the integer nearest to $a/b$:

$$m \;\approx\; \dfrac{a}{b} \;=\; 4$$

$$k_{c} \;=\; \left(m \cdot \dfrac{b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{m \cdot b}\right)^{2} \;=\; \left(4 \cdot \dfrac{600}{2400} + \dfrac{2400}{4 \cdot 600}\right)^{2} \;=\; (1.0 + 1.0)^{2} \;=\; 4.0$$

$$\boxed{k_{c} \;=\; 4.0}$$

---

**(ii) Critical Plate Buckling Stress (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_{cr}^{p} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{c}\,\pi^{2}\,E}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})}\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{t}{b} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{600} \;=\; 0.01667$$

$$\sigma_{cr}^{p} \;=\; \dfrac{4.0 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 205{,}000}{12 \cdot (1 - 0.09)} \cdot (0.01667)^{2}$$

$$= \dfrac{8{,}092{,}072}{10.92} \cdot 2.778 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 740{,}940 \cdot 2.778 \times 10^{-4} \;\approx\; 205.8 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr}^{p} \;\approx\; 205.8 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

**(iii) Yield vs. Buckle Comparison (2 marks):**

Compare $\sigma_{cr}^{p}$ with $\sigma_y$:

$$\sigma_{cr}^{p} \;=\; 205.8 \text{ MPa} \;<\; \sigma_y \;=\; 235 \text{ MPa}$$

**Conclusion:** Since the critical buckling stress is **less than** the yield stress, the plate **will buckle before it yields**. The panel is **slender** and must be classified as a *buckling-governed* panel. Designers must either reduce the stiffener spacing $b$, increase the plate thickness $t$, or use a higher-grade steel (e.g., AH36 with $\sigma_y = 355$ MPa) to push the design into the yield-governed regime.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option II
**[KTU University Exam - December 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **structural advantages** of biaxial stiffening over uniaxial stiffening in ship hull panels. State, with reasoning, when biaxial stiffening is **mandatory**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During the pre-assembly of a hull side panel, the welder observed **angular distortion** of 4° at a fillet weld. The weld leg is 7 mm and the plate thickness is 12 mm. Using the empirical formula $\delta = 0.2\,A_w/t^2$, **estimate the corrective action** required. Also discuss the **welding sequence** you would recommend to minimize this distortion in future pre-assemblies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Structural Advantages of Biaxial Stiffening (7 marks):**

1. **Higher Buckling Load (1.5 marks):** Adding transverse stiffeners divides the plate into smaller sub-panels. Since $\sigma_{cr} \propto (t/b)^2$, halving $b$ quadruples the critical buckling stress. This allows **thinner plates** for the same load, reducing ship displacement.

2. **Reduced Slenderness of Longitudinal Stiffeners (1.5 marks):** The unsupported length $L$ of a longitudinal stiffener equals the transverse stiffener spacing. Adding transverse stiffeners **reduces $L$**, hence reduces slenderness $\lambda = L/r$, increasing Euler or Johnson–Ostenfeld capacity.

3. **Improved Stress Distribution (1 mark):** Biaxial stiffening redistributes in-plane and out-of-plane loads more uniformly, lowering peak stresses and reducing fatigue hot-spots (especially at stiffener intersections, if properly detailed with scallops).

4. **Better Damage Tolerance (1 mark):** A biaxially stiffened panel can still carry significant load even if one stiffener is damaged (e.g., by collision or corrosion), due to load-redistribution paths in two directions. Uniaxial panels have only one load path.

5. **Mandatoriness — When Biaxial is Required (2 marks):**
   - **Hull girder bending regions** (deck, bottom, sheer strake) where bidirectional stresses occur (longitudinal bending + transverse hydrostatic/water pressure).
   - **Panels subjected to lateral pressure** (tank bulkheads, outer hull, deck plates under sea loads) where $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ are both significant.
   - **High-speed vessels** (warships, container ships) where slamming and whipping stresses are biaxial.
   - When the **plate slenderness** $b/t$ exceeds classification society limits (e.g., LR's $b/t \leq 100\sqrt{235/\sigma_y}$ for Grade A steel) without transverse stiffening, buckling is unacceptable, and biaxial stiffening becomes **mandatory**.

---

**(b) Distortion Estimation and Welding Sequence (7 marks):**

**Distortion Check (3 marks):**

Given: $a_w = 7$ mm (weld leg), $t = 12$ mm.

Effective throat: $A_w = 0.707 \cdot a_w^2 = 0.707 \cdot 49 = 34.6$ mm² (per unit length).

$$\delta_{weld} \;=\; \dfrac{0.2 \cdot A_{w}}{t^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.2 \cdot 34.6}{144} \;=\; 0.048 \text{ rad} \;\approx\; 2.76°$$

The **calculated** angular distortion is about 2.76°. The **observed** distortion of 4° exceeds the typical tolerance of 0.5°–1.5° for ship hull panels, and corrective action is required.

**Corrective Actions (2 marks):**
1. **Mechanical straightening** using hydraulic jacks or rolling presses (for small bow distortions).
2. **Heat straightening** (oxy-acetylene flame) using controlled line heating on the convex side — the most common shipyard practice for large panels.
3. **Re-welding with symmetric sequence** if rework is permitted (rare, expensive).

**Recommended Welding Sequence to Minimize Distortion (2 marks):**
- **Skip welding:** Weld every second or third stiffener first, then return to fill the gaps. This distributes heat input in time and space.
- **Back-step welding:** Each pass is laid in the **opposite direction** to the overall weld progression, reducing accumulated shrinkage.
- **Symmetric welding:** Weld from the **center outward** in both directions along the panel.
- **Balanced welding around the panel neutral axis:** Avoid clustering welds on one side.
- **Use of strongbacks and clamps** during welding to mechanically restrain shrinkage.
- **Multi-electrode / tandem welding** for thick plates to deposit faster with less total heat input.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing plate buckling stress $\sigma_{cr}^{p}$ with column buckling of stiffener $\sigma_{cr}^{s}$** — these are *different formulas* with different assumptions. Plate buckling is governed by the smaller panel dimension $b$; stiffener column buckling is governed by the unsupported length $L$ of the stiffener.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1-\nu^2$ factor in the plate rigidity** — this costs a full mark in derivations. Many students write $D = Et^3/12$ and lose the correction.
> 3. **Not mentioning the welding sequence** in pre-assembly answers — examiners expect at least two of: skip, back-step, symmetric, or balanced welding.
> 4. **Using `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table syntax. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 5. **Sketch quality:** Always include a labeled sketch for 7-mark descriptive questions. A poorly labeled or missing sketch can cost 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Units:** Always state units explicitly (MPa, mm, etc.). KTU evaluators deduct marks for missing units.
> 7. **Effective width concept** is often forgotten in stiffener design — but it's essential for slender (post-buckled) panels, and is the basis of classification society rules.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Biaxial stiffened panel** = plate + stiffeners in **two perpendicular directions** (longitudinal + transverse).
- **Pre-assembly** = workshop fabrication of the panel *before* block erection → improves quality, productivity, and safety.
- **Critical plate buckling stress** (Bryan-Rihm): $\sigma_{cr}^{p} = \dfrac{k_c \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$, with $k_c = 4$ for long simply-supported plates.
- **Plate flexural rigidity:** $D = \dfrac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ — **never forget the $(1-\nu^2)$ factor**.
- **Euler column buckling** of stiffener: $\sigma_{cr}^{s} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$ for $\lambda \geq \lambda_0$.
- **Johnson–Ostenfeld** correction (intermediate $\lambda$): $\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y \left[1 - \dfrac{\sigma_y \lambda^2}{4\pi^2 E}\right]$.
- **Effective width** (post-buckling): $b_{eff}/b = \sqrt{\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_a}$ for $\sigma_a > 0.5\,\sigma_{cr}$.
- **Slenderness limit:** $\lambda_0 \approx 131$ for mild steel ($\sigma_y = 235$ MPa).
- **Pre-assembly steps:** Lofting → cutting → jig laying → stiffener fitting (long first, then transverse) → tack welding → full welding (skip / back-step / symmetric) → distortion control → NDT → marking → transport.
- **Quality control checks:** dimensional, weld (UT/MPI), distortion ($\delta \leq 1°$), paint, marking.
- **Why biaxial is mandatory:** biaxial stress states (hull girder + lateral pressure), high slenderness, damage tolerance, classification society rules.
- **Materials:** Mild steel (Grade A: 235 MPa) to high-tensile (AH36: 355 MPa, DH36: 355 MPa, EH36: 355 MPa). $E = 205{,}000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$.
- **Stiffener types:** T-bar, bulb plate, flat bar, L-angle — choice depends on design rule and fabrication economics.
- **Welding standards:** Class rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) dictate leg size, weld sequence, NDT, and tolerance.
- **Key design equation to memorize (highest weightage in KTU):** $\sigma_{cr}^{p} = \dfrac{k_c \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$.
- **Common mistakes to avoid:** confusing $a$ and $b$, missing Poisson factor, ignoring effective width, missing units, omitting sketch.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly of Various Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Assembly of Various Components # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of shipbuilding, **Assembly** refers to the systematic joining, aligning, and fastening of individual fabricated structural components (plates, sections, stiffeners, frames, bulkheads, and outfit units) into larger, integrated sub-assemblies, pre-fabricated blocks, and ultimately the complete ship hull. Under the KTU 2024 scheme for *Basics of Ship Construction*, Module 3 specifically deals with the **erection philosophy, joint preparation, dimensional control, and sequential joining methodology** of major ship components using welding (primarily), mechanical fastening, and modular integration techniques. The hierarchy of ship assembly can be classified into three structural tiers: | Tier | Name | Description | |------|------|-------------| | **Tier 1** | Sub-assembly (Piece) | Smallest fabricated unit (e.g., a single stiffened panel, a transverse ring) | | **Tier 2** | Block / Unit / Grand Block | Multiple sub-assemblies welded together (e.g., double bottom block, engine room block) | | **Tier 3** | Ship (Erected Hull) | All blocks erected and welded on the building berth/slipway/dock | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically emphasizes the **sequential logic** of assembly — *why* a bottom shell is erected *before* a side shell, *why* a deck is fitted *after* bulkheads, and *why* outfitting is integrated with structural assembly. Memorizing the sequence is a guaranteed 7–8 mark question in Part B. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of ship assembly as **building a giant 3D jigsaw puzzle made of steel** — but unlike a flat puzzle, this one is built in three simultaneous directions: - 🧱 **Imagine constructing a 20-story building from prefabricated bathroom pods.** Each pod (bathroom) is built completely in the factory with tiles, plumbing, and wiring. They are then craned into the building's skeleton. Shipbuilding does **exactly the same** with **grand blocks** — engine rooms, accommodation units, and cargo holds are built fully outfitted (piping, cables, machinery) at ground level, then lifted into the ship's hull structure. - 🔧 **Alternatively, think of it as LEGO construction at industrial scale.** A modern shipyard does NOT build the ship from scratch on the slipway. Instead, the slipway is reserved only for the **final 30%** of joining work. The **first 70%** happens in workshops where workers lie flat on platforms (panels) and weld comfortably — far more efficient than welding overhead on a berth. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuitive Takeaway:** Ship assembly is a **gravity-defying, multi-directional convergence process** where flat steel plates are progressively transformed into 3D volume blocks via the **sub-assembly → block → grand block → ship** pyramid. Every component has a designated **erection window** in the build strategy. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Assembly The following engineering metrics govern assembly quality in modern shipbuilding: - **Dimensional Tolerance:** Typically **±3 mm to ±10 mm** for block dimensions, governed by classification society rules (e.g., **IACS UR Z17** for hull tolerances). - **Weld Shrinkage Allowance:** Approximately **1 mm per 1000 mm** of welded length for butt welds in mild steel. - **Block Lifting Weight:** Modern gantry cranes (Goliath-type) typically handle **300 tonnes to 1,200 tonnes** per block. - **Block Size (Modern):** Up to **40 m × 30 m × 8 m** in mega-block construction. - **Docking Tolerance (Keel Line):** **±5 mm** over the full length of the ship (e.g., 300 m ship → ±5 mm tolerance). > [!IMPORTANT] > These metrics are **directly testable** in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions as quick numerical or definition questions. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Stage Assembly Philosophy Modern ship construction universally adopts a **three-stage hierarchical assembly** that maximizes workshop productivity, minimizes work-at-height, and parallelizes production. ### Stage 1: Sub-Assembly (Panel Fabrication) - **Definition:** The joining of individual plates with attached stiffeners, frames, or brackets to form stiffened panels. - **Operation:** Plates are first shot-blasted and primed. Stiffeners (T-sections, L-sections, bulb plates) are fitted using ** tack welds** and then fully welded — typically by **submerged arc welding (SAW)** or **flux-cored arc welding (FCAW)**. - **Typical sub-assemblies:** - Shell panels (bottom, side, sheer strake) - Deck panels - Bulkhead panels - Transverse ring webs (floors + frames welded into a complete transverse ring) - **Working position:** Flat or vertical-position welding (ergonomically efficient). > [!NOTE] > **Why this stage exists:** Welding in the **flat (1G/1F) position** is **3× to 5× faster** than overhead (4G/4F) welding, and produces vastly superior radiographic quality. ### Stage 2: Block Assembly (Unit Construction) - **Definition:** Multiple sub-assemblies are joined to form a three-dimensional **block** (also called a "section," "module," or "unit"). - **Block Types:** - **Double Bottom Block** (bottom shell + inner bottom + floors + girders) - **Wing Tank Block** (side shell + transverse bulkheads) - **Deck Block** (deck plate + deck girders + pillars) - **Engine Room Block** (double bottom + side shell + platforms + machinery seats) - **Superstructure Block** (complete accommodation decks built as a single unit) - **Critical Operation:** **Dimensional control** using **theodolites, laser total stations, and laser alignment systems** to maintain block squareness, verticality, and centerline accuracy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Pattern:** A 7-mark question is frequently asked on the **distinction between unit construction (block building) and conventional (berth-building) construction**, often with a comparative table. Memorize the pros and cons. ### Stage 3: Grand Block / Hull Erection - **Definition:** Final joining of large blocks (often 200–1,200 tonnes) on the building berth, slipway, or in dry dock to form the complete hull. - **Joining Methods at Grand Block Stage:** - **Butt welding** of shell and deck plating between blocks - **Mechanical alignment** using hydraulic jacks, strong-backs, and aligners - **Surveyor's check** of fairness, butt gap (typically **2–4 mm**), and root face - **Outfitting Integration:** Pre-outfitted blocks reduce final-stage work by up to **70%**. ## 2.2 Assembly of Major Structural Components ### 2.2.1 Assembly of the Keel Block (Foundation Member) - The **keel** is the first structural member laid on the building berth. - **Sequence:** 1. Centerline marked on the berth with a **theodolite**. 2. Keel plates (or center girder for double bottom) positioned and aligned. 3. Bottom shell panels erected symmetrically on **both sides** of the centerline to maintain balance. 4. Bottom transverses (floors) fitted between the center girder and side girders. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world metric:** For a typical 200 m bulk carrier, the keel-laying to launch period averages **6 to 9 months** in modern Asian shipyards. ### 2.2.2 Assembly of Side Shell Frames and Transverse Rings - **Method A (Conventional):** Frames are erected one-by-one on the berth, with shell plating added progressively. - **Method B (Modern Ring Building):** A complete transverse ring (floor + side frames + deck beam + brackets) is pre-assembled flat in the workshop, lifted as a unit, and welded into position. This is the **upend erection** method. - **Tolerance:** Frame spacing tolerance = **±2 mm** per frame station; cumulative = **±10 mm** over a 30 m ring. ### 2.2.3 Assembly of Decks and Deckhouses - **Deck assembly** must occur **after** the lower hull structure is sufficiently welded to maintain dimensional stability. - **Sequence:** 1. Deck plating sub-assembled into large panels. 2. Deck girders (longitudinal and transverse) attached. 3. Pillars / stanchions installed to transmit deck loads to the bottom. 4. Deck panels hoisted onto erected side shell and welded in position. - **Superstructure:** Built as **separate multi-deck blocks**, then lifted by **gantry cranes** onto the deck. Modern cruise ship superstructures can weigh **>5,000 tonnes** per block. ### 2.2.4 Assembly of Bulkheads - **Types:** - **Watertight (WT) bulkheads** — full penetration welds, tested by hose test or air test. - **Non-watertight / dust-tight** — fillet welds. - **Method:** Bulkhead plates stiffened with **corrugation, gussets, or flat bars**, then erected as complete panels between already-fitted deck and bottom. - **Critical:** Corrugated bulkheads are pre-formed by **cold forming** or **hot forming** (for thick plates > 20 mm). ### 2.2.5 Assembly of Stem, Sternpost, and Rudder - **Stem (Fore):** Either **plate stem** (welded fabrication) or **cast steel stem** for large ships. Assembled early — before bow shell panels. - **Sternpost (Aft):** Carries the **propeller shaft bracket** (or "A-frame"). Cast or fabricated. Mounted before stern shell plating. - **Rudder:** Hinged on the rudder stock via **pintles and gudgeons**. Assembled **post-launch** in most cases (requires underwater alignment). ## 2.3 Joining Techniques Used in Assembly | Joining Method | Application | Advantages | Limitations | |----------------|-------------|------------|-------------| | **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** | Field welding, all positions | Portable, versatile | Slower, more spatter | | **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** | Panel fabrication (flat) | High speed, deep penetration | Flat position only | | **Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)** | Heavy plate, all positions | High deposition rate | Generates fumes | | **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG)** | Thin plates, aluminum | Clean, controllable | Sensitive to wind | | **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Aluminum superstructures | No melting, low distortion | Capital intensive | | **Riveting (legacy)** | Older ships | Proven | Superseded by welding | | **Bolting (mechanical fastening)** | Outfit items, some joints | Removable | Slow, weight penalty | > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Note:** Welding is **not just a joining method** — it introduces **residual stresses, distortion, and shrinkage**. Modern assembly planning always specifies a **welding sequence** (often **back-step or skip-weld sequence**) to control distortion. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Engineering Meaning | |---|---------------------|------------|------|---------------------| | 1 | **Transverse Weld Shrinkage** | $\Delta_s = 0.1 \times t$ | mm | $t$ = plate thickness in mm; approx. 0.1 mm shrink per mm of thickness | | 2 | **Longitudinal Weld Shrinkage** | $\Delta_L = \dfrac{\Delta_s \times A_w}{t \times L}$ | mm | $A_w$ = weld cross-section area; $L$ = plate length | | 3 | **Block Lift Weight (estimate)** | $W = \rho_s \times V \times g$ | tonnes | $\rho_s$ = steel density = **7.85 t/m³**; $V$ = block volume | | 4 | **Welding Heat Input** | $E = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{s \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $V$ = arc voltage (V); $I$ = current (A); $s$ = travel speed (mm/min) | | 5 | **Cumulative Tolerance** | $T_c = \sqrt{\sum t_i^2}$ | mm | Root-sum-square tolerance accumulation | | 6 | **Block Center of Gravity (lift point)** | $x_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i}$ | m | Used for sling positioning | | 7 | **Plate Flatness Deviation (allowable)** | $\delta \le 0.01 \times L$ | mm | $L$ = plate length in mm | | 8 | **Keel Sag Allowance** | $S = \dfrac{5 \times w \times L^4}{384 \times E \times I}$ | mm | $w$ = distributed load; $L$ = unsupported length; $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm² | > [!TIP] > **Avoid the `|` character** in your answer sheets when writing absolute values — use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX (this also prevents broken tables in markdown). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Estimating Block Lift Weight and Center of Gravity This is a **frequently tested problem** in KTU Part B (often 7 marks under "numerical from assembly planning"). ### Problem Statement A shipyard plans to lift a double-bottom block with the following composition: - **Bottom plate:** 12 m × 14 m × **0.018 m** (thickness) - **Inner bottom plate:** 12 m × 14 m × **0.014 m** - **8 longitudinal girders:** 14 m × 0.5 m × **0.012 m** (web) - **12 transverse floors:** 12 m × 0.6 m × **0.012 m** (web) - **Outfit mass (pipes, valves):** **3.5 tonnes** (assumed at center) Steel density: $\rho_s = 7.85$ **t/m³**. Acceleration during lift: $a = 0.2g$ (dynamic factor for crane operation). **Find:** 1. Total block weight 2. Sling force per pick (4-pick lift, symmetric) 3. Total dynamic load on crane hook ### Step 1: Volume of Each Component **Bottom plate volume:** $$ V_{bp} = L \times B \times t_{bp} = 12 \times 14 \times 0.018 $$ $$ V_{bp} = 168 \times 0.018 = 3.024 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Inner bottom plate volume:** $$ V_{ibp} = 12 \times 14 \times 0.014 = 168 \times 0.014 = 2.352 \text{ m}^3 $$ **8 longitudinal girders total volume:** $$ V_{lg} = 8 \times (14 \times 0.5 \times 0.012) = 8 \times 0.084 = 0.672 \text{ m}^3 $$ **12 transverse floors total volume:** $$ V_{tf} = 12 \times (12 \times 0.6 \times 0.012) = 12 \times 0.0864 = 1.0368 \text{ m}^3 $$ ### Step 2: Total Steel Volume and Mass $$ V_{total} = 3.024 + 2.352 + 0.672 + 1.0368 = 7.0848 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ M_{steel} = \rho_s \times V_{total} = 7.85 \times 7.0848 $$ $$ M_{steel} = 55.6157 \text{ tonnes} $$ ### Step 3: Total Block Mass (Steel + Outfit) $$ M_{block} = M_{steel} + M_{outfit} = 55.6157 + 3.5 $$ $$ M_{block} = 59.1157 \text{ tonnes} \approx 59.12 \text{ tonnes} $$ ### Step 4: Dynamic Load Factor (DLF) For crane lifts with controlled acceleration, the dynamic load factor is applied to account for inertial effects: $$ W_{static} = M_{block} \times g $$ $$ W_{static} = 59.1157 \times 9.81 = 579.92 \text{ kN} $$ $$ W_{dynamic} = W_{static} \times (1 + a/g) = W_{static} \times 1.2 $$ $$ W_{dynamic} = 579.92 \times 1.2 = 695.90 \text{ kN} $$ ### Step 5: Sling Force per Pick (4-Point Symmetric Lift) If the block is lifted using **4 symmetric slings**, and assuming the block is perfectly balanced: $$ F_{sling} = \dfrac{W_{dynamic}}{4} = \dfrac{695.90}{4} $$ $$ F_{sling} = 173.98 \text{ kN per pick} \approx 174 \text{ kN} $$ ### Step 6: Sling Tension Including Sling Angle If slings are inclined at angle $\theta = 60°$ from horizontal (typical for stability), the sling force increases: $$ F_{sling,\text{inclined}} = \dfrac{W_{dynamic}}{4 \times \sin\theta} = \dfrac{695.90}{4 \times \sin 60°} $$ $$ F_{sling,\text{inclined}} = \dfrac{695.90}{4 \times 0.866} = \dfrac{695.90}{3.464} $$ $$ F_{sling,\text{inclined}} = 200.95 \text{ kN per pick} \approx 201 \text{ kN} $$ ### Step 7: Summary of Results | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Total block mass (static) | **59.12 tonnes** | | Static weight | **579.92 kN** | | Dynamic weight (1.2 DLF) | **695.90 kN** | | Sling force per pick (vertical) | **173.98 kN** | | Sling force per pick (60° inclined) | **200.95 kN** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Valuation Tip:** Always state units explicitly. A common mistake is forgetting the dynamic factor, costing 1–2 marks. Examiners specifically check for the **1.2 multiplier** in crane lift problems. ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis: Welding Heat Input Calculation This derivation tests the KTU outcome of relating **welding parameters to distortion control** — a core assembly concept. ### Problem Statement A fillet weld is deposited using GMAW with the following parameters: - Arc voltage: $V = 28$ V - Welding current: $I = 250$ A - Travel speed: $s = 350$ mm/min **Find the heat input per unit length.** ### Step 1: Apply the Heat Input Formula $$ E = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{s \times 1000} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute Values $$ E = \dfrac{28 \times 250 \times 60}{350 \times 1000} $$ ### Step 3: Evaluate Numerator $$ 28 \times 250 = 7000 $$ $$ 7000 \times 60 = 420{,}000 $$ ### Step 4: Evaluate Denominator $$ 350 \times 1000 = 350{,}000 $$ ### Step 5: Final Calculation $$ E = \dfrac{420{,}000}{350{,}000} = 1.2 \text{ kJ/mm} $$ ### Step 6: Interpret the Result According to **IACS and classification society guidelines** (e.g., DNV, LR), the typical heat input limits for mild steel hull plates are: | Plate Grade | Max Heat Input (kJ/mm) | Reason | |-------------|------------------------|--------| | Mild steel (Grade A) | **2.0 – 3.5** | Avoid excessive HAZ softening | | AH36 (high tensile) | **1.5 – 2.5** | Prevent HAZ toughness degradation | | EH36 (extra high) | **1.0 – 1.8** | Critical CTOD requirements | Since $E = 1.2$ kJ/mm, this weld is **within the acceptable range** for AH36 steel but **borderline** for EH36 — a note the examiner would reward. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Construction Methods This matrix is a **direct KTU Board question template** — 7-mark questions are often set on this comparison. | Parameter | Conventional (Berth) Construction | Modern (Block) Construction | |-----------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------| | **Work location** | Mostly on the berth (vertical, overhead) | Mostly in workshop (flat) | | **Welding position** | Mix of 2G, 3G, 4G (overhead) | Predominantly 1G/1F (flat) | | **Welding speed** | Slow (overhead fatigue) | Fast (3–5× quicker) | | **Labor productivity** | 10–20 man-hours/tonne | 5–10 man-hours/tonne | | **Outfitting efficiency** | Post-launch outfitting (50–60%) | Pre-outfitting (80–90%) | | **Block size** | Small units | Up to 1,200 tonnes | | **Crane dependency** | Low | High (gantry cranes essential) | | **Dimensional control** | Difficult on berth | Workshop-controlled (laser) | | **Initial investment** | Lower | Higher (cranes, automation) | | **Build time** | Longer (12–18 months) | Shorter (6–9 months) | | **Best suited for** | Small vessels, repair ships | Large tankers, bulkers, containers | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Assembly Pyramid (Block Building Philosophy) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["PLATE LEVEL - Flat Steel Plates
Incoming material 1.5 to 50 mm thick"] A2["SUB ASSEMBLY - Stiffened Panels
Plate plus stiffeners welded together"] A3["BLOCK UNIT - 3D Module
Multiple panels joined as unit"] A4["GRAND BLOCK - Mega Block
200 to 1200 tonnes combined units"] A5["COMPLETE HULL - Final Ship
All grand blocks erected and welded"] A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 B1["Steel stock arrives at yard"] -.-> A1 A5 -.-> B2["Launching and outfitting phase"] style A1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style A2 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b style A3 fill:#81d4fa,stroke:#01579b style A4 fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#01579b,color:#fff style A5 fill:#0288d1,stroke:#01365e,color:#fff style B1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style B2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.2 Hull Erection Sequence on Building Berth ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1 - Keel Blocks Laid
Centerline marked, keel positioned"] S2["STEP 2 - Bottom Shell Panels Erected
Symmetric on both sides of keel"] S3["STEP 3 - Transverse Floors Fitted
Bottom transverse members installed"] S4["STEP 4 - Inner Bottom Plating
Double bottom formed"] S5["STEP 5 - Side Shell Frames Erected
Transverse ring sections built"] S6["STEP 6 - Side Shell Plating Added
Strakes fitted from bottom to sheer"] S7["STEP 7 - Bulkheads Installed
WT and non-WT bulkheads erected"] S8["STEP 8 - Deck Beams and Girders
Deck transverse members fitted"] S9["STEP 9 - Deck Plating Closed
Deck panels welded in place"] S10["STEP 10 - Superstructure Blocks
Lifted by gantry crane onto deck"] S11["STEP 11 - Final Welding and Survey
NDT inspection and dimensional check"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10 S10 --> S11 style S1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style S2 fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100 style S3 fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#e65100 style S4 fill:#ffa726,stroke:#e65100 style S5 fill:#ff9800,stroke:#e65100,color:#fff style S6 fill:#fb8c00,stroke:#e65100,color:#fff style S7 fill:#f57c00,stroke:#e65100,color:#fff style S8 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#e65100,color:#fff style S9 fill:#e65100,stroke:#bf360c,color:#fff style S10 fill:#d84315,stroke:#bf360c,color:#fff style S11 fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#7f0000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Component Assembly Function Map (Outfit Integration) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph StructuralLayer["STRUCTURAL ASSEMBLY LAYER"] S1A["Keel and Bottom"] S2A["Side Shell"] S3A["Deck and Bulkheads"] S4A["Superstructure"] end subgraph OutfitLayer["OUTFIT ASSEMBLY LAYER"] O1["Piping Systems
Sea chest to manifold"] O2["Electrical and Electronics
Cables, navigation"] O3["Machinery Foundations
Engines, pumps, motors"] O4["HVAC and Ventilation
Ducts and fans"] O5["Accommodation Furniture
Cabins, galley, mess"] end subgraph IntegrationLayer["INTEGRATION POINTS"] I1["Pre-outfitting in workshop"] I2["Block-level outfitting"] I3["Grand block outfitting"] I4["On-berth final outfitting"] I5["Post-launch outfitting"] end S1A --> I1 S2A --> I2 S3A --> I3 S4A --> I4 I1 --> O1 I2 --> O2 I2 --> O3 I3 --> O4 I4 --> O5 O1 --> I5 O2 --> I5 O3 --> I5 O4 --> I5 O5 --> I5 style StructuralLayer fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style OutfitLayer fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style IntegrationLayer fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c ``` ## 4.4 Block Joining Methods at Grand Block Interface ```mermaid flowchart TD J1["Two Grand Blocks Positioned"] J2{"Butt Gap
check 2 to 4 mm"} J3["High-Low and Misalignment
Corrected by Strongbacks"] J4["Fit-Up Tack Welds Applied"] J5["Root Run Welded SMAW or FCAW"] J6["Back Gouging from Reverse Side"] J7["Cap and Fill Passes by SAW or FCAW"] J8["NDT Inspection
UT, RT, MPI, DPI"] J9{"Defects
Detected?"} J10["Repair and Re-test"] J11["Block Welding Accepted"] J1 --> J2 J2 -->|Acceptable| J3 J2 -->|Not Acceptable| J10 J3 --> J4 J4 --> J5 J5 --> J6 J6 --> J7 J7 --> J8 J8 --> J9 J9 -->|Yes| J10 J10 --> J3 J9 -->|No| J11 style J1 fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#004d40 style J2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style J3 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40 style J4 fill:#80cbc4,stroke:#004d40 style J5 fill:#4db6ac,stroke:#004d40,color:#fff style J6 fill:#26a69a,stroke:#004d40,color:#fff style J7 fill:#009688,stroke:#004d40,color:#fff style J8 fill:#00897b,stroke:#004d40,color:#fff style J9 fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17 style J10 fill:#ef5350,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#fff style J11 fill:#66bb6a,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define "Sub-assembly" and "Grand Block" in ship construction. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | CO3 | Bloom's: Remembering]** **Model Answer:** A **sub-assembly** is the smallest unit in the shipbuilding assembly hierarchy, formed by welding a single plate to its attached stiffeners, frames, or brackets (e.g., a stiffened shell panel or a transverse ring). It is the most basic output of the panel fabrication shop. A **grand block** (also called a **mega-block**) is a very large integrated assembly, typically ranging from **200 to 1,200 tonnes**, formed by joining several pre-fabricated blocks. Grand blocks represent the largest unit that can be lifted by the yard's gantry crane and are joined on the building berth to form the final hull. > [!TIP] > **Exam Smart Move:** Always cite the **tonnage range (200–1,200 t)** and the **three-tier hierarchy** in your answer — this signals depth and earns full 3 marks. ### Question 2: What is meant by "pre-outfitting" in modern shipbuilding? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | CO3 | Bloom's: Understanding]** **Model Answer:** **Pre-outfitting** is the assembly-line practice of installing outfit items (piping, electrical cables, machinery foundations, insulation, ventilation ducts, furniture) into a block **while it is still in the workshop, before** it is erected on the building berth. This contrasts with the older **post-outfitting** method where all outfitting was done after launch. **Advantages of pre-outfitting:** - ✅ Work performed at **ground level** (ergonomic, safer) - ✅ Welding performed in **flat position** (faster, higher quality) - ✅ **Parallel production** of structure and outfit - ✅ **70–80% reduction** in final-stage labor on the berth - ✅ Reduced overall build time by **30–40%** > [!TIP] > **Pair it with a contrasting point** ("unlike conventional post-launch outfitting…") to demonstrate comparative understanding. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | CO3, CO4 | Bloom's: Applying, Analyzing]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the hierarchical assembly philosophy of modern shipbuilding with a neat sketch showing the three-tier hierarchy. Discuss the advantages of block construction over conventional berth construction.** **Model Answer Outline:** **1. Three-Tier Hierarchy (3 marks):** - **Tier 1 — Sub-assembly:** Plate + stiffeners welded in the panel shop (flat position). - **Tier 2 — Block / Unit:** Multiple sub-assemblies joined to form 3D modules (double bottom, engine room, etc.). - **Tier 3 — Grand Block / Ship:** Blocks joined on the berth/dock to form the complete hull. **2. Block Construction Advantages (4 marks):** | S.No. | Advantage | Explanation | |-------|-----------|-------------| | 1 | **Productivity** | Welding predominantly in 1G/1F flat position; 3–5× faster | | 2 | **Quality** | Workshop-controlled environment → fewer defects | | 3 | **Safety** | Less work-at-height; reduced accident risk | | 4 | **Parallelism** | Multiple blocks built simultaneously → shorter build time | | 5 | **Outfitting efficiency** | Pre-outfitting → 70–80% outfit completion before erection | | 6 | **Dimensional accuracy** | Laser total station control → tolerance ±3 mm | > **Sketch to draw:** A simple block diagram showing Plate → Panel → Block → Grand Block → Ship (5 boxes with arrows). #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A double-bottom block measures 14 m × 12 m × 1.2 m. The bottom and inner bottom plates are 18 mm and 14 mm thick respectively. The block contains 8 longitudinal girders (14 m × 0.5 m × 12 mm web) and 12 transverse floors (12 m × 0.6 m × 12 mm web). The outfit mass is 4 tonnes. Calculate: (i) total block weight, (ii) dynamic load on a 4-pick lift with 60° sling angle and DLF = 1.2.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Volumes** $$ V_{bp} = 14 \times 12 \times 0.018 = 3.024 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ V_{ibp} = 14 \times 12 \times 0.014 = 2.352 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ V_{lg} = 8 \times (14 \times 0.5 \times 0.012) = 0.672 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ V_{tf} = 12 \times (12 \times 0.6 \times 0.012) = 1.0368 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ V_{total} = 3.024 + 2.352 + 0.672 + 1.0368 = 7.0848 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 2: Steel Mass** $$ M_{steel} = 7.85 \times 7.0848 = 55.62 \text{ tonnes} $$ **Step 3: Total Block Mass** $$ M_{block} = 55.62 + 4 = 59.62 \text{ tonnes} $$ **Step 4: Static Weight** $$ W_{static} = 59.62 \times 9.81 = 584.87 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 5: Dynamic Weight (DLF = 1.2)** $$ W_{dynamic} = 584.87 \times 1.2 = 701.85 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 6: Sling Force per Pick (60° from horizontal, 4 picks)** $$ F_{sling} = \dfrac{W_{dynamic}}{4 \times \sin 60°} = \dfrac{701.85}{4 \times 0.866} = \dfrac{701.85}{3.464} $$ $$ F_{sling} = 202.65 \text{ kN per pick} $$ **Final Answer:** (i) **Block weight = 59.62 tonnes** (ii) **Sling force ≈ 202.65 kN per pick** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | CO3, CO4 | Bloom's: Understanding, Applying]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Describe with sketches: (i) the sequence of hull erection on a building berth, (ii) the assembly procedure for a transverse ring in modern ring-building method.** **Model Answer Outline:** **(i) Hull Erection Sequence (4 marks):** 1. **Keel laying** — centerline marked, keel blocks positioned. 2. **Bottom shell panels** erected symmetrically. 3. **Transverse floors** (bottom transverse webs) fitted. 4. **Inner bottom plating** completed → double bottom closed. 5. **Side shell frames** erected using ring-building or progressive method. 6. **Side shell plating** fitted from bilge upward to sheer strake. 7. **Watertight bulkheads** installed (tested after welding). 8. **Deck beams and girders** fitted. 9. **Deck plating** welded. 10. **Superstructure blocks** lifted by gantry crane. 11. **Final NDT inspection** and dimensional survey. **(ii) Transverse Ring Assembly (3 marks):** - A complete transverse ring comprises **floor + side frames + deck beam + brackets**, pre-assembled **flat on the shop floor**. - The ring is then **rotated 90° (upended)** using a special ring-turning device. - It is **lifted by crane** and lowered into position between two already-erected blocks. - Welded to the bottom and to adjacent rings. - **Advantage:** 70% of welding done in flat position, much faster than building the ring in-situ. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often describe ring-building as building the entire hull flat — this is **wrong**. The ring is a **transverse section** of the hull, not the whole ship. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A fillet weld is deposited using SAW with V = 32 V, I = 600 A, and travel speed = 500 mm/min. Calculate the heat input. Comment on whether this heat input is suitable for AH36 grade steel plate.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Apply the Heat Input Formula** $$ E = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{s \times 1000} = \dfrac{32 \times 600 \times 60}{500 \times 1000} $$ **Step 2: Numerator** $$ 32 \times 600 = 19{,}200 $$ $$ 19{,}200 \times 60 = 1{,}152{,}000 $$ **Step 3: Denominator** $$ 500 \times 1000 = 500{,}000 $$ **Step 4: Result** $$ E = \dfrac{1{,}152{,}000}{500{,}000} = 2.304 \text{ kJ/mm} $$ **Step 5: Comment on Suitability** According to **IACS UR W2 and classification society guidelines**, the maximum recommended heat input for **AH36 grade steel** is typically in the range of **1.5 to 2.5 kJ/mm** to prevent: - Excessive **HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone)** softening - Reduction in **toughness** (Charpy impact values) - Grain coarsening in the coarse-grained HAZ region Since $E = 2.304$ kJ/mm, the heat input is **at the upper limit but still within the acceptable range** for AH36 steel. For **EH36 or DH36 grades**, this heat input would be **excessive** and would require either reducing current, increasing travel speed, or using a different welding process (e.g., multi-arc SAW with controlled parameters). > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to **comment** on the suitability of the heat input. Always add a 2–3 line interpretation. This typically fetches **2 bonus marks**. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Examiner's Pitfall Callouts — Read Carefully!** | # | Common Student Mistake | Marks Lost | How to Avoid | |---|------------------------|------------|--------------| | 1 | Confusing **sub-assembly** with **block** | 1–2 marks | Memorize the 3-tier hierarchy precisely | | 2 | Forgetting the **dynamic load factor** in lift calculations | 1 mark | Always multiply by **1.2** for crane lifts | | 3 | Using `\|x\|` in markdown tables (breaks the table) | (presentation) | Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX | | 4 | Wrong units (using mm for kN/mm answer) | 0.5–1 mark | Always write **kJ/mm** not just **kJ** | | 5 | Describing ring-building as "building whole ship flat" | 2–3 marks | Ring = **transverse section**, not whole ship | | 6 | Missing the **sling angle** in 4-pick lift formula | 1 mark | Use $F = W / (n \times \sin\theta)$ when slings are inclined | | 7 | Not commenting on **heat input suitability** | 2 marks | Always add interpretation, not just the number | | 8 | Writing "riveting" as primary joining method | 1 mark | Welding is **primary**; riveting is **legacy** for old ships | --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Assembly hierarchy:** Sub-assembly → Block → Grand Block → Ship (3-tier) - ✅ **Steel density:** $\rho_s = 7.85$ **t/m³** (memorize this!) - ✅ **Dynamic load factor (DLF):** Typically **1.2** for crane lifts - ✅ **Sling force formula:** $F = W_{dynamic} / (n \times \sin\theta)$ for inclined slings - ✅ **Heat input formula:** $E = (V \times I \times 60) / (s \times 1000)$ in kJ/mm - ✅ **Heat input limits:** Mild steel ≤ 3.5 kJ/mm | AH36 ≤ 2.5 kJ/mm | EH36 ≤ 1.8 kJ/mm - ✅ **Block weight range:** Modern grand blocks = **200 to 1,200 tonnes** - ✅ **Primary joining method:** **Welding** (SAW for panels, FCAW/SMAW for field) - ✅ **Erection sequence:** Keel → Bottom → Floors → Inner bottom → Side shell → Bulkheads → Deck → Superstructure - ✅ **Tolerance:** Block ±3–10 mm, Keel line ±5 mm, Frame spacing ±2 mm - ✅ **Pre-outfitting** = outfitting in workshop, **Post-outfitting** = outfitting after launch - ✅ **Block construction advantages:** Faster (3–5×), safer, parallel production, better quality - ✅ **Ring building** = pre-assemble a transverse ring flat, upend, and lower into position > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** If you remember **only 3 things** from this module, remember these: > 1. **3-tier hierarchy** (sub-assembly → block → grand block) > 2. **Welding is primary**, riveting is obsolete > 3. **Pre-outfitting** distinguishes modern shipyards from old ones > **End of Module 3 Notes — Assembly of Various Components**

topics.title: Assembly of Flat and Corrugated Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assembly of Flat and Corrugated Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Plate Assembly in Ship Construction

**Plate Assembly** in shipbuilding refers to the systematic joining of individual steel plates (and sections) to form larger structural components such as shell plating, deck plating, bulkheads, floors, girders, and tank tops. The two dominant categories are:

- **Flat Section Assembly**: The joining of plates arranged in a single plane (or with joggles) to form surfaces like decks, bulkheads, and inner bottoms.
- **Corrugated Section Assembly**: The joining of plates that have been pressed or rolled into a corrugated (wave-like or trapezoidal) profile to form components such as corrugated bulkheads, where the geometry itself provides stiffness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Assembly quality directly governs hull structural integrity, longitudinal strength, watertightness, and resistance to buckling. Mastering this topic is essential for both Module 3 written examinations and viva-voce assessments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of building a ship like assembling a giant **3D puzzle of steel sheets**. Two strategies exist:

- **Flat Plate Strategy** → Like a plain cardboard box wall. It's flat, simple, but easily bends under load. To prevent bending, you must add ribs (stiffeners) on the back.
- **Corrugated Plate Strategy** → Like a corrugated cardboard wall (think of those wavy shipping boxes). The wavy shape itself is strong, so you may not need extra ribs. The geometry does the strengthening work for free.

In shipbuilding:
- **Plain flat plates** = flat sections (decks, shell, etc.) — they need stiffeners to resist buckling.
- **Corrugated plates** = bulkheads, engine room bulkheads — the corrugation geometry increases rigidity dramatically.

### 1.3 Standard Units and Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Description |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| Plate Thickness ($t$) | **mm** | Typically 4 mm to 50 mm in merchant ships |
| Plate Length ($L$) | **m** | Standard panels 6 m to 12 m |
| Plate Breadth ($b$) | **m** | 1.5 m to 3 m typical |
| Corrugation Pitch ($p$) | **mm** | 300 mm to 1200 mm |
| Corrugation Depth ($d$) | **mm** | 100 mm to 350 mm |
| Welding Standards | **AWS / ISO 3834 / IACS S21** | International quality benchmarks |
| Steel Grade | **AH36, DH36, EH36, AH40** | High-tensile shipbuilding steel per IACS |

> [!NOTE]
> The structural efficiency of a **corrugated plate** compared to a **flat plate with stiffeners** can be 20–40% higher in terms of strength-to-weight ratio, which is why corrugated bulkheads dominate modern commercial ship design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Joints Used in Flat Plate Assembly

Flat plates are joined using one of the following joint configurations:

1. **Butt Joint** — Two plates meet edge-to-edge in the same plane. Used between plate strakes in shell plating.
2. **Lap Joint (Overlap Joint)** — One plate overlaps the edge of the other. Common in riveted shipbuilding; rare in modern welded ships.
3. **Tee Joint (T-Joint)** — One plate is welded perpendicular to another (e.g., stiffener to plate web).
4. **Corner Joint** — Two plates meet at a right angle with both edges prepared (e.g., bilge corner).
5. **Edge Joint** — Two plates joined along their edges to make a wider plate.
6. **Joggled Joint** — Plates are offset by a joggle (small step) to avoid four-plate intersections.

### 2.2 Welding Processes Used in Ship Plate Assembly

| Welding Process | Abbreviation | Application in Ship Assembly |
|-----------------|--------------|------------------------------|
| Manual Metal Arc Welding | **MMAW (SMAW)** | Out-of-position welds, root passes |
| Gas Metal Arc Welding | **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** | Long straight seams in workshops |
| Flux Cored Arc Welding | **FCAW** | Heavy plates, semi-automatic work |
| Submerged Arc Welding | **SAW** | Long butt joints in flat/horizontal position |
| Electro-Slag Welding | **ESW** | Very thick plates (>30 mm), vertical seams |
| Electro-Gas Welding | **EGW** | Thick vertical seams, lower heat input than ESW |
| Friction Stir Welding | **FSW** | Aluminum hulls, panels for naval ships |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:** *Compare MMAW and SAW.* The expected answer must cover heat input, deposition rate, position capability, slag removal, and typical applications.

### 2.3 Sequence of Assembly of Flat Sections (Standard KTU Answer)

1. **Marking and Cutting** — Plates are marked using templates and cut by oxy-fuel or plasma.
2. **Edge Preparation** — Plate edges are bevelled (typically V or X) for full-penetration welding.
3. **Pre-Fitting / Tacking** — Plates are clamped and tack-welded to hold alignment.
4. **Backing Run / Root Pass** — The first weld pass is deposited from the reverse side.
5. **Filling Passes** — Subsequent layers fill the groove.
6. **Capping Pass** — Final pass provides a smooth, strong surface.
7. **NDT Inspection** — Ultrasonic Testing (UT), Radiographic Testing (RT), Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) to detect defects.
8. **Stress Relieving** — Heat treatment for critical joints in high-strength steel.

### 2.4 Corrugated Section Assembly — The Core Concept

A **corrugated section** is a plate whose cross-section has been deformed into a series of repeating ridges and valleys. Common profiles:

- **Trapezoidal Corrugation** (most common — used in bulkheads)
- **Triangular Corrugation** (older, used in some bulkhead stiffeners)
- **Semi-Circular Corrugation** (used in some bulk carriers)
- **Hat-Shape Corrugation** (used for stiffened panels)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use corrugations?**
