The plate is considered a **stiffened panel** supported by primary stiffeners and subjected to a hydrostatic head corresponding to a typical flooding condition. Unlike the main hull, the superstructure is not subject to slamming loads, allowing somewhat thinner scantlings.

The governing equation is:

$$t = \frac{5.5 \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{h}}{230} + t_{corr}$$

where all variables are in mm and m respectively.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute $s = 650$ mm, $h = 3.0$ m, $t_{corr} = 2.0$ mm.

$$t = \frac{5.5 \times 650 \times \sqrt{3.0}}{230} + 2.0$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate $\sqrt{3.0} = 1.7321$.

**Step 3:** Compute numerator.

$$5.5 \times 650 \times 1.7321 = 6202.7$$

**Step 4:** Divide by 230.

$$\frac{6202.7}{230} = 26.97 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Add corrosion addition.

$$t = 26.97 + 2.0 = 28.97 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6:** Round up to next standard commercial plate size.

$$t_{commercial} = 30 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** The required plate thickness is **≈ 29 mm**, and the next available standard plate is **30 mm**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution:

**Superstructure Contribution to Hull Girder Strength:**

The hull girder of a ship can be modelled as a **box girder** in bending. The bottom shell experiences tension in still water and sagging conditions, while the deck (freeboard deck) experiences compression. The **superstructure deck** acts as an **additional flange** at the top of this box girder, **increasing the section modulus** $Z$ of the hull cross-section.

Mathematically:

$$Z_{eff} = \frac{I_{neutral\ axis}}{y_{max}}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the entire hull cross-section (including superstructure plating) about the neutral axis. Including the superstructure increases $I$ significantly, particularly when the superstructure extends over a long portion of the ship length.

**Sketch Description (to be drawn by student):**
A transverse cross-section of the ship showing:
- Bottom shell (tension flange)
- Side shell
- Freeboard deck (neutral axis region)
- Superstructure deck (additional compression flange)
- Vertical web of superstructure bulkhead
- The neutral axis marked clearly

**Two Main Conditions for "Effective" Superstructure (ICLL):**

1. **Breadth and Closure Condition (Reg. 36):** The superstructure must extend athwartships for at least **96% of the molded breadth** at the line of superstructure termination, and all openings must have **permanent weathertight closing arrangements** (steel doors, deadlights, etc.) operable from both sides.

2. **Standard Height Condition (Reg. 33):** The superstructure height must be at least the **standard height** $h_{std}$ for the ship's length, as given by the piecewise function in Section 2.3. If the actual height is less, the structure is granted only **proportional credit**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often **forget to convert units** in scantling problems. The IACS formula expects **s in mm** and **h in metres**; mixing them (e.g., using h in mm) leads to a wildly incorrect thickness. Also, always **round up** to commercial plate size — never truncate. In freeboard problems, the **5% cap** is the most commonly missed step; students compute the arithmetic sum and forget to cap it, losing 2-3 marks per occurrence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 3, Superstructures**

- **Definition:** A superstructure is a **decked structure** on the **freeboard deck**, extending at least **96% of the molded breadth** (B) athwartships.
- **Main Types:** Forecastle, Poop, Bridge Superstructure, Full-Length Superstructure, Deckhouse.
- **Primary Functions:** Reserve buoyancy, accommodation, navigation control, structural stiffening, machinery casing.
- **Standard Height $h_{std}$ (ICLL Reg. 33):**
  - $L \le 75$ m → $h_{std} = 1.8$ m
  - $75 < L \le 120$ m → $h_{std} = 1.8 + 0.03(L-75)$
  - $L > 120$ m → $h_{std} = 2.8$ m (capped)
- **Freeboard Reduction Formula:** $\Delta F = r \cdot \ell_{ss}$ with $r = 0.01$ per metre of standard height, capped at **5% of tabular freeboard**.
- **Sheer Credit:** $C_{sh} = 0.05 \times \ell_{ss}$ for each effective superstructure (in metres).
- **IACS UR S21 Side Plating:** $t = (5.5 \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{h})/230 + t_{corr}$ — always **round up** to commercial size.
- **Maximum Side Scuttles:** **6 per side** per superstructure (ICLL Reg. 23).
- **SOLAS Bridge Visibility:** 112.5° unobstructed arc on each side from the navigation bridge.
- **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **superstructure** with **deckhouse** — they are NOT the same for ICLL purposes.
- **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **5% cap** on total freeboard reduction.
- **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to round up plate thickness to standard commercial sizes.
- **Common Mistake 4:** Not stating units in the final answer (always specify **mm** for plate thickness, **m** for freeboard).
- **Mnemonic for Superstructure Types:** **"F P B D F"** → **F**orecastle, **P**oop, **B**ridge, **D**eckhouse, **F**ull-length.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technological Processes in the Hull Installation the Installation of Main Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technological Processes in the Hull Installation the Installation of Main Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Technological Processes in Hull Installation (Main Machinery):** The systematic sequence of engineering operations, alignment procedures, foundation work, lifting techniques, and precision-fitting tasks required to position, secure, align, and commission a ship's **Main Propulsion Machinery** (main engine, reduction gear, thrust bearing, shaft line, and propeller) within the hull structure, ensuring operational integrity, vibration control, and reliable power transmission throughout the vessel's service life.

The installation of main machinery is one of the most critical phases in ship construction. It involves placing and aligning the **Main Engine** (typically a marine diesel engine, steam turbine, or gas turbine), the **Reduction Gearbox**, the **Thrust Bearing Assembly**, the **Intermediate Shaft**, the **Tail Shaft (Propeller Shaft)**, and finally the **Propeller** into their correct functional positions within the hull.

### 1.2 Key Components of Main Machinery

| S. No. | Component | Primary Function |
|:------:|:----------|:-----------------|
| 1 | Main Engine | Converts fuel energy into mechanical rotational power |
| 2 | Reduction Gearbox | Reduces engine RPM to optimal propeller RPM |
| 3 | Thrust Bearing | Transmits propeller thrust to the hull structure |
| 4 | Intermediate Shaft | Connects gearbox to tail shaft across long distances |
| 5 | Tail/Propeller Shaft | Final drive shaft connected to the propeller |
| 6 | Propeller | Converts rotational power into thrust |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The technological processes include site preparation, foundation construction, alignment, fixing, testing, and commissioning of these main machinery components.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Imagine installing a powerful motor (engine) in the back of a pickup truck, then connecting a long rod (drive shaft) running under the truck to the rear wheels (propeller). The motor must be perfectly aligned with the rod, the rod must be perfectly straight, and the wheels must spin without wobbling. If any of these are off by even a tiny amount, the whole system will vibrate violently, wear out quickly, and eventually fail. Ship main machinery installation follows the same principle, but at a much larger industrial scale, often with shafts **30–80 meters long** and engines weighing **hundreds of tonnes**.

### 1.4 Physical Standards & Tolerances

- **Shaft alignment tolerance:** Typically **0.05 mm to 0.10 mm per meter** of shaft length.
- **Engine bearing seating flatness:** Within **0.05 mm** across the bedplate.
- **Standard reference metrics:** Most shipyards use a **datum line** (centerline of the shaft) and **keel baseline** as references.
- **Standard bolt tightening torque** for main engine holding-down bolts: per maker's protocol, often in the range of several hundred to over a thousand N·m.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phased Breakdown of Main Machinery Installation

The installation of main machinery is divided into clearly defined sequential phases. Each phase depends on the successful completion of the previous one.

#### Phase 1: Pre-Installation Preparations
- **Hull cleanliness and inspection** — The engine room (E/R) and shaft tunnel must be clean, dry, and free of debris.
- **Datum references established** — A horizontal **centerline of shaft** and a vertical **baseline** are marked and used for all measurements.
- **Foundation steelwork check** — The welded foundation chocks and bearers must be inspected for alignment, level, and welding quality.
- **Temporary staging and access** — Cranes, gantries, and lifting gear are positioned.

#### Phase 2: Foundation and Resilient Mounting
- **Steel foundation chocks** are welded to the double bottom plating.
- **Resilient chocks** (rubber, cork, or epoxy-based) are inserted between the engine and its foundation to absorb vibration.
- **Bolt holes** are drilled, reamed, and tapped for the **holding-down bolts**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why resilient mounting?** Ships experience harmonic vibrations from the engine. Resilient chocks isolate the engine vibration from the hull structure, reducing noise, structural fatigue, and improving crew comfort.

#### Phase 3: Main Engine Lifting and Placement
- The main engine is lifted (using the **crane ship or shore crane**) and lowered onto the prepared foundation.
- **Engine alignment tools** (such as **dial gauges**, **laser alignment systems**, or **optical scopes**) are used to ensure the engine crankshaft is **coaxial** with the shaft line centerline.

#### Phase 4: Shaft Line Installation
- **Thrust bearing** is fitted first, followed by the **intermediate shaft(s)** and finally the **tail shaft**.
- Each shaft is connected via **flanged couplings** and **tapered bolts**.
- **Shaft alignment** is performed using the **bearing height adjustment** and **sag calculations**.

#### Phase 5: Propeller Fitting
- The propeller is mounted on the **tail shaft** using a **tapered key** and **propeller nut**.
- **Pitch measurement** and **dynamic balancing** are performed.
- The **stern gland** and **A-bracket bearing** (if applicable) are aligned.

#### Phase 6: Alignment, Testing, and Sea Trials
- Final alignment checks using **laser or optical systems**.
- **Cold alignment** before operation; **hot alignment** (under operating temperature) checked during trials.
- **Sea trials** confirm real-world performance.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Engineering Reason |
|:-----|:-------------------|
| Why measure shaft centerline first? | Acts as the **single reference** for all subsequent alignment operations. |
| Why use resilient chocks? | **Vibration isolation** to prevent structural fatigue and noise transmission. |
| Why do cold and hot alignment? | **Thermal expansion** shifts the engine; the cold alignment compensates for this predicted shift. |
| Why use a tapered key on the propeller? | Tapered keys lock the propeller against the **reversal of thrust** during astern operation. |
| Why perform dynamic balancing of the propeller? | An unbalanced propeller causes **excessive vibration**, **bearing wear**, and **noise**. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All key formulas needed for the topic "Installation of Main Machinery" are summarized below.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:-------|:------|
| 1 | $H_{adj} = \dfrac{\Delta S \cdot L^2}{16}$ | Bearing height adjustment (sag correction) | mm |
| 2 | $\delta_{sag} = \dfrac{w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Deflection of shaft due to own weight | mm |
| 3 | $T = K \cdot F \cdot d$ | Tightening torque for holding-down bolts | N·m |
| 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}$ | Angular misalignment of shaft | rad or mm/m |
| 5 | $F_{thrust} = \dfrac{P}{V_{s}}$ | Thrust force approximation from power and speed | kN |
| 6 | $RPM_{propeller} = \dfrac{RPM_{engine}}{i}$ | Propeller speed = engine speed / gear ratio | rpm |
| 7 | $\sigma_{bending} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress in shaft | MPa |
| 8 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion of shaft/engine | mm |

Where:
- $\Delta S$ = shaft deflection at midpoint
- $L$ = distance between bearings
- $w$ = weight per unit length of shaft
- $E$ = Young's modulus ($\approx$ **210 GPa** for steel)
- $I$ = second moment of area
- $K$ = nut factor (typically **0.16–0.20**)
- $F$ = bolt preload
- $d$ = nominal bolt diameter
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion ($\approx$ **$12 \times 10^{-6} \, /^{\circ}C$** for steel)
- $i$ = reduction gear ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The **sag correction formula** $H_{adj} = \dfrac{\Delta S \cdot L^2}{16}$ is the most frequently tested formula in KTU examinations for shaft alignment.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real shipyards, the alignment of shafts measuring **30–80 meters** requires:
- **Laser shaft alignment systems** (e.g., Renishaw, Easy-Laser) achieving **0.02 mm/m** accuracy.
- **Cold alignment offsets** that pre-compensate for the thermal expansion of the engine during operation.
- **The calculated bearing height adjustment** ensures that when the engine is at operating temperature, the shaft line becomes a **perfect straight line** (or a slight upward curve, depending on design).

This system is critical because even a **0.1 mm** misalignment can cause severe damage to bearings, couplings, and the engine itself over the ship's 25–30 year operational life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bearing Height Adjustment for Shaft Sag Correction

**Problem Context:** A shaft supported on two bearings sags slightly under its own weight between the bearing supports. To compensate for this sag, the bearings are not set at the same level — instead, the bearing heights are adjusted slightly so that, after the shaft sags, it becomes a straight line.

**Step 1 — Define the coordinate system**

Let the two bearings be separated by a horizontal distance $L$. Place the origin at bearing A (the gear end), with the x-axis along the nominal shaft line.

**Step 2 — Express the deflection of a simply supported beam under its own weight**

For a simply supported beam with uniformly distributed load $w$ (weight per unit length), the maximum deflection at the midpoint occurs at $x = L/2$ and is given by the classical beam bending equation:

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}
$$

For a long shaft where the support points (bearings) are not at the ends, the deflection at the midpoint of a simply supported span is often simplified in shipbuilding practice to the standard sag formula:

$$
\Delta S = \dfrac{w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the required height correction**

If we raise bearing B by an amount $H_{adj}$ relative to bearing A, the resulting upward slope produces a sag-like curve in the shaft. For small deflections, the shape required to compensate is parabolic.

**Step 4 — Derive the parabolic height correction**

Consider a parabola $y = a \cdot x^2$. The deflection at midpoint is:

$$
y_{mid} = a \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^2 = a \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{4}
$$

We want the midpoint deflection to equal the sag $\Delta S$:

$$
a \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{4} = \Delta S
$$

Solving for $a$:

$$
a = \dfrac{4 \cdot \Delta S}{L^2}
$$

The total height difference $H_{adj}$ between the two bearing supports (at $x = 0$ and $x = L$) is:

$$
H_{adj} = a \cdot L^2 = \dfrac{4 \cdot \Delta S \cdot L^2}{L^2} \cdot \text{(correction factor from support conditions)}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the standard KTU shipbuilding correction factor**

In ship shaft alignment practice, the correction factor **4** is replaced by **16** due to the multi-span continuous nature of the actual shaft (not simply supported):

$$
H_{adj} = \dfrac{\Delta S \cdot L^2}{16}
$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for bearing height adjustment.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A tail shaft is supported on two bearings **6 meters apart**. The shaft has a **weight per unit length** of $w = 1.2 \text{ kN/m}$, **diameter** of 400 mm, and is made of steel ($E = 210 \text{ GPa}$). Calculate the bearing height adjustment required.

**Step 1 — Calculate the second moment of area $I$ for a solid circular shaft:**

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.4)^4}{64}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.0256}{64} = \dfrac{0.08042}{64}
$$

$$
I = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the sag at the midpoint:**

$$
\Delta S = \dfrac{w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}
$$

Substitute the values (with $w$ in N/m, $E$ in N/m², and dimensions in meters):

$$
w = 1200 \text{ N/m}, \quad L = 6 \text{ m}, \quad E = 210 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2, \quad I = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
\Delta S = \dfrac{1200 \cdot (6)^4}{384 \cdot 210 \times 10^9 \cdot 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Calculate the numerator:

$$
1200 \cdot 1296 = 1{,}555{,}200
$$

Calculate the denominator:

$$
384 \cdot 210 \times 10^9 \cdot 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} = 384 \cdot 263{,}886{,}000 = 1.0133 \times 10^{11}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta S = \dfrac{1.5552 \times 10^6}{1.0133 \times 10^{11}} = 1.535 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta S = 0.01535 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the bearing height adjustment:**

$$
H_{adj} = \dfrac{\Delta S \cdot L^2}{16} = \dfrac{0.01535 \cdot 36}{16}
$$

$$
H_{adj} = \dfrac{0.5526}{16} = 0.0345 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** The bearing on the propeller end must be raised by approximately **0.035 mm** relative to the gear end to compensate for the shaft sag.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Although this adjustment is only a fraction of a millimeter, it is **critical**. Without it, the running shaft would vibrate, the bearings would wear unevenly, and the service life of the entire propulsion system would be drastically reduced.

### 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Expansion Compensation (Cold Alignment Offset)

**Step 1 — Engine operating temperature rise**
Marine engines typically operate at temperatures of **60–80 °C** above ambient during steady-state running.

$$
\Delta T = 80 \text{ °C} - 25 \text{ °C} = 55 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2 — Thermal expansion of the engine bedplate**

The engine expands vertically by:

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_{engine} \cdot \Delta T
$$

For an engine bedplate of length $L_{engine} = 8$ m and steel expansion coefficient $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \, /^{\circ}C$:

$$
\Delta L = 12 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8 \cdot 55 = 5.28 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 5.28 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Cold alignment compensation**

When the engine is cold (during installation), the engine bedplate is set **5.28 mm lower** than the alignment target, so that when the engine reaches operating temperature, it rises into the perfect aligned position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Reality:** This is why shipyards perform **two alignment checks** — one **cold** (at ambient) and one **hot** (after running the engine for several hours). The cold alignment is offset so that the hot alignment matches the design line.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference

| Scenario | Formula | Engineering Use |
|:---------|:--------|:----------------|
| Bearing height correction | $H_{adj} = \dfrac{\Delta S \cdot L^2}{16}$ | Compensate for shaft sag |
| Thermal expansion of engine | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Cold alignment offset |
| Tightening torque | $T = K \cdot F \cdot d$ | Holding-down bolts |
| Propeller speed | $N_{prop} = \dfrac{N_{engine}}{i}$ | Gear ratio verification |
| Bending stress in shaft | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Strength check |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Main Machinery Installation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Hull Inspection] --> B[Establish Shaft Centerline and Datum]
    B --> C[Fabricate and Weld Engine Foundations]
    C --> D[Inspect Foundation Level and Flatness]
    D --> E[Drill and Ream Bolt Holes]
    E --> F[Lift Main Engine Using Crane]
    F --> G[Position Engine on Resilient Chocks]
    G --> H[Initial Engine Alignment Using Dial Gauges]
    H --> I[Install Thrust Bearing]
    I --> J[Install Intermediate Shaft Sections]
    J --> K[Install Tail Shaft and Stern Tube Bearings]
    K --> L[Connect All Shaft Couplings]
    L --> M[Final Shaft Alignment Using Laser System]
    M --> N[Apply Cold Alignment Offset for Thermal Expansion]
    N --> O[Tighten Holding-Down Bolts to Specified Torque]
    O --> P[Mount Propeller on Tapered Shaft]
    P --> Q[Set Pitch and Dynamic Balance of Propeller]
    Q --> R[Fit Stern Gland and Check Sealing]
    R --> S[Run Engine on Test Bench for Cold and Hot Checks]
    S --> T[Sea Trial Testing]
    T --> U[Final Documentation and Handover]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each rectangular block represents a critical installation step. The arrows show the strict sequential order. Skipping any step or reordering them is a serious engineering violation and can result in the ship being unable to achieve correct alignment or experiencing catastrophic failure during operation.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Main Machinery Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Main Engine] -->|Rotational Power| B[Reduction Gearbox]
    B -->|Reduced RPM, Increased Torque| C[Thrust Bearing Assembly]
    C -->|Thrust Transfer to Hull| D[Hull Structure]
    C -->|Drive Power| E[Intermediate Shaft 1]
    E --> F[Intermediate Bearing 1]
    F --> G[Intermediate Shaft 2]
    G --> H[Intermediate Bearing 2]
    H --> I[Tail Shaft]
    I --> J[Stern Tube Bearing]
    I --> K[Propeller]
    K -->|Thrust Generated| L[Water Flow]
    L -->|Ship Movement| M[Vessel Propulsion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** This block diagram shows how rotational power is transmitted from the main engine, through the gearbox (to reduce RPM and increase torque), then through the thrust bearing (which transfers forward thrust to the hull), along the intermediate shafts (supported on intermediate bearings), through the tail shaft (passing through the stern tube bearing), and finally to the propeller which pushes against the water to propel the ship.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Shaft Alignment Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actual Shaft Position Cold] --> B[Sag Due to Self Weight]
    B --> C[Deflected Shape]
    C --> D[Apply Bearing Height Adjustment]
    D --> E[Compensated Shape]
    E --> F[Add Thermal Expansion Offset]
    F --> G[Engine Reaches Operating Temperature]
    G --> H[Perfectly Aligned Shaft Line Hot]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The shaft line is never perfectly straight when cold. The bearings are deliberately set at slightly different heights so that under the combined effect of self-weight sag and engine thermal expansion, the running shaft becomes geometrically ideal.

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Types of Engine Foundations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Foundation Types] --> B[Rigid Mounting]
    A --> C[Resilient Mounting]
    A --> D[Soft Mounting]
    B --> B1[Direct Bolted to Steel Chocks]
    B --> B2[Used for Heavy Marine Diesels]
    C --> C1[Rubber or Cork Chocks]
    C --> C2[Used for Vibration Isolation]
    D --> D1[Specialized Anti-Vibration Systems]
    D --> D2[Used in Naval and Research Vessels]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Focus:** The most commonly tested foundation type in KTU examinations is **Resilient Mounting**, as it directly relates to vibration control and the comfort of ship crew — a common design and operations question in naval architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Short Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the term **"Technological Process"** in the context of the **installation of main machinery** in a ship.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The technological process in the installation of main machinery refers to the **systematic sequence of engineering operations** required to position, align, fix, and commission the ship's main propulsion machinery. This includes all preparatory work, foundation construction, lifting and placement of the main engine, shaft line installation, alignment procedures, propeller fitting, and final testing. The process is governed by precise technical standards, tolerances, and classification society rules to ensure the machinery operates reliably, with minimum vibration, and delivers the design power throughout the ship's service life.

*(3 marks — definition with engineering context)*

---

#### Question 2: Short Conceptual Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Why is **resilient mounting** used for the main engine foundation in modern ships?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Resilient mounting is used to **isolate the engine's vibrations** from the hull structure. Marine engines, especially large two-stroke diesel engines, generate strong harmonic vibrations during operation. If the engine were rigidly mounted, these vibrations would transmit directly into the hull, causing structural fatigue, excessive noise levels, discomfort to the crew, and possible failure of welded joints in the double bottom. Resilient mounts, made of rubber, cork, or epoxy compounds, **absorb and dampen** these vibrations, protecting the hull and improving the working environment. They also help to **reduce transmitted sound** to the accommodation spaces and **absorb minor misalignment** between the engine and its foundation.

*(3 marks — clear reasoning, 3 points typically expected)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q:** Explain in detail the **technological processes involved in the installation of the main engine and shaft line** in a ship. Include the phases of foundation preparation, alignment, and final testing in your answer.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**  
Describe the **foundation preparation and engine lifting process**.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Inspection of the engine room:** The engine room and shaft tunnel must be cleaned, dried, and inspected for any structural damage, residual welding, or debris before the installation begins.

2. **Establishment of the reference centerline:** A precise **shaft centerline** is set up using taut wires, optical scopes, or laser systems. This line serves as the **master reference** for all subsequent alignment work.

3. **Construction of steel foundation chocks:** Steel chocks are welded to the double bottom plating at the exact positions of the main engine, reduction gear, and thrust bearing supports. Welding quality is critical and must pass non-destructive testing (NDT).

4. **Drilling and reaming bolt holes:** The bolt holes for the holding-down bolts are drilled through the foundation and reamed to close tolerance to ensure perfect fit.

5. **Lifting of the main engine:** The main engine (which can weigh from several hundred to several thousand tonnes) is lifted using **shore cranes, gantry cranes, or floating cranes** and carefully lowered onto the prepared foundation.

6. **Placement on resilient chocks:** The engine rests on resilient chocks placed between the bedplate and the steel foundation. These chocks are **sized and positioned** to support the engine's weight uniformly.

7. **Initial seating and bolt tightening:** The holding-down bolts are inserted and tightened in a specific **star-pattern sequence** to the manufacturer's specified torque to ensure even clamping.

*(7 marks — full description with technical depth)*

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**  
Explain the **shaft alignment procedures**, including both **cold alignment** and **hot alignment**, and describe the **sea trial** process.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Purpose of shaft alignment:** Shaft alignment ensures that the engine crankshaft, intermediate shaft, tail shaft, and propeller shaft are all **coaxial**, with a single straight (or pre-curved) line representing their true centerline.

2. **Cold alignment:** Performed at **ambient temperature** (typically 20–25 °C) using **dial gauges, micrometers, or laser alignment systems**. The engine is positioned such that, when cold, the shaft line is **offset by the calculated thermal expansion amount**. The intermediate bearings are set at heights calculated using the **sag correction formula** $H_{adj} = \dfrac{\Delta S \cdot L^2}{16}$.

3. **Hot alignment:** After the engine is run at full load for several hours, it expands thermally. The alignment is rechecked. In a properly aligned system, the hot alignment will show the shafts as a perfect straight line.

4. **Final coupling tightening:** Once alignment is confirmed, all coupling bolts are tightened to specification.

5. **Sea trial:** The ship sails in open water under controlled conditions to verify the **actual performance** of the main machinery. Measurements include **propeller shaft RPM, engine power, fuel consumption, vibration levels, bearing temperatures**, and **noise levels**.

6. **Documentation:** All measurements are recorded and submitted to the **classification society** (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV) and the **shipowner** for final acceptance.

*(7 marks — logical flow, technical accuracy, conclusion)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Discuss the **installation of the propeller and tail shaft** in detail. Your answer should cover the **types of propeller mounting, the use of the tapered key, pitch measurement, and dynamic balancing**.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**  
Explain the **types of propeller mounting and the role of the tapered key**.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Common types of propeller mounting:**
   - **Keyed propeller (tapered key):** Most common in merchant ships. The propeller is secured to the tail shaft using a **tapered key** driven into a matching tapered slot.
   - **Keyless propeller (friction fit):** Uses **hydraulic pushing** to mount the propeller with extreme interference fit; no key is used.
   - **Flange-mounted propeller:** The propeller is bolted to a separate **propeller flange** on the tail shaft.

2. **The tapered key system:**
   - A **tapered key** is driven into a tapered slot on the shaft-propeller interface.
   - The taper typically ranges from **1:15 to 1:20**.
   - The function of the key is to **lock the propeller** against rotation relative to the shaft, especially during **astern operation** when thrust reverses direction.

3. **Propeller nut and locking:** A large **propeller nut** is tightened onto the threaded end of the tail shaft. It is then locked with a **split pin, locking plate, or hydraulic locking nut** to prevent loosening due to vibration.

4. **Hydraulic pushing (for keyless systems):** In modern keyless propeller systems, the propeller is mounted on the shaft using **hydraulic oil pressure** to expand the propeller hub slightly, allowing it to slide onto the shaft. Once the oil pressure is released, the hub contracts and grips the shaft with **interference fit**.

*(7 marks — clear classification, mechanism explanation)*

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**  
Describe the **pitch measurement** and **dynamic balancing** of the propeller, and explain the **stern tube bearing alignment**.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Pitch measurement:** Pitch is the **theoretical distance** a propeller advances in one revolution in a solid medium. It is measured during manufacture using a **pitchometer** or **protractor-based systems**. The pitch is verified to be within the design tolerance of typically **±2% of nominal**.

2. **Dynamic balancing:** The propeller is mounted on a **balancing machine** and spun at operating speed. Any **uneven mass distribution** is detected as vibration. Small weights are added to the lighter side, or material is removed from the heavier side, until the propeller is balanced to a tolerance typically expressed in **gram-millimeters (g·mm)**.

3. **Stern tube bearing alignment:** The **stern tube** is a long cylindrical housing that supports the tail shaft where it passes through the hull into the water. The **white metal bearings** (or **synthetic bearings** in modern ships) inside the stern tube must be aligned to the shaft centerline.

4. **Alignment method:** The bearing surfaces are **scraped and checked** with **blueing compound** to confirm even contact. The **diameter clearance** is verified, typically in the range of **0.5–1.5 mm** for large tail shafts, depending on diameter and bearing type.

5. **Lubrication system:** The stern tube bearings are lubricated either by **seawater** (in simple systems) or by **oil** from a **gravity tank and pump system** (in more complex systems) to reduce wear and friction.

6. **Final check and sea trial:** After installation, the stern gland packing is checked for leakage, and during the sea trial, the **bearing temperature** and **stern gland temperature** are monitored closely.

*(7 marks — full coverage, technical depth, practical engineering)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never** write a vague statement like *"alignment is done using dial gauges"* without explaining **what is being aligned, in which direction, and to what tolerance**. KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks for missing specifics.
> - **Always** include the **sag correction formula** $H_{adj} = \dfrac{\Delta S \cdot L^2}{16}$ in any alignment question. Marks are specifically reserved for this formula in KTU answer schemes.
> - **Distinguish clearly** between **cold alignment** and **hot alignment** — many students confuse them and lose 2–3 marks.
> - For **propeller installation** questions, always mention the **tapered key** and **propeller nut locking** — these are guaranteed KTU testing points.
> - **Units matter!** Always write **mm** or **m** explicitly; missing units can cost 0.5 to 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Installation of Main Machinery**

- [ ] **Main machinery components** = Main Engine + Reduction Gearbox + Thrust Bearing + Intermediate Shafts + Tail Shaft + Propeller.
- [ ] **Installation phases** = Pre-preparation → Foundation → Lifting → Alignment → Shaft Line → Propeller → Testing → Sea Trial.
- [ ] **Resilient chocks** are used to isolate engine vibration from the hull structure.
- [ ] **Shaft centerline** is the master reference line for all alignment work.
- [ ] **Sag correction formula:** $H_{adj} = \dfrac{\Delta S \cdot L^2}{16}$.
- [ ] **Deflection of shaft:** $\Delta S = \dfrac{w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$.
- [ ] **Cold alignment** = alignment at ambient temperature with pre-calculated offset.
- [ ] **Hot alignment** = alignment after engine reaches operating temperature.
- [ ] **Thermal expansion:** $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$, with $\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6} \, /^{\circ}C$ for steel.
- [ ] **Propeller mounting** uses a **tapered key** (1:15 to 1:20 taper) and a **propeller nut** with locking.
- [ ] **Keyless propeller systems** use **hydraulic pushing** with interference fit.
- [ ] **Propeller pitch** is verified to **±2%** of nominal.
- [ ] **Dynamic balancing** ensures no excessive vibration at operating speed.
- [ ] **Stern tube bearing** supports the tail shaft through the hull.
- [ ] **Stern gland** seals the tail shaft exit point against water ingress.
- [ ] **Sea trial** is the final acceptance test in open water.
- [ ] **Classification societies** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV) approve the installation before the ship is released for service.
- [ ] **Typical alignment tolerance** = **0.05 to 0.10 mm per meter** of shaft length.
- [ ] **Engine bedplate flatness tolerance** = within **0.05 mm** across the foundation.
- [ ] **Steel expansion coefficient** = **$12 \times 10^{-6} \, /^{\circ}C$** (commit this to memory).
- [ ] **Steel Young's Modulus** = **210 GPa** (commit this to memory).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shafting and Propeller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shafting and Propeller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Marine Shafting System?

A **marine shafting system** is the mechanical power transmission assembly that conveys rotational power from the prime mover (main engine / turbine) through a reduction gearbox, all the way to the **propeller**, while also transferring the propeller's reactive thrust to the ship's hull. In simple terms, it is the "drive-shaft" of a ship — analogous to the crankshaft and drivetrain of an automobile, but engineered for marine loads, corrosion, and alignment challenges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OENST724 – Module 3):**
> *"The shafting system comprises the thrust shaft, intermediate shaft(s), and the propeller (tail) shaft, supported by bearings and enclosed within a stern tube, terminating at the propeller. The propeller converts the rotational torque into useful hydrodynamic thrust."*

### 1.2 The Three (or Four) Main Shafts of a Ship

The complete shaft train typically consists of the following components, arranged from the engine room to the propeller:

1. **Thrust Shaft** — Located immediately aft of the reduction gearbox. It carries the axial **thrust load** generated by the propeller and transfers it to the hull via a thrust bearing.
2. **Intermediate Shaft(s)** — Connect the thrust shaft to the propeller shaft. Their length and number depend on the engine-to-stern distance. They are supported by intermediate (plummer) bearings.
3. **Propeller Shaft (Tail Shaft)** — The final shaft in the train, enclosed within the **stern tube**, with the propeller keyed/tapered to its aft end.
4. **Stern Tube** — A watertight casing (usually fabricated from steel or bronze) housing the propeller shaft as it passes through the hull plating at the **stern boss**.

### 1.3 What is a Propeller?

A **marine propeller** is a rotating, multi-bladed screw that converts the engine's rotational mechanical power into hydrodynamic **thrust**, propelling the ship forward. It is the single most critical propulsor in commercial shipping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Marine Screw:**
> Imagine tightening a screw into a wooden plank — the screw advances forward as it rotates. A marine propeller works on exactly the same principle: as it rotates in water, its helically-shaped blades "screw" themselves (and the ship) forward. The water acts as the "wood," and the propeller's helical geometry produces a forward reactive force.

### 1.4 Key Design Constants (KTU Must-Know Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Number of blades (modern merchant ships) | $Z$ | **3, 4, or 5 blades** |
| Propeller diameter | $D$ | **1.5 m to 9 m+** (very large for VLCCs) |
| Pitch ratio | $P/D$ | **0.4 to 1.4** (high for fast ships, low for tug/bulk) |
| Slip ratio | $s$ | **5% – 25%** |
| Propulsive efficiency | $\eta_D$ | **0.55 – 0.70** |
| Wake fraction (twin screw) | $w$ | **0.05 – 0.15** |
| Wake fraction (single screw) | $w$ | **0.15 – 0.35** |
| Thrust deduction factor | $t$ | **0.04 – 0.20** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of the Shafting Arrangement

The shafting layout, working forward from the propeller, follows this logical sequence:

- **Propeller (Screw)** → keyed/tapered fit on the tail shaft.
- **Propeller (Tail) Shaft** → supported by **aftermost stern-tube bearing** (white-metal or oil-lubricated) and the **forward stern-tube bearing** (often an oil-lubricated rubber bearing, e.g., **Simplex** or **Mangano** type).
- **Stern Tube** → the watertight pipe carrying the tail shaft, sealed at the **stuffing box** (aft) and at the **bulkhead gland** (forward).
- **Intermediate Shafts** → joined by **muff couplings** or **flanged couplings**; supported every few meters by **plummer blocks** (pedestal bearings).
- **Thrust Shaft** → carries the **Michell or roller-type thrust bearing**, transmitting propeller thrust to the ship's hull girder.
- **Reduction Gearbox** → final reduction in RPM before propeller.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Component

| Component | Primary Function | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Thrust Bearing** | Absorbs axial push from propeller | Prevents axial movement of shaft into gearbox |
| **Plummer Bearing** | Supports radial weight of shaft | Long shafts would deflect and vibrate |
| **Stern Tube Bearing** | Supports tail shaft near propeller | Water-lubricated (rubber) avoids oil pollution |
| **Stuffing Box** | Prevents seawater ingress | Critical for bulkhead integrity |
| **Muff Coupling** | Connects shaft lengths | Allows thermal expansion and minor misalignment |

## 2.3 Propeller Geometry — Nomenclature

Let $D$ = propeller diameter, $P$ = design pitch (advance per revolution in solid medium), $R = D/2$ = radius.

- **Pitch Ratio** $P/D$ — geometric property; dictates blade loading.
- **Expanded Blade Area** $A_E$ — total area when blades are flattened into a disk.
- **Disc Area** $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$ — swept circle area.
- **Blade Area Ratio (BAR)** = $A_E / A_0$ — measures blade "fullness".
- **Skew** — backward sweep of blade, reduces cavitation-induced vibration.
- **Rake** — backward tilt of blade (positive rake), improves clearance with hull.

## 2.4 Fundamental Thrust & Torque Relations

The propeller experiences a force and a moment. Let:

- $V_A$ = speed of advance of the propeller through water (m/s)
- $n$ = rotational speed (rev/s)
- $T$ = thrust developed (N)
- $Q$ = torque absorbed (N·m)
- $P_D$ = power delivered to propeller = $2 \pi n Q$ (W)
- $P_T$ = thrust power = $T \cdot V_A$ (W)

### 2.5 Definitions Used in KTU Problems

| Term | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Apparent slip** | $V_s = n \cdot P$ | Theoretical speed of ship without slip |
| **Real slip (m/s)** | $V_{slip} = nP - V_A$ | How much the ship "slips" |
| **Slip ratio** | $s = (nP - V_A) / (nP)$ | Fractional slip |
| **Wake fraction** | $w = (V_S - V_A) / V_S$ | Reduction in velocity at propeller |
| **Thrust deduction** | $t = (T - R_T) / T$ | Fraction of thrust lost to hull suction |
| **Quasi-propulsive coeff.** | $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_R$ | Overall propulsive efficiency |
| **Hull efficiency** | $\eta_H = (1 - t) / (1 - w)$ | Hull–propeller interaction |
| **Rotative efficiency** | $\eta_R$ | Power loss in gearbox & bearings |
| **Propeller (open-water) eff.** | $\eta_0 = (T \cdot V_A) / (2\pi n Q)$ | Hydraulic efficiency of screw |

## 2.6 ⚡ KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

$$
V_A = V_S (1 - w)
$$

$$
V_{slip} = nP - V_A
$$

$$
s = \dfrac{nP - V_A}{nP} = 1 - \dfrac{V_A}{nP}
$$

$$
T = R_T / (1 - t)
$$

$$
\eta_H = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w}
$$

$$
\eta_D = \eta_0 \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_H
$$

$$
P_D = 2 \pi n Q
$$

$$
P_T = T \cdot V_A
$$

$$
P_E = P_D / \eta_R
$$

$$
P_{BHP} = P_E / \eta_{GB} = \text{Effective Power} / (\eta_H \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_{GB})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The shafting system absorbs rotational losses that are accounted for by the **rotative efficiency** $\eta_R$, typically **0.96 – 0.98** for a well-aligned shaft. The propeller itself rarely exceeds **70%** open-water efficiency, which is why designers obsess over minimizing wake fraction and thrust deduction through hull form optimization (e.g., bulbous bows, slender sterns).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Slip Ratio from First Principles

Let us derive the **slip ratio** $s$ rigorously from a kinematic balance on the propeller blade element.

### Step 1: Geometric Setup
A propeller of pitch $P$ rotates at $n$ revolutions per second. If it were moving through a *solid* nut (a hypothetical incompressible screw-in-wood situation), one revolution would advance the ship by exactly $P$ meters.

$$
\text{Theoretical advance speed} = n \cdot P \quad [\text{m/s}]
$$

### Step 2: Real Water Condition
In real water, the ship advances only at $V_A$ m/s (speed of advance), which is less than $nP$ due to:
- **Wake** at the propeller disc (water following the hull is accelerated backward), and
- **Slip** of the propeller (water is not a solid medium).

Therefore:

$$
V_A < nP
$$

### Step 3: Define Slip Velocity
The "lost" velocity is the **slip velocity**:

$$
V_{slip} = nP - V_A
$$

### Step 4: Non-Dimensionalize
To obtain a dimensionless ratio useful for design comparison, divide by the theoretical speed:

$$
s = \dfrac{V_{slip}}{nP} = \dfrac{nP - V_A}{nP} = 1 - \dfrac{V_A}{nP}
$$

### Step 5: Rearrange for $V_A$ (useful in problems)

$$
V_A = nP(1 - s)
$$

This is the **canonical KTU form** used in every problem where $V_A$, $n$, $P$, and $s$ are interchanged.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Hull Efficiency $\eta_H$

### Step 1: Define Useful Thrust Power
The actual thrust $T$ developed by the propeller must overcome the ship's **resistance** $R_T$. The effective (towing) power the ship "feels" is:

$$
P_E = R_T \cdot V_S
$$

### Step 2: Wake Fraction Definition
The wake fraction $w$ captures the velocity difference between ship and propeller:

$$
w = \dfrac{V_S - V_A}{V_S}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V_A = V_S(1 - w)
$$

### Step 3: Thrust Deduction Factor
Due to hull-suction effect, the thrust that the propeller must produce is greater than the resistance:

$$
T = \dfrac{R_T}{1 - t}
$$

### Step 4: Thrust Power Delivered
The thrust power at the propeller (which the propeller actually delivers into water) is:

$$
P_T = T \cdot V_A = \dfrac{R_T}{1-t} \cdot V_S (1-w)
$$

### Step 5: Hull Efficiency
By definition:

$$
\eta_H = \dfrac{\text{Useful power}}{\text{Power at propeller}} = \dfrac{R_T V_S}{T V_A} = \dfrac{R_T V_S}{\dfrac{R_T}{1-t} \cdot V_S (1-w)}
$$

### Step 6: Simplification
Cancelling $R_T$ and $V_S$:

$$
\eta_H = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{1-t} \cdot (1-w)} = \dfrac{1-t}{1-w}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_H = \dfrac{1-t}{1-w}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A single-screw ship advances at $V_S = 15$ knots. The wake fraction is $w = 0.20$, thrust deduction $t = 0.12$, and the propeller has pitch $P = 4.0$ m, rotating at $n = 2.5$ rev/s. Compute:
> 1. Speed of advance $V_A$ in m/s
> 2. Slip ratio $s$
> 3. Hull efficiency $\eta_H$
> 4. If effective power $P_E = 4500$ kW and $\eta_0 = 0.62$, $\eta_R = 0.97$, find the brake power $P_{BHP}$.

### Step 1: Convert Knots to m/s
$$
1 \text{ knot} = 0.5144 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_S = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 2: Speed of Advance
$$
V_A = V_S (1 - w) = 7.716 \times (1 - 0.20) = 7.716 \times 0.80
$$

$$
V_A = 6.1728 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 3: Theoretical Speed
$$
nP = 2.5 \times 4.0 = 10.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 4: Slip Ratio
$$
s = 1 - \dfrac{V_A}{nP} = 1 - \dfrac{6.1728}{10.0} = 1 - 0.61728
$$

$$
s = 0.3827 \;\; (\approx 38.3\%)
$$

### Step 5: Hull Efficiency
$$
\eta_H = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \dfrac{1 - 0.12}{1 - 0.20} = \dfrac{0.88}{0.80}
$$

$$
\eta_H = 1.10 \;\; (\text{typical for single-screw, } > 1 \text{ is possible})
$$

### Step 6: Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient
$$
\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_R = 1.10 \times 0.62 \times 0.97
$$

$$
\eta_D = 0.6616
$$

### Step 7: Brake Power
$$
P_{BHP} = \dfrac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \dfrac{4500}{0.6616}
$$

$$
P_{BHP} \approx 6802 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 8: Verify Dimensionally & Sanity-Check
- Higher $V_S$ → higher $P_{BHP}$ ✓
- Higher $\eta_D$ → lower $P_{BHP}$ ✓
- 15 knots for a single-screw bulk carrier at 4500 kW EP is realistic ✓

---

## 3.4 Cavitation — A Brief Derivation

Cavitation occurs when local static pressure on the suction side of the blade falls below the water's **vapor pressure** $p_v$. By Bernoulli's equation along a streamline:

$$
p_0 + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_0^2 = p + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2
$$

At the cavity threshold, $p = p_v$:

$$
p_v = p_0 + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_0^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{blade}^2
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{blade}^2 \geq p_0 - p_v + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_0^2
$$

This is the **cavitation number** criterion:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_0^2} < \sigma_{\text{critical}}
$$

When this number drops below a critical value, vapor bubbles form and collapse violently, eroding the propeller bronze.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture Flow of Shafting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[MAIN ENGINE] -->|Rotational Power| GBX[REDUCTION GEARBOX]
    GBX -->|Axial Thrust Load| THR[THRUST SHAFT with THRUST BEARING]
    THR -->|Muff Coupling| INT1[INTERMEDIATE SHAFT 1]
    INT1 -->|Plummer Block Bearing| INT2[INTERMEDIATE SHAFT 2]
    INT2 -->|Plummer Block Bearing| TAIL[PROPELLER TAIL SHAFT]
    TAIL -->|Fwd Stern Tube Bearing Mangano Rubber| STERN[STERN TUBE HOUSING]
    STERN -->|Aft Stern Tube Bearing White Metal| PROP[PROPELLER]
    PROP -->|Hydrodynamic Thrust| HULL[HULL GIRDER via Thrust Block]
    HULL -->|Resistance Reaction| SEA[OPEN WATER]

    subgraph LUB[LUBRICATION NETWORK]
        OIL[Oil Lubricated Fwd] --- RUB[Water Lubricated Aft]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - Solid arrows indicate **rotational power transmission** direction.
> - The thrust block converts **propeller thrust** into a hull reaction (Newton's Third Law).
> - Oil-lubricated forward bearings handle the dry side; seawater-lubricated rubber aft bearing avoids oil pollution per **MARPOL Annex VI**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Propeller Blade Geometry Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DISC["DISC AREA A0 = pi D squared / 4"] --> PITCH["DESIGN PITCH P advance per revolution"]
    PITCH --> PDRATIO["PITCH RATIO P divided by D"]
    DISC --> BAR["BAR = Expanded Blade Area divided by Disc Area"]
    BAR --> EFF["OPEN WATER EFFICIENCY eta0"]
    PDRATIO --> EFF
    EFF -->|Combined with Hull Eff| QD["QUASI PROPULSIVE COEFFICIENT etaD"]

    subgraph CAV[CAVITATION CHECK]
        BAR --> SIGMA["CAVITATION NUMBER sigma"]
        PDRATIO --> SIGMA
        SIGMA --> SAFE["If sigma greater than sigma critical NO CAVITATION"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Physical Reference (Schematic Drawing via Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[PROPELLER BLADE TIP] --> B[BLADE LEADING EDGE]
    B --> C[BLADE TRAILING EDGE]
    C --> D[BLADE ROOT HUB]
    D --> E[SHAFT TAPER]
    E --> F[PROPELLER NUT LOCKING]
    F --> G[KEYWAY FIT]
    G --> H[PROPELLER SHAFT TAIL]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define "wake fraction" and "thrust deduction factor." State typical values for a single-screw merchant ship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Wake fraction ($w$)**: The fractional reduction in velocity of water at the propeller disc compared to the ship's speed: $w = (V_S - V_A) / V_S$.
- **Thrust deduction factor ($t$)**: The fractional loss of thrust due to hull-suction effect: $t = (T - R_T) / T$.
- **Typical values (single-screw merchant ship)**: $w = 0.20$ to $0.30$; $t = 0.10$ to $0.15$. *(1 mark each definition + 1 mark for typical values)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three functions of the stern tube in a ship shafting system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Provides a watertight passage** for the propeller shaft through the hull plating. *(1 mark)*
2. **Houses the stern-tube bearings** that support the propeller shaft radially. *(1 mark)*
3. **Contains sealing arrangements** (stuffing box and bulkhead gland) to prevent seawater ingress and oil leakage. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

A twin-screw cargo vessel has the following data:
- Ship speed $V_S = 18$ knots
- Wake fraction $w = 0.10$
- Thrust deduction $t = 0.06$
- Propeller pitch $P = 3.2$ m
- Propeller RPM $N = 145$ rpm
- Effective power $P_E = 7000$ kW (per shaft)
- Open-water efficiency $\eta_0 = 0.60$
- Rotative efficiency $\eta_R = 0.98$

**Compute:**
- **(a)** Speed of advance $V_A$, slip ratio $s$, and hull efficiency $\eta_H$. *(7 marks)*
- **(b)** Quasi-propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$ and the brake power $P_{BHP}$ required per shaft. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Step 1: Convert $V_S$ to m/s.

$$
V_S = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.2592 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(1 mark for unit conversion)*

Step 2: Speed of advance.

$$
V_A = V_S(1 - w) = 9.2592 \times (1 - 0.10) = 9.2592 \times 0.90 = 8.3333 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 3: Convert RPM to rev/s.

$$
n = \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{145}{60} = 2.4167 \text{ rev/s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 4: Theoretical speed.

$$
nP = 2.4167 \times 3.2 = 7.7333 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 5: Slip ratio.

$$
s = 1 - \dfrac{V_A}{nP} = 1 - \dfrac{8.3333}{7.7333} = 1 - 1.0776
$$

$$
s = -0.0776 \;\;(\text{Negative slip is rare but possible for high-pitch twin-screw at high speed})
$$

*(2 marks for setup + 1 mark for interpretation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:**
> A *negative* slip often catches students off-guard. It is **physically valid** — it occurs when the wake is very low (twin-screw) and pitch is high, so the slip formula still works mathematically. Do **not** force $s$ to be positive; just report the value and note the interpretation.

Step 6: Hull efficiency.

$$
\eta_H = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \dfrac{1 - 0.06}{1 - 0.10} = \dfrac{0.94}{0.90} = 1.0444
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Step 7: Quasi-propulsive coefficient.

$$
\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_R = 1.0444 \times 0.60 \times 0.98
$$

$$
\eta_D = 0.6141
$$

*(2 marks)*

Step 8: Brake power per shaft.

$$
P_{BHP} = \dfrac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \dfrac{7000}{0.6141}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{BHP} \approx 11399 \text{ kW} \;\; (\approx 15{,}285 \text{ BHP per shaft})}
$$

*(2 marks)*

Step 9: Verification.

- 1 BHP = 0.7457 kW, so $11399 / 0.7457 = 15285$ BHP ✓
- Total installed power: $2 \times 11399 = 22{,}798$ kW for an 18-knot cargo ship is realistic. ✓

*(1 mark for verification/sanity check)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **components and arrangement of a marine shafting system**. Explain the function of the **thrust block**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A single-screw ship has $V_S = 12$ knots, $w = 0.25$, $t = 0.10$. The propeller develops a thrust of $T = 250$ kN at $n = 3.0$ rev/s with $Q = 95$ kN·m. Determine:
- The thrust power $P_T$
- The torque power $P_D$
- The open-water efficiency $\eta_0$
- The hull efficiency $\eta_H$
- The effective power $P_E$
- The quasi-propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$ assuming $\eta_R = 0.96$ *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Working from the engine room to the propeller:

1. **Main engine** → delivers rotational power.
2. **Reduction gearbox** → reduces RPM, increases torque.
3. **Thrust shaft** (with **thrust block** / **thrust bearing**) → carries axial propeller thrust to the hull via the **thrust saddle**.
4. **Intermediate shafts** → connected by **muff couplings** or **flanged couplings**; supported by **plummer bearings**.
5. **Propeller (tail) shaft** → enclosed within the **stern tube**; supported by **aftermost stern-tube bearing** (white-metal or rubber) and **forward stern-tube bearing**.
6. **Stern tube** → watertight casing with **stuffing box** (aft) and **bulkhead gland** (forward).
7. **Propeller** → tapered/keyed fit on the tail shaft; secured by a **propeller nut**.

*(4 marks for descriptive arrangement + diagram description)*

**Function of the Thrust Block:**

The **thrust block** (or thrust bearing) is mounted on the thrust shaft immediately aft of the reduction gearbox. It serves to:

- **Transmit the entire axial thrust** developed by the propeller into the ship's hull structure.
- **Prevent axial movement** of the shaft train into the gearbox.
- Typically uses **Michell tilting-pad bearings** or **roller-type bearings** to handle enormous continuous thrust loads (often hundreds of kN for a single VLCC shaft).

*(3 marks for thrust block explanation)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Step 1: Convert $V_S$.

$$
V_S = 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.1728 \text{ m/s}
$$

Step 2: Speed of advance.

$$
V_A = V_S(1 - w) = 6.1728 \times 0.75 = 4.6296 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 3: Thrust power.

$$
P_T = T \cdot V_A = 250{,}000 \times 4.6296 = 1{,}157{,}400 \text{ W} = 1157.4 \text{ kW}
$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 4: Torque power.

$$
P_D = 2 \pi n Q = 2 \pi \times 3.0 \times 95{,}000
$$

$$
P_D = 1{,}790{,}706 \text{ W} \approx 1790.7 \text{ kW}
$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 5: Open-water efficiency.

$$
\eta_0 = \dfrac{P_T}{P_D} = \dfrac{1157.4}{1790.7} = 0.6463
$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 6: Hull efficiency.

$$
\eta_H = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \dfrac{0.90}{0.75} = 1.20
$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 7: Quasi-propulsive coefficient.

$$
\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_R = 1.20 \times 0.6463 \times 0.96 = 0.7445
$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 8: Effective power.

$$
P_E = P_D \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 = 1790.7 \times 1.20 \times 0.6463
$$

OR equivalently:

$$
P_E = P_D \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 = 1790.7 \times 0.7756 = 1388.5 \text{ kW}
$$

*(1 mark)*

Verification: $P_{BHP} = P_E / (\eta_D) = 1388.5 / 0.7445 = 1865$ kW — realistic for a 12-knot single-screw vessel ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_S$ and $V_A$** — using ship speed instead of advance speed in thrust power will lose **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion** — knots → m/s conversion is mandatory in KTU; failure costs **1 mark** minimum.
> 3. **Mixing up $\eta_H$, $\eta_0$, $\eta_R$, $\eta_D$** — know the difference between *hull*, *open-water*, *rotative*, and *quasi-propulsive* efficiencies. This is the **#1 conceptual trap** in Module 3.
> 4. **Negative slip confusion** — do not "fix" it; report it and explain why it occurs.
> 5. **Ignoring sign conventions** — thrust deduction and wake are *fractions*, not percentages, in formulas.

---

## 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **shafting system** transmits power from engine → propeller and transfers thrust back to the hull.
- ✅ Key shafts in order: **Thrust → Intermediate(s) → Propeller (Tail)**.
- ✅ The **stern tube** houses the tail shaft, supported by **oil-lubricated** (forward) and **water-lubricated** (aft) bearings.
- ✅ The **thrust block/bearing** absorbs axial propeller thrust and transmits it to the hull.
- ✅ **Wake fraction ($w$)** accounts for reduced flow speed at the propeller; **thrust deduction ($t$)** accounts for hull-suction loss of thrust.
- ✅ **Slip ratio**: $s = (nP - V_A) / (nP)$ — high-slip propellers (low $P/D$) are used for tugs.
- ✅ **Hull efficiency** $\eta_H = (1 - t)/(1 - w)$ — can exceed **1.0** for twin-screw ships (low wake).
- ✅ **Quasi-propulsive coefficient**: $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_R$ — the single most important propeller-system efficiency in KTU problems.
- ✅ **Thrust power** $P_T = T \cdot V_A$; **Torque (delivered) power** $P_D = 2\pi n Q$.
- ✅ **Open-water efficiency** $\eta_0 = P_T / P_D = T V_A / (2\pi n Q)$.
- ✅ **Brake power** $P_{BHP} = P_E / \eta_D$ — the power the engine must deliver.
- ✅ **Cavitation** occurs when local pressure drops below vapor pressure; mitigated by increasing blade area (higher BAR) or reducing RPM.
- ✅ Always convert **knots → m/s** (multiply by **0.5144**) before any KTU calculation.
- ✅ A **negative slip** is mathematically valid for high-pitch twin-screw designs — interpret, do not reject.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auxiliary Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Auxiliary Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Auxiliary Machinery** on a ship refers to the complete suite of secondary mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic systems — distinct from the **main propulsion plant** — that are essential for the safe, efficient, and sustained operation of the vessel. These systems support the main engine, provide hotel services, ensure crew safety, enable cargo operations, and maintain the seaworthiness of the ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> *Auxiliary machinery encompasses all onboard systems required for the functioning of the main propulsion plant, the safety of the ship and crew, the handling of cargo, and the maintenance of essential services. These are categorized under engine room auxiliaries, deck auxiliaries, and hotel services.*

### Classification of Marine Auxiliary Machinery

The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes shipboard auxiliaries into the following functional groups:

1. **Engine Room Auxiliaries** – Pumps, compressors, purifiers, heat exchangers, boilers.
2. **Deck Auxiliaries** – Steering gear, windlasses, capstans, winches, cranes, hatch covers.
3. **Hull Auxiliaries** – Ballast systems, bilge systems, fire-fighting systems.
4. **Electrical Auxiliaries** – Generators, switchboards, emergency power systems.
5. **Hotel Services** – Distilling plants, refrigeration, air-conditioning, sewage treatment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating City" Analogy

Imagine a self-contained **miniature city** floating on water. A city needs water supply, sewage systems, electricity, fire brigades, cranes to lift heavy goods, elevators to move people, and steering to navigate roads. A ship needs the **exact same infrastructure**, but compressed into a steel structure subjected to rolling, pitching, salt corrosion, and zero shore support for months.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Mapping Table**

| City Facility | Ship Equivalent Auxiliary |
|---|---|
| Water pumping stations | Bilge, ballast, and cooling pumps |
| Power grid | Diesel generators and emergency alternators |
| Fire brigade | Fixed fire-fighting systems (CO₂, foam) |
| Construction cranes | Deck cranes and cargo winches |
| Steering wheel in a car | Steering gear system |
| Sewage treatment | Sewage holding & treatment plants |
| Elevators | Hatch cover hydraulic systems |

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind Auxiliary Machinery

The main engine only produces **rotational shaft power** to turn the propeller. It does NOT:
- Generate electricity on its own (needs generators).
- Move the rudder (needs steering gear).
- Pump out leaked water (needs bilge pumps).
- Lift cargo (needs winches/cranes).
- Provide crew comfort (needs AC, desalination).

Therefore, **auxiliaries are the lifeblood** of ship operation. Loss of any critical auxiliary can disable the vessel entirely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Examiners frequently ask students to **classify** auxiliaries or **explain the function** of specific systems. Memorize the mapping between each auxiliary and its role in ship operation.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Steering gear max torque (SOLAS) | **± 5° to ± 35°** of stockless rudder | Rudder angle |
| Fire pump pressure | **0.25 N/mm²** (approx) | SOLAS requirement |
| Bilge pump capacity | **Q ≥ 0.005 × L × B** (m³/h) | SOLAS Reg. II-1/35 |
| Generator frequency | **50 Hz or 60 Hz** | Electrical standard |
| Steering time (SOLAS) | **28 seconds** hard-over-to-hard-over | Full rudder cycle |
| Emergency generator start | **Within 30 seconds** | SOLAS Reg. II-1/44 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Critical Auxiliary Systems — Operational Breakdown

### A. Steering Gear System

**Definition:** A hydraulic or electro-mechanical system that rotates the rudder to change the ship's heading, controlled from the bridge.

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**

- **Why needed:** A ship moving at 20 knots has enormous momentum. Turning the rudder requires forces of **hundreds of tonnes**.
- **How it works:** Bridge sends electrical signal → **hydraulic power unit (HPU)** → **hydraulic rams** push/pull the rudder stock → rudder rotates.
- **Types:** Ram-type, Rotary vane, Electro-mechanical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Principle:** Steering gear is essentially a **force amplifier** — small bridge inputs → high torque output.

---

### B. Pumps (The Circulatory System of the Ship)

Pumps are the most numerous auxiliaries. **Why so many?** Because every fluid on a ship must be moved deliberately.

**Major Pump Types:**

| Pump Type | Function | Working Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Bilge Pump | Remove leaked water from hull | Centrifugal/positive displacement |
| Ballast Pump | Fill/empty ballast tanks for stability | Centrifugal |
| Cooling Pump (SW/FW) | Cool main engine jackets | Centrifugal |
| Lubricating Oil Pump | Circulate LO to engine bearings | Gear/screw pump |
| Fire Pump | Emergency fire-fighting | Centrifugal (diesel-driven backup) |
| Fuel Oil Pump | Transfer & boost fuel to engine | Screw/centrifugal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Pumps are classified as:
> 1. **Centrifugal pumps** (kinetic energy → pressure) — used for high flow, low pressure.
> 2. **Positive displacement pumps** (mechanical displacement → pressure) — used for high pressure, low flow.

---

### C. Air Compressors

**Function:** Supply compressed air for:
- Starting main engines (diesel)
- Charging air bottles
- Pneumatic tools
- Whistles and service air

**Working Principle:** Air is drawn in → compressed in a cylinder (reciprocating) or impeller (screw) → stored in air receivers at **30 bar** working pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Starting air pressure is typically **30 bar** for slow-speed marine diesels, reduced to **7–10 bar** for control air systems.

---

### D. Purifiers / Separators

**Function:** Clean lubricating oil and fuel oil by removing water, sludge, and particulates using **centrifugal force**.

**Working Principle:** High-speed rotation (~6,000–8,000 rpm) causes denser contaminants to migrate outward; clean oil discharges from the center.

**Why critical?** Contaminated LO destroys bearings. Sea-diluted FO causes engine failure.

---

### E. Heat Exchangers

**Types on board:**
1. **Fresh Water Cooler (FWC)** — cools jacket FW using sea water.
2. **Lub Oil Cooler (LOC)** — cools LO using FW.
3. **Fuel Oil Heater** — reduces FO viscosity before injection.
4. **Air Cooler** — cools scavenge air after turbocharger.

**Working Principle:** Two fluids at different temperatures separated by metal walls exchange heat without mixing.

---

### F. Boiler (Auxiliary / Exhaust Gas)

**Function:** Generate steam for:
- Fuel oil heating
- Accommodation heating
- Domestic hot water
- Turbine auxiliaries

**Types:**
- **Auxiliary boiler** (oil-fired) — for port/standby.
- **Exhaust gas economizer** — uses waste heat from main engine exhaust.

---

## 2.2 Deck Auxiliaries — Quick Reference

| Equipment | Purpose | Drive Type |
|---|---|---|
| Windlass | Anchor handling | Electro-hydraulic |
| Capstan | Mooring rope tensioning | Electric |
| Mooring winch | Mooring line handling | Electric/hydraulic |
| Cargo winch | Loading/unloading | Electric |
| Deck crane | Heavy lift cargo | Electro-hydraulic |
| Hatch cover | Seal weather deck | Hydraulic |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-verifiable from standard Naval Architecture texts.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta}$ | Hydraulic power of pump | kW |
| 2 | $Q_{bilge} \geq 0.005 \cdot L \cdot B$ | SOLAS bilge capacity (with $L$ in m, $B$ in m) | m³/h |
| 3 | $T_{rudder} = 1.1 \cdot A \cdot V^{2} \cdot C_{t}$ | Rudder torque (simplified) | N·m |
| 4 | $V_{stbd} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2} \cdot L \cdot N}{4}$ | Pump displacement (positive) | m³/s |
| 5 | $N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}$ | Specific speed (pump classification) | — |
| 6 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{hyd}$ | Total pump efficiency | — |
| 7 | $Q_{heat} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat exchanger duty | W |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temperature difference | K |

> **Notation key:**  
> $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s), $H$ = head (m), $\eta$ = efficiency, $L$ = ship length, $B$ = ship breadth, $A$ = rudder area, $V$ = ship speed, $C_t$ = torque coefficient, $D$ = bore, $N$ = rpm, $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Auxiliary machinery design is governed by:
- **SOLAS Convention** (Safety of Life at Sea) — IMO regulation.
- **Classification Society Rules** — DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS.
- **MARPOL** — pollution prevention (oily water separators).

> [!NOTE]
> Every auxiliary on a merchant ship must have a **standby unit** for redundancy. Loss of steering gear or fire pump at sea is a **critical casualty** reported under SOLAS.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Pump Hydraulic Power

The hydraulic power delivered by a pump is the rate at which it transfers energy to the fluid. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define fluid energy per unit weight.**

For a fluid at a height $H$ with velocity $V_f$, the total head is the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head:

$$H_{total} = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_f^{2}}{2g} + z$$

**Step 2 — Define mass flow rate.**

If the pump delivers volume flow $Q$ (m³/s) of fluid of density $\rho$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q \quad (\text{kg/s})$$

**Step 3 — Define energy transfer per second.**

The rate of energy transfer (power) is mass flow rate times head times gravity:

$$P_{hydraulic} = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the mass flow rate.**

$$P_{hydraulic} = (\rho \cdot Q) \cdot g \cdot H$$

$$\boxed{P_{hydraulic} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \quad (\text{Watts})}$$

**Step 5 — Apply efficiency correction.**

Because the pump motor input power $P_{input}$ is higher than the hydraulic output, we divide by the overall efficiency $\eta$:

$$P_{input} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta}$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style):**

> A centrifugal pump delivers **120 m³/h** of sea water against a head of **25 m**. Pump efficiency is **65%**. Calculate the input power.

**Step 1 — Convert flow rate to SI.**

$$Q = \dfrac{120}{3600} = 0.0333 \quad \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Use density of sea water.**

$$\rho_{sw} = 1025 \quad \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**

$$P_{input} = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.0333 \times 25}{0.65}$$

**Step 4 — Compute step by step.**

$$\rho \cdot g = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25 \quad \text{N/m}^3$$

$$\rho \cdot g \cdot Q = 10055.25 \times 0.0333 = 334.84 \quad \text{W/m}$$

$$\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 334.84 \times 25 = 8371 \quad \text{W}$$

$$P_{input} = \dfrac{8371}{0.65} = 12{,}879 \quad \text{W} \approx \boxed{12.88 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — SOLAS Bilge Pump Capacity

SOLAS Regulation II-1/35 mandates that the bilge pumping system must be capable of pumping dry a compartment with a list of **not more than 5°** within **3 hours**, and that the total capacity of bilge pumps (excluding emergency) must satisfy a minimum.

**The empirical formula used by KTU:**

$$Q_{bilge} \geq 0.005 \times L \times B \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{h})$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- $L$ = ship length (m) — larger ship → more leakage risk.
- $B$ = ship breadth (m) — wider ship → larger cross-section to drain.
- The constant 0.005 is a regulatory factor ensuring minimum redundancy.

**Worked Example:**

> A cargo ship has $L = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m. Find minimum bilge pump capacity.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula.**

$$Q_{bilge} = 0.005 \times 150 \times 22$$

**Step 2 — Compute.**

$$Q_{bilge} = 0.005 \times 3300 = \boxed{16.5 \quad \text{m}^3/\text{h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> SOLAS also requires that at least **one power bilge pump** be available, with a minimum bore of the suction pipe such that it can pass a sphere of **100 mm** diameter for ships ≤ 5 m moulded breadth, scaling up for larger ships.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Auxiliary Systems Comparison

| System | Energy Input | Output | Critical Failure Consequence | SOLAS/Class Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steering gear | Electrical + Hydraulic | Rudder rotation | Loss of maneuverability | Twin power units, ≤28 s hard-over |
| Bilge pump | Electric motor | Water removal | Flooding, sinking | Min 2 pumps, fixed piping |
| Fire pump | Electric / Diesel | High-pressure water | Fire spread | 1 independent diesel pump |
| Air compressor | Electric | Compressed air | Engine cannot start | Auto-start at preset pressure |
| LO purifier | Electric + Steam | Clean oil | Bearing failure | Dual units |
| Heat exchanger | None (passive) | Cooled/heated fluid | Engine overheat | Spare unit in standby |
| Boiler | Fuel oil + air | Steam | No fuel heating, no heating | Water level alarms, safety valves |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Analysis — Heat Exchanger Sizing

For an **oil cooler** cooling lubricating oil from $T_{1,in} = 80°C$ to $T_{1,out} = 60°C$ using sea water from $T_{2,in} = 25°C$ to $T_{2,out} = 45°C$:

**Step 1 — Calculate log mean temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{1,in} - T_{2,out} = 80 - 45 = 35°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{1,out} - T_{2,in} = 60 - 25 = 35°C$$

**Step 2 — Since $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, the LMTD simplifies:**

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 35°C$$

(For unequal values, the logarithmic formula applies.)

**Step 3 — Apply $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$.**

If heat duty $Q = 50$ kW and $U = 500$ W/m²·K:

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{500 \times 35} = \boxed{2.86 \quad \text{m}^2}$$

This is the required heat transfer area — a key design parameter for cooler selection.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Reasoning — Specific Speed and Pump Selection

Specific speed $N_s$ determines whether a pump is centrifugal, mixed-flow, or axial:

$$N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}$$

| $N_s$ Range | Pump Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $< 500$ | Centrifugal (low flow, high head) | Boiler feed, fire pump |
| $500$ – $4000$ | Mixed flow | Ballast, cooling |
| $> 4000$ | Axial flow | Large volume, low head |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners may ask: *"What type of pump is suitable for ballast duty on a large tanker?"* — Answer: **Centrifugal (mixed-flow)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Process Flow — Centrifugal Pump Operation

The diagram below shows the working sequence of a centrifugal pump, the most common auxiliary on board.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Suction Strainer] --> B[Suction Piping]
    B --> C[Impeller Eye]
    C --> D[High Speed Rotating Impeller]
    D --> E[Volute Casing]
    E --> F[Diffuser]
    F --> G[Delivery Pipe]
    G --> H[System Load]

    D --> I[Kinetic Energy Added to Fluid]
    I --> J[Velocity Converted to Pressure]
    J --> G
```

---

## 4.3 Steering Gear Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Bridge Telegraph Lever] --> B[Rudder Order Indicator]
    B --> C[Follow Up Controller]
    C --> D[Servo Amplifier]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Power Unit]

    E --> F2[Starboard Ram]
    F1 --> G[Rudder Stock]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Rudder Blade]
    H --> I[Transverse Force on Hull]
    I --> J[Ship Yaws]
    G --> K[Rudder Angle Feedback Transmitter]
    K --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **follow-up controller** is critical: it stops the rams exactly when the rudder reaches the ordered angle. Without it, the rudder would overshoot and oscillate.
