# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any four engine room auxiliaries of a modern merchant ship.**

**Model Answer:**

Engine room auxiliaries support the main propulsion plant. The four key types are:

1. **Fuel Oil Purifier:** Uses centrifugal force to remove water, sludge, and solids from heavy fuel oil before it enters the engine, preventing injector damage and combustion problems.

2. **Lubricating Oil Purifier:** Cleans the LO by separating out metallic wear particles, water, and oxidation products, ensuring long bearing life.

3. **Cooling Water Pump (Sea Water & Fresh Water):** Circulates sea water through heat exchangers to cool fresh water, which in turn cools the main engine jackets, pistons, and turbochargers.

4. **Air Compressor:** Supplies high-pressure compressed air (30 bar) for starting the main diesel engine and maintains air receivers for control systems and safety services.

*(Any four valid auxiliaries with one-line functions fetch full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State SOLAS requirements for the steering gear system.**

**Model Answer:**

SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 29 specifies:

1. The steering gear must be capable of turning the rudder from **35° on one side to 30° on the other side** in **not more than 28 seconds** at maximum ahead service speed.
2. For ships of 100,000 GT and above, the rudder must move from **35° on one side to 30° on the other side** in not more than **28 seconds** at maximum service speed.
3. **Two independent power actuating systems** must be provided for ships above 10,000 GT.
4. An **emergency steering gear** must be provided with adequate means of communication to the bridge.
5. Indicators must show rudder angle, power unit running, and any failure.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a centrifugal pump used as a bilge pump on board ships. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages.**

#### (a) Construction and Working — (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:** A centrifugal pump is the most common bilge pump on merchant ships due to its simplicity, smooth flow, and ability to handle dirty water.

**Construction (Major Parts):**

1. **Impeller** — The rotating component with curved vanes. There are two types:
   - **Open impeller** — used for dirty water (e.g., bilge).
   - **Closed impeller** — used for clean fluids.

2. **Casing (Volute)** — Surrounds the impeller. The volute casing has an increasing cross-sectional area that converts the high-velocity fluid into pressure.

3. **Shaft** — Connects impeller to drive motor, supported by bearings.

4. **Suction Eye** — The central inlet where fluid enters the impeller.

5. **Mechanical Seal** — Prevents leakage along the shaft.

6. **Casing Wear Ring** — Reduces recirculation between impeller and casing.

**Working Principle:**

1. The impeller rotates at high speed (typically 1450–3500 rpm) driven by an electric motor.
2. Fluid enters the **eye of the impeller** axially through the suction pipe.
3. The rotating vanes impart **kinetic energy** to the fluid (centrifugal force throws fluid outward).
4. The fluid moves into the **volute casing**, where the expanding cross-section converts kinetic energy into **pressure energy**.
5. Pressurized fluid leaves through the **delivery port**.
6. A small quantity of fluid recirculates back via the **sealing clearances**, reducing efficiency slightly.

**Suitability for Bilge Service:** Centrifugal pumps can pass small solids and fibrous matter, making them ideal for bilge water contaminated with debris.

#### (b) Advantages, Disadvantages, and Performance — (7 Marks)

**Advantages:**

1. Simple construction with no valves, pistons, or gears.
2. Smooth, continuous, non-pulsating flow.
3. Can handle dirty water with suspended solids (open impeller design).
4. Compact and lightweight for given capacity.
5. Direct electric motor drive — easy maintenance.
6. High flow rates achievable.

**Disadvantages:**

1. Cannot develop very high pressures (limited head).
2. Requires priming (self-priming types overcome this).
3. Performance drops with increasing viscosity.
4. Sensitive to air entrainment — air locks reduce flow.
5. High starting current on motor.

**Performance Characteristics (for KTU):**

The **Head–Flow (H–Q) curve** of a centrifugal pump is **downward sloping** — as flow increases, head decreases. The **Power–Flow** curve rises with flow. Efficiency peaks at a specific **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

**Pump Laws (Affinity Laws):**

$$Q \propto N, \quad H \propto N^{2}, \quad P \propto N^{3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **centrifugal pumps with positive displacement pumps**. Remember — centrifugal pumps move fluid by **imparting kinetic energy** (rotodynamic), while reciprocating pumps move fluid by **mechanical displacement** of a piston. In KTU valuation, wrong classification costs **2 marks instantly**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Explain the steering gear system of a merchant ship. With a block diagram, describe the working of an electro-hydraulic steering gear. State SOLAS requirements for steering gear.**

#### (a) Steering Gear System Overview — (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Function:** The steering gear converts the **rudder command** from the bridge into the **mechanical rotation of the rudder stock**, producing a transverse force that yaws the ship.

**Main Components:**

1. **Bridge Control Unit** — Steering wheel, telegraph lever, or follow-up control.
2. **Rudder Angle Indicator** — Shows actual rudder position.
3. **Servo System** — Compares ordered vs actual rudder angle, generates error signal.
4. **Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU)** — Motor-driven pumps supplying pressurized oil.
5. **Hydraulic Actuators (Rams)** — Push the tiller arm to rotate the rudder stock.
6. **Rudder Stock & Rudder** — The actual rotating blade in the water.

**Types of Steering Gear:**

1. **Ram-type (Hydraulic)** — Most common. Two or four hydraulic cylinders push the tiller. Very high thrust capacity.
2. **Rotary Vane Type** — A vane rotates inside a sealed housing carrying hydraulic oil. Used on smaller vessels.
3. **Electro-mechanical** — Electric motor drives gear train to rotate rudder stock. Used on smaller ships and as emergency gear.

#### (b) Working of Electro-Hydraulic Steering Gear with Block Diagram — (7 Marks)

**Working Sequence:**

1. **Order Given:** The bridge officer moves the steering wheel by a small angle. The follow-up transmitter generates an electrical signal proportional to the ordered rudder angle.
2. **Error Detection:** The follow-up receiver compares the ordered angle with the actual rudder angle from the feedback transmitter mounted on the rudder stock. The difference is the **error signal**.
3. **Servo Amplification:** The error signal is amplified and sent to the **solenoid-operated directional control valve** in the HPU.
4. **Hydraulic Actuation:** The valve directs high-pressure oil (typically 200 bar) to one side of the hydraulic rams and vents the other side.
5. **Mechanical Movement:** The piston in the ram extends or retracts, pushing the **tiller arm** which rotates the rudder stock.
6. **Feedback:** As the rudder rotates, the feedback transmitter sends signals back to the receiver. When the actual angle equals the ordered angle, the error becomes zero and the valve returns to neutral, locking the rudder.
7. **Continuous Control:** The process is continuous, allowing precise course-keeping.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Bridge Wheel] → [Order Transmitter] → [Follow-up Receiver] 
        → [Error Signal] → [Solenoid Valve] → [HPU Pumps] 
        → [High Pressure Oil] → [Rams] → [Tiller/Rudder] 
        → [Feedback Transmitter] → back to Receiver
```

**SOLAS Requirements:**

- **Two independent power units** (each capable of meeting required performance) for ships > 10,000 GT.
- Time: **28 seconds** from 35° one side to 30° other side at service speed.
- **Emergency steering gear** in a separate location with communication to bridge.
- **Steering gear fluid reservoir**, low-level alarms, and power-failure alarms.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students write only about the **mechanical parts** and forget to mention the **follow-up control loop**. In electro-hydraulic steering, the **follow-up mechanism is the heart of the system** — without it, the rudder overshoots and oscillates. Missing this concept costs **3–4 marks** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Auxiliary Machinery**

- **Auxiliaries ≠ Main Engine.** Main engine produces shaft power; auxiliaries support all other functions.
- **Centrifugal vs Reciprocating Pumps:** Centrifugal = kinetic energy, high flow low head. Reciprocating = positive displacement, high pressure low flow.
- **SOLAS Bilge Capacity:** $Q \geq 0.005 \cdot L \cdot B$ in m³/h.
- **Steering gear time limit:** 28 seconds hard-over-to-hard-over.
- **Pump Hydraulic Power:** $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$.
- **LO Purifier speed:** 6,000–8,000 rpm — separates water & sludge.
- **Heat Exchanger Duty:** $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$.
- **Air Compressor working pressure:** 30 bar (starting), 7 bar (control).
- **Specific Speed $N_s$** determines pump type: low $N_s$ = centrifugal, high = axial.
- **Fire pump** must have independent diesel-driven backup.
- **Boilers** generate steam for fuel heating, accommodation, and turbines.
- **Exhaust gas economizer** uses waste heat from main engine.
- **Class rules** require standby units for ALL critical auxiliaries.
- **Decks auxiliaries** include: windlass, capstan, winches, cranes, hatch covers.
- **Hull auxiliaries** include: bilge, ballast, fire, oily water separator.
- **Electrical auxiliaries** include: diesel generators, emergency generator, switchboard.
- **Hotel services** include: fresh water generator, AC, refrigeration, sewage plant.
- **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- **Emergency generator** must auto-start within **30 seconds** of power failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When a question asks *"Explain any auxiliary"*, structure your answer in this order for maximum marks:  
> **Definition → Function → Construction → Working Principle → Diagram → Advantages/Disadvantages → SOLAS/Class requirements.** This 7-step structure is what the KTU Board examiner's marking scheme rewards.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding in Shipbuilding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Welding in Shipbuilding # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Welding in Shipbuilding (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):** > Welding is a **fusion joining process** in which two or more pieces of shipbuilding-grade steel are made to coalesce (unite) by heating them to a suitable temperature, with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of filler metal, to form a permanent metallurgical bond that is at least as strong as the parent metal. In modern merchant shipbuilding, **approximately 90% of all joints** in a vessel's hull are made by welding rather than riveting. The use of welding has enabled **lighter, stronger, and more watertight hulls**, which directly translates to higher cargo capacity, lower fuel consumption, and improved vessel speed. > [!NOTE] > **Historical Note (KTU Context):** Before the 1940s, ships were predominantly **riveted**. The catastrophic failure of Liberty Ships during World War II (notably the *SS Schenectady* which split in half at the dock) accelerated the transition to all-welded construction and brought about rigorous welding standards enforced today by classification societies such as **ABS, LR, DNV, BV, IRS, and IACS**. ## 1.2 The Seven Pillars of Shipbuilding Welding For a KTU Board Examiner, the topic of shipbuilding welding revolves around **seven fundamental concepts** that must be mastered: 1. **Welding Processes** — The methods used to fuse the steel (SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW, GTAW, ESW, EGW). 2. **Weld Types & Joint Geometry** — Butt, Fillet, Lap, Tee, Edge, Corner joints. 3. **Edge Preparation & Groove Design** — Square, V, X, U, J, bevel preparations. 4. **Welding Positions** — 1G/2G/3G/4G/1F/2F/3F/4F (plate) and 1G/2G/5G/6G (pipe). 5. **Weld Symbols & Standards** — AWS A2.4 / ISO 2553 conventions. 6. **Welding Defects & Inspection** — NDT methods (VT, RT, UT, MPT, DPT). 7. **Classification Society Rules** — IACS UR W series (Unified Requirements for Welding). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Stitching Steel" Analogy:** > Imagine you are making a heavy-duty **denim jeans** from thick canvas, but instead of using thread and needle, you use a **hot iron that melts the fibers together** along a seam. That is exactly what welding does to steel plates in a ship. The "thread" is the **weld bead**, the "needle" is the **welding torch/electrode**, and the "denim" is the **ship plate**. The seam must be at least as strong as the fabric itself — otherwise the jeans (the ship) will tear at the joint when stressed by the wearer's movements (the sea's loads). A second analogy: **welding is to a shipbuilder what stapling is to a cardboard box maker** — it is the *primary joining technology* that holds the structure together. A poorly welded ship is as dangerous as a stapled cardboard box trying to hold water. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Shipbuilding Welding Metrics (Memorize for KTU 2024):** > - **Melting point of mild ship steel:** approximately **1500 °C** > - **Preheat temperature range:** **75 °C to 200 °C** (depends on CE — Carbon Equivalent) > - **Interpass temperature maximum:** **250 °C** (typical for Grade A/DH/EH ship steel) > - **Standard ship plate thickness range:** **4 mm to 100 mm** > - **Common shipbuilding steel grades:** ASTM A131 Grade A, B, D, E, AH32, DH36, EH36, FH36 > - **Standard test plate thickness for procedure qualification:** **20 mm** > - **Welding productivity target:** **1.5 to 2.5 metres of fillet weld per hour per welder** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Welding Processes Used in Shipbuilding Shipbuilding relies almost exclusively on **arc welding processes** because of the high productivity and deep penetration they offer. Below is the KTU-relevant hierarchy: ### 2.1.1 Primary Arc Welding Processes **(a) Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) — "Stick Welding"** - Uses a **flux-coated consumable electrode**. - Most common on shipyards for **out-of-position work** (vertical, overhead). - **Advantages:** Simple, portable, low cost, works in wind/field conditions. - **Disadvantages:** Slag removal required, lower deposition rate (~1.5 kg/h). - **Shipyard application:** Hull internal stiffeners, brackets, small-diameter pipe. **(b) Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) — "MIG/MAG Welding"** - Uses a **continuous solid wire** and an external shielding gas (CO₂ or Ar/CO₂ mix). - **High deposition rate** (3–6 kg/h); preferred for **long fillet welds on flat panels**. - **Shipyard application:** Panel line welding in sub-block construction, CO₂-shielded for mild steel. **(c) Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)** - Uses a **tubular electrode filled with flux**; can be self-shielded (FCAW-S) or gas-shielded (FCAW-G). - **High deposition (4–8 kg/h)**, deep penetration, suitable for thick plates. - **Shipyard application:** Major hull seams, double-bottom plates, sheer strake. **(d) Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** - Arc is **submerged under a granular flux**; no visible arc, no spatter. - **Highest deposition rate (8–15 kg/h)**; excellent for long straight seams in **1G/1F position**. - **Shipyard application:** Flat panel seams, longitudinal stiffener welds, bulkhead panel fabrication. **(e) Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) — "TIG Welding"** - Uses a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** with separate filler wire. - **Highest quality welds**; used for stainless steel piping, copper-nickel, aluminium superstructures. - **Shipyard application:** Stainless exhaust lines, LNG fuel piping, non-ferrous metals. ### 2.1.2 Special / Mechanized Processes **(f) Electroslag Welding (ESW)** - Used for **very thick plates (50–400 mm)** in a vertical position in a single pass. - **Shipyard application:** Large stem and stern post castings, thick plate panel butt joints. **(g) Electrogas Welding (EGW)** - Similar to ESW but uses an arc, with gas shielding. - **Shipyard application:** Vertical box girder seams in superstructures. ## 2.2 Weld Types and Joint Geometry > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** A student must be able to **draw and name all five basic weld types** in a KTU exam. | Weld Type | Sketch Description | Primary Shipyard Use | |-----------|--------------------|----------------------| | **Butt Weld (Square/V/X)** | Two plates joined edge-to-edge in the same plane | Hull shell plate seams, deck plate seams | | **Fillet Weld** | Triangular cross-section weld in a corner joint | Stiffener-to-plate, frame-to-shell | | **T-Joint (Fillet or Butt)** | One plate perpendicular to another | Bulkhead-to-deck intersections | | **Lap Joint** | Two plates overlapping each other | Less common — used in small brackets | | **Edge Flange Weld** | Edge of one plate flanged and welded to the other | Lightweight partitions, mast sections | ## 2.3 Welding Position Codes (AWS / ISO 2553) > [!NOTE] > **The "G" vs "F" Rule (Memorize for 2-mark questions):** > - **"G" = Groove welds** (mostly butt welds). > - **"F" = Fillet welds.** > - **The number 1, 2, 3, 4** indicates the welding position (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead). | Code | Position | Weld Type | Typical Use in Shipbuilding | |------|----------|-----------|----------------------------| | **1G / 1F** | Flat | Groove / Fillet | Panel line welding on horizontal tables | | **2G / 2F** | Horizontal | Groove / Fillet | Side shell seams, vertical panel seams | | **3G / 3F** | Vertical | Groove / Fillet | Bulkhead-to-deck, vertical butt joints | | **4G / 4F** | Overhead | Groove / Fillet | Overhead stiffener welds, overhead brackets | | **5G** | Pipe fixed, multiple positions | Groove | Pipe spool welding | | **6G** | Pipe fixed, 45° inclined | Groove | All-position pipe qualification (hardest) | ## 2.4 Edge Preparation (Groove Geometry) > [!NOTE] > **Why edge preparation is critical (KTU 2024):** Plates thicker than **6 mm** for butt joints must be bevelled to ensure **full penetration**. The choice of groove depends on plate thickness, welding process, and position. | Groove Type | Plate Thickness Range | Typical Process | |-------------|------------------------|-----------------| | **Square Butt** | Up to 6 mm | SMAW, GTAW | | **Single V** | 6–25 mm | SMAW, GMAW, FCAW | | **Single U / J** | > 25 mm (costly prep, less filler) | SAW | | **Double V (X)** | 20–50 mm (balanced heat input) | SMAW, GMAW | | **Double U** | > 50 mm | SAW / ESW | **Standard groove angle:** 60° ± 5° (single V/X). **Root face / land:** 1.5–2 mm. **Root gap:** 2–3 mm. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Welding in Shipbuilding > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Cheat Sheet — Print and Memorize:** | # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|-----------|---------|----------------|-------| | 1 | **Heat Input** | $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000}$ | V=arc voltage, I=current, v=travel speed | **kJ/mm** | | 2 | **Effective Throat (45° fillet)** | $t_{e} = 0.707 \times s$ | s = leg size | mm | | 3 | **Fillet Weld Shear Capacity** | $P = 0.707 \times s \times L_{e} \times \tau_{allow}$ | L_e = effective length, τ = allowable shear | N | | 4 | **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)** | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Alloying contents in % | dimensionless | | 5 | **Weld Metal Dilution** | $D = \dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100$ | A = cross-sectional area | % | | 6 | **Preheat Temperature (Sumiyoshi)** | $T_{p} = 700 \times CE - 300$ (approx., °C) | Empirical formula | °C | | 7 | **Welding Speed (deposition)** | $v = \dfrac{60 \times A \times d}{I \times 1000}$ | d = wire feed speed | mm/min | | 8 | **Thermal Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{H_{actual}}{H_{gross}}$ | η for SAW ≈ 1.0, GMAW ≈ 0.8, SMAW ≈ 0.8 | ratio | | 9 | **Plate Bending Distortion** | $\delta = \dfrac{0.5 \times H \times t}{L^{2}}$ (simplified) | t = thickness, L = plate length | mm | | 10 | **WPS Heat Input Limit (IACS UR W11)** | $H_{max} \leq 60$ kJ/mm for EH36 | Higher → lower toughness | kJ/mm | ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in actual shipyards:** Every modern shipyard — from Hyundai Heavy Industries (Korea) and Daewoo (Korea) to Cochin Shipyard (India) — enforces these formulas in their **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** documents. The classification society surveyor signs off on each WPS before the welder is allowed to lay a single bead. The heat input formula in particular is **THE most watched parameter** because excessive heat input causes **coarse grains, reduced toughness, and brittle fractures** — the very failures that sank the Liberty Ships in the 1940s. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input (Most Important KTU Formula) The **heat input** is defined as the **arc energy delivered per unit length of weld**. The derivation follows directly from electrical power principles. ### Step 1 — Electrical Power of the Arc The arc behaves as an electrical load with voltage $V$ (Volts) and current $I$ (Amperes). The instantaneous electrical power consumed by the arc is given by: $$P_{arc} = V \times I$$ where $P_{arc}$ is in **Watts (W)**, $V$ is arc voltage in **Volts**, and $I$ is welding current in **Amperes**. ### Step 2 — Total Energy per Unit Length The welding torch moves along the joint at a travel speed $v$ measured in **mm/min**. The time taken to lay **1 mm of weld** is therefore: $$t = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{v \text{ mm/min}} = \dfrac{1}{v} \text{ minutes}$$ $$t = \dfrac{60}{v} \text{ seconds}$$ ### Step 3 — Energy per mm of Weld Total energy delivered per mm of weld (before thermal losses) is: $$E_{gross} = P_{arc} \times t = V \times I \times \dfrac{60}{v} \text{ Joules per mm}$$ ### Step 4 — Accounting for Thermal Efficiency Not all electrical energy reaches the weld — some is lost as radiation, spatter, and convection. The thermal efficiency $\eta$ corrects this: $$E_{net} = \eta \times V \times I \times \dfrac{60}{v}$$ ### Step 5 — Converting to kJ/mm Dividing Joules by 1000 to get kilojoules: $$H = \dfrac{\eta \times V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000}$$ For simplicity, taking $\eta = 1$ (which is the convention used by IACS UR W11 for reporting limits): $$H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000} \text{ kJ/mm}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the KTU 2024 textbook heat input equation.** Always state the units explicitly: **kJ/mm** (not J/mm, not kJ/m). ### Step 3.1.1 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A fillet weld is laid using GMAW with arc voltage 28 V, current 280 A, and travel speed 360 mm/min. The thermal efficiency is 0.85. Calculate the heat input. **Solution — Step by Step:** Given: $V = 28$ V, $I = 280$ A, $v = 360$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.85$. Step 1 — Substitute into the heat input equation: $$H = \dfrac{\eta \times V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000}$$ Step 2 — Insert numerical values: $$H = \dfrac{0.85 \times 28 \times 280 \times 60}{360 \times 1000}$$ Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator: $$H = \dfrac{0.85 \times 28 \times 280 \times 60}{360000}$$ Step 4 — Evaluate numerator piece by piece: - $28 \times 280 = 7840$ - $7840 \times 60 = 470400$ - $0.85 \times 470400 = 399840$ Step 5 — Divide by denominator: $$H = \dfrac{399840}{360000} = 1.1107 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ **Answer: $H \approx 1.11$ kJ/mm** — well within the IACS UR W11 limit of 60 kJ/mm. ✅ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Strength of a Fillet Weld Fillet welds are the **most common weld in a ship's hull** (used at every stiffener-to-plate joint). Their design strength is calculated as follows. ### Step 1 — Geometry of the Fillet Weld A standard 90° fillet weld is **isosceles right-angled** in cross-section, with two legs of length $s$ (the leg size, in mm). The hypotenuse — the **theoretical throat** — has length $\sqrt{s^2 + s^2}$. ### Step 2 — Effective Throat Thickness The throat is the **shortest distance from the root of the weld to the face**, which is the line along which the weld is assumed to fail in shear. For a 45° fillet: $$t_{e} = s \times \cos(45°) = s \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \times s$$ ### Step 3 — Shear Area For a fillet weld of length $L$ (mm), the effective shear area resisting failure is: $$A_{s} = t_{e} \times L_{e} = 0.707 \times s \times L_{e}$$ where $L_{e}$ is the **effective length** = total length minus 2 × leg size (deductions for start/stop craters). $$L_{e} = L_{total} - 2s$$ ### Step 4 — Allowable Shear Capacity The load capacity of the fillet weld in shear is then: $$P_{allow} = \tau_{allow} \times A_{s}$$ $$P_{allow} = 0.707 \times s \times L_{e} \times \tau_{allow}$$ where $\tau_{allow}$ is the **allowable shear stress in the weld throat** (typically 110 N/mm² for E-grade weld electrodes on Grade A ship steel). ### Step 3.2.1 — Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A 200 mm-long double fillet weld with leg size 8 mm is to carry a tensile load. If the allowable shear stress in the weld is 110 N/mm², find the safe load capacity. **Solution:** Given: $s = 8$ mm, $L = 200$ mm, $\tau = 110$ N/mm². Step 1 — Effective length: $$L_{e} = L - 2s = 200 - 2(8) = 200 - 16 = 184 \text{ mm}$$ Step 2 — Effective throat: $$t_{e} = 0.707 \times s = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.656 \text{ mm}$$ Step 3 — Shear area: $$A_{s} = t_{e} \times L_{e} = 5.656 \times 184 = 1040.7 \text{ mm}^2$$ Step 4 — Load capacity: $$P_{allow} = \tau \times A_{s} = 110 \times 1040.7 = 114477 \text{ N}$$ $$P_{allow} \approx 114.5 \text{ kN}$$ **Answer: The fillet weld can safely carry ~114.5 kN.** ✅ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Carbon Equivalent (IIW Formula) The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** determines whether a steel needs **preheating** before welding. Higher CE means **higher cold-cracking susceptibility**. ### Step 1 — Why CE Exists Pure iron does not crack on welding. Each alloying element (C, Mn, Cr, Mo, V, Ni, Cu) raises the **hardenability** of the heat-affected zone (HAZ). The CE equation weighs each element by its hardenability contribution relative to carbon. ### Step 2 — The IIW Formula (Adopted by IACS UR W11) $$CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$ where each element is the **weight percentage** from the **ladle / product analysis**. ### Step 3 — Interpretation - **CE < 0.40** → Low carbon steel → **No preheating required** (e.g., Grade A ship steel). - **0.40 ≤ CE < 0.60** → Medium CE → **Preheat to 100–150 °C**. - **CE ≥ 0.60** → High CE → **Preheat to 200 °C + PWHT** (e.g., EH36 may fall in this range). ### Step 3.3.1 — Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A Grade AH32 ship plate has the following ladle analysis: C = 0.16%, Mn = 1.30%, Cr = 0.10%, Mo = 0.02%, V = 0.005%, Ni = 0.15%, Cu = 0.20%. Calculate the CE and recommend preheat. **Solution:** Step 1 — Substitute into IIW formula: $$CE = 0.16 + \dfrac{1.30}{6} + \dfrac{0.10 + 0.02 + 0.005}{5} + \dfrac{0.15 + 0.20}{15}$$ Step 2 — Evaluate each term: - Carbon term: $0.16$ - Mn term: $1.30 / 6 = 0.2167$ - (Cr+Mo+V) term: $0.125 / 5 = 0.025$ - (Ni+Cu) term: $0.35 / 15 = 0.0233$ Step 3 — Sum: $$CE = 0.16 + 0.2167 + 0.025 + 0.0233 = 0.425$$ **Answer: CE = 0.425 → Preheat to ~100–125 °C is recommended.** ✅ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Welding Defects in Shipbuilding > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — This matrix is the most tested content in Module 3.** | Defect | Type | Cause | Detection Method | Remedy | |--------|------|-------|------------------|--------| | **Porosity** | Cavity | Contaminants, moisture in electrode, shielding gas failure | RT, UT | Improve cleaning, dry electrodes | | **Slag Inclusion** | Solid | Inadequate slag removal between passes | RT, UT | Grind out, re-weld | | **Undercut** | Groove | Excessive current, wrong angle | VT, DPT | Grind and re-pass | | **Lack of Fusion** | Bond | Insufficient heat input, fast travel | RT, UT | Re-weld with higher H | | **Lack of Penetration** | Depth | Low current, wrong root gap | RT, UT | Back-gouge and re-weld | | **Crack (Hot)** | Crack | High H, wrong filler | VT, RT, MPT | Grind out, repair procedure | | **Crack (Cold / HAZ)** | Crack | High CE, hydrogen, no preheat | UT, MPT | Preheat, low-hydrogen electrodes | | **Distortion** | Geometric | Shrinkage from cooling | VT | Use strong-back, back-step sequence | | **Overlap** | Excess | Too slow, too low current | VT | Grind flush | | **Spatter** | Surface | Long arc, gas failure | VT | Wire brush, anti-spatter spray | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchy of Welding Processes Used in Shipbuilding ```mermaid graph TD rootA["WELDING PROCESSES
