Mermaid flowcharts are an excellent way to demonstrate the **sequence of welding operations** in a modern shipyard's panel line, which is a frequently asked question in the 14-mark essay section. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Note to Students (KTU 2024):** Part A carries **3 marks** and Part B carries **14 marks** (with internal choice). Marks are awarded for **neat sketches, labelled diagrams, and step-by-step derivations** — not just final answers. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **"List the principal types of welding used in modern shipbuilding. Why is welding preferred over riveting for hull construction?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The principal types of welding used in modern shipbuilding are: 1. **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** — Manual stick welding for out-of-position work. 2. **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG-MAG)** — Semi-automatic for high-deposition panel welding. 3. **Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)** — For thick plates and field joints. 4. **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** — Mechanized, highest deposition for flat seams. 5. **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)** — For stainless steel and non-ferrous metals. 6. **Electroslag Welding (ESW)** — For very thick plates in the vertical position. **Why welding is preferred over riveting (2 supporting points):** - **Welding is lighter** — eliminates rivet holes, overlapping plates, and rivet heads, reducing hull weight by 10–15%. - **Welding is watertight** — no gaps for water ingress, allowing oil tankers and bulk carriers to carry liquid cargo. - **Welding is faster** — mechanized SAW can deposit up to 15 kg/h per arc, vastly outpacing riveting. - **Welding is stronger** — monolithic joint has uniform stress distribution. --- ### Question A2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Define heat input. What is the significance of controlling heat input in shipbuilding welding?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Heat input is the **arc energy delivered per unit length of weld**, expressed in **kJ/mm**. It is given by: $$H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000} \text{ kJ/mm}$$ where $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = welding current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/min). **Significance of controlling heat input (1.5 marks):** - **Excessive heat input** → coarse grains in the HAZ, reduced toughness, susceptibility to brittle fracture (this was the cause of the Liberty Ship failures in WWII). - **Insufficient heat input** → lack of fusion, lack of penetration, slag inclusion, weak joints. - Classification societies (IACS UR W11) impose a **maximum heat input of 60 kJ/mm** for high-tensile grades (AH32, DH36, EH36) to maintain Charpy impact toughness at –20 °C or –40 °C. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Skipping a sketch loses 2 marks.** Always include a **labelled diagram** of the weld/joint/process. > - **Not writing units** in numerical problems → **½ mark deducted per instance**. > - **Mentioning welding processes without specifying the shipyard application** → only 50% credit for that section. > - **Using layman terms** like "iron joint" instead of "weld joint" or "fillet" → examiners downgrade by one cognitive level. > - **Forgetting to mention the AWS/ISO code (e.g., 3G position)** → at least 1 mark lost in questions on positions. --- ### **Question B-A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — July 2024]** > **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **five basic types of welds and joints** used in shipbuilding. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the **edge preparation** of plates for butt welding. How does plate thickness influence the choice of groove design? **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** The five basic types of welds and joints in shipbuilding are: **1. Butt Joint (Square / V / X Groove):** - Two plates are placed edge-to-edge in the same plane and welded. - For plates ≤ 6 mm, a square butt is sufficient. - For thicker plates, single-V, double-V (X), single-U, or double-U grooves are used. - **Shipyard use:** Hull shell plate seams, deck plating, longitudinal bulkhead seams. **2. Tee Joint (Fillet / Full Penetration Butt):** - One plate is placed perpendicular to another, forming a "T" shape. - Welded either with a **fillet weld** (most common, partial strength) or a **full-penetration butt weld** (high strength). - **Shipyard use:** Floor-to-bottom plating, transverse bulkhead-to-shell, deck-beam-to-deck-plate. **3. Corner Joint:** - Two plates meet at a 90° edge to form an "L" or box section. - Welded on the outside (or both inside and outside) with fillet or butt welds. - **Shipyard use:** Hatch coaming, mast sections, built-up stiffener sections. **4. Lap Joint:** - Two plates overlap each other and are joined by **fillet welds** on both edges. - Less common in primary hull structure due to stress concentration, but used in brackets and non-critical parts. **5. Edge Joint:** - Two plates are placed with their edges parallel and close together (a few mm gap), welded along the edges. - **Shipyard use:** Light bulkheads, mast and derrick posts, ventilation ducting. (Include **labelled sketches** of each joint — minimum 1 mark per correct sketch.) #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Definition:** Edge preparation is the process of machining a **bevel or groove** along the edge of a plate to be welded, to ensure **full penetration** of the weld metal into the joint root. **Common edge preparation types:** 1. **Square edge (no preparation):** Used for plates ≤ 6 mm. The plates are butted together with a 2–3 mm root gap and welded in a single pass from one side. 2. **Single-V preparation:** The plate edge is bevelled on one side to form a "V" with an included angle of 60° ± 5°. Used for plates 6–25 mm thick. Welded from the bevelled side; the root may be back-gouged and welded from the back. 3. **Double-V (X) preparation:** Both edges are bevelled to form an "X". Used for plates 20–50 mm. Distributes heat input more evenly and reduces angular distortion. 4. **Single-U preparation:** A "U"-shaped groove is machined (or plasma-arc gouged) on one side. Used for plates > 25 mm. Requires less filler metal than a V-groove. 5. **Double-U preparation:** Two "U" grooves, one on each side. Used for very thick plates (> 50 mm) — used in the construction of bulk carriers and tankers. **Influence of plate thickness on groove design:** | Plate Thickness | Recommended Groove | Reason | |-----------------|--------------------|--------| | ≤ 6 mm | Square butt | Single-pass weld achieves full penetration | | 6–12 mm | Single-V | Standard groove, economical filler use | | 12–25 mm | Single-V (wide) or Double-V | To access the root for full penetration | | 25–50 mm | Double-V or Single-U | Reduces filler metal; balanced heat input | | > 50 mm | Double-U or ESW | Mechanized welding, minimum passes | **Conclusion:** As plate thickness increases, groove design progresses from simple **square → V → X → U**, balancing the need for full penetration, low filler metal consumption, and minimal heat-induced distortion. --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Explain the **various welding positions** used in ship construction with the help of AWS/ISO position codes. Mention the **most and least difficult** positions to weld. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the **non-destructive testing (NDT) methods** used to inspect welds in a ship's hull. Compare any two methods. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Welding positions** are defined by **AWS A2.4 / ISO 2553** codes. The "G" suffix denotes **groove welds** (butt joints) and "F" denotes **fillet welds**. The numerical code (1, 2, 3, 4) indicates the position of the workpiece. **Plate welding positions:** | Code | Position | Description | Difficulty | |------|----------|-------------|------------| | **1G / 1F** | Flat | Plate horizontal; weld on top | Easiest | | **2G / 2F** | Horizontal | Plate vertical; weld on horizontal line | Easy | | **3G / 3F** | Vertical | Plate vertical; weld travels vertically | Moderate | | **4G / 4F** | Overhead | Plate horizontal; weld on underside | Hard | **Pipe welding positions:** | Code | Position | Description | Difficulty | |------|----------|-------------|------------| | **1G** | Pipe horizontal, rollable | Easy | Easy | | **2G** | Pipe vertical, fixed | Moderate | Moderate | | **5G** | Pipe horizontal, fixed, multiple positions | Hard | Hard | | **6G** | Pipe inclined at 45°, fixed, all positions | **Hardest** | **Hardest** | **Most difficult position:** **6G (45° inclined pipe, all positions)** — it requires the welder to be qualified in flat, horizontal, vertical, and overhead welding in a single test. **This is the gold standard for welder qualification** in shipyards. **Easiest position:** **1G / 1F (flat)** — gravity assists the molten metal, allowing high deposition rates and the use of mechanized processes like SAW. #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Methods for Hull Welds:** 1. **Visual Testing (VT):** The most basic inspection; uses the naked eye or magnifying glasses, weld gauges, and dial gauges. Done before and after each pass. Detects surface defects like undercut, overlap, and visible cracks. 2. **Radiographic Testing (RT):** Uses **X-rays or gamma rays (Ir-192, Co-60)** to produce a film image of internal weld defects. Detects porosity, slag, lack of fusion, root cracks, and tungsten inclusions. Mandatory for **butt welds in high-strength steel** (IACS UR Z17). 3. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT):** Uses high-frequency sound waves (1–10 MHz) transmitted through the weld via a probe. Detects planar defects (cracks, lack of fusion) with high sensitivity. Faster than RT and can be done on-site. 4. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT):** The part is magnetized; magnetic particles reveal surface and near-surface defects. Limited to **ferromagnetic materials** (i.e., not for austenitic stainless steel). 5. **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT / LPI):** A red or fluorescent dye is applied to the cleaned surface, penetrates surface cracks, and is revealed by a developer. Detects only **surface-breaking defects**. 6. **Eddy Current Testing (ECT):** Uses electromagnetic induction. Mainly used for non-ferrous metals (aluminium superstructures, copper-nickel pipework). **Comparison: RT vs UT** | Parameter | Radiographic Testing (RT) | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | |-----------|---------------------------|-------------------------| | **Principle** | X-rays / gamma rays pass through the weld | Sound waves reflected from defect | | **Defect type** | Volumetric (porosity, slag) — best for these | Planar (cracks, lack of fusion) — best for these | | **Output** | Permanent film record (radiograph) | Real-time screen display + digital record | | **Speed** | Slower (film processing) | Faster (real-time) | | **Cost** | Higher (film, chemicals, radiation safety) | Lower (no film, no radiation) | | **Safety** | Radiation hazard — strict controls | Safe (no radiation) | | **Surface prep** | Minimal | Requires smooth surface, couplant gel | | **Use in shipbuilding** | Mandatory for butt welds in EH-grade steels | Widely used for fillet welds, field joints | --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Pre-Exam Quick Revision (Last-Minute Checklist):** - [ ] **Heat input formula** $H = (V \times I \times 60) / (v \times 1000)$ — memorize with units (kJ/mm). - [ ] **Maximum heat input for EH36** = **60 kJ/mm** (per IACS UR W11). - [ ] **Five basic joint types** — Butt, Tee, Corner, Lap, Edge. - [ ] **AWS/ISO position codes** — 1G to 4G (plate), 5G & 6G (pipe). **6G is the toughest.** - [ ] **Fillet weld throat = 0.707 × leg size** — derive in every numerical. - [ ] **Carbon Equivalent (IIW) formula** — CE < 0.40 → no preheat; CE > 0.60 → mandatory preheat + PWHT. - [ ] **Process hierarchy in shipyards** — SMAW (out-of-position) + GMAW/FCAW (panel line) + SAW (long flat seams) + GTAW (pipe/stainless). - [ ] **NDT methods** — VT, RT, UT, MPT, DPT, ECT; **RT is best for porosity/slag, UT is best for cracks/lack of fusion**. - [ ] **Edge preparation progression** — Square → V → X → U → Double U as plate thickness increases. - [ ] **Liberty Ship failure** — remember to cite it in any essay on weld defects; it is the **most-mentioned case study** in KTU 2024 shipbuilding modules. - [ ] **IACS = International Association of Classification Societies** — the governing body for unified welding rules. - [ ] **Always include a labelled sketch** in any 7-mark sub-question; **marks are explicitly reserved for diagrams**. - [ ] **Mention classification society approval** (ABS/LR/DNV/BV/IRS) in WPS-related answers — it adds +1 to the cognitive level in valuation. - [ ] **Differentiate between welding processes** by giving the **specific shipyard application** for each — generic answers get only 50% credit. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** For the 14-mark question, structure your answer in **three blocks:** (1) definition + sketch, (2) derivation / step-by-step procedure, (3) shipyard application + classification society rule. Examiners award marks block-by-block, and this structure ensures you don't miss any marking layer.

topics.title: Erection of Hull and Launching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Erection of Hull and Launching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Hull Erection?

**Hull Erection** is the critical shipbuilding phase in which all the prefabricated structural components (plates, frames, stiffeners, brackets, and sub-assemblies) are progressively joined, aligned, and welded together on the building berth to form the complete hull structure of the vessel.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, hull erection is defined as:

> The *systematic assembly operation* by which the ship's hull is transformed from a collection of fabricated parts into a single, watertight, structurally continuous body, ready to be launched into the water.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Skeleton and the Skin" 🏗️🚢

Imagine you are **assembling a giant tin-can robot**:
- First, you build the **skeleton** (frames and transverse webs) — like a ribcage.
- Then you wrap the **skin** (shell plating) over it — like tin sheets on a model airplane.
- Finally, you **weld/fasten** everything so the structure becomes a single rigid object.

Hull erection is *exactly this process*, only at a massive industrial scale. The "skeleton" is erected first on the slipway, the "skin" is fitted next, and the whole structure is then welded, tested, and prepared for launching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hull erection is the *bridge between the fabrication shop and the launch event*. Everything that happens in Module 4 (erection) directly determines whether Module 5 (launching) is safe and successful.

---

## 1.2 What is Ship Launching?

**Ship Launching** is the carefully engineered operation of transferring a newly constructed ship from the land-based building berth/slipway into the water, so that it becomes a free-floating vessel for the first time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Definition (KTU Board Examiner's Wording):**
> *Launching is the controlled transition of the hull from a static, land-supported condition to a dynamically buoyant, water-borne condition, achieved through a combination of gravity, mechanical release devices, and hydrostatic support systems.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Baby's First Bath" 👶🛁

Think of a **mother lowering a baby into a bathtub**:
- The baby is heavy, but the mother supports it carefully.
- At some point, the **buoyancy of the water** starts helping.
- The mother must **let go at exactly the right moment**, neither too early (baby falls) nor too late (awkward entry).
- If the water "slaps" too hard, it can hurt the baby.

**Ship launching is identical**:
- The ship is heavy (hundreds to thousands of tonnes).
- The slipway is the "mother's hands" (gravity-assisted slide).
- Water entry creates a huge **hydrodynamic slap load**.
- **Triggers** are the "let-go" mechanism.
- A failed launch = a maritime disaster (e.g., the *MV Princess Victoria*, 1952).

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-World Risk:** A poorly executed launch can crack the hull, capsize the ship, or even kill workers. Hence KTU expects students to understand **both** the *mechanics* and the *safety philosophy* behind it.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of static friction (steel on wood, greased) | $\mu_s \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$ | dimensionless | Used for trigger release force |
| Coefficient of kinetic friction (sliding ways greased) | $\mu_k \approx 0.03$ to $0.06$ | dimensionless | Used for post-release motion |
| Typical launchway slope | $\frac{1}{8}$ to $\frac{1}{24}$ | ratio | Common KTU exam value: $\frac{1}{12}$ or $\frac{1}{20}$ |
| Launching velocity at water entry | 5 to 12 | m/s | Depends on ship weight and slope |
| Trigger release angle (dog-shore angle) | 12° to 18° | degrees | Determines release timing |
| Standard density of seawater | **1025 kg/m³** | kg/m³ | Used in buoyancy calculations |
| Standard density of freshwater | **1000 kg/m³** | kg/m³ | Used in dock launching |

---

## 1.4 Why Are Erection and Launching Treated as One Module?

In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, these two operations are grouped because:

1. **Sequential dependency** — Erection is the *preparation*; launching is the *execution*.
2. **Shared structural planning** — The same hull girder must withstand both erection stresses and launch-induced bending moments.
3. **Same workforce and site** — Both happen on the building berth / slipway.
4. **Common safety engineering** — Stability, alignment, and load control are critical to both.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever a KTU question asks "explain the *stages of shipbuilding*," hull erection and launching together account for **30–40%** of the answer's weightage. Always draw a clear labeled diagram and bullet-point the stages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hull Erection — Methods, Stages, and Engineering Logic

Hull erection is *not* a single activity; it is a **multi-stage process** governed by the shipyard's chosen construction philosophy. KTU examiners expect students to know the following three classical methods.

### 2.1.1 Method 1 — The Progressive (Sectional) Method (Traditional)

This is the **oldest and most intuitive** method. The hull is built from the **keel upward, fore-and-aft outward**, like a real ship "growing" on the slipway.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Keel Laying** — The centerline keel plate (or bar keel) is positioned on the slipway blocks.
2. **Floor Plates & Center Girder Erection** — The transverse floors and longitudinal center girder are fitted.
3. **Frame Erection** — Transverse frames and longitudinals are raised and welded to the floors.
4. **Shell Plating — Bottom First** — The bottom shell plates are fitted and welded from the centerline outward.
5. **Side Shell Plating** — The bilge and side shell plates are progressively added.
6. **Deck Plating** — The strength deck and superstructure decks are fitted last.
7. **Closing of Hull** — The stem and stern posts are welded, and the hull becomes a closed box.

**Why this method?**
- It is **visually intuitive** — workers see a "ship" growing before their eyes.
- It requires **minimal heavy-lift cranes** (only for the largest plates).
- It is **ideal for small-to-medium shipyards** with limited infrastructure.

**Why is it used less today?**
- It is **slow** (often 12–24 months for a small ship).
- The **working environment is poor** (low to the ground, inside the hull).
- Quality control is **difficult** because much welding is done in cramped spaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "explain progressive erection," always mention that this method is also called the *"bottom-up" or "keel-up" method*. KTU examiners love that exact terminology.

---

### 2.1.2 Method 2 — The Section (Unit) Method (Modern)

This is the **dominant modern method** in KTU 2024-aligned shipyards. The hull is fabricated as **3D blocks (units)** in workshops, and these blocks are then **welded together on the berth**.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Block Fabrication in Workshop** — The hull is divided into 30–80+ manageable blocks (typically 15–25 m long, 3-stories high).
2. **Pre-Outfitting** — Pipes, cables, equipment, and machinery are installed in the blocks *before* erection (this is the key advantage).
3. **Block Transportation** — Blocks are moved to the building berth using **Self-Propelled Modular Transporters (SPMTs)** or heavy-lift gantry cranes.
4. **Block Erection** — Blocks are lifted and welded together, starting from the **mid-body and working outward** (or vice versa, depending on yard policy).
5. **Block Joining Welding** — Critical ring welds are made between adjacent blocks.
6. **Hull Closure** — The final ring (usually at midships or aft) is welded last, "closing" the hull.

**Why this method is the KTU-preferred answer:**

- **Speed** — Parallel work (many blocks built simultaneously) cuts shipbuilding time by 30–50%.
- **Quality** — Workshop conditions allow better welding, NDT, and outfitting.
- **Safety** — Workers are at ground level, not on scaffolds inside the hull.
- **Pre-outfitting** — Reduces post-launch work and improves productivity.

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires **huge capital investment** in cranes (e.g., 600-tonne gantry cranes).
- Demands **precision block alignment** (within 2–3 mm tolerance).
- Block-joint welds are critical — failure here can be catastrophic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Buzzword:** The unit/section method is also referred to as the **"Modular Construction Method"** or **"Block Construction Method."** Use these terms in your answers to score full marks.

---

### 2.1.3 Method 3 — The Isherwood System (Longitudinal Framing Variant)

A specialized framing system (developed by Sir John Isherwood in the 1940s) that uses **heavy longitudinal framing** instead of transverse frames. Erection logic is similar to progressive erection, but with longitudinal strength members.

**KTU Relevance:** KTU may ask a 3-mark question on *"Name the system that uses longitudinal framing throughout."* Answer: **Isherwood System**.

---

### 2.1.4 Comparison Matrix (High-Yield Table)

| Feature | Progressive Method | Section/Unit Method | Isherwood System |
|---|---|---|---|
| Construction philosophy | Bottom-up on slipway | Pre-built blocks joined on berth | Longitudinal framing variant |
| Speed | Slow (12–24 months) | Fast (6–12 months) | Medium |
| Pre-outfitting | Limited (post-launch) | Maximum (in workshop) | Limited |
| Capital investment | Low | **Very High** | Medium |
| Quality control | Difficult | **Excellent** | Medium |
| Crane requirement | Small | **Massive (gantry)** | Small |
| KTU exam weightage | High (definition) | **Very High (modern)** | Medium |

---

## 2.2 Ship Launching — Types, Mechanics, and Engineering Logic

KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes **five principal launching methods**. Each has a unique mechanical principle.

### 2.2.1 Type 1 — End (Longitudinal) Launching

The ship slides **bow- or stern-first** down an inclined slipway into the water, like a giant sled.

**Key Components (Memorize These — High KTU Yield!):**

1. **Groundways (Fixed Sliding Ways)** — Heavy wooden or steel tracks fixed to the slipway. They stay *stationary*.
2. **Bilgeways (Movable Sliding Ways)** — Sliding tracks attached to the ship's bilge. They move *with* the ship.
3. **Poppets (Fore and Aft)** — Vertical wooden posts that hold the ship on the cradle *until the moment of release*.
4. **Trigger (Sole Piece / Dog Shore)** — A weak timber or steel piece designed to **fail in shear** at a precise trigger load.
5. **Sliding Ways Cradle** — The framework of shores and braces that transfers ship weight to the bilgeways.
6. **Slipway (Berth)** — The inclined concrete or timber surface that supports the entire operation.
7. **Capstan / Winch** — For controlled descent and post-launch retrieval.
8. **Drag Chains / Drag Shoes** — Friction brakes to limit velocity and prevent runaway.

**Sequence of Launching (KTU Board-Expected Answer):**

1. The ship is built on the cradle, supported by fore and aft poppets.
2. Just before launch, the **trigger is weakened** (e.g., by sawing partway through).
3. A **hydraulic jack or wedge system** lifts the ship slightly, transferring its weight from the poppets to the sliding ways.
4. The **trigger is released** (e.g., by removing a retaining pin, or by the ship's weight overcoming the trigger's shear strength).
5. The ship begins to slide down the inclined slipway under gravity.
6. **Friction brakes (drag chains/shoes)** control the speed.
7. The stern enters the water first; the bow follows as the ship rotates on the water surface.
8. Tugs take over and tow the ship to the fitting-out berth.

**Why is this method the KTU "default" answer?**
- It is the **most widely used** method globally.
- It is **conceptually simple** and easy to diagram.
- It is the **most question-rich** topic (forces, friction, energy, stability — all derivable).

---

### 2.2.2 Type 2 — Side (Broadside) Launching

The ship is launched **sideways**, rotating over a series of rollers or sliding ways. Used in **narrow rivers** where end launching is impossible.

**Advantages:**
- Suitable for **confined waterways**.
- Can launch **very wide vessels**.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Huge side-roll moment** stresses the hull.
- **Tipping risk** — the ship can roll too far and capsize.
- Requires **extensive cameling and weighting** to maintain balance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The *RMS Titanic*'s hull was assembled using a side-launching-like method at Harland & Wolff, Belfast, although the final launch was end-launching. KTU examiners sometimes use such trivia in 1-mark MCQs.

---

### 2.2.3 Type 3 — Float-On / Float-Off Launching

The ship is built in a **graving dock** or **floating dry dock**. The dock is flooded, and the ship floats out. Modern mega-yachts and offshore platforms use this method.

---

### 2.2.4 Type 4 — Mechanical (Syncrolift / Shiplift) Launching

The ship is built on a **platform** that is mechanically raised and lowered into the water. Common in **naval bases and ship-repair yards**.

---

### 2.2.5 Type 5 — Dock Launching (Floating Dry Dock)

The ship is built inside a **pontoon-like floating dock**, which is then submerged, allowing the ship to float off. Used for **very large vessels** (e.g., aircraft carriers).

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Eight Formulas — They Cover ~90% of KTU Numerical Problems on Launching:**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Meaning of Variables | Typical Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static Friction (Trigger Release) | $F_s = \mu_s \cdot W$ | $\mu_s$ = static friction coefficient; $W$ = ship weight on ways | N, kg, — | **Very High** |
| 2 | Kinetic Friction (Sliding) | $F_k = \mu_k \cdot W$ | $\mu_k$ = kinetic friction coefficient; $W$ = ship weight | N, kg, — | **Very High** |
| 3 | Net Accelerating Force | $F_{net} = W \sin\theta - \mu_k W \cos\theta$ | $\theta$ = slipway angle | N, kg, rad | **Very High** |
| 4 | Launching Velocity (Energy Method) | $v = \sqrt{2 g h \left(1 - \mu_k \cot\theta\right)}$ | $h$ = vertical drop; $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | m/s | **High** |
| 5 | Trigger Shear Force | $F_{trig} = \tau_{allow} \cdot A_{trig}$ | $\tau_{allow}$ = shear strength; $A_{trig}$ = trigger cross-section | N, Pa, m² | Medium |
| 6 | Buoyancy Force | $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | $\rho$ = water density; $V_{disp}$ = displaced volume | N, kg/m³, m³ | High |
| 7 | Hull Bending Moment at Water Entry | $M_{bend} = W \cdot L_{cantilever}$ | $L_{cantilever}$ = overhang from last support | N·m, m | Medium |
| 8 | Stopping Drag Force | $F_{drag} = \mu_d \cdot W$ | $\mu_d$ = drag-shoe friction coefficient | N, kg, — | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in real shipyards to (a) size trigger mechanisms, (b) select slipway slope, (c) determine whether the ship will self-launch (gravity > friction) or need winch assistance, and (d) check hull strength at the moment of water entry — the most critical loading case in the entire launch sequence.

---

## 2.4 Stability & Safety Logic During Launching

A ship is most **vulnerable to capsize** at the moment it pivots on the water surface (heel angle ≈ 0° to 30°). KTU examiners test two key concepts:

1. **Condition for Self-Launching:**
   The component of weight along the slope must exceed static friction:
   $$W \sin\theta \geq \mu_s W \cos\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\theta \geq \mu_s$$
   If this condition is **not** met, the ship will **not move on its own** after trigger release, and a **winch is required**.

2. **Condition for Stable Floating After Water Entry:**
   The metacenter (M) must remain **above** the center of gravity (G):
   $$GM = KB + BM - KG > 0$$
   If $GM < 0$, the ship will **capsize** at the moment of water entry — a launch disaster.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **transverse stability** ($GM_T$) and **longitudinal stability** ($GM_L$) during launching. During launch, **transverse** stability is critical because the ship rolls sideways as it enters the water. KTU always specifies *transverse* unless otherwise stated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Launching Velocity at Water Entry (Energy Method)

**KTU Problem Type:** *"A ship of weight W is launched down a slipway of slope θ. The coefficient of kinetic friction is μₖ. Derive an expression for the velocity of the ship at the instant it reaches the water."*

### Step 1: Define the Geometry

Let:
- $L$ = length of sliding ways (m)
- $\theta$ = slipway angle with horizontal (radians or degrees)
- $h$ = vertical drop of ship's center of gravity = $L \sin\theta$ (m)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $v$ = velocity at water entry (m/s)
- $\mu_k$ = coefficient of kinetic friction between bilgeways and groundways
- $W$ = total weight of ship + cradle (N)

### Step 2: Apply the Work-Energy Theorem

The work done by **gravity** minus the work done against **friction** equals the kinetic energy gained:

$$W_{gravity} - W_{friction} = \Delta KE$$

### Step 3: Write Each Term Explicitly

**Work done by gravity** (force component along the slope × distance traveled):
$$W_{gravity} = (W \sin\theta) \cdot L$$

**Work done against kinetic friction** (friction force × distance):
$$F_{friction} = \mu_k \cdot N = \mu_k \cdot (W \cos\theta)$$
$$W_{friction} = (\mu_k W \cos\theta) \cdot L$$

**Kinetic energy gained** (assuming the ship starts from rest):
$$\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{g} \cdot v^2$$

> (Here we use $m = W/g$ to convert weight to mass.)

### Step 4: Substitute into the Energy Equation

$$W L \sin\theta - \mu_k W L \cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{g} \cdot v^2$$

### Step 5: Simplify by Canceling $W$ (Common Factor)

$$L \sin\theta - \mu_k L \cos\theta = \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

### Step 6: Factor Out $L$

$$L (\sin\theta - \mu_k \cos\theta) = \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

### Step 7: Substitute $h = L \sin\theta$

$$L \sin\theta - \mu_k L \cos\theta = h - \mu_k L \cos\theta$$

But $L \cos\theta = h \cot\theta$ (since $\cot\theta = \cos\theta / \sin\theta$).

So:
$$h - \mu_k h \cot\theta = \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

### Step 8: Solve for $v$

$$v^2 = 2 g h \left(1 - \mu_k \cot\theta\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{2 g h \left(1 - \mu_k \cot\theta\right)}\,}$$

### Step 9: Physical Interpretation (Mandatory for KTU Full Marks)

- If $\mu_k \cot\theta < 1$, the ship **gains speed** during launch.
- If $\mu_k \cot\theta = 1$, the ship **moves at constant velocity** (no acceleration).
- If $\mu_k \cot\theta > 1$, the ship **decelerates** and may **stop** on the slipway (a launch failure!).
- The term $(1 - \mu_k \cot\theta)$ is sometimes called the **"launching efficiency factor"**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Condition for Self-Launching (Gravity vs. Friction)

**KTU Problem Type:** *"Find the minimum slipway angle required for a ship of weight W to self-launch, given static friction coefficient μₛ."*

### Step 1: Force Balance Along the Slope

At the moment of trigger release, two forces act along the slipway:
- **Driving force** = $W \sin\theta$ (gravity component along slope)
- **Resisting force** = $\mu_s W \cos\theta$ (maximum static friction)

### Step 2: Apply the Incipient-Motion Condition

For the ship to just begin moving:
$$W \sin\theta = \mu_s W \cos\theta$$

### Step 3: Cancel $W$

$$\sin\theta = \mu_s \cos\theta$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\theta$

$$\tan\theta = \mu_s$$

$$\boxed{\,\theta_{min} = \arctan(\mu_s)\,}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example (For Practice)

If $\mu_s = 0.08$ (greased wood), then:
$$\theta_{min} = \arctan(0.08) \approx 4.57°$$

This means the slipway must be **steeper than 4.57°** for self-launching. Typical shipyard slopes (1/8 to 1/24, i.e., 7.1° to 2.4°) are chosen with a safety margin above this value.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Trigger Shear Strength Required

**KTU Problem Type:** *"A trigger (sole piece) is to be designed for a 5000-tonne ship. If the allowable shear stress of the trigger material is 25 MPa, find the cross-sectional area of the trigger."*

### Step 1: Identify Forces on the Trigger

The trigger must fail in shear when:
$$\text{Force on trigger} = W \sin\theta - \mu_s W \cos\theta = W(\sin\theta - \mu_s \cos\theta)$$

### Step 2: Equate to Shear Strength

$$\tau_{allow} \cdot A = W(\sin\theta - \mu_s \cos\theta)$$

### Step 3: Solve for Area

$$\boxed{\,A = \frac{W(\sin\theta - \mu_s \cos\theta)}{\tau_{allow}}\,}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Computation

Given:
- $W = 5000 \text{ tonnes} = 5000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 4.905 \times 10^7$ N
- $\theta = 5°$ → $\sin\theta \approx 0.0872$, $\cos\theta \approx 0.9962$
- $\mu_s = 0.06$
- $\tau_{allow} = 25 \times 10^6$ Pa

$$A = \frac{4.905 \times 10^7 \cdot (0.0872 - 0.06 \cdot 0.9962)}{25 \times 10^6}$$

$$A = \frac{4.905 \times 10^7 \cdot (0.0872 - 0.0598)}{25 \times 10^6}$$

$$A = \frac{4.905 \times 10^7 \cdot 0.0274}{25 \times 10^6}$$

$$A = \frac{1.344 \times 10^6}{25 \times 10^6} \approx 0.0538 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\,A \approx 0.054 \text{ m}^2 = 540 \text{ cm}^2\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always include **units** in your final answer. Marks are often deducted for unitless numerical answers in Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Launching Methods

| Launching Method | Best Suited For | Advantages | Disadvantages | KTU Mark Allocation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **End Launching** | Most ship types; standard shipyards | Simple, well-understood, economical | Requires long slipway, deep water | 7–10 marks |
| **Side Launching** | Narrow rivers, very wide hulls | Works in confined waters | High hull stress, capsize risk | 3–5 marks |
| **Float-On/Off** | Mega-yachts, offshore platforms | No slipway needed, very gentle | Requires graving dock investment | 2–3 marks |
| **Syncrolift / Shiplift** | Naval bases, repair yards | Reusable platform, precise control | Limited by platform capacity | 2–3 marks |
| **Floating Dry Dock** | Aircraft carriers, large warships | Handles very heavy vessels | Extremely high capital cost | 2–3 marks |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution — KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A ship of displacement 8000 tonnes is to be launched end-first down a slipway inclined at 1 in 12. The coefficient of friction is 0.05. Calculate: (a) the friction force resisting motion, (b) the net accelerating force, and (c) the velocity at the end of a 60 m sliding way. *(Use g = 9.81 m/s²)*

### Given Data
- Weight $W = 8000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 7.848 \times 10^7$ N
- Slope: 1 in 12 → $\tan\theta = \frac{1}{12} = 0.0833$
  - $\theta = \arctan(0.0833) \approx 4.76°$
  - $\sin\theta \approx 0.0830$, $\cos\theta \approx 0.9965$
- $\mu_k = 0.05$
- $L = 60$ m → $h = L \sin\theta = 60 \times 0.0830 \approx 4.98$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Part (a): Friction Force

$$F_{friction} = \mu_k \cdot W \cos\theta = 0.05 \times 7.848 \times 10^7 \times 0.9965$$

$$F_{friction} \approx 3.910 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 3910 \text{ kN}$$

### Part (b): Net Accelerating Force

$$F_{net} = W \sin\theta - \mu_k W \cos\theta$$

$$F_{net} = 7.848 \times 10^7 \times 0.0830 - 3.910 \times 10^6$$

$$F_{net} \approx 6.514 \times 10^6 - 3.910 \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{\,F_{net} \approx 2.604 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 2604 \text{ kN}\,}$$

### Part (c): Velocity at End of 60 m

Using the energy method:
$$v = \sqrt{2 g h \left(1 - \mu_k \cot\theta\right)}$$

First, $\cot\theta = \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \frac{0.9965}{0.0830} \approx 12.0$

$$\mu_k \cot\theta = 0.05 \times 12.0 = 0.6$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.98 \times (1 - 0.6)}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.98 \times 0.4}$$

$$v = \sqrt{39.09}$$

$$\boxed{\,v \approx 6.25 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** A launching velocity of 5–7 m/s at water entry is **typical and safe**. Velocities above 10 m/s indicate inadequate friction control (runaway risk).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hull Erection Process Flow (Block Construction Method)

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional architecture** of the unit (block) construction method — the modern KTU-preferred approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Keel Laying on Building Berth] --> B[Step 2: Workshop Fabrication of Hull Blocks]
    B --> C[Step 3: Pre-Outfitting of Blocks Pipes Cables Machinery]
    C --> D[Step 4: Block Transportation via SPMT or Gantry Crane]
    D --> E[Step 5: Block Erection on Slipway Welding Alignment]
    E --> F[Step 6: Ring Joint Welding between Adjacent Blocks]
    F --> G[Step 7: Hull Closure Final Ring Welded]
    G --> H[Step 8: Inspection NDT Hydrostatic Test]
    H --> I[Step 9: Hull Ready for Launching]
    I --> J[Step 10: Outfitting Painting Pre Launch]
    J --> K[Step 11: Launching Event]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - Each block represents a **discrete shipyard operation**.
> - The flow is **strictly sequential** (you cannot do Step 5 before Step 4).
> - In modern yards, **Steps 2 and 3 happen in parallel** with Steps 4–6, dramatically reducing total construction time.

---

## 4.2 End Launching — Block Architecture Flow

The following diagram represents the **functional interaction of launching components** on a traditional end-launching slipway. (Adapting physical drawings to a block flow per protocol safeguard.)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SLIPWAY_AREA[Slipway Inclined Berth]
        A1[Groundways Fixed to Slipway] --- A2[Bilgeways Attached to Ship Hull]
        A2 --- A3[Sliding Ways Cradle]
        A3 --- A4[Fore Poppet Vertical Post]
        A3 --- A5[Aft Poppet Vertical Post]
        A4 --- A6[Trigger Sole Piece Shear Pin]
        A5 --- A6
        A6 --- A7[Drag Chains and Drag Shoes]
    end
    A7 --> B1[Ship Slides Down Under Gravity]
    B1 --> B2[Stern Enters Water]
    B2 --> B3[Ship Pivots on Water Surface]
    B3 --> B4[Ship Floats Free]
    B4 --> B5[Tugs Take Over Tow to Fitting Out Berth]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram in a KTU exam:**
> - The **groundways** stay still.
> - The **bilgeways** move with the ship (relative motion = friction).
> - The **trigger** is the weakest link — it fails in shear at the right moment.
> - The **poppets** are the temporary vertical supports that hold the ship *before* the trigger fails.
> - **Drag chains/shoes** are the "brakes" that prevent runaway acceleration.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Launching Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Ship Type Slipway Site Available] --> Q1{Shipyard Has Long Inclined Slipway?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Sufficient Water Depth at Toe?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> End1[Use End Launching Longitudinal]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Confined Waterway Narrow River?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q4{Available Graving or Floating Dock?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Side1[Use Floating Dry Dock Launching]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Site Has Syncrolift or Shiplift?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Lift1[Use Mechanical Syncrolift Launching]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Very Wide Hull Barge Like?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Side2[Use Side Broadside Launching]
    Q6 -- Yes --> Side3[Use Side Broadside Launching]
    Q6 -- No --> End2[Consult Naval Architect Re Design Slipway]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** If a question asks *"Which launching method would you recommend for a small shipyard in a narrow river with no graving dock?"*, follow this flow:
> 1. No long slipway → eliminate end launching.
> 2. No dock → eliminate floating dry dock.
> 3. No syncrolift → eliminate mechanical launching.
> 4. Narrow river → **Side launching** is the answer.

---

## 4.4 Force Diagram Schematic — Trigger Release (Text-Based Schematic)

The following is a **block-level force interaction diagram** for the trigger release moment:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Weight W acting vertically down] -->|Resolved| W1[Component W sin theta along slope]
    W -->|Resolved| W2[Component W cos theta perpendicular to slope]
    W2 --> N[Normal Reaction N equals W cos theta]
    N -->|Multiplied by mu_s| FR[Friction Force mu_s W cos theta opposing motion]
    W1 --> NET[Net Force W sin theta minus mu_s W cos theta]
    FR --> NET
    NET --> TRIG[If NET exceeds Trigger Shear Strength trigger fails]
    TRIG --> MOVE[Ship begins to slide]
```

**Interpretation:**
- $W$ (weight) is the **driving force** (via its $\sin\theta$ component).
- $\mu_s W \cos\theta$ is the **resisting force** (static friction).
- The **trigger fails** when the net force exceeds the trigger's shear capacity.
- Once the trigger fails, the ship **accelerates** down the slipway.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each — Short Answer)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Hull Erection" in shipbuilding. List the three principal methods of hull erection.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Expected Wording):**

**Definition:**
Hull erection is the shipbuilding phase in which all fabricated structural components — plates, frames, stiffeners, and sub-assemblies — are progressively joined, aligned, and welded on the building berth to form the complete watertight hull of the vessel.

**Three Principal Methods:**
1. **Progressive (Sectional / Keel-Up) Method** — Hull is built from the keel upward on the slipway.
2. **Unit (Block / Modular) Method** — Hull is fabricated as 3D blocks in the workshop and joined on the berth.
3. **Isherwood System** — A specialized method employing heavy longitudinal framing throughout the hull.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is meant by "Launching" of a ship? Name any four types of launching methods used in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Launching is the controlled operation of transferring a newly built ship from the building berth/slipway into the water so that it floats free for the first time.

**Four Types of Launching:**
1. **End (Longitudinal) Launching** — Ship slides bow/stern-first down an inclined slipway.
2. **Side (Broadside) Launching** — Ship rolls sideways into the water.
3. **Float-On/Float-Off Launching** — Ship is built in a graving or floating dock and floated out.
4. **Mechanical Launching (Syncrolift / Shiplift)** — Ship is raised/lowered on a mechanical platform.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Both alternatives are presented below.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q1. (a)** Explain the **Unit (Block) Construction Method** of hull erection in detail, with its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labeled diagram, describe the **end launching** of a ship. List the major launching accessories and their functions. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q1(a): Unit Construction Method

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The unit (or block) construction method is the most widely used modern technique for building ship hulls. In this method, the hull is divided into large, manageable **three-dimensional blocks** that are fabricated in workshops and then transported to the building berth for final assembly.

**Step-by-Step Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Hull Division & Planning:** The naval architect divides the hull into 30–80+ blocks based on the ship's size, available crane capacity, and yard infrastructure. Each block typically weighs 50–600 tonnes.

2. **Workshop Fabrication:** Blocks are built in covered workshops under controlled conditions. Welding, fitting, and inspection are performed with the block in a comfortable orientation.

3. **Pre-Outfitting:** Pipes, electrical cables, machinery foundations, insulation, and even equipment are installed in the blocks *before* erection. This is a major advantage, as it eliminates post-launch work in confined spaces.

4. **Transportation to Building Berth:** Blocks are moved using **Self-Propelled Modular Transporters (SPMTs)** — multi-axle hydraulic trailers — or **gantry cranes** capable of lifting 600–1000 tonnes.

5. **Block Erection:** Blocks are lifted and welded together on the building berth. Erection typically proceeds from midships outward (or from one end inward), with each new block welded to the existing structure.

6. **Ring Joint Welding:** The critical welds between adjacent blocks (called **ring joints**) are subjected to stringent NDT (non-destructive testing) — ultrasonic, radiographic, and magnetic particle inspection.

7. **Hull Closure:** The final block is welded last, completing the hull. The choice of closure location is a critical engineering decision (commonly midships for large vessels).

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Faster construction (30–50% time saving).
- Higher quality (workshop conditions).
- Improved safety (ground-level work).
- Maximum pre-outfitting.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Very high capital investment (gantry cranes, SPMTs).
- Demands precise block alignment (tolerance ±2–3 mm).
- Critical ring-joint welds.

---

#### Model Solution — Q1(b): End Launching

**Diagram (3 marks — Mandatory for Full Marks):**
A neat labeled diagram showing:
- Inclined slipway with slope angle θ
- Groundways (fixed) and bilgeways (sliding)
- Fore poppet and aft poppet
- Trigger (sole piece)
- Ship hull in cradle
- Drag chains/shoes
- Water at the toe of the slipway

**Sequence of Launching (3 marks):**

1. **Pre-launch preparation:** The ship is built on the cradle, supported by fore and aft poppets. Just before launch, the trigger is partially cut/weakened to a precise shear capacity.

2. **Weight transfer:** Hydraulic jacks or wedges transfer the ship's full weight from the poppets to the bilgeways (and hence to the groundways).

3. **Trigger release:** The trigger is released — by removing a shear pin, cutting a final strand, or by the ship's weight overcoming the remaining shear strength.

4. **Sliding motion:** The ship slides down the inclined slipway under gravity. Bilgeways slide over greased groundways; friction is kept low (μ ≈ 0.03–0.06) using tallow, grease, or modern PTFE-based lubricants.

5. **Speed control:** Drag chains and drag shoes apply friction to limit velocity. Without these, the ship could accelerate dangerously (runaway launch).

6. **Water entry:** The stern (or bow) enters the water first. A massive hydrodynamic load (the "slam" load) acts on the entry point.

7. **Pivot and float-off:** As the ship continues forward, it pivots on the water surface and eventually floats free.

8. **Tug assistance:** Tugs take over and tow the ship to the fitting-out berth.

**Launching Accessories & Functions (1 mark):**

| Accessory | Function |
|---|---|
| Groundways | Fixed sliding tracks on the slipway |
| Bilgeways | Sliding tracks attached to the ship's hull |
| Fore & Aft Poppets | Temporary vertical supports holding the ship |
| Trigger (Sole Piece) | Weak shear element that fails at the precise release moment |
| Sliding Ways Cradle | Framework transferring ship weight to bilgeways |
| Drag Chains/Shoes | Friction brakes to control launching speed |
| Capstan/Winch | Backup pulling/braking mechanism |

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q2. (a)** Compare **end launching** and **side launching** of ships. Under what circumstances is side launching preferred? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship of displacement **10,000 tonnes** is launched down a slipway inclined at **1 in 15**. The coefficient of kinetic friction is **0.04** and the length of the sliding way is **80 m**. Calculate: (i) the net accelerating force, (ii) the velocity of the ship at the end of the sliding way. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q2(a): End vs. Side Launching

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | End Launching | Side Launching |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Bow or stern first | Sideways (broadside) |
| Slipway | Long, inclined | Short, often nearly horizontal |
| Hull stress | Moderate bending | **High bending + torsion** |
| Site requirement | Deep water, long foreshore | **Narrow rivers, confined sites** |
| Speed control | Drag chains/shoes | Camels, counterweights |
| Risk | Runaway, grounding | **Capsize during roll** |
| Common usage | ~90% of shipyards | ~5–10% (special cases) |

**When Side Launching is Preferred (3 marks):**
1. **Confined waterways** — When the river or harbor is too narrow for end launching (e.g., older European shipyards on the Thames, Seine).
2. **Very wide vessels** — When the ship's beam is so large that the side-launch orientation provides better stability.
3. **Short foreshore** — When there is insufficient land area to build a long inclined slipway.
4. **Historical preservation sites** — Where the original side-launch infrastructure already exists (e.g., Harland & Wolff's Belfast yard).
5. **Barges and pontoons** — Flat-bottomed, wide vessels that are inherently more stable in side-launch orientation.

---

#### Model Solution — Q2(b): Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- Displacement = $10{,}000$ tonnes → $W = 10^4 \times 10^3 \times 9.81 = 9.81 \times 10^7$ N
- Slope: 1 in 15 → $\tan\theta = \frac{1}{15} = 0.0667$
- $\theta = \arctan(0.0667) \approx 3.81°$
- $\sin\theta \approx 0.0664$, $\cos\theta \approx 0.9978$
- $\mu_k = 0.04$
- $L = 80$ m → $h = L \sin\theta = 80 \times 0.0664 \approx 5.31$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Part (i): Net Accelerating Force**

$$F_{net} = W \sin\theta - \mu_k W \cos\theta$$

$$F_{net} = 9.81 \times 10^7 \times 0.0664 - 0.04 \times 9.81 \times 10^7 \times 0.9978$$

$$F_{net} = 6.514 \times 10^6 - 3.916 \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{\,F_{net} \approx 2.598 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 2598 \text{ kN}\,}$$

**Part (ii): Velocity at End of Sliding Way**

First, compute $\cot\theta$:
$$\cot\theta = \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \frac{0.9978}{0.0664} \approx 15.0$$

Compute the launching efficiency factor:
$$1 - \mu_k \cot\theta = 1 - 0.04 \times 15.0 = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40$$

Apply the energy equation:
$$v = \sqrt{2 g h \left(1 - \mu_k \cot\theta\right)}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 5.31 \times 0.40}$$

$$v = \sqrt{41.67}$$

$$\boxed{\,v \approx 6.45 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert tonnes to newtons ($1 \text{ tonne-force} = 1000 \times 9.81$ N). This alone can cost **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Slope Confusion:** Mistaking "1 in 15" as $1/15$ radians (it is dimensionless — just the ratio). Always take $\tan\theta = 1/15$.
> 3. **Wrong Friction Direction:** Friction *opposes* motion, so it is *subtracted* from gravity component.
> 4. **Sign Error in Energy Equation:** The term $(1 - \mu_k \cot\theta)$ can be negative; if so, the ship decelerates and may stop.
> 5. **No Diagram in Descriptive Parts:** For a 7-mark question, **always** draw a labeled diagram — at least 1–2 marks are reserved for it.
> 6. **Incomplete Listing of Accessories:** Naming only 2–3 launching accessories when 5+ are expected — partial marking will apply.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist the night before your KTU exam. Each bullet is a potential 1–3 mark question.**

- ✅ **Hull Erection Definition:** Assembly of fabricated parts into a complete watertight hull.
- ✅ **Three Methods:** Progressive (keel-up), Unit (block), and Isherwood.
- ✅ **Modern Preference:** Unit/Block method is the KTU-preferred answer.
- ✅ **Pre-outfitting:** Installing pipes, cables, and machinery in blocks *before* erection — a key advantage of the block method.
- ✅ **Launching Definition:** Transfer of the built ship from land to water.
- ✅ **Five Launching Types:** End, Side, Float-on/off, Syncrolift, Floating Dry Dock.
- ✅ **End Launching is default** — used in ~90% of shipyards.
- ✅ **Groundways = Fixed; Bilgeways = Move with ship.**
- ✅ **Trigger (Sole Piece)** = weak shear element that fails at the precise release moment.
- ✅ **Poppets** = temporary vertical supports holding the ship pre-launch.
- ✅ **Drag Chains/Shoes** = friction brakes controlling launch velocity.
- ✅ **Static Friction Formula:** $F_s = \mu_s W$
- ✅ **Kinetic Friction Formula:** $F_k = \mu_k W$
- ✅ **Launching Velocity Formula:** $v = \sqrt{2 g h (1 - \mu_k \cot\theta)}$
- ✅ **Self-Launch Condition:** $\tan\theta \geq \mu_s$
- ✅ **Trigger Area Formula:** $A = \frac{W(\sin\theta - \mu_s \cos\theta)}{\tau_{allow}}$
- ✅ **Buoyancy Formula:** $F_B = \rho g V_{disp}$
- ✅ **Typical Slipway Slope:** 1/8 to 1/24 (KTU common values: 1/12, 1/15, 1/20).
- ✅ **Typical Friction Coefficient:** $\mu_s \approx 0.05$–$0.10$; $\mu_k \approx 0.03$–$0.06$.
- ✅ **Typical Launching Velocity:** 5–7 m/s (safe range).
- ✅ **Side Launching:** Used for narrow rivers and very wide vessels; high hull stress.
- ✅ **Stability After Launch:** $GM > 0$ (metacenter above center of gravity).
- ✅ **Key Density Values:** Seawater = 1025 kg/m³; Freshwater = 1000 kg/m³.
- ✅ **Real-world disasters** (Titanic launch issues, *MV Princess Victoria*, *Costa Concordia*) make excellent 1-mark MCQ material — KTU loves them.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When you write a launching answer, **always follow this 4-step structure** for full marks:
> 1. **Definition** (1 mark)
> 2. **Neat labeled diagram** (2 marks)
> 3. **Step-by-step procedure** (3–4 marks)
> 4. **Accessories & their functions in a table** (1–2 marks)
>
> This template covers **90% of KTU ESE Part B questions** on Module 4. Practice it once, and you can adapt it to almost any launching question in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling of Pre-Assembled Units in the Erection Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Handling of Pre-Assembled Units in the Erection Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In modern modular shipbuilding, a **Pre-Assembled Unit (PAU)** — also called a **ship block**, **sub-block**, **mega-block**, or **pre-fabricated unit** — is a three-dimensional structural assembly comprising plates, stiffeners, frames, bulkheads, brackets, and outfit items that is welded and outfitted in the **panel fabrication shop** or **block assembly shop** (also called the *pre-erection shop*) before being transported and erected on the **building berth** (or in a **building dock / dry dock**) in the **erection area**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Erection Area (KTU Definition):**
> The **Erection Area** is the designated zone of the shipyard — comprising the inclined or horizontal **building berth**, **gantry crane tracks**, **transporter routes**, and **stock areas** — where pre-assembled units are lifted, transported, positioned (set-up), aligned, and welded together to form the complete hull.

**Handling of Pre-Assembled Units** therefore refers to the integrated engineering process of:
1. **Lifting** (hoisting by gantry/portal/tower cranes)
2. **Transferring** (moving on skids, trailers, or Self-Propelled Modular Transporters — SPMTs)
3. **Positioning / Setting-up** (placing on berth supports, aligning with adjacent blocks)
4. **Temporary securing** (tack welding, dogging pins, strong-backs)
5. **Final integration welding** (joining the erected units)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Building a Ship = Assembling a Giant LEGO Castle on a Table**
>
> Imagine constructing a massive LEGO castle. Instead of attaching one tiny brick at a time on the dining table (the **old method — "stick-building"** or *unit construction* on the berth), you build large pre-assembled wings of the castle on smaller side-tables in your bedroom (the **pre-assembly shop**). When the wings are ready, you **carefully carry them** (lifting/transport) to the dining table, **align the studs perfectly** (positioning/alignment), and **press them together** (welding).
>
> The "carrying" of large wings requires:
> - **Strong arms** → Cranes (gantry, portal, tower)
> - **Steady hands** → Spreader beams and correct sling angles
> - **Careful stepping** → Controlled transport on skids or SPMTs
> - **Knowing where the heavy side is** → Center of Gravity (CoG) management

Without proper handling, the "wing" can swing, drop, or break the dining table — exactly as mishandled ship blocks can damage the berth, the block itself, or injure personnel.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Modern Shipyard) |
|---|---|
| **Block weight range** | **100 tonnes – 800 tonnes** (mega-blocks up to **1,500+ tonnes**) |
| **Block length (typical)** | **12 m – 36 m** |
| **Block breadth** | **10 m – 32 m** |
| **Block height** | **8 m – 15 m** (up to full ship depth for ring sections) |
| **Gantry crane lifting capacity** | **300 tonnes – 1,200 tonnes** (giant shipyards up to **2,000 tonnes**) |
| **SPMT axle load per line** | **40 tonnes – 60 tonnes** (modular: 4/5/6 axles per line) |
| **Design safety factor on rigging** | **SF = 2.5 to 4.0** (per ISO 12480 / ASME B30.9) |
| **Wind speed limit for lifting** | **≤ 15 m/s (54 km/h)** typically; **≤ 20 m/s** for heavy lifts with permits |
| **Sling angle from horizontal** | **Minimum 60°** preferred (i.e., sling length ≥ block span) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Students must be able to describe the **lifting gear**, **crane selection**, **transport methods**, **block-setting procedure**, and **safety considerations** in the erection area. The "Why" behind every step — *load distribution*, *CoG offset*, *sling tension*, *wind heel moment* — is a frequent KTU exam focus.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Block-Handling Workflow (Structured Logic)

The end-to-end process of handling a pre-assembled unit in the erection area follows a strict, **engineered sequence**. Skipping or weak execution of any step leads to accidents, block damage, or schedule slippage.

### Step 1: Block Fabrication & Pre-Equipping (Pre-Erection Stage)
- Plates and stiffeners are welded into **panels** in the panel shop.
- Panels are joined into **sub-assemblies** (e.g., double-bottom panels, side-shell panels, bulkhead panels).
- Sub-assemblies are welded into **blocks** in the block assembly shop.
- **Pre-outfitting** (piping, cable trays, machinery seats, insulation) is done at this stage — this is the essence of the **modern "pre-outfitting" philosophy** that reduces work at height on the berth.

### Step 2: Rigging Plan Preparation
A **Rigging Engineer** (or Method Engineer) prepares a **Rigging Plan / Lift Plan** that includes:
- **Block weight** (from weighbridge or CAD mass property report).
- **Center of Gravity (CoG)** location — *X*, *Y*, *Z* coordinates from the 3D model.
- **Lift point locations** (lifting lugs, pad-eyes, trunnions).
- **Sling / spreader-beam configuration** (4-leg, 8-leg, or spreader bar).
- **Crane selection** and **load chart verification**.
- **Wind speed forecast and limits**.
- **Exclusion zone** (personnel clearance).

### Step 3: Crane Selection & Verification
- Choose a crane with **Rated Capacity (RC)** at the required **radius** and **lift height** ≥ Block weight.
- Verify using the manufacturer's **Load Chart** (also called *Safe Working Load Chart*).
- Account for **dynamic factor** (typically **× 1.1 to × 1.25**) for sudden lifts or sea-state (in dry-dock, lower; on floating dock, higher).

### Step 4: Pre-Lift Checks
- **Weight ticket** vs. **crane chart** cross-check.
- **Sling / shackle inspection** (visual + tag verification).
- **Lifting lugs load-tested** (proof load = **2× SWL**).
- **Tag-line assignment** (ropes to control swing).
- **Communication protocol** (hand signals, radio, banksman).

### Step 5: Lift-Off
- **Tension all slings evenly** before lifting (avoid jerking).
- **Trial lift** to **150–300 mm** above ground → check for balance, swing, sling tensions.
- **Sling tension readings** (if load cells fitted) must match the rigging calculation within **±10 %**.

### Step 6: Transportation
- Move via **gantry crane travel** (along berth), OR
- **Land on skidway** and pull with **winches / hydraulic jacks**, OR
- **Load onto SPMT** and drive to erection site.

### Step 7: Setting-Up (Erection)
- **Block lowered onto berth supports / docking blocks**.
- **Alignment** to adjacent blocks using **theodolites, laser total stations, or iGPS** (precision: **±2 mm to ±5 mm**).
- **Tack welding** to hold position.
- **Strong-backs** may be temporarily fitted to resist welding shrinkage.

### Step 8: Final Welding & Inspection
- **Butt-welding** of seams between blocks (NDE: UT, RT, MPI as per classification society rules).
- **Block becomes part of the hull** — its structural identity ends.

## 2.2 Types of Cranes Used in Erection Area

| Crane Type | Capacity Range | Travel | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gantry Crane** (full ship length) | 300 – 1,200 t | On rails along berth | Primary erection crane in modern yards |
| **Portal Crane** (jib type) | 50 – 300 t | On rails / tyres | Mid-size blocks, outfit lifting |
| **Tower Crane** | 10 – 80 t | Fixed base, slewing | Vertical construction, superstructure |
| **Mobile Crane (Crawler)** | 100 – 1,000 t | Tracked, low ground pressure | Dry-dock, module yards, offshore |
| **Telescopic Boom Crane (Truck)** | 30 – 500 t | Roadable | Auxiliary lifts, outfit |
| **Sheerleg / Floating Crane** | 200 – 10,000 t | Sea-based | Mega-blocks, ship-section joining afloat |

## 2.3 Transport Methods (Beyond Crane-Lift)

1. **Skidding**: Block rests on **skid shoes** (Teflon-coated or greased) on **rail tracks**. Pulled by hydraulic winches. Used for very heavy units in ship-to-shore transfer.
2. **Bogie / Trailer (conventional)**: Multi-axle hydraulic trailer. Limited to ~**500 t** for road transport.
3. **Self-Propelled Modular Transporter (SPMT)**: Modular platform (e.g., **Scheuerle, Kamag, Nicolas**) with **4 to 6 axles per line**, hydraulically suspended, can be combined side-by-side and end-to-end. Capacity **up to 5,000+ t** (e.g., 192-axle SPMT). **Gold standard** for mega-block transport.
4. **Float-On / Float-Off (FLO/FLO)**: Block loaded onto a **semi-submersible barge** via ballasting. Used for very large sections (FPSO topsides, ship sections).
5. **Straddle Carrier**: Lifts block from above on a portal frame; used in pre-erection yards.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for Handling of Pre-Assembled Units (Module 4)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sling tension (vertical component)** | $T_v = \dfrac{W}{n}$ | $W$ = block weight, $n$ = number of sling legs | $T_v$ in tonnes-force (tf) or kN |
| 2 | **Sling tension (actual, with sling angle θ from horizontal)** | $T = \dfrac{T_v}{\sin\theta} = \dfrac{W}{n \sin\theta}$ | $\theta$ = sling angle from horizontal | $\theta \geq 60°$ preferred (so $\sin 60° = 0.866$) |
| 3 | **Sling horizontal force (compression on block)** | $H = T \cos\theta = \dfrac{W \cot\theta}{n}$ | Acts inward, crushes block | Critical if $\theta < 30°$ |
| 4 | **Crane required capacity** | $C_{req} = W \times F_s \times F_d$ | $F_s$ = static factor (≥ 1.25), $F_d$ = dynamic factor (1.1 – 1.25) | Compare with **$RC$** at required radius |
| 5 | **Center of Gravity (CoG) for asymmetric lift** | $x_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Weighted average of sub-assemblies | From 3D CAD model |
| 6 | **Sling tension (un-equal loading due to CoG offset)** | $T_i = \dfrac{W \cdot d_{opp}}{n \cdot d_{avg}}$ for a 4-leg system | If load is symmetric, all $T_i$ equal | If $d_{opp} \neq d_{avg}$ → one pair takes more load |
| 7 | **Wind heel moment on lifted block** | $M_w = F_w \cdot h$ | $F_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$, $h$ = CoG height | Limits lift in wind |
| 8 | **Wind force (per IS 875 / ISO 4302)** | $F_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | $\rho$ = air density (1.225 kg/m³), $C_d$ = drag coefficient (~1.2 for flat plate), $A$ = projected area (m²), $v$ = wind speed (m/s) | $F_w$ in Newtons |
| 9 | **Number of SPMT axles required** | $N_{axles} = \dfrac{W \cdot F_s}{P_{axle}}$ | $P_{axle}$ = per-axle capacity (typically 40–60 t) | Each "line" has 4, 5, or 6 axles |
| 10 | **Proof load on lifting lug** | $P_{proof} = 2 \times SWL$ | SWL = Safe Working Load | Mandatory test on every new lug |
| 11 | **Hook block + rigging mass deduction** | $W_{lift} = W_{block} + W_{rigging} + W_{hook}$ | Must be deducted from crane chart | Often **5–10 %** of block weight |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR KTU STUDENTS:**
> In KTU answer scripts, when using $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) or $\mid$, never write it as a **bare pipe** $\vert x \vert$ in a sentence — always use the LaTeX command $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ in math mode. In markdown notes, use $\lvert$ and $\rvert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **lifting, transport and erection** of pre-assembled units is the **physical heart of modern shipbuilding productivity**. Key engineering benefits include:

- **Productivity:** Block assembly in shop (with overhead cranes, jigs, turn-over devices) is **3×–5× faster** than stick-building on the berth.
- **Quality:** Down-hand welding in the shop gives **better NDT results** than overhead welding on the berth.
- **Safety:** Work at height is shifted to ground-level jigs; the erection lift is a planned, engineered operation rather than a high-risk build-up.
- **Schedule compression:** Parallel work — multiple blocks built simultaneously — reduces total build time.
- **Pre-outfitting** (machinery, pipes, cables) installed at block stage cuts **commissioning time by 30–50 %**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sling Tension with Sling Angle

### Problem Setup
A pre-assembled **double-bottom block** weighs $W = 200$ tonnes. It is to be lifted using a **4-leg sling** (one sling at each corner). The slings make an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. Find the tension in each sling leg and the horizontal compressive force on the block.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Vertical equilibrium of the lifted block.**

The four sling legs together support the full weight. The vertical component of the total sling tension equals the weight:

$$
\sum T_v = W
$$

For a *symmetric* 4-leg sling where all legs have equal angle $\theta$ and equal tension $T$:

$$
4 \cdot T \sin\theta = W
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for sling tension $T$.**

$$
T = \frac{W}{4 \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values for $\theta = 60°$.**

$$
T = \frac{200}{4 \times \sin 60°} = \frac{200}{4 \times 0.866} = \frac{200}{3.464}
$$

$$
T \approx 57.74 \text{ tonnes-force per leg}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values for $\theta = 30°$ (poor practice, shown for contrast).**

$$
T = \frac{200}{4 \times \sin 30°} = \frac{200}{4 \times 0.5} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ tf per leg}
$$

**Observation:** Sling tension **almost doubles** when the sling angle drops from 60° to 30°.

**Step 5 — Horizontal compressive force on the block.**

The horizontal component of each sling pulls **inward** on the block:

$$
H = T \cos\theta = \frac{W \cos\theta}{4 \sin\theta} = \frac{W \cot\theta}{4}
$$

For $\theta = 60°$:

$$
H = \frac{200 \times \cot 60°}{4} = \frac{200 \times 0.577}{4} \approx 28.87 \text{ tf (inward crushing force)}
$$

For $\theta = 30°$:

$$
H = \frac{200 \times \cot 30°}{4} = \frac{200 \times 1.732}{4} \approx 86.60 \text{ tf}
$$

**Conclusion:** A shallow sling angle **triples the horizontal crushing force** on the block — this can buckle thin plates. **KTU students must always prefer $\theta \geq 60°$.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wind Force on a Suspended Block

### Problem Setup
A side-shell block has projected area $A = 200$ m², drag coefficient $C_d = 1.2$, and is lifted when wind speed is $v = 12$ m/s. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Find the wind force and the overturning moment at the CoG, if CoG is at $h = 8$ m above the lift points.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the wind force equation (from drag theory).**

$$
F_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
F_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 1.2 \times 200 \times (12)^2
$$

$$
F_w = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1.2 \times 200 \times 144
$$

$$
F_w = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 240 \times 144
$$

$$
F_w = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 34{,}560
$$

$$
F_w = 0.5 \times 42{,}336 = 21{,}168 \text{ N} \approx 21.17 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to tonnes-force (1 tf ≈ 9.81 kN).**

$$
F_w = \frac{21.17}{9.81} \approx 2.16 \text{ tf}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate overturning moment at CoG (taking lift point as reference, lever arm = $h = 8$ m).**

$$
M_w = F_w \times h = 21.17 \text{ kN} \times 8 \text{ m} = 169.36 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the stabilising moment of the block weight around the edge of the block footprint.**

Assume footprint breadth $B = 12$ m. The block weight $W$ acts at CoG (centroid). The block will tip if:

$$
M_w > W \times \frac{B}{2}
$$

For $W = 200$ tf ≈ 1,962 kN:

$$
W \times \frac{B}{2} = 1{,}962 \times 6 = 11{,}772 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare moments.**

$$
M_w = 169.36 \text{ kN·m} \ll 11{,}772 \text{ kN·m}
$$

The block is **stable** in **12 m/s wind**. The wind limit is normally driven by **swing control** (tag-line forces) and **crane chart derating** in wind, not by overturning.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SPMT Axle Count for Mega-Block Transport

### Problem Setup
A mega-block weighs $W = 800$ tonnes (including rigging). Static safety factor $F_s = 1.25$. SPMT axle capacity $P_{axle} = 40$ tonnes. Each SPMT "line" has 4 axles. Find the number of SPMT lines required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the safety factor to the load.**

$$
W_{design} = W \times F_s = 800 \times 1.25 = 1{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the number of axles required.**

$$
N_{axles} = \frac{W_{design}}{P_{axle}} = \frac{1{,}000}{40} = 25 \text{ axles}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to lines of 4 axles each.**

$$
N_{lines} = \frac{N_{axles}}{4} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25
$$

**Step 4 — Round UP to the next whole number (mandatory).**

$$
N_{lines} = 7
$$

**Step 5 — Total capacity check.**

$$
\text{Capacity} = 7 \times 4 \times 40 = 1{,}120 \text{ tonnes} \geq 1{,}000 \text{ tonnes} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Result:** Use **7 SPMT lines (28 axles)**, typically arranged as **2×2 + 3×2** or similar configuration to balance the CoG.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Crane or SPMT?

| Criterion | Favours **Crane** | Favours **SPMT** |
|---|---|---|
| Block weight | < 1,200 t | > 500 t, up to several thousand t |
| Travel distance | Short (along gantry rail) | Long (pre-yard to berth, across yard) |
| Site access | Gantry covers erection bay | Open yard, multiple destinations |
| Time per move | Minutes | 1 – 4 hours per move |
| Cost per move | Lower (gantry is sunk cost) | Higher (SPMT rental / mobilisation) |
| Repeatability | High (same path) | Flexible routing |
| CoG accuracy | Slings can be re-adjusted | Hydraulic suspension re-levels on the move |
| Wind sensitivity | High (swinging load) | Low (load on ground) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Handling Flow in the Erection Area

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture flow** of handling a pre-assembled unit — from fabrication shop to final welding on the berth. It captures the interactions between methods, rigging, lifting, transport, alignment, and integration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Panel Fabrication Shop] --> B[Block Assembly Shop]
    B --> C[Pre-Outfitting Stage]
    C --> D[Weighbridge and CoG Survey]
    D --> E[Rigging Plan Preparation]
    E --> F[Pre-Lift Safety Check]
    F --> G{Crane vs SPMT Decision}
    G -->|Crane Lift| H[Gantry or Portal Crane]
    G -->|SPMT Transport| I[SPMT Hydraulic Platform]
    H --> J[Block Set-Up on Berth]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Alignment via Laser Total Station]
    K --> L[Tack Welding]
    L --> M[Strong-Back Fitting if Required]
    M --> N[Final Butt Welding]
    N --> O[NDT and Inspection]
    O --> P[Hull Integration Complete]
    P --> Q[Next Block Cycle Repeats]
```

flowchart LR
    subgraph HookBlock[Crane Hook Block]
        H1[Hook]
    end

    end

    end

## 4.3 Force Resolution on a Single Sling Leg

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Sling Tension T] --> M1[Vertical Component T sin theta]
    M --> M2[Horizontal Component T cos theta]
    M1 --> M1A[Supports weight W slash n]
    M2 --> M2A[Inward crushing force on block]
    M2A --> M2B[Risk if theta less than 30 deg]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Lift Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Block Ready for Erection] --> w[Input Block Weight W]
    w --> r[Input Lift Radius R]

    check -->|Yes| go[Proceed with Crane]

```
