# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define the term "pre-assembled unit" as used in modern shipbuilding. List any three advantages of pre-assembling ship sections before erection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **pre-assembled unit (PAU)** — also called a *ship block* or *pre-fabricated block* — is a three-dimensional structural assembly of welded plates, stiffeners, frames, bulkheads and outfit components that is built and partially outfitted in the panel/block assembly shop *before* being lifted and erected on the building berth (KTU 2024 Module 4 definition).

**Three advantages (1 mark each):**
1. **Higher productivity** — shop welding is 3–5× faster than berth welding.
2. **Better quality** — down-hand welding in jigs gives superior NDT results compared with overhead welding at height.
3. **Improved safety** — work is shifted from height to ground-level jigs; erection becomes a planned engineered lift.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is meant by "sling angle" in lifting operations? Why is a sling angle of at least 60° from the horizontal preferred in block-handling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **sling angle $\theta$** is the angle a sling leg makes with the *horizontal plane* at the lifting lug (KTU Module 4).

A **sling angle $\theta \geq 60°$** is preferred because (1 mark each):
1. **Sling tension is lower** — $T = W / (n \sin\theta)$ decreases as $\theta$ increases, so each leg carries less load.
2. **Horizontal crushing force on the block is lower** — $H = W \cot\theta / n$ decreases as $\theta$ increases, protecting thin plates from buckling.
3. **Load distribution is more vertical and predictable** — reducing swinging and the risk of the block slipping out of slings.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **sequence of operations involved in the handling of a pre-assembled unit in the erection area**, from the block assembly shop to its final integration on the building berth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pre-assembled **side-shell block** weighs **320 tonnes** and is to be lifted by a **4-leg sling** system. Each sling makes an **angle of 45°** with the horizontal. Calculate:
   (i) The **tension in each sling leg**. (3 marks)
   (ii) The **horizontal compressive force** acting on the block. (2 marks)
   (iii) The **minimum crane capacity required** at the working radius, assuming a static safety factor of 1.25 and a dynamic factor of 1.15. (2 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Sequence of operations (7 marks):**

1. **Block completion in shop** — panels joined into block; pre-outfitting (pipes, seats, cables) done. (1 mark)
2. **Weighing and CoG survey** — block weighed; CoG coordinates obtained from 3D CAD model. (1 mark)
3. **Rigging plan preparation** — sling configuration, lift points, crane selection from load chart, wind limits. (1 mark)
4. **Lifting gear inspection** — slings, shackles, lugs visually checked and tagged. (1 mark)
5. **Trial lift** to 200–300 mm; sling tension and balance verified. (1 mark)
6. **Lift and transport** — by gantry crane or via SPMT to erection site. (1 mark)
7. **Set-up, alignment, tack welding and final welding** — block lowered on docking supports, aligned by laser total station, tack-welded, then butt-welded with adjacent blocks. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Sling tension in each leg (3 marks):**

$$
T = \frac{W}{n \sin\theta} = \frac{320}{4 \times \sin 45°} = \frac{320}{4 \times 0.7071} = \frac{320}{2.828}
$$

$$
T \approx 113.13 \text{ tf per leg}
$$

**(ii) Horizontal compressive force (2 marks):**

$$
H = T \cos\theta = 113.13 \times \cos 45° = 113.13 \times 0.7071 \approx 80.0 \text{ tf}
$$

*(Equivalently, $H = \dfrac{W \cot\theta}{n} = \dfrac{320 \times 1}{4} = 80 \text{ tf}$.)*

**(iii) Required crane capacity (2 marks):**

$$
C_{req} = W \times F_s \times F_d = 320 \times 1.25 \times 1.15
$$

$$
C_{req} = 320 \times 1.4375 = 460 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Crane must have rated capacity ≥ 460 t** at the working radius, after deducting hook-block and rigging masses (typically 5–10 %).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **crane-based lifting** and **SPMT-based transport** for handling pre-assembled units in the erection area, with respect to **block weight, travel distance, time, cost, and wind sensitivity**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **double-bottom block** of weight **600 tonnes** is to be moved from the pre-erection shop to the building berth using SPMTs. Each SPMT **line has 4 axles**, and each axle has a **capacity of 45 tonnes**. Apply a **static safety factor of 1.25**. Determine:
   (i) The **total number of axles** required. (3 marks)
   (ii) The **number of SPMT lines** required. (2 marks)
   (iii) Comment on how the **CoG of the block** influences the axle-load distribution and what happens if the CoG is not centred over the SPMT platform. (2 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison table (7 marks — 1 mark per correct row + 2 marks for any one detail):**

| Criterion | Crane Lifting | SPMT Transport |
|---|---|---|
| Block weight | Up to ~1,200 t (gantry) | Up to 5,000+ t (mega-blocks) |
| Travel distance | Limited to gantry rail reach | Long, flexible routing across yard |
| Time per move | Minutes (fast for short moves) | 1–4 hours per move (slower) |
| Cost | Lower per move (gantry is sunk cost) | Higher (SPMT rental + crew) |
| Wind sensitivity | High — load swings, lift may be aborted | Low — load is on the ground |

(Any well-explained comparative point is awarded.)

**Part (b) — SPMT calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Total number of axles (3 marks):**

$$
W_{design} = 600 \times 1.25 = 750 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
N_{axles} = \frac{W_{design}}{P_{axle}} = \frac{750}{45} = 16.67 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 17
$$

**17 axles required** (2 marks for calculation + 1 mark for rounding up).

**(ii) Number of SPMT lines (2 marks):**

$$
N_{lines} = \frac{17}{4} = 4.25 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 5
$$

**5 SPMT lines (20 axles) required** (1 mark for calculation + 1 mark for rounding up).

**Final capacity check:** $5 \times 4 \times 45 = 900 \text{ t} \geq 750 \text{ t}$ — OK.

**(iii) Effect of CoG offset (2 marks):**

- SPMTs have **hydraulic suspension** that can be re-leveled, but the **load still has to be balanced** across the axles.
- If the **CoG is offset** from the geometric centre of the SPMT platform, **some axles carry more load** than others; the most-loaded axle may exceed its rating — risking tyre/bogie failure.
- The **rigging plan must include CoG offset compensation** — e.g., by using unequal-length slings, by adding **bogies under the heavy end**, or by **shifting the platform**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting to ROUND UP** when calculating axles / cranes / lines — examiners *will* deduct marks. The number of axles or lines must be an **integer ≥ required value**.
> 2. **Using the wrong sling-angle reference** — KTU answers sometimes confuse "angle with vertical" vs. "angle with horizontal". The KTU Module 4 convention is **angle with horizontal** ($\theta$).
> 3. **Ignoring dynamic factor** — just using $W$ is incomplete. A **static factor + dynamic factor** is expected in any crane-capacity question.
> 4. **Forgetting hook block / rigging deduction** — real-life Rated Capacity must be **after deduction**, but KTU questions usually give you a clean number to verify.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — KTU scripts demand **units** (tf, kN, tonnes). Marks lost for missing units.
> 6. **Conflating "block weight" with "lifted weight"** — the lifted weight includes **rigging, hook block, and spreader beam** (often 5–10 % more).
> 7. **Sling-angle in radians by mistake** — always in **degrees** unless the question specifies otherwise; $\sin 60° = 0.866$ is the typical KTU value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Revision — read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- **Pre-Assembled Unit (PAU) = Ship Block = 3-D welded subassembly built in shop before erection.**
- **Erection Area = berth + cranes + transporters + alignment equipment.**
- **Modern shipbuilding = Block philosophy** (replaces stick-building).
- **Block weights** range from **100 t** (small) to **1,500+ t** (mega-blocks).
- **Crane types:** Gantry (300–1,200 t), Portal, Tower, Mobile, Sheerleg.
- **Transport beyond crane:** Skidding, SPMT (40–60 t/axle), FLO/FLO barge, Straddle carrier.
- **Sling tension** $T = \dfrac{W}{n \sin\theta}$ — **always prefer $\theta \geq 60°$.**
- **Horizontal crushing force** $H = \dfrac{W \cot\theta}{n}$ — **doubled or tripled if $\theta$ drops below 30°**.
- **Crane capacity** $C_{req} = W \times F_s \times F_d$ — typical $F_s = 1.25$, $F_d = 1.15$.
- **Wind force** $F_w = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ — limits lifting to **$v \leq 15$ m/s** typically.
- **SPMT axles:** $N_{axles} = \dfrac{W \cdot F_s}{P_{axle}}$ — **always round up**.
- **CoG offset** redistributes sling/axle loads — must be compensated in the rigging plan.
- **Lifting lug proof load = 2 × SWL** (mandatory).
- **Trial lift** to 200–300 mm is a **mandatory pre-lift step**.
- **Alignment** uses **laser total station** or **iGPS** (precision ±2–5 mm).
- **Strong-backs** are temporary fittings to control **welding shrinkage distortion**.
- **Tag-lines** control **swing** during the lift.
- **Wind, CoG, sling angle, dynamic factor** — the **four pillars** of safe block handling.
- **KTU Module 4 weightage** typically has **1 short Q (3 marks)** + **1 long Q (14 marks)** covering this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hull Assembly - Different Methods of Hull Assembly. Block Erection Methods - Floor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hull Assembly - Different Methods of Hull Assembly. Block Erection Methods - Floor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Hull Assembly?

**Hull Assembly** is the critical phase in shipbuilding where all prefabricated structural components—plates, frames, stiffeners, bulkheads, and sub-assemblies—are progressively joined together to form the watertight shell (hull) of a vessel. It is the stage that transforms a collection of cut and shaped steel parts into a recognizable, three-dimensional ship structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Hull assembly refers to the systematic joining of fabricated hull components either in a single sequence on the berth/slipway or, more commonly today, in the form of large pre-assembled **blocks** or **units** that are transported and erected to form the complete hull.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are building a **giant 3D jigsaw puzzle** of a whale-shaped steel bathtub, but you are allowed to first build it as **separate Lego "mega-blocks"** in different rooms of your house, and then use a giant crane to bring those blocks together and snap them into a single complete structure.

- The **cut steel plates** = individual Lego pieces
- The **small sub-assemblies (panels, units)** = small Lego constructions
- The **blocks/sections** = the mega-blocks built in separate workshops
- The **giant crane (Goliath crane)** = the tool that brings it all together
- The **slipway/dry dock** = the final floor where everything is connected

This Lego-block philosophy is the heart of **modern hull assembly**, replacing the old "piece-small" method where every plate was fitted directly on the slipway from keel upwards.

## 1.3 Why Hull Assembly Method Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of hull assembly method directly affects:
> - **Total construction time** (faster = more ships/year = more revenue)
> - **Welding quality** (workshop welds > out-of-position slipway welds)
> - **Labor cost** (less on-site work = lower cost)
> - **Material handling** (fewer lifts, more efficient cranes)
> - **Safety** (less work at height on open slipway)

The standard industrial target in modern shipyards is to complete the hull to **80% outfitting completion before erection**, and to launch a ship with **up to 60-70% outfitting** already done.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hull Assembly Methods

The evolution of hull assembly can be grouped into **three major eras**, each corresponding to a construction philosophy:

| Era | Method | Block Size | Modern Use |
|-----|--------|-----------|-----------|
| **Era 1 (Pre-1950s)** | **Piece-Small Method** | Individual plates & frames | Obsolete |
| **Era 2 (1950s–1970s)** | **Unit / Panel Method** | Panels & small units | Rarely used |
| **Era 3 (Modern)** | **Block Construction Method** | Large 3D blocks (200–800 T) | Universal |

### 2.1.1 Piece-Small Method (Traditional / "On-Berth" Method)

- Hull is built **plate-by-plate** directly on the building berth.
- Construction begins at the **keel** and progresses **upward and outward** (and forward/aft).
- Every plate, frame, and stiffener is **individually lifted** into place and welded.
- Used historically for **warships** and very small vessels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Excessive out-of-position welding, slow cycle time, dependence on weather, and high accident risk on the open berth.

### 2.1.2 Unit / Panel Method

- Flat **panels** (plating + stiffeners welded flat on a jig) are prefabricated in a workshop.
- These panels are then erected on the slipway, joined along their seams.
- The hull grows in **two dimensions first** (flat panels) before being curved into three dimensions on the berth.
- A **transitional method** between piece-small and full block construction.

### 2.1.3 Block Construction Method (Modern Standard)

- The hull is divided into a **grid of large 3D blocks** (sometimes called *sections*, *sub-blocks*, or *units*).
- Each block is **fully welded, painted, and outfitted** in a workshop with overhead cranes, jigs, and turn-over facilities.
- Blocks are then transported to the building dock/slipway and **erected** by a Goliath crane.
- Block weights today routinely reach **300–800 tonnes**; super-block concepts exceed **1,000 tonnes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:**
> Modern hull assembly is essentially a logistics problem: the **Block Erection Sequence (BES)** is pre-planned digitally (4D simulation) before a single plate is cut.

## 2.2 Block Construction: Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Sub-block** | Smallest 3D assembly (e.g., a double-bottom panel bent into a U). |
| **Block** | A large 3D assembly made by joining several sub-blocks. |
| **Mega-block / Grand block** | Very large assembly covering multiple decks; often exceeds crane capacity. |
| **Island** | A block erected temporarily and then connected to neighbouring erected blocks. |
| **Erection** | The act of lifting and placing a block in its final position on the berth/dock. |
| **Joint / Block joint** | The welded seam between two adjacent blocks. |
| **Pre-erection outfitting** | Outfitting work done at ground level inside the block before erection. |

## 2.3 Different Methods of Hull Assembly (Schematic)

```
Traditional (Piece-Small)        Modern (Block)
─────────────────────            ───────────────────
Keel laid first                  Many blocks built in parallel
│                                │
Frames erected                   Blocks lifted by Goliath crane
│                                │
Plating added piece-by-piece     Blocks welded together on dock
│                                │
Hull grows vertically            Hull grows in 3D from many blocks
                                  joined simultaneously
```

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Hull Assembly Planning)

Most hull assembly planning is qualitative/logistical, but a few engineering relationships are testable:

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Purpose |
|--------------------|-----------|-------|---------|
| **Block Weight (W_b)** | $W_b = V_b \cdot \rho_s \cdot g$ | tonnes (t) | Crane capacity check |
| **Block Centre of Gravity offset (e)** | $e = \dfrac{\sum W_i \cdot x_i}{\sum W_i}$ | m | Crane slinging geometry |
| **Erection Productivity Index** | $P_e = \dfrac{\text{Tonnage erected}}{\text{Man-hours}}$ | t/man-hour | Yard efficiency KPI |
| **Block Lift Capacity Check** | $W_{crane} \ge W_b \cdot SF$ | t | $SF$ = safety factor (typically 1.25) |
| **Launch Block Sling Tension (T_s)** | $T_s = \dfrac{W_b}{2 \cos \theta}$ | t | $\theta$ = sling angle from vertical |
| **Keel Block Reaction (during erection)** | $R_k = \dfrac{W_{hull}}{N_{kb}}$ | t/block | $N_{kb}$ = number of keel blocks |
| **Block Welding Shrinkage Allowance** | $\delta_s = k_s \cdot L_{joint}$ | mm | $k_s$ ≈ 0.001 to 0.002 per joint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, memorize the **sling tension formula** $T_s = \dfrac{W_b}{2\cos\theta}$ — it is the single most-tested numerical in module 4.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The block method is what allows South Korean and Japanese shipyards to deliver a **VLCC in 14–16 months** and a **LNG carrier in 28–30 months**.
- The same philosophy is used in **offshore platform construction**, **bridge building** (segmental construction), and even **aerospace fuselage assembly** (Boeing/Airbus).
- The block joint is the **weakest link** structurally — its design quality governs fatigue life of the hull girder.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sling Tension During Block Lift

When a Goliath crane lifts a block using two slings, the geometry determines the load on each sling. This is the **most-asked KTU numerical** in this module.

### Step 1 — Define the Geometry
Consider a symmetric two-sling lift. Let:
- $W_b$ = total weight of the block (tonnes)
- $\theta$ = angle each sling makes with the **vertical**
- The slings are attached at the top of the block and meet at the crane hook above.

### Step 2 — Force Equilibrium (Vertical Direction)
The vertical components of the two sling tensions must balance the block weight.

$$2 T_s \cos\theta = W_b$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Sling Tension

$$T_s = \frac{W_b}{2 \cos\theta}$$

### Step 4 — Verify the Limiting Cases
- If $\theta = 0°$ (slings vertical): $T_s = W_b/2$ — **minimum tension, safest**.
- If $\theta = 60°$: $T_s = W_b$ — tension equals the full block weight.
- If $\theta \to 90°$: $T_s \to \infty$ — **slings fail / block swings**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design rule (KTU):** $\theta$ is always kept **≤ 45°**, so that $T_s \le 0.707 \cdot W_b$, ensuring comfortable margin on sling capacity.

### Step 5 — Worked Example
A block weighs $W_b = 400$ tonnes. Slings are rigged at $\theta = 30°$ from vertical. Find the sling tension.

$$T_s = \frac{400}{2 \cos 30°} = \frac{400}{2 \times 0.866} = \frac{400}{1.732}$$

$$T_s = 230.9 \text{ tonnes per sling}$$

So each sling carries **≈ 231 t**, requiring a sling rated to at least $231 \times 1.25 = 289$ t working load.

## 3.2 Derivation: Erection Productivity

Yard productivity in block construction is measured as tonnage erected per man-hour.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables
- $M_e$ = total man-hours spent on erection (welding, fitting, NDT, crane time converted to man-hours)
- $T_e$ = total tonnage of blocks erected in that period

### Step 2 — Productivity Formula

$$P_e = \frac{T_e}{M_e} \quad \text{[t / man-hour]}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
- **Top Korean yards:** $P_e \approx 0.8$ – $1.2$ t/man-hour
- **Average global yard:** $P_e \approx 0.3$ – $0.5$ t/man-hour
- **Traditional piece-small yard:** $P_e < 0.15$ t/man-hour

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Assembly Methods

| Parameter | Piece-Small | Unit / Panel | Block (Modern) |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Welding position** | Mostly overhead/vertical | Mixed | Mostly flat/down-hand |
| **Weld quality** | Low to medium | Medium | High |
| **Cycle time** | 18–30 months | 12–18 months | 6–14 months |
| **Outfitting % at launch** | ~20% | ~40% | ~60–80% |
| **Crane dependency** | Low | Medium | Very high (Goliath) |
| **Weather sensitivity** | Very high | Medium | Low (workshop-based) |
| **Capital cost** | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Work-at-height risk** | Very high | High | Low |
| **Throughput** | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Used for** | Heritage, naval | Mid-size yards | All modern yards |

## 3.4 Block Erection Sequence (BES) Logic

The order in which blocks are placed on the building dock follows a logical matrix:

1. **Bottom blocks (double bottom, outer bottom)** are erected first along the central line of the dock.
2. **Side shell blocks (port and starboard)** are then lifted and welded to the bottom blocks.
3. **Deck blocks** follow, working outward from the centreline.
4. **Superstructure / deckhouse blocks** are added at the top.
5. **Engine room and bow/stern blocks** are integrated as the sequence allows.
6. The sequence proceeds **from the middle of the ship outward**, OR **from bow-to-stern**, OR **from centre-to-ends** depending on yard philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Construction Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steel Plates and Sections Received] --> B[Cutting and Shaping Workshop]
    B --> C[Sub-block Assembly Shop]
    C --> D[Block Assembly Shop with Jigs]
    D --> E[Pre-erection Outfitting and Painting]
    E --> F[Block Transported to Goliath Crane]
    F --> G[Block Lifted and Placed on Building Dock]
    G --> H[Block Welded to Adjacent Blocks]
    H --> I[NDT and Inspection of Block Joints]
    I --> J[Continued Erection of Next Blocks]
    J --> K[Complete Hull Formed on Dock]
    K --> L[Dock Flooded or Ship Launched]
```

## 4.2 Hull Assembly Method Comparison (Mermaid Graph)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Hull Assembly Methods] --> B[Piece Small Method]
    A --> C[Unit or Panel Method]
    A --> D[Block Construction Method]
    B --> B1[Plate by Plate]
    B --> B2[Keel Upward]
    B --> B3[Obsolete Today]
    C --> C1[Flat Panels First]
    C --> C2[Curved Later on Berth]
    C --> C3[Transitional Era]
    D --> D1[Large 3D Blocks]
    D --> D2[Goliath Crane Lift]
    D --> D3[Modern Universal Method]
    D1 --> D4[Pre-outfitted]
    D2 --> D5[Block Joint Welded]
    D3 --> D6[Faster Cycle Time]
```

## 4.3 Block Erection Sequence on Building Dock (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Dock["Building Dock Layout"]
        direction TB
        L1[Step 1: Centre Bottom Block Laid]
        L2[Step 2: Port and Starboard Bottom Blocks]
        L3[Step 3: Bilge and Lower Side Shell Blocks]
        L4[Step 4: Upper Side Shell Blocks]
        L5[Step 5: Deck and Platform Blocks]
        L6[Step 6: Superstructure and Deckhouse Blocks]
        L7[Step 7: Bow and Stern Integrations]
        L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7
    end
```

## 4.4 Floor Placement in Hull Cross-Section (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Keel Plate] --> B[Floor Plate Transverse]
    B --> C[Bottom Shell Plating Port]
    B --> D[Bottom Shell Plating Starboard]
    C --> E[Side Frame Vertical]
    D --> F[Side Frame Vertical]
    E --> G[Side Shell Plating]
    F --> H[Side Shell Plating]
    G --> I[Sheer Strake at Deck Edge]
    H --> J[Sheer Strake at Deck Edge]
    B -.Welded to.-> A
    B -.Provides Transverse Strength.-> C
    B -.Provides Transverse Strength.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above shows the **floor** as the **transverse bottom frame** running port-to-starboard, perpendicular to the keel. In modern double-bottom ships, the floor is usually a **gusset plate** connecting the port and starboard bottom longitudinals — see Section 5 for the detailed types.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*List any three advantages of the block construction method of hull assembly over the piece-small method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher welding quality** – Most block welding is done in flat/down-hand position in workshops rather than overhead/vertical on the slipway (1 mark).
2. **Reduced cycle time** – Parallel production of blocks in multiple shops allows the hull to be assembled in months rather than years (1 mark).
3. **Improved safety and lower labour cost** – Most work is done at ground level indoors, eliminating work-at-height and weather exposure (1 mark).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*What is a "floor" in ship construction? Mention its primary function.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **floor** is the **transverse bottom frame** of a ship, running **port-to-starboard** across the bottom of the hull and connecting the two sides of the bottom plating through the centreline (1 mark).
- It is usually a **plate** (solid floor) or a **brackets + gusset** combination welded to the keel and to the bottom plating (1 mark).
- **Primary function:** to provide **transverse strength** to the hull girder, support the bottom shell plating against water pressure, and transmit loads between the port and starboard sides of the hull (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **block construction method of hull assembly**, with a neat sketch of the erection sequence on a building dock. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A block weighs **500 tonnes** and is to be lifted by two slings making an angle of **30°** with the vertical. Calculate: (i) the tension in each sling, and (ii) the minimum sling capacity if a safety factor of **1.5** is required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Block Construction Method (7 marks):**

The **block construction method** is the modern universal technique of hull assembly where the complete hull is divided into a **grid of large 3-dimensional blocks**, each weighing from a few hundred to over a thousand tonnes. These blocks are fully fabricated, welded, and outfitted in **workshops** before being transported to the building dock and erected by a **Goliath crane**.

**Stages of the method:**

1. **Planning and Division (1 mark):**
   The hull is digitally divided (CAD/4D simulation) into hundreds of blocks. Division respects:
   - Crane capacity (block weight ≤ crane safe working load).
   - Transportation route (block dimensions fit road/waterway).
   - Minimum number of block joints (longer blocks = fewer joints).

2. **Sub-block Fabrication (1 mark):**
   Cut plates are first welded into small sub-blocks (panels, T-sections, U-panels for double bottoms) in workshops.

3. **Block Assembly (1 mark):**
   Sub-blocks are joined in jigs to form full 3D blocks. Welding is mostly in the **flat (1G/1F) position** — the highest-quality weld.

4. **Pre-erection Outfitting (1 mark):**
   Pipes, cables, machinery seats, insulation, and paint are installed **inside the block at ground level**. This is the single biggest time-saver of the method — typically **60–80% of outfitting** is done before launch.

5. **Erection on Building Dock (2 marks):**
   - The **central bottom block** is laid first on keel blocks.
   - **Port and starboard bottom blocks** are lifted and welded.
   - **Bilge and side shell blocks** are added.
   - **Deck and platform blocks** follow.
   - **Superstructure blocks** are placed last.
   - The sequence is: **centreline → outward**, or **mid-ship → ends**, depending on the yard.
   - Adjacent blocks are joined by **block joint welding** (typically full-strength butt welds, 100% NDT).

6. **Inspection and Launch (1 mark):**
   - NDT (radiographic, ultrasonic, magnetic particle) is performed on every block joint.
   - The completed hull is launched (gravitational or mechanical) and towed to the fitting-out quay.

**Sketch of Erection Sequence (ASCII representation for answer sheet):**

```
   STEP 1          STEP 2          STEP 3          STEP 4
  ________        ________        ________        ________
 |        |      |   |   |      |   |   |      |___|___|
 |  KEEL  |  →  | B | S |  →  |DBL|SHL|  →  |___|___|
 |________|      |___|___|      |___|___|      |___|___|
                                       ↑
                          B = Bottom, S = Side, DBL = Deck
```

(Students may draw a more elaborate step-wise sequence on the answer sheet using a 5-step side view.)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Block weight $W_b = 500$ tonnes
- Sling angle from vertical $\theta = 30°$
- Two slings, symmetric
- Safety factor $SF = 1.5$

**(i) Tension in each sling (3 marks):**

The vertical equilibrium of the block:

$$2 T_s \cos\theta = W_b$$

$$T_s = \frac{W_b}{2 \cos\theta} = \frac{500}{2 \cos 30°}$$

$$T_s = \frac{500}{2 \times 0.8660} = \frac{500}{1.7321}$$

$$T_s = 288.68 \text{ tonnes per sling}$$

**(ii) Minimum sling capacity (4 marks):**

$$\text{Minimum Sling Capacity} = T_s \times SF$$

$$C_{min} = 288.68 \times 1.5 = 433.02 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Answer:** Each sling experiences **288.68 t** of tension, and the minimum safe working capacity of each sling must be **≈ 433 t**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the **"piece-small" method of hull assembly**. Compare it with the **"block construction method"** in terms of welding quality, cycle time, and safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **different types of floors** used in ship construction, with neat sketches. State where each type is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Piece-Small Method and Comparison (7 marks):**

The **piece-small method** is the **traditional hull assembly technique** in which individual plates, frames, and stiffeners are erected and welded one at a time on the building berth, starting from the **keel** and progressing **upward, outward, forward, and aft** until the entire hull shell is completed.

**Steps (3 marks):**
1. Keel plate is laid on keel blocks.
2. Centre girder, floors, and bottom longitudinals are erected.
3. Bottom shell plates are fitted plate-by-plate.
4. Side frames, side shell plates, and decks are added progressively.
5. Superstructure is built last.
6. Outfitting is done mostly **after launch** on the fitting-out quay.

**Comparison with Block Construction (4 marks — 1.5 + 1.5 + 1):**

| Aspect | Piece-Small | Block Construction |
|--------|------------|-------------------|
| **Welding quality** | Mostly overhead and vertical welds (lower quality, more defects) | Mostly flat (1G/1F) welds (highest quality) |
| **Cycle time** | 18–30 months for a mid-size vessel | 6–14 months for the same vessel |
| **Safety** | High work-at-height exposure; weather-dependent; high accident rate | Most work at ground level; indoor; much lower accident rate |

The block method is therefore **clearly superior** in all three aspects, which is why no modern commercial yard uses pure piece-small construction today.

---

**(b) Types of Floors in Ship Construction (7 marks):**

The **floor** is the **transverse structural member** running across the bottom of the ship, from the port side bottom shell to the starboard side bottom shell, **perpendicular to the keel**. It provides **transverse strength** and **local support** to the bottom plating.

**1. Solid Plate Floor (1.5 marks):**
- A single flat plate welded across the bottom, typically with a **centreline slot** or **lightening hole**.
- Most common in **single-bottom ships** and in **double-bottom ships** between the centre girder and the side girders.
- Provides maximum transverse strength.

**Sketch:**
```
   _______________
  |               |
  |     SLOT      |
  |_______________|   ← solid plate floor
  |               |
  |_______________|  ← bottom shell plating
```

**2. Bracket Floor (1.5 marks):**
- Made of **two triangular brackets** welded back-to-back, with a **centre gusset plate** for stiffening.
- Used in **single-bottom ships** with longitudinal framing where a continuous plate floor would interfere.
- Lighter than a solid floor.

**Sketch:**
```
   /\    /\
  /  \  /  \
 /____\/____\
 |             |
```

**3. Open Floor (1.5 marks):**
- Consists of an **angle or T-section frame** crossing the bottom, with a **small gusset plate** at the centreline.
- No continuous plate — hence "open".
- Common in **small vessels** and in **double-bottom ships** at every frame station.

**4. Intercostal Floor (1.5 marks):**
- A **short floor plate** fitted **between two bottom longitudinals** (does not span the full width of the bottom).
- Used in **double-bottom ships** with longitudinal framing to provide local transverse stiffening without interfering with the longitudinals.

**Sketch (intercostal):**
```
   keel  ───────────────
         |    |    |
         |    |    |    ← intercostal floor (short)
         |    |    |
         ─────|─────    ← bottom longitudinal
         |    |    |
```

**Where Each Type is Preferred (1 mark — wrap-up):**
- **Solid floor:** Single-bottom ships; double-bottom wing spaces.
- **Bracket floor:** Single-bottom longitudinal-framed ships.
- **Open floor:** Small vessels; double-bottom ships at frame stations.
- **Intercostal floor:** Double-bottom ships with longitudinal framing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Module 4:**
> 1. **Sling-tension numericals** – Forgetting that $T_s = W_b / (2 \cos\theta)$, not $W_b / (2 \sin\theta)$. The angle is measured from the **vertical**, not the horizontal.
> 2. **Confusing "floor" with "frame"** – A floor is **transverse and at the bottom only**; frames are transverse and on the sides/decks. Drawing a frame in answer to a floor question is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing block types** – In Part B answers, students often describe solid, bracket, open, and intercostal floors as if they are interchangeable. Each has a **specific ship-type application** — losing 1 mark if the application is missing.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch** – KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a labelled sketch in any descriptive question. A textual answer without a sketch caps your marks.
> 5. **Outfitting % at launch** – Always quote the **60–80% pre-outfitting** figure for block construction; this is a favourite one-liner question.
> 6. **Erection sequence** – The standard answer is **bottom → sides → decks → superstructure**, in that order. Reversing it loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Hull Assembly & Floor)**

- **Hull assembly** = joining all prefabricated hull components to form the watertight ship shell.
- **Three methods:** Piece-Small, Unit/Panel, Block Construction (modern standard).
- **Block construction** = hull divided into 3D blocks built in workshops, erected by Goliath crane on building dock.
- **Block size** is governed by **crane capacity**, **transport route**, and **minimum block-joint count**.
- **Pre-erection outfitting** (60–80% of total) is the single biggest advantage of block construction.
- **Block erection sequence:** centreline bottom → port/starboard bottom → bilge/side shell → decks → superstructure → bow/stern.
- **Erection methods:** bottom-up (most common), top-down (rare, large ships), pioneer/island (separate islands joined later).
- **Sling tension formula:** $T_s = \dfrac{W_b}{2 \cos\theta}$ — angle measured from **vertical**.
- **Safety factor** for slings: typically **1.25 to 1.5**; angle $\theta$ should not exceed **45°**.
- **Productivity KPI:** $P_e = T_e / M_e$ in t/man-hour; modern yards achieve **0.5–1.2 t/man-hour**.
- **Block joints** are the **weakest fatigue links** of the hull girder — always 100% NDT.
- **Floor** = transverse bottom frame, runs **port-to-starboard**, perpendicular to keel.
- **Floor types:** **Solid plate floor**, **bracket floor**, **open floor**, **intercostal floor**.
- **Solid floor** → single-bottom ships, double-bottom wing spaces.
- **Bracket floor** → single-bottom longitudinal-framed ships.
- **Open floor** → small vessels, double-bottom frame stations.
- **Intercostal floor** → short plate between bottom longitudinals in double-bottom longitudinal-framed ships.
- **Floor functions:** transverse strength, support to bottom plating, load transmission between port and starboard sides.
- **Modern shipyards** (Korea, Japan, China) deliver a VLCC in **14–16 months** purely due to efficient block construction.
- **KTU golden rule:** in any block-construction answer, always mention the **Goliath crane**, the **building dock/slipway**, and the **60–80% pre-outfitting** number.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ring and Pyramid. Single/Multi Point Erection Methods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ring and Pyramid. Single/Multi Point Erection Methods

# Ring and Pyramid. Single/Multi Point Erection Methods

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Ship Erection Problem — Setting the Stage

Ship erection is the **final assembly stage** in modern shipbuilding, where pre-fabricated blocks, panels, sections, and sub-assemblies (manufactured in workshops and on the berth) are joined together to form the complete hull structure. The sequence, orientation, and location at which these units are brought together is governed by the **Erection Method** (also called the *Erection Philosophy* or *Block Erection Plan*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Erection methods* are systematic construction philosophies that define the **order, location, and direction** in which hull sub-assemblies are assembled on the building berth (or in dry dock) to achieve the complete ship. The four classical philosophies are: **Ring Erection, Pyramid Erection, Single-Point Erection, and Multi-Point Erection.**

### 1.2 The Four Classical Erection Methods — Bird's-Eye View

| # | Method | Construction Axis | Direction of Build | Typical Vessel |
|---|--------|-------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Ring Erection** | Transverse (frame-wise) | Section-by-section from midship outward | Small/medium warships, submarines |
| 2 | **Pyramid Erection** | Mixed (transverse + longitudinal) | Central block → outward in pyramid shape | Tankers, bulk carriers |
| 3 | **Single-Point Erection** | Longitudinal (one centerline) | From one end of the ship to the other | Small coastal vessels |
| 4 | **Multi-Point Erection** | Longitudinal (multiple stations) | From several stations concurrently | All modern large vessels |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of erection method is governed by: **ship size, hull form complexity, available cranes, berth length, workforce availability, and delivery schedule**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "How a Ship is Built Like a Skeleton"

Imagine you are assembling a giant **dinosaur skeleton** in a museum hall:

- **Ring Erection** = Build the *ribcage ring-by-ring* (like stacking barrels), then string the spine through them.
- **Pyramid Erection** = Build the *central body block* first, then add rings around it in expanding layers (like a wedding cake being decorated outward).
- **Single-Point Erection** = Start at the *tail* and assemble bone-by-bone moving towards the *head* along one straight line.
- **Multi-Point Erection** = Multiple teams start at *tail, middle, and head simultaneously*, joining in the middle like express trains converging.

The skeleton metaphor helps visualize the **geometry of progress** rather than the actual welding sequence.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Erection Planning

- **Erection Tolerance:** $\pm$ **3 mm** per block, $\pm$ **6 mm** cumulative per 100 m length (per IACS S21/S22 standards).
- **Block Lift Weight Range:** **20 T to 600 T** for modern Goliath / Hammerhead cranes.
- **Berth Occupancy Time (BOT):** Modern target — **45 to 90 days** depending on vessel size.

> [!TIP]
> KTU students should memorize: *Modern shipyards (Hyundai, Daewoo, Cochin Shipyard) almost universally use the **Multi-Point Erection** method on inclined building berths (IACS angle ≈ **1/100 to 1/60 slope**).*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 RING ERECTION METHOD (Transverse Section Method)

**Definition:** The hull is constructed by assembling complete transverse sections (rings) one after another, with each ring being a fully welded cross-section of the ship containing shell plating, decks, bulkheads, frames, and stiffeners at one station.

#### 2.1.1 Operational Philosophy

1. The **midship section** is erected first on the building berth.
2. Adjacent rings are welded on either side, moving **forward and aft simultaneously**.
3. The longitudinal strength members (keel, sheer strake, deck stringers) are integrated as each ring is completed.
4. Ring erection proceeds until the **stem and stern appendages** are added.

#### 2.1.2 Construction Sequence (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1 — Ground Frame Laying:** The first ring's transverse frame skeleton is erected on the berth chocks.
- **Step 2 — Shell Plating:** Bottom, side, and deck shell plates of that ring are positioned and tack-welded.
- **Step 3 — Internal Structure:** Frames, bulkheads, stiffeners, and longitudinals within that ring are welded.
- **Step 4 — Ring Closure:** The ring is fully welded and inspected (NDT — Ultrasonic, Radiographic).
- **Step 5 — Progression:** The next adjacent ring is erected and welded to the first.
- **Step 6 — Completion:** The bow and stern rings are last, fitted with stem and stern frame castings.

#### 2.1.3 Mathematical Foundation — Ring Geometry

Each ring is a **truncated frustum** (or prismatic section) of the hull. The geometric parameters of a typical ring are:

$$A_{\text{ring}} = \pi \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2) \quad \text{[m}^2\text{]}$$

where:
- $A_{\text{ring}}$ = cross-sectional area of the ring
- $R_o$ = outer radius of hull at that station
- $R_i$ = inner radius (radius to inside of plating)

For a ship-shaped ring (non-circular), the **wetted surface area** of a single ring is approximated by:

$$S_{\text{ring}} = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{1}{2}\left[R_o^2(\theta) - R_i^2(\theta)\right] d\theta$$

For a ring of **frame spacing $L_f$** (typically **0.6 m to 1.0 m** in modern practice), the **number of rings** in a ship of length $L_{bp}$ is:

$$N_{\text{rings}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{L_f} + 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 'Ring'?** Each complete transverse section resembles a giant **steel ring** (with the keel cut at the bottom) — hence the name.

#### 2.1.4 Advantages & Disadvantages of Ring Erection

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------------|---------------|
| High dimensional accuracy (each ring is self-aligned) | Requires heavy lift cranes for whole rings |
| Easy alignment of transverse members | Limited to small/medium ships (rings become too heavy) |
| Reduced distortion (symmetric welding) | Slow longitudinal strength build-up |
| Excellent for curved hull forms (submarines) | Berth space inefficient |

---

### 2.2 PYRAMID ERECTION METHOD

**Definition:** Construction starts with a **central hull block (the "island")** at midship, and additional sections are erected around it in a **pyramidal progression** — both transversely (outboard) and longitudinally (fore and aft).

#### 2.2.1 Operational Philosophy

1. A **double-bottom + side shell + deck block** is fabricated at the midship region first.
2. From this central block, erection proceeds **upward, downward, forward, and aft** simultaneously.
3. The growing structure looks like a **pyramid** at every stage of construction.
4. The work front expands radially from the central erection point.

#### 2.2.2 The "Pyramid Geometry" — Mathematical Model

The pyramid erection creates a **tetrahedral growth front**. If the central block has a base length $L_0$ and width $B_0$, then at any time $t$, the construction front is bounded by:

$$L(t) = L_0 + 2 \cdot v_t \cdot t$$

$$B(t) = B_0 + 2 \cdot v_b \cdot t$$

$$H(t) = H_0 + v_h \cdot t$$

where:
- $v_t$ = longitudinal erection velocity (m/day)
- $v_b$ = transverse erection velocity (m/day)
- $v_h$ = vertical erection velocity (m/day)
- $t$ = time in days

The total **volume of erected steel** at time $t$ is:

$$V(t) = L(t) \cdot B(t) \cdot H(t)$$

#### 2.2.3 Construction Sequence

- **Step 1 — Foundation Block:** Double-bottom panel with centerline girder is erected.
- **Step 2 — Side Shells:** Port and starboard side shell panels are joined to the foundation.
- **Step 3 — Deck Panels:** Main deck and platform decks are added — completing the **central island**.
- **Step 4 — Pyramid Growth:** Adjacent ring-sections (forward/aft) and tier-sections (above/below) are welded to the island, expanding the pyramid.
- **Step 5 — Bow & Stern Closure:** The pyramid front reaches the stem and stern, which are added as final pieces.

#### 2.2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------------|---------------|
| Combines accuracy of ring method with speed of zone method | Complex scheduling — many concurrent work fronts |
| Excellent longitudinal strength build-up | Requires skilled workforce coordination |
| Suitable for large vessels (tankers, bulkers) | Heavy plate distortion risk in the central block |
| Allows parallel work in multiple zones | Expensive initial block investment |

> [!NOTE]
> The pyramid method is the **direct ancestor** of modern **Zone Construction / Outfitting-During-Erection (ODE)** philosophy. Many textbooks call the pyramid method a transitional philosophy between pure ring erection and modern multi-point erection.

---

### 2.3 SINGLE-POINT ERECTION METHOD (Longitudinal, One-End Start)

**Definition:** The hull is erected along a **single longitudinal line** (typically the centerline), starting from **one end** of the ship (usually the stern) and progressing continuously to the other end (the bow).

#### 2.3.1 Operational Philosophy

1. A **ground ways / building slip** is prepared with the keel blocks laid.
2. The **stern frame casting** is positioned first.
3. Plates, frames, and sections are erected **sequentially along the centerline**, moving forward.
4. The ship "grows" like a caterpillar from stern to bow.

#### 2.3.2 Construction Sequence

- **Step 1 — Keel Laying:** Centerline keel plates are positioned on the berth.
- **Step 2 — Stern Frame:** Stern frame casting (or stern tube) is erected and aligned.
- **Step 3 — Aft Sections:** Aft peak, engine room, and accommodation blocks are erected.
- **Step 4 — Forward Progression:** Sections are added sequentially moving toward the bow.
- **Step 5 — Bow Closure:** Stem casting and bulbous bow (if any) are welded last.
- **Step 6 — Launching:** The ship is launched (sideways or end-launch) when fully erected.

#### 2.3.3 Mathematical Model — Linear Progression

The position of the work-front at time $t$ measured from the stern is:

$$x(t) = x_0 + v_e \cdot t$$

where:
- $x_0$ = starting position (m from stern baseline)
- $v_e$ = erection velocity (m/day), typically **1.5 to 4.0 m/day** for this method
- $t$ = time in days

The **total erection time** for a ship of length $L_{bp}$ is:

$$T_{\text{erection}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{v_e}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a typical **Panamax bulker** ($L_{bp} \approx$ **229 m**) with $v_e =$ **2.5 m/day**, the erection time is **91.6 days** — clearly demonstrating why single-point is now obsolete for large vessels.

#### 2.3.4 Advantages & Disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------------|---------------|
| Simplest method, easiest to plan | Very slow — linear time growth |
| Low capital investment (no heavy cranes) | Longitudinal distortion accumulates |
| Suitable for small vessels, river boats, fishing boats | Inefficient use of berth space |
| Single crew can execute | No parallel work possible |

---

### 2.4 MULTI-POINT ERECTION METHOD (Modern Standard)

**Definition:** The hull is erected **simultaneously from multiple workstations** distributed along the length of the ship. Each workstation is called an **Erection Point** or **Erection Station**, typically spaced at **30 m to 60 m intervals** along the berth.

#### 2.4.1 Operational Philosophy

1. The berth is divided into **N erection zones** (typically **3 to 6**).
2. Each zone is staffed by an independent erection team with its own crane(s).
3. Blocks are erected concurrently in all zones.
4. Adjacent zones are joined (welded) once a sufficient work-front gap remains.

#### 2.4.2 Construction Sequence

- **Step 1 — Berth Preparation:** Keel blocks laid, ground ways prepared (often with **1/100 slope** for gravity-assisted launching).
- **Step 2 — Block Pre-fabrication:** Mega-blocks (often **400–800 T**) are pre-fabricated in workshops.
- **Step 3 — Synchronized Erection:** Multiple cranes lift blocks into position at each erection point **simultaneously**.
- **Step 4 — Zone Joining:** Adjacent zones are welded together, typically starting from the **center** and moving outward.
- **Step 5 — Bow & Stern Ring Closure:** Bow and stern blocks (often lifted by the **hammerhead crane**) are added last.
- **Step 6 — Pre-launch Outfitting:** Scaffolding, pipes, cables, and equipment are installed before launch (Pre-Outfitting / Pre-Equipment Installation).

#### 2.4.3 Mathematical Model — Multi-Point Progression

If there are **N erection points** with individual velocities $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_N$, the **effective total erection velocity** is:

$$V_{\text{eff}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i$$

The **total erection time** is:

$$T_{\text{multi}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{V_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Speed-up factor** compared to single-point: $\eta = N$ (theoretically). In practice, $\eta \approx 0.7N$ to $0.85N$ due to joining inefficiency.

#### 2.4.4 Number of Erection Points — Design Equation

The optimum number of erection points is given by the **economic balance**:

$$N_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{\text{fixed}} \cdot L_{bp}}{C_{\text{per-point}} \cdot v_e \cdot T_{\text{target}}}}$$

where:
- $C_{\text{fixed}}$ = fixed cost per day of berth occupancy (INR/day or USD/day)
- $C_{\text{per-point}}$ = cost of operating one erection point (cranes + crew per day)
- $T_{\text{target}}$ = desired total erection time (days)

#### 2.4.5 Advantages & Disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------------|---------------|
| **Fastest method** (parallelism) | High capital cost (multiple cranes) |
| Reduced distortion (symmetric build) | Complex logistics & scheduling |
| Pre-outfitting during erection | Skilled workforce management required |
| Industry standard for all modern yards | Risk of inter-zone misalignment |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | $N_{\text{rings}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{L_f} + 1$ | Number of transverse rings | dimensionless |
| 2 | $A_{\text{ring}} = \pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2)$ | Ring cross-section area | m$^2$ |
| 3 | $T_{\text{erection}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{v_e}$ | Single-point erection time | days |
| 4 | $T_{\text{multi}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{\sum v_i}$ | Multi-point erection time | days |
| 5 | $\eta = \frac{T_{\text{single}}}{T_{\text{multi}}}$ | Speed-up factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_{\text{steel}}(t) = L(t)\cdot B(t)\cdot H(t)$ | Pyramid growth volume | m$^3$ |
| 7 | $\Delta = \rho_{\text{steel}} \cdot V \cdot g$ | Block weight | N |
| 8 | $S_{\text{berth}} = L_{bp} + 2\cdot L_{\text{extension}}$ | Berth length required | m |
| 9 | $\theta_{\text{berth}} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{S}\right)$ | Berth slope (1/S notation) | degrees |
| 10 | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{fixed}}\cdot T + N\cdot C_{\text{point}}$ | Total erection cost | INR / USD |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard shipbuilding slope notations: **1/100, 1/80, 1/60** (i.e., 1 m rise per 100/80/60 m horizontal). Steeper slopes assist gravity launching but increase stability risk during construction.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Cochin Shipyard (India):** Uses **Multi-point erection on a flat berth** with **600 T hammerhead crane** for naval and commercial vessels.
- **Hyundai Heavy Industries (Korea):** Uses **Multi-point + inclined berth (1/100)** for VLCCs and container ships.
- **Mazan shipyards (Russia, older practice):** Historically used **Ring erection** for submarines and small combatants.
- **Inland shipyards (Europe, China):** Use **Pyramid erection** for medium-sized tankers, especially when block transport is constrained.
- **Small boat yards (India — Goa, Kerala backwaters):** Still use **Single-point erection** for fishing boats and houseboats.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Speed-up Factor for Multi-Point Erection

**Problem Statement:** A ship of length between perpendiculars $L_{bp} =$ **200 m** is to be erected. Single-point method takes **80 days**. If the yard switches to a 4-point multi-point erection with each point having $v_i =$ **2.5 m/day**, find the speed-up factor.

#### Step 1 — Identify the given parameters

- $L_{bp} = 200$ m
- $T_{\text{single}} = 80$ days (given)
- Number of points $N = 4$
- Velocity per point $v_i = 2.5$ m/day (assumed equal for all points)

#### Step 2 — Compute single-point erection velocity

From $T_{\text{erection}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{v_e}$, rearrange:

$$v_e = \frac{L_{bp}}{T_{\text{single}}} = \frac{200}{80} = 2.5 \text{ m/day}$$

> This matches the multi-point per-station velocity, confirming consistency.

#### Step 3 — Compute effective total velocity for multi-point

Since all 4 points have the same velocity:

$$V_{\text{eff}} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} v_i = 4 \times 2.5 = 10.0 \text{ m/day}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute theoretical multi-point erection time

$$T_{\text{multi, theory}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{V_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{200}{10.0} = 20 \text{ days}$$

#### Step 5 — Apply practical efficiency factor $\eta = 0.80$

$$T_{\text{multi, actual}} = \frac{T_{\text{multi, theory}}}{\eta} = \frac{20}{0.80} = 25 \text{ days}$$

#### Step 6 — Compute speed-up factor

$$\eta_{\text{speed-up}} = \frac{T_{\text{single}}}{T_{\text{multi, actual}}} = \frac{80}{25} = 3.2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** A 4-point method gives only a **3.2× speed-up** (not the theoretical 4×) due to the 20% efficiency loss from zone joining, alignment, and shared resource contention.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimum Number of Erection Points

**Problem Statement:** A yard has fixed berth cost $C_{\text{fixed}} =$ **INR 5,00,000/day**, length $L_{bp} =$ **250 m**, per-point cost $C_{\text{point}} =$ **INR 1,50,000/day**, target time $T_{\text{target}} =$ **50 days**, and per-point velocity $v_e =$ **1.5 m/day**. Find the optimum number of erection points.

#### Step 1 — Apply the design equation

$$N_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{\text{fixed}} \cdot L_{bp}}{C_{\text{per-point}} \cdot v_e \cdot T_{\text{target}}}}$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$N_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 250}{1{,}50{,}000 \times 1.5 \times 50}}$$

#### Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately

$$\text{Numerator} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 250 = 1.25 \times 10^8$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 1.5 \times 50 = 1.125 \times 10^7$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the ratio

$$\frac{\text{Numerator}}{\text{Denominator}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^8}{1.125 \times 10^7} = 11.111$$

#### Step 5 — Take square root

$$N_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{11.111} \approx 3.33$$

#### Step 6 — Round to nearest integer

$$N_{\text{opt}} = 3 \text{ erection points}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision Rule:** Always round $N_{\text{opt}}$ to the **nearest practical integer** that satisfies berth length constraints. Here, **3 points** at intervals of approximately **83 m** each is feasible for a 250 m ship.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Weight of a Single Ring Block (Pyramid Method)

**Problem Statement:** A pyramidal central block for a tanker has dimensions: $L_0 =$ **40 m**, $B_0 =$ **30 m**, $H_0 =$ **18 m**, and average plating thickness $t =$ **20 mm**. Compute the steel weight of the block.

#### Step 1 — Approximate the block as a rectangular box

The block is treated as a **hollow box** (since the interior is open for outfitting access). Approximate the surface area of all six faces:

$$A_{\text{surface}} = 2(L_0 \cdot B_0) + 2(L_0 \cdot H_0) + 2(B_0 \cdot H_0)$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$A_{\text{surface}} = 2(40 \times 30) + 2(40 \times 18) + 2(30 \times 18)$$

$$A_{\text{surface}} = 2400 + 1440 + 1080 = 4920 \text{ m}^2$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the steel volume

$$V_{\text{steel}} = A_{\text{surface}} \times t = 4920 \times 0.020 = 98.4 \text{ m}^3$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the steel mass

Using $\rho_{\text{steel}} =$ **7850 kg/m$^3$**:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 7850 \times 98.4 = 7.724 \times 10^5 \text{ kg} \approx 772.4 \text{ T}$$

#### Step 5 — Compute the weight

$$W = m \cdot g = 7.724 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 = 7.577 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 7.58 \text{ MN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **772 T** block exceeds the capacity of standard gantry cranes. Such mega-blocks are typically lifted by **hammerhead / bridge-type cranes** rated **800 T to 1200 T** (e.g., Hyundai's 1200 T Goliath).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Four Methods

| Parameter | Ring Erection | Pyramid Erection | Single-Point | Multi-Point |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|--------------|-------------|
| Build axis | Transverse | Mixed | Longitudinal | Longitudinal |
| Direction | From midship outward | Central island outward | Stern to bow | Multiple stations |
| Typical ship size | Small–medium | Medium–large | Small | Any (esp. large) |
| Erection time (relative) | **Medium** | **Medium-Fast** | **Slow** | **Fastest** |
| Accuracy | **Highest** | **High** | **Low** | **Medium-High** |
| Crane requirement | Heavy (full rings) | Heavy (mega-blocks) | Light | Heavy (multiple) |
| Workforce | Concentrated | Distributed | Single team | Multi-team |
| Berth space use | Poor | Good | Poor | Excellent |
| Modern relevance | Specialized (subs) | Legacy | Obsolete | **Universal** |
| Distortion risk | Low (symmetric) | Medium | High (longitudinal) | Low (symmetric) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Mnemonic — "RPSM"**: **R**ing → **P**yramid → **S**ingle-point → **M**ulti-point. Modern yards use them in the **reverse** priority: M is most preferred, S is least preferred.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Overview of Erection Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Erection Methods] --> B[Ring Erection]
    A --> C[Pyramid Erection]
    A --> D[Single-Point Erection]
    A --> E[Multi-Point Erection]

    B --> B1[Step 1: Midship frame skeleton]
    B --> B2[Step 2: Shell plating of ring]
    B --> B3[Step 3: Internal structure]
    B --> B4[Step 4: Adjacent rings welded]
    B --> B5[Step 5: Bow and stern closure]

    C --> C1[Step 1: Foundation double bottom]
    C --> C2[Step 2: Side shells added]
    C --> C3[Step 3: Deck panels island complete]
    C --> C4[Step 4: Pyramid growth outward]
    C --> C5[Step 5: Bow and stern closure]

    D --> D1[Step 1: Keel laid on slip]
    D --> D2[Step 2: Stern frame erected]
    D --> D3[Step 3: Sequential progression]
    D --> D4[Step 4: Bow closure]

    E --> E1[Step 1: Berth with N stations]
    E --> E2[Step 2: Mega-blocks fabricated]
    E --> E3[Step 3: Synchronized erection]
    E --> E4[Step 4: Zone joining]
    E --> E5[Step 5: Bow and stern rings]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparative Flow of the Four Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RingMethod
        R1[Midship Ring] --> R2[Ring 2 Aft]
        R1 --> R3[Ring 2 Forward]
        R2 --> R4[Ring 3 Aft]
        R3 --> R5[Ring 3 Forward]
    end

    subgraph PyramidMethod
        P1[Central Island Block] --> P2[Aft Block]
        P1 --> P3[Forward Block]
        P1 --> P4[Side Block]
        P1 --> P5[Deck Block]
    end

    subgraph SinglePointMethod
        S1[Stern] --> S2[Midship] --> S3[Bow]
    end

    subgraph MultiPointMethod
        M1[Station 1] --> M4[Joint 1-2]
        M2[Station 2] --> M4
        M2 --> M5[Joint 2-3]
        M3[Station 3] --> M5
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Modern Multi-Point Erection Yard

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hull Design Office] --> B[Block Fabrication Shop]
    A --> C[Outfitting Shop]
    B --> D[Pre-Erection Storage]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Building Berth N Stations]
    E --> F[Zone Joining Welding Bay]
    F --> G[Pre-Launch Outfitting]
    G --> H[Launching Ways]
    H --> I[Fit-Out Berth]
    I --> J[Sea Trials]
    J --> K[Delivery]

    L[Goliath Crane] --> E
    M[Hammerhead Crane] --> E
    N[Transporters] --> D
```

```mermaid

    dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD
    section Station 1
    Block 1 Lift        :a1, 2025-01-01, 5d
```
