<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1a]**
>
> **Q1a. Define Ring Erection Method. List any two advantages and two disadvantages.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Ring erection is a ship construction method in which the hull is built by assembling complete transverse sections (rings) one after another on the building berth, with each ring containing shell plating, frames, decks, and bulkheads at one station.
>
> **Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
> - High dimensional accuracy due to self-aligned ring geometry.
> - Reduced welding distortion owing to symmetric heat input.
>
> **Disadvantages (1 mark — any two):**
> - Limited to small/medium vessels because ring weights become prohibitively high.
> - Slow longitudinal strength build-up between adjacent rings.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1b]**
>
> **Q1b. Distinguish between Single-Point and Multi-Point Erection Methods.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Single-Point | Multi-Point |
> |---------|--------------|-------------|
> | Number of work fronts | 1 | N (typically 3–6) |
> | Build direction | Stern to bow along centerline | Multiple stations simultaneously |
> | Erection time | $T = L_{bp}/v_e$ | $T = L_{bp}/\sum v_i$ |
> | Modern use | Obsolete (small boats) | Universal (modern yards) |
>
> **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table/contrast, 1 mark for example)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4, Q7]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this option:**

> **Q7a. Explain the Ring Erection Method of ship construction in detail, with a neat sketch of the construction sequence. List its advantages and disadvantages.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** The Ring Erection Method is a transverse-section-based construction philosophy where the hull is built ring-by-ring, starting from the midship section and expanding outward to the bow and stern. Each ring is a complete transverse section including shell plating, frames, decks, and bulkheads.

**2. Construction Sequence (3 marks):**

- **Step 1:** Ground frames and keel blocks are laid for the midship ring.
- **Step 2:** Bottom, side, and deck shell plates are positioned and tack-welded.
- **Step 3:** Internal frames, longitudinals, stiffeners, and bulkheads are welded inside the ring.
- **Step 4:** The ring is subjected to NDT inspection (UT/RT).
- **Step 5:** Adjacent rings (one forward, one aft) are erected and welded to the completed midship ring.
- **Step 6:** The process continues until the entire hull length is covered.
- **Step 7:** Bow (stem) and stern (stern frame) castings are added as the final rings.

**3. Sketch Description (2 marks):**

*(Refer to the Mermaid Ring Method diagram in Section 4.2.)* The diagram shows the midship ring at the center, with rings 2A (aft) and 2F (forward) attached on either side, followed by rings 3A, 3F, and so on, terminating with the bow and stern blocks.

**4. Advantages (0.5 marks):** High accuracy, reduced distortion, suitable for submarines, parallel work possible.

**5. Disadvantages (0.5 marks):** Limited to small ships, heavy lift cranes required, slow build.

---

> **Q7b. Explain the Pyramid Erection Method in detail. Compare it with the Ring Erection Method.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):** The Pyramid Erection Method is a hybrid construction philosophy in which a central "island block" is erected first at midship, and subsequent sections are added around it in a pyramidal progression — both transversely and longitudinally — until the complete hull is formed.

**2. Construction Sequence (2.5 marks):**

- **Step 1:** Foundation block (double bottom + centerline girder) is erected on the berth.
- **Step 2:** Side shell panels (port and starboard) are added to form the central "island."
- **Step 3:** Deck panels (main, second, platform) are welded on top, completing the island.
- **Step 4:** Pyramid growth begins — adjacent ring sections are added forward and aft, and tier sections are added above and below the island.
- **Step 5:** The pyramid front reaches the bow and stern; stem and stern frame are welded as closure pieces.

**3. Pyramid Geometry Formula (1.5 marks):**

The construction front grows according to:

$$L(t) = L_0 + 2 v_t t, \quad B(t) = B_0 + 2 v_b t, \quad H(t) = H_0 + v_h t$$

The total steel volume at time $t$ is $V(t) = L(t) \cdot B(t) \cdot H(t)$.

**4. Comparison with Ring Erection (2 marks):**

| Feature | Ring Erection | Pyramid Erection |
|---------|---------------|------------------|
| Axis | Pure transverse | Mixed (trans + long) |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Accuracy | Very high | High |
| Vessel size | Small/medium | Medium/large |
| Workforce | Concentrated | Distributed |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative option:**

> **Q7a. Describe the Single-Point and Multi-Point Erection Methods. Derive the relationship between the speed-up factor and the number of erection points.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Single-Point Erection (1.5 marks):**
The hull is erected along a single longitudinal line, starting from the stern. A single crew progresses forward at velocity $v_e$ (m/day). The erection time is:

$$T_{\text{single}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{v_e}$$

**2. Multi-Point Erection (1.5 marks):**
The berth is divided into $N$ zones, each with its own erection team operating at velocity $v_i$. The total effective velocity is $V_{\text{eff}} = \sum v_i$, and:

$$T_{\text{multi}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{V_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i}$$

**3. Derivation of Speed-up Factor (3 marks):**

The theoretical speed-up factor (no losses) is:

$$\eta_{\text{theory}} = \frac{T_{\text{single}}}{T_{\text{multi, theory}}} = \frac{L_{bp}/v_e}{L_{bp}/\sum v_i} = \frac{\sum v_i}{v_e} = N$$

(assuming all $v_i = v_e$). With practical efficiency $\alpha$ (typically 0.70–0.85):

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = \alpha \cdot N$$

**4. Numerical Example (1 mark):** For $N = 4$ and $\alpha = 0.80$:

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 0.80 \times 4 = 3.2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply the practical efficiency factor $\alpha$ and incorrectly report $\eta = N$ exactly. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this. Always state that the speed-up is $\alpha N$, not $N$.

---

> **Q7b. A ship of $L_{bp} = 200$ m is to be erected using a 5-point multi-point method, each point having $v_i = 2.0$ m/day. The single-point method would have taken 100 days. Find (i) the actual multi-point erection time, (ii) the actual speed-up factor (assume $\alpha = 0.85$).** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L_{bp} = 200$ m, $N = 5$, $v_i = 2.0$ m/day, $T_{\text{single}} = 100$ days, $\alpha = 0.85$.

**Part (i) — Actual multi-point erection time (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute effective velocity:

$$V_{\text{eff}} = N \cdot v_i = 5 \times 2.0 = 10.0 \text{ m/day}$$

**Step 2:** Compute theoretical erection time:

$$T_{\text{multi, theory}} = \frac{L_{bp}}{V_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{200}{10.0} = 20 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3:** Apply efficiency factor $\alpha = 0.85$:

$$T_{\text{multi, actual}} = \frac{T_{\text{multi, theory}}}{\alpha} = \frac{20}{0.85} = 23.53 \text{ days}$$

**Part (ii) — Actual speed-up factor (3 marks):**

**Step 4:** Compute speed-up factor:

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = \frac{T_{\text{single}}}{T_{\text{multi, actual}}} = \frac{100}{23.53} = 4.25$$

**Step 5:** Compare with theoretical ($N = 5$):

$$\eta_{\text{theory}} = N = 5, \quad \eta_{\text{actual}} = 4.25$$

**Conclusion:** A 5-point method yields a **4.25× speed-up** (not 5×) due to the 15% efficiency loss. The actual time saved is $100 - 23.53 = 76.47$ days.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B part (b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to apply $\alpha$ and writing $T_{\text{multi}} = 20$ days directly. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Computing $\eta$ as $N = 5$ instead of 4.25. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not stating units (m/day, days). **Penalty: −0.5 marks.**
> - **Tip:** Always write the formula first, then substitute, then compute — in **three separate steps** to earn full method marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Frequently Asked Concepts (Quick-Reference List)

> [!TIP]
> High-frequency exam topics students MUST prepare:
> 1. **Definition** of each of the 4 methods (1–2 marks each)
> 2. **Sketch** of construction sequence (3–4 marks)
> 3. **Comparison table** between any two methods (3–4 marks)
> 4. **Numerical** on $T_{\text{erection}}$ and speed-up factor (4–6 marks)
> 5. **Advantages / Disadvantages** of each method (2–3 marks)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Ring Erection** = Transverse ring-by-ring from midship outward. Best for **submarines and small warships**. High accuracy, slow build.
- ⭐ **Pyramid Erection** = Central island + radial growth. Hybrid of ring and longitudinal. Best for **tankers and bulkers**.
- ⭐ **Single-Point Erection** = Stern to bow along one line. **Obsolete** for large vessels. $T = L_{bp}/v_e$.
- ⭐ **Multi-Point Erection** = N stations simultaneously. **Modern universal standard**. $T = L_{bp}/\sum v_i$. Effective speed-up $= \alpha N$ (with $\alpha \approx 0.7$–$0.85$).
- ⭐ **Number of erection points** in modern yards = **3 to 6**, spaced **30–60 m apart**.
- ⭐ **Block weight range** in modern yards = **20 T to 1200 T** (mega-blocks).
- ⭐ **Berth slope** notation: **1/100, 1/80, 1/60** (1 m rise per horizontal distance).
- ⭐ **Erection tolerance** (IACS): $\pm$ **3 mm per block, $\pm$ 6 mm cumulative per 100 m**.
- ⭐ **Modern shipyards** (Hyundai, Daewoo, Cochin) almost universally use **multi-point + inclined berth + Goliath/Hammerhead cranes**.
- ⭐ **Sequence mnemonic for KTU:** "**RPSM**" — Ring, Pyramid, Single-point, Multi-point.
- ⭐ **Numerical formula sheet** to memorize: $N_{\text{rings}} = L_{bp}/L_f + 1$; $T_{\text{erection}} = L_{bp}/v_e$; $\eta = \alpha N$.
- ⭐ **Pyramid geometry**: $L(t) = L_0 + 2 v_t t$, $B(t) = B_0 + 2 v_b t$, $H(t) = H_0 + v_h t$.
- ⭐ **Steel density** to remember: $\rho_{\text{steel}} =$ **7850 kg/m$^3$**.
- ⭐ **Steel volume of a block**: $V = A_{\text{surface}} \times t$ where $A_{\text{surface}}$ is the total plate surface area of the block.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always **draw a labelled sketch** (even a simple block diagram) for full-marks questions on erection methods. Examiners allocate **1.5 to 2 marks** specifically for the sketch in a 7-mark sub-question. A clear sketch with arrows showing the build direction is sufficient — you do NOT need artistic 3D drawings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technological Processes in the Installation of Main Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Technological Processes in the Installation of Main Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Technological Processes in the Installation of Main Machinery** refers to the systematic sequence of engineering operations, rigging procedures, alignment methodologies, fastening techniques, and commissioning protocols employed to position, secure, align, and functionally integrate the prime movers (main propulsion engines, main generators, boilers/turbines, and reduction gears) and their auxiliary support systems within a vessel's machinery space, ensuring compliance with classification society rules (e.g., **IRS**, **DNV**, **Lloyd's Register**, **ABS**), statutory regulations (**SOLAS**, **MARPOL**), and the shipbuilder's outfitting schedule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** This topic sits at the intersection of **Naval Architecture** and **Marine Engineering**. It emphasizes the *practical shipyard workflows* rather than the thermodynamic design of engines themselves. Students must master both the *sequence of operations* and the *tolerance engineering* principles involved.

## 1.2 Constituents of "Main Machinery" in a Vessel

The term *main machinery* encompasses the following critical equipment groups:

1. **Main Propulsion Engine** – Slow-speed two-stroke diesel (direct drive), medium-speed four-stroke diesel (with reduction gearbox), or marine steam turbine (with reduction gearing).
2. **Main Reduction Gearbox** – Couples the engine output to the propeller shaft at the required RPM ratio.
3. **Main Generators (Auxiliary Diesel Generators / Shaft Generators)** – Provide electrical power to the ship's network.
4. **Main Boilers / Steam Generators** – In steam-powered vessels.
5. **Shaft Line Components** – Thrust bearing, intermediate shaft, tail shaft, and propeller.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Heart Transplant Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine performing open-heart surgery on a giant. Installing main machinery into a ship is *almost exactly* this concept.
>
> - The **ship's hull** = the chest cavity (must be opened up and prepared).
> - The **double bottom and platform decks** = the operating table and tissue beds (foundation).
> - The **main engine** = the new heart (must be lowered in, sewn to the vessels, and connected to arteries).
> - The **propeller shaft** = the aorta (must be precisely aligned with the heart's outflow).
> - The **propeller** = the cells receiving oxygenated blood.
> - The **alignment process** = the surgeon's stitching (any misalignment = failure of the entire organism).
>
> Just as a heart cannot be "shoved" into a chest, a 200-tonne marine engine cannot be placed arbitrarily — it requires carefully orchestrated **rigging, lifting, positioning, grouting, aligning, and securing** — each step is a *technology*.

## 1.4 Sequential Process Overview

The technological processes can be grouped into **six logical stages**:

| Stage | Stage Name | Key Output |
|:-----:|:-----------|:-----------|
| 1 | Pre-Installation Survey | Verified foundation and clearances |
| 2 | Foundation Preparation | Machined base, grout pockets, chock locations |
| 3 | Lifting & Lowering | Engine placed on supports within tolerance |
| 4 | Alignment & Leveling | Shaft, engine, gearbox coaxial within **0.05 mm/m** |
| 5 | Securing & Grouting | Permanent fastening, chock tightness, epoxy grout |
| 6 | Hook-Up & Commissioning | Piping, cabling, trials, and class endorsement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each stage is governed by a **Method Statement** and **Risk Assessment (MSRA)** approved by the shipyard's quality assurance (QA/QC) department, classification surveyor, and the engine manufacturer's technical representative.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Pre-Installation Survey

The *engineer's first visit* to the machinery space after hull erection. Critical activities:

- **Dimensional verification** of foundation plate flatness using a precision level and straightedge. Permissible deviation: **≤ 0.05 mm per 1000 mm**.
- **Survey of grout pockets** — the recesses in the foundation designed to receive epoxy or cementitious non-shrink grout.
- **Inspection of bolt holes** for alignment with engine bedplate dowel pins.
- **Verification of clearances** for overhead crane lifts, especially in twin-engine configurations.
- **Checklist of pre-delivery items** from the engine maker (main engine, gear box, turning gear, tools, spares, manuals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual "Why":** The pre-survey catches errors *before* the expensive lift. A 500-tonne engine hoisted into a misaligned foundation wastes riggers' time, damages equipment, and may require costly rework.

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Foundation Preparation and Bedplate Mounting

The foundation of a main engine is typically a **fabricated steel structure** built into the double bottom and tied into transverse webs and longitudinal girders. The *top mounting surface* is machine-finished to **± 0.1 mm flatness across the full bedplate footprint**.

Key sub-processes:
- **Chock placement** — Mild steel or epoxy chocks (typically **0.4 mm to 1.0 mm** thinner than measured gap) are positioned beside holding-down bolts.
- **Grout pouring** — Non-shrink epoxy grout (e.g., **Chockfast**, **Sikafloor**) fills the gap between bedplate and foundation.
- **Curing** — Grout cures for **24 to 72 hours** under controlled temperature (typically **> 15 °C**) before bolt tightening.

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Lifting and Lowering

Main engines are lifted using:
- **Goliath / portal cranes** for new construction (lifting capacity up to **1500 tonnes**).
- **Tandem lift using two shipyard cranes** when the center of gravity is offset.
- **Lifting beams / spreader bars** to distribute load and prevent engine distortion.
- **Hydraulic jacks and skidding systems** for very large slow-speed engines (e.g., MAN B&W 14K98ME-C, weight ≈ **2300 tonnes**).

The **lifting eyes** are pre-engineered points on the engine casing. Lifting is done at a *controlled rate* (typically **≤ 0.5 m/min near final position**) to avoid impact loading.

## 2.4 Stage 4 — Alignment and Leveling

This is the **most critical** technological process in installation. Three alignment types are practiced:

1. **Cold Alignment (Static)** — Performed at ambient temperature with no thermal expansion effects. Tolerances are read from **dial gauges** or **laser alignment systems**.
2. **Hot Alignment (Dynamic)** — Performed after the engine has been warmed up to operating temperature, accounting for thermal growth.
3. **Final Operating Alignment (Sea Trial Alignment)** — Adjusted under actual load conditions during sea trials.

The classic alignment parameters for a main propulsion line are:

- **Parallel misalignment (offset)**: $\Delta r$
- **Angular misalignment (slope)**: $\Delta \theta$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **directly testable** in KTU examinations. Memorize the forms and units.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|:-:|:--|:--|:-:|:--|
| 1 | Lifting Safety Factor | $SF = \dfrac{W_{\text{crane}}}{W_{\text{engine}}}$ | dimensionless | Crane selection; $SF \geq 1.25$ for single lift, $\geq 1.5$ for tandem |
| 2 | Foundation Flatness Tolerance | $T_f = \dfrac{\delta h}{L}$ | mm/m | Engine bed acceptance; $T_f \leq 0.05$ mm/m for main engines |
| 3 | Bedplate Grout Gap | $g = h_f - h_b$ | mm | Selecting chock + grout thickness; typically **25–75 mm** |
| 4 | Parallel Offset Tolerance | $\Delta r_{\max} \leq \dfrac{D_{\text{shaft}}}{1000}$ | mm | Shaft coupling alignment |
| 5 | Angular Tolerance | $\Delta \theta_{\max} \leq \dfrac{0.1}{L_{\text{coup}}}$ | mm/m | Across coupling face |
| 6 | Shaft Slope (Hogging Allowance) | $\theta_s = \dfrac{W_s}{E I}$ | rad/m | Propeller shaft deflection check |
| 7 | Grout Compressive Strength (typical epoxy) | $\sigma_g \geq 80$ | N/mm² (MPa) | Foundation integrity |
| 8 | Holding-Down Bolt Pre-load (Tension) | $T_b = \sigma_{\text{bolt}} \cdot A_{\text{core}}$ | N | Bolt torque verification: $T = k \cdot F_b \cdot d$ |
| 9 | Torque-to-Preload Relationship | $T_q = k \cdot F_i \cdot d$ | N·m | Bolt tightening; $k \approx 0.20$ (lubricated) |
| 10 | Shaft Center Deflection (Bending) | $\delta_{\max} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | m | Mid-bearing alignment |
| 11 | Thermal Expansion of Shaft Line | $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$ | m | Hot alignment correction; $\alpha_{\text{steel}} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \;\vert K^{-1}$ |
| 12 | Specific Foundation Pressure | $p = \dfrac{W_{\text{eng}}}{A_{\text{foot}}}$ | N/mm² | Bearing capacity check; $p \leq 0.5$ MPa typical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation:** Absolute values (e.g., $\vert K^{-1} \vert$) are written with `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax — a critical formatting safeguard for KTU submissions.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas and processes underpin:

- **Shipyard efficiency KPIs** — Hours-to-engine-start vs. planned schedule.
- **Class survey approval** — Failure at the alignment stage can delay vessel delivery by **weeks**, costing **$50,000–$200,000/day** in charter-equivalent losses.
- **Vibration engineering** — Misalignment is the **#1 cause** of bearing failures, propeller-induced vibrations, and stern tube seal damage.
- **Lifecycle performance** — A properly aligned engine delivers **30+ years** of service; a misaligned unit may fail catastrophically within **5 years**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Alignment Tolerance for a Marine Shaft Line

We derive the permissible parallel offset between two coupled shafts as a function of shaft diameter and bearing span.

**Given:**
- Shaft diameter: $D_s$
- Distance between coupling faces: $L_c$
- Permissible misalignment: $\Delta r$

**Step 1 — Empirical Shipyard Rule (Lloyd's Register Recommendation):**

The maximum permissible parallel offset at the coupling face is empirically defined as:

$$
\Delta r_{\max} = \frac{D_s}{1000}
$$

**Step 2 — Conversion to mm/m for angular check:**

The angular misalignment per meter of shaft length must not exceed:

$$
\Delta \theta_{\max} = \frac{0.1}{L_c} \quad \text{[mm per m]}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example:**

Consider a tail shaft of diameter $D_s = 600$ mm coupled to the intermediate shaft at a flange $L_c = 0.8$ m from the engine thrust bearing.

$$
\Delta r_{\max} = \frac{D_s}{1000} = \frac{600}{1000} = 0.6 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

The maximum angular misalignment across the coupling is therefore:

$$
\Delta \theta_{\max} = \frac{0.1}{L_c} = \frac{0.1}{0.8} = 0.125 \;\vert mm/m \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- The dial gauge reading at the coupling rim (radius $r_g$) will be:
  - $r_g = D_c / 2$ where $D_c$ is the coupling diameter.
- If $D_c = 1200$ mm, then $r_g = 600$ mm.
- The maximum *gauge reading* at the rim is $\delta_{gauge} = \Delta r \cdot 2 = 1.2$ mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** We multiplied by 2 because the dial gauge reads the *diameter difference* when rotated 180°, which is twice the *radius offset*. This is the classic "face-and-rim" dial gauge method.

## 3.2 Derivation of Bedplate Grout Gap and Bolt Pre-Load

**Given:**
- Foundation surface height (measured from reference datum): $h_f = 250.0$ mm
- Bedplate underside thickness (at chock location): $h_b = 195.0$ mm
- Engine mass (full assembly with oil): $W_e = 2{,}100{,}000$ N (≈ 214 tonnes)
- Number of holding-down bolts: $n_b = 36$
- Bolt nominal diameter: $d_b = 48$ mm (M48)
- Bolt core (tensile) area: $A_{\text{core}} = 1473 \;\vert mm^2 \vert$

**Step 1 — Compute the grout gap:**

$$
g = h_f - h_b = 250.0 - 195.0 = 55.0 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the chock thickness:**

The chock must be approximately **0.4 mm thinner** than the gap to allow grout penetration:

$$
h_{\text{chock}} = g - 0.4 = 55.0 - 0.4 = 54.6 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the per-bolt preload:**

Assuming each bolt carries an equal share and a preload ratio of 60% of the engine weight:

$$
F_{\text{preload}} = 0.6 \cdot \frac{W_e}{n_b} = 0.6 \cdot \frac{2{,}100{,}000}{36} = 35{,}000 \;\vert N \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required tightening torque:**

Using $T_q = k \cdot F_i \cdot d$ with $k = 0.20$ (typical for lubricated steel-on-steel):

$$
T_q = 0.20 \cdot 35{,}000 \cdot 0.048 = 336 \;\vert N \cdot m \vert
$$

**Step 5 — Bolt stress check:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{bolt}} = \frac{F_{\text{preload}}}{A_{\text{core}}} = \frac{35{,}000}{1473} = 23.76 \;\vert MPa \vert
$$

This is well below the yield strength of grade 8.8 bolts (**640 MPa**), confirming the design is safe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This derivation blends *mechanics of materials* (bolt stress), *machine design* (torque-preload), and *shipyard practice* (chock + grout) — a classic inter-disciplinary question worth **7–14 marks**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Thermal Expansion Correction (Hot Alignment)

**Given:**
- Shaft line total length: $L = 50$ m
- Cold temperature: $T_c = 20 \;°C$
- Operating temperature: $T_o = 60 \;°C$
- Coefficient of linear expansion: $\alpha_{\text{steel}} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \;\vert K^{-1} \vert$

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature rise:**

$$
\Delta T = T_o - T_c = 60 - 20 = 40 \;\vert K \vert
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear expansion:**

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 50 \cdot 40 = 0.024 \;\vert m \vert = 24 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Hot Alignment Offset:**

If the engine is rigidly fixed and the shaft expands by 24 mm, the **tail shaft bearing centerline shifts by 24 mm forward (aft)** depending on engine mount rigidity. Therefore, during cold alignment the bearing heights must be *pre-set* by a calculated amount to ensure hot alignment is correct.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often compute $\Delta L$ but forget that the engine bedplate *also* expands thermally. The actual relative displacement between engine and tail shaft is the **differential expansion**:

$$
\Delta L_{\text{rel}} = \Delta L_{\text{shaft}} - \Delta L_{\text{engine}}
$$

For a typical installation:

$$
\Delta L_{\text{engine}} \approx (12 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (8) \cdot 40 = 0.00384 \;\vert m \vert = 3.84 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

So:

$$
\Delta L_{\text{rel}} = 24 - 3.84 = 20.16 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

This corrected value must be used in the alignment procedure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Main Machinery Installation Sequence

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **complete technological workflow** for installing main machinery in a vessel. Each node represents a shipyard activity with its deliverable.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Erection Complete] --> B[Pre-Installation Survey]
    B --> C[Foundation Flatness Check]
    C --> D{Quality OK?}
    D -- No --> E[Rework Foundation]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Position Chocks and Bolts]
    F --> G[Lift Main Engine via Crane]
    G --> H[Lower onto Chocks]
    H --> I[Rough Alignment using Dial Gauges]
    I --> J[Fine Alignment using Laser System]
    J --> K{Within Tolerance?}
    K -- No --> L[Adjust Bolts and Shim]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[Pour Epoxy Grout]
    M --> N[Curing Period 48-72 Hours]
    N --> O[Tighten Holding-Down Bolts]
    O --> P[Connect Piping and Cables]
    P --> Q[Hook-Up Tests on Berth]
    Q --> R[Dock Trial]
    R --> S[Sea Trial]
    S --> T{Class Survey Pass?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Final Acceptance and Delivery]
    T -- No --> V[Defect Rectification]
    V --> S
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Alignment System

This diagram explains the **functional subsystems** of a typical alignment measurement system used during main machinery installation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MEAS[Measurement Subsystem]
        A1[Laser Emitter] --> A2[Beam Splitter]
        A2 --> A3[Position Sensitive Detector PSD]
        A3 --> A4[Signal Conditioner]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Processing Subsystem]
        A4 --> B1[Microcontroller]
        B1 --> B2[Display Unit]
        B1 --> B3[Data Logger]
    end
    subgraph ACT[Actuation Subsystem]
        B2 --> C1[Hydraulic Jack Controller]
        C1 --> C2[Hydraulic Jacks under Engine Feet]
    end
    subgraph VAL[Validation Subsystem]
        B2 --> D1[Tolerance Comparator]
        D1 --> D2[Pass or Fail Indicator]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Main Engine and Shaft Line Architecture

The architecture below shows how the **main engine**, **gearbox**, **shaft line**, and **propeller** are physically and functionally arranged in a typical merchant vessel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENG[Engine Room]
        ME[Main Engine] --> RG[Reduction Gearbox]
        RG --> TB[Thrust Bearing]
        RG --> MG1[Main Generator 1]
        RG --> MG2[Main Generator 2]
    end
    subgraph SHAFT[Shaft Tunnel]
        TB --> IS[Intermediate Shaft]
        IS --> TS[Tail Shaft]
    end
    subgraph STERN[Stern Region]
        TS --> ST[Stern Tube Bearing]
        ST --> PR[Propeller]
        ST --> RU[Rudder]
    end
```

## 4.4 Installation Tolerance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Engine Placed] --> B{Cold Alignment within 0.05 mm/m?}
    B -- No --> C[Re-shim and Re-set]
    C --> B
    B -- Yes --> D[Run Engine Idle for 2 Hours]
    D --> E{Hot Alignment within 0.10 mm/m?}
    E -- No --> F[Adjust Thrust Bearing Position]
    F --> E
    E -- Yes --> G[Lock Bolts and Final Grouting]
    G --> H[Dock and Sea Trials]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Notes (KTU-Compliant):**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `ME`, `RG`) — no reserved keywords used.
> - Labels contain only plain uppercase text; no markdown bold/italic markers inside node labels to ensure clean rendering in mermaid-cli.
> - Subgraph containers (e.g., `ENG`, `SHAFT`, `STERN`) are used to logically group engine-room, shaft-tunnel, and stern-region components — analogous to a real ship GA drawing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the importance of foundation flatness in main machinery installation. What tolerance is generally specified?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Foundation flatness ensures uniform load distribution from the engine bedplate to the ship's structure. Any deviation creates **localized stress concentrations**, leading to:
1. Bedplate distortion under operating loads.
2. Misalignment of the crankshaft and shaft line.
3. Premature bearing wear and crankcase vibration.

The **generally accepted tolerance** for main engine foundations in merchant vessels is **≤ 0.05 mm per meter** of length, with overall flatness within **0.10 mm** across the entire bedplate footprint. This is verified using a precision straightedge and feeler gauge, or a laser plane system. Classification societies such as **IRS** and **DNV** require this tolerance to be recorded in the ship's construction file. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a "chock" in main machinery installation? Why is the chock thickness kept slightly less than the measured gap?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **chock** is a precisely machined mild-steel or epoxy shim placed between the engine bedplate and the foundation to provide initial support and to maintain the correct alignment before grouting. There are two types: **side chocks** (resist lateral and vertical forces) and **top chocks** (resist vertical loads directly).

The chock thickness is kept approximately **0.4 mm less** than the measured gap to:
1. **Allow grout penetration** into the space beneath the chock.
2. **Avoid load transfer through the chock alone** — the grout must share the load for composite action.
3. **Permit a small compression set** during bolt tightening without spalling the chock edges.

After chock placement, **non-shrink epoxy grout** is poured to fill the residual gap, ensuring a monolithic bond. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> 1. Students often confuse **parallel offset** with **angular misalignment** in alignment problems — they are two distinct quantities measured with different dial gauge configurations (face vs. rim method).
> 2. In thermal expansion problems, failing to use the **differential expansion** rather than absolute expansion costs 3 marks.
> 3. Students forget that **lifting safety factor** for marine engine lifts must be **≥ 1.5 for tandem lifts** — quoting only 1.0 (static) loses credit.
> 4. Drawing the alignment diagram correctly (with **dial gauge positions** marked) is worth **2 marks** by itself — skipping it costs marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe with the help of a flow diagram, the technological processes involved in the installation of main propulsion machinery in a ship. Mention the tolerances at each critical stage. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The technological processes are arranged in **six sequential stages**:

1. **Pre-Installation Survey:** Dimensional verification, foundation inspection, bolt hole check, lifting path survey. Tolerance: foundation flatness **≤ 0.05 mm/m**.

2. **Foundation Preparation:** Machining of the foundation top, cleaning of grout pockets, drilling bolt holes. Tolerance: bolt hole centerline deviation **≤ 2 mm**.

3. **Lifting and Lowering:** Using Goliath cranes (single lift) or tandem lift. Safety factor **≥ 1.25 (single) / 1.5 (tandum)**. Maximum approach velocity **≤ 0.5 m/min** at final position.

4. **Alignment and Leveling:** Using dial gauges (face-and-rim) or laser system.
   - Parallel offset: **$\Delta r \leq D_s/1000$**
   - Angular misalignment: **$\Delta \theta \leq 0.10 \;\vert mm/m \vert$**

5. **Securing and Grouting:** Pour non-shrink epoxy grout (compressive strength **≥ 80 MPa**). Tighten holding-down bolts to preload using torque method. Torque: $T_q = k F_i d$.

6. **Hook-Up and Commissioning:** Connect piping, cabling, control systems. Conduct dock and sea trials per class-approved procedures.

(A flow diagram must be drawn — see SECTION 4.1.) **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) A main engine of mass 1800 tonnes (with oil) is to be installed using a tandem lift. The two cranes have a combined rated capacity of 2800 tonnes. The bedplate footprint measures 8.5 m × 4.0 m. Verify the lifting safety factor and compute the specific foundation pressure. State your conclusion. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Engine mass: $m = 1800$ tonnes = $1{,}800{,}000$ kg
- Combined crane capacity: $W_{\text{crane}} = 2800$ tonnes = $2{,}800{,}000$ kg (mass-equivalent)
- Footprint area: $A_f = 8.5 \times 4.0 = 34.0 \;\vert m^2 \vert$

**Step 1 — Compute the engine weight:**

$$
W_{\text{engine}} = m \cdot g = 1{,}800{,}000 \cdot 9.81 = 17{,}658{,}000 \;\vert N \vert \approx 17.66 \;\vert MN \vert
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lifting safety factor:**

$$
SF = \frac{W_{\text{crane}}}{W_{\text{engine}}} = \frac{2800}{1800} = 1.556
$$

Since $SF = 1.556 \geq 1.5$ (KTU / standard tandem lift requirement), the lift is **SAFE**. **(3 marks)**

**Step 3 — Compute the specific foundation pressure:**

$$
p = \frac{W_{\text{engine}}}{A_f} = \frac{17{,}658{,}000}{34.0 \times 10^6} = 0.519 \;\vert N/mm^2 \vert = 0.519 \;\vert MPa \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

The specific foundation pressure of **0.519 MPa** is **slightly above the typical 0.5 MPa design limit** for ship foundations. The shipyard must either:
- (a) Increase the bedplate footprint area to **≥ 35.32 m²** (e.g., add 0.4 m to length), or
- (b) Verify with structural calculation that the foundation's local bearing capacity exceeds 0.519 MPa.

The lifting safety factor is **acceptable**; the foundation pressure requires minor redesign or verification. **(4 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Discuss the alignment procedures used during the installation of main propulsion machinery. Explain the difference between cold and hot alignment with a relevant derivation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Alignment — Definition:**
Alignment is the process of ensuring that the **centerlines of the main engine, reduction gearbox, intermediate shaft, tail shaft, and propeller** are coaxial within permissible tolerances under both static (cold) and operating (hot) conditions.

**Cold Alignment:**
Performed at ambient temperature (typically **20 °C**). The engine, gearbox, and shaft are aligned using:
- **Dial gauge method** (face-and-rim) — two gauges per coupling, one reads face, one reads rim.
- **Laser alignment system** — uses a laser beam and position-sensitive detector for higher accuracy (**± 0.01 mm**).

**Hot Alignment:**
Performed after the engine is warmed up to operating temperature. Thermal expansion causes the engine bedplate to grow longitudinally. For a steel shaft line of length $L = 50$ m and temperature rise $\Delta T = 40 \;K$:

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = 12 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50 \cdot 40 = 24 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

The engine bedplate (length ~ 8 m) expands by:

$$
\Delta L_{\text{eng}} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8 \cdot 40 = 3.84 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

**Differential expansion:**

$$
\Delta L_{\text{rel}} = \Delta L_{\text{shaft}} - \Delta L_{\text{engine}} = 24 - 3.84 = 20.16 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

**Conclusion:** During cold alignment, the tail shaft bearing must be pre-offset by approximately **20 mm** (in the opposite direction to expected growth) so that at operating temperature, the alignment becomes correct. If only cold alignment is done, the hot condition will have a **20 mm offset** leading to bearing failure. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) During a tail-shaft alignment measurement, the following readings were taken using dial gauges mounted at the coupling face (radius = 400 mm): Top: +0.18 mm, Bottom: -0.18 mm, Left: +0.05 mm, Right: -0.05 mm. The coupling diameter is 800 mm. Determine the parallel offset and the angular misalignment, and comment on the alignment quality. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Coupling radius: $r = 400$ mm
- Coupling diameter: $D_c = 800$ mm
- Top reading: $+0.18$ mm
- Bottom reading: $-0.18$ mm
- Left reading: $+0.05$ mm
- Right reading: $-0.05$ mm

**Step 1 — Identify the dial gauge positions:**

The pattern of readings (T+/B- and L+/R-) indicates **symmetric readings**, suggesting a pure **angular misalignment** rather than a parallel offset.

**Step 2 — Compute the parallel offset:**

The parallel offset is the *average* of all four readings:

$$
\Delta r = \frac{(+0.18) + (-0.18) + (+0.05) + (-0.05)}{4} = \frac{0}{4} = 0 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

So **parallel offset = 0**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Compute the angular misalignment:**

The vertical face-reading difference between top and bottom:

$$
\delta_v = 0.18 - (-0.18) = 0.36 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

The angular misalignment per meter is:

$$
\Delta \theta_v = \frac{\delta_v}{2 r} = \frac{0.36}{2 \cdot 400} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \;\vert mm/mm \vert = 0.45 \;\vert mm/m \vert
$$

Similarly for horizontal:

$$
\delta_h = 0.05 - (-0.05) = 0.10 \;\vert mm \vert
$$

$$
\Delta \theta_h = \frac{\delta_h}{2 r} = \frac{0.10}{2 \cdot 400} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \vert mm/mm \vert = 0.125 \;\vert mm/m \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Permissible tolerance check:**

Standard tolerance is **$\Delta \theta_{\max} = 0.10 \;\vert mm/m \vert$** for main propulsion shafting.

- Vertical angular misalignment = $0.45 \;\vert mm/m \vert$ — **EXCEEDS tolerance by 4.5 times**. NOT acceptable.
- Horizontal angular misalignment = $0.125 \;\vert mm/m \vert$ — **Slightly exceeds tolerance**. Borderline. **(3 marks)**

**Step 5 — Conclusion and corrective action:**

The alignment is **NOT acceptable**. The engine must be re-shimmed vertically by approximately **$0.18 - 0.04 = 0.14$ mm** under the appropriate bearing feet to bring vertical angular misalignment within tolerance. After correction, hot alignment should be verified by running the engine at operating temperature for at least **2 hours** before final tightening. **(2 marks)**

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Technological Processes in the Installation of Main Machinery**

- ☐ Main machinery installation is a **six-stage process**: Survey → Foundation → Lift → Align → Secure → Commission.
- ☐ Foundation flatness tolerance: **≤ 0.05 mm/m** (engine bedplate acceptance).
- ☐ Chock thickness = Measured gap **− 0.4 mm** (to allow grout penetration).
- ☐ Epoxy grout compressive strength: **≥ 80 MPa**; curing time **24–72 hours**.
- ☐ Lifting safety factor: **≥ 1.25 (single) / ≥ 1.5 (tandem)** per shipyard standards.
- ☐ Parallel offset tolerance: **$\Delta r \leq D_s/1000$**.
- ☐ Angular tolerance: **$\Delta \theta \leq 0.10 \;\vert mm/m \vert$** across coupling.
- ☐ Bolt tightening torque: **$T_q = k \cdot F_i \cdot d$**; for lubricated steel, **$k \approx 0.20$**.
- ☐ Thermal expansion: $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$; **differential expansion** must be used for hot alignment correction.
- ☐ Coefficient of linear expansion for marine steel: $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \;\vert K^{-1} \vert$.
- ☐ Specific foundation pressure: typically **≤ 0.5 MPa**; check before engine placement.
- ☐ Always use **cold alignment + hot alignment correction** before final bolt tightening.
- ☐ **Dial gauge face-and-rim method** is the classic shipyard alignment technique.
- ☐ **Laser alignment** is preferred for modern high-speed engines and shaft lines.
- ☐ Class societies (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require **documented records** of every alignment measurement.
- ☐ Hook-up (piping, cabling) follows alignment — never align after hook-up, as pipe loads distort the bedplate.
- ☐ Dock trial → Sea trial → Class endorsement is the **mandatory** final sequence before delivery.
- ☐ Common pitfalls: confusing $\Delta r$ with $\Delta \theta$, forgetting differential expansion, ignoring safety factor in tandem lifts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shafting and Propeller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shafting and Propeller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ship Shafting — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Ship shafting* is the longitudinal line of forged-steel shafts and their supporting bearings, couplings, and sealing arrangements that transmit the rotational power from the main propulsion engine (or reduction gearbox) to the marine propeller, while also transmitting the resulting thrust force back to the ship's hull.

The system must therefore perform **two simultaneous jobs**:

1. **Torque Transmission** — Carry the twisting moment (torque) from the engine to the propeller.
2. **Thrust Transmission** — Push the propeller's reaction force forward into the hull so the ship moves ahead.

## 1.2 Marine Propeller — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A *marine propeller* is a multi-bladed, helicoidal rotating component fitted to the tail-end of the propeller shaft, designed to convert the engine's rotational mechanical power into a useful thrust force that propels the vessel through water.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Spinning Screwdriver in a Watermelon":**
> Imagine pushing a rotating screwdriver through the skin of a watermelon. The helical threads "bite" into the flesh and pull (or push) the screwdriver forward. A marine propeller works on exactly the same principle, except the "flesh" is seawater and the "screwdriver" is a precision-machined bronze or nickel-aluminium-bronze blade assembly.
> 
> - The **engine** is your hand turning the screwdriver handle.
> - The **shafting** is the long shaft of the screwdriver.
> - The **thrust block** is your other hand, which keeps the shaft from sliding backwards as the propeller pulls forward (it "takes the reaction").
> - The **propeller blades** are the helical threads cutting through the water.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard seawater properties must be memorised for any KTU numerical:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Sea water density | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Kinematic viscosity of sea water | $\nu$ | **1.05 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
| Conversion factor (knots → m/s) | — | **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** |
| Atmospheric pressure (gauge baseline) | $p_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Saturated vapour pressure of water at 25 °C | $p_v$ | **3.17 kPa** |

---

> **End of Section 1 — Continue to Section 2 for the complete KTU Formula Sheet.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shafting System — Component-by-Component Logic

A complete shafting arrangement is built from the following elements, ordered from the engine to the propeller:

### Step 1 — Engine to Gearbox Output Coupling
The main engine (or reduction gearbox output flange) is bolted to the **thrust shaft** through a high-tensile **flange coupling**. This is the only coupling in the line that is axially rigid and transmits both torque **and** thrust.

### Step 2 — Thrust Block (Forward Thrust Bearing)
The **thrust block** is a multi-collar (Michell-type) tilting-pad bearing. Its only job is to absorb the axial push of the propeller and transfer it as a forward load into the hull girder via **thrust seatings**. It also carries some radial load.

### Step 3 — Intermediate Shaft(s) and Plummer Blocks
Between the thrust block and the stern tube there are usually **one or two intermediate shafts**, joined by forged-steel **flange couplings**. Each intermediate shaft rests in a **Plummer block** (a pedestal bearing lined with white metal) that supports radial weight only. They are *horizontally* removable to allow shaft withdrawal.

### Step 4 — Stern Tube and Its Bearings
The stern tube is a watertight cast-iron or fabricated steel casing passing through the stern frame. Inside, the shaft is supported by:
- **Forward stern tube bearing** — usually white metal.
- **After stern tube bearing** — historically **lignum vitae** (a tropical hardwood that is self-lubricating when wet), now often replaced by synthetic rubber or composite bearings.
- The bearings are water-lubricated (no oil allowed because any leak pollutes the sea).

### Step 5 — Stuffing Box (or Sealing Gland)
Just aft of the stern tube, a **stuffing box** with packed flax or modern face-type seals prevents sea water from entering the engine room through the propeller shaft aperture.

### Step 6 — A-Bracket (Outside the Hull)
The shaft's final overhang outside the hull is supported by an **A-bracket** (or "P-bracket") — a casting bolted to the outside of the stern frame, fitted with a rubber/white-metal lined bearing.

### Step 7 — Tail Shaft and Propeller Attachment
The final shaft section is the **tail shaft** (or propeller shaft). It is fitted with a **tapered end** (typically 1:15 taper) and a **keyway** so the propeller can be hydraulically forced on and locked with a **propeller nut** and **securing pin**.

## 2.2 The Propeller — Component-by-Component Logic

### Step 1 — Hub / Boss
The **hub** is the central solid body bored with a tapered hole for the tail shaft. It houses the keyway and forms the structural connection between shaft and blades.

### Step 2 — Blades (Number, Material)
Modern propellers have **3, 4, 5, or 6 blades** (4-blade most common for merchant ships). Materials:
- **Manganese bronze** for small vessels.
- **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB)** for larger ships (≈ 12 % Al, 7 % Fe, 4 % Ni) — high corrosion resistance in sea water.

### Step 3 — Blade Geometry
Each blade has:
- **Face** (pressure side, low-pressure) — the smooth, concave side facing forward in the wake.
- **Back** (suction side, high-pressure) — the rough side facing aft.
- **Leading edge** — the edge that cuts into the water first.
- **Trailing edge** — the edge that leaves the water last.
- **Root** — where the blade joins the hub.
- **Tip** — the outermost edge (most prone to cavitation).

### Step 4 — Pitch and Slip Concepts
- **Geometric pitch (P):** the theoretical forward advance of the propeller per revolution *if* there were no slip.
- **Pitch speed (Apparent advance speed, Vₐ):** $V_a = P \times n$ where *n* is in revolutions per second.
- **Apparent slip:** the difference between *Vₐ* and the actual ship speed *V*ₛ.
- **Real slip:** the difference between *Vₐ* and the wake-augmented velocity *Vᵣ* (the actual speed of water past the propeller disc).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *cheat-sheet* you must commit to memory. Every 14-mark numerical in this module derives from these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apparent advance speed | $V_a = P \times n$ | m/s |
| 2 | Wake fraction | $w = (V_a - V_R) / V_a$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Wake-augmented velocity | $V_R = V_s / (1 - w)$ | m/s |
| 4 | Apparent slip | $S_a = (V_a - V_s) / V_a$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Real slip | $S_r = (V_a - V_R) / V_a$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Advance coefficient | $J = V_a / (n \times D)$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Thrust | $T = \rho \times K_T \times n^2 \times D^4$ | N |
| 8 | Torque | $Q = \rho \times K_Q \times n^2 \times D^5$ | N·m |
| 9 | Thrust power | $P_T = 2\pi \times n \times Q$ | W |
| 10 | Effective power | $P_E = T \times V_s$ | W |
| 11 | Open-water (propeller) efficiency | $\eta_O = (J \times K_T) / (2\pi \times K_Q)$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Hull efficiency (Taylor) | $\eta_H = (1 - w) / (1 - t)$ (assume *t ≈ w* ⇒ $\eta_H \approx 1 - w$) | dimensionless |
| 13 | Rotational (shaft) efficiency | $\eta_R = 1 - (\text{friction losses} / P_T)$ ≈ **0.97 to 0.99** | dimensionless |
| 14 | Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient (overall) | $\eta_D = \eta_H \times \eta_O \times \eta_R$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Cavitation number (minimum pressure) | $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v) / (0.5 \times \rho \times V_0^2)$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | Ship-speed conversion | $1\ \text{knot} = 0.5144\ \text{m/s}$ | — |
| 17 | Delivered Power (to propeller) | $P_D = P_E / \eta_D$ | W |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The shafting-propeller system is the **heart of the marine propulsion plant**. Its engineering utility spans:

- **Naval architecture & ship design:** Slip, efficiency, and cavitation numbers drive the choice of diameter (D), pitch (P), RPM (n), and blade number (Z) during early design.
- **Classification society rules:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS all prescribe minimum tail-shaft diameter using $D_{min} = k \sqrt[3]{P / n}$, mandating safety against torsional and fatigue failure.
- **Maintenance scheduling:** Stern-tube bearing wear, stern-gland oil/grease leakage, and propeller blade tip-clearance are routine survey items.
- **Vibration engineering:** Propeller-excited hull vibration (singing) and shaft whirling are predicted using $n_{critical} = 9.55 \sqrt{K / I_{polar}}$ for shaft lateral vibration.

---

> **End of Section 2 — Continue to Section 3 for full step-by-step derivations and a numerical walk-through.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Apparent Slip and Real Slip

### 3.1.1 Apparent Slip (Sₐ)

We begin with the geometric concept that in one revolution a propeller *should* advance by an axial distance equal to its geometric pitch *P*. If the propeller turns at *n* revolutions per **second**, the theoretical forward speed is:

$$
V_a = P \times n
$$

The actual speed of the ship through still water, *V*ₛ, is less than *Vₐ* because water "slips" past the blades (like an oar slipping through water). The fractional loss in advance is:

$$
S_a = \frac{V_a - V_s}{V_a} = \frac{P \times n - V_s}{P \times n}
$$

### 3.1.2 Real Slip (Sᵣ) — Why It Exists

Because the hull drags water along with it (the **wake**), the propeller actually sees water approaching it at a velocity *Vᵣ* > *V*ₛ. The wake fraction is defined as:

$$
w = \frac{V_a - V_R}{V_a}
$$

Rearranging for the wake-augmented velocity:

$$
V_R = V_a \times (1 - w) = \frac{V_s}{1 - w}
$$

The **real slip** is then:

$$
S_r = \frac{V_a - V_R}{V_a} = 1 - \frac{V_R}{V_a} = 1 - (1 - w) = w
$$

This yields the elegant KTU identity:

$$
S_r \equiv w \quad \text{(for a free propeller, neglecting thrust-deduction)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU:** Real slip is generally *much smaller* than apparent slip because the wake partially compensates the slip. In practice, $S_a$ is 15–30 % while $S_r$ is only 3–8 %.

## 3.2 Derivation of Open-Water Propeller Efficiency ($\eta_O$)

Open-water (or "behind-no-ship") efficiency is the ratio of useful thrust work to shaft torque work delivered to the propeller in open water:

$$
\eta_O = \frac{\text{Thrust work rate}}{\text{Torque work rate}} = \frac{T \times V_a}{2\pi \times n \times Q}
$$

Substituting the standard thrust and torque relations:

$$
T = \rho \, K_T \, n^2 \, D^4
$$
$$
Q = \rho \, K_Q \, n^2 \, D^5
$$

We obtain:

$$
\eta_O = \frac{\rho \, K_T \, n^2 \, D^4 \, V_a}{2\pi \, n \times \rho \, K_Q \, n^2 \, D^5}
$$

The $\rho$ and $n^2$ cancel; one $n$ cancels out leaving $n$ in the denominator:

$$
\eta_O = \frac{K_T \, V_a}{2\pi \, n \, K_Q \, D}
$$

Recognising the advance coefficient $J = V_a / (n D)$:

$$
\eta_O = \frac{J \, K_T}{2\pi \, K_Q}
$$

This is the most-tested propeller-efficiency formula on KTU papers.

## 3.3 Derivation of Cavitation Number

Consider a small fluid element at the leading edge of the propeller blade. The local static pressure is *p*. Cavitation (boiling of water at ambient temperature) begins when *p* falls to the vapour pressure *pᵥ*.

The pressure coefficient at a point is:

$$
C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{0.5 \, \rho \, V_0^2}
$$

The most negative pressure achievable on the blade corresponds to the minimum $C_p$ value, $C_{p,min}$. Cavitation onset corresponds to:

$$
p_\infty + 0.5 \, \rho \, V_0^2 \, C_{p,min} = p_v
$$

Solving for the critical ambient pressure:

$$
p_{0,\text{crit}} - p_v = -0.5 \, \rho \, V_0^2 \, C_{p,min}
$$

Defining the cavitation number $\sigma$ as:

$$
\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{0.5 \, \rho \, V_0^2}
$$

Cavitation occurs when $\sigma < -C_{p,min}$. The empirical KTU rule of thumb is **avoid $\sigma < 0.4$** for merchant-ship propellers.

## 3.4 Complete Numerical Walk-Through (14-Mark Style)

### Problem Statement

A 4-bladed propeller of diameter **4 m** and pitch **3 m** rotates at **150 rpm**. The ship moves at **12 knots**. Given the wake fraction *w* = **0.15**, thrust coefficient $K_T$ = **0.15**, torque coefficient $K_Q$ = **0.03**, sea-water density $\rho$ = **1025 kg/m³**, and rotational efficiency $\eta_R$ = **0.98**, compute:
1. Apparent slip.
2. Real slip.
3. Advance coefficient *J*.
4. Thrust and torque developed.
5. Thrust power and effective power.
6. Open-water efficiency, hull efficiency, and quasi-propulsive coefficient.

### Step 1 — Convert Units
The ship speed must be in SI units for consistency:

$$
V_s = 12\ \text{knots} \times 0.5144\ \frac{\text{m/s}}{\text{knot}} = 6.173\ \text{m/s}
$$

The propeller rotational speed must be in revolutions per second:

$$
n = \frac{150\ \text{rpm}}{60\ \text{s/min}} = 2.5\ \text{rps}
$$

### Step 2 — Apparent Advance Speed

$$
V_a = P \times n = 3\ \text{m} \times 2.5\ \text{rps} = 7.5\ \text{m/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Apparent Slip

$$
S_a = \frac{V_a - V_s}{V_a} = \frac{7.5 - 6.173}{7.5} = \frac{1.327}{7.5} = 0.1769
$$

So $S_a \approx 17.7\%$.

### Step 4 — Wake-Augmented Velocity

$$
V_R = \frac{V_s}{1 - w} = \frac{6.173}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{6.173}{0.85} = 7.262\ \text{m/s}
$$

### Step 5 — Real Slip

$$
S_r = \frac{V_a - V_R}{V_a} = \frac{7.5 - 7.262}{7.5} = \frac{0.238}{7.5} = 0.0317
$$

So $S_r \approx 3.17\%$ (which equals *w* as expected for a single-screw ship).

### Step 6 — Advance Coefficient

$$
J = \frac{V_a}{n \times D} = \frac{7.5}{2.5 \times 4} = \frac{7.5}{10} = 0.75
$$

### Step 7 — Thrust Developed

$$
T = \rho \times K_T \times n^2 \times D^4 = 1025 \times 0.15 \times (2.5)^2 \times (4)^4
$$

Evaluating the powers:

$$
n^2 = 6.25, \quad D^4 = 256
$$

$$
T = 1025 \times 0.15 \times 6.25 \times 256
$$

Doing it in two stages:

$$
T = 1025 \times 240 = 246{,}000\ \text{N} = 246\ \text{kN}
$$

### Step 8 — Torque Developed

$$
Q = \rho \times K_Q \times n^2 \times D^5 = 1025 \times 0.03 \times (2.5)^2 \times (4)^5
$$

Evaluating:

$$
D^5 = 1024
$$

$$
Q = 1025 \times 0.03 \times 6.25 \times 1024 = 1025 \times 192 = 196{,}800\ \text{N·m} = 196.8\ \text{kN·m}
$$

### Step 9 — Thrust Power (Delivered Power to Propeller)

$$
P_T = 2\pi \times n \times Q = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 2.5 \times 196{,}800
$$

$$
P_T = 3.092 \times 10^6\ \text{W} = 3092\ \text{kW} \approx 3.09\ \text{MW}
$$

### Step 10 — Effective Power (Useful Tow-Rope Power)

$$
P_E = T \times V_s = 246{,}000\ \text{N} \times 6.173\ \text{m/s} = 1.519 \times 10^6\ \text{W} = 1519\ \text{kW}
$$

### Step 11 — Open-Water Efficiency

Using the derived closed-form expression:

$$
\eta_O = \frac{J \times K_T}{2\pi \times K_Q} = \frac{0.75 \times 0.15}{2\pi \times 0.03} = \frac{0.1125}{0.1885} = 0.5970
$$

So $\eta_O \approx 59.7\%$.

### Step 12 — Hull Efficiency

Taylor's approximation for single-screw ships with thrust deduction fraction $t \approx w$:

$$
\eta_H = \frac{1 - w}{1 - t} \approx \frac{1 - w}{1 - w} = 1 - w = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85
$$

So $\eta_H = 85\%$.

### Step 13 — Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient

$$
\eta_D = \eta_H \times \eta_O \times \eta_R = 0.85 \times 0.597 \times 0.98
$$

$$
\eta_D = 0.497 \approx 49.7\%
$$

### Step 14 — Brake Power (BHP) Required

$$
P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \frac{1519}{0.497} = 3056\ \text{kW} \approx 3.06\ \text{MW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Verification:** The delivered power $P_T$ (3092 kW) is slightly higher than the brake power (3056 kW) because of the rotational efficiency loss in the shaft — this matches the expected relationship $P_T = \eta_R \times P_B$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 model]`

### Question A1 (3 marks)
**Define the terms *apparent slip* and *real slip* of a marine propeller. Why is real slip generally much smaller than apparent slip?**

**Model Answer:**
Apparent slip is the fractional loss in ship speed compared to the theoretical pitch speed, given by $S_a = (V_a - V_s) / V_a$ where $V_a = P \times n$ is the theoretical advance per second and $V_s$ is the actual ship speed.
Real slip is the fractional loss compared to the wake-augmented velocity $V_R = V_s / (1 - w)$, given by $S_r = (V_a - V_R) / V_a$.
Real slip is smaller because the hull drags a column of water along with it (wake), so the water actually approaches the propeller faster than the ship's speed, partially recovering the slip.

### Question A2 (3 marks)
**What is cavitation in a marine propeller? List any two methods used to prevent it.**

**Model Answer:**
Cavitation is the formation and collapse of vapour bubbles on the suction (back) side of a propeller blade when local static pressure drops to or below the saturated vapour pressure of water. The collapsing bubbles erode the blade surface and reduce thrust and efficiency.
Prevention methods:
1. Increasing blade area (using more blades or wider blades) to reduce local pressure drop.
2. Reducing propeller rpm by using a reduction gearbox, thereby lowering blade-tip velocity.
3. Polishing the blade surface and avoiding sharp edges.
4. Using high-skew or highly-raked blade shapes.
