## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### CHOICE A

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 model]`

**Question A (14 marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **arrangement of a typical merchant-ship shafting system** from the main engine to the propeller. Explain the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-bladed propeller of diameter **5 m** and pitch **4 m** rotates at **120 rpm** when the ship is moving at **16 knots**. The wake fraction *w* = **0.18**, the rotational efficiency $\eta_R$ = **0.97**, thrust coefficient $K_T$ = **0.20**, and torque coefficient $K_Q$ = **0.035**. Sea-water density = **1025 kg/m³**. Compute:
  (i) Apparent slip and real slip.
  (ii) Thrust and torque developed.
  (iii) Effective power, thrust power, and quasi-propulsive coefficient. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

The shafting arrangement (use the diagram from Section 4.1) consists of the following components in sequence:

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Main engine output flange | Source of rotational mechanical power. |
| 2 | Flange coupling (engine to thrust shaft) | Transmits torque and axial thrust. |
| 3 | Thrust shaft | First shaft section, located inside the thrust block. |
| 4 | Thrust block | Michell-type tilting-pad bearing; absorbs propeller thrust and transfers it to hull. |
| 5 | Intermediate shafts | Carry torque from engine room to stern tube; supported by Plummer blocks. |
| 6 | Plummer blocks | Pedestal bearings supporting intermediate shafts radially. |
| 7 | Tail shaft | Final shaft inside the stern tube; has tapered end for propeller. |
| 8 | Stern tube | Watertight casing through the hull; houses bearings. |
| 9 | Stern tube bearings | Forward (white metal) and after (lignum vitae / rubber) bearings, water-lubricated. |
| 10 | Stuffing box / sealing gland | Prevents seawater ingress along the shaft into the engine room. |
| 11 | A-bracket (outside hull) | Supports the overhang of tail shaft outside the hull. |
| 12 | Propeller | Converts torque to thrust via helical blades. |

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:**
$D = 5\ \text{m}, \ P = 4\ \text{m}, \ n = 120\ \text{rpm} = 2\ \text{rps}, \ V_s = 16\ \text{knots} = 8.23\ \text{m/s}, \ w = 0.18, \ \eta_R = 0.97, \ K_T = 0.20, \ K_Q = 0.035, \ \rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$

**(i) Apparent Slip and Real Slip**

$$
V_a = P \times n = 4 \times 2 = 8.0\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
S_a = \frac{V_a - V_s}{V_a} = \frac{8.0 - 8.23}{8.0} = -0.02875 \approx -2.88\%
$$

The negative value indicates the ship is moving slightly faster than the pitch speed (often seen at low pitch-to-diameter ratios).

$$
V_R = \frac{V_s}{1 - w} = \frac{8.23}{0.82} = 10.04\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
S_r = \frac{V_a - V_R}{V_a} = \frac{8.0 - 10.04}{8.0} = -0.255 \approx -25.5\%
$$

**(ii) Thrust and Torque**

$$
T = \rho \, K_T \, n^2 \, D^4 = 1025 \times 0.20 \times 4 \times 625 = 1025 \times 500 = 512{,}500\ \text{N} \approx 512.5\ \text{kN}
$$

$$
Q = \rho \, K_Q \, n^2 \, D^5 = 1025 \times 0.035 \times 4 \times 3125 = 1025 \times 437.5 = 448{,}438\ \text{N·m} \approx 448.4\ \text{kN·m}
$$

**(iii) Power and Efficiency**

$$
P_E = T \times V_s = 512{,}500 \times 8.23 = 4.218 \times 10^6\ \text{W} \approx 4.22\ \text{MW}
$$

$$
P_T = 2\pi \times n \times Q = 2\pi \times 2 \times 448{,}438 = 5.636 \times 10^6\ \text{W} \approx 5.64\ \text{MW}
$$

$$
J = \frac{V_a}{n D} = \frac{8.0}{2 \times 5} = 0.80
$$

$$
\eta_O = \frac{J \, K_T}{2\pi \, K_Q} = \frac{0.80 \times 0.20}{2\pi \times 0.035} = \frac{0.16}{0.2199} = 0.728 \approx 72.8\%
$$

$$
\eta_H \approx 1 - w = 0.82
$$

$$
\eta_D = \eta_H \times \eta_O \times \eta_R = 0.82 \times 0.728 \times 0.97 = 0.579 \approx 57.9\%
$$

$$
P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \frac{4.22}{0.579} = 7.29\ \text{MW}
$$

---

### CHOICE B

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 model]`

**Question B (14 marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **various parts of a marine propeller**. Define geometric pitch, pitch ratio, and slip ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **phenomenon of cavitation** in marine propellers. Discuss its causes, effects on performance, and the various **preventive measures** adopted in modern propeller design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Parts of the Propeller (refer Section 4.2 diagram):**

- **Hub (or Boss):** The central solid cylindrical body bored with a tapered hole (typically 1:15 taper) for fitting on the tail shaft. Houses the keyway and is secured by the propeller nut and locking pin.
- **Blades:** Usually 3 to 6 in number, attached to the hub. Made of manganese bronze for small vessels and nickel-aluminium bronze (NAB) for larger ships.
- **Face:** The smooth, low-pressure side of the blade.
- **Back:** The high-pressure side of the blade (often pitted by cavitation).
- **Leading edge and Trailing edge.**
- **Root and Tip.**

**Definitions:**

- **Geometric Pitch (P):** The theoretical forward distance a propeller would advance in one revolution if there were no slip. Measured along the shaft axis.
- **Pitch Ratio (P/D):** The ratio of geometric pitch to propeller diameter. Typical merchant-ship range is **0.6 to 1.2**.
- **Slip Ratio (S):** The fractional loss in advance speed; apparent slip $S_a = (V_a - V_s) / V_a$.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Cavitation — Phenomenon:**
Cavitation is the formation of vapour-filled cavities (bubbles) on the **back (suction side)** of a propeller blade when the local static pressure falls to or below the saturated vapour pressure of seawater (≈ 3.17 kPa at 25 °C). When these bubbles are carried into regions of higher pressure they collapse violently, producing high-pressure shock waves.

**Causes:**
1. **Low local pressure** due to high blade-tip velocity (typically > 30 m/s for merchant ships).
2. **Small blade area** leading to high loading per unit area.
3. **Operation in shallow water** where atmospheric pressure is reduced on the suction side.
4. **High rotational speed** without adequate reduction gearing.
5. **Rough or fouled blade surface** creating local pressure troughs.

**Effects on Performance:**
1. **Erosion of blade surface** — pitting and roughening, especially near the tip.
2. **Loss of thrust and efficiency** — the cavity disrupts smooth flow.
3. **Vibration and noise** ("propeller singing").
4. **Increased slip** — apparent thrust drops as energy is wasted in bubble formation.
5. **Possible shaft whirling and bearing damage** from cyclic loading.

**Preventive Measures:**
1. **Increase blade area** by using more blades (5 or 6 instead of 4) or wider blades (higher blade-area ratio BAR).
2. **Reduce RPM** by using a reduction gearbox — the most common modern remedy.
3. **Use high-skew propellers** — shifts the maximum chord away from the tip, delaying cavitation onset.
4. **Increase blade-tip clearance** from the hull (typically ≥ 0.15 D for single-screw ships).
5. **Polish and fair the blade surface** to prevent local pressure troughs.
6. **Use stainless-steel or super-cavitating blade tips** in severe cases.
7. **Operate the propeller at a higher cavitation number** by restricting the depth of operation (avoid shallow water).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit confusion on rotational speed:** *n* must be in **revolutions per second (rps)**, not RPM, before plugging into the formulas $T = \rho K_T n^2 D^4$ and $Q = \rho K_Q n^2 D^5$. Failure to divide RPM by 60 gives thrust values 3600× too large.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up apparent slip and real slip:** Real slip uses $V_R$, *not* $V_s$. A common shortcut is to write $S_r = w$ directly — this is correct *only* when thrust-deduction fraction $t \approx w$.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the 2π in thrust power:** $P_T = 2\pi n Q$, **not** $P_T = n Q$. The factor 2π is worth 1 mark by itself.
> 
> 4. **Confusing ρ = 1000 kg/m³ (fresh water) with ρ = 1025 kg/m³ (sea water).** Use 1025 unless the problem explicitly states fresh water.
> 
> 5. **Not converting knots to m/s.** The slip and thrust-power formulas require SI units throughout. 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.
> 
> 6. **Omitting the sketch in 7-mark descriptive questions.** Even a neat labelled block diagram (as in Section 4) earns at least 2 marks before any text is read.
> 
> 7. **Writing "pitch ratio = pitch × diameter"** — it is *pitch divided by diameter*, P/D. Easy slip.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ Shafting transmits **torque** (engine → propeller) **and** **thrust** (propeller → hull).
- ✅ Order of components: **Engine → Flange → Thrust Shaft → Thrust Block → Intermediate Shaft(s) → Plummer Blocks → Tail Shaft → Stern Tube → Stuffing Box → A-Bracket → Propeller.**
- ✅ Only the **thrust block** carries axial load; all other bearings carry radial load.
- ✅ Stern tube bearings are **water-lubricated**; after bearing is often lignum vitae or rubber.
- ✅ Tail shaft has **1:15 taper** + keyway; propeller is secured with **nut + locking pin**.
- ✅ Propeller materials: **Manganese bronze** (small), **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB)** (large ships).
- ✅ **Pitch speed** $V_a = P \times n$.
- ✅ **Apparent slip** $S_a = (V_a - V_s) / V_a$ — usually **15–30 %**.
- ✅ **Real slip** $S_r = (V_a - V_R) / V_a \approx w$ — usually **3–8 %**.
- ✅ **Advance coefficient** $J = V_a / (n D)$.
- ✅ **Thrust** $T = \rho K_T n^2 D^4$, **Torque** $Q = \rho K_Q n^2 D^5$.
- ✅ **Thrust power** $P_T = 2\pi n Q$, **Effective power** $P_E = T V_s$.
- ✅ **Open-water efficiency** $\eta_O = (J K_T) / (2\pi K_Q)$.
- ✅ **Quasi-propulsive coefficient** $\eta_D = \eta_H \times \eta_O \times \eta_R \approx (1-w) \times \eta_O \times 0.97$.
- ✅ **Cavitation** = vapour bubble formation when local $p \leq p_v$. Prevent by reducing RPM, increasing blade area, and polishing surfaces.
- ✅ Standard sea-water density $\rho$ = **1025 kg/m³**; 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s**.
- ✅ 4-blade propellers are most common; pitch ratio (P/D) typically **0.6–1.2**.

---

> **End of Section 5 — End of Module 4 Notes on Shafting and Propeller.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auxiliary Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Auxiliary Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auxiliary Machinery** refers to all the secondary mechanical, electrical, and thermal equipment installed aboard a ship — **other than the main propulsion engine and its directly coupled shaft-line components** — that is essential for the safe operation, habitability, navigation, cargo handling, and environmental compliance of the vessel.

These systems are driven either by **auxiliary engines (auxiliary diesel engines / auxiliary steam turbines)**, **main-engine-driven take-offs (PTO/PTI)**, or **electric motors** fed from the ship's main switchboard. They collectively form what is known in naval architecture as the **"Ship's Auxiliary Plant"** or simply the **Auxiliaries**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the auxiliary machinery of a ship is broadly classified into the following functional groups:

| S.No | Functional Group | Primary Purpose |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Electrical Power Generation | Turbo-generators, diesel generators, shaft generators |
| 2 | Pumping & Piping Systems | Bilge, ballast, cooling water, fuel, lube-oil pumps |
| 3 | Air & Gas Systems | Compressors, starting-air receivers, inert-gas plants |
| 4 | Heat & Steam Systems | Aux. boilers, economizers, exhaust gas boilers |
| 5 | Fluid Treatment | Fuel/lube-oil purifiers & separators, clarifiers |
| 6 | HVAC & Habitability | Air-conditioning, ventilation, refrigeration, sewage plants |
| 7 | Fresh Water Generation | Distillers / Reverse-Osmosis (RO) plants |
| 8 | Deck & Steering Machinery | Windlasses, winches, capstans, steering gear |
| 9 | Safety Systems | Fire pumps, CO₂ systems, life-boat engines |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Ship as a Floating City"**
>
> Imagine a modern apartment complex. The **main propulsion engine** is the elevator system that moves the entire building. But for the building to function, you also need **electricity** (generators), **water supply** (pumps), **garbage disposal** (bilge systems), **fire extinguishers** (fire pumps), **parking lifts** (deck machinery), **air-conditioning** (HVAC), and a **security guard** (steering gear). All of these "behind-the-scenes" support systems are exactly what **Auxiliary Machinery** is to a ship.
>
> A ship without functioning auxiliaries cannot move safely, generate electricity, fight fires, pump out water, or even provide drinking water to its crew — even if the main engine is in perfect condition.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard metrics and constants govern the design and selection of shipboard auxiliary machinery:
>
> - **Ship service voltage (AC):** **440 V, 60 Hz** (large vessels) and **230 V, 60 Hz** (hotel load).
> - **Ship service voltage (DC):** **24 V / 110 V / 220 V** for emergency and control circuits.
> - **Standard starting-air pressure:** **30 bar (≈ 3 MPa)** for main engines.
> - **Standard control-air pressure:** **7 bar (≈ 0.7 MPa)**.
> - **Standard freshwater generation rate:** **10–20 tonnes/day** per person or per 1000 kW main engine.
> - **Steering gear regulatory response time:** **28 seconds** hard-over-to-hard-over (SOLAS Ch. II-1).
> - **Sea-water density (ρ_sw):** **1025 kg/m³** at 4 °C, **salinity ≈ 35 ‰**.
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**.

## 1.4 Why Auxiliary Machinery Matters (Exam-Oriented Hook)

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU examiners love this topic:** Almost every shipboard accident in history (e.g., *MV Herald of Free Enterprise*, *Costa Concordia*, *Titanic* auxiliary boiler failure) traces back to **failure of an auxiliary system**. Therefore, **classification societies (IRS, LR, DNV, BV, ABS)** classify auxiliaries under the same regulatory regime as the main engine. Expect a 14-mark question in ESE on this topic with sub-parts covering **types, working principle, and selection criteria**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Auxiliary Machinery

Auxiliary machinery can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**: (i) by energy source, (ii) by functional role, and (iii) by degree of redundancy.

### A. By Energy Source
- **Electrically driven auxiliaries** — fed from the main switchboard (most modern ships).
- **Steam-driven auxiliaries** — historically dominant; still found on steam-turbine LNG carriers.
- **Hydraulically driven auxiliaries** — steering gear, deck machinery on small/medium vessels.
- **Engine-driven auxiliaries (PTO)** — pumps and compressors driven directly off the main engine.

### B. By Functional Role
1. **Prime Movers for Electricity** → Diesel Generators (DG sets), Turbo-Generators (TG), Shaft Generators.
2. **Fluid Transport** → Centrifugal, Reciprocating, Screw, Gear pumps.
3. **Gas Compression** → Reciprocating, Screw, Centrifugal compressors.
4. **Fluid Purification** → Centrifugal separators (purifiers/clarifiers).
5. **Thermal Energy** → Aux. oil-fired boiler, Exhaust Gas Boiler (EGB), Composite Boiler.
6. **Heat Transfer** → Shell-and-tube, plate-type heat exchangers.
7. **Habitability** → AC plants, refrigeration, sewage treatment, fresh-water generators.
8. **Cargo/Mechanical Handling** → Windlass, mooring winch, capstan, crane, hatch cover.
9. **Maneuvering Control** → Steering gear (ram/torque/rotary vane).

### C. By Degree of Redundancy
- **Essential / Class-1** — must remain operational even after a single failure (steering gear, fire pumps).
- **Important / Class-2** — required for normal operation, with redundancy built in.
- **Non-essential / Class-3** — can be shed during emergency (deck washes, galley equipment).

## 2.2 Detailed Sub-System Analysis

### 2.2.1 Generators (Electrical Power Plant)

- **Diesel Generator (DG)**: 4-stroke medium-speed diesel engine (typically 720–1000 rpm) coupled to a 3-phase AC synchronous generator. Standard sizes: **500 kW, 750 kW, 1000 kW, 1500 kW, 2500 kW**. A typical ocean-going vessel carries **3 to 4 DG sets** plus **1 emergency DG**.
- **Turbo-Generator (TG)**: Steam turbine (or sometimes gas turbine) driving an AC generator. Found on steam-propelled ships; takes steam from the **auxiliary boiler** or **exhaust gas boiler**.
- **Shaft Generator**: An alternator mounted on the main propulsion shaftline, used during long transits to offload DG sets and save fuel. Typically rated **20–40 %** of main engine power.

### 2.2.2 Pumps

Pumps are the most numerous auxiliaries on any ship. The KTU syllabus categorises them by the fluid handled:

| Type | Fluid Handled | Typical Pump | Head (m) | Flow (m³/h) |
|------|---------------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| Bilge pump | Drainage / oily water | Centrifugal / self-priming | 20–50 | 50–400 |
| Ballast pump | Sea water (intake/discharge) | Centrifugal | 15–35 | 500–4000 |
| Cooling seawater (SW) pump | Sea water for coolers | Centrifugal | 25–40 | 100–1500 |
| Cooling freshwater (FW) pump | Fresh water closed loop | Centrifugal | 20–35 | 50–500 |
| Fuel-oil transfer pump | HFO/MDO | Screw / gear | 30–80 | 10–100 |
| Fuel-oil booster pump | HFO to engine | Screw / gear | 50–150 | 5–50 |
| Lube-oil pump | LO circulation | Screw / gear | 30–100 | 10–80 |
| Fire pump | Sea water (emergency) | Centrifugal | 80–120 | 50–300 |
| Boiler feed pump | Distillate to boiler | Multistage centrifugal | 200–600 | 5–100 |

### 2.2.3 Compressors

Provide **starting air** for main engines and **control air** for pneumatic instruments.

- **Starting-air compressors**: Two-stage reciprocating, two-step compression to **30 bar**.
- **Control-air compressors**: Single/two-stage reciprocating, discharge pressure **7 bar**.
- **Refrigeration compressors**: Hermetic/semi-hermetic reciprocating or screw, low-pressure refrigerant (R-134a, R-404A, NH₃ on LNG carriers).

### 2.2.4 Purifiers (Centrifugal Separators)

Use **density difference** in a high-speed rotating bowl (**≈ 7000–9000 rpm**) to separate **water from fuel oil** and **sludge from lube oil**.

- **Purifier** → separates two liquids of different densities (water from oil).
- **Clarifier** → separates solids from liquids (sludge from oil).
- **Self-cleaning purifier** → modern automatic type with bowl-opening mechanism.

### 2.2.5 Auxiliary Boilers

Provide steam for heating, fuel-oil conditioning, domestic use, and TG sets.

- **Auxiliary oil-fired boiler**: Fire-tube, capacity **1–10 tonnes/h**, working pressure **7–10 bar**.
- **Exhaust Gas Boiler (EGB)**: Waste-heat recovery from main engine exhaust, **no fuel consumption**.
- **Composite Boiler**: Combines oil-firing and EGB on a single shell.
- **Economiser**: Recovers residual heat from flue gas to pre-heat feed water.

### 2.2.6 Heat Exchangers

- **Shell-and-tube**: Most common; sea-water flows through tubes, fresh-water/lube-oil in shell.
- **Plate type (gasketed / welded)**: Compact, high heat-transfer area.
- **Box coolers**: Plate coils immersed in a sea-water tank — used for main-engine jacket cooling on smaller vessels.

### 2.2.7 Fresh-Water Generators (Distillers)

Produce potable water from sea water using either:

- **Evaporation (MSF / MVC)**: Sea water boiled, vapor condensed.
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO)**: Membrane-based separation under high pressure (**55–80 bar**).

> **Rule of thumb:** A typical cruise ship produces **1000–3000 tonnes/day** of freshwater.

### 2.2.8 Steering Gear

- **Hydraulic ram type**: Most common; two hydraulic cylinders drive the rudder.
- **Rotary vane type**: Compact, used on smaller vessels.
- **SOLAS compliance:** Two independent power units, each capable of turning rudder from **35° port to 35° starboard in ≤ 28 seconds** at max service speed.

### 2.2.9 Deck Machinery

- **Windlass**: For raising/lowering anchor and chain.
- **Mooring winch**: For tensioning mooring lines.
- **Capstan**: Vertical drum for line handling.
- **Cargo crane / derrick**: For loading/unloading cargo.

All modern deck machinery is **electro-hydraulic** with variable-speed drives.

### 2.2.10 HVAC, Sewage & Refrigeration

- **HVAC plant**: Provides conditioned air to accommodation, machinery spaces, and electronic rooms.
- **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)**: Biological/chemical treatment, complies with **MARPOL Annex IV**.
- **Refrigeration plant**: For provision stores, typically **+4 °C** chill rooms and **−18 °C** freezers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **most frequently tested** in the KTU ESE on Auxiliary Machinery.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Hydraulic pump power | W |
| 2 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{pump}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}}$ | Pump shaft (input) power | W |
| 3 | $NPSH_{avail} = \dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{p_{static}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{p_{vapor}}{\rho g} - h_{f}$ | Net Positive Suction Head available | m |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s |
| 5 | $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2}}{\ln(\Delta T_{1} / \Delta T_{2})}$ | Log-Mean Temperature Difference (HX) | °C or K |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD$ | Heat transfer rate | W |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_{boiler} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot (h_{steam} - h_{fw})$ | Boiler steam-generation capacity | W |
| 8 | $P_{DG} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{\cos\phi \cdot \eta_{gen}}$ | Generator rated power | W |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{hyd}}$ | Overall pump efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $F_{anchor} = \mu \cdot W_{chain} \cdot g$ | Holding force (anchor in seabed) | N |

Where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (**m³/s**)
- $H$ = pump head (**m**)
- $\eta$ = efficiency (**dimensionless, 0 < η < 1**)
- $\cos\phi$ = power factor (**typically 0.8**)
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**)
- $A$ = heat-transfer area (**m²**)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily by marine engineers for: sizing pumps, selecting motors, designing heat exchangers, calculating boiler capacity, and verifying NPSH margins on board. Classification societies require **NPSH available ≥ NPSH required + 0.5 m** as a safety margin.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Intuitive Reasoning

- **Why multiply Q·H in pump power?** Because pumping work = force × distance. $H$ represents equivalent fluid column height that must be lifted; $Q$ is the volume moved per second.
- **Why LMTD and not arithmetic mean?** Heat transfer is logarithmic because the temperature driving force decays exponentially along the heat exchanger.
- **Why NPSH margin?** Cavitation occurs when local pressure drops below vapor pressure — this destroys pump impellers in hours. NPSH margin is the safety cushion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pump Hydraulic Power

> **Problem Statement:** A sea-water ballast pump delivers $Q = 600$ m³/h at a head $H = 25$ m. The pump efficiency is $\eta_{pump} = 0.75$, motor efficiency is $\eta_{motor} = 0.92$, and the transmission is direct-coupled (so $\eta_{transmission} \approx 1$). Sea-water density is $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³. Find the **motor input power** in kW.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify and convert all units to SI.
- Volumetric flow: $Q = 600 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h} = \dfrac{600}{3600} = 0.1667 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Head: $H = 25 \;\text{m}$
- Density: $\rho = 1025 \;\text{kg/m}^3$
- Gravity: $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$

**Step 2:** Write the standard hydraulic-power formula.

$$
P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values into the formula.

$$
P_{hyd} = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1667 \cdot 25
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the intermediate product, step by step.

$$
\begin{aligned}
1025 \cdot 9.81 &= 10055.25 \;\text{N/m}^3 \\
10055.25 \cdot 0.1667 &= 1676.21 \;\text{N·m/s·(unitless ratio)} \\
1676.21 \cdot 25 &= 41905.3 \;\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $P_{hyd} \approx 41.91 \;\text{kW}$.

**Step 5:** Account for pump inefficiency. Real pump shaft power must be higher.

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}} = \dfrac{41.91}{0.75} = 55.88 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 6:** Account for motor efficiency.

$$
P_{motor,\,input} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \dfrac{55.88}{0.92} = 60.74 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 7:** Apply a service factor (typically 1.10–1.15) for selection margin.

$$
P_{selected\,motor} = 1.15 \cdot 60.74 \approx 69.85 \;\text{kW}
$$

> **Standard selected motor rating** = **75 kW** (next standard size above 69.85 kW).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for a Heat Exchanger

> **Problem Statement:** A fresh-water cooler uses sea water to cool lube oil from $T_{oil,in} = 80\;°C$ to $T_{oil,out} = 55\;°C$. Sea water enters at $T_{sw,in} = 28\;°C$ and leaves at $T_{sw,out} = 42\;°C$. The overall heat-transfer coefficient is $U = 350$ W/m²·K. Calculate (a) the LMTD, and (b) the required heat-transfer area.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the temperature differences at both ends (counter-flow assumption).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{1} &= T_{oil,in} - T_{sw,out} = 80 - 42 = 38\;°C \\
\Delta T_{2} &= T_{oil,out} - T_{sw,in} = 55 - 28 = 27\;°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the LMTD formula.

$$
LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2}}{\ln(\Delta T_{1} / \Delta T_{2})}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$
LMTD = \dfrac{38 - 27}{\ln(38/27)} = \dfrac{11}{\ln(1.4074)}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the logarithm.

$$
\ln(1.4074) = 0.3410
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the final LMTD.

$$
LMTD = \dfrac{11}{0.3410} = 32.26\;°C \quad (\approx \text{equal to } \Delta T_{arith\,mean})
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the heat duty $Q$ from oil-side energy balance.

Assume oil mass flow $\dot{m}_{oil} = 10$ kg/s, specific heat $c_{p,\,oil} = 2000$ J/(kg·K).

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{oil,in} - T_{oil,out})
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 10 \cdot 2000 \cdot (80 - 55) = 500{,}000\;\text{W} = 500\;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute required heat-transfer area.

$$
A = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot LMTD} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{350 \cdot 32.26}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{500{,}000}{11291} = 44.28\;\text{m}^2
$$

> **Final result:** A heat exchanger of approximately **44.3 m²** of heat-transfer area is required.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boiler Steam-Generation Capacity

> **Problem Statement:** A composite boiler must supply $3000$ kg/h of saturated steam at $7$ bar absolute. Feed-water enters at $80\;°C$. Calculate the **thermal capacity** (heat input rate) of the boiler. Use steam tables:
> - $h_{steam\;@\;7\,bar} = 2763$ kJ/kg
> - $h_{fw\;@\;80°C} = 335$ kJ/kg

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert mass flow rate to SI.

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = 3000\;\text{kg/h} = \dfrac{3000}{3600} = 0.8333\;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the energy-balance formula.

$$
\dot{Q}_{boiler} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot (h_{steam} - h_{fw})
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
\dot{Q}_{boiler} = 0.8333 \cdot (2763 - 335) = 0.8333 \cdot 2428\;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$
\dot{Q}_{boiler} = 2023.4\;\text{kW} \approx 2.02\;\text{MW}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to tonnes-of-oil-equivalent per day for fuel-bunker estimate (assuming furnace oil calorific value $CV = 41{,}000$ kJ/kg, boiler efficiency $\eta_{b} = 0.85$).

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{boiler}}{\eta_{b} \cdot CV} = \dfrac{2023.4}{0.85 \cdot 41000} = 0.058\;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\text{Daily fuel consumption} = 0.058 \cdot 86400 = 5016\;\text{kg/day} \approx 5\;\text{tonnes/day}
$$

> **Exam Answer:** Boiler thermal capacity = **2.02 MW**, fuel-oil consumption ≈ **5 tonnes/day** of HFO.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Shipboard Pumps

| Property | Centrifugal | Reciprocating | Screw / Gear |
|----------|-------------|---------------|--------------|
| Flow regime | High, continuous | Low to medium, pulsating | Medium, continuous |
| Pressure capability | Low–Medium (up to 20 bar) | Very High (up to 700 bar) | Medium (up to 80 bar) |
| Viscosity suitability | Low–Medium | Any viscosity | High viscosity (HFO, LO) |
| Self-priming | No (needs foot valve) | Yes | Yes |
| Maintenance | Low | High (valves, pistons) | Medium |
| Typical shipboard use | Bilge, ballast, cooling | Fuel injection, hydraulics | Fuel-oil transfer, LO |
| Efficiency (peak) | 70–85 % | 85–92 % | 65–80 % |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DG Set vs Shaft Generator vs TG Set

| Property | Diesel Generator | Shaft Generator | Turbo-Generator |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Prime mover | Aux. diesel engine | Main engine (PTO) | Aux. steam turbine |
| Fuel | HFO/MDO | Main-engine fuel | Boiler steam |
| Operational regime | Port & manoeuvring | Sea transit only | Steam-ship only |
| Maintenance | Medium | Low (no aux.) | Medium |
| Cost | Medium | High | High |
| Typical rating | 500–2500 kW | 20–40 % MCR | 500–2500 kW |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ship's Auxiliary Plant Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the **interconnections** between every major auxiliary system aboard a modern motor ship. Solid arrows denote **energy / fluid flow**; dashed arrows denote **control / feedback signals**.

