
> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Main Switchboard** is the **electrical heart** of the ship — every motor-driven auxiliary draws power from it. The **Auxiliary Boiler** and **Exhaust Gas Boiler** form a **dual steam-generation plant** feeding both heating and the turbo-generator (if fitted). The **Emergency Diesel Generator** is a **lifeboat** for the electrical plant — it kicks in within **45 seconds** of main-board blackout.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Steering Gear Hydraulic Power Pack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MainPump[Main Hydraulic Pump Motor Driven]
    StandbyPump[Standby Pump Motor Driven]
    Tank[Hydraulic Oil Reservoir]
    Cylinder1[Rudder Cylinder Port]
    Cylinder2[Rudder Cylinder Starboard]
    Rudder[Rudder]

    MainPump --> Tank
    StandbyPump --> Tank
    Tank --> Cylinder1
    Tank --> Cylinder2
    Cylinder1 --> Rudder
    Cylinder2 --> Rudder

    classDef power fill:#ff9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef hyd fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mech fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class MainPump,StandbyPump power
    class Tank,Cylinder1,Cylinder2 hyd
    class Rudder mech
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Centrifugal Purifier Working Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Feed[Fuel Oil Feed from Settling Tank]
    Bowl[Rotating Bowl 7500 rpm]
    DiscStack[Disc Stack Inside Bowl]
    HeavyPhase[Heavy Phase Water Outlet]
    LightPhase[Light Phase Clean Oil Outlet]
    Sludge[Sludge Outlet Periodic Discharge]

    Feed --> Bowl
    Bowl --> DiscStack
    DiscStack --> HeavyPhase
    DiscStack --> LightPhase
    Bowl -->|every 4 hours| Sludge

    classDef inlet fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef core fill:#ff9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outlet fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class Feed inlet
    class Bowl,DiscStack core
    class HeavyPhase,LightPhase,Sludge outlet
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical principle:** The high rotational speed creates **g-forces of 5000–8000 g** at the bowl wall. This is what separates water (ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³) from oil (ρ ≈ 900 kg/m³) — a density difference of only ~10 % is magnified by 8000 g into a complete separation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "auxiliary machinery" as applied to a ship. List any four major auxiliaries with their function.**

**Model Answer:**

> Auxiliary machinery refers to all supporting equipment on board a ship, **other than the main propulsion engine**, which is necessary for the safe and efficient operation of the vessel. These systems perform critical functions such as electrical generation, fluid transport, heating, cooling, cargo handling, and navigation.

**Four major auxiliaries:**

1. **Diesel Generator (DG)** → Converts diesel fuel into electrical energy for the entire ship.
2. **Ballast Pump** → Pumps sea water in/out of ballast tanks to control trim, draft, and stability.
3. **Auxiliary Boiler** → Generates steam for fuel heating, domestic use, and turbo-generator.
4. **Steering Gear** → Controls the rudder for directional maneuvering.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a purifier and a clarifier used on board ships.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Purifier | Clarifier |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| Function | Separates two **liquids** (water from oil) | Separates **solid** from liquid (sludge from oil) |
| Operating principle | Uses density difference | Uses density difference + settling |
| Specific gravity of oil | Higher than purifier bowl (water-rich) | Lower than purifier bowl |
| Discharge | Continuous water outlet | Periodic sludge discharge |
| Application | HFO purification pre-engine | LO cleaning in service tanks |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Module carries an internal-choice question. Two alternatives are provided below.

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a centrifugal separator (purifier)** used for fuel-oil purification on board ships. List the criteria for selecting the correct purifier size. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship is fitted with **three diesel generator sets**, each rated **1250 kVA at 0.8 power factor**. Calculate: (i) the **active power output** of each generator, (ii) the **total installed capacity** in kW, and (iii) the **running load** if the ship's hotel load is 800 kW and the cargo-plant load is 1200 kW, with one DG on standby. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Construction:**
A centrifugal purifier consists of:

1. **Bowl** — A vertical hollow cylindrical body, rotating at 7000–9000 rpm, inside which separation takes place.
2. **Disc stack** — A pack of conical discs (60–80 discs), each ~0.5 mm thick, spaced 0.5–1 mm apart. They reduce the distance droplets must travel to coalesce.
3. **Top disc and gravity disc (discharge ring)** — Regulates the interface position inside the bowl.
4. **Drive assembly** — Gear-driven by a horizontal shaft with clutch and brake.
5. **Feed inlet** — Tangential entry that imparts initial rotational motion to the oil.
6. **Water discharge & oil discharge ports** — Located at different radial positions based on density.

**Working Principle:**
Dirty HFO enters the rotating bowl tangentially and is flung against the bowl wall. Under high centrifugal force ($F_{c} = m \cdot \omega^{2} \cdot r$), the **denser water droplets** move outward and exit through the **water outlet at the bowl periphery**, while the **lighter oil** moves inward and exits through the **oil outlet at the bowl centre**. The disc stack provides multiple thin parallel layers that greatly accelerate coalescence.

**Selection Criteria:**

- **Throughput** ($Q$): typically 1.2–1.5 × maximum oil-consumption rate of the main engine.
- **Density of oil** at the operating temperature.
- **Viscosity** (target: 12–25 cSt at purifier temperature, usually 95–98 °C).
- **Bowl size** determined by the **Sigma value** (equivalent clarification area).
- **Water content** in inlet oil (typically < 1 %).

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**(i)** Active power per generator:

$$
P = S \cdot \cos\phi = 1250 \cdot 0.8 = 1000\;\text{kW}
$$

**(ii)** Total installed capacity (3 × DG):

$$
P_{total} = 3 \cdot 1000 = 3000\;\text{kW}
$$

**(iii)** Running load (1 DG standby → 2 DG share the load):

$$
P_{load} = 800 + 1200 = 2000\;\text{kW}
$$

$$
P_{per\,running\,DG} = \dfrac{2000}{2} = 1000\;\text{kW per generator}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Each running DG is at **100 % load** — exactly at its rated capacity. This is acceptable but leaves **no margin for transient loads**. The ship operator should consider load-shedding or running 2 out of 3 DG sets with a **20–25 %** margin for safety.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification and working of shipboard pumps** based on the principle of operation. State any two applications of each category on board. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** cools lubricating oil from $90\;°C$ to $60\;°C$ using sea water entering at $25\;°C$ and leaving at $40\;°C$. The overall heat-transfer coefficient $U = 400\;\text{W/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K}$. The oil mass-flow rate is $\dot{m} = 8\;\text{kg/s}$, with $c_p = 2100\;\text{J/(kg\cdot K)}$. Calculate the **(i)** heat duty $\dot{Q}$, **(ii)** LMTD, and **(iii)** required heat-transfer area. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Classification of Shipboard Pumps (by operating principle):**

**1. Centrifugal Pumps:**
- *Principle:* Convert rotational kinetic energy into fluid pressure using an impeller.
- *Applications:* Ballast pump, bilge pump, sea-water cooling pump, fire pump.

**2. Reciprocating Pumps:**
- *Principle:* Positive displacement using piston-and-cylinder; fluid pushed by mechanical motion.
- *Applications:* High-pressure boiler feed pump, fuel-oil injection pump.

**3. Rotary Pumps (Screw / Gear / Vane):**
- *Principle:* Fluid trapped between rotating elements and casing, then displaced.
- *Applications:* Lube-oil pump, fuel-oil transfer pump, hydraulic steering pump.

**4. Jet / Ejector Pumps:**
- *Principle:* High-velocity fluid jet entrains a secondary fluid (Venturi effect).
- *Applications:* Bilge eductor, priming pump for centrifugal pumps.

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**(i) Heat duty $\dot{Q}$:**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 8 \cdot 2100 \cdot (90 - 60)
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 8 \cdot 2100 \cdot 30 = 504{,}000\;\text{W} = 504\;\text{kW}
$$

**(ii) LMTD** (using counter-flow assumption):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{oil,in} - T_{sw,out} = 90 - 40 = 50\;°C \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{oil,out} - T_{sw,in} = 60 - 25 = 35\;°C \\
LMTD &= \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \\
&= \dfrac{50 - 35}{\ln(50/35)} = \dfrac{15}{\ln(1.4286)} = \dfrac{15}{0.3567} \\
LMTD &\approx 42.06\;°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Required area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot LMTD} = \dfrac{504{,}000}{400 \cdot 42.06} = \dfrac{504{,}000}{16824} \approx 29.96\;\text{m}^2
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\dot{Q} = 504$ kW, LMTD $\approx 42.1\;°C$, **$A \approx 30.0$ m²**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (auxiliary-machinery questions):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Purifier and Clarifier** — Examiners often award **0 marks** if the student swaps definitions. Memorise: purifier = **two liquids**; clarifier = **solid from liquid**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** in pump-power problems — using $Q$ in m³/h instead of m³/s is a **classic 1-mark loss**.
> 3. **Ignoring η in the wrong direction** — some students divide instead of multiply by efficiency. Always sanity-check: real motor power > hydraulic power.
> 4. **Not mentioning regulatory bodies** (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS) — SOLAS and Classification Society rules are **mandatory** in steering-gear and fire-pump answers.
> 5. **Skipping a sketch** — KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if no labelled diagram is drawn for machinery-based answers (purifier, steering gear, boiler).
> 6. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$** in the LMTD formula — this gives a numerical answer **wrong by a factor of 2.303**. Always use **natural logarithm**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Auxiliary Machinery):**
>
> - **Auxiliary machinery = all non-propulsion equipment** essential for ship operation.
> - **Main auxiliaries:** DG sets, pumps, compressors, purifiers, boilers, HX, FW generator, HVAC, steering gear, deck machinery.
> - **DG set sizing:** $P = S \cos\phi$ — use power factor 0.8 standard.
> - **Pump power formula:** $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$ — always convert Q to m³/s.
> - **NPSH margin:** NPSH available must exceed NPSH required by at least 0.5 m.
> - **LMTD formula:** uses $\ln$, not $\log_{10}$; use counter-flow $\Delta T$ assignments.
> - **Purifier vs Clarifier:** liquid–liquid vs liquid–solid separation.
> - **Aux. boiler pressure:** typically **7–10 bar**; saturated steam.
> - **Steering gear:** **≤ 28 s** hard-over-to-hard-over; **2 independent power units**.
> - **Fire pump:** dedicated, sea-water-driven, with prime mover separate from main engine.
> - **Ballast pump:** typically centrifugal, large flow, moderate head.
> - **Standard ship voltage:** **440 V AC, 60 Hz**; emergency **24 V DC**.
> - **Class requirements:** All auxiliaries are classified as Class-1 (essential), Class-2, or Class-3.
> - **Boiler thermal capacity:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{steam}(h_{steam} - h_{fw})$.
> - **FW generator capacity:** RO or MSF, 10–20 t/day per 1000 kW engine.
> - **Heat-exchanger area:** $A = \dot{Q} / (U \cdot LMTD)$.
> - **Hotelling load** typically = **800–1500 kW** for a mid-size bulker.
> - **Emergency DG** must auto-start within **45 seconds** of blackout.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding - Weld Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Welding - Weld Defects # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Weld Defect** — *A discontinuity or imperfection in a weld that exceeds the acceptable limits specified by the relevant code or standard, thereby rendering the joint unfit for its intended service application.* In the context of **ship construction**, weld defects are classified under **ISO 6520** and **ISO 5817** (for arc-welded joints in steel) into six broad categories, each assigned a unique numerical reference. The **International Institute of Welding (IIW)** and classification societies such as **Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, and Bureau Veritas** prescribe strict acceptance criteria for welds in hull structures, pressure vessels, and offshore platforms. **Weld Imperfection vs. Weld Defect — A Critical Distinction:** - **Weld Imperfection**: Any discontinuity that does not necessarily violate code limits. - **Weld Defect**: An imperfection whose size, type, or location exceeds the permissible threshold — making the joint non-conforming. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Ship structural welds are governed by **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** UR W-series requirements. A defect in a Class-A butt weld in a ship's hull girder is treated far more strictly than a defect in a non-critical stiffener fillet weld. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Surgery of Metals" Imagine **welding is surgery on metal**. Two pieces of metal are like two patients that need to be fused together permanently. The welder is the surgeon; the electrode/wire is the suturing thread; the arc is the scalpel; and the molten pool is the wound being closed. - A **healthy weld** = a clean, scar-free healed surgery where tissue is fully bonded. - A **crack** = the wound reopened (catastrophic — can propagate under stress). - A **porosity** = tiny air bubbles trapped in the tissue (like pimples under the skin). - A **slag inclusion** = foreign material (dirt/suture remnant) left inside the wound. - A **lack of fusion** = the surgeon didn't actually sew the two tissues together — they just sit next to each other. - An **undercut** = the surgeon cut away too much healthy tissue at the edges. Just as a surgeon must follow sterile procedure, a welder must control **heat input, cleanliness, travel speed, and joint preparation** to prevent defects. ## 1.3 The Six ISO 6520 Reference Families | ISO Code | Defect Family | Severity in Shipbuilding | |:--------:|:--------------|:------------------------:| | **1xxx** | Cracks | **Critical — Reject always** | | **2xxx** | Cavities (porosity, blowholes) | Major | | **3xxx** | Solid inclusions (slag, tungsten, oxide) | Major | | **4xxx** | Lack of fusion & lack of penetration | **Critical** | | **5xxx** | Imperfect shape (undercut, overlap, misalignment) | Moderate | | **6xxx** | Miscellaneous (spatter, arc strikes, surface irregularities) | Minor | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Six Families** rule is a high-yield KTU 2024 question item. Memorize the ISO category codes — they appear in **Part A (3 marks)** almost every exam cycle. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Weld Defects — Master Logical Framework The ISO 6520 / ISO 5817 framework classifies weld defects along a **Cause → Mechanism → Manifestation → Consequence** chain. Understanding this chain is essential for KTU 2024 application-level questions. ### **Family 1: Cracks (ISO 1xxx)** Cracks are **planar discontinuities** with zero width tolerance — they are **always rejectable** in ship hull welds under IACS UR W11 / W13. | Sub-type | ISO Code | Formation Mechanism | |:---------|:--------:|:--------------------| | Longitudinal crack | 1001 | Stress along weld axis (Hydrogen-assisted) | | Transverse crack | 1002 | Stress across weld axis (Solidification shrinkage) | | Crater crack | 1003 | Arc termination without crater fill | | Branching / Star crack | 1004 | High restraint + Sulphur segregation | | Heat-affected zone (HAZ) crack | 1005 | Coarse grain + Hydrogen embrittlement | | Root crack | 1006 | High dilution at root face | | Toe crack | 1007 | Stress concentration at weld toe | | Lamellar tearing | 1008 | Through-thickness shrinkage in rolled plate | **Why cracks form** — *The "TRIP" mnemonic*: - **T**hermal contraction (the weld shrinks as it cools, ~11% volume change in steel). - **R**estraint (the surrounding rigid base metal resists shrinkage). - **I**mpurities (S, P, H, C segregate to grain boundaries). - **P**rocess parameters (wrong heat input, wrong preheat). > [!NOTE] > **Hydrogen-Induced Cold Cracking** is the most common HAZ cracking in C-Mn shipbuilding steels. It occurs below ~200 °C, often 24–72 hours after welding. The control measure is **Preheat + Interpass Temperature Control + Low-Hydrogen Electrodes (e.g., E7018 instead of E6010)**. ### **Family 2: Cavities (ISO 2xxx)** Cavities are **volumetric (3D) discontinuities** caused by gas entrapment in the solidifying weld pool. | Sub-type | ISO Code | Cause | |:---------|:--------:|:------| | Uniform porosity | 2011 | Distributed gas bubbles from contaminated base metal | | Cluster porosity | 2012 | Localized gas pocket — usually from surface rust/moisture | | Linear / aligned porosity | 2014 | Gas channel along solidification grain boundaries | | Wormhole porosity | 2015 | Elongated tunnel from gas streaming upward | | Surface pore / blowhole | 2017 | Gas escaped after solidification started | | Crater pipe | 2013 | Shrinkage void at arc termination | **KTU mnemonic — "PROBLEMS"** for porosity causes: - **P**oor cleaning (rust, oil, paint) - **R**apid solidification (travel speed too high) - **O**verheated electrode (moisture pickup) - **B**ase metal contamination - **L**ow shielding gas flow (MIG/TIG) - **E**xcessive manganese/sulphur in base metal - **M**oisture in electrode coating - **S**urface moisture on plates ### **Family 3: Solid Inclusions (ISO 3xxx)** Foreign solid material trapped in the weld. The dominant form is **slag** — a molten flux coating that solidifies and becomes entrapped if not floated out. | Sub-type | ISO Code | Description | |:---------|:--------:|:------------| | Slag inclusion — linear | 3011 | Continuous slag line between passes | | Slag inclusion — isolated | 3012 | Discrete entrapped slag pocket | | Tungsten inclusion | 3021 | Tungsten electrode tip broken off (TIG welding) | | Oxide inclusion | 3031 | Aluminium oxide stringers in aluminium welds | | Copper inclusion | 3041 | MIG nozzle dip into weld pool | **Prevention:** Inter-run cleaning with **grinder/wire brush**, correct weaving pattern, proper slag detachability electrode selection. ### **Family 4: Lack of Fusion & Lack of Penetration (ISO 4xxx)** These are the **most insidious defects** because they are **subsurface, often missed by visual inspection**, and create **planar stress risers** with zero fatigue life. | Sub-type | ISO Code | Definition | |:---------|:--------:|:-----------| | Lack of fusion between passes | 4011 | Adjacent weld beads did not metallurgically merge | | Lack of fusion at side wall | 4012 | Weld metal didn't fuse to groove face | | Lack of fusion at inter-run | 4013 | Cold-lap between layers | | Lack of root penetration | 4021 | Weld metal didn't reach root of joint | | Lack of penetration between passes | 4022 | Incomplete fusion in multi-pass groove weld | **The three root causes of lack of fusion:** 1. **Insufficient heat input** — arc too cold, travel too fast, current too low. 2. **Improper joint preparation** — groove angle too narrow, root face too thick, gap too tight. 3. **Wrong electrode angle** — arc force directed away from sidewall. ### **Family 5: Imperfect Shape / Profile (ISO 5xxx)** Geometric defects on the weld surface. These are **mostly cosmetic** but can become fatigue initiation sites. | Sub-type | ISO Code | Description | |:---------|:--------:|:------------| | Undercut | 5011–5015 | Groove melted into base metal at weld toe | | Excess weld metal (overfill) | 502 | Convex over-deposition | | Excessive convexity | 503 | Reinforcement too high | | Excessive concavity | 504 | Weld sags inward (underfill) | | Overlap | 506 | Weld metal flows over base metal without fusion | | Misalignment / Hi-lo | 507 | Plates offset in butt joint | | Root concavity | 508 | Excessive grinding or over-arc at root | > [!NOTE] > **Undercut** is the most common defect in **horizontal fillet welds in ship decks**. It reduces the throat thickness and creates a sharp notch — a fatigue crack initiator. Acceptance per AWS D1.1: undercut ≤ 0.5 mm for cyclically loaded welds. ### **Family 6: Miscellaneous (ISO 6xxx)** | Sub-type | ISO Code | Risk | |:---------|:--------:|:-----| | Spatter | 602 | Cosmetic / surface contamination | | Arc strike | 601 | Hardened HAZ spot — crack initiation | | Stray arc / arc start | 603 | Random surface arc damage | | Root shrinkage / sink | 510 | Top surface depression at root pass | ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — Defect-Cause-Prevention Triad | Defect | Primary Cause | Diagnostic Method | Prevention | |:-------|:--------------|:------------------|:-----------| | **Crack** | Hydrogen + restraint | MT / PT / UT | Preheat, low-H electrode | | **Porosity** | Gas entrapment | RT / Visual | Clean surface, dry electrode | | **Slag inclusion** | Poor interpass cleaning | RT / UT | Grind between passes | | **Lack of fusion** | Low heat input | UT (TOFD best) | Right current, right angle | | **Lack of penetration** | Wrong root gap | RT / UT | Root face ≤ 2 mm, gap ≥ 2 mm | | **Undercut** | High current / wrong angle | Visual / DP | Lower current, weave pattern | | **Overlap** | Travel speed too slow | Visual | Increase travel speed | | **Lamellar tearing** | Through-thickness stress | UT phased array | Butter layer, low-sulphur steel | ## 2.3 Inspection Methods (NDT) — Brief Map | Method | Best Detects | Symbol | |:-------|:-------------|:-------| | Visual Testing (VT) | Surface shape, undercut, spatter | VT | | Magnetic Particle (MT) | Surface/near-surface cracks in ferromagnetic | MT | | Dye Penetrant (PT) | Surface cracks (any metal) | PT | | Radiographic (RT) | Internal porosity, slag, lack of penetration | RT | | Ultrasonic (UT) | Internal cracks, lack of fusion, lamellar tearing | UT | | Phased Array UT / TOFD | All UT + accurate sizing | PAUT/TOFD | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Note**: Class-A butt welds in the **amidships hull** (high fatigue zone) of a ship are inspected using **100% RT or 100% UT** — not just visual. Fillet welds in secondary structures are typically **10–20% MT/PT sampling**. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix (Defect Analysis Procedure) ## 3.1 KTU-Mandated Defect Analysis Procedure For any weld defect, the KTU 2024 examiner expects the student to follow a structured 6-step analytical path. Below is the **canonical procedure** with each step fully expanded. ### **Step 1 — Identify the Defect** - Use the visual or NDT signature to assign the correct ISO 6520 reference number. - Example: A fine linear crack in a fillet weld toe running parallel to the weld axis = **ISO 1001 (Longitudinal crack)**. ### **Step 2 — Determine the Location** - Weld metal (fusion zone)? - HAZ (heat-affected zone)? - Parent metal (base plate)? - Surface or sub-surface? ### **Step 3 — Establish the Welding Process** - **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc)** — prone to slag, porosity, undercuts. - **GMAW/MIG** — prone to lack of fusion, porosity (gas issues). - **GTAW/TIG** — prone to tungsten inclusion, lack of fusion at sidewall. - **FCAW** — prone to slag inclusion, porosity. - **SAW (Submerged Arc)** — prone to slag inclusion, solidification cracking. ### **Step 4 — Root-Cause Analysis (5M + E Framework)** $$ \text{Root Cause} = f(\text{Man}, \text{Machine}, \text{Material}, \text{Method}, \text{Measurement}, \text{Environment}) $$ - **Man**: Welder skill, certification level (in shipbuilding, must be **Class A/B per IACS UR W17**). - **Machine**: Power source calibration, wire feed, gas flow. - **Material**: Base metal CE (carbon equivalent), electrode dryness, shielding gas purity. - **Method**: WPS (Welding Procedure Specification) compliance, heat input. - **Measurement**: Preheating, interpass temperature, voltage/amperage. - **Environment**: Wind (>2 m/s disrupts shielding), humidity, temperature. ### **Step 5 — Quantify via Heat Input Formula** The single most important process parameter governing weld defects is **heat input**: $$ HI = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} $$ where: - $HI$ = **Heat input** in **kJ/mm** - $V$ = Arc voltage in **Volts** - $I$ = Welding current in **Amperes** - $v$ = Travel speed in **mm/s** - $k$ = Thermal efficiency factor (dimensionless) > [!IMPORTANT] > Standard **k** values per process (must memorize for KTU 2024): > - **SMAW**: $k = 0.80$ > - **GMAW (MIG)**: $k = 0.80$ > - **GTAW (TIG)**: $k = 0.60$ > - **FCAW**: $k = 0.80$ > - **SAW**: $k = 1.00$ (highest efficiency) > - **Plasma / Laser**: $k = 0.60$ – $0.70$ **Defect interpretation via heat input:** $$ \begin{aligned} HI &< 0.5 \ \text{kJ/mm} &\Rightarrow& \ \text{Lack of fusion, lack of penetration} \\ 0.5 \le HI &\le 1.5 \ \text{kJ/mm} &\Rightarrow& \ \text{Optimal for C-Mn ship steel} \\ HI &> 2.0 \ \text{kJ/mm} &\Rightarrow& \ \text{Coarse HAZ grains, HAZ cracking, undercut} \\ HI &> 4.0 \ \text{kJ/mm} &\Rightarrow& \ \text{Lamellar tearing risk in thick plates} \\ \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 6 — Carbon Equivalent (CE) Check (IIW formula)** The **IIW Carbon Equivalent** determines preheat requirements for shipbuilding steel: $$ CE_{IIW} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15} $$ where all elemental values are in weight percent. **KTU Decision Table for Preheat:** $$ \begin{aligned} CE &\le 0.30 \ &\Rightarrow& \ \text{No preheat required (for } t \le 25 \text{ mm)} \\ 0.30 < CE &\le 0.40 \ &\Rightarrow& \ \text{Preheat } 75 \text{ °C} \\ 0.40 < CE &\le 0.50 \ &\Rightarrow& \ \text{Preheat } 100 \text{ °C} \\ CE &> 0.50 \ &\Rightarrow& \ \text{Preheat } 150 \text{ °C + Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap**: Students often calculate CE but forget to include the **combined thickness** of the joint. For multi-pass welds on plates ≥ 40 mm, the interpass temperature—not just preheat—must be controlled within a 100–230 °C window per IACS UR W11. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Step-by-Step Defect Analysis **Problem Statement (KTU 2019 pattern):** > A **12 mm thick AH36 shipbuilding steel plate** is joined by **single-V butt SMAW welding**. After inspection, **linear porosity** is found distributed throughout the weld length. Identify the defect, calculate the heat input, and recommend corrective action. **Given Data:** - Voltage: $V = 24$ V - Current: $I = 180$ A - Travel speed: $v = 4$ mm/s - Process: SMAW → $k = 0.80$ - Plate: AH36 (C-Mn, low alloy), $t = 12$ mm - Porosity pattern: Linear, distributed (ISO 2014) ### **Step 1 — Identify the Defect (ISO 6520)** Linear distributed porosity = **ISO 2014 — Linear porosity**. Mechanism: Gas channels aligned along the **solidification grain boundaries** of columnar dendrites growing from the fusion line upward. ### **Step 2 — Heat Input Calculation** Apply the heat input formula: $$ HI = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} $$ Substitute values: $$ HI = \frac{0.80 \times 24 \times 180}{4 \times 1000} $$ Evaluate the numerator: $$ 0.80 \times 24 = 19.2 $$ $$ 19.2 \times 180 = 3456 $$ Evaluate the denominator: $$ 4 \times 1000 = 4000 $$ Final result: $$ HI = \frac{3456}{4000} = 0.864 \ \text{kJ/mm} $$ $$ \boxed{HI = 0.864 \ \text{kJ/mm}} $$ ### **Step 3 — Heat Input Verdict** $$ 0.5 \le 0.864 \le 1.5 \ \text{kJ/mm} \Rightarrow \textbf{Heat input is within the optimal range.} $$ **Conclusion**: Heat input is NOT the cause of porosity. The defect is from a **material / cleanliness** source, not a thermal source. ### **Step 4 — Root-Cause Analysis** | Suspect Factor | Investigation | Verdict | |:---------------|:-------------|:--------:| | Heat input | Calculated 0.864 kJ/mm — Optimal | **Ruled out** | | Surface cleanliness | Likely rusted/mill scale on AH36 plate | **Probable** | | Electrode condition | Electrode left in open humid store | **Probable** | | Wind / draft | Outdoor shipyard — wind > 2 m/s? | **Probable** | | Base metal S content | AH36 max S = 0.035% (acceptable) | **Ruled out** | | Shielding gas (N/A) | SMAW uses flux coating, not gas | **N/A** | ### **Step 5 — Corrective Action Recommendations** 1. **Pre-weld cleaning**: Grind or shot-blast the groove faces to **Sa 2.5** (SIS 05 5900 / ISO 8501-1). 2. **Electrode baking**: Re-bake E7018 electrodes at **350 °C for 1 hour** before use. 3. **Use low-hydrogen, low-moisture electrodes** (e.g., AWS E7018 instead of E6013). 4. **Wind shield**: Erect a screen if wind speed exceeds **2 m/s**. 5. **Reduce travel speed slightly** to allow gas to escape (e.g., from 4 to 3.5 mm/s) — this raises heat input marginally to 0.987 kJ/mm (still optimal). ### **Step 6 — Acceptance / Repair Decision (IACS UR W11)** Per **IACS UR W11**, for Class-A butt welds in AH36 ≤ 20 mm thickness: - Individual pore ≤ 3 mm or 4% of plate thickness: **Acceptable**. - Distributed porosity: total projected area ≤ 2% of weld area in any 100 mm length. - Linear porosity > 6 pores in 100 mm: **Reject → Repair**. > [!NOTE] > If porosity exceeds the limit, the repair procedure per **ISO 5817** is: **gouge out the defective length using arc-air gouging**, grind clean, re-weld using a qualified WPS, then re-inspect using RT. --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — ISO 6520 Weld Defect Classification Tree ```mermaid graph TD A["WELD DEFECTS
ISO 6520"] --> B["1xxx
CRACKS"] A --> C["2xxx
CAVITIES"] A --> D["3xxx
SOLID INCLUSIONS"] A --> E["4xxx
LACK OF FUSION / PENETRATION"] A --> F["5xxx
IMPERFECT SHAPE"] A --> G["6xxx
MISCELLANEOUS"] B --> B1["1001 Longitudinal"] B --> B2["1002 Transverse"] B --> B3["1003 Crater"] B --> B4["1005 HAZ Crack"] B --> B5["1008 Lamellar Tearing"] C --> C1["2011 Uniform Porosity"] C --> C2["2012 Cluster Porosity"] C --> C3["2014 Linear Porosity"] C --> C4["2015 Wormhole"] C --> C5["2017 Blowhole"] D --> D1["3011 Slag Linear"] D --> D2["3012 Slag Isolated"] D --> D3["3021 Tungsten"] E --> E1["4011 Interpass"] E --> E2["4012 Sidewall"] E --> E3["4021 Root Penetration"] F --> F1["5011 Undercut"] F --> F2["506 Overlap"] F --> F3["508 Root Concavity"] F --> F4["507 Misalignment"] G --> G1["601 Arc Strike"] G --> G2["602 Spatter"] G --> G3["510 Root Shrinkage"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cause-Mechanism-Consequence Chain ```mermaid graph LR IN["INPUT FACTORS"] --> PR["WELDING PROCESS
SMAW GMAW GTAW SAW FCAW"] PR --> DE["DEFECT FORMATION
ISO 6520 Categories"] DE --> IF["INSPECTION
VT MT PT RT UT"] IF --> AC["ACCEPT / REPAIR / REJECT
ISO 5817 / IACS UR W11"] AC --> SH["SHIP HULL
FIT FOR SERVICE"] IN1["Material
CE Chemistry"] --> IN IN2["Heat Input
k V I v"] --> IN IN3["Welder Skill
IACS W17"] --> IN IN4["Joint Prep
Groove Angle Gap"] --> IN IN5["Environment
Wind Humidity"] --> IN ``` ## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Weld Cross-Section Showing Defect Locations ``` WELD METAL ┌─────────────────────┐ │ • Slag Inclusion │ ← ISO 3011 │ ○ Porosity │ ← ISO 2011 │ ╱ Crater Crack │ ← ISO 1003 ├─────────────────────┤ ← Fusion Line │ ░░░ HAZ ░░░ │ ← HAZ Crack (1005) │ // Lamellar Tear │ ← ISO 1008 └─────────────────────┘ PARENT METAL (AH36) Side view of butt weld showing typical defect locations: Toe crack ────────────╮ Undercut ───────╮ │ 5011 │ │ Overlap ────╮ │ │ │ │ │ Lack of fusion (sidewall) → 4012 ═══════════════════════════════════ Fusion boundary ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ Lack of root penetration → 4021 Root concavity → 508 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Defect Detection & Repair Action ```mermaid graph TD START["WELD COMPLETED"] --> VT["Visual Inspection 100%"] VT --> Q1{"Defect Found?"} Q1 -->|No| AC1["ACCEPT"] Q1 -->|Yes, Surface| N1["MT or PT Test"] Q1 -->|Yes, Suspect Internal| N2["RT or UT Test"] N1 --> Q2{"Code Limit
Exceeded?"} N2 --> Q2 Q2 -->|No| AC2["ACCEPT WITH
RECORD"] Q2 -->|Yes| REPAIR["GOUGE OUT
ARC AIR / GRIND"] REPAIR --> REWELD["RE-WELD PER
QUALIFIED WPS"] REWELD --> RETEST["RE-INSPECT
100% RT or UT"] RETEST --> Q3{"Pass?"} Q3 -->|Yes| AC3["ACCEPT"] Q3 -->|No| ESC["ESCALATE TO
CLASS SURVEYOR"] ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Differentiate between a weld *imperfection* and a weld *defect*. Why is this distinction important under classification society rules (e.g., IACS UR W11) for ship hull construction?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **weld imperfection** is any discontinuity present in a welded joint, irrespective of whether it violates the design code. A **weld defect** is an imperfection whose size, type, or location exceeds the permissible limit set by the relevant standard (e.g., ISO 5817, AWS D1.1, IACS UR W11). **(1 mark)** for the imperfection definition. **(1 mark)** for the defect definition. **(1 mark)** for the IACS relevance: in ship hulls, defects above acceptance limits compromise fatigue life, fracture toughness, and watertight/oiltight integrity — leading to rejection, repair, or classification society withholding the class certificate. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **List any six weld defects classified under ISO 6520 and write their numerical category codes.** **Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):** | # | Defect | ISO Code | |---|:-------|:---------| | 1 | Cracks (general family) | 1xxx | | 2 | Porosity / cavities | 2xxx | | 3 | Slag inclusions | 3xxx | | 4 | Lack of fusion | 4xxx | | 5 | Undercut | 5xxx | | 6 | Spatter / arc strike | 6xxx | (Any six correctly listed with codes = **3 marks**.) --- ## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- ### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of weld defects as per ISO 6520**, explaining the six families with at least two examples from each. Also explain how the **weld location** (butt weld in hull girder vs. fillet weld in secondary stiffener) influences the **acceptance criteria** under IACS UR W11. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **single-V butt joint in 16 mm EH36 shipbuilding plate** is welded by **SMAW** with the following parameters: - Voltage $V = 26$ V - Current $I = 200$ A - Travel speed $v = 5$ mm/s - SMAW efficiency $k = 0.80$ **Calculate:** - (i) The heat input $HI$ in kJ/mm. - (ii) The preheat temperature required if the carbon equivalent $CE_{IIW} = 0.42$. - (iii) If the heat input is doubled by reducing travel speed to 2.5 mm/s, predict **two likely defects** in the weld. **(7 marks)** --- #### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A (a)** **1. ISO 6520 Six Families (4 marks):** | Family | Description | Examples | |:-------|:------------|:---------| | **1xxx — Cracks** | Planar discontinuities; always rejectable | Longitudinal (1001), Transverse (1002), HAZ crack (1005), Lamellar tearing (1008) | | **2xxx — Cavities** | Volumetric gas voids | Uniform porosity (2011), Cluster porosity (2012), Linear porosity (2014), Blowhole (2017) | | **3xxx — Solid inclusions** | Entrapped foreign solids | Slag inclusion linear (3011), Tungsten inclusion (3021), Oxide inclusion (3031) | | **4xxx — Lack of fusion/penetration** | No metallurgical bond | Sidewall lack of fusion (4012), Lack of root penetration (4021) | | **5xxx — Imperfect shape** | Geometric surface defects | Undercut (5011), Overlap (506), Misalignment (507) | | **6xxx — Miscellaneous** | Surface damage | Arc strike (601), Spatter (602) | **2. Influence of Weld Location on Acceptance (2 marks):** **(1 mark)** Class-A butt welds in the **amidships hull girder** (high stress, fatigue-loaded) require 100% RT/UT inspection and tight limits (e.g., undercut ≤ 0.3 mm, no linear porosity > 3 pores per 100 mm). Any defect above limit = repair. **(1 mark)** Fillet welds in **secondary stiffeners or bulkheads** are less critical — acceptance is per lower quality level (ISO 5817 Level C or D), with sampling inspection (10–20% MT). Larger undercut, spatter, and surface porosity are tolerated. **3. Concluding statement (1 mark):** Classification society rules are risk-based: **the higher the consequence of failure, the tighter the acceptance limit**. --- #### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A (b)** **(i) Heat Input Calculation (3 marks):** $$ HI = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} $$ Substitute $k = 0.80$, $V = 26$ V, $I = 200$ A, $v = 5$ mm/s: $$ HI = \frac{0.80 \times 26 \times 200}{5 \times 1000} $$ Numerator: $$ 0.80 \times 26 = 20.8 $$ $$ 20.8 \times 200 = 4160 $$ Denominator: $$ 5 \times 1000 = 5000 $$ $$ HI = \frac{4160}{5000} = 0.832 \ \text{kJ/mm} $$ $$ \boxed{HI = 0.832 \ \text{kJ/mm}} $$ **(ii) Preheat for CE = 0.42 (2 marks):** From the KTU decision table: $$ 0.40 < CE = 0.42 \le 0.50 \Rightarrow \textbf{Preheat} = 100 \ \text{°C} $$ With interpass temperature controlled in the range **100–230 °C** for 16 mm EH36 per IACS UR W11. **(iii) Doubled Heat Input — Two Predicted Defects (2 marks):** New heat input with $v = 2.5$ mm/s: $$ HI_{new} = \frac{0.80 \times 26 \times 200}{2.5 \times 1000} = 1.664 \ \text{kJ/mm} $$ This is **2× the original** and now **above the 1.5 kJ/mm threshold** for shipbuilding C-Mn steels. **Two likely defects:** 1. **HAZ softening / coarse grain growth** — the HAZ experiences prolonged time above 1100 °C, leading to **coarse austenite grains** that transform to brittle ferrite-pearlite or M-A constituents. This produces **HAZ cracking (ISO 1005)** and reduced toughness. 2. **Undercut (ISO 5011)** — high heat input causes the arc force to melt the base metal at the weld toe faster than filler can fill the groove, creating a longitudinal groove. Also risk of **overlap (ISO 506)** at the cap. (Alternative accepted defect: **lamellar tearing (1008)** in thick plate, or **excess penetration / sagging** at the root.) --- ### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2022 Pattern]** **(a)** What are **weld cracks**? Classify the major types of cracks encountered in **ship hull welding** with reference to ISO 6520. Discuss the **mechanism of hydrogen-induced cold cracking** in the HAZ of C-Mn shipbuilding steel, and state the **three primary control measures**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the aid of a **cause–effect diagram**, explain the various factors leading to **porosity in arc welds**. For a **FCAW process** welding a **10 mm AH36 plate** at $V = 30$ V, $I = 250$ A, $v = 6$ mm/s, $k = 0.80$, calculate the heat input and determine whether it is **optimal, low, or excessive** for C-Mn shipbuilding steel. **(7 marks)** --- #### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B (a)** **1. Definition of Weld Cracks (1 mark):** A **weld crack** is a **planar discontinuity** produced by **localized fracture** of the weld metal or HAZ during solidification, cooling, or in-service loading. Cracks have **zero permissible width** in IACS UR W11 and are always rejectable. **2. Classification (3 marks):** | Crack Type | ISO Code | Location | |:-----------|:---------|:---------| | Longitudinal crack | 1001 | Along weld axis, in weld metal or HAZ | | Transverse crack | 1002 | Across weld axis, usually weld metal | | Crater crack | 1003 | At arc start/stop | | HAZ crack | 1005 | Coarse-grain HAZ | | Lamellar tearing | 1008 | Step-like in through-thickness of base plate | **3. Hydrogen-Induced Cold Cracking Mechanism (2 marks):** HIC cracks form **below 200 °C**, often **24–72 hours after welding** (delayed cracking). The mechanism is the **TRIP-D** model: - **T**rap: Diffusible hydrogen from electrode moisture / surface contaminants enters the weld pool and becomes trapped in the HAZ. - **R**estraint: Shrinkage stresses in the rigid joint act on the coarse HAZ. - **I**mpurity: Sensitive microstructure (M-A constituents, hard martensite). - **P**rocess: Insufficient preheat. - **D**iffusion time: Hydrogen migrates to stress concentrators → microvoids coalesce → crack. **4. Three Primary Control Measures (1 mark):** 1. **Preheat + interpass temperature control** (typically 100–150 °C for AH36/EH36). 2. **Use low-hydrogen electrodes** (e.g., AWS E7018, E8018-G) — rebake at 350 °C for 1 hr before use. 3. **Post-weld hydrogen bake-out** (250 °C for 1 hour) or **PWHT** for thick sections. --- #### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B (b)** **1. Cause–Effect Diagram for Porosity (3 marks):** ``` POROSITY │ ┌────────┬────────┼────────┬────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ Material Process Welder Equipment Environment │ │ │ │ │ ┌────┴───┐ ┌──┴──┐ │ ┌──┴──┐ ┌──┴──┐ │ │ │ │ Travel Gas │ Wind Rust S,P Heat Speed Flow Humidity Input ``` **Verbal explanation (3 marks — 1 mark each category):** - **Material** (1 mark): Rust, oil, paint, moisture on base metal or filler; high S/P content; damp electrode. - **Process & Parameters** (1 mark): Excessive travel speed → fast solidification traps gas; incorrect voltage/current → unstable arc. - **Equipment & Environment** (1 mark): Inadequate shielding gas flow (MIG/FCAW); leaks in gas hose; wind > 2 m/s disrupts shielding; high ambient humidity. **2. Heat Input Calculation (3 marks):** $$ HI = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} = \frac{0.80 \times 30 \times 250}{6 \times 1000} $$ Numerator: $$ 0.80 \times 30 = 24 $$ $$ 24 \times 250 = 6000 $$ Denominator: $$ 6 \times 1000 = 6000 $$ $$ HI = \frac{6000}{6000} = 1.000 \ \text{kJ/mm} $$ $$ \boxed{HI = 1.000 \ \text{kJ/mm}} $$ **3. Verdict (1 mark):** $$ 0.5 \le HI = 1.000 \le 1.5 \ \text{kJ/mm} \Rightarrow \textbf{OPTIMAL} $$ This is a healthy heat input for 10 mm AH36 — no risk of HAZ coarsening, no risk of lack of fusion. Porosity (if present) is therefore due to **cleanliness / gas-flow** issues, **not** heat input. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Weld Defect Questions:** > > 1. **ISO Code Confusions**: Students write "502" instead of "5011" for undercut. The **3-digit suffix** is part of the code — examiners deduct ½ mark. > 2. **Forgetting thermal efficiency (k)**: Many answers compute $HI = V \cdot I / v$ without $k$. Always include the process-specific **k** value. > 3. **Mixing up undercut and overlap**: Undercut = groove melted **into** base metal at toe. Overlap = filler metal flows **over** base metal without fusion. > 4. **Wrong NDT pairing**: Cracks → **MT/PT** (surface) or **UT** (sub-surface). Porosity → **RT** is best. Lack of fusion → **UT (TOFD)** is best. > 5. **Confusing CE formula**: The **IIW** formula and **PCM (Kojima)** formula are different. For shipbuilding C-Mn steel, use **IIW**. > 6. **Forgetting the "kJ/mm" unit**: Heat input **must** be in kJ/mm, not J/mm or kJ/m. > 7. **Lamellar tearing misnamed**: It is a **base-metal** defect (in rolled plate through-thickness), NOT a weld-metal defect. Often confused with HAZ cracking. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Notes — Weld Defects** ### A. The "Big Six" ISO 6520 Families - **1xxx → Cracks** (always reject) - **2xxx → Cavities** (porosity, blowholes) - **3xxx → Solid inclusions** (slag, tungsten) - **4xxx → Lack of fusion / penetration** (sub-surface, dangerous) - **5xxx → Imperfect shape** (undercut, overlap, misalignment) - **6xxx → Miscellaneous** (spatter, arc strikes) ### B. Critical Formulas to Memorize - **Heat Input:** $HI = (k \cdot V \cdot I) / (v \times 1000)$ in **kJ/mm** - **IIW Carbon Equivalent:** $CE = C + Mn/6 + (Cr+Mo+V)/5 + (Ni+Cu)/15$ ### C. Thermal Efficiency (k) Values - SMAW = **0.80**, GMAW = **0.80**, GTAW = **0.60**, FCAW = **0.80**, SAW = **1.00** ### D. Heat Input vs. Defect Relationship - $HI < 0.5$ → Lack of fusion / lack of penetration - $HI = 0.5$–$1.5$ → **Optimal** for C-Mn ship steel - $HI > 2.0$ → HAZ coarsening, undercut, lamellar tearing risk ### E. CE → Preheat Mapping - $CE \le 0.30$ → No preheat - $0.30 < CE \le 0.40$ → 75 °C - $0.40 < CE \le 0.50$ → 100 °C - $CE > 0.50$ → 150 °C + PWHT ### F. Top 5 Defects in Ship Hull Welds (By Frequency) 1. **Porosity** (cleanliness issue) 2. **Slag inclusion** (interpass cleaning) 3. **Undercut** (parameter control) 4. **Lack of fusion** (technique) 5. **HAZ cracking** (hydrogen, restraint) ### G. NDT Method → Defect Pairing - **Cracks** → MT / PT / UT - **Porosity** → RT (best), UT (acceptable) - **Slag** → RT / UT - **Lack of fusion** → UT (TOFD) — RT may miss sidewall LOF - **Lamellar tearing** → UT phased array - **Undercut / overlap** → Visual only ### H. IACS / Classification Society References - **IACS UR W11** — Hull girder welding - **IACS UR W13** — Welding procedure qualification - **IACS UR W17** — Welder qualification (Class A/B/C) - **AWS D1.1** — Structural welding code - **ISO 6520** — Defect classification - **ISO 5817** — Quality levels (B, C, D) ### I. Mnemonics to Lock In - **TRIP** → Why cracks form: **T**hermal, **R**estraint, **I**mpurity, **P**rocess - **PROBLEMS** → Why porosity: **P**oor cleaning, **R**apid solidification, **O**verheated electrode, **B**ase contamination, **L**ow gas, **E**xcess S, **M**oisture, **S**urface moisture - **Big Six** → **C-C-I-S-I-M** → **C**racks, **C**avities, **I**nclusions, (lack of fusion/**S**?), **I**mperfect shape, **M**isc — *(Or memorize the 1xxx to 6xxx sequence directly)* ### J. Ship-Specific Critical Notes - **Butt welds in hull girder** = Class A = 100% RT/UT, tightest limits. - **Fillet welds in secondary structure** = Class C/D = VT + sampled MT. - **EH36/DH36** (higher tensile) → mandatory **preheat** even at low CE values. - **Arc strikes** outside the weld groove → must be **ground out** (harden-spot → crack). - **Lamellar tearing** prevention → **butter layer** run + **low-sulphur** (≤ 0.005% S) Z-direction steel (e.g., ASTM A770). > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Mantra**: For any KTU 2024 weld defect question, your answer should always flow in this order: > **(i) Name the defect with ISO code → (ii) Locate it (weld / HAZ / parent) → (iii) Identify the cause (Material / Method / Man / Environment) → (iv) Recommend prevention (WPS / Preheat / NDT) → (v) State acceptance criterion (IACS UR W11 / ISO 5817 level).** > Following this 5-step flow consistently earns full marks and leaves no room for examiner ambiguity. ---

topics.title: Distortions and Their Preventive Measures. Testing - Destructive/Non-Destructive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distortions and Their Preventive Measures. Testing - Destructive/Non-Destructive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Distortions in Ship Construction

### Formal Academic Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distortion** in shipbuilding is defined as the *unwanted and irreversible change in shape, dimension, or geometric configuration* of a structural component (plate, section, or assembly) caused primarily by **non-uniform thermal cycles**, **welding-induced residual stresses**, and **fabrication-induced plastic deformations**. In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus, distortions are categorized into **Plate Distortion**, **Line Distortion (Longitudinal)**, **Angular Distortion**, and **Compound Distortion** — each arising from a specific thermal-mechanical interaction during fabrication.

**Standard Metric (KTU Reference):** Allowable distortion tolerance per major classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) is generally limited to:
- **Longitudinal distortion:** ≤ 1/200 of the member length
- **Angular distortion (butt welds):** ≤ 5° (≈ 0.087 rad) for plates up to 10 mm thickness

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Think of distortion like **a paper sheet drying unevenly in the sun**. If one side of the paper receives more heat and dries faster, it curls up and warps. Similarly, when a steel plate is welded, the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** expands and contracts asymmetrically — the metal "curls" out of its original flat shape. The weld seam itself acts like the "sun" — it heats one region intensely while the rest of the plate remains cool, producing uneven shrinkage that bends, buckles, or twists the structure.

### Physical Constants & Standards
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion (α) for mild steel:** **12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** (≈ 6.5 × 10⁻⁶ /°F)
- **Yield strength reduction threshold:** Above **600°C**, structural steel loses approximately **50% of its room-temperature yield strength**
- **Volumetric shrinkage per weld pass:** Approximately **5–8%** of the weld metal volume

---

## 1.2 Testing — Destructive and Non-Destructive

### Formal Academic Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Testing** in ship construction refers to the systematic evaluation of materials, welds, and structural assemblies to verify conformance with design codes, ensure structural integrity, and detect sub-surface or surface flaws.
> - **Destructive Testing (DT):** Tests that render the specimen unusable — providing quantitative data on mechanical properties (e.g., tensile, impact, hardness).
> - **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** Tests that preserve the integrity of the component while detecting surface or sub-surface defects using physical principles (e.g., ultrasonic, radiographic, magnetic).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you buy a watermelon. **Destructive testing** is like cutting the watermelon open to check if the seeds are ripe — you gain full information, but the watermelon is gone. **Non-destructive testing** is like tapping the watermelon and listening to the sound, or shining a flashlight through its skin — you get useful information without destroying it. In ships, NDT is preferred for in-service vessels because we cannot "cut up" the hull, while DT is used on **sample coupons** (test plates welded alongside the real component).

### Key Classification Reference
> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Codes for Ship Testing (KTU-highlighted):**
> - **IACS** (International Association of Classification Societies) — UR Z17, Z23
> - **AWS D1.1** — Structural Welding Code (for testing acceptance)
> - **ASTM E1106**, **ASTM E164** — Ultrasonic and Liquid Penetrant standards
> - **ISO 9712** — Qualification of NDT personnel

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Welding Distortions

The four canonical types of distortions, as defined by the **KTU 2024 OENST724 syllabus**, are:

### 2.1.1 Transverse Shrinkage (Plate Distortion)
- **Cause:** Contraction of weld metal **perpendicular** to the weld line.
- **Why it occurs:** The molten weld pool solidifies and shrinks in the transverse direction.
- **Typical magnitude:** For a single V-groove butt weld in a 10 mm plate:
$$\Delta_{transverse} \approx 0.1 \times t \;\; \text{(mm)}$$
where **t** is plate thickness in mm.

### 2.1.2 Longitudinal Shrinkage (Line Distortion)
- **Cause:** Contraction of weld metal **parallel** to the weld line.
- **Effect:** Shortens the welded member, leading to bowing of long assemblies.
- **Formula (for a fillet weld on a stiffener):**
$$\Delta_{longitudinal} = \frac{A_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{L}$$
where:
- $A_w$ = cross-sectional area of weld (mm²)
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion (12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature drop during cooling (°C)
- $L$ = length of member (mm)

### 2.1.3 Angular Distortion
- **Cause:** **Asymmetric heating** across the plate thickness in a single-V or single-bevel butt weld.
- **Mechanism:** More material is molten on the top face than bottom face → uneven shrinkage → the plate tilts.
- **KTU Empirics:** For a single-V butt weld in a plate of thickness *t*, with groove angle *θ*:
$$\alpha_{angular} \approx \frac{0.02 \cdot t \cdot (1 + \tan^2(\theta/2))}{B}$$
where **B** is plate width.

### 2.1.4 Buckling Distortion
- **Cause:** **Compressive residual stresses** in thin plates exceed the critical buckling load.
- **Condition:** Occurs when:
$$\sigma_{residual} \geq \sigma_{critical} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$
where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus (210 GPa for steel)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (0.3 for steel)
- $k$ = buckling coefficient (≈ 4.0 for simply supported plate)
- $t$ = thickness, $b$ = width

### 2.1.5 Compound (Complex) Distortion
- Combinations of the above four; typical in 3D assemblies like **bulkheads** and **brackets**.

---

## 2.2 Preventive Measures (Distortion Control)

### 2.2.1 Design-Phase Measures
| Measure | Mechanism | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Balanced weld geometry** | Symmetric groove & weld size | Reduces angular distortion |
| **Minimum weld size** | Lower heat input | Direct reduction in shrinkage |
| **Intermittent (staggered) welds** | Reduces continuous shrinkage | Used for long seams |

### 2.2.2 Process-Phase Measures
1. **Welding Sequence (Backstep / Block Sequence):**
   - Weld short segments in alternating directions to distribute heat.
2. **Pre-Setting (Peaking):**
   - Plate edges are intentionally set at a counter-angle before welding.
   - Preset angle (KTU empirical):
$$\theta_{preset} = \frac{\alpha_{angular}}{2}$$
3. **Restraint (Strongbacks, Jigs):**
   - Mechanical fixtures prevent free movement during cooling.
4. **Heat Sink / Cooling Backing:**
   - Use copper or aluminum backing strips to extract heat.
5. **Lower Heat Input Welding:**
   - Heat input (H) is calculated as:
$$H = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{(kJ/mm)}$$
   - Where $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/min).
   - **KTU threshold:** Heat input should be kept **below 2.5 kJ/mm** for mild steel plates to minimize HAZ softening.
6. **Symmetric Multi-Pass Welding:**
   - Alternate weld passes on opposite sides of the plate.
7. **Preheating (100–200°C):**
   - Reduces thermal gradient → reduces distortion & HAZ hardness.
8. **Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT):**
   - Stress relief at **595–705°C** for carbon-manganese steel.

---

## 2.3 Destructive Testing Methods

| Test | Property Measured | Standard | KTU Critical Detail |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Test** | Ultimate Tensile Strength, Yield Strength, % Elongation | ASTM A370, ISO 6892 | Required for **lot qualification** of base metal |
| **Charpy V-Notch Impact** | Notch toughness at sub-zero temps | ASTM E23, ISO 148 | Tested at **−40°C** for arctic-class vessels |
| **Vickers / Brinell Hardness** | Local hardness | ASTM E92, E10 | Max **350 HV** in weld HAZ (DNV rule) |
| **Bend Test (Root/Face/Side)** | Ductility, fusion quality | ASTM E190, ISO 5173 | Mandated for procedure qualification |
| **Macro / Micro Examination** | Fusion, porosity, HAZ size | ASTM E3, E1351 | Reveals grain coarsening |
| **Fatigue Test** | Endurance limit | ASTM E466 | Critical for cyclically loaded structures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Destructive tests are mandatory** during **Welding Procedure Qualification (WPQ)** and **Welder Performance Qualification (WPQ-W)** before production welding begins. They are *not* performed on the ship itself.

---

## 2.4 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Methods

### 2.4.1 Visual Testing (VT)
- **Method:** Direct observation; magnifier, borescope, dye-pen.
- **Detects:** Surface cracks, undercut, porosity, profile defects.
- **Standard:** **AWS D1.1 Clause 8**; **IACS UR Z17.1**

### 2.4.2 Radiographic Testing (RT)
- **Method:** X-rays or Gamma rays (Ir-192, Co-60) penetrate the weld; defects appear as dark spots on film.
- **Detects:** Internal porosity, slag, lack of fusion, cracks.
- **Standard:** **ASTM E94**, **ISO 17636**
- **KTU Note:** RT acceptance per **AWS D1.1** — porosity ≤ 2% projected area, no linear defects.

### 2.4.3 Ultrasonic Testing (UT)
- **Method:** High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) reflect off internal defects.
- **Detects:** Sub-surface laminations, lack of fusion, cracks.
- **Standard:** **ASTM E164**, **ISO 17640**
- **Probe types:** Normal beam (straight), Angle beam (45°/60°/70°) for welds.

### 2.4.4 Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)
- **Method:** Magnetize the part; iron particles cluster at flux-leakage points.
- **Detects:** Surface & slightly sub-surface cracks in **ferromagnetic** materials only.
- **Standard:** **ASTM E709**, **ISO 17638**

### 2.4.5 Dye Penetrant Testing (PT)
- **Method:** Penetrant → Cleaner → Developer → defect shows up as red/white indication.
- **Detects:** **Surface-breaking** cracks, porosity in non-magnetic alloys (e.g., austenitic SS, aluminum).
- **Standard:** **ASTM E165**, **ISO 3452-1**

### 2.4.6 Eddy Current Testing (ET)
- **Method:** Induced eddy currents; defects disturb the current flow.
- **Detects:** Surface & near-surface cracks, conductivity variations.
- **Standard:** **ASTM E309**, **ISO 17643**

### 2.4.7 Acoustic Emission Testing (AET)
- **Method:** Listens to "cracking sounds" under load.
- **Detects:** Active crack propagation in pressure vessels.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Phenomenon | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transverse shrinkage | $\Delta_t \approx 0.1 t$ | mm | Empirical, butt weld |
| Longitudinal shrinkage | $\Delta_L = \frac{A_w \alpha \Delta T}{L}$ | mm | Fillet welds |
| Heat input | $H = \frac{60 V I}{1000 v}$ | kJ/mm | GTAW/GMAW |
| Angular distortion | $\alpha_a = \frac{0.02 t (1 + \tan^2(\theta/2))}{B}$ | rad | Single-V |
| Plate buckling stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | MPa | Thin plate |
| UT wavelength | $\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$ | mm | For 5 MHz in steel, λ ≈ 1.2 mm |
| UT sound speed in steel | $v_{steel} = 5900$ | m/s | Reference constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** In numerical problems, always convert the **heat input formula** to consistent units. A common error is mixing mm/min with mm/s. Use:
> $$H \text{ (kJ/mm)} = \frac{0.06 \cdot V \cdot I}{v \text{ (mm/min)}}$$

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transverse Shrinkage from First Principles

### Problem Setup
> **Given:** A butt weld in two 12 mm mild steel plates (Grade A), 1000 mm long. Single-V groove, 60° included angle. Determine the expected transverse shrinkage using the KTU empirical formula and validate via thermal expansion logic.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the empirical formula:**
$$\Delta_{transverse} = 0.1 \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the plate thickness:**
$$t = 12 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta_{transverse} = 0.1 \times 12 = 1.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Validate using thermal expansion principle:**

The molten weld pool solidifies and contracts. Treating the weld as a "thermal strain release":
$$\epsilon_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

Using $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ for mild steel and $\Delta T \approx 1500°C$ (from liquidus to room temperature):
$$\epsilon_{thermal} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 1500 = 0.018 \;\; (\text{or } 1.8\%)$$

The effective contraction perpendicular to the weld line on a 12 mm plate:
$$\Delta_{theoretical} = 0.018 \times 12 \times \cos(30°) = 0.187 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Reconcile:**
- The empirical value (**1.2 mm**) is larger because it accounts for the **entire plastically deformed zone** (HAZ + weld + constraint effect), not just the linear thermal strain.
- The theoretical value (**0.187 mm**) is the **unconstrained** strain.
- The ratio 1.2 / 0.187 ≈ **6.4×** corresponds to the **plastic constraint factor** typical of restrained butt welds.

**Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion:**
The expected transverse shrinkage is approximately **1.2 mm** and the **allowable tolerance** per IACS UR Z17 is ± 2 mm, so the joint is acceptable without correction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Input Calculation

### Problem
> **Given:** SAW (Submerged Arc Welding) process on 20 mm plate. Voltage = 32 V, Current = 600 A, Travel speed = 400 mm/min. Determine heat input and check against KTU threshold of 2.5 kJ/mm.

**Step 1 — Recall the heat input equation:**
$$H = \frac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**
$$H = \frac{60 \times 32 \times 600}{1000 \times 400}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**
$$60 \times 32 = 1920$$
$$1920 \times 600 = 1{,}152{,}000 \;\; (\text{J/mm equivalent numerator})$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**
$$1000 \times 400 = 400{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Final ratio:**
$$H = \frac{1{,}152{,}000}{400{,}000} = 2.88 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 6 — KTU Comparison:**
$$H_{actual} = 2.88 \text{ kJ/mm} > H_{KTU\,threshold} = 2.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
The heat input **exceeds** the KTU-recommended limit by 0.38 kJ/mm (≈ 15%). To stay within limits, the welder must either:
- **Reduce current** to $I = \frac{2.5 \times 400000}{60 \times 32} = 520.8$ A
- **Increase travel speed** to $v = \frac{60 \times 32 \times 600}{1000 \times 2.5} = 460.8$ mm/min

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Buckling Threshold for a Thin Plate

### Problem
> **Given:** A 6 mm steel plate, 500 mm wide, simply supported. Young's modulus E = 210 GPa, Poisson's ratio ν = 0.3. Determine the critical buckling stress.

**Step 1 — Recall Euler-type plate buckling formula:**
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Select buckling coefficient for simply supported plate (k = 4.0):**
$$k = 4.0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute t = 6 mm, b = 500 mm, E = 210 × 10³ MPa, ν = 0.3:**
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \times \pi^2 \times 210{,}000}{12(1 - 0.3^2)} \times \left(\frac{6}{500}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Calculate denominator factor:**
$$1 - 0.3^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$$
$$12 \times 0.91 = 10.92$$

**Step 5 — Calculate plate slenderness ratio squared:**
$$\left(\frac{6}{500}\right)^2 = \left(0.012\right)^2 = 0.000144$$

**Step 6 — Combine:**
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 210{,}000}{10.92} \times 0.000144$$

**Step 7 — Numerator:**
$$4.0 \times 9.8696 = 39.478$$
$$39.478 \times 210{,}000 = 8{,}290{,}380$$

**Step 8 — Divide and multiply by 0.000144:**
$$\frac{8{,}290{,}380}{10.92} = 759{,}188$$
$$759{,}188 \times 0.000144 = 109.32 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 9 — Conclusion:**
$$\sigma_{critical} \approx 109.3 \text{ MPa}$$

This is the threshold above which a 6 mm × 500 mm plate will buckle. If welding residual stresses exceed this value, **buckling distortion** will occur.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Distortion vs. NDT Compatibility

| Distortion Type | Cause | Primary NDT Used | Destructive Test Cross-Verification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transverse** | Weld metal contraction | RT, UT | Macro-section |
| **Longitudinal** | Longitudinal shrinkage | VT, dimensional check | Tensile test |
| **Angular** | Asymmetric heat input | VT, MT, PT | Macro examination |
| **Buckling** | Compressive residual stress | VT, AET | Compression coupon |
| **Cyclic/Fatigue** | Repeated loading | UT-TOFD, AET | Fatigue test (ASTM E466) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define welding distortion. List any four types of distortions commonly encountered in ship construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Welding distortion is the unwanted deformation (change in shape, size, or position) of a welded structure or its components caused by non-uniform thermal expansion and contraction during the welding cycle, leading to residual stresses and dimensional inaccuracy.

**Four Types (1.5 marks):**
1. **Transverse shrinkage** — contraction perpendicular to the weld.
2. **Longitudinal shrinkage** — contraction parallel to the weld.
3. **Angular distortion** — tilting of plates due to asymmetric heat input.
4. **Buckling distortion** — wave-like deformation of thin plates due to compressive residual stress.

*(Allow also: compound distortion as the 4th)*

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Differentiate between destructive and non-destructive testing. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Destructive Testing (DT) | Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen | Destroyed after test | Preserved |
| Information | Quantitative (strength, ductility) | Qualitative (defect presence) |
| Application | Lab, qualification | Production, in-service |
| Cost | High (sample destruction) | Lower (no material loss) |

**Destructive examples:** Tensile test, Charpy impact test.
**Non-destructive examples:** Ultrasonic testing (UT), Radiographic testing (RT).

*(Accept any valid DT and NDT methods with correct logic.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Classify the various types of welding distortions with neat sketches. Explain the causes for each type and suggest preventive measures. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the principle, procedure, and applications of **Radiographic Testing (RT)** in ship construction. Compare RT with **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution A(a) — Distortion Classification (7 marks)

**1. Plate (Transverse) Distortion — 2 marks**

**Cause:** When two plates are joined by a butt weld, the molten weld pool solidifies and contracts in the direction perpendicular to the weld line. Since the surrounding base metal resists this contraction, the plates bend inward.

**Prevention:**
- Use **backstep welding sequence** (weld short segments in alternating directions).
- Use **lower heat input** welding.
- Apply **preset (peaking)** — set the plates at a counter-angle before welding.
- Use **strongbacks** (clamping fixtures) to restrain the assembly.

**2. Line (Longitudinal) Distortion — 1.5 marks**

**Cause:** Contraction of the weld metal along the length of the joint causes a longitudinal shortening of the member, which leads to bowing or warping.

**Prevention:**
- Symmetric placement of welds about the neutral axis.
- Use of intermittent welds.
- Pre-camber the member in the opposite direction.

**3. Angular Distortion — 2 marks**

**Cause:** In single-V or single-bevel groove welds, more material is melted on the top face than the bottom. The resulting uneven shrinkage tilts the plate.

**Prevention:**
- Use **double-V** or **double-bevel** grooves for thicker plates.
- Weld alternately on both sides.
- Reduce groove angle and weld size.

**4. Buckling Distortion — 1.5 marks**

**Cause:** Compressive residual stresses in thin plates exceed the **critical buckling stress**, causing wave-like deformation.

**Prevention:**
- Reduce panel width-to-thickness ratio by adding **intermediate stiffeners**.
- Use **intermittent welds** instead of continuous welds.
- Use **lower heat input** to reduce residual stresses.

#### Solution A(b) — Radiographic & Ultrasonic Testing (7 marks)

**1. Radiographic Testing (RT) — 3.5 marks**

**Principle:** X-rays or gamma rays (Ir-192, Co-60) are passed through the welded joint. The radiation is absorbed differently by sound metal and by defects. A film placed on the opposite side records the variation in intensity, producing a radiographic image.

**Procedure:**
1. **Surface preparation** — clean the weld surface.
2. **Source placement** — place the radioactive source on one side.
3. **Film placement** — place the film cassette on the opposite side.
4. **Exposure** — controlled time depending on thickness and source.
5. **Film development** — standard darkroom process.
6. **Interpretation** — defects appear as dark regions (porosity, slag) or dark lines (cracks).

**Applications in shipbuilding:**
- Detection of **internal porosity, slag inclusions, lack of fusion, and root cracks** in butt welds of hull plates, bulkheads, and pressure vessels.
- Mandatory for **class-approved welds** of high-strength steel.

**2. Ultrasonic Testing (UT) — 2.5 marks**

**Principle:** High-frequency (1–25 MHz) sound waves are introduced into the metal via a **piezoelectric transducer**. The waves reflect off internal defects and the time of flight is measured. The location and size of the defect are determined from the echo pattern.

**Procedure:**
1. Apply **couplant** (gel or oil) to ensure sound transmission.
2. Move the probe along the weld.
3. Analyze echo signals on the **A-scan display**.

**Applications:** Detection of **laminations, sub-surface cracks, lack of fusion, and slag** in thick plate welds.

**3. Comparison Table — 1 mark:**

| Aspect | RT | UT |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Radiation absorption | Sound wave reflection |
| Best for | Volumetric defects | Planar defects (cracks) |
| Sensitivity to cracks | Low | High |
| Safety hazard | High (radiation) | None |
| Cost of consumables | High (film) | Low |
| Permanent record | Yes (film) | Optional |

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the various **preventive measures** for controlling welding distortion in ship hull fabrication, with emphasis on **welding sequence, pre-setting, and heat input control**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **principle, procedure, advantages, and limitations of Ultrasonic Testing (UT) and Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)**. Indicate which defects each method is best suited to detect. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution B(a) — Preventive Measures for Distortion (7 marks)

**1. Welding Sequence Control (2 marks):**

- **Backstep Sequence:** Each weld segment is deposited in a direction opposite to the overall progression. This distributes heat input evenly and prevents cumulative shrinkage at one end.
- **Block Sequence:** The joint is divided into blocks, and welding alternates between blocks, allowing each block to cool before adjacent welding resumes.
- **Symmetric Sequence:** For assemblies like I-sections, welds on opposite sides are made simultaneously or alternately to balance shrinkage.

**2. Pre-Setting / Peaking (2 marks):**

The plates or sections are deliberately **set at a counter-angle** before welding, so that after welding-induced shrinkage, the final geometry returns to the desired straight configuration. The preset angle is empirically determined:
$$\theta_{preset} \approx \frac{\alpha_{angular}}{2}$$
where $\alpha_{angular}$ is the expected angular distortion.

**3. Heat Input Control (2 marks):**

Heat input is controlled via the relationship:
$$H = \frac{60 V I}{1000 v} \;\; (\text{kJ/mm})$$

For ship-grade mild steel (Grade A), the KTU recommended limit is **2.5 kJ/mm**. Methods to control H:
- Reduce welding current (I).
- Increase travel speed (v).
- Use **narrow-gap welding** techniques.
- Use **low-heat-input processes** like GMAW-pulsed.

**4. Other Measures (1 mark):**
- **Preheating** (100–200°C) reduces thermal gradient.
- **Restraint fixtures** (strongbacks, jigs) prevent movement.
- **PWHT** (Post-Weld Heat Treatment) at 595–705°C relieves residual stresses.
- **Intermittent welds** reduce total shrinkage.

#### Solution B(b) — UT and MT Comparison (7 marks)

**1. Ultrasonic Testing (UT) — 3.5 marks**

**Principle:** High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) are propagated into the material through a piezoelectric transducer. The sound waves reflect back from any discontinuity (defect or back surface). The **time of flight** of the reflected wave determines the **depth** of the defect; the **amplitude** indicates its **size**.

**Procedure:**
1. Clean the test surface and apply **couplant** (grease, oil, or water).
2. Select the appropriate probe (straight beam, angle beam 45°/60°/70°).
3. Move the probe along the weld in a grid pattern.
4. Interpret the A-scan display for echo amplitude and position.
5. Mark the defect location for repair or record.

**Advantages:**
- Detects both surface and sub-surface defects.
- High sensitivity to planar defects (cracks, lack of fusion).
- Portable equipment, suitable for in-service inspection.
- No radiation hazard.
- Works on thick sections (UT can inspect > 200 mm thickness).

**Limitations:**
- Requires skilled and certified operators (ISO 9712 Level 2 minimum).
- Surface must be smooth and accessible.
- Difficult for complex geometries or rough surfaces.
- Couplant required; not suitable for hot surfaces (> 60°C).

**Defects Best Detected:** **Sub-surface cracks, laminations, lack of fusion, slag inclusions** — particularly planar defects.

**2. Magnetic Particle Testing (MT) — 3.5 marks**

**Principle:** The part is magnetized, and fine **ferromagnetic particles** (iron filings, dry powder, or wet suspension) are applied to the surface. At defect locations, the **magnetic flux lines leak out** of the material, creating local magnetic poles that attract the particles, forming a **visible indication** of the defect.

**Procedure:**
1. Clean the surface thoroughly.
2. Magnetize the part using a **permanent magnet, electromagnet, or yokes**.
3. Apply magnetic particles (dry or wet).
4. Inspect under suitable lighting (UV light for fluorescent particles).
5. Demagnetize the part after testing.

**Advantages:**
- Highly sensitive to **surface and near-surface cracks** in ferromagnetic materials.
- Simple, fast, and inexpensive.
- Visual indication; suitable for field use.
- Works on irregular shapes.

**Limitations:**
- Only applicable to **ferromagnetic materials** (carbon steel, low-alloy steel). Cannot be used on austenitic SS, aluminum, or copper.
- Only detects **surface and slightly sub-surface** defects (depth ≤ 6 mm).
- Requires **demagnetization** afterward (otherwise affects downstream machining).
- Requires a power source for magnetization.

**Defects Best Detected:** **Surface cracks, welding undercut, lack of fusion, fatigue cracks** in **ferromagnetic materials**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DT and NDT examples** — Students often list "Ultrasonic" under destructive testing. *Loss: 0.5–1 mark per error.*
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units in heat input formula** — Mixing mm/min with m/s. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Stating "MT works on aluminum"** — Incorrect; MT is **ferromagnetic only**. *Loss: 0.5–1 mark.*
> 4. **Omitting sketches/diagrams in Part B** — Even a hand-drawn block diagram fetches **1–1.5 marks** in KTU valuation. *Always draw a sketch!*
> 5. **Writing RT "uses sound waves"** — RT uses **X-rays or gamma rays**, NOT sound. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 6. **Not mentioning standards** — Citing "ASTM E1106" or "AWS D1.1" adds professional credibility and **0.5 bonus mark** in KTU valuation.
> 7. **Forgetting the KTU heat input threshold (2.5 kJ/mm)** — Most examiners test this specific value. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 8. **Mixing longitudinal vs. transverse shrinkage directions** — Be precise: *transverse = perpendicular to weld; longitudinal = parallel to weld.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Distortion Types — Rapid Revision
- **Transverse Shrinkage:** ⊥ to weld; ~0.1t mm; **Fix:** backstep, lower heat input
- **Longitudinal Shrinkage:** ∥ to weld; bowing; **Fix:** symmetric welds, strongbacks
- **Angular Distortion:** Plate tilting; **Fix:** double-V groove, pre-setting
- **Buckling Distortion:** Thin plate wave; **Fix:** stiffeners, intermittent welds
- **Compound Distortion:** Combination; **Fix:** FEM simulation, trial blocks

### 🔑 Distortion Prevention — Checklist
- ✅ Welding sequence (backstep / block / symmetric)
- ✅ Pre-setting (peaking)
- ✅ Heat input control (**H ≤ 2.5 kJ/mm** for mild steel)
- ✅ Preheating (100–200°C)
- ✅ Restraint fixtures (strongbacks, jigs)
- ✅ Heat sink backing strips
- ✅ PWHT (595–705°C for stress relief)

### 🔑 Heat Input Formula — Must Memorize
$$H = \frac{60 V I}{1000 v} \;\; (\text{kJ/mm})$$

### 🔑 Destructive Tests — Quick List
Tensile • Charpy Impact • Hardness (Vickers/Brinell) • Bend • Macro/Micro • Fatigue

### 🔑 NDT Tests — Quick Reference

| Method | Detects | Material Restriction |
|---|---|---|
| **VT** | Surface defects | All |
| **RT** | Internal volumetric (porosity, slag) | All |
| **UT** | Internal planar (cracks, lack of fusion) | All |
| **MT** | Surface & near-surface cracks | **Ferromagnetic only** |
| **PT** | Surface cracks in any material | All (clean surface) |
| **ET** | Surface & near-surface defects | Conductive materials |

### 🔑 Key Material Constants (Memorize)
- **α (steel) = 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**
- **E (steel) = 210 GPa**
- **ν (steel) = 0.3**
- **v (sound in steel) = 5900 m/s**
- **Heat input threshold (KTU) = 2.5 kJ/mm**
- **PWHT range (C-Mn steel) = 595–705°C**
- **Charpy test temperature (arctic) = −40°C**

### 🔑 Key Standards to Cite
- **AWS D1.1** — Structural Welding Code
- **ASTM E1106** — Radiographic testing
- **ASTM E164** — Ultrasonic testing
- **ASTM E709** — Magnetic particle testing
- **ASTM E165** — Liquid penetrant testing
- **ISO 9712** — NDT personnel qualification
- **IACS UR Z17, Z23** — Ship-specific testing requirements

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Launching - Mechanical Launching Methods (Slip
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Launching - Mechanical Launching Methods (Slipway Launching)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Mechanical Launching** is the process of transferring a newly constructed ship from the building site (building berth) to the water (floating condition) using gravity-driven, mechanical, or hydraulic systems, without relying primarily on flooding a dry dock. It encompasses a family of engineered methods — **Slipway Launching, End Launching, Side Launching, and Floating Dock Launching** — that are designed by **Naval Architects** based on the vessel's displacement, length, beam, and the yard's infrastructure.

Among these, **Slipway Launching** is the most prevalent mechanical method. A ship is constructed on an **inclined plane (slipway)** at an angle typically between **1/12 to 1/24** (i.e., a slope of **4.76° to 2.38°**). On completion, the vessel — supported on a **launching cradle** — is allowed to slide down the inclined ways into the water under the action of **gravity**, controlled by drag chains, triggers, and way lubricants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OENST724 Module 4):**
> The student is expected to understand the **mechanical launching systems**, classify the **types of slipways** (Longitudinal, Sideways, Patent, and Rail Slipways), and identify the **principal components** such as groundways, bilgeways, side shores, triggers, and cradle assemblies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Skating Slope

Imagine a **child sitting on a sled at the top of a snowy hill**. The hill is inclined, and the sled has greased runners to reduce friction. The child holds a rope (drag chain) tied to a fixed post (trigger) at the top. When the child releases a pin (trigger), the sled slides down.
