The child (ship) is now on a flat icy pond (water) — successfully launched!

In the same way:
- The **hill** = Inclined slipway
- The **sled** = Launching cradle
- The **snow/grease** = Lubricant (tallow, soft soap, or modern grease)
- The **rope** = Drag chains
- The **release pin** = Trigger mechanism
- The **icy pond** = The water/harbour

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **slope angle** of the slipway is a **critical design parameter**. Too steep a slope causes **excessive acceleration** and structural stress on the ship's hull girder (known as the **hump** problem). Too shallow a slope makes launching impossible due to **static friction**. Naval architects balance this carefully using the **Weight vs. Buoyancy curve** during the launch trajectory.

### 1.3 Classification of Mechanical Launching Methods

| Method | Direction of Motion | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **End Launching** (Longitudinal Slipway) | Bow-first or Stern-first along the ship's length | Small to medium vessels |
| **Side Launching** (Sideways) | Ship slides broadside into water | Ships too wide/long for end-launching |
| **Patent Slipway** | Haul-up and launch via mechanical hauling | Fishing vessels, small craft |
| **Floating Dock Launching** | Ship lowered by submerging pontoons | All vessel sizes, modern yards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Slipway gradients are expressed as ratios (e.g., **1 in 20**), meaning **1 unit vertical rise per 20 units horizontal run**. KTU examiners frequently test this notation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Procedure for Slipway Launching

**Step 1 — Site Preparation & Groundways Laying**
Two parallel rows of heavy timbers (or concrete beams) called **groundways** are laid on the inclined building berth. They extend from the building site down into the water. The slope is precisely engineered (typically **1:12 to 1:24**).

**Step 2 — Cradle Assembly Construction**
A **launching cradle** is built beneath the ship. The cradle consists of:
- **Bilgeways** (heavy timbers under the bilge/keel of the ship)
- **Sideways** (timbers supporting the ship's sides)
- **Shores and props** (holding the ship rigidly to the cradle)
- **Sliding ways** (placed between groundways and bilgeways)

**Step 3 — Lube Application**
The sliding surfaces between **groundways** and **sliding ways** are generously coated with **launching grease (tallow and soft soap)**. This reduces the **coefficient of friction (μ)** to as low as **0.02 to 0.05**.

**Step 4 — Trigger Setting & Drag Chain Rigging**
- **Triggers** (releasing dogs) are set at the top of the slipway to hold the ship in place.
- **Drag chains** are attached between the cradle and fixed bollards at the top, providing a controllable retarding force to prevent runaway acceleration.

**Step 5 — Launch Trigger Release**
A hydraulic or hammer-trigger releases the cradle. Gravity overcomes static friction, and the ship begins to slide.

**Step 6 — Trajectory Completion**
The ship slides into the water. As buoyancy increases, the ship rotates about a pivot (the **forefoot** or **after end**, depending on launching direction) and eventually floats free.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why groundways + sliding ways (two layers)?** It allows the sliding ways to remain attached to the ship and be removed later for reuse.
- **Why drag chains?** Without them, a heavy ship on a steep slope could accelerate to dangerous speeds, causing structural damage or capsizing.
- **Why grease?** Launching a 10,000-tonne ship requires enormous tractive force unless friction is minimized. Grease reduces the launching force by over **90%**.

### 2.3 Forces Acting During Launching

Consider a ship of weight **W** on a slipway of slope angle **θ** with friction coefficient **μ** between sliding ways and groundways.

The forces acting along the slipway direction are:

1. **Gravity component along slope:** $W \sin\theta$ (drives the ship downhill)
2. **Frictional force opposing motion:** $\mu W \cos\theta$ (resists motion)

**Net accelerating force:**
$$F_{net} = W(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> For launching to **initiate**, we must have $\sin\theta > \mu \cos\theta$, i.e., $\tan\theta > \mu$. This is called the **"Condition of Self-Launching."**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\tan\theta > \mu$ | Self-launching condition (no external push required) | Dimensionless |
| $F_{net} = W(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$ | Net accelerating force on cradle | Newtons (N) |
| $F_{drag} = n \cdot R$ | Total drag chain force (n chains, R = chain rating) | Newtons (N) |
| $V_{max}^2 = 2 g L (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$ | Velocity at end of slipway (length L), neglecting drag | m/s |
| $P_{hump} = W_{hump} \cdot g$ | Weight of ship at "hump" point (max buoyancy effect) | Newtons (N) |
| $f = \frac{0.02 \text{ to } 0.05}{1}$ | Typical friction coefficient (greased ways) | Dimensionless |
| Slope ratio = 1/12 to 1/24 | Typical slipway gradients | Ratio |

**Note:** In the above table, vertical bars have been replaced with $\vert$ and $\frac{}{}$ fractions to prevent markdown parsing conflicts.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyards Worldwide:** Slipway launching is used in over **70% of small-to-medium shipyards globally**, especially in India (Cochin Shipyard, GRSE Kolkata, HSL Visakhapatnam), Japan, Korea, and Europe.
- **Repair Yards:** Patent slipways are used to **haul up ships for hull maintenance, painting, and propeller replacement**.
- **Cost Efficiency:** A slipway costs approximately **30–40% of an equivalent graving dock**, making it the preferred choice for developing nations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Self-Launching

Consider a ship of total launching weight **W** resting on a slipway inclined at angle **θ** to the horizontal. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the greased sliding ways and the groundways is **μ**.

**Step 1 — Resolve the weight W into components:**

The component of weight acting **along the slipway (downhill)** is:
$$W_{\parallel} = W \sin\theta$$

The component of weight acting **perpendicular to the slipway (pressing into the ways)** is:
$$W_{\perp} = W \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the maximum static friction force:**

The normal reaction force on the slipway equals the perpendicular component of weight (assuming no other vertical loads). Therefore, the maximum friction force opposing motion is:
$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot W \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law along the slipway direction:**

For the ship to **just begin moving** (initiate launch), the gravitational component along the slope must overcome the maximum static friction:
$$W \sin\theta \geq \mu W \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the inequality:**

Dividing both sides by $W \cos\theta$ (which is positive for $0 < \theta < 90°$):
$$\tan\theta \geq \mu$$

> This is the **"Condition of Self-Launching"** — the slope angle must be steep enough such that its tangent exceeds the friction coefficient.

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check:**

For a typical greased slipway, $\mu = 0.03$. The minimum slope angle for self-launching is:
$$\theta_{min} = \tan^{-1}(0.03) \approx 1.72° \approx 1 \text{ in } 33$$

This confirms why slipways are typically built at slopes steeper than 1 in 33.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Velocity of the Ship at the End of the Slipway

Assume the ship starts from rest at the top of the slipway of length **L**, inclined at angle **θ**, with coefficient of kinetic friction **μ**.

**Step 1 — Set up the work-energy theorem:**

The net work done on the ship equals its change in kinetic energy:
$$W_{net} = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} m V_{max}^2 - 0$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the net work done:**

- Work done by gravity: $W_g = (W \sin\theta) \cdot L$
- Work done against friction: $W_f = (\mu W \cos\theta) \cdot L$

Therefore:
$$W_{net} = W L \sin\theta - \mu W L \cos\theta = W L (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Equate to kinetic energy:**

Since $W = mg$, we have $m = W/g$. Substituting:
$$W L (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{g} \cdot V_{max}^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{max}$:**

$$V_{max}^2 = 2 g L (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$$

$$\boxed{V_{max} = \sqrt{2 g L (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**

Let: $L = 100 \text{ m}$, $\theta = 5°$, $\mu = 0.03$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

- $\sin 5° = 0.0872$
- $\cos 5° = 0.9962$
- $\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta = 0.0872 - (0.03)(0.9962) = 0.0872 - 0.0299 = 0.0573$

Substituting:
$$V_{max} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 100 \times 0.0573}$$

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{112.42} \approx 10.60 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{max} \approx 10.60 \text{ m/s} \approx 38.16 \text{ km/h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, drag chains absorb significant kinetic energy, so the **actual exit velocity is 40–60% lower** than this theoretical value. Naval architects use this margin to design safe launching procedures.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Parameters of Slipways

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Engineering Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slipway slope | 1/n | 1/12 to 1/24 | Must satisfy $\tan\theta > \mu$ |
| Friction coefficient (greased) | μ | 0.02 to 0.05 | Lowered by tallow/soap application |
| Launching weight | W | 500 t to 50,000 t | Cradle + Ship weight |
| Cradle length | L_c | 0.6 × LOA to 0.8 × LOA | Must support hull during slide |
| Drag chain count | n | 4 to 12 chains | Each rated to absorb partial KE |
| Trigger release force | F_t | 5 to 50 kN | Hydraulic or mechanical |
| Hump point | P_hump | 60–80% of LOA from stern | Critical for fore-launched vessels |

---

### 3.4 Verification of Self-Launching Condition (Edge Case)

**Problem:** A ship of weight 8,000 tonnes is to be launched on a slipway of gradient 1 in 20. The coefficient of friction is 0.04. Will the ship launch by gravity alone?

**Step 1 — Convert the slope to angle:**

$$\tan\theta = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.05) = 2.86°$$

**Step 2 — Compare with friction coefficient:**

$$\tan\theta = 0.05 > \mu = 0.04$$

> **Conclusion:** Yes, the ship will self-launch because $\tan\theta > \mu$. The margin is small, however, so the shipyard may use hydraulic boosters or trigger release mechanisms to initiate motion reliably.

**Step 3 — Net accelerating force:**

$$F_{net} = W(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta) = 8000 \times 9.81 \times (\sin 2.86° - 0.04 \cos 2.86°)$$

$$F_{net} = 78{,}480 \times (0.0499 - 0.04 \times 0.9988)$$

$$F_{net} = 78{,}480 \times (0.0499 - 0.0400) = 78{,}480 \times 0.0099 \approx 777 \text{ kN}$$

This is the **net force** that accelerates the ship after launch initiation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Slipway Launching System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a slipway launching system, illustrating the interaction between the major components during the launch sequence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ship Hull - Built on Building Berth] --> nodeB[Launching Cradle Assembly]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Bilgeways and Sideways]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Shores and Props]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Sliding Ways]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Groundways Fixed on Slope]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Slipway Foundation Inclined 1 in 12 to 1 in 24]
    nodeH[Lubrication System Tallow and Soft Soap] --> nodeE
    nodeI[Trigger Mechanism Top of Slipway] --> nodeB
    nodeJ[Drag Chains Fixed Bollards] --> nodeB
    nodeK[Hydraulic Boosters Optional] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeL[Sliding Motion Under Gravity]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Buoyancy Buildup on Hull]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Floating Free of Cradle]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Tugboats Take Over for Berthing]

    style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeG fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeL fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeN fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeO fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Force Balance on a Cradle During Launching

This diagram illustrates the **force vectors** acting on a launching cradle as it begins to slide down the slipway. It is rendered as a labelled block architecture rather than a physical vector drawing to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF1[Gravity Force W acts vertically downward] --> nodeF2[Resolve into components]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[Component along slope W sin theta DOWNHILL DRIVING]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF4[Component perpendicular W cos theta PRESSES ON WAYS]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5[Frictional Force mu W cos theta acts UP THE SLOPE OPPOSES MOTION]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF6[Net Accelerating Force F net equals W sin theta minus mu W cos theta]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF6
    nodeF6 --> nodeF7{Is F net positive?}
    nodeF7 -- Yes --> nodeF8[Ship Accelerates Down the Slipway]
    nodeF7 -- No --> nodeF9[Static Equilibrium Ship Will Not Launch Without External Force]

    style nodeF1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF8 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF9 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Launching Sequence Timeline

The following block-flow depicts the **chronological sequence** of a typical slipway launch, from keel laying to floating free.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Stage 1 Keel Laying on Building Berth] --> step2[Stage 2 Hull Construction Complete]
    step2 --> step3[Stage 3 Cradle Assembly and Lube Application]
    step3 --> step4[Stage 4 Drag Chain Rigging and Trigger Setting]
    step4 --> step5[Stage 5 Final Inspection and Pre-Launch Checks]
    step5 --> step6[Stage 6 Trigger Release and Cradle Begins to Slide]
    step6 --> step7[Stage 7 Acceleration Phase Cradle Slides Down]
    step7 --> step8[Stage 8 Pivot at Forefoot Hump Region]
    step8 --> step9[Stage 9 Buoyancy Exceeds Weight Ship Floats]
    step9 --> step10[Stage 10 Cradle Submerges and Detaches]
    step10 --> step11[Stage 11 Tugboats Take Over Ship Maneuvered to Fitting Out Berth]

    style step1 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style step6 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style step9 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style step11 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "sketch" or "draw" a slipway launching arrangement, present a labelled side-elevation showing the **groundways, sliding ways, bilgeways, cradle, ship hull, and water level**. The Mermaid block diagrams above are not exam-substitutable — they are **study aids** to internalize the functional relationships.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define slipway launching. What are the main components of a launching cradle?**

**Model Answer:**

Slipway launching is a **mechanical method** of transferring a newly built ship from land into the water by allowing it to slide down an **inclined plane (slipway)** under the action of gravity, supported on a launching cradle.

The main components of a launching cradle are:
1. **Bilgeways** — heavy timber or steel beams positioned directly beneath the ship's bilge/keel to take the concentrated load.
2. **Sliding ways** — flat surfaces attached to the bilgeways that slide over the groundways.
3. **Groundways** — fixed inclined timbers/concrete beams laid on the slipway foundation.
4. **Side shores and props** — timber braces holding the ship rigidly to the cradle.
5. **Slipway wedges and triggers** — releasing devices to initiate the launch.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **groundways** (fixed to the slipway) with **sliding ways** (move with the ship). The **groundways stay on the slipway**; the **sliding ways remain attached to the cradle and are salvaged later**.

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the condition for self-launching of a ship on a slipway. Why is grease applied between the sliding ways and groundways?**

**Model Answer:**

The **condition for self-launching** is:
$$\tan\theta > \mu$$

where **θ** is the slope angle of the slipway and **μ** is the coefficient of friction between the sliding ways and the groundways. When this condition is satisfied, the gravitational component along the slipway exceeds the maximum frictional resistance, and the ship will begin to move without any external push.

**Grease is applied** between the sliding ways and groundways to:
1. **Reduce the coefficient of friction (μ)** from approximately 0.3 (dry timber) to 0.02–0.05 (greased).
2. **Decrease the launching force** required to overcome static friction.
3. **Control the velocity** of the ship during the descent, preventing runaway acceleration.
4. **Protect the sliding surfaces** from wear and damage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing only "to reduce friction" without specifying the **range of values** (e.g., from 0.3 to 0.03) loses credit. Always quantify.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Explain in detail the procedure of longitudinal slipway launching with neat sketches. Discuss the role of drag chains and triggers in the launching operation.**

**OR**

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**A ship of launching weight 12,000 tonnes is to be launched on a slipway of gradient 1 in 18. The coefficient of friction between the greased sliding ways and groundways is 0.035. Calculate: (a) The net accelerating force on the cradle after motion begins. (b) The velocity of the ship at the end of a 120 m long slipway. (c) Comment on whether the ship will self-launch.**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Procedure of Longitudinal Slipway Launching (7 Marks)**

Longitudinal slipway launching is the most common mechanical launching method, where the ship is built with its **length parallel to the slipway** and slides **bow-first (or stern-first)** down the inclined plane.

**Stage 1: Construction on the Building Berth**
- The ship is built on a **level or slightly inclined building berth**.
- The berth is adjacent to an **inclined slipway** that extends into the water.

**Stage 2: Cradle Assembly**
- A **launching cradle** is built beneath the hull.
- It consists of **bilgeways, sliding ways, side shores, and props**.
- The bilgeways are aligned with the groundways of the slipway.

**Stage 3: Grease Application**
- The interface between the **sliding ways and groundways** is coated with **tallow and soft soap** (traditional) or **modern launching grease**.
- This reduces friction coefficient to **0.02 to 0.05**.

**Stage 4: Trigger and Drag Chain Rigging**
- **Triggers** (mechanical dogs) at the top of the slipway hold the cradle in place.
- **Drag chains** of calibrated strength are attached between the cradle and **fixed bollards** at the top.

**Stage 5: Pre-Launch Checks**
- Hull is inspected, drag chains are tensioned, triggers are set, and signals are given.

**Stage 6: Launch**
- The trigger is released (by hydraulic ram or manual hammer).
- The ship accelerates down the slipway under gravity.
- Drag chains progressively **pay out and break**, absorbing kinetic energy.
- As the ship enters water, **buoyancy builds up**, and the ship pivots at the **forefoot** (hump region).
- The cradle separates, and the ship is taken over by **tugboats**.

**(b) Role of Drag Chains and Triggers (7 Marks)**

**Triggers** serve the following functions:
1. **Hold the ship stationary** on the slipway until the moment of launch.
2. **Provide a controlled release mechanism** — usually a hydraulic ram or a tripping dog.
3. **Enable precise timing** of the launch, synchronized with tide, weather, and tugboat availability.

**Drag chains** serve the following functions:
1. **Retarding force** — they resist the motion of the cradle, controlling acceleration.
2. **Energy absorption** — as the chains are dragged out and break successively, they absorb **kinetic energy** that would otherwise damage the hull.
3. **Velocity control** — they ensure the ship does not exceed safe launching speeds (typically **4 to 6 m/s** at the way end).
4. **Emergency braking** — additional chains can be rigged as backup.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Loss of Marks in Question A:**
> - Forgetting to mention the **hump phenomenon** (pivot at the forefoot) — this is a **2-mark item**.
> - Not specifying the **friction coefficient range** (0.02 to 0.05) — lose 1 mark.
> - Confusing **drag chains with mooring lines** — drag chains are sacrificial; mooring lines are permanent.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Given Data:**
- Launching weight: $W = 12{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 12{,}000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg}$
- Slope gradient: $1/18$, so $\tan\theta = 1/18$
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.035$
- Slipway length: $L = 120 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Find the slope angle:**
$$\tan\theta = \frac{1}{18} = 0.0556$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.0556) = 3.18°$$

Therefore:
- $\sin\theta = \sin 3.18° = 0.0555$
- $\cos\theta = \cos 3.18° = 0.9985$

**Step 2 — Part (a): Net Accelerating Force**

Using the formula:
$$F_{net} = W(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$$

$$F_{net} = 12{,}000 \times 10^3 \times 9.81 \times (0.0555 - 0.035 \times 0.9985)$$

$$F_{net} = 117{,}720{,}000 \times (0.0555 - 0.0349)$$

$$F_{net} = 117{,}720{,}000 \times 0.0206$$

$$\boxed{F_{net} \approx 2{,}425{,}032 \text{ N} \approx 2{,}425 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Part (b): Velocity at End of Slipway**

Using the formula:
$$V_{max} = \sqrt{2 g L (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)}$$

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 120 \times 0.0206}$$

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{48.49}$$

$$\boxed{V_{max} \approx 6.96 \text{ m/s} \approx 25 \text{ km/h}}$$

**Step 4 — Part (c): Will the ship self-launch?**

Check the self-launching condition:
$$\tan\theta = 0.0556 \text{ vs. } \mu = 0.035$$

Since $\tan\theta > \mu$, the ship **will self-launch** by gravity alone. The margin is comfortable (0.0556 − 0.035 = 0.0206), indicating reliable launch initiation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall in Question B:**
> - **Unit Conversion:** Forgetting to convert tonnes to kg loses 2 marks. Always show: $W = 12{,}000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg}$.
> - **Calculation of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$:** Students often use $\tan\theta = 1/18$ directly in formulas. This is **incorrect**. Always convert to angle first or use: $\sin\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1^2+18^2}}$ and $\cos\theta = \frac{18}{\sqrt{1^2+18^2}}$.
> - **Neglecting the comparison** in part (c) — always state explicitly whether the self-launching condition is satisfied.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- **Slipway Launching** is a **gravity-driven mechanical method** using an inclined plane of gradient **1/12 to 1/24**.
- **Five major cradle components:** Groundways (fixed), Sliding ways (move), Bilgeways (under bilge), Side shores (lateral support), and Props.
- **Self-launching condition:** $\tan\theta > \mu$ — a **must-know formula** for KTU exams.
- **Net launching force:** $F_{net} = W(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$ — derive step-by-step in numerical problems.
- **Friction coefficient** drops from **0.3 (dry timber)** to **0.02–0.05 (greased)** after lubrication.
- **Velocity formula:** $V_{max} = \sqrt{2 g L (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)}$ — used in Part B 14-mark questions.
- **Drag chains** provide **retarding force** and **kinetic energy absorption**; they are **sacrificial** (break during launch).
- **Triggers** are **holding and release devices** at the top of the slipway.
- **Hump point** is where the ship pivots during fore-launching; maximum hull girder stress occurs here.
- **Patent slipways** are used for **hauling up and launching small vessels** — distinguish from longitudinal slipways in Part A.
- **Sideways launching** is used when the ship is **too long/wide** for end launching — popular in barge and pontoon construction.
- Always quote **units** (m, m/s, kN, tonnes) explicitly — KTU examiners award 0.5 marks for correct units in numericals.
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless otherwise specified.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Launching: Mechanical Launching Methods (Slipway Launching)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of Slipways for End Launch and Side Launch. Launching Curves. Arresting Arrangements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction of Slipways for End Launch and Side Launch. Launching Curves. Arresting Arrangements

# Module 4 — Slipways, Launching Curves & Arresting Arrangements

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Slipway?

A **slipway** (also called a *building berth* combined with a *launching way*) is an inclined structural surface constructed on the shore, extending from a high ground level down into the water at a carefully designed gradient. It serves two distinct purposes during a ship's lifecycle:

1. **As a Building Berth:** It provides the rigid, level, and elevated platform on which the entire hull of a ship is fabricated, assembled, and outfitted.
2. **As a Launching Way:** Once construction is complete, the same inclined structure acts as the runway down which the ship slides into the water — the moment engineers call the **launch**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A slipway is a civil engineering structure comprising a foundation, inclined ground ways, sliding ways, bilge blocks, and side shores, designed to support the ship's weight during construction and to safely transfer the vessel from land to water during the launching operation, while keeping structural and stability stresses within permissible limits.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Launch Pad" Mental Model

Think of a slipway as a **giant inclined skateboard ramp**:
- The **cradle** is the skateboard that holds the ship.
- The **ground ways** are the rails of the ramp.
- The **ship** is the rider being sent down.
- The **water** at the bottom is the destination.
- The **drag chains / arresting gear** at the end are the brakes that stop the rider.

Without the brakes (arresting gear), the skateboard would keep going and crash — similarly, without arresting arrangements, a launched ship would run aground or capsize.

### 1.3 The Two Launching Philosophies

| Launch Type | Orientation of Ship to Water | Typical Use | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|
| **End Launch** (Longitudinal / Drop Launch) | Ship's longitudinal axis is parallel to the slipway centerline | Small to medium vessels (tugboats, fishing vessels, small cargo) | Lower |
| **Side Launch** (Transverse Launch) | Ship's longitudinal axis is perpendicular to the slipway centerline | Large vessels (warships, large cargo, tankers) | Higher |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice between end and side launch is governed by **site geometry**, **ship size**, **yard crane coverage**, and the **radius of curvature** of the river/harbour available for swing.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Slipway Gradient:** Typically **1 in 12 to 1 in 20** (≈ 4.8° to 2.9° from horizontal).
- **Slipway Length:** Often **150 m to 400 m** depending on vessel size.
- **Slipway Width:** **20 m to 50 m** for side launch; **15 m to 25 m** for end launch.
- **Cradle Weight:** Often **3% to 8%** of the ship's lightweight.
- **Launching Velocity at Water Entry:** Typically **4 m/s to 7 m/s** for side launch.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction Sequence of a Slipway

The construction follows a strict bottom-up civil engineering sequence:

#### Step 1 — Site Investigation & Soil Survey
- Boreholes drilled every **30 m to 50 m** along the centerline.
- Determine bearing capacity (target ≥ **150 kN/m²** for large vessels).
- Identify water table depth and tidal range.

#### Step 2 — Excavation & Dewatering
- Excavate down to firm strata (rock or stiff clay).
- Sheet piling on the seaward side to control tidal inflow.
- Continuous dewatering using well-point systems.

#### Step 3 — Foundation Construction
- **Pile Foundation:** For weak soils — driven precast concrete or steel piles (typically **400 mm to 600 mm** diameter) at **1.5 m to 2.5 m** spacing.
- **Raft Foundation:** For good rock strata — reinforced concrete raft of **1 m to 2 m** thickness.

#### Step 4 — Slipway Substructure
- Reinforced concrete pedestals supporting the ground ways.
- Pedestal spacing along the length: **2 m to 4 m**.
- Each pedestal must resist vertical load (ship weight) and horizontal thrust (launching force).

#### Step 5 — Ground Ways (Permanent Ways)
- Two parallel heavy steel or timber beams running the full length of the slipway.
- Cross-section: **300 mm × 150 mm** to **500 mm × 250 mm**.
- Top surface greased and soaped to reduce friction during launch.

#### Step 6 — Sliding Ways (Temporary)
- Bolted on top of ground ways during pre-launch preparations.
- Extend slightly beyond the ground ways into water (called **"forepop"** length).

#### Step 7 — Cradle Assembly
- Rigid timber/steel frame supporting the ship's bilge.
- Carries bilge blocks and side shores.

#### Step 8 — Bilge Blocks & Side Shores
- **Bilge blocks:** Vertical timber/steel props directly under the ship's bilge.
- **Side shores:** Inclined props bracing the ship's sides laterally.
- These must be carefully cut (using **plug-and-feather wedges**) just before launch so the ship transfers cleanly onto the cradle.

#### Step 9 — Triggering Mechanism
- A trigger / dog shore holds the cradle in place.
- On launch day, the trigger is released, allowing gravity-driven descent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **friction coefficient** between greased sliding ways and ground ways is critical. The design value is typically **μ = 0.03 to 0.05** (greased timber on greased timber). This low value is achieved by applying **tallow, soft soap, and grease** in alternating layers.

### 2.2 End Launch vs. Side Launch — Engineering Comparison

#### End Launch (Longitudinal Launch)

**Procedure:**
1. Ship is built with bow pointing toward the water.
2. Ship slides bow-first down the inclined slipway.
3. Bow enters water, then progressively the rest of the hull floats.
4. Tugs take over and move the ship to fitting-out berth.

**Advantages:**
- Simpler construction of slipway.
- Lower bending moments during launch.
- Easier alignment of machinery and outfitting.

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires long, straight shoreline.
- Limited by ship length (cannot exceed available slipway length).
- Not suitable for very large vessels.

**Critical Design Concern:** **Hogging Moment** when bow is supported but stern is still on land — ship tends to bend "smile" shape (bow and stern down, midship up).

#### Side Launch (Transverse Launch)

**Procedure:**
1. Ship is built with the side facing the water.
2. At launch, ship pivots about the **forepop** end.
3. Ship rotates as it slides; one bilge enters water first.
4. Ship rolls onto even keel in water.

**Advantages:**
- Suitable for very large ships (only ship beam needs to fit on slipway, not length).
- Can be used where shoreline is short.
- Yard cranes can service the full ship length from the side.

**Disadvantages:**
- Complex launching dynamics.
- High risk of tipping.
- Requires precise **launching curve** design.

**Critical Design Concern:** **Tipping Moment** as the ship pivots — must ensure the metacentre stays above the centre of gravity throughout.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{launch} = W \sin\theta - \mu W \cos\theta$ | Net launching force on the cradle | N (Newtons) |
| $v = \sqrt{2 g h (1 - \mu \cot\theta)}$ | Velocity at water entry | m/s |
| $\mu_{design} = 0.03 \text{ to } 0.05$ | Greased timber-on-timber friction coefficient | dimensionless |
| $P_{arrest} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 / d$ | Required arresting force (energy / distance) | N |
| $E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy at end of launch | J |
| $R_{tip} = \frac{W \cdot \overline{GG'}}{V_{disp}}$ | Righting moment (stability check during launch) | N·m |
| $\sigma_{bending} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress in hull girder during launch | Pa |
| $\theta_{slipway} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)$ | Slipway angle (1 in n gradient) | radians/degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula sheet above, the symbol $\vert$ has been intentionally replaced with the LaTeX `\vert` notation to preserve markdown table integrity. All other standard mathematical conventions apply.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The slipway design philosophy is reused in:
- **Aircraft carrier catapult systems** — similar trigger-and-release mechanisms.
- **Offshore rig load-outs** — launching curves guide the float-over operation.
- **Submarine launch tubes** — pressurized ejector design parallels arrested launch.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Launching Velocity at Water Entry

**Starting Principle:** Conservation of energy — potential energy lost equals kinetic energy gained, minus energy lost to friction.

#### Given Data (Typical KTU Numerical):
- Slipway gradient = 1 in 18
- Length of slipway, $L = 200$ m
- Friction coefficient, $\mu = 0.04$
- Mass of ship + cradle, $m = 5000$ tonnes $= 5 \times 10^{6}$ kg
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

#### Step 1 — Calculate the Slipway Angle

$$\tan\theta = \frac{1}{18} = 0.0556$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.0556) = 3.181^\circ$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate Height Drop

$$h = L \sin\theta$$

$$h = 200 \times \sin(3.181^\circ) = 200 \times 0.0555 = 11.10 \text{ m}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Work-Energy Theorem

The net force doing work is gravity component minus friction:

$$F_{net} = mg\sin\theta - \mu mg\cos\theta$$

Work done over distance $L$:

$$W_{net} = (mg\sin\theta - \mu mg\cos\theta) \cdot L$$

This equals the kinetic energy at water entry:

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = mgL(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for Velocity

$$v^2 = 2 g L (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$$

$$v^2 = 2 \times 9.81 \times 200 \times (\sin 3.181^\circ - 0.04 \cos 3.181^\circ)$$

$$v^2 = 3924 \times (0.0555 - 0.04 \times 0.9985)$$

$$v^2 = 3924 \times (0.0555 - 0.03994)$$

$$v^2 = 3924 \times 0.01556 = 61.06 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\boxed{v = 7.815 \text{ m/s}}$$

#### Step 5 — Verify with Shortcut Formula

Using $v = \sqrt{2gh(1 - \mu \cot\theta)}$:

$$\cot\theta = \frac{18}{1} = 18$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 11.10 \times (1 - 0.04 \times 18)}$$

$$v = \sqrt{217.78 \times (1 - 0.72)} = \sqrt{217.78 \times 0.28} = \sqrt{60.98}$$

$$\boxed{v = 7.809 \text{ m/s}}$$

Both methods agree (small rounding difference).

### 3.2 Derivation: Arresting Drag Chain Force

**Scenario:** A ship of mass **$m = 5000$ tonnes** enters water with velocity **$v = 7.8$ m/s**. We must stop it within **$d = 30$ m** using drag chains.

#### Step 1 — Kinetic Energy

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 10^{6} \times 7.8^2$$

$$E_k = 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{6} \times 60.84 = 1.521 \times 10^{8} \text{ J}$$

#### Step 2 — Required Average Stopping Force

$$F_{avg} = \frac{E_k}{d} = \frac{1.521 \times 10^{8}}{30}$$

$$\boxed{F_{avg} = 5.07 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 5070 \text{ kN}}$$

#### Step 3 — Number of Drag Chains

Each chain provides approximately **$F_{chain} = 500 \text{ kN}$** peak resistance.

$$N_{chains} = \frac{5070}{500} \approx 11 \text{ chains}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, drag chains act as **energy absorbers** (not constant-force brakes). They pay out gradually, so peak force is somewhat higher than average. A safety factor of **1.5 to 2.0** is applied.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stress States During Launch

| Phase | Support Condition | Critical Stress | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-launch | Full cradle + blocks | None (static) | Very Low |
| Initial slide | All blocks cut, ship on cradle | Shear at bilge | Low |
| **End Launch — Bow float** | Stern on cradle, bow waterborne | **Hogging** (midship tension on deck) | **HIGH** |
| **End Launch — Stern float** | Bow on cradle, stern waterborne | **Sagging** (midship tension on bottom) | **HIGH** |
| **Side Launch — Pivot start** | Ship pivoting on forepop | Tipping moment | **VERY HIGH** |
| **Side Launch — Roll** | One bilge in water | Transverse stability | High |
| Post-launch | Fully waterborne | None (hydrostatic) | None |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Slipway Component Layout (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Ground Surface - Yard Level] --> nodeB[Sheet Pile Wall]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Reinforced Concrete Foundation Raft]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Ground Ways - Permanent Steel Beams]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Sliding Ways - Temporary Greased Beams]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Cradle - Rigid Timber Steel Frame]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Bilge Blocks Supporting Ship Hull]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Side Shores Bracing Hull Laterally]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Ship Hull Under Construction]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Trigger Dog Shore - Pre-Launch Lock]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Drag Chain Anchor Points]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Hydraulic Buffer Stop]
    nodeC --> nodeM[Pile Group - Deep Foundation]
```

### 4.2 Side Launch Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Phase 1: Ship Built Side-on to Water] --> nodeB[Phase 2: Blocks Cut, Weight on Cradle]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Phase 3: Trigger Released, Cradle Slides]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Phase 4: Pivot on Forepop End]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Phase 5: One Bilge Enters Water]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Phase 6: Ship Rolls, Achieves Even Keel]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Phase 7: Tugs Take Over, Drag Chains Slow]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Phase 8: Ship Berthed at Fitting Out Wharf]
```

### 4.3 End Launch Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Phase 1: Ship Built Bow Pointing to Water] --> nodeP2[Phase 2: Blocks Removed, Cradle Loaded]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Phase 3: Trigger Released, Sliding Begins]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Phase 4: Bow Enters Water, Stern on Slipway]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Phase 5: Stern Floats - Maximum Sagging Check]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[Phase 6: Tugs Move Ship, Drag Chains Engage]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7[Phase 7: Fitting Out Berthing]
```

### 4.4 Force Diagram — Forces on the Cradle During Launch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Weight of Ship W acting downward at Centre of Gravity] --> nodeB[Resolved into Components]
    nodeB --> nodeC[W sin theta acting along Slipway Down the Slope]
    nodeB --> nodeD[W cos theta acting perpendicular to Slipway]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Normal Reaction from Ground Ways]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Frictional Resistance mu times W cos theta - opposes motion]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Net Accelerating Force equals W sin theta minus mu W cos theta]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a slipway. List any four major components of a slipway.*

**Model Answer:**
A slipway is an inclined structure extending from a shipyard ground level into water, used for constructing and launching ships.

Four major components:
1. **Ground ways** — permanent inclined beams forming the runway.
2. **Sliding ways** — temporary greased beams on which the cradle slides.
3. **Cradle** — rigid frame supporting the ship's bilge.
4. **Bilge blocks and side shores** — supporting the hull during construction.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 0.5 each for components)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between end launch and side launch of a ship.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | End Launch | Side Launch |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Longitudinal axis parallel to slipway | Longitudinal axis perpendicular |
| Ship size | Smaller vessels | Larger vessels |
| Critical stress | Hogging/Sagging | Tipping moment |
| Slipway shape | Long and narrow | Short and wide |

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, students must answer ONE full question from Module 4. Each question carries **14 marks** split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **construction sequence of a slipway** for **side launch** of a large vessel. List all major civil works involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship of displacement **8000 tonnes** is to be launched end-on down a slipway of gradient **1 in 16** and length **220 m**. The friction coefficient between greased sliding ways and ground ways is **0.045**. Calculate:
(i) The velocity of the ship at water entry.
(ii) The net accelerating force on the cradle at the start of the launch.
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

The construction sequence is as follows:

1. **Site investigation** — borehole survey, soil bearing capacity assessment (target ≥ 150 kN/m²), tidal range measurement.
2. **Excavation** — cut down to firm strata, dewatering using well-points, sheet piling on seaward side.
3. **Foundation** — driven concrete piles (400–600 mm dia) at 1.5–2.5 m spacing, capped with pile cap.
4. **Pedestals** — RCC pedestals at 2–4 m spacing along slipway length to support ground ways.
5. **Ground ways** — two parallel heavy steel beams (300 × 150 mm to 500 × 250 mm) running full length, bolted to pedestals.
6. **Sliding ways** — temporary greased timber beams bolted to ground ways 2–3 days before launch.
7. **Cradle** — fabricated rigid timber/steel frame sized to ship beam, placed on sliding ways.
8. **Bilge blocks and side shores** — erected under and against hull, locked with wedges.
9. **Trigger mechanism** — dog shores or hydraulic triggers installed at forepop end.
10. **Arresting gear** — drag chain anchor points and hydraulic buffers fitted at inboard end.

*(7 marks — 0.5 to 1 mark per step, with sketch carrying 1 mark)*

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 8000 \text{ t} = 8 \times 10^{6}$ kg
- Gradient = 1 in 16, so $\tan\theta = 1/16 = 0.0625$
- Length $L = 220$ m
- $\mu = 0.045$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Slipway angle**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.0625) = 3.576^\circ$$

$$\sin\theta = 0.0623, \quad \cos\theta = 0.9981$$

**Step 2: Height drop**

$$h = L \sin\theta = 220 \times 0.0623 = 13.71 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Velocity at water entry**

Using $v^2 = 2gL(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$:

$$v^2 = 2 \times 9.81 \times 220 \times (0.0623 - 0.045 \times 0.9981)$$

$$v^2 = 4316.4 \times (0.0623 - 0.0449)$$

$$v^2 = 4316.4 \times 0.0174 = 75.10 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\boxed{v = 8.67 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4: Net accelerating force**

$$F_{net} = mg(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$$

$$F_{net} = 8 \times 10^{6} \times 9.81 \times 0.0174$$

$$\boxed{F_{net} = 1.366 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 1366 \text{ kN}}$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for velocity, 2 marks for force)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** What are the different **arresting arrangements** used to stop a ship after launch? Explain the working of **drag chains** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the concept of **launching curves** for **side launch**. Explain why a ship pivots during side launch and what determines the curvature of its trajectory. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Arresting Arrangements — Types:**

1. **Drag Chains** — heavy chains paid out from the cradle, scraping the slipway and absorbing kinetic energy through friction.
2. **Drag Weights** — heavy steel weights attached by wire ropes, dragging along the ground.
3. **Cable and Winch System** — wire rope connected to a winch on shore; the winch pays out rope under controlled tension.
4. **Hydraulic Buffers** — hydraulic cylinders at the inboard end of the slipway acting as shock absorbers.
5. **Wooden Dolphins / Pile Clusters** — piles driven in water to physically arrest the ship if other systems fail.

**Working of Drag Chains:**

- Each drag chain is a heavy steel chain (typically **25 mm to 50 mm** link diameter) attached at one end to the cradle and the other end to a strong point on the ground ways (or anchor).
- During launch, the chain is laid in a coil or heap at the inboard end of the slipway.
- As the cradle slides forward, the chain is dragged along, paying out and creating frictional resistance.
- The frictional work done ($F \times d$) equals the kinetic energy of the ship, gradually bringing it to rest.
- Multiple chains are used in parallel; the operator can selectively engage additional chains if ship speed is too high.

**Design Calculations:**
- Energy to be absorbed: $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$
- Force per chain: $F_{chain} = \mu_{chain} \times W_{chain}$
- Number of chains: $N = F_{required} / F_{chain}$

*(7 marks — 2 marks for types, 5 marks for drag chain details)*

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Launching Curves for Side Launch:**

A launching curve is the **trajectory traced by the ship's centre of gravity** from the moment the trigger is released until the ship reaches even keel in water.

**Why does the ship pivot?**

In side launch, the cradle is supported on **two ground ways** running across the slipway. The ship, being rigidly attached to the cradle, initially moves **straight down the slope**. However, as the forepop (inboard end of sliding ways) submerges, the ship begins to **pivot** because:

1. The buoyancy force acts on the submerged portion of the hull.
2. The cradle loses contact progressively from forepop toward the after end.
3. The ship starts **rotating about its centre of buoyancy** while still sliding.
4. Eventually the hull contacts water along its full length and floats.

**Factors Determining the Curvature:**

1. **Slipway gradient** — steeper gradients produce more downward motion, less rotation.
2. **Ship's metacentre height (GM)** — higher GM produces faster righting action.
3. **Initial trim and ballast condition** — affects the moment of inertia.
4. **Weight distribution** — heavier bow causes more resistance.
5. **Cradle friction** — affects how quickly rotation begins.
6. **Water depth at forepop** — determines pivot point geometry.

**Importance of Launching Curves:**

- Used to predict **tipping moment** — must ensure ship doesn't capsize sideways.
- Used to compute **maximum velocity** during rotation.
- Used to design **drag chain engagement timing** — chains must engage at the right moment.
- Used to verify that **no structural member** of the hull exceeds allowable stress during pivot.

**Critical Phase — The Pivot Point:**
The pivot point is theoretically at the **intersection of the slipway centerline and the ship's waterline**. The actual pivot moves dynamically; engineers use **launching curve plots** (centre of gravity trajectory vs. time) to monitor it.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for curve concept, 2 marks for pivot explanation, 2 marks for factors and importance)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ground ways and sliding ways** — ground ways are permanent; sliding ways are temporary and greased.
> 2. **Forgetting the cosine term** in friction — friction is $\mu W \cos\theta$, not $\mu W$. This is a frequent mark-deduction error in numerical problems.
> 3. **Mixing end launch and side launch bending stresses** — end launch stresses are **hogging/sagging** (longitudinal), side launch stresses are **tipping** (transverse).
> 4. **Not mentioning the trigger mechanism** — this is a key component examiners look for.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units (m/s, kN, etc.).
> 6. **Drawing poor sketches** — a neat labelled diagram of slipway components can fetch 1–2 bonus marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 4 Slipways:**

- A **slipway** has a dual role: **building berth + launching way**.
- Construction sequence: site survey → excavation → foundation → pedestals → ground ways → sliding ways → cradle → bilge blocks → side shores → trigger → arresting gear.
- **End launch** — bow-first; critical stresses are **hogging** and **sagging** of the hull girder.
- **Side launch** — pivot launch; critical concern is **tipping moment** and **transverse stability**.
- **Friction coefficient** μ for greased timber-on-timber = **0.03 to 0.05**.
- **Slipway gradient** = typically **1 in 12 to 1 in 20**.
- **Launching velocity** at water entry: **4 m/s to 7 m/s** for side launch.
- **Key formula:** $v = \sqrt{2 g L (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)}$.
- **Key formula:** $F_{net} = mg(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$.
- **Key formula:** $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ (used for arresting gear design).
- **Arresting arrangements** — drag chains, drag weights, winch-and-cable, hydraulic buffers, dolphins.
- **Launching curves** are trajectory plots of the centre of gravity used to verify stability and structural integrity during launch.
- The **trigger mechanism** (dog shores) holds the cradle before launch; its release initiates the launch.
- **Forepop** is the inboard end of the sliding ways, which submerges first and becomes the pivot point.
- **Bilge blocks** support the ship's hull vertically; **side shores** brace it laterally.
- A **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0** is applied in arresting gear design.
- **Plug-and-feather wedges** are used to finely adjust block heights and to safely release them before launch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tipping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tipping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tipping** (also called **End Launching** or **Longitudinal Launching**) is a ship launching technique in which a partially or fully constructed vessel is allowed to slide down an inclined building slip (berth) and **pivots about a fixed point** at the end of the launching ways. The pivot causes the ship to rotate longitudinally so that one end (typically the **stern**) enters the water first, while the other end (the **bow**) rises temporarily before the vessel floats free.

The pivot about which the ship rotates is known as the **Tipping Pivot** or **Tipping Fulcrum**, located at the extremity of the building slip near the waterline. The operation is governed by the principles of **statical stability, longitudinal moment balance, and energy conservation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Tipping is the *most economical* method of launching for vessels built in locations where the shore gradient is steep and a side-launching cradle is impractical. It is the preferred launching method for **large cargo ships, tankers, and naval vessels** constructed on traditional slipways.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **long wooden plank** lying on the floor with one end pointing toward the edge of a swimming pool:

1. You push the plank horizontally — it slides along the floor toward the pool.
2. When the front end reaches the pool edge, instead of falling flat, it begins to **pivot** downward about the edge.
3. The plank rotates like a giant lever until enough of it is submerged to float.
4. Finally, the plank floats free in the pool.

That downward rotation about the pool's edge is **exactly what "tipping"** means in ship construction. The "plank" is the ship, the "floor" is the inclined building slip, the "pool edge" is the **tipping pivot**, and the "rotation" is the **tipping motion** that delivers the ship safely into the water.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Slipway Gradient** | **1 : 12 to 1 : 24** (typical **1 : 18**) | Determines sliding velocity & tipping kinematics |
| **Friction Coefficient (μ)** | **0.02 to 0.05** (sliding ways with tallow/grease) | Energy loss during slide |
| **Drag Chain Drag Ratio** | **0.5 × to 1 × the ship's weight** | Retarding force after water entry |
| **Critical Tipping Angle (θ_c)** | **2° to 6°** (depending on vessel form) | Angle at which pivot shifts from forward to aft |

> [!NOTE]
> A **drag chain ratio of 1.0× the ship's lightweight** is the *safe upper limit* recommended by classification societies (Lloyd's Register, IRS) to prevent overspeed at the moment of water entry.

## 1.4 When Is Tipping Used?

- When the **shore topography** is steep (e.g., riverside yards, cliff-side slipways).
- When the **ship is too long or too wide** for a side-launching cradle.
- When the **building berth is narrow** (longitudinally aligned berths).
- For **vessels with reinforced bow/stern** structures to withstand impact loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **stern** typically enters the water first in tipping because the **propeller, rudder, and after-body** are reinforced and can absorb the impact; the **bow**, with its finer entry, must remain lifted until sufficient buoyancy develops.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tipping Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The tipping operation is divided into **four distinct phases**, each governed by a different set of physical principles:

### Phase 1: Pre-Launch (Static Rest)
- The ship sits on the **building berth** supported by:
  - **Ground ways** (fixed wooden/steel tracks laid on the slip)
  - **Sliding ways** (movable tracks attached to the ship's hull via the cradle)
  - **Fore poppet** and **aft poppet** (trigger supports)
  - **Shores and stagings** (lateral supports)
- The **dogging shores** and **trigger mechanisms** hold the ship stationary.

### Phase 2: Release & Sliding
- Triggers are released; the ship begins to slide under gravity.
- Sliding ways move over ground ways, lubricated by **tallow, soft soap, or grease**.
- Drag chains begin to pay out, providing retarding force.
- **Governing physics:** Newton's Second Law + Energy Conservation.

### Phase 3: Tipping (Rotation about Pivot)
- The **forward end of the sliding ways** reaches the **tipping pivot** at the water's edge.
- The ship **rotates** about this pivot; the stern descends into water.
- Buoyancy builds up rapidly on the after-body.
- **Governing physics:** Rotational dynamics + Moment balance (Statical Stability).

### Phase 4: Water Entry & Float-Off
- Sufficient buoyancy develops to float the ship off the sliding ways.
- Drag chains arrest the forward motion.
- Tugs take over and tow the vessel to fitting-out berth.
- **Governing physics:** Archimedes' Principle + Hydrodynamic braking.

## 2.2 Why and How of Each Step

| Step | Why? | How? |
|---|---|---|
| Slipway inclination | To convert weight into horizontal sliding force | Gravity component along the slip = W·sin(α) |
| Lubrication of ways | To reduce friction and prevent damage | Tallow/grease layer between sliding & ground ways |
| Trigger release | To initiate controlled motion | Mechanical/hydraulic release of fore poppet |
| Tipping pivot design | To provide a fixed rotation axis | Reinforced timber/steel blocks at ways end |
| Drag chains | To limit velocity at water entry | Chains paid out from drums, paying out lengthens effective lever |
| Poppets | To support the ship until launch | Wooden/steel posts under fore & aft overhangs |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value markers — never the bare pipe symbol `|` — to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{\text{slide}} = W \cdot \sin(\alpha) - \mu \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | Net sliding force down the slip (W = weight) | N or kN |
| 2 | $W \cdot \sin(\alpha) = \mu \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | **Condition for ship to start sliding** (overcomes static friction) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\tan(\alpha_{\min}) = \mu$ | **Minimum slipway gradient** for self-launching | Degrees |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} = m g h - F_{\text{friction}} \cdot s - W_{\text{drag}}$ | **Energy conservation** from start to pivot (s = slide distance) | Joules |
| 5 | $v = \sqrt{2 g h \left(1 - \mu \cot(\alpha)\right)}$ | **Launching velocity** at the pivot (idealized) | m/s |
| 6 | $M_{\text{tip}} = W \cdot \overline{GP}_{\text{horiz}} - B \cdot \overline{CB_{\text{horiz}}}$ | **Tipping moment** about pivot P | kN·m |
| 7 | $\theta_{c} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{W \cdot \overline{GP}}{B \cdot \overline{P\,CB}}\right)$ | **Critical tipping angle** | Radians / Degrees |
| 8 | $F_{\text{drag}} = k_{\text{chain}} \cdot W$ | **Drag chain retarding force** (k_chain ≈ 0.5 to 1.0) | kN |
| 9 | $B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | **Archimedes' buoyancy** (∇ = submerged volume) | N |
| 10 | $BM_{L} = \dfrac{I_{T}}{\nabla}$ | **Longitudinal metacentric radius** (I_T = 2nd moment of waterplane) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $\nabla$ (nabla) denotes the **displaced volume** of water in naval architecture — a single Greek letter representing $\mathcal{O}(10^3 \text{ m}^3)$ for large ships.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of tipping are applied in:

- **Shipyards worldwide** (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, Hyundai Heavy Industries) for launching bulk carriers, tankers, and naval frigates.
- **Submarine launching** — submarines are often end-launched with reinforced after-decks.
- **Offshore platform skidding** — the same energy-balance equations govern the skid-launching of jack-up rigs.
- **Civil engineering** — bridge girder launching and dam caisson lowering use identical pivot-and-slide mechanics.
- **Aerospace** — rocket transporter-erector (T/E) vehicles use rotational tipping to lift rockets vertical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Slipway Gradient for Self-Launching

We derive the angle $\alpha_{\min}$ at which a ship will begin to slide on its own, without external pushing force.

### Given
- Weight of ship = $W$ (acting vertically downward)
- Coefficient of static friction between sliding and ground ways = $\mu$
- Slipway inclination to horizontal = $\alpha$

### Step 1: Resolve weight along and perpendicular to the slip

The component of weight **along the slip** (driving force):

$$F_{\parallel} = W \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

The component of weight **perpendicular to the slip** (normal reaction):

$$N = W \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

### Step 2: Maximum static friction force

The maximum friction opposing motion is:

$$F_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

### Step 3: Condition for impending motion

For the ship to *just begin* sliding, the driving force must equal the maximum static friction:

$$W \cdot \sin(\alpha) = \mu \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

### Step 4: Simplify (divide both sides by $W \cdot \cos(\alpha)$)

$$\tan(\alpha_{\min}) = \mu$$

### Step 5: Numerical illustration

> [!NOTE]
> If $\mu = 0.04$, then $\tan(\alpha_{\min}) = 0.04$, so $\alpha_{\min} \approx 2.29°$, corresponding to a gradient of approximately **1 : 25**. Real slipways use steeper gradients (1 : 18 to 1 : 12) to ensure positive acceleration throughout the slide.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Launching Velocity at the Tipping Pivot

We derive the velocity of the ship at the moment the forward sliding way end reaches the tipping pivot.

### Given
- Launching drop height (vertical fall from rest to pivot) = $h$
- Slipway inclination = $\alpha$
- Slide distance along slip = $s$
- Coefficient of kinetic friction = $\mu$

### Step 1: Geometric relation between drop and slide

The ship descends a vertical height $h$ while travelling a distance $s$ along the slip:

$$\sin(\alpha) = \frac{h}{s} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s = \frac{h}{\sin(\alpha)}$$

### Step 2: Energy conservation (work-energy theorem)

The loss in gravitational potential energy equals the gain in kinetic energy plus the energy dissipated by friction:

$$W \cdot h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{g} \cdot v^{2} + \mu \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha) \cdot s$$

### Step 3: Substitute $s = h / \sin(\alpha)$

$$W \cdot h = \frac{W \cdot v^{2}}{2 g} + \mu \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha) \cdot \frac{h}{\sin(\alpha)}$$

### Step 4: Divide through by $W$ and rearrange

$$h - \mu \cdot h \cdot \cot(\alpha) = \frac{v^{2}}{2 g}$$

$$h \left[1 - \mu \cdot \cot(\alpha)\right] = \frac{v^{2}}{2 g}$$

### Step 5: Solve for $v$

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h \left[1 - \mu \cdot \cot(\alpha)\right]}$$

### Step 6: Numerical illustration

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example:** Let $h = 6 \text{ m}$, $\alpha = 4°$ (≈ 1 : 14.3), $\mu = 0.03$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Step 6a — Compute $\cot(\alpha)$:

$$\cot(4°) = \frac{\cos(4°)}{\sin(4°)} = \frac{0.9976}{0.0698} \approx 14.30$$

Step 6b — Compute the bracket:

$$1 - 0.03 \times 14.30 = 1 - 0.429 = 0.571$$

Step 6c — Compute $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 6 \times 0.571} = \sqrt{67.21} \approx 8.20 \text{ m/s}$$

**Result:** $v \approx \mathbf{8.2 \text{ m/s}} \;(\approx 15.9 \text{ knots})$ — the ship would launch at nearly **16 knots** if uncontrolled! This is precisely why **drag chains** are mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Tipping Angle

We derive the angle $\theta_c$ at which the buoyancy moment about the tipping pivot overcomes the weight moment, allowing the bow to lift and the ship to tip over.

### Given
- $W$ = ship weight (acts at center of gravity $G$)
- $B$ = buoyancy when partially submerged (acts at center of buoyancy $CB$)
- $P$ = tipping pivot at the end of the slip
- $\overline{GP}_{\text{horiz}}$ = horizontal distance from $G$ to $P$
- $\overline{P\,CB}_{\text{horiz}}$ = horizontal distance from $P$ to $CB$

### Step 1: Take moments about the pivot $P$

The tipping moment that **rotates the ship stern-down** is produced by weight:

$$M_{W} = W \cdot \overline{GP}_{\text{horiz}}$$

The restoring moment that **rotates the ship bow-down** is produced by buoyancy:

$$M_{B} = B \cdot \overline{P\,CB}_{\text{horiz}}$$

### Step 2: Critical condition (incipient tipping)

At the critical angle, the two moments are equal (rotational equilibrium just broken):

$$W \cdot \overline{GP}_{\text{horiz}} = B \cdot \overline{P\,CB}_{\text{horiz}}$$

### Step 3: Form the ratio

$$\frac{B \cdot \overline{P\,CB}_{\text{horiz}}}{W \cdot \overline{GP}_{\text{horiz}}} = 1$$

### Step 4: Solve for the critical angle using the ship's geometry

For a small angular rotation $\theta$ about the pivot, the horizontal arm of the buoyancy changes approximately as $\overline{P\,CB} \cdot \cos(\theta)$ while gravity arm changes as $\overline{GP} \cdot \cos(\theta)$. Combining with the geometry of the partially submerged hull:

$$\tan(\theta_c) = \frac{W \cdot \overline{GP}}{B \cdot \overline{P\,CB}}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $\theta_c$ is **too small** (< 1°), the ship will tip too easily and uncontrollably — this is dangerous. If $\theta_c$ is **too large** (> 8°), the stern will hit the bottom of the waterway before sufficient buoyancy develops. Designers aim for $\theta_c \approx \mathbf{3° \text{ to } 5°}$.

### Step 6: Numerical illustration

Let $W = 50{,}000 \text{ kN}$, $B = 30{,}000 \text{ kN}$ (initial buoyancy at water entry), $\overline{GP} = 80 \text{ m}$, $\overline{P\,CB} = 35 \text{ m}$.

$$\tan(\theta_c) = \frac{50{,}000 \times 80}{30{,}000 \times 35} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{1{,}050{,}000} \approx 3.81$$

$$\theta_c = \arctan(3.81) \approx 75.3°$$

> [!WARNING]
> This extreme angle indicates that with only 30,000 kN of buoyancy (i.e., very early in the slide), the ship **cannot** tip — the stern would simply dig in. Tipping only proceeds once $B$ grows sufficiently large (typically when 40–60% of the after-body is submerged).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Phase-Wise Force Balance

| Phase | Dominant Forces | Critical Equation | Failure Mode if Violated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Pre-launch** | $W$, normal reaction $N$, friction | $W \sin\alpha < \mu N$ | Premature sliding |
| **2. Sliding** | $W \sin\alpha$, $\mu N$, drag chain $F_d$ | $W \sin\alpha - \mu N - F_d = ma$ | Excessive velocity |
| **3. Tipping** | $W$, $B$, pivot reaction $R_P$ | $W \cdot \overline{GP} = B \cdot \overline{P\,CB}$ | Bow-diving or stern-impact |
| **4. Float-off** | $B$, residual kinetic energy, hydrodynamic drag | $B = W$ (final equilibrium) | Grounding on ways |

## 3.5 Why Drag Chains Are Indispensable — Energy Argument

Without drag chains, all gravitational potential energy converts to kinetic energy:

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} = m g h \quad \Longrightarrow \quad v_{\max} = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

For $h = 6 \text{ m}$: $v_{\max} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 6} \approx 10.85 \text{ m/s} \approx 21 \text{ knots}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **10,000-tonne ship** entering the water at 10 m/s carries kinetic energy $E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^7 \times 10^2 = 5 \times 10^8 \text{ J} \approx \mathbf{139 \text{ kWh}}$. This is the energy of a fully-loaded truck travelling at 100 km/h — concentrated at the stern-water interface for a fraction of a second. Drag chains dissipate 50–70% of this energy safely.

Drag chain work done:

$$W_{\text{drag}} = F_d \cdot s_{\text{chain}} = k_{\text{chain}} \cdot W \cdot s_{\text{chain}}$$

The chain length $s_{\text{chain}}$ is pre-engineered to absorb exactly the kinetic energy surplus.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tipping Launch

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the four-phase tipping operation, showing inputs, control elements, physical sub-systems, and outputs for each phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: Pre-Launch Static Rest] --> A2[Phase 2: Slide Initiation]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Tipping Rotation]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Water Entry and Float Off]
    
    A1 --> B1[Ship Hull on Cradle]
    A1 --> B2[Ground Ways Fixed]
    A1 --> B3[Sliding Ways Attached]
    A1 --> B4[Trigger Dogging System]
    
    A2 --> C1[Slipway Gradient 1:18]
    A2 --> C2[Way Lubrication Tallow]
    A2 --> C3[Drag Chain Drums]
    A2 --> C4[Trigger Release Mechanism]
    
    A3 --> D1[Tipping Pivot Reinforced Block]
    A3 --> D2[Buoyancy on Afterbody]
    A3 --> D3[Weight at Center of Gravity]
    A3 --> D4[Moment Balance Solver]
    
    A4 --> E1[Archimedes Buoyancy Rising]
    A4 --> E2[Hydrodynamic Drag Surge]
    A4 --> E3[Tugboats Take Over]
    A4 --> E4[Drag Chain Arrest]
    
    B1 --> F1[Input: Ship Weight W]
    C1 --> F1
    D3 --> F1
    E1 --> F2[Output: Floating Vessel]
    E3 --> F2
    E4 --> F2
    
    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F2 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Force & Moment Interaction Map During Tipping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Weight W at Center of Gravity G] -->|Acts downward| M1[Moment about Pivot P]
    B1[Buoyancy B at Center of Buoyancy CB] -->|Acts upward| M1
    M1 --> R1[Net Rotational Moment]
    R1 --> R2{Is Moment Balanced?}
    R2 -->|Yes| R3[Ship Continues Rotation]
    R2 -->|No| R4[Ship Stalls or Dives]
    
    P1[Tipping Pivot at Ways End] --> M1
    C1[Drag Chain Force] -->|Opposes motion| R1
    H1[Hydrodynamic Pressure] --> B1
    
    style W1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style B1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style M1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style R2 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style R3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style R4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Component Hierarchy of Launching Ways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LAUNCHSYS[Launching Ways System] --> GW[Ground Ways Fixed to Slip]
    LAUNCHSYS --> SW[Sliding Ways Move with Ship]
    LAUNCHSYS --> CR[Craddle and Bilge Blocks]
    LAUNCHSYS --> SUP[Support and Trigger System]
    LAUNCHSYS --> DC[Drag Chain System]
    LAUNCHSYS --> TP[Tipping Pivot Assembly]
    
    GW --> GW1[Hardwood Timbers]
    GW --> GW2[Concrete Foundation]
    
    SW --> SW1[Hardwood Runners]
    SW --> SW2[Steel Sole Plates]
    SW --> SW3[Grease Tallow Layer]
    
    CR --> CR1[Bilge Block]
    CR --> CR2[Side Shores]
    CR --> CR3[Poppets]
    
    SUP --> SUP1[Fore Poppet]
    SUP --> SUP2[Aft Poppet]
    SUP --> SUP3[Trigger Wedges]
    
    DC --> DC1[Chain Drum]
    DC --> DC2[Chain Pay out Length]
    DC --> DC3[Chain Shackle]
    
    TP --> TP1[Pivot Block Hardwood]
    TP --> TP2[Steel Cap Plate]
    TP --> TP3[Deflector Face]
    
    style LAUNCHSYS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style GW fill:#DEB887
    style SW fill:#F4A460
    style CR fill:#D2B48C
    style SUP fill:#BC8F8F
    style DC fill:#B22222,color:#fff
    style TP fill:#4682B4,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **tipping pivot assembly** is the *single most critical component* — it must absorb the **entire weight of the ship plus the dynamic impact load** at the moment of rotation initiation. Failure of the pivot is catastrophic and historically has caused total loss of vessels during launch.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define tipping in ship construction. Under what topographical conditions is this method preferred over side launching?**

**Model Answer:**

**Tipping** is a longitudinal ship-launching method in which a vessel slides down an inclined building slip and **pivots about a fixed point** (the tipping pivot) at the end of the launching ways, causing the stern to enter the water first while the bow rises temporarily before float-off.

**Topographical conditions favouring tipping:**
1. **Steep shore gradient** — sites where the land falls rapidly toward deep water (e.g., riverside yards, cliff-side slipways).
2. **Narrow building berths** — long, narrow sites unsuitable for the wide cradles required in side launching.
3. **Sufficient water depth at the slip end** — to accommodate the descending stern without grounding.
4. **Length-to-beam ratio > 6** — long slender ships tip more controllably than short beamy vessels.
5. **Availability of reinforced after-body** — to absorb the impact of stern-first water entry.

*(Word count target: 130–150 words for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What are the functions of drag chains and poppets in a tipping launch?**

**Model Answer:**

**Drag Chains:**
- Heavy steel chains wound on drums at the top of the slipway.
- Connected to the ship and **paid out under controlled friction** as the ship slides.
- Function: **dissipate kinetic energy** and **limit velocity** at the moment of water entry.
- Typical drag ratio: 0.5× to 1.0× the ship's launching weight.

**Poppets (Fore and Aft):**
- Reinforced wooden or steel supports placed under the bow and stern overhangs.
- Function: **support the ship's overhangs** during construction and before launch, transferring load to the ground ways via triggers.
- Released sequentially (aft first, then fore) to initiate the **controlled tipping motion**.

*(Word count target: 130–150 words for full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A bulk carrier of displacement 25,000 tonnes is to be launched by tipping on a slipway of gradient 1 : 18. The coefficient of friction between sliding and ground ways is 0.04. The vertical drop from rest position to the tipping pivot is 5.5 m. Determine:**

**(a)** The minimum slipway gradient for self-launching and comment on whether the given gradient is adequate.

**(b)** The velocity of the ship at the tipping pivot, and the kinetic energy at that instant. State two design measures to control this velocity.

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Displacement $W = 25{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 25{,}000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg}$
- Given gradient = $1 : 18$ → $\tan(\alpha) = 1/18$
- Friction coefficient $\mu = 0.04$

**Step 1:** Find the given angle of inclination:

$$\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{1}{18}\right) = \arctan(0.0556) \approx 3.18°$$

**Step 2:** Compute minimum gradient using $\tan(\alpha_{\min}) = \mu$:

$$\tan(\alpha_{\min}) = 0.04 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha_{\min} = \arctan(0.04) \approx 2.29°$$

$$\text{Minimum gradient} = 1 : 25$$

**Step 3:** Compare:

$$\alpha_{\text{given}} = 3.18° > \alpha_{\min} = 2.29°$$

Therefore, the given gradient (1 : 18) is **adequate** — the ship will self-launch with positive margin.

**Step 4:** Compute the safety margin:

$$\text{Margin} = \tan(\alpha) - \mu = 0.0556 - 0.04 = 0.0156$$

$$\text{Margin (\%)} = \frac{0.0156}{0.04} \times 100 = 39\%$$

A **39% margin** above the static friction threshold is healthy and provides assurance against variations in lubrication.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Vertical drop $h = 5.5 \text{ m}$
- $\alpha = 3.18°$, $\mu = 0.04$

**Step 1:** Compute $\cot(\alpha)$:

$$\cot(3.18°) = \frac{\cos(3.18°)}{\sin(3.18°)} = \frac{0.9985}{0.0555} \approx 17.99 \approx 18$$

**Step 2:** Compute the velocity at the tipping pivot:

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h \left[1 - \mu \cot(\alpha)\right]}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 5.5 \times [1 - 0.04 \times 18]}$$

$$v = \sqrt{107.91 \times [1 - 0.72]}$$

$$v = \sqrt{107.91 \times 0.28} = \sqrt{30.21} \approx 5.50 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the kinetic energy at the pivot:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 25{,}000 \times 10^3 \times (5.50)^2$$

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 25 \times 10^6 \times 30.25$$

$$E_k = 378.1 \times 10^6 \text{ J} \approx 378 \text{ MJ}$$

$$E_k \approx 105 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4: Two design measures to control velocity:**

1. **Drag chains** — heavy chains paid out under controlled friction; can absorb 50–70% of $E_k$.
2. **Way lubrication control** — using higher-viscosity grease to increase $\mu$ from 0.04 to ~0.06, reducing $v$ significantly.
3. *(Alternative)* — softwood buffers / hydraulic snubbers at the pivot.
4. *(Alternative)* — reduce slipway gradient (steeper drop = higher $v$).

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **four phases of a tipping launch** and the dominant forces acting in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A frigate of weight 8,000 tonnes is launched by tipping. At a certain instant during the tipping motion, the buoyancy on the submerged after-body is 4,500 tonnes acting at a horizontal distance of 12 m from the pivot, and the centre of gravity is 35 m horizontally from the pivot. Determine the **net tipping moment** and comment on whether the ship will continue to rotate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**The Four Phases of a Tipping Launch:**

**Phase 1 — Pre-Launch (Static Rest):**
- Ship rests on the cradle, supported by ground ways, sliding ways, fore poppet, aft poppet, and side shores.
- **Dominant forces:** Weight $W$ (down), normal reactions $N$ on ways, friction forces $F_s$ (static), reaction at poppets.
- Trigger mechanisms are engaged; ship is stationary.

**Phase 2 — Release and Sliding:**
- Triggers are released; ship begins to accelerate down the inclined slip.
- **Dominant forces:** Weight component $W \sin(\alpha)$ (driving), friction $\mu W \cos(\alpha)$ (opposing), drag chain force $F_d$ (opposing), normal reaction $N$ (perpendicular).
- Equation of motion: $W \sin(\alpha) - \mu W \cos(\alpha) - F_d = ma$.

**Phase 3 — Tipping (Rotation about Pivot):**
- Forward end of sliding ways reaches the tipping pivot at the water's edge.
- Ship rotates about this pivot; stern descends into water; bow rises.
- **Dominant forces:** Weight $W$ at $G$ (rotating moment stern-down), buoyancy $B$ at $CB$ (rotating moment bow-down once water enters), pivot reaction $R_P$.
- Moment balance governs whether rotation continues.

**Phase 4 — Water Entry and Float-Off:**
- Buoyancy increases rapidly as more of the after-body submerges.
- Ship floats off the sliding ways; drag chains arrest the forward motion.
- **Dominant forces:** Buoyancy $B$ (rising), hydrodynamic drag $F_H$ (opposing), residual weight $W$, tugboat bollard pull $T$.
- Final equilibrium when $B = W$ and ship is waterborne.

*(Neat sketch must show inclined slip, ship profile, pivot location, force vectors $W$, $B$, $R_P$, $F_d$.)*

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Ship weight $W = 8{,}000 \text{ t}$ → weight moment arm from pivot: $\overline{GP} = 35 \text{ m}$
- Buoyancy $B = 4{,}500 \text{ t}$ → buoyancy moment arm from pivot: $\overline{P\,CB} = 12 \text{ m}$

**Step 1:** Compute the weight moment (stern-down direction):

$$M_W = W \times \overline{GP} = 8{,}000 \times 35 = 280{,}000 \text{ t·m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the buoyancy moment (opposing rotation):

$$M_B = B \times \overline{P\,CB} = 4{,}500 \times 12 = 54{,}000 \text{ t·m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the net tipping moment:

$$M_{\text{net}} = M_W - M_B = 280{,}000 - 54{,}000 = 226{,}000 \text{ t·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_{\text{net}} = 226{,}000 \text{ t·m} \approx 2.22 \times 10^6 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation — Will the ship continue to rotate?**

**Yes, the ship will continue to rotate** in the stern-down direction. Since $M_W \gg M_B$ at this instant, the buoyancy has not yet developed sufficiently to arrest the rotation. The ship will continue tipping until buoyancy grows (more of the hull submerges) and eventually $M_B = M_W$.

**Step 5: Critical angle for incipient tipping reversal:**

$$\tan(\theta_c) = \frac{M_W}{M_B} = \frac{280{,}000}{54{,}000} \approx 5.19$$

$$\theta_c = \arctan(5.19) \approx 79.1°$$

> [!NOTE]
> At the current instant, $\theta_c$ is very large because buoyancy is only **56%** of weight. As more hull submerges, $B$ grows rapidly and $\theta_c$ will decrease. By the time $B \approx 6{,}500$ t, the critical angle drops to ~50°, indicating the bow will begin to descend as the ship reaches float-off equilibrium.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Tipping Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up weight and mass units** — Always convert tonnes to kilograms (×10³) before using $g = 9.81$ in SI energy equations.
