> 2. **Forgetting to subtract friction energy** in launching velocity derivations — the naive formula $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ overestimates velocity by 30–60% and yields unrealistic values.
> 3. **Incorrect moment-arm direction** — the buoyancy arm $\overline{P\,CB}$ and the weight arm $\overline{GP}$ must both be measured **horizontally** (or both vertically with consistent convention). Mixing directions is the single most common error.
> 4. **Omitting the drag-chain contribution** in KTU numericals — many model answers lose 2 marks because students neglect $F_d$ in the work-energy balance.
> 5. **Skipping units in intermediate steps** — examiners expect units at every stage of the derivation (kN, kN·m, m/s, etc.).
> 6. **Confusing tipping with side launching** — in tipping the rotation is **longitudinal** (about a transverse axis at the pivot); in side launching the rotation is **transverse** (about a longitudinal axis). This distinction is frequently tested for 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting the $\cot(\alpha)$ term** — the friction term in the energy balance uses $\cot(\alpha)$, NOT $\tan(\alpha)$. Writing $\mu \tan(\alpha)$ instead of $\mu \cot(\alpha)$ is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tipping (Module 4)**

- **Tipping** = longitudinal end-launching; ship pivots about a fixed point at the end of the slip.
- Preferred for **steep-shore sites**, **narrow berths**, and **long vessels**.
- **Four phases**: Pre-launch → Sliding → Tipping → Float-off.
- **Minimum slipway gradient condition:** $\tan(\alpha_{\min}) = \mu$ → typically gradient ≈ **1 : 25** for $\mu = 0.04$.
- **Standard shipyard gradient:** **1 : 12 to 1 : 24** (commonly **1 : 18**).
- **Velocity at pivot (with friction):** $v = \sqrt{2gh[1 - \mu\cot(\alpha)]}$.
- **Drag chains** absorb **50–70% of kinetic energy**; drag ratio = 0.5× to 1.0× W.
- **Critical tipping angle:** $\tan(\theta_c) = (W \cdot \overline{GP}) / (B \cdot \overline{P\,CB})$.
- **Stern enters water first** because propeller/rudder zone is reinforced; bow must remain lifted.
- **Poppets (fore & aft)** support overhangs before launch; released sequentially (aft first).
- **Tallow / soft soap lubrication** reduces $\mu$ to 0.02–0.05.
- **Tipping pivot assembly** is the most critical structural component — failure = catastrophic loss.
- **Longitudinal BM** governs stability during the float-off phase: $BM_L = I_T / \nabla$.
- **Energy balance** governs velocity: $mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + F_{\text{friction}} \cdot s + W_{\text{drag}}$.
- **Real shipyard examples**: Cochin Shipyard (India), Hyundai Heavy Industries (South Korea), Fincantieri (Italy), Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (Japan) — all use tipping for large vessel launches.
- **Classification society rules** (Lloyd's, IRS, DNV) mandate drag-chain sizing, pivot structural design, and post-launch inspection protocols.
- **Alternative launching methods** to remember: side launching, float-out (dry dock), piggy-back, and mechanical synchronized launch (used for very large vessels like cruise ships).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pivoting/Plunging. Drop Off
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pivoting/Plunging. Drop Off

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pivoting / Plunging in Plate Development

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Term):**
> **Pivoting (also called Plunging)** is a graphical/semi-graphical method of developing (flattening) a curved hull plate into a flat pattern. In this method, one straight edge of the plate is treated as a **pivot line** (held fixed), while the opposite edge of the plate is **rotated (plunged)** about this pivot through the dihedral angle so that both edges of the developed plate lie in the same plane.

In simple terms — imagine a book lying half-open on a table. The spine of the book acts as the **pivot**, and the two covers form an angle (the dihedral angle). If you push the upper cover down flat while holding the spine fixed, you are performing a **"plunging"** action. The same idea is used by the shipwright to "unfold" a double-curved shell plate onto a flat steel surface for marking and cutting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use Pivoting?**
> Many hull plates (especially in the bilge and turn-of-the-bilge region) are **doubly curved** (curved in two directions). True mathematical flattening is impossible without distortion. Pivoting provides a **practical, low-distortion approximation** by distributing the stretch uniformly along the plate.

## 1.2 Drop Off in Shell Plating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Drop Off** refers to the deliberate staggering of the **transverse seams (butt joints)** between adjacent plates in the same strake. The end of one plate does not meet the end of the next plate on the same frame line — instead, the next plate is offset (shifted forward or aft) by one or more frame spaces. The plate that ends short is said to "**drop off**" at its terminating frame.

### Conceptual Analogy (Drop Off)

Think of a brick wall. The vertical joints between bricks in one row are **never aligned** with the joints in the row above or below — they are offset by half a brick. This staggering (called "**breaking joints**") gives the wall its strength. In ships, **drop offs** perform the same function for the shell plating, ensuring that no continuous weak seam runs across the hull.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Facts:**
> - **Drop offs** are typically placed **at every 4th to 6th frame** spacing in merchant ships.
> - Drop off plates are usually **lighter / shorter** than the through plates.
> - Two consecutive drop-offs in the same strake must be separated by at least **one full plate** (a through plate) — this is a Classification Society rule.
> - A **"scrive board"** (or **loft floor**) is used to lay out the drop-off pattern during the *lofting* process.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

- **Dihedral angle (α)** — the angle between the developed plate's two edges; measured in **degrees (°)**.
- **Frame spacing** — the standard longitudinal distance between adjacent frames; for merchant ships typically **600 mm to 1000 mm**.
- **Plate curvature (sweep)** — expressed in mm rise per metre of chord.
- **Standard plate length** — usually **8 m to 12 m** before drop off.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pivoting/Plunging — Underlying Principle

When a curved plate has its **butt lines** (the short edges running transversely across the hull) lying on **frame lines**, and its **seam lines** (the long edges running fore-aft) lying on the **chines/gore lines**, the plate can be developed by rotation.

**Step-by-step Logic of the Pivoting Method:**

1. **Identify the Pivot Line** — Select the **centerline (or longest straight seam)** of the plate as the pivot. This is the line about which the plate will rotate.
2. **Project the Plate's Far Edge** — Using the *mould loft* floor, project the **far seam line** of the plate perpendicular to the pivot edge on both the body plan and the half-breadth plan.
3. **Determine the Dihedral Angle** — Measure the dihedral angle (α) between the projection of the far edge and the projected position in the developed view.
4. **Rotate (Plunge) the Plate** — Treat the projected far-edge position as a circular arc of radius equal to the **true length of the seam**. Swing the edge down/up to coincide with the pivot plane.
5. **Lay Off True-Length Boundaries** — The developed width equals the **true developed girth length** along each seam.
6. **Check for Distortion** — Any small discrepancy in diagonal measurements indicates the inherent stretch — accepted within Classification Society tolerance (typically **±1 mm per metre**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pivoting is Preferred Over True Triangulation (Geodesic Method):**
> Pivoting is **faster**, requires **fewer measurements**, and produces a result with **negligible error** for plates of moderate curvature. The lost accuracy is on the order of **< 0.5 %** — well within fabrication tolerance.

## 2.2 Drop Off — Underlying Principle

Drop off in shell plating is governed by:

1. **Stagger Rule** — No two adjacent strakes should have their drop-offs on the same frame line. (Refer to the **"two-frame" rule**.)
2. **Continuity of Strength** — A drop off must NOT coincide with a **transverse bulkhead** or a **high-stress region** (such as amidships, where bending moments are maximum).
3. **Plate Economy** — Drop off allows use of **shorter, lighter plates** at the ends of the ship where shell curvature is greater and full-length plates are difficult to obtain in standard mill sizes.
4. **Welding Efficiency** — Proper staggering minimizes the number of **cross joints** (joints that meet three plates at one point — these are forbidden by IACS rules).

> [!WARNING]
> **IACS Rule S7.3 / S7.4 (Common Pitfall):**
> Drop off plates in the **bottom and side shell** must be located such that they are **at least 0.4L** (40 % of ship length) from the ends, OR **two standard frame spaces** away from any other plate termination in adjacent strakes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Relationship** | **Unit** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dihedral angle | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{x_{\text{true}}}{x_{\text{proj}}}\right)$ | degrees (°) | $x_{\text{true}}$ = true length, $x_{\text{proj}}$ = projected length |
| True developed length of seam | $L_{s}$ | $L_{s} = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2}}$ | mm | 3-D Pythagorean expansion |
| Stretch (distortion) error | $E$ | $E = \dfrac{L_{\text{developed}} - L_{\text{actual}}}{L_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | % | Must be $< 0.5\%$ for shell plates |
| Minimum drop-off stagger | $n_{\text{min}}$ | $n_{\text{min}} = 2$ frame spaces | frames | IACS rule for adjacent strakes |
| Plate curvature (sweep) | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{chord}} \times 1000$ | mm/m | Used in plate ordering |
| Frame spacing | $s_{f}$ | $s_{f} = 600 \text{ to } 1000$ | mm | Standard merchant ships |
| Number of through-plates between drop-offs | $N_{t}$ | $N_{t} \geq 1$ | — | Cannot have back-to-back drop-offs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> In modern shipyards, the lofting of drop-offs is increasingly done in **CAD/CAM systems** (e.g., AVEVA Marine, FORAN, Tribon). However, the *principles* taught in this module remain the **foundation of plate nesting software** — the computer still performs a pivoting calculation when generating the flat pattern of a curved plate.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dihedral Angle for Pivoting

### Given
A doubly-curved hull plate has its two longitudinal seam lines (S₁ and S₂) on gore lines of a ship. In the **body plan view**, these seams project as straight lines making angles $\beta_{1}$ and $\beta_{2}$ with the horizontal. The **transverse curvature** is given by the camber $h$ over a frame spacing $s_{f}$.

### Step 1: Define projected and true lengths
Let the **developed length of seam S₁** be $L_{1,\text{true}}$ and its projection onto the baseline (in the half-breadth plan) be $L_{1,\text{proj}}$.

$$
L_{1,\text{proj}} = L_{\text{LOA,contribution}} \quad \text{(longitudinal projection)}
$$

The **true length** of the seam, considering the vertical rise (sweep) $v_{1}$ over its length, is:

$$
L_{1,\text{true}} = \sqrt{L_{1,\text{proj}}^{2} + v_{1}^{2}}
$$

where $v_{1} = s_{f} \cdot \tan(\beta_{1})$.

### Step 2: Compute the dihedral angle
The dihedral angle $\alpha$ between the two seam lines at any frame is:

$$
\alpha = \beta_{2} - \beta_{1}
$$

where $\beta_{1}$ and $\beta_{2}$ are the inclination angles of seams S₁ and S₂ with the horizontal, measured from the **body plan** view.

### Step 3: Set up the pivoting rotation
Treat the **centerline of the plate** as the pivot. The developed half-width from the pivot to seam S₂ is:

$$
w_{\text{dev}} = L_{2,\text{true}} \cdot \cos(\alpha)
$$

### Step 4: Lay out the developed plate
The full developed width of the plate, pivoted flat, becomes:

$$
W_{\text{developed}} = 2 \cdot w_{\text{dev}} = 2 L_{2,\text{true}} \cos(\alpha)
$$

### Step 5: Error analysis
The stretch (distortion) inherent in pivoting is:

$$
E = \frac{W_{\text{developed}} - W_{\text{actual}}}{W_{\text{actual}}} \times 100\%
$$

where $W_{\text{actual}}$ is the **true curved girth** of the plate measured along the hull surface.

> Substituting typical values for a bilge plate ($\alpha \approx 12°$, $L_{2,\text{true}} = 4500 \text{ mm}$):
>
> $$W_{\text{developed}} = 2 \times 4500 \times \cos(12°)$$
> $$W_{\text{developed}} = 9000 \times 0.978$$
> $$W_{\text{developed}} = 8802 \text{ mm}$$
>
> If $W_{\text{actual}} = 8820 \text{ mm}$, then:
> $$E = \frac{8802 - 8820}{8820} \times 100\% = -0.20\%$$
>
> This is well within the **±0.5 %** tolerance, confirming that **pivoting is a valid method** for this plate.

## 3.2 Drop-Off — Analytical Layout

### Problem Setup
A strake consists of 8 plates, numbered P1 through P8 from forward to aft. Frame spacing is $s_{f} = 750 \text{ mm}$. Plating direction is **longitudinal (in-line strake)**.

### Step 1: Determine the position of through-plates
Through-plates (full-length) are placed such that their forward edge is at frame F-20, and they extend over **5 frame spaces** (= $5 \times 750 = 3750 \text{ mm}$). So P1 spans F-20 → F-25, and the next through-plate P5 starts at F-25 + 1 frame gap = F-26.

### Step 2: Position the drop-off plate
The drop-off plate P3 is shorter, spanning **3 frame spaces** (e.g., F-32 to F-35). Its forward edge is at F-32 and its aft edge (the **drop-off edge**) is at F-35.

### Step 3: Verify the stagger rule
Check that the drop-off at F-35 in this strake is **not aligned** with the drop-off in the strake above/below:
- Strake above: drop-off at F-36 ✓ (offset by 1 frame — acceptable)
- Strake below: drop-off at F-33 ✗ (only 2 frames away — **VIOLATION**)
- **Correction:** Move the lower strake drop-off to F-37.

### Step 4: Final arrangement table

| **Plate ID** | **Type** | **Forward Frame** | **Aft Frame** | **Length (mm)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | Through | F-20 | F-25 | 3750 |
| P2 | Through | F-25 | F-30 | 3750 |
| P3 | Drop-off | F-30 | F-33 | 2250 |
| P4 | Through | F-33 | F-38 | 3750 |
| P5 | Drop-off | F-38 | F-41 | 2250 |
| P6 | Through | F-41 | F-46 | 3750 |
| P7 | Through | F-46 | F-51 | 3750 |
| P8 | Drop-off | F-51 | F-54 | 2250 |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice: drop-offs occur at F-33, F-41, and F-54. The minimum separation between consecutive drop-offs is $41 - 33 = 8$ frames (more than 1 plate) ✓ — this satisfies the **"no back-to-back drop-offs"** rule.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pivoting/Plunging — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps out the **workflow of the pivoting method** as a block-level functional flow — adapted per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (no reserved keywords, no formatting inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Plate on Body Plan] --> B[Select Pivot Line at Centerline]
    B --> C[Project Seam Lines to Half Breadth Plan]
    C --> D[Measure Dihedral Angle alpha]
    D --> E{Alpha Acceptable?}
    E -- No --> F[Use Geodesic Triangulation Instead]
    E -- Yes --> G[Compute True Seam Lengths]
    G --> H[Swing Far Seam about Pivot to Flat Plane]
    H --> I[Mark Developed Plate Outline on Loft Floor]
    I --> J[Add Shrinkage and Bevel Allowances]
    J --> K[Transfer to Steel Plate for Cutting]
    K --> L[Final Developed Plate Ready for Fabrication]
```

## 4.2 Drop-Off — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Shell Expansion Drawing] --> B[Identify Strakes from Keel to Deck Edge]
    B --> C[Mark Frame Lines on Each Strake]
    C --> D[Place Through Plates Maximum Length First]
    D --> E[Identify Curved End Regions of Hull]
    E --> F[Insert Drop Off Plates at Staggered Frames]
    F --> G[Verify Stagger Rule Adjacent Strakes]
    G --> H{Rule Satisfied?}
    H -- No --> I[Shift Drop Off Location by Min 2 Frames]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Check No Drop Off at Bulkheads]
    J --> K{Rule Satisfied?}
    K -- No --> L[Relocate Drop Off Plate]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[Finalize Plate Nesting and Cutting Plan]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Relationship Between Pivoting and Drop-Off

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Hull Surface Curved] --> B[Shell Expansion on Loft Floor]
    B --> C[Each Plate Developed via Pivoting]
    B --> D[Strake Layout with Drop Offs]
    C --> E[Final Flat Plate Pattern]
    D --> F[Final Plate Nesting Plan]
    E --> G[Fabrication Workshop]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Assembled Ship Hull]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Schematics:**
> - The **first diagram** walks through the *mathematical* and *geometric* decisions during a single plate development.
> - The **second diagram** captures the *production planning* aspect of arranging multiple plates in a strake with proper drop-off staggering.
> - The **third diagram** shows the **unified engineering flow** from curved hull to fabricated ship.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Drop Off" as used in ship shell plating. State two rules governing the placement of drop off plates.**

**Model Answer:**
Drop off refers to the termination of a plate in a strake, where the next plate in the same strake begins at a different frame line, producing a staggered seam. **Two rules are:**

1. The drop-offs in adjacent strakes must be staggered by at least **two frame spaces** (IACS rule).
2. Drop-off plates must not be located at **transverse bulkheads** or in the **amidships 0.4L region** where bending stresses are highest.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for rules)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by Pivoting (Plunging) in plate development? Why is it used?**

**Model Answer:**
Pivoting is a method of developing a curved hull plate into a flat pattern by rotating one edge of the plate about a chosen pivot line (usually the centerline) until the entire plate lies in a single plane.

**It is used because:**
- It is **faster** and **simpler** than true geodesic triangulation.
- It introduces **minimal stretch error** (< 0.5 %).
- It provides a **practical flat pattern** suitable for marking and cutting steel plates.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for advantages)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **"drop off"** with **"butt"** — remember: a butt is a **joint** between two plates; a drop-off is a **plate** that ends short within a strake.
> - In Pivoting questions, students forget to **add shrinkage/bevel allowance** before final plate dimensions — this loses 2 marks.
> - Always mention **IACS / Classification Society rule** when answering drop-off placement questions — examiners reward rule citations.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **pivoting (plunging) method** of plate development with the help of a neat sketch. Mention the sources of error in this method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bottom plate in a ship has two seam lines making angles of $8°$ and $14°$ with the horizontal respectively, in the body plan. The projected length of each seam is 4.2 m. Determine the **developed width** of the plate using the pivoting method. Take the dihedral angle as the difference between the two seam angles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(Detailed answer — refer to Sections 2.1 and 3.1 of these notes for the underlying theory.)*

- Definition of pivoting and the role of the pivot line.
- The plate is rotated about its centerline (or longest straight edge) until the far edge lies in the same plane.
- **Sources of error:**
  1. The plate is doubly curved, so flattening introduces **stretch** (longitudinal fibres must lengthen or shorten).
  2. **Dihedral angle assumption** is uniform — in reality, it varies across the plate.
  3. **Mould loft measurement errors** during lofting.
  4. **Plate thickness** is ignored in the flat development.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for procedure, 2 for error sources)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Dihedral angle
$$\alpha = \beta_{2} - \beta_{1} = 14° - 8° = 6°$$

**Step 2:** True length of far seam
$$L_{2,\text{true}} = \frac{L_{2,\text{proj}}}{\cos(\beta_{2})} = \frac{4.2}{\cos(14°)} = \frac{4.2}{0.9703} = 4.329 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Developed half-width
$$w_{\text{dev}} = L_{2,\text{true}} \cdot \cos(\alpha) = 4.329 \times \cos(6°) = 4.329 \times 0.9945 = 4.305 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Full developed width
$$W_{\text{developed}} = 2 \times w_{\text{dev}} = 2 \times 4.305 = 8.610 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{developed}} \approx 8.61 \text{ m}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for dihedral angle, 2 for true length, 2 for developed width, 1 for final boxed answer with units)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(a)** What is **drop off** in shell plating? With the help of a diagram, explain the **stagger rule** for placing drop-off plates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A side strake of a ship is to be plated over a length of 30 metres. Frame spacing is 750 mm. The strake contains **through-plates of length 5 frame spaces** and **drop-off plates of length 3 frame spaces**, arranged alternately. Calculate:
- (i) Total number of plates
- (ii) Total number of drop-off plates
- (iii) Total number of through-plates **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Sections 1.2 and 2.2 of these notes.

- Drop off is the **termination of a plate** in a strake, with the next plate beginning at a different frame line.
- Stagger rule: drop-offs in adjacent strakes must be offset by at least **two frame spaces**.
- Drop-offs must NOT coincide with transverse bulkheads, and NO two drop-offs should occur on the same frame line in the same strake.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for rule with diagram, 2 for additional conditions)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total number of frame spaces in the strake
$$n_{\text{frames}} = \frac{30{,}000}{750} = 40 \text{ frame spaces}$$

**Step 2:** Pattern unit length (one through + one drop-off)
$$L_{\text{unit}} = 5 \text{ spaces} + 3 \text{ spaces} = 8 \text{ frame spaces}$$

**Step 3:** Number of complete pattern units
$$N_{\text{units}} = \frac{40}{8} = 5 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Total number of plates
$$N_{\text{total}} = 2 \times N_{\text{units}} = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ plates}$$

**Step 5:** Number of drop-off plates
$$N_{\text{drop}} = N_{\text{units}} = 5 \text{ drop-off plates}$$

**Step 6:** Number of through-plates
$$N_{\text{through}} = N_{\text{units}} = 5 \text{ through-plates}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{total}} = 10, \quad N_{\text{drop}} = 5, \quad N_{\text{through}} = 5}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for step 1, 2 for step 2-3, 3 for steps 4-6 with units)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pivoting (Plunging)** = rotating a curved plate about a **pivot line** to develop a flat pattern; introduces **< 0.5 % stretch error**.
- **Drop Off** = a **shorter plate** within a strake that terminates at a frame; the offset it creates is critical for **structural continuity**.
- **Stagger Rule:** drop-offs in adjacent strakes must differ by **≥ 2 frame spaces**.
- **No back-to-back drop-offs:** two drop-offs in the same strake must have **at least one through-plate** between them.
- **Forbidden Locations:** drop-offs must NOT coincide with **transverse bulkheads** or fall within the **amidships 0.4L** region.
- **Source of error in pivoting:** doubly-curved plates cannot be flattened exactly; dihedral angle varies across the plate.
- **Modern Practice:** Pivoting logic is now embedded in **CAD/CAM nesting software** (AVEVA, FORAN, Tribon) — but the manual understanding is still examined by KTU.
- **Standard frame spacing** in merchant vessels: **600 mm – 1000 mm**.
- **Dihedral angle formula** for a plate with two seam inclinations: $\alpha = \beta_{2} - \beta_{1}$.
- **True seam length**: $L_{\text{true}} = L_{\text{proj}} / \cos(\beta)$ — always use this in pivoting problems.
- **Always include** shrinkage allowance (~1.5 mm/m for mild steel), bevel allowance, and marking allowance in the final plate dimensions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Methods of Launching - Launching by Floating Off (Building Dock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Other Methods of Launching - Launching by Floating Off (Building Dock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Building Dock Launching (Floating-Off Method)** is a ship launching technique in which a vessel is completely constructed inside a dry, water-retaining structure (graving dock, floating dock, or building berth with caisson), and is then "launched" by deliberately **flooding the dock with water** until the buoyancy force acting on the hull exceeds the vessel's lightweight, causing the ship to **float off** the keel blocks and rise freely. The vessel is subsequently towed out of the dock by tugs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This method is classified under "Other Methods of Launching" and is reserved for vessels that are either **too large**, **too wide**, or have **insufficient structural strength** to withstand the dynamic loads of a conventional end-launching or side-launching slipway. Common applications include **ULCCs, VLCCs, LNG carriers, aircraft carriers, drilling rigs, and submarines.**

## 1.2 Types of Building Docks

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Graving Dock** | Permanent shore-based dry dock with gate entrance | All ship types |
| **Floating Dock** | Self-propelled submersible pontoon structure | Repair, small-mid ship building |
| **Building Berth + Caisson** | Open quay with removable watertight caisson | Very large ships |
| **Inclined Building Berth** | Slightly sloped platform (floods evenly) | Large warships, icebreakers |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Explanation:** Imagine you have built a model boat inside your empty bathtub and supported it with two small blocks under its keel. To "launch" it, you simply turn on the tap and let the tub fill with water. As the water rises, the boat eventually lifts off the blocks and floats. Now, simply pull the plug (open the dock gate) and the boat sails out with a little tug (your hand). This is exactly how the building-dock floating-off method works — only on a scale of hundreds of thousands of tonnes!

## 1.4 Why Use the Floating-Off Method?

- **Eliminates dynamic launching loads** (no stern-first impact, no whipping forces).
- **No longitudinal or transverse groundways required** — saves costly slipway infrastructure.
- Allows construction of **non-traditional hull forms** (cylindrical, multi-hull, SWATH).
- Permits outfitting and painting **at the most convenient working height** (dry condition).
- **Critical metric:** Standard dock floor load capacity = **50 kN/m² to 150 kN/m²**.
- **Critical metric:** Standard graving dock width = **up to ~80 m**, length = **up to ~500 m** for modern mega-docks.

## 1.5 Comparison with Conventional Launching

| Parameter | End Launching | Side Launching | Floating Off (Dock) |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Peak hull load | Very high | Very high | **Negligible (quasi-static)** |
| Suitable size | ≤ 100,000 DWT | ≤ 50,000 DWT | **Unlimited** |
| Capital cost | High | High | **Very High** |
| Construction height | Above water | Above water | **At ground level** |
| Launching risk | High | High | **Very Low** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principle — Why a Ship Floats Off

When the dock is flooded, the **buoyancy force** acting upward on the hull equals the **weight of water displaced** by the submerged volume of the vessel. The condition for floating-off is:

$$ F_{buoyancy} \geq W_{ship} $$

Until the water level reaches the keel, the ship rests entirely on **keel blocks** and **bilge blocks**. The moment the buoyancy first equals the lightweight, the ship is said to be **"waterborne."** Further flooding raises the vessel above the blocks.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Pre-flooding Checks**
   - All underwater fittings, sea valves, and stern glands installed.
   - Rudder, propeller, and bow thruster fitted.
   - Dock gates sealed; caisson tested for watertightness.
   - Bilge blocks and keel blocks aligned to structural members.
   - Stability calculation (GM) verified for floating condition.

2. **Flooding the Dock**
   - Flooding valves (sluice gates) opened gradually.
   - Filling time: typically **4 to 12 hours** depending on dock size and pumping capacity.
   - Water introduced symmetrically to avoid list.
   - Rate of rise monitored to prevent air entrapment under hull.

3. **Ship Becomes Waterborne**
   - Buoyancy = Lightweight weight.
   - Ship lifts off keel blocks by **~50 mm to 150 mm** (safety clearance).
   - All block loads transferred to water pressure.

4. **Continued Flooding to Floating Draft**
   - Water raised until dock level matches harbour/sea level.
   - Ship attains its **launching draft** (typically deeper than service draft due to incomplete outfitting).

5. **Opening the Dock**
   - Gates or caisson removed.
   - Ship is held by **mooring lines / winches** during this operation.

6. **Towing Out**
   - 2 to 4 tugs (depending on size) take the ship out.
   - Ship moved to fitting-out berth or sea trials area.

## 2.3 Critical Engineering Considerations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus Point:** The following parameters are almost always tested:
> - **Block Load Distribution** — how keel blocks share hull weight
> - **Buoyancy Curve (B-P Curve)** — determines draft at floating-off
> - **GM Stability** at the instant of floating-off
> - **Under-keel Clearance** during towing out

### 2.3.1 Block Load Analysis

The ship is supported by **keel blocks** (along the centerline) and **side/bilge blocks** (lateral). The total block reaction = lightweight W.

$$ R_{total} = R_{keel} + R_{bilge} = W_{ship} $$

For a typical block arrangement:

$$ R_{keel} \approx 0.55\,W \quad \text{and} \quad R_{bilge} \approx 0.45\,W $$

Block load per unit area:

$$ p_{block} = \frac{W_{ship}}{A_{contact}} $$

where A_contact is the total bearing area of all blocks against the hull.

### 2.3.2 Draft at Floating-Off

Using Archimedes' principle:

$$ \nabla_{float-off} = \frac{W_{lightweight}}{\rho_{water} \cdot g} $$

The corresponding draft T_float is read from the ship's **hydrostatic curves** at this displacement volume ∇.

### 2.3.3 Stability at Floating-Off

The metacentric height must satisfy:

$$ GM = KB + BM - KG > 0 $$

For the floating-off instant, the ship has **minimal freeboard**, so the righting arm GZ must be adequate at small heel angles (typically 5°–10°).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Markdown Tables:** Vertical bars `|` have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking table syntax in KTU's online renderer.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Description** |
|--------------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Buoyancy Force | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | kN or MN | Upward force on submerged hull |
| Floating-off Condition | $\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla \geq W_{ship}$ | — | When equality holds, ship floats |
| Lightweight Displacement | $\nabla_{lo} = W_{lw} \,/\, (\rho_{sw}\,g)$ | m³ | Volume at floating-off |
| Draft at Float-off | $T_{lo} = f(\nabla_{lo})$ — from hydrostatic | m | Read from Bonjean/hydrostatic |
| Block Pressure | $p_{block} = W_{lw} \,/\, A_{contact}$ | kN/m² | Must be ≤ block allowable |
| Metacentric Height | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | m | Must be positive |
| BM (transverse) | $BM = I_T \,/\, \nabla$ | m | I_T = 2nd moment of waterplane |
| Freeboard at LO | $F_{lo} = D - T_{lo}$ | m | Often very small |
| Dock Flooding Time | $t_f = V_{dock} \,/\, Q_{pump}$ | hours | V_dock = dock volume; Q = flow rate |
| Tug Bollard Pull | $BP_{total} = k \cdot W_{ship}$ | kN | Typically k = 0.005 to 0.015 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ULCCs & VLCCs:** Built in the **Okpo Geoje, Daewoo, and Hyundai Mega-Docks** (South Korea).
- **Aircraft Carriers:** US Navy uses **dry dock #6 at Newport News Shipbuilding**.
- **LNG Carriers:** Moss-type and membrane-type routinely use dock launching.
- **Submarines:** Universally built in graving docks due to cylindrical pressure hull.
- **Repair Industry:** Floating docks (e.g., AASTO, Johnson dock systems) used globally.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Draft at the Instant of Floating-Off

We need to determine the **draft T_lo** of the ship the moment it lifts off the keel blocks.

### Given Data (Standard KTU Problem Setup)

- Lightweight of ship, $W_{lw} = 25{,}000$ tonnes
- Seawater density, $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Waterplane area coefficient, $C_W = 0.85$
- Length between perpendiculars, $L_{BP} = 180$ m
- Beam, $B = 28$ m$
- Block coefficient at float-off draft, $C_B = 0.72$

### Step 1 — Express Lightweight in Newton-force Units

The lightweight in mass units is converted to weight units:

$$ W_{lw} = m_{lw} \cdot g = 25{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 $$

$$ W_{lw} = 2.4525 \times 10^{8} \text{ N} $$

In kilonewtons:

$$ W_{lw} = 245{,}250 \text{ kN} \approx 245.25 \text{ MN} $$

### Step 2 — Apply Archimedes' Principle to Find Submerged Volume

At floating-off, buoyancy equals weight:

$$ F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{lo} = W_{lw} $$

Rearranging for the displaced volume $\nabla_{lo}$:

$$ \nabla_{lo} = \frac{W_{lw}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} $$

Substituting in consistent units (N, kg/m³, m/s²):

$$ \nabla_{lo} = \frac{2.4525 \times 10^{8}}{1025 \times 9.81} $$

$$ \nabla_{lo} = \frac{2.4525 \times 10^{8}}{10055.25} $$

$$ \nabla_{lo} = 24{,}389.5 \text{ m}^3 $$

### Step 3 — Express Displaced Volume in Standard Ship Form

The displaced volume of a ship is given by:

$$ \nabla = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B $$

Solving for the floating-off draft $T_{lo}$:

$$ T_{lo} = \frac{\nabla_{lo}}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot C_B} $$

$$ T_{lo} = \frac{24{,}389.5}{180 \times 28 \times 0.72} $$

$$ T_{lo} = \frac{24{,}389.5}{3628.8} $$

$$ T_{lo} = 6.72 \text{ m} $$

### Step 4 — Verify Waterplane Area at This Draft

The waterplane area is:

$$ A_W = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot C_W $$

$$ A_W = 180 \times 28 \times 0.85 = 4284 \text{ m}^2 $$

The first moment of area (used in BM calculation):

$$ I_T = \frac{L_{BP} \cdot B^3 \cdot C_W}{12} $$

$$ I_T = \frac{180 \times 28^3 \times 0.85}{12} $$

$$ I_T = \frac{180 \times 21952 \times 0.85}{12} $$

$$ I_T = \frac{3{,}358{,}656}{12} = 279{,}888 \text{ m}^4 $$

### Step 5 — Calculate Transverse BM

$$ BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla_{lo}} $$

$$ BM = \frac{279{,}888}{24{,}389.5} $$

$$ BM = 11.476 \text{ m} $$

### Step 6 — Compute KB (Height of Centre of Buoyancy Above Keel)

For a typical prismatic hull form:

$$ KB \approx 0.53 \cdot T_{lo} $$

$$ KB \approx 0.53 \times 6.72 = 3.56 \text{ m} $$

### Step 7 — Take KG from Lightweight Distribution

Assume the vertical centre of gravity (lightweight condition):

$$ KG = 0.45 \cdot D_{moulded} $$

With moulded depth D = 18 m:

$$ KG = 0.45 \times 18 = 8.10 \text{ m} $$

### Step 8 — Compute GM and Check Stability

$$ GM = KB + BM - KG $$

$$ GM = 3.56 + 11.476 - 8.10 $$

$$ GM = 6.94 \text{ m} $$

### Step 9 — Verify Adequate Stability

For a ship at the floating-off instant, KTU/IMO guidelines require:

$$ GM \geq 0.15 \text{ m (minimum positive stability)} $$

Since $GM = 6.94 \text{ m} \gg 0.15 \text{ m}$, the ship has **excellent initial stability** at the floating-off condition. ✓

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Engineering Parameters Summary

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Computed Value** | **KTU Threshold** | **Status** |
|---------------|------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------|
| Lightweight | $W_{lw}$ | 25,000 t | — | Given |
| Displacement Vol. | $\nabla_{lo}$ | 24,389.5 m³ | — | Computed |
| Float-off Draft | $T_{lo}$ | 6.72 m | — | Computed |
| Waterplane Area | $A_W$ | 4284 m² | — | Computed |
| Transverse BM | $BM$ | 11.48 m | > 0 | ✓ Pass |
| KB | $KB$ | 3.56 m | > 0 | ✓ Pass |
| KG | $KG$ | 8.10 m | < KB+BM | ✓ Pass |
| GM | $GM$ | 6.94 m | ≥ 0.15 m | ✓ Pass |
| Freeboard | $F$ | 18 - 6.72 = 11.28 m | > 0 | ✓ Pass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Note that the floating-off draft (6.72 m) is significantly less than the service load draft because the vessel is in a **lightweight condition** (no fuel, water, cargo, or crew). This low freeboard is a critical period for stability monitoring — any sudden shift in weight could be hazardous.

## 3.3 Derivation: Dock Flooding Time

Given:
- Dock internal length, $L_d = 350$ m
- Dock internal breadth, $B_d = 65$ m
- Initial water depth on keel blocks, $h_i = 0.5$ m
- Target water depth for floating-off, $h_f = T_{lo} + 0.15$ m = 6.87 m
- Flooding pump capacity, $Q = 4$ m³/s

Volume of water to be pumped in:

$$ V_{water} = L_d \cdot B_d \cdot (h_f - h_i) $$

$$ V_{water} = 350 \times 65 \times (6.87 - 0.50) $$

$$ V_{water} = 22{,}750 \times 6.37 $$

$$ V_{water} = 144{,}917.5 \text{ m}^3 $$

Flooding time:

$$ t_f = \frac{V_{water}}{Q} = \frac{144{,}917.5}{4} $$

$$ t_f = 36{,}229.4 \text{ s} \approx 10.06 \text{ hours} $$

This is consistent with industry practice (**4 to 12 hours**).

## 3.4 Derivation: Tug Bollard Pull Requirement

Empirical rule: Total bollard pull = **1% to 1.5% of ship displacement**.

$$ BP_{total} = 0.012 \times W_{lw} $$

$$ BP_{total} = 0.012 \times 25{,}000 \times 9.81 $$

$$ BP_{total} = 2943 \text{ kN } \approx 300 \text{ tonnes force} $$

If each tug provides **75 tonnes bollard pull**, number of tugs required:

$$ N_{tugs} = \frac{300}{75} = 4 \text{ tugs} $$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flowchart — Building Dock Floating-Off Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage A: Ship Construction Complete] --> A2{Pre-Flooding Inspections}
    A2 --> A3[Verify Sea Valves Sealed]
    A2 --> A4[Confirm Rudder and Propeller Fitted]
    A2 --> A5[Stability Calculation Approved]
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B1

    B1[Stage B: Close Dock Gates / Set Caisson] --> B2[Open Flooding Valves Slowly]
    B2 --> B3[Monitor Water Level Rise]
    B3 --> B4{Buoyancy = Lightweight?}
    B4 -- No --> B3
    B4 -- Yes --> C1[Stage C: Ship Becomes Waterborne]

    C1 --> C2[Continue Flooding to Sea Level]
    C2 --> C3[Ship Rises to Floating Draft]
    C3 --> D1[Stage D: Open Dock Gates]
    D1 --> D2[Attach Tow Lines]
    D2 --> D3[Stage E: Tugs Tow Ship Out]
    D3 --> D4[Move to Fitting-Out Berth]
    D4 --> E1[Sea Trials and Delivery]

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Forces During Floating-Off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Lightweight W_lw] -->|Downward| NodeX[Ship Hull]
    NodeX -->|Upward| F1[Buoyancy Force F_b]
    F1 -.->|Equals at Float-Off| L1
    NodeX -->|Rests On| K1[Keel Blocks]
    NodeX -->|Supported By| K2[Bilge Blocks]
    K1 -->|Reactions| NodeX
    K2 -->|Reactions| NodeX

    style L1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style NodeX fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style K1 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K2 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Types of Building Docks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Building Dock Methods] --> M2[Graving Dock]
    M1 --> M3[Floating Dock]
    M1 --> M4[Building Berth + Caisson]
    M1 --> M5[Inclined Building Berth]

    M2 --> M2a[Permanent Shore Structure]
    M2 --> M2b[Gate or Caisson Entrance]
    M2 --> M2c[Used for All Ship Types]

    M3 --> M3a[Self-Propelled Pontoons]
    M3 --> M3b[Submerge by Ballasting]
    M3 --> M3c[Repair and Small Building]

    M4 --> M4a[Open Quay Construction]
    M4 --> M4b[Removable Watertight Caisson]
    M4 --> M4c[Very Large Ships ULCCs]

    M5 --> M5a[Slight Slope for Water Runoff]
    M5 --> M5b[Used for Icebreakers Warships]
    M5 --> M5c[Even Flooding Distribution]

    style M1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style M2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M3 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Floating-Off Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1[Phase 1 — Pre-Flooding]
        P1[Construction Complete]
        P2[All Sea Valves Closed]
        P3[Stability Calculated]
        P4[Keel and Bilge Blocks Verified]
    end

    subgraph S2[Phase 2 — Flooding]
        Q1[Open Flooding Sluices]
        Q2[Symmetric Water Rise]
        Q3[Monitor Buoyancy Buildup]
        Q4[Ship Lifts Off Blocks]
    end

    subgraph S3[Phase 3 — Float-Off]
        R1[Buoyancy Equals Weight]
        R2[Ship at Floating Draft]
        R3[GM Verified Positive]
        R4[Open Dock Gate]
    end

    subgraph S4[Phase 4 — Departure]
        T1[Mooring Lines Released]
        T2[Tugs Attached]
        T3[Towing Commences]
        T4[Transit to Fitting Berth]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4

    style S1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All Mermaid diagrams above follow the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** — every node uses purely alphanumeric prefixed labels (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `NodeX`, `P1`, `Q3`). No reserved keywords are used as standalone node IDs, ensuring clean rendering in all KTU-compatible markdown viewers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*"Define the launching method known as 'floating off' using a building dock. Mention any TWO situations where this method is preferred over conventional end launching."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (2 Marks):** Floating-off using a building dock is a launching method in which the ship is constructed inside a watertight dock structure (graving dock or floating dock). After completion, the dock is gradually flooded until the buoyancy force on the hull equals (and then exceeds) the vessel's lightweight, causing it to **lift off the keel blocks and float freely**. The dock gates are then opened and the ship is towed out by tugs.

2. **Preferred Situations (½ Mark each, any two):**
   - For **very large vessels** (ULCCs, VLCCs, LNG carriers) that exceed the capacity of slipways.
   - For vessels with **insufficient longitudinal strength** to withstand end-launching dynamic loads.
   - For **non-conventional hull forms** (submarines, SWATH, cylindrical FPSOs).
   - When **safe, low-stress launching** is essential for the hull.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*"What is a keel block? Why are bilge blocks provided alongside keel blocks during building-dock construction?"*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Keel Block (1 Mark):** A keel block is a vertical timber, concrete, or steel support placed along the centerline of the dock floor directly beneath the ship's keel. It bears the **primary share of the ship's lightweight** during construction and before flooding.

2. **Function of Bilge Blocks (2 Marks):** Bilge blocks are placed on either side of the centerline, in line with the ship's **transverse frames** (ring frames). They serve to:
   - Provide **lateral support** to the hull and prevent rolling/shifting.
   - **Distribute the hull's weight** over a wider contact area, reducing localised hull plate stress.
   - Maintain the **intended hull shape** (preventing deformation) during construction.
   - Share approximately **45% of the lightweight load**, with keel blocks taking the remaining 55%.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **various types of building docks** used for ship construction and launching. Discuss the merits and demerits of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship of **lightweight 18,500 tonnes** is being constructed in a graving dock. The dock is to be flooded to launch the ship. Given:
- $L_{BP} = 160$ m, $B = 26$ m$, $C_B = 0.70$, $C_W = 0.83$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $D_{moulded} = 16$ m, $KG = 7.5$ m

Calculate:
(i) The **displacement volume** at floating-off.  
(ii) The **floating-off draft**.  
(iii) The **metacentric height GM** at floating-off.  
(iv) Comment on the **stability** of the ship at the floating-off instant. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The various types of building docks are:

**1. Graving Dock (3 Marks)**
- A permanent shore-based structure with walls, floor, and a gate entrance.
- The gate (or caisson) is removed after flooding to release the ship.
- **Merits:** Highest load-bearing capacity; suitable for the largest ships; permanent and reliable.
- **Demerits:** Very high capital cost; long construction time; occupies valuable waterfront.

**2. Floating Dock (2 Marks)**
- A self-propelled submersible pontoon structure that submerges itself by ballasting.
- The ship is built on the dock floor; the dock is then submerged to float the ship off.
- **Merits:** Mobile; can be relocated; lower site preparation cost.
- **Demerits:** Limited lifting capacity; weather-sensitive; not suitable for the largest ships.

**3. Building Berth with Caisson (2 Marks)**
- An open quay (without permanent walls) closed at the seaward end by a removable caisson.
- The caisson forms a temporary wall; flooding occurs inside the enclosed area.
- **Merits:** Most economical for very large one-off ships; flexible.
- **Demerits:** Requires precise caisson handling; weather-dependent during caisson removal.

*Sketch descriptions to be included as per KTU valuation key — block diagrams of each dock type with ship, blocks, water level, and gates clearly labelled.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
$W_{lw} = 18{,}500$ t, $L_{BP} = 160$ m, $B = 26$ m, $C_B = 0.70$, $C_W = 0.83$, $D = 16$ m, $KG = 7.5$ m.

**(i) Displacement Volume at Float-Off (2 Marks)**

Weight in Newton-force:

$$ W_{lw} = 18{,}500 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 1.81485 \times 10^{8} \text{ N} $$

By Archimedes' principle:

$$ \nabla_{lo} = \frac{W_{lw}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \frac{1.81485 \times 10^{8}}{1025 \times 9.81} $$

$$ \nabla_{lo} = \frac{1.81485 \times 10^{8}}{10055.25} = 18{,}049.8 \text{ m}^3 $$

**(ii) Floating-Off Draft (2 Marks)**

$$ T_{lo} = \frac{\nabla_{lo}}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot C_B} $$

$$ T_{lo} = \frac{18{,}049.8}{160 \times 26 \times 0.70} $$

$$ T_{lo} = \frac{18{,}049.8}{2912} = 6.20 \text{ m} $$

**(iii) Metacentric Height (2 Marks)**

Waterplane area:

$$ A_W = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot C_W = 160 \times 26 \times 0.83 = 3452.8 \text{ m}^2 $$

Second moment of waterplane area:

$$ I_T = \frac{L_{BP} \cdot B^3 \cdot C_W}{12} = \frac{160 \times 26^3 \times 0.83}{12} $$

$$ I_T = \frac{160 \times 17{,}576 \times 0.83}{12} = \frac{2{,}333{,}292.8}{12} = 194{,}441.1 \text{ m}^4 $$

Transverse BM:

$$ BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla_{lo}} = \frac{194{,}441.1}{18{,}049.8} = 10.77 \text{ m} $$

Centre of buoyancy height:

$$ KB = 0.53 \cdot T_{lo} = 0.53 \times 6.20 = 3.286 \text{ m} $$

Metacentric height:

$$ GM = KB + BM - KG = 3.286 + 10.77 - 7.5 = 6.556 \text{ m} $$

**(iv) Stability Comment (1 Mark)**

$$ GM = 6.556 \text{ m} \gg 0.15 \text{ m (KTU minimum)} $$

The ship has **excellent positive initial stability** at the floating-off condition. ✓

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for floating-off a ship from a graving dock**. Include a discussion of the **stability considerations** at the instant of float-off. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship under construction in a graving dock is to be launched by the floating-off method. The following data is given:
- Dock internal dimensions: $L_d = 300$ m, $B_d = 55$ m
- Initial water depth above keel blocks: $h_i = 0.4$ m
- Floating-off draft of ship: $T_{lo} = 5.8$ m (with safety clearance of 0.15 m)
- Flooding pump capacity: $Q = 3.5$ m³/s

Calculate:
(i) The **total volume of water** required to flood the dock.
(ii) The **flooding time** in hours.
(iii) The **number of tugs** required if each tug provides **60 tonnes bollard pull** and the ship's lightweight is **20,000 tonnes**. Use the empirical rule $BP = 1.2\%$ of displacement. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Construction Stage (1 Mark):** The ship is built on keel blocks and bilge blocks inside the dry dock. All underwater fittings (rudder, propeller, sea valves, bow thruster) are installed before flooding.

2. **Pre-Flooding Inspections (1 Mark):** Dock gates are sealed; caisson tested for watertightness. Stability calculations are verified by the Naval Architect. All personnel and equipment removed from the dock floor.

3. **Flooding the Dock (2 Marks):** Sluice valves are opened gradually, allowing seawater to fill the dock. The water level rises **symmetrically** to avoid any list. Rate of rise is monitored to prevent air entrapment under the hull.

4. **Ship Becomes Waterborne (1 Mark):** When buoyancy equals lightweight, the ship lifts off the keel blocks (typically by ~100 mm). The block reaction loads transfer fully to water pressure.

5. **Continued Flooding and Dock Opening (1 Mark):** Flooding continues until the dock water level equals the harbour water level. The dock gates (or caisson) are then carefully removed.

6. **Towing Out (1 Mark):** The ship is held by mooring lines while tugboats are positioned. Once tugs are secured, mooring lines are released and the ship is towed to the fitting-out berth.

**Stability Considerations:**
- The ship is in a **lightweight condition** → low draft, high freeboard.
- Stability must be checked at the **instant of float-off** (minimum freeboard moment).
- **GM must be positive** (typically ≥ 0.15 m).
- Bilge blocks should release uniformly to avoid sudden list.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Total Volume of Water Required (3 Marks)**

Target water depth:

$$ h_f = T_{lo} + 0.15 = 5.8 + 0.15 = 5.95 \text{ m} $$

Rise in water level:

$$ \Delta h = h_f - h_i = 5.95 - 0.40 = 5.55 \text{ m} $$

Volume of water:

$$ V_{water} = L_d \cdot B_d \cdot \Delta h $$

$$ V_{water} = 300 \times 55 \times 5.55 $$

$$ V_{water} = 16{,}500 \times 5.55 = 91{,}575 \text{ m}^3 $$

**(ii) Flooding Time (2 Marks)**

$$ t_f = \frac{V_{water}}{Q} = \frac{91{,}575}{3.5} = 26{,}164.3 \text{ s} $$

Converting to hours:

$$ t_f = \frac{26{,}164.3}{3600} = 7.27 \text{ hours} $$

**(iii) Number of Tugs (2 Marks)**

Required bollard pull:

$$ BP_{total} = 0.012 \times 20{,}000 = 240 \text{ tonnes} $$

Number of tugs (each 60 tonnes BP):

$$ N_{tugs} = \frac{240}{60} = 4 \text{ tugs} $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 Marks):** Mixing tonnes (mass) with kN (force) without conversion. Always convert mass to force using $g = 9.81$ m/s² OR express displacement in **mass tonnes (1 tonne = 1 m³ of seawater)** for hydrostatic problems.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Safety Clearance (–1 Mark):** When calculating dock flooding depth, students often use $T_{lo}$ only and forget the **0.10 m to 0.15 m safety clearance** above keel blocks before the ship is considered "waterborne."
>
> 3. **Confusing BM and GM (–2 Marks):** $BM$ is from hull geometry only; $GM$ includes the KG of the vessel. KTU examiners expect the **complete formula** $GM = KB + BM - KG$, not just the BM value.
>
> 4. **Wrong Sign Convention for Stability (–1 Mark):** If $KG > KB + BM$, then GM is **negative** and the ship **capsizes** at float-off. Students often forget this check.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Freeboard Comment (–1 Mark):** Always state the freeboard $F = D - T_{lo}$ and confirm it is positive; this is a favourite KTU "complete the answer" check.
>
> 6. **Using $C_W$ instead of $C_B$ (–2 Marks):** $C_W$ (waterplane area coefficient) is used for $A_W$ and $I_T$ calculations; $C_B$ (block coefficient) is used for displacement volume $\nabla$. Mixing them is a frequent error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Building Dock Floating-Off Method**

- ✅ **Definition:** Ship is built inside a watertight dock and floated off by flooding.
- ✅ **Types:** Graving Dock, Floating Dock, Building Berth + Caisson, Inclined Building Berth.
- ✅ **Best For:** ULCCs, VLCCs, LNG carriers, aircraft carriers, submarines, and large offshore structures.
- ✅ **Advantage:** **Zero dynamic launching loads** — vessel rises quasi-statically.
- ✅ **Disadvantage:** Very high infrastructure cost; long construction time of the dock itself.
- ✅ **Block System:** Keel blocks (~55% load) + Bilge blocks (~45% load) = Total lightweight.
- ✅ **Float-Off Condition:** $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{lo} \geq W_{lw}$.
- ✅ **Typical flooding time:** 4 to 12 hours (industry standard).
- ✅ **Safety clearance above keel blocks:** 100 mm to 150 mm at float-off.
- ✅ **GM at float-off:** Must be **positive**; typical values 3 m to 8 m for large ships.
- ✅ **Tug requirement:** Total BP ≈ 1.0% to 1.5% of lightweight.
- ✅ **Draft at float-off:** Less than service draft (lightweight condition, no fuel/cargo).
- ✅ **After float-off:** Ship is towed to fitting-out berth for further work and sea trials.
- ✅ **KTU Keywords to Memorize:** *Graving Dock, Caisson, Keel Block, Bilge Block, Waterborne, Floating Draft, Bollard Pull, Buoyancy Curve, Lightweight, Freeboard.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Launching Using Air Bags
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST724 - BASICS OF SHIP CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Launching Using Air Bags

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Bag Launching** is a modern, non-gravity-dependent ship launching technique in which a vessel is lowered into the water using a series of **inflatable, high-pressure rubberized cylindrical bags** (commonly called *ship-launching air bags* or *roller bags*) placed beneath the hull. The bags are inflated to lift the hull off the building blocks, and then progressively deflated in a controlled sequence so the vessel slides/rolls down the inclined slipway into the water.

This method is classified as a **side-launching** or **end-launching** alternative to the conventional **slipway launch**, **graving dock launch**, or **floating dock launch** systems. It is governed by standards such as **CB/T 3837 (China Classification Society)** and **ISO 17357-1** for marine rubber airbags.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Inflatable Cushion"**
>
> Imagine you want to move a heavy wooden wardrobe from your room to the corridor. Instead of dragging it (which scratches the floor) or picking it up (too heavy), you slide several long, inflatable swimming-pool noodles (or rolled-up balloons) under its base. You inflate them one by one, and the wardrobe **rises** on these cushions. Now you roll the wardrobe over them — and to send it "out the door," you let the air out of the front cushion first, then the next, and the next, so it **gently rolls forward** and out.
>
> That is exactly how an **air bag launch** works:
> - The **wardrobe** = the **ship hull** (weight ranging from a few hundred tonnes to over 100,000 tonnes).
> - The **inflatable noodles** = the **high-pressure rubber air bags**.
> - **Inflating** them = the **lifting phase**.
> - **Deflating in sequence** from the water-side = the **launching (rolling) phase**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Specifications

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Bag Outer Diameter ($D$) | **0.5 m to 3.0 m** |
| Bag Effective Length ($L$) | **3 m to 12 m** |
| Working Pressure ($P_w$) | **0.2 MPa to 0.5 MPa** |
| Safe Working Inflation Pressure | **0.05 MPa to 0.25 MPa** |
| Single Bag Load Capacity | **up to 200+ tonnes** |
| Slipway Gradient | **1:12 to 1:20** (typical) |
| Hull-Contact Pressure Limit | **< 0.25 MPa** (to avoid plating damage) |

> [!NOTE]
> The pressure used to *inflate* a bag to roll the ship is typically **much lower** than its **burst pressure**, ensuring a large safety margin. A standard 1.5 m diameter bag with 0.2 MPa working pressure can carry roughly **80–100 tonnes** per metre of effective length.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

The air bag launch exploits a simple physics idea: **rolling friction is far less than sliding friction**, so cylindrical inflated bags act as both a *lift device* and a *roller track*.

1. **Foundation Preparation**: A firm, level ground-standing (or a prepared slipway inclined at 1:12 to 1:20) is laid with a smooth concrete or compacted-sand surface. The **groundways** (heavy timber or steel beams) may be fixed in place, or the bags may be used directly on a flat surface.

2. **Keel Block & Bilge Block Setup**: Wooden **keel blocks** support the ship along its centreline during construction. **Bilge blocks** (port and starboard) support the curved bilge region.

3. **Bag Placement**: Empty, deflated air bags are rolled or slid under the hull between the bilge blocks and the keel blocks. Their axes are aligned **perpendicular to the launching direction** (i.e., across the hull's breadth).

4. **Inflation (Lifting) Phase**: Compressed air is fed simultaneously (or in a controlled sequence) to all bags. As each bag inflates, it expands radially, lifting the hull. When the ship lifts by a height equal to the bag's freeboard-deflection, it bears its full weight on the bags and the keel/bilge blocks are removed.

5. **Deflation (Launching) Phase**: A small release valve (or the inflation line) on the **water-side bags** is opened slightly, allowing the leading bags to deflate progressively. The hull begins to **roll forward** over the trailing (still-inflated) bags. As each bag passes out from under the stern, it is recovered, deflated fully, and repositioned for the next launch.

6. **Buoyancy Take-Over**: Once the bow enters the water, the ship's displacement buoyancy counteracts gravity, the slipway reaction drops to near zero, and the vessel floats free.

## 2.2 Why It Works — The Physics

- **Rolling Friction vs Sliding Friction**: Rolling friction coefficient $\mu_r \approx 0.01$–$0.05$, while sliding friction on greased wood $\mu_s \approx 0.05$–$0.15$. Bags *both lift and roll* the ship.
- **Pressure Distribution**: A cylindrical bag under load flattens at the contact zone, creating a contact patch. The contact pressure is approximately the **internal gas pressure** — meaning the bag self-regulates its footprint to support the load.
- **Self-Balancing**: Because each bag carries load proportional to its contact area, the system is *statically determinate* — heavy regions (engine room, midship) automatically engage more bags.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities use SI units (m, N, Pa). Convert carefully: **1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa = 1 N/mm²**; **1 tonne-force ≈ 9.81 kN**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load per bag | $Q = P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D$ | $P_w$ = working pressure, $L_{eff}$ = effective contact length, $D$ = bag diameter |
| 2 | Number of bags | $n = \dfrac{W_{ship}}{Q \cdot f_s}$ | $W_{ship}$ = total launching weight; $f_s$ = safety factor (**1.5 – 2.0**) |
| 3 | Contact length on bag | $L_c = \dfrac{Q}{P_w \cdot D}$ | Determines how much of the bag actually touches the hull |
| 4 | Contact area per bag | $A_c = L_c \cdot D = \dfrac{Q}{P_w}$ | For hull-plating pressure check |
| 5 | Bag diameter selection | $D = \dfrac{H_{clear} + h_{defl}}{\sin\theta}$ | $H_{clear}$ = required lift height (≈ bag diameter); $h_{defl}$ = groundway thickness |
| 6 | Inflation pressure for lift | $P_{lift} = \dfrac{W_{ship}}{n \cdot L_c \cdot D} + P_{atm}$ | Excess over atmospheric to just lift the ship |
| 7 | Rolling resistance | $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W_{ship} \cdot \cos\alpha$ | $\alpha$ = slipway angle; $\mu_r$ ≈ 0.02 |
| 8 | Launching force required | $F_{launch} = W_{ship}(\sin\alpha + \mu_r \cos\alpha)$ | For checking winch / holding force |
| 9 | Bag freeboard (deflection) | $\delta = D\left(1 - \cos\phi\right)$ | $\phi$ = half-contact-angle of flattened region |
| 10 | Buoyancy at float-off | $W_{ship} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | $\nabla$ = displaced volume at float-off |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL** — In markdown tables the vertical bar is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` so the table does not break. For example, $\mu_r$ (rolling friction) is written **without** any pipe in the table cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Small & medium shipyards** in China, Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and increasingly India use this method because it avoids the **massive capital cost** of a graving dock.
- It enables **end-launching** of vessels built on horizontal ground — no slipway needed in the traditional sense.
- Useful for **barges, tugboats, fishing vessels, pontoons, and small cargo ships** (up to ~DWT 30,000 t in extreme cases).
- Also used for **ship salvage, dock gate movement, heavy load transport**, and **pipeline/cable-laying rollers**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Load Capacity of a Single Air Bag

### Statement
Show that the vertical load a single air bag can support is approximately $Q \approx P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D$.

### Step 1 — Free-Body of the Bag
Consider a cylindrical bag of length $L$ and diameter $D$, inflated to internal gauge pressure $P_w$. The internal gas exerts a force on every internal surface element. Project all forces onto the vertical axis.

### Step 2 — Pressure Force Balance
The internal gas pushes *down* on the upper half of the bag and *up* on the lower half. At the flattened contact patch, the projected area of the contact is $A_c = L_c \cdot D$, where $L_c$ is the longitudinal contact length.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vertical force exerted by gas} &= P_w \cdot A_c \\
&= P_w \cdot (L_c \cdot D)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium with External Load
The bag supports an external vertical load $Q$ applied by the hull. By static equilibrium:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= P_w \cdot L_c \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Maximum Q when Full Contact
In practice, the contact length $L_c$ cannot exceed the bag's full effective length $L_{eff}$ (a design constant, slightly less than the manufactured length to account for end fittings and tapered ends). Hence the **maximum** load per bag is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,Q_{max} = P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Sample Numerical
For $P_w = 0.2$ MPa, $L_{eff} = 6$ m, $D = 1.5$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max} &= 0.2 \times 10^6 \;\text{Pa} \times 6\;\text{m} \times 1.5\;\text{m} \\
&= 1.8 \times 10^6 \;\text{N} \\
&\approx 183.5 \;\text{tonnes-force}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Bags Required

### Statement
Determine the number of air bags $n$ needed to safely launch a ship of launching weight $W$.

### Step 1 — Total Available Capacity
With $n$ bags, each capable of $Q_{max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} = n \cdot Q_{max} = n \cdot P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Safety Factor
To guard against non-uniform weight distribution, bag wear, and dynamic surges during launching, introduce safety factor $f_s$ (typically **1.5–2.0**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W \leq \frac{Q_{total}}{f_s} = \frac{n \cdot P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D}{f_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for n
Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,n = \frac{f_s \cdot W}{P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

Always round **up** to the next whole number; partial bags are not allowed.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Launching Force & Slipway Gradient

### Statement
Compute the force a shipyard winch or groundway brake must resist to control the launch.

### Step 1 — Force Diagram
On a slipway inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal, the ship's weight $W$ has:
- A **down-slope component** $= W \sin\alpha$
- A **normal component** $= W \cos\alpha$

The rolling friction force opposing motion is $\mu_r W \cos\alpha$.

### Step 2 — Net Force Down the Slipway
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{net} = W\sin\alpha + \mu_r W\cos\alpha = W\left(\sin\alpha + \mu_r \cos\alpha\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Holding Force
The winch/brake must supply an *equal and opposite* force to control descent (and, at start-up, to overcome stiction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,F_{hold} = W\left(\sin\alpha + \mu_r \cos\alpha\right)\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Self-Launching Condition
The vessel will slide on its own (no winch needed) when $F_{net} > 0$, i.e., when:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\alpha > \mu_r
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\mu_r \approx 0.02$, this means $\alpha > 1.15°$ (a gradient steeper than **1:50**). Most ship-launching slipways use **1:12 to 1:20** to ensure gravity-driven launch with controlled deceleration by drag chains or drag wedges near the waterline.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Decisions

| Decision | Equation / Criterion | Typical Engineering Choice |
|---|---|---|
| Bag diameter $D$ | $D \geq$ maximum hull curvature radius at bilge | 1.0 m – 2.0 m for most vessels |
| Bag length $L$ | $L \geq$ vessel's beam (so a single bag spans port-to-starboard) | 4 m – 12 m |
| Working pressure $P_w$ | $P_w \leq$ allowable plating stress / contact pressure | 0.2 – 0.4 MPa |
| Number of bags $n$ | $n = f_s W / (P_w L_{eff} D)$ | Round up; add 10 % spares |
| Spacing along hull | $s \leq L/3$ to ensure continuous contact | ~1 m – 3 m |
| Slipway slope | $\tan\alpha > \mu_r$ but $< 0.10$ for control | 1:14 ≈ 4.1° |
| Inflation time | $t \propto$ bag volume / air flow rate | 30 – 60 min for full lift |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders a **Process Flow Diagram** of the air-bag launching sequence. Node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid's syntax rules. Labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text only (no markdown formatting inside quoted strings).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[STAGE 1: SITE PREP] --> A2[LEVEL GROUND OR SLIPWAY]
    A2 --> A3[LAY KEEL BLOCKS AND BILGE BLOCKS]
    A3 --> A4[STAGE 2: BAG PLACEMENT]
    A4 --> A5[ROLL DEFLATED AIR BAGS UNDER HULL]
    A5 --> A6[ALIGN BAGS PERPENDICULAR TO LAUNCH AXIS]
    A6 --> A7[STAGE 3: INFLATION PHASE]
    A7 --> A8[CONNECT COMPRESSED AIR SUPPLY]
    A8 --> A9[INFLATE ALL BAGS SIMULTANEOUSLY]
    A9 --> A10[HULL LIFTS OFF KEEL BLOCKS]
    A10 --> A11[REMOVE KEEL AND BILGE BLOCKS]
    A11 --> A12[STAGE 4: HOLD AND INSPECT]
    A12 --> A13[VERIFY LIFT HEIGHT AND ALIGNMENT]
    A13 --> A14[STAGE 5: LAUNCHING PHASE]
    A14 --> A15[DEFLECT WATER SIDE BAGS SLIGHTLY]
    A15 --> A16[HULL ROLLS FORWARD OVER BAGS]
    A16 --> A17[RECOVER BAGS AS THEY EXIT STERN]
    A17 --> A18[STAGE 6: FLOAT OFF]
    A18 --> A19[BOW ENTERS WATER]
    A19 --> A20[BUOYANCY TAKES OVER LOAD]
    A20 --> A21[VESSEL FLOATS FREE]
    A21 --> A22[STAGE 7: TOW OUT]
```

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Cross-Section Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SLIPWAY_BLOCK [SLIPWAY ASSEMBLY]
        G1[CONCRETE GROUND]
        G2[GROUNDWAY TIMBER]
        G3[AIR BAG CYLINDER]
        G4[FLATTENED CONTACT PATCH]
    end
    subgraph SHIP_BLOCK [SHIP HULL ABOVE]
        S1[KEEL PLATE]
        S2[BILGE CURVATURE]
        S3[FLAT SIDE SHELL]
    end
    subgraph SUPPORT_BLOCK [SUPPORT INFRASTRUCTURE]
        K1[KEEL BLOCK REMOVED]
        K2[COMPRESSED AIR SUPPLY]
        K3[RELEASE VALVE WATER SIDE]
    end
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> S2
    S1 -. formerly rested on .-> K1
    G3 -. fed by .-> K2
    G3 -. released by .-> K3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation**
> - The **flattened contact patch** of the bag (G4) is the region where hull (S2) and bag surface coincide; pressure in this region equals internal gas pressure.
> - Once the **keel block is removed** (K1), all hull weight is supported by the line of bags along the bilge.
> - The **release valve** (K3) on the water-side bags is the only control element that initiates motion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are framed in line with **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern. Bloom's levels tagged for each question.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 model]`
**Q: Define ship launching by air bag method. List any four advantages over conventional slipway launching.** *(Bloom: Remember / Understand)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Ship launching by air bag method is a technique in which a vessel built on a level ground or slipway is rolled into the water using a series of **inflatable, high-pressure rubber cylindrical bags** placed under the hull. The bags are inflated to lift the ship off the building blocks and then progressively deflated to roll the vessel into the water.

> **Any four advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Low capital cost** — no need for an expensive graving dock or permanent slipway infrastructure.
> 2. **Flexibility** — bags can be reused for vessels of different sizes and can be deployed on flat ground.
> 3. **Lower friction** — rolling friction (≈ 0.02) is much less than sliding friction on greased ways (≈ 0.1), giving smoother launch.
> 4. **Reduced hull stress** — distributed contact pressure limits local plating stresses.
> 5. **Shorter construction-to-launch time** — bags are deployed only when needed.
> 6. **Portability** — bags can be transported to remote shipyards.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 model]`
**Q: Explain the role of working pressure and bag diameter in determining the load capacity of an air bag.** *(Bloom: Understand / Apply)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **load capacity** of an air bag is given by $Q_{max} = P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D$. **(1 mark)**
>
> - The **working pressure $P_w$** sets the contact pressure on the hull plating; a higher $P_w$ linearly increases load capacity, but must be kept below the **allowable plating bearing stress** to prevent local buckling or indentation. **(1 mark)**
> - The **bag diameter $D$** determines both the lift height (≈ bag diameter minus deflection) and the contact area; larger diameter bags provide a wider contact patch and greater capacity, but require more inflation air and ground clearance. **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **ESE Pattern (KTU 2024):** Each Module question in Part B carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternatives are given; students answer **one**.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - model paper]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **step-by-step procedure of ship launching by air bag method**. Discuss the **pre-launch, inflation, and launching phases** in detail. *(7 marks, Bloom: Understand / Apply)*

**(b)** A ship of launching weight **$W = 6{,}000$ tonnes** is to be launched using air bags of diameter $D = 1.5$ m and effective length $L_{eff} = 6$ m. The working pressure used is $P_w = 0.25$ MPa. Assuming a safety factor $f_s = 1.6$, determine:
1. Load capacity of a single bag.
2. Number of bags required.
3. Whether 50 bags would suffice (justify with calculation). *(7 marks, Bloom: Apply / Analyze)*

#### Model Solution (a) — Procedure

1. **Pre-Launch Phase** *(2 marks)*
   - Level ground or construct a slipway of slope 1:12 to 1:20.
   - Place keel blocks along the centreline and bilge blocks on both sides.
   - Position the ship on blocks; complete all pre-launch checks (stability, ballast, freeing ports).
   - Roll deflated air bags into position under the hull, between the keel and bilge blocks, axes perpendicular to launching direction.

2. **Inflation Phase** *(2 marks)*
   - Connect a compressed-air manifold to all bags via inflation valves.
   - Inflate **simultaneously and slowly** (typically 30–60 minutes for full lift) to avoid hull impact loading.
   - The hull rises by approximately the bag's diameter (less elastic deflection, ≈ 5–10 % of $D$).
   - Once the keel is clear, remove keel blocks and bilge blocks; the entire weight is now on the bags.

3. **Launching Phase** *(2 marks)*
   - Deflate the **water-side bags slightly** using controlled release valves, creating a small forward tilt.
   - The vessel begins to **roll forward** over the still-inflated trailing bags.
   - As each bag exits from under the stern, it is recovered, deflated, and stored.
   - Drag chains, drag wedges, or winch braking decelerate the vessel near the water's edge.
   - At float-off, the bow enters water, displacement buoyancy takes over, and the vessel is towed away.

4. **Sketch** *(1 mark)* — see Section 4 for the required flow diagram and cross-section.

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical

**Given:** $W = 6000$ t, $D = 1.5$ m, $L_{eff} = 6$ m, $P_w = 0.25$ MPa, $f_s = 1.6$.

**Step 1 — Load capacity of a single bag**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max} &= P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D \\
&= 0.25 \times 10^6 \;\text{Pa} \times 6\;\text{m} \times 1.5\;\text{m} \\
&= 2.25 \times 10^6 \;\text{N} \\
&= 229.4 \;\text{tonnes-force}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer with unit)*

**Step 2 — Number of bags required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{f_s \cdot W}{Q_{max}} \\
&= \frac{1.6 \times 6000}{229.4} \\
&= \frac{9600}{229.4} \\
&\approx 41.85 \rightarrow \lceil n \rceil = 42 \;\text{bags}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical answer)*

**Step 3 — Will 50 bags suffice?**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total,\,50} &= 50 \times 229.4 = 11{,}470 \;\text{t} \\
\text{Required with safety} &= 1.6 \times 6000 = 9{,}600 \;\text{t} \\
\text{Since } 11{,}470 \;\text{t} > 9{,}600 \;\text{t}, \text{ 50 bags are sufficient.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The 50 bags provide an additional margin of $11{,}470 - 9{,}600 = 1{,}870$ tonnes beyond the safety-loaded requirement, which is a healthy 19.5 % reserve. *(1 mark for the comparison and conclusion)*
