### Question B `[KTU University Exam - model paper]`

**(a)** Compare **air bag launching** with **conventional slipway launching** under the following heads: principle, cost, site requirement, hull stress, control during launch, and reuse. *(7 marks, Bloom: Analyze / Evaluate)*

**(b)** Derive the **equation for launching force** $F_{launch} = W(\sin\alpha + \mu_r \cos\alpha)$ for a vessel sliding down an inclined slipway. State the **self-launching condition** and explain why a shipyard uses a slope gentler than 1:8 even though the self-launching condition would be satisfied at much smaller angles. *(7 marks, Bloom: Apply / Analyze / Evaluate)*

#### Model Solution (a) — Comparison Table

| Head | Air Bag Launching | Conventional Slipway Launching |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** *(1 mark)* | Rolling on inflated rubber bags; bags lift and roll the vessel. | Sliding on greased wooden or steel ways (groundways and sliding ways). |
| **Capital Cost** *(1 mark)* | Low — only bags, compressor, and ground preparation required. | High — permanent slipway, groundways, sliding ways, and keel blocks must be constructed. |
| **Site Requirement** *(1 mark)* | Flat or mildly sloped ground; can launch from a hard standing. | Requires engineered inclined slipway (often with a curved launch-way profile). |
| **Hull Stress** *(1 mark)* | Distributed contact along bag length — low local stress. | Concentrated load at bilge ways and sliding ways — higher local stress; ways must be faired. |
| **Launch Control** *(1 mark)* | Excellent — deflation rate is easily throttled valve-by-valve. | Moderate — relies on drag chains, drag wedges, and trigger mechanisms. |
| **Reuse** *(1 mark)* | Highly reusable; bags deflate and can be re-rolled under a new vessel. | Wooden ways are often damaged and partially rebuilt per launch. |
| **Vessel Size** *(1 mark)* | Best for small to medium vessels (up to ~30,000 DWT). | Used for very large vessels including warships and large tankers. |

#### Model Solution (b) — Derivation of Launching Force

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram** *(1 mark)*
A vessel of weight $W$ rests on an inclined slipway of angle $\alpha$. The contact force normal to the slope is $N = W\cos\alpha$. The gravitational component along the slope (down-slope) is $W\sin\alpha$. The rolling-friction force opposing motion is $\mu_r N = \mu_r W\cos\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Net Force Equation** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{net} &= W\sin\alpha - \mu_r W\cos\alpha \\
&= W(\sin\alpha - \mu_r \cos\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Force That Must Be Resisted / Applied** *(1 mark)*
If the ship is being *held* (i.e., we want zero acceleration and zero impending motion), the winch or drag system must apply an *up-slope* force equal to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,F_{launch} = W(\sin\alpha + \mu_r \cos\alpha)\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

(The plus sign appears because the holding force must counter both the down-slope gravity component *and* the up-slope friction component.)

**Step 4 — Self-Launching Condition** *(1 mark)*
The vessel will accelerate on its own when $F_{net} \geq 0$, i.e., when:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W\sin\alpha \geq \mu_r W\cos\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,\tan\alpha \geq \mu_r\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\mu_r \approx 0.02$ (typical for air-bag rolling), self-launching requires $\alpha \gtrsim 1.15°$ (slope steeper than about **1:50**).

**Step 5 — Why a Gentler Slope Is Used in Practice** *(2 marks)*
Although the self-launching threshold is only ~1.15°, shipyards typically use slopes of **1:12 to 1:20** (≈ 4.8° to 2.9°). Reasons:

- **Excessive kinetic energy**: A steep slope gives the ship too much velocity by the time it reaches the water, making it hard to stop and risking **run-up, broaching, or hull impact** with the opposite bank.
- **Uncontrolled buoyancy take-over**: At high entry speed, the rapid transition from bag-support to water-buoyancy causes a **shock load** (slamming) on the bow.
- **Safety of personnel and equipment** near the water's edge.
- **Decelerating drag chains, drag wedges, and winches** are sized for a particular velocity profile — a too-steep slope overwhelms these.

Hence the slope is chosen to give just enough self-launching energy while leaving a controllable velocity margin for the final deceleration phase.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Most common mistake:** Students often **omit the safety factor** in the bag-count formula and arrive at unrealistically low numbers. Always carry $f_s$ in the equation.
> - **Unit trap:** Pressure must be in **Pa** (not MPa) when multiplied by area in m². A pressure of 0.25 MPa equals $0.25 \times 10^6$ Pa. Mixing units is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> - **Direction of friction:** In the launching force equation, friction *opposes motion* — the winch must therefore resist **gravity + friction**, hence the *plus* sign. Students frequently write the minus sign and lose a mark.
> - **Rounding:** Always round *up* the number of bags to the next integer. Writing 41.85 bags implies a fractional bag, which is meaningless.
> - **Forgetting to mention keel-block removal** during the inflation phase is another common omission; it costs 1 full mark in the procedure question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Launching Using Air Bags**

- ✅ **Definition**: A method where a ship is rolled into the water on **inflatable high-pressure rubber cylindrical bags** placed under the hull.
- ✅ **Phases**: Pre-launch → Inflation (lift) → Hold & inspect → Deflation (launch) → Float-off → Tow-out.
- ✅ **Bag axes** are placed **perpendicular to the launch direction** (across the hull's beam).
- ✅ **Load capacity formula**: $Q_{max} = P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D$ — pressure × effective contact length × diameter.
- ✅ **Number of bags formula**: $n = \dfrac{f_s \cdot W}{P_w \cdot L_{eff} \cdot D}$ with $f_s = 1.5$ to $2.0$.
- ✅ **Hull-contact pressure** ≈ internal gas pressure; keep it below **0.25 MPa** to protect plating.
- ✅ **Rolling friction** $\mu_r \approx 0.02$ is much lower than sliding friction, which is the key advantage.
- ✅ **Launching force**: $F_{launch} = W(\sin\alpha + \mu_r \cos\alpha)$.
- ✅ **Self-launching condition**: $\tan\alpha > \mu_r$ (i.e., $\alpha > 1.15°$ for $\mu_r \approx 0.02$).
- ✅ **Slipway slope in practice**: **1:12 to 1:20** (gentler than self-launching threshold) for safe control.
- ✅ **Bag removal**: Keel and bilge blocks are removed **only after** the hull is fully lifted by the inflated bags.
- ✅ **Bags are recovered** from the stern as they exit during the launch; they are **reusable** for subsequent vessels.
- ✅ **Typical vessel size**: small to medium ships up to ~30,000 DWT; not used for very large vessels.
- ✅ **Standards to cite**: ISO 17357-1 (marine rubber airbags), CB/T 3837 (Chinese standard for ship-launching airbags).
- ✅ **Safety reminders**: Always check bag pressure gauges, inspect for abrasions/cuts, maintain a 1.5–2.0× safety factor, and stage drag chains/wedges before launch.
- ✅ **For KTU exams**: Memorise the four formulae ($Q_{max}$, $n$, $F_{launch}$, self-launching condition) and the four phases of the procedure.
